Hamilton notes that their 1963 arrests came under the NAACP while their return to jail in 1964 came under King's Southern Christian Leadership Conference.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: The Monson Motor Lodge Arrest With King
clips.summary: At St. Mary's church Dr. King personally chose Hamilton to join his group to test the Monson Motor Lodge, alongside Reverend Shuttlesworth and a young white woman named Diane Mitchell. Owner Jimmy Brock refused to serve them, police arrested the group as a dog walked beside them, and Hamilton recalls the dog "kissing" Dr. King and King leading prayer and "I Woke Up This Morning with My Mind Stayed on Freedom" in jail before being taken to Jacksonville.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Jackie Robinson Hosts Them in Stamford
clips.summary: After her release Jackie Robinson came to St. Augustine, embraced Hamilton at the Catholic church, and flew her and Ulmer first class to his home in Stamford, Connecticut. The Robinson family hosted them warmly, took them to a museum and a sandal workshop, and his daughter conducted many interviews, with photographs now held by the Jackie Robinson Foundation and young David trailing Hamilton on his horse.
related_events: Jackie Robinson's support of St. Augustine movement

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: College, Samuel White, and Jacksonville
clips.summary: After the Civil Rights Act passed the four enrolled at Florida Memorial College, but Hamilton dropped out for lack of funds when the college relocated, while Samuel White completed about three years. Hamilton remembers White as very smart and devoted to the brotherhood; he died in Germany in 1995 and his mother had his body shipped home, and Ulmer moved to Jacksonville, where she married and stayed.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Kindling the Fire of Change
clips.summary: Reflecting after a break, Ulmer frames their work as a passive, nonviolent demonstration that hurt no one and "just started the kindling of the fire," prompting Dr. Hayling to call in a larger force that finished with Dr. King and the Civil Rights Bill. She adds that the experience shaped her resolve to raise her children differently in Jacksonville and to keep speaking her mind.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Weighing the Cost and Lost History
clips.summary: Looking back, Hamilton feels they accomplished little in St. Augustine, which "remains the same," and that the hardest part is how much of their history was lost even as their children gained more equal opportunity. She speaks of fighting second-class citizenship as the descendants of those who built the country, of raising her family and her son the St. Augustine High quarterback, and of the town pushing out Dr. Hayling and others who put their foot down.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: The FBI File and a Memorial Home
clips.summary: Hamilton reflects that her activism record limited her livelihood but was never destroyed, and that at her 2008 Habitat for Humanity house dedication historian David Nolan presented her with her FBI file, revealing they had been monitored as children aged 14, 15, and 16. She describes her home as a memorial standing beside the cemetery where Willie Carl Singleton, Samuel White, and JoeAnn's mother are buried.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: The Foot Soldiers Monument and 2004 Recognition
clips.summary: Asked about the Foot Soldiers Monument newly placed in the plaza, Hamilton recalls the 2004 acknowledgement of the St. Augustine Four, when their former lawyer's son Earl Johnson Jr. and Mr. Dews spoke at a celebration and the newspapers finally made their story public. She concludes that what they did was right whether or not anyone acknowledged it, because God knows their heart.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: ACCORD, Erasure, and Somebody Will Know
clips.summary: Hamilton credits ACCORD as the first to mark the movement downtown next to Woolworth's, praising Gwendolyn Duncan's work with former City Commissioner Henry Twine and Katherine Twine and her referral of Duncan to Dr. Hayling. She describes St. Augustine sweeping the whole movement under the rug, tearing down the Ponce de Leon site on US 1 and the Monson Motor Lodge and trying to change the Slave Market, until Barbara Vickers, Dr. Hayling, Duncan, and others resolved that somebody would know what happened there.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Interview Opening and Setting
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern oral history program opens the interview at the Bayfront Inn in St. Augustine, Florida, on September 13, 2011, for the Civil Rights History Project. Dr. Robert Hayling is present as a fellow series interviewee, and Mosnier frames the session around St. Augustine's movement history through the monument.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project oral history series, 2011

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Schooling at St. Benedict and Excelsior
clips.summary: Vickers describes being raised in St. Augustine, attending the parochial St. Benedict Catholic school near her home through eighth grade, then graduating from Excelsior High School, the only Black high school in the area, around 1942.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Parents Working the Hotels
clips.summary: Vickers recalls that both parents did hotel work. Her mother waitressed at the hotel that was then the Monson Motor Lodge, riding a bike to work, while her father was the head waiter at the Bennett Hotel a few doors down and the only one in the family with a car. She remembers her mother, grandmother, and aunt all working there in their uniforms.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Her Sisters Gwendolyn and Charlotte
clips.summary: Vickers explains that she was the oldest sister, nine years older than Gwendolyn and nineteen years older than the baby sister Charlotte. She mentions losing the sister beneath her about two years before the interview and says she and her surviving sister talk every day.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Leaving a Racist St. Augustine
clips.summary: Vickers describes her urgency to leave St. Augustine after finishing school, calling it a racist town. She recounts having to move to the back of the grocery line when white customers entered and being forced off the sidewalk, and her resolve to find a better life anywhere else, intending never to return except to visit her parents.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: New York Shipyard and Tack Welding
clips.summary: Vickers recounts moving to New York right after high school, around 1942, and staying about four years. With wartime demand for welders, she was sent to school and learned to tack weld, then got a job on a ship. Being from the South among all white men who first ignored her greetings, she kept saying good morning until she had the whole crew laughing and greeting her first.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Seattle and Boeing Aircraft
clips.summary: Vickers recalls leaving for Seattle expecting to tack weld at Boeing Aircraft, with the New York crew sorry to see her go. In Seattle she found she was being used as a mechanic helper rather than a welder. She accepted it and worked the graveyard shift because she disliked early mornings.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Corrine and the Train to Seattle
clips.summary: Vickers tells the story of persuading her friend Corrine to leave a job she liked and travel with her to Seattle when Vickers was about nineteen. Corrine missed the train, leaving Vickers in tears, but a telegram asked her to wait in Chicago. Reunited there, Vickers rode the El for the first time before they continued on to Seattle together.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Marriage and Return to St. Augustine
clips.summary: Vickers describes coming back to St. Augustine after marrying Adell Vickers, who was from the city and devoted to it. He worked as a bellhop, and after they married he insisted they live in St. Augustine, giving her a week to follow him back. She gave up a good job and returned in the late 1950s, just before the civil rights movement broke out there.
related_events: Post-WWII return migration to the South

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Dr. King Calls Her to Serve
clips.summary: Vickers recalls Dr. Robert B. Hayling as a neighbor across the street whose rallies and meetings she attended. The night Dr. King came to the church, Hayling said he needed people to go to the churches, and King looked at her and asked, young lady, will you go. She said yes, then immediately wondered what she had committed to.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Sent Church to Church for Kneel-Ins
clips.summary: Vickers describes her first kneel-ins, going to churches and being sent by the two young white men to report back to Hayling's office. When she thought she had done her duty and could go home, Hayling told her to go to the next church and the next, and that is how she became fully involved in every meeting.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: The Scott Street Defense Network
clips.summary: Vickers describes Scott Street, eleven houses whose residents each had a job in the kneel-ins, sit-ins, and swim-ins. Julia Conway, Purcell Conway's mother, organized them into a group to protect Hayling, and the West Side Boys took positions in the trees around his house. Vickers worried at his fearlessness while he stayed active in the street.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Phone Alerts When the Klan Rode Through
clips.summary: Vickers describes the network the Scott Street residents formed to call one another when the Klan came through the street. They warned each other to get down because the riders were shooting low, and people would crawl from the refrigerator and open its door for cover. It was real scary, she says, as she and the others kept watch to make sure Hayling got into the house safely.
related_events: 1963 Klan attacks on St. Augustine movement

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: A 3 a.m. Ride and a Night Deposit
clips.summary: Vickers recounts the night her aunt was sick and her car would not start, so she asked Hayling for a ride home around 3 a.m. Instead of driving straight back, he went downtown to make his night deposit with her in the car, telling her plainly he had to do it in case he was not there the next morning. She never rode with him again.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: The Shooting at Hayling's Home
clips.summary: Vickers describes how the group cooked meals for the boys protecting Hayling, but the night of the shooting most of them had fallen asleep when gunfire hit his home. Arriving back, she found an officer blocking Scott Street, and Julia Conway said she had given the heavyset cop a chair. Hayling's dogs, including Madonna and a boxer, were killed.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Sending the Children to Tallahassee
clips.summary: Vickers recalls that after the attack Hayling needed friends to keep his children until he could take them to Tallahassee, and a next-door neighbor offered to keep the girls. His wife, pregnant with their third child, had just gotten off a sofa struck by bullets. Vickers reflects that no one was safe, and Hayling took the children away because there was no telling what would happen.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: The Drive to Petition the Governor
clips.summary: Vickers describes traveling to Tallahassee with Hayling to talk to the governor, going because the movement wanted people from different churches and she was Catholic. She calls it another scary ride, watching behind her for cars that might shoot, recalling how drivers ran them off the road heading to Butler's Beach. They spoke with the governor but it did little good at the time.
related_events: St. Augustine movement appeals to state government

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Her Salon and Her Husband's Work
clips.summary: Asked what she and her husband did for work, Vickers explains she was a beautician with her own home salon, licensed to operate from the house. Adell Vickers worked at a drive-in movie theater and was a skilled gambler who always had money, though she disliked that he had no steady job. Solomon Calhoun, her former high school basketball coach, later got him a position at the Recreation Center.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Her Husband, Father Figure to the Kids
clips.summary: Vickers describes her husband as recreation director and a father figure who put children first, once buying a boy shoes so he could play softball. Mothers asked him to find their children, and he always knew where they hung out, scolding them and sending them home. The children answered him with respect.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Keeping the Salon Open Under Threat
clips.summary: Asked how she managed through the stress and violence, Vickers explains she hung blankets in the shop so no light showed outside, since most customers were afraid to be seen there, just as Hayling's patients feared coming to his office. She got by because Adell Vickers was a good provider, and she enjoyed the work anyway, often not charging mothers with several children at Easter even when some con customers never brought back the money they owed.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: The Klan and the Tire Iron Arrest Threat
clips.summary: Asked whether the Klan marched openly down Scott Street, Vickers says they marched in Lincolnville and came through Scott Street like bullies, shooting and intimidating. She recounts coming from a rally when a cop stopped her, searched her trunk, and called the tire iron a weapon he could arrest her for, leaving her nervous and powerless.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: A Drive to Be Part of the Change
clips.summary: Vickers describes still going back and forth every night despite a car that broke down on every corner, driven by a need to witness the meetings herself rather than trust secondhand accounts. She recalls that after Hayling finally brought Dr. King and King asked her to participate in the churches, she thought that if there was going to be a change, this was the time, and people could not just sit and wait but had to take part.
related_events: King's involvement in the St. Augustine movement

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Her Grandmother Begged Her to Stop
clips.summary: Vickers describes her family's fear: one sister was afraid and away at Bethune-Cookman College in Daytona, and her terminally ill grandmother tried to keep her from going. Watching the protests on TV, her grandmother warned the activism would get her killed and said white people would never let them eat together. Vickers answered that they wanted to be free and pay their money at the restaurant, and told her plainly she was going anyway because the time was right.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: No Children of Her Own Yet
clips.summary: Asked whether she had children at the time, Vickers says she had a son she inherited at five years old, now a certified professional and the best thing that ever happened to her, but she did not have children of her own during the movement years and so was not also watching over them.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Refusing the Life of a Maid
clips.summary: Asked for her most vivid memories, Vickers reflects on the few who were genuinely evil and on her refusal to do housework for white families. She describes the humiliation of having to enter through the back door to fetch a newspaper from the front porch, and says she never wanted to live that kind of life, which is partly why she went to New York and did factory work.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Mrs. Hayling and the Family's Strain
clips.summary: After a short break, Vickers describes Mrs. Hayling as a very quiet, sweet, low-key person rarely seen outside, appearing mainly when she brought her two little girls across the street for hair care. Reflecting on the family's strain, Vickers recounts meeting Mrs. Hayling later in Fort Lauderdale at Christmas, where the traumatized woman kept crossing the street until Vickers identified herself, then explained a relative had just been robbed and she was trying to get the dogs home.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Missing the First Visit With Dr. King
clips.summary: Asked about further contact with Dr. King, Vickers explains that the first time he came, she and Hayling set up business owners to meet him, but as president of the beautician association she had to attend a convention in Daytona instead. She asked Queenie Mitchell McCall to represent the beauticians, telling her King was not yet famous, and only later regretted missing the chance.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Shaking Dr. King's Hand
clips.summary: Vickers describes the night she finally met King, arriving late because of a customer and finding no room at St. Paul AME Church, so she went to First Baptist where Reverend Abernathy was speaking. King came over afterward, and everyone got to shake his hand. She remembers his hands as soft as cotton and his reputation for never forgetting a name.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: The Insurance Man at Grace Methodist
clips.summary: Asked how she responded to being turned away at kneel-ins, Vickers recounts going to Grace Methodist Church, where a white Metropolitan Life insurance agent who collected her premium every Monday stood over her kneeling form with his foot positioned as if to kick. When he came to her door the next day she warned him never to return and dropped the policy, refusing to talk to him after he had treated her that way at his church.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Why They Call Her a Fanatic
clips.summary: Vickers compares the era to the film The Help, telling her niece that people really did live that way and that mothers with babies to feed had to step out of line and wait to get baby food. She says people call her a fanatic about civil rights, but she felt good knowing they would finally have a chance and that the children of today would not have to endure all of that.
related_events: Civil rights and voting rights legacy

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: What the Monument Means to Her
clips.summary: Vickers expresses disbelief that people are not donating to help build the monument, while her sister explains they simply do not feel about it the way she does. To Vickers the monument means the world because it recognizes everyone who came, not only Hayling, Dr. Young, and Dr. King, but the people who put their lives on the line, including college students and white participants who gave up money and safety.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Insisting on a Foot Soldiers Monument
clips.summary: Vickers explains that the monument idea came to her about ten years earlier, when other groups were discussing a King monument rather than one for the foot soldiers. She wanted to honor the rank-and-file participants, and when she talked to Dr. Hayling he urged her to settle for a plaque or marker. She refused, insisting they would get a full monument.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Organizing Through the Council on Aging
clips.summary: Asked how she began organizing, Vickers describes the Council on Aging, where director Kathy Brown formed a Mayor's Senior Advisory Committee to advise the city. After the first president quit, Vickers was made president and kept the effort alive, correcting a sign-up process that had been blocking open meetings and pressing forward across changes in the mayor's office.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Choosing the Foot Soldiers Over King
clips.summary: Vickers tells Kathy Brown her dream of a foot soldiers monument, and Brown advises that a Martin Luther King monument would draw far better participation and get done quickly. Vickers insists she wants the foot soldiers, the people who really put their lives on the line, and resolves to organize her own group and secure 501(c) status to make it happen.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: First Donations and a UPS Grant
clips.summary: Vickers recounts that a donor she names as Nina Verilam called and, asking what it would take to get started, pledged the roughly $20,000 needed to retain the sculptor, leaving Vickers speechless. Other donations came in, and her grandson, who works for UPS, pursued a company grant that ultimately came through at around $7,400, convincing her there was no stopping the effort.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Signing the Sculptor and Raising More
clips.summary: Vickers explains the budget reached roughly $70,000, up from an early estimate near $60,000. The sculptor came to the plaza to sign the contract and received $20,000 to begin. A supporter named Phil McDaniel told her she was almost there, and when she said they needed at least another $20,000, he promised to raise it in one night.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: The Dinner at Patrick Canan's Office
clips.summary: Vickers describes meeting at attorney Patrick Canan's office, where she told the civil rights story and her reason for honoring the foot soldiers. Canan had his secretary invite guests at $100 a head, asking only that she bring people who took part in the movement to speak and provide a little music. She recruited Del Kahn to play and Freddie Thompson, who had been under the hedges with her the night Andrew Young was beaten and the rocks kept coming.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Only One Black Church Gave
clips.summary: Vickers recounts that the dinner raised $24,000 and the money seemed to keep coming, yet she was upset, telling her sister she could not understand why. She had expected every Black church in St. Augustine to help, but only one small, poor church sent $100. When Phil invited the American Legion, which she had pursued in vain, they finally came through with their money.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Bringing the Monument to the Plaza
clips.summary: Mosnier frames how little St. Augustine had done to mark its civil rights history, and asks how Vickers navigated placing a prominent monument on the central plaza. She describes going to the City Commission with a proposal while two rival groups feuded over their own monument ideas. She left one feuding group, joined another, then organized fresh after backers doubted it would work, eventually proceeding with Kathy.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: The City's Slow and Late Help
clips.summary: Asked about the city government's reaction, Vickers says the city was very slow and did not help as she had hoped it might. City Commissioner Earl Jones, who was African American, said he had $5,000 allotted for downtown work and would give it, though it came down to the city using the money for prep work at the last minute. The city later paid for the walkway with footsteps.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Where the Money Came From
clips.summary: Vickers credits good rapport from the mayor, city manager, and city attorney on legal questions, even as she notes the senior advisory effort still has far to go. Nearly all the money raised came from the white business community, with very little from the Black community beyond small individual gifts. Hank Thomas of the Freedom Riders gave a sizable donation, while major support came from Phil McDaniel, Patrick Canan, and the white attorneys they gathered.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Overcome at the Installation
clips.summary: Asked how the installation day felt, Vickers says she just lost it as it was happening. Twelve family members came from Fort Lauderdale, and though they said she did not seem nervous, she was. Worn down by the long campaign, she was lost for words when called to speak, so she waved the crowd off and they laughed and applauded. Hayling then quietly credits her with the tremendous organization and determination that carried the monument through.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: A Monument to Define the City
clips.summary: After the break, Mosnier reflects as an outsider that giving this history a physical presence through a piece of art on the central plaza is a significant act that may carry long and important implications for the city in the years to come. Vickers answers simply that she hopes so.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: How the Work Changed Her View
clips.summary: Invited to share final thoughts, Vickers says the years of work on the committee changed her perspective on white people, having carried a bitter taste and expecting their true colors to turn. Instead the effort brought closeness with people who took part in the movement, including her enduring friendship with Dr. Hayling, which means a great deal to her.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: At 88, Still Ready to Go
clips.summary: Vickers invokes her grandmother's stories of people dragged from their homes, lynched, and caught after dark in the plaza, measuring her own experience against that history. She says there is still so much to do, that neither she nor Hayling can do it all, but they can keep young people motivated, and at 88 she is still ready to go. Mosnier thanks her and closes the interview.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Opening and the Blues at the Smithsonian
clips.summary: David Cline opens the April 2013 Los Angeles session, and Caldwell recalls teaching at Howard, working with Bernice Johnson Reagon, and talking blues with Alan Lomax and Bill Barlow.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: A Grandfather Who Was a Power Broker
clips.summary: Caldwell traces his family of migrant farmers and his light-skinned grandfather, an uneducated man who bought property, knew the mayor, and worked as a segregated-theater projectionist.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Deming, a Border Crossroads
clips.summary: He describes Deming, New Mexico as a crossroads of Pancho Villa, Geronimo, Cochise, and the Butterfield Overland Mail, a place of constant influx and Boy Scout stories.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: The Colored Baseball Team
clips.summary: His grandfather ran a barnstorming colored baseball team of Indian, Asian, and Black players across the Southwest, while his father, a train foreman, had reached Marquette.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Storytellers and the Boxcar
clips.summary: Caldwell recalls storytellers who performed the Signifying Monkey and once killed a rattlesnake mid-tale, and his early life lived partly in a railroad boxcar.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Born in 1945, Around the Movies
clips.summary: Born in 1945, Caldwell spent his early years with his grandfather and was always around the movie world as his grandfather projected drive-in films.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: The Only Black Child in the White School
clips.summary: By an accident of which side of the railroad track he lived on, Caldwell became the only Black child at the all-white Smith School, was pelted with rocks, then learned to fight and became a jock.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: A Proud Family Stands Up
clips.summary: With about 150 Black migrants in town, Caldwell's father taught his children pride and never to cow-tail to anyone over race, a lesson Caldwell passed on to his siblings.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Cowboys, Police, and a Town's Protection
clips.topic: Standing Up in a Pioneer Town
clips.summary: Caldwell recounts his brothers proving themselves to cowboys and his sister fighting a New Mexico cop, after which the hometown judge, knowing the respected family, let them off, an outcome he says would have meant prison or death elsewhere.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Photography, the Desert, and the Draft
clips.summary: After six months at an Arizona art school, Caldwell was drafted, and on returning began photographing his family and the desert, seeing Black skin against the earth.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Vietnam Strips Away the Veil
clips.summary: Vietnam pulled the veil from Caldwell's eyes and ended his religiousness when preachers sanctioned killing; he saw shared humanity in the Montagnards, read Malcolm X, and reflected on pride mislabeled as militancy.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Trapped in the Army, Refusing the Knife
clips.summary: Hating to be trapped, Caldwell refused exploratory knee surgery, was made an ambulatory patient, and was shipped to Japan.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Learning Bureaucracy in Japan
clips.summary: In Japan he sought out power, eating Thanksgiving with the American ambassador and Christmas with the base general, then used that schooling when shipped back to Vietnam during the Tet Offensive.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: A Pacifist in the War Zone
clips.summary: During Tet, Caldwell's unit pulled an old man in, refused an order to kill him, and walked nearly 150 villagers to safety; he resolved to become a pacifist in a war zone, killing only to survive.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Additive Paint and Phoenix College Football
clips.summary: Out of the military, Caldwell added paint to his photographs of Black bodies in the desert, work that won him a full ride to UCLA, and recalls proving himself as a fullback at Phoenix College while refusing to be called boy.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Quitting Economics for Revolution
clips.summary: He abandoned economics when it could not predict the depression it studied, turning to still photography and a revolutionary-philosophy class taught by a defected KGB agent.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: The Artist Who Crosses Every Border
clips.summary: Caldwell argues the artist survives every revolution because art, unlike politics, lets people communicate across borders, a lesson he carried teaching Nazis at CalArts and entering Cuba and Ghana as a spiritual person.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Art as a Political Act
clips.summary: Pressed on whether art is still political, Caldwell explains he runs his space like television, renting to everyone, even anti-police events, refusing the police's demand for an understanding.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Founding the Community Video Workshop
clips.summary: Returning to Los Angeles after Howard, Caldwell won a grant as one of the state's first video artists and ran workshops in Shirley Clarke's media-democracy spirit, putting cameras in children's hands.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: The Fused Art World of 1970s Watts
clips.summary: Caldwell describes the holistic Los Angeles art scene he found in 1971 around Watts and John Outterbridge: fused musicians, poets, and painters, African co-ops, drum classes, and the Crenshaw performers that drew him in.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Los Angeles and the History of Forgetting
clips.summary: Using his ethnographic instinct, Caldwell uncovered Los Angeles as a place colonized by Black Hispanics, the Calle de los Negros, and what Norman Klein called the history of forgetting, which became his banner.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Electronic Cafe and Global Black History
clips.summary: From the 1984 Electronic Cafe that linked communities through early internet, Caldwell pursued the confluence of cultures and a global Black history of Calafia, Black Moors, the Phoenicians, and Hannibal's Carthage.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Project Blowed and Hip-Hop as Remix
clips.summary: Caldwell founded KAOS Network and worked with young people, framing hip-hop as historical remix and homage, introducing kids to James Brown even as they scratched up his albums.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Filming the History of African People
clips.summary: He describes his films Babylon Is Falling, Medea, and the chant-driven work compressing the history of African people into minutes, fusing animation, Amiri Baraka's poetry, and a heartbeat into visual ritual.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: I and I, Capoeira, and the Nubian
clips.summary: Caldwell explains his film I & I on the making of the other, his drive to infuse soul into film's rhythm, capoeira as a coded martial art, and his planned Nubian series tracing the unwritten western migration.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Against Blaxploitation
clips.summary: Caldwell distinguishes true stories like Superfly, Shaft, and Sweet Sweetback from the imitations he calls blaxploitation, work that exploits conditions society itself created.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Music as Cosmology
clips.summary: He argues that Black music is a hologram of African cosmology seeded by the Egyptians, and that the great migrations followed invasions by figures like Alexander and Caesar.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: The UCLA Cohort and the L.A. Rebellion
clips.summary: As the first film students of color in UCLA's regular program, treated as ethnographic others, Caldwell and about thirteen peers gathered around Haile Gerima, won and lost the grants, and produced some fifty-five films.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Naming the Rebellion, Embraced in Europe
clips.summary: Caldwell reflects on the L.A. Rebellion name, the group's stand against blaxploitation, and how their work was celebrated in Europe while shown only in American museums and colleges.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Repurposing Payphones With Sankofa
clips.summary: Caldwell describes his current Sankofa-principled work repurposing payphones into Wi-Fi totems and computers, developed with a diverse class at the School of Innovation to serve the poor and vulnerable.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Occupying the Virtual Space
clips.summary: He frames the project as occupying virtual space with Black iconography to resist gentrification, hacking a privately owned payphone in front of his building and meeting LA Metro to help define what they dream toward.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Mapping the Neighborhood's Genius
clips.summary: Caldwell shows the label and Phoenix logo, QR-coded payphone ideas, and a map of the cultural density of a five-mile radius where nearly every famous Black figure once lived or worked, an economic engine he wants recognized.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Phantom Galleries and Empty Buildings
clips.summary: He showcases cutting-edge young musicians and religious hip-hop at his space and the phantom-galleries effort that filled nine empty buildings with art and helped upgrade a derelict neighborhood theater.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Cuba, the Babalawos, and an Army of Healers
clips.summary: In 1999 Caldwell took Black yoga teachers to Cuba to tap cultural memory through the Babalawos, learning of immunization-based body markings studied by physicists and a society that sent healers and 300,000 soldiers against apartheid.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Cuban Music and the Limits of Socialism
clips.summary: Caldwell recalls Los Munequitos de Matanzas, watching hip-hop age across eighteen years, and the heavy bureaucracy of Cuban socialism that nonetheless guaranteed school, healthcare, and an enriched artistic life.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: The Rodney King Verdict and the Uprising
clips.summary: Caldwell describes the shock of the Rodney King acquittal and the multicultural rage that followed, the anarchy of broad-daylight looting, and his suspicion at how thoroughly the buildings burned.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Saving the Gallery, the Korean Community
clips.summary: He recounts neighbors saving a Black gallery of African sculpture from fire and the complicated standing of Korean families, whom he saw as the Black people of Asia, tied to Black soldiers from Vietnam.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: The Emmett Till Parallel and the Gang Myth
clips.summary: Drawing on a study, Caldwell compares the uprising's spark to the whisper that killed Emmett Till and argues the gang and crack epidemics were too organized and well armed to be homegrown, pointing to outside agents.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Refusing to Film Degradation
clips.topic: Art Over the Documentation of Pain
clips.summary: Caldwell explains he cannot film people in states of degradation, choosing instead works like Word Sound and Power that make flowers from compost, and his lifelong distance from a communism he found bureaucratic and racist, before the closing credit.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Born in 1939 to a Professor's Family
clips.summary: Garman Robinson was born in New York City in 1939 to a father who rose from a Pennsylvania German farm to a physics and chemistry professorship at NYU, and a working-class mother of Irish and German descent who was the first in her family to attend college.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: A Wartime Childhood in Cambridge
clips.summary: During World War II the family moved to Cambridge so her father could work at the MIT radiation lab, and she remembers the air raids and blackout curtains she later connected to her anti-war feeling, before a move to suburban Pleasantville.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: A Father Tormented by Corporate Life
clips.summary: She recalls her father coming home anguished by the backbiting of corporate science, a memory that fixed her and her brother against corporate America, alongside her mother's deep prejudice against anyone of color.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Managerial Scientist and Class Unease
clips.summary: Garman Robinson describes her father's managerial role atop his scientific knowledge, the cutthroat competition from non-scientists, and how both parents felt unaccepted in their new class and never shared their deepest feelings.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Confronting Her Mother's Racism
clips.summary: After she and her brother hosted a party for YMCA-camp counselors including a Black Vassar student, she found her mother weeping in the basement over the visit, a slur on her lips, a moment that crystallized the contradiction in her mother's prejudice.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Race and Religion in a White Town
clips.summary: She reflects on the near-total absence of Black students in her schooling, her confusion about religious difference, and a phase of fervent Catholicism that ended in a Cornell confessional when a priest condemned her for reading Locke.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: A Godmother and a Mother's Isolation
clips.summary: Garman Robinson tells how her Mexican godmother Lucy Droz became part of the family yet drew her mother's contempt, reading the episode as a sign of her mother's deep isolation and her reflexive fear of communism.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Finding Politics at Skidmore
clips.summary: At Skidmore she stumbled into the National Student Association, and the 1958 National Student Congress, where students debated apartheid and segregation, was the life-changing experience that turned her from a psychology to a political science major.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: First Campaigns and the Loyalty Oath
clips.topic: Student Voice and the Loyalty Oath
clips.summary: As National Student Association chairman she took up campus democracy and the fight against the National Defense Education Act loyalty oath, applying the civil-liberties course she had taken and learning the citizen's role in a democracy.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Supporting the Sit-Ins at Saratoga
clips.summary: When the 1960 sit-ins broke out, four heated campus meetings led 200 Skidmore women to march on the local Woolworth's, and the first students sent to picket the next day were taken to the police station.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Shut Down, and the Road Not Taken to Shaw
clips.summary: The college president shut the protest down and spring break dissipated its energy, and though she considered attending the Shaw University meeting that founded SNCC that same break, fear of her mother's reaction held her back.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Awakening to White Privilege
clips.summary: Garman Robinson describes how the courage of the sit-in students pulled the cover off the country's contradictions for her, exposing the white privileges, GI Bill, FHA and VA loans, she had never noticed, and recalls Ella Baker's reminder that it was more than a hamburger.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The CIA Behind the Student Association
clips.summary: Recruited after graduation into the International Student Relations Seminar, she later understood it as a training ground for National Student Association officers and the CIA, whose international program quietly funneled student travelers' notes on foreign movements to the agency.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Witting: How the Vetting Worked
clips.summary: She explains how the CIA planted agents in the seminars, vetted officers for sympathy with US foreign policy, and let them in on the secret with the code word witting, and how Tim Jenkins, distrusted, was maneuvered from international to national vice president.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Algerian Students and California
clips.summary: At the 1960 Minnesota congress she was swept up by the energy of visiting Algerian revolutionary students, then took a job as a National Student Association campus traveler selling Europe tours up and down the West Coast.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Meeting SDS, and Casey Hayden
clips.summary: Back in Philadelphia she organized the Wisconsin student congress and met the SDS circle around Al Haber and Tom Hayden in the run-up to Port Huron, and remembers the electric charge of Casey Hayden stepping out on a panel supporting the sit-ins.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Recording Resumes: The Early Years Retold
clips.summary: After a recording break the interview restarts from the top, and Garman Robinson retells her professor father, her prejudiced mother, the YMCA-camp incident, and her break from Catholicism, before the conversation moves forward to 1963.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Singing at the Howard Meeting
clips.summary: Her strongest memory of the 1963 Thanksgiving SNCC meeting at Howard is the singing, a circle of freedom songs and tears at We Shall Overcome that gave her the emotional connection and courage she had not grown up with.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Helsinki, and Choosing SNCC Over SDS
clips.topic: World Youth Festival and the SDS Split
clips.summary: She recounts the CIA-funded 1962 World Youth Festival in Helsinki, where American students were sent as an anti-communist alternative to the Soviet bloc line, and the bitter anti-communism of the League for Industrial Democracy that fractured early SDS.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Affirmation in SNCC
clips.summary: Recalling the screaming LID-SDS split and her own wallflower silence among SDS men, she explains that SNCC was where she was affirmed, by James Forman and Casey Hayden, given concrete work, and drawn by an organizing energy SDS lacked.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Coming South to the Atlanta Office
clips.summary: She contrasts the male-dominated SDS with SNCC, then describes coming South in March 1964 to work alongside Dinky Romilly as Northern Coordinator and going to the Greenwood office for Freedom Summer.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Inside the Atlanta Office
clips.summary: Garman Robinson paints the Atlanta office as a dedicated family of support workers like Wilson Brown and Willie McCray, and explains the WATS line and the collect-call operator tricks that let projects report in twice a day while dodging phone charges.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Southern Student Freedom Fund
clips.summary: She describes the Southern Student Freedom Fund, a coalition of four student organizations that raised money for expelled students, bail, and field secretaries, and underscores how black students drove the work forward.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Privilege, and a Family's Alarm
clips.topic: Black Leadership and Her Own Privilege
clips.summary: She affirms that black students led and sacrificed in ways she as a Northerner with a degree did not, and recounts her family's conviction that she had been brainwashed even as they shipped an air conditioner to the sweltering Greenwood office.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Where She Lived and What She Was Paid
clips.summary: Garman Robinson recalls the integrated and Black freedom houses she moved through in Atlanta, the question of who paid the rent, and SNCC's subsistence pay of nine dollars and sixty-four cents a week that rose to forty dollars for Atlanta staff.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Work of the Northern Coordinator
clips.summary: As Northern Coordinator she wrote handwritten thank-you notes for unsolicited donations and coordinated the Friends of SNCC offices, relaying daily reports from McComb, Greenwood, Albany, and Selma and channeling them into rallies and press calls, all without any science of messaging.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Documenting the Atrocities
clips.summary: She details how the office paid the projects, documented the daily brutality of each community, and coordinated with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund, SCLC, CORE, and church bodies, taking up fights over the ASCS farm subsidies and school desegregation under the Civil Rights Act.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Print Shop and the Fundraising Appeal
clips.summary: Garman Robinson describes the Atlanta print shop and the Student Voice, the late-night envelope-stuffing parties, and a fundraising appeal that framed the movement as a fight for democratic ideals rather than charity for the poor.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Into the Field at Greenwood
clips.summary: She recalls co-editing Hands on the Freedom Plow decades later, her pull toward the field even while safe in the office, and going canvassing for voter registration in Greenwood with summer volunteer Peter Orris.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Federal Programs Investigation
clips.summary: Taking up federal-programs work in 1965, she traveled to Arkansas to collect affidavits on discriminatory benefits and, with Marion Barry, combed the Office of Education's files in Washington, exposing the dispensations granted to foot-dragging school districts in a report presented with John Lewis.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: After Atlantic City: Soul-Searching and Selma
clips.summary: She frames the rejection of the MFDP delegation as a turning point that sent SNCC into deep soul-searching over the Black Belt summer project, local organizing, and federal benefits, just as Selma erupted and SNCC chafed at Dr. King's arrival.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Plane to Selma and the Car Fleet
clips.summary: After the Edmund Pettus Bridge attack she chartered a plane to rush staff to Selma, and she recalls the forty-car Sojourner Motor Fleet bought at dealer cost through the United Auto Workers, and the unwritten history of where the movement's money came from.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Privilege, Structure, and Funding Fights
clips.summary: She reflects on how white and educational privilege let her carve out the federal-programs role, where she stood in SNCC's structure debate, and the unwritten resentment that Mississippi drew volunteers and money while Georgia, Alabama, and Arkansas felt like stepchildren.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Waveland and the Women's Position Paper
clips.summary: Garman Robinson describes the Waveland meeting where papers were written on every theme, including the anonymous women's position paper later tied to Mary King and Casey Hayden, and explains why, feeling affirmed and holding the freedom struggle paramount, she stayed out of women's liberation then.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The DC Office, and Leaving SNCC
clips.summary: Moving full-time to the Washington office, she worked on Free DC and a bus boycott under Marion Barry, felt the staff sidelined by his alliance with an older outsider, and finally quit with a letter protesting that the internal culture had stopped being participatory.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Racial Dynamics and the Closest White People
clips.summary: She gives a candid account of SNCC's racial dynamics, the interracial relationships and Black-only parties, the absence of anyone to school white staff on privilege, and her understanding that as the closest white people, organizers like her absorbed a justified Black anger, once a blow on an 18th Street sidewalk.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Whites in the White Community
clips.summary: Garman Robinson examines why SNCC's push for whites to organize the white community faltered, the harassment such volunteers met, the Southern Student Organizing Committee, and the closed, clan-versus-us thinking that kept activists inside their own beloved community of struggle.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: SURJ and Black Lives Matter
clips.summary: She traces Showing Up for Racial Justice from its 2009 origins through Trayvon Martin and Ferguson to its accountability work with Black Lives Matter, and her own door-knocking with Black Lives Matter signs after the 2015 Baltimore uprising.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Highlander
clips.summary: Visiting Highlander at last in 2015, she recounts its 1930s roots bringing Black and white workers together, its citizenship education with Septima Clark and Rosa Parks, and the repression that forced it to move.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: The Years Between: Factory Work and Public Health
clips.summary: Garman Robinson sketches the four decades after SNCC, a Black community arts program, the anti-war and women's movements, eight years in a Baltimore factory, taking medical students on factory tours, asbestos screening, and fourteen years at Johns Hopkins public health.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: A Return to Organizing
clips.summary: The organizing bug returned through the National Organizers Alliance, leading her to a lead-organizer job in Baltimore neighborhood and anti-crime work and to writing community-college curriculum aimed at growing the number of organizers of color.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Closing Reflections
clips.summary: In closing she reflects on what a movement is and how briefly its window stays open, the duty of the white community to put its resources behind Black-led organizations, the value of scholarship and history, and her conviction that the Black struggle must stay at the center.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Laugh, and a Character Built on Love
clips.summary: Russell traces his famous laugh to his mother's advice never to hold anything back, and roots his character in never doubting that both parents loved him, and in his father's lesson that other people's problems with him were their own little red wagon.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Old Man Faces Down the Klan
clips.summary: He tells how his grandfather, the old man, scattered a Klan mob with birdshot and later, finding no school for Black children, organized neighbors to buy lumber and build a schoolhouse over a white merchant's threats, then raised fifty-three dollars to hire a teacher.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Named for a College President
clips.summary: Russell describes his family's leap from a grandfather with no schooling to a daughter at Harvard Law, his mother's reverence for education that led her to name him Felton after a university president, and her deathbed plea that his father send the boys to college.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Widower Raises His Two Boys
clips.summary: After his mother died in 1946 when he was twelve, his father refused to let relatives split the boys up, sold his business, and took a foundry job at a fraction of the pay so he could be home every night, and Russell recalls their cooking rotation and the father who once carried the whole family across a field.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Hundred Thousand Dollars, and a Father's Pride
clips.summary: When Russell heard Wilt Chamberlain had signed for a hundred thousand dollars he demanded a dollar more, then called his father to say he could stop working, only to hear his father refuse the money: he had given the factory his best years and would now give them the bad ones.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Leaving Louisiana in the Great Migration
clips.summary: Russell recounts his father's place in the Great Migration, a boss who refused a raise and used a slur to his face, his father's fear that staying would end in killing, and the move to Detroit and Ford against a draft-board threat meant to keep him in the plant.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Oakland, Shipyards, and a Fruit Truck
clips.summary: Finding Detroit too cold, his father moved the family to Oakland for shipyard work, and when the war ended bought a truck and built a business hauling fruit pickers, the labor that bought young Russell his school clothes.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Cut From the Team, Found by a Coach
clips.summary: Raised by a mother who kept him away from white people, Russell hit cultural shock at the University of San Francisco, where he was cut from the junior varsity until a varsity coach, his old junior-high teacher, took him on and bought him a year at the boys' club where he taught himself to play.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Coach Who Hated His Game
clips.summary: Russell tells how his college coach saw his style as a waste of a scholarship, how four varsity players objected to playing with Black teammates, and how in his first varsity game he blocked five straight shots only to be told that was not how to play defense.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bringing the Vertical Game
clips.summary: He describes bringing a vertical game to a sport that forbade leaving your feet, a leap that could mark chalk thirteen feet up, and how, though his purist coach never approved, he and his teammates won fifty-five straight games and back-to-back national championships.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Learning the Game by Imagination
clips.summary: Russell explains how he used his imagination to learn basketball, studying first-class players down to their footwork and head fakes and then trying the moves himself the next day, picturing what he would become before he arrived.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Finish Every Fight You Start
clips.summary: He remembers his mother marching him through the projects to fight five boys one at a time, telling him it mattered not that he lost but that he stood up for himself, a toughness balanced by his philosophical father, whom he later thanked with a car that earned the old man his first speeding ticket.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: 1956: Final Four, Olympics, and Gold
clips.summary: Russell recalls the extraordinary year of 1956, a second straight national title over Iowa and an Olympic gold medal won by beating the Soviet Union by thirty-five, before he married and headed to Boston as a near amateur.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Celtics' Quiet Firsts
clips.summary: He credits owner Walter Brown and the Celtics with a run of firsts often overlooked: the first Black player drafted in Chuck Cooper, the first team to start five Black players, almost by accident, and the first to draft a player from a Negro college in the first round, Sam Jones.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Becoming the First Black Coach
clips.summary: Russell describes how Red Auerbach, retiring, promised to hire no coach Russell did not approve, and how after their lists of names matched no one, Russell took the job himself in 1966 as a player-coach, refusing an assistant in the charged Boston atmosphere.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Red Auerbach's System
clips.summary: He recounts learning to coach as captain when Auerbach was ejected, the late-night gin games where they talked philosophy, Auerbach's rule of taking players as they were and building a place for each in the system, and his willingness to get thrown out of games to fight for his team.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Sharman's Fists and Protecting Cousy
clips.summary: Russell remembers Bill Sharman, the most thoughtful player he knew and a constant fighter, and the night Auerbach had Jim Loscutoff answer Syracuse's beatings of Bob Cousy by flattening their best player Dolph Schayes, until the fans waved cardboard hatchets.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Guarding Behind Cousy, and the MVP Snub
clips.summary: He explains how he covered for Cousy's defense by steering shooters into the lane, turning Russell's blocks and Cousy's steals into fast breaks, and how even after winning the players' MVP overwhelmingly he was named only second-team all-league, the only man to do both.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Why Cousy Called Him Arnold
clips.summary: Russell tells how Auerbach, true to his theory of building a team to beat the best, drafted a big man over hometown favorite Bob Cousy and was crucified by a Boston press that also threw antisemitic threats at him, all of which Auerbach met with their little red wagon.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Scout Who Said They Couldn't Play
clips.summary: He relishes the Oklahoma City tournament where a Wichita scout, having watched their one loss, told his team that USF could not play a lick, only to be buried by K.C. Jones's full-court press, a rout that led Auerbach's old coach to call him at midnight about the player to get for Boston.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Remembering Every Play
clips.summary: Russell describes a memory that let him narrate a decades-old game his late wife found on tape before each play happened, a recall he built for scouting so his defense could be action rather than reaction, and which he has since tried to forget.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: McClymonds: Frank Robinson and Johnny Mathis
clips.summary: He recalls the talent that came out of McClymonds High and his American Legion coach, schoolmates Frank Robinson and Curt Flood, and his friend Johnny Mathis, a fellow high-jumper whose acapella song at a sports dinner left the room in stunned silence.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Sam Jones and the Poet Heinsohn
clips.summary: Russell paints Sam Jones, who could have been an all-time great but never wanted the daily responsibility yet never missed the shot that decided a season, and Tom Heinsohn, seen as a bruiser but really an artist and poet whom Auerbach used as a whipping boy.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Ramsey, the Sixth Man, and Mutual Respect
clips.summary: He describes Frank Ramsey, whom Auerbach turned into the proud sixth starter rather than a substitute, the mutual respect between two teammates from opposite worlds, and the night Ramsey answered Russell's knocked-out teeth by pulling out his own bridge.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Celtic Way: Kindness as Strength
clips.summary: Russell explains the Celtic way, every play built from five men each doing what they did well, and his belief that kindness is an act of strength, illustrated by John Havlicek's shooting and by Satch Sanders rattling Bill Bradley with a line Russell fed him at the foul line.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: King Kong, and Why He Wouldn't Fight
clips.summary: He offers his own reading of King Kong as a story of sacrifice, his high-school coach's warning that a fighting Black team would be branded thugs, and how he answered a Fort Wayne center who hit him not with his fists but with a spike learned in the shipyards.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Last Team to Integrate
clips.summary: Russell recalls a Boston where the Red Sox were the last team to integrate and the busing fights ran hot, and how after a 1969 championship a reporter asked Auerbach whether a better coach would have won more, a slight that helped settle his decision to retire.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Books, and a Contract He Wouldn't Collect
clips.summary: He tells how Walter Brown opened the Celtics' books to show they could not yet pay him what he was worth, and how Russell, given an eight-year no-cut contract he could have drawn without playing, refused to take money he had not earned, just as his father had.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Sport as Art and War
clips.summary: Russell describes thinking philosophically about sport as a mixture of art and war that gathers people, anchored by the conviction that it is far more important to understand than to be understood, and that happiness follows from taking control through understanding.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Best Player He Could Be
clips.summary: He recounts being passed over for a regional player award the year he was a first-team All-American, and how he simply dismissed the snub, refusing to tie his self-esteem to what others said and fixing instead on being the best player he could be.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Looking Out for Each Other
clips.summary: Russell argues the Celtics' fun was essential to winning because they looked out for one another, and remembers a bad night when K.C. Jones deliberately picked a fight to fire him up, taking a chance on his behalf because he could see Russell needed help.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: George Mikan's Kindness
clips.summary: Against memories of minor-league players who spat slurs at the boys outside the ballpark, Russell sets George Mikan, the era's biggest star, who treated him kindly as an unknown teenager and years later asked the favor of introducing Russell to his son.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: No Such Thing as Other People's Kids
clips.summary: He shares his rule of waiting for the second thought so as never to injure a child, and the Fort Worth speech where he answered a man who resented taxes for other people's kids: there are no other people's children, only the next generation of Americans.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Medical Clinics in Mississippi
clips.summary: Wary of organizations that could dismiss him as a mouthpiece, Russell still answered Charlie Evers's call after Medgar's murder, going to Jackson in 1964 to run open basketball and medical clinics for any child who came, guarded discreetly like a man under the Secret Service.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Do Not Be Afraid
clips.summary: Recalling that he went to Mississippi the summer three civil rights workers were murdered their first night, Russell passes on the lesson a young Colorado player told him she would carry for life: do not be afraid, because decisions made out of fear are usually bad.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The March, and the Night King Died
clips.summary: He recounts briefly knowing Malcolm X, his affection for the gold-medalist Cassius Clay, declining King's invitation to the March on Washington stage because he had not done the work, and playing a game the night King was killed to keep a crowd calm and off the streets.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Standing With Muhammad Ali
clips.summary: Russell calls Ali a great boxer, psychologist, and brave man, refuses a demand that he disavow him even at the cost of being mocked as Felton X, and explains joining Jim Brown's group of athletes to support Ali after his draft status was changed arbitrarily without a hearing.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Beard, the White Shoes, and Being True
clips.summary: Asked about his most controversial stands, Russell offers his refusal to shave his beard or trade his white shoes for the team's black ones, lessons in analyzing from his own perspective that he later pressed on Bill Walton and Yao Ming, telling the latter to embrace being a very tall Chinese man.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Never Made Peace With Boston
clips.summary: He says flatly that his peace with Boston never came, that he kept control and let the media's misconceptions pass, and dismisses greatest-player debates as unanswerable before recalling a CBS production meeting where he told an announcer he knew more about basketball than anyone he would meet.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Calling the Game Before It Happened
clips.summary: Russell lays out the matchup analysis by which he predicted the Lakers would beat the Sixers with a rookie Magic Johnson at center, every adjustment, every speed mismatch, every familiarity, and how the game then unfolded exactly as he had told the disbelieving announcer.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: I Don't Sign Autographs
clips.summary: He dates his no-autograph policy to a 1964 decision over signing basketballs, frames it as a matter of free will that not even God could override, notes the counterfeit Russell signatures teammates forged, and recalls offering kids a conversation instead of a name.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: I Played for the Celtics, Not Boston
clips.summary: Russell insists he played for the Celtics rather than Boston, jokes about Jack Kent Cooke's offer to make him Wilt's backup, and explains why he would have nothing to do with the Red Sox, whose owner had vowed never to field a Black player, until they showed a real program for change.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Mentoring, and the Red Sox Make Amends
clips.summary: He describes his one-to-one Mentoring charity, open to any child and grown to millions of volunteers, his condition that a Red Sox owner join its board to make real policy, and how the team then embraced mentoring at Fenway until Russell finally threw out a first pitch.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Statue and the Tombstone
clips.summary: Russell recounts resisting a statue that sounded to him like a tombstone, being persuaded by Mayor Menino that it was for the city, and shaping it at City Hall Plaza with a Valley of Champions and his own sayings, tied to a legacy foundation funding mentoring and education over war.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Good Second to His Father
clips.summary: Asked about the Presidential Medal of Freedom from President Obama, Russell calls it a good strong second to the day his father told him at seventy-five that he was proud to be his son and that Russell was his hero.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Pallbearer for Jackie Robinson
clips.summary: He describes the honor of being the only non-Dodger pallbearer at Jackie Robinson's funeral, called by Rachel Robinson because he had been Jackie's favorite athlete, and frames his own role as carrying the game from point B to C after Jackie took it from A to B, with the first Black coaching job mattering only once race no longer governed hiring.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Rivals, and Respecting Every Approach
clips.summary: Russell says rivals like Wilt Chamberlain and Elgin Baylor made him better, recounts apologizing to Wilt for words said in jest, and extends the same respect to the many civil rights organizations and to Nelson Mandela, whom he honored for refusing to disavow violence and abandon his comrades.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: No Regrets, and a Changed Game
clips.summary: He voices no real regrets beyond wishing he could have done more to expose racism, and reflects on a changed NBA he will not compare across eras, noting that he effectively invented the blocked shot at eighteen and that the zone defense he once held in contempt is now integral.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: What I Do, Not What I Am
clips.summary: Russell closes with advice to approach what you do well professionally, the airport exchange where he insisted basketball was what he did and not what he was, the total team concept behind the championships, and his reasons for never returning to a West Monroe whose sheriff once humiliated his mother.
keywords: what I do not what I am

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Introductions in the Jefferson Building
clips.summary: Interviewer Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the July 13, 2011 session in the Library of Congress Jefferson Building, welcoming journalists Simeon Booker and Moses Newson for the Civil Rights History Project.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Booker From the Afro to Jet
clips.summary: Booker traces his start at the Baltimore Afro-American in 1942 under publisher Carl Murphy, his move to the Cleveland Call & Post, a Nieman Fellowship at Harvard, and his jump to John H. Johnson's new pocket magazine Jet, where he opened the Washington bureau.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Newson From the Navy to Memphis
clips.summary: Newson recalls leaving the Navy, being drawn to Black papers like the Pittsburgh Courier and Chicago Defender, studying at Storer College in West Virginia and Lincoln University's journalism school in Missouri, then joining the Tri-State Defender in Memphis in 1952.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Hoxie, Arkansas Opens Its Classrooms
clips.summary: Newson describes covering early school desegregation in Hoxie, Arkansas in 1955, an unusual case where reporters were invited into classrooms and onto the buses, and where integration went smoothly until outside agitators arrived.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Clinton, Tennessee and Foley Hill
clips.summary: Newson recounts the 1956 crisis in Clinton, Tennessee, where roughly 200 Black residents lived on Foley Hill, students were jeered walking down to integrate the school, John Kasper and the KKK fired up white residents, a white minister was run out of town, and the governor called out the National Guard.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Booker's First Trip to Mississippi
clips.summary: Booker, then based in Chicago, explains that the Emmett Till killing turned his attention to Mississippi, a place wholly new to a reporter raised in Baltimore. He set out with a photographer to investigate how two men had killed the boy over a whistling incident.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Mamie Till and the Open Casket
clips.summary: Booker recalls staying up through the night with Mamie Till as the body came in from Mississippi and her insistence that the casket be opened so the world could see what was done to her boy. He remembers a piece of the skull falling off and his photographer replacing it before shooting.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Photographer David Jackson
clips.summary: Booker praises Jet photographer David Jackson as a brave and skilled cameraman who handled the murder cases, working in Mississippi at a time when cameras were large and conspicuous and being a Black photographer there was genuinely dangerous.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: The Till Photo Lifts Jet's Circulation
clips.summary: Booker describes how Jet's circulation took off when it ran the funeral picture, forcing the magazine's first-ever reprint, as the entire Black community grew aware of the need to act on the Till case.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: A Hateful Climate and George Lee
clips.summary: Newson sets the Mississippi scene after the 1954 school ruling: a hateful climate, unpunished killings, and the murder of George Lee for registering Black voters. He went down for Lee's funeral, where many came to gauge how forceful new NAACP head Roy Wilkins would be.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: How the Till Killing Unfolded
clips.summary: Newson lays out the facts of the Till case: the whistling incident at the Bryant store, Roy Bryant and his half-brother J.W. Milam being out of town until a young friend mentioned it, and Newson photographing the small whistle-stop hamlet and interviewing Till's grandfather in a cotton field.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Finding Witnesses With Medgar Evers
clips.summary: Newson recalls the Tallahatchie County trial roughly three weeks after the killing, being chosen to go out with NAACP teams to round up Black witnesses on Delta plantations, changing into plantation-worker clothes, and riding with driver Medgar Evers to find people who would testify.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Jet's Stringer System
clips.summary: Newson explains the stringer network Jet built across rural areas: readers submitted weekly story ideas for pay and became informers and local guides, building a forgotten audience and feeding exclusive items into a weekly column that lit up communities.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Segregated Courthouse Press Tables
clips.summary: Newson describes the segregated press arrangements at the Till trial, where the sheriff boasted of no integration and Black reporters had no press table until Congressman Charles C. Diggs Jr. and veteran reporters complained to the judge. He recalls also covering a rape case from a courthouse balcony.
related_events: Charles C. Diggs Jr. observes Till trial 1955

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Knowing the Little Rock Nine
clips.summary: Booker recalls arriving early in Little Rock in 1957 with a photographer and getting to know the nine students and their parents before the story broke, so he could write who these people were. He also notes how his Washington bureau covered Congress and government to fill the magazine.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Ordered Out by the Colonel
clips.summary: Newson describes the National Guard turn at Little Rock after the governor reversed course, his first day being met by a colonel near 16th and Park who said his presence might cause problems, and being escorted back to his car by troops while two men trailed him with threats.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: The Mob Attack on L. Alex Wilson
clips.summary: Newson recounts hanging around the home of Daisy and L.C. Bates, who led the NAACP and worked closest with the nine students, then walking into a mob with three other reporters. When the children slipped into the school another way, the crowd turned violent and beat L. Alex Wilson of the Tri-State Defender.
related_events: Little Rock mob attack on reporters 1957

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Why Wilson Would Not Run
clips.summary: Newson explains that L. Alex Wilson refused to run because of an earlier KKK encounter in Leesburg, Florida, after which Wilson vowed never to run again. That vow left him to be beaten badly, and his wife believed the beating hastened his early death.
related_events: Little Rock mob attack on reporters 1957

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Eisenhower Sends in the Military
clips.summary: Newson argues Little Rock was among the most important civil rights cases because, after the attack and NAACP complaints, President Eisenhower sent in the military, meaning violence could no longer be used openly to block desegregation and reporters and students gained real protection.
related_events: Eisenhower sends troops to Little Rock 1957

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Elizabeth Eckford Walks Alone
clips.summary: Newson tells the story he most looks back on: Elizabeth Eckford missing word that the students were not to come, arriving alone, turned away by the guard, and surrounded by a hostile white crowd. He interviewed the fifteen-year-old, who said she never thought people could be so cruel.
related_events: Elizabeth Eckford walk to Central High 1957

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Eisenhower's Silence, Kennedy's Opening
clips.summary: Asked about the mood by the early 1960s, Booker recalls a retirement interview in which Eisenhower acknowledged he had never spoken about civil rights, contrasting that silence with Kennedy, who opened things up to the press and at whose funeral Booker was named a pallbearer.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Expectations and the Courts
clips.summary: Newson says people expected more than was delivered, but the administration set a good mood against lawmakers threatening to leave the union, while organizations like the NAACP, Urban League, CORE, and SNCC worked on. He credits judges such as Frank M. Johnson and Earl Warren for knocking down Jim Crow barriers.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: After Till, a Different Attitude
clips.summary: Newson reflects that the struggle long predated the 1950s and 1960s, citing a Florida family killed during the Christmas season, but contends that the killing of Emmett Till for supposedly whistling at a white woman pushed young people and adults toward greater activism.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: A Sharecropper's Sacrifice in Church
clips.summary: Newson describes a mass meeting in a Mississippi church near Money, with a Howard University professor speaking while police and KKK photographed attendees to get them fired from plantations. A middle-aged sharecropper, evicted for trying to register, told the crowd to forget him and keep registering to vote.
