clips.summary: Williams reflects on how well-dressed and orderly the demonstrators were even before Martin Luther King's call for nonviolent resistance. He shows a photograph of a 1963 downtown Orangeburg demonstration of 3,000 people, including his own mother, who prayed for peace, opportunity, and the employment of Black citizens in a town with almost no Black police, council members, or county officials.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Books and Unsung Heroes
clips.summary: Williams describes Out of the Box in Dixie as a top-quality record of the movement's dignity and unsung heroes, with almost no images of Martin Luther King, Rosa Parks, or Jesse Jackson, running from Clarendon County through 2008 or 2009. He notes Freedom & Justice and The Orangeburg Massacre, the latter carrying interviews with those who were shot.
keywords: Out of the Box in Dixie
related_events: publication of Out of the Box in Dixie

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: John Stroman, Cleveland Sellers, and Closing
clips.summary: Williams describes John Stroman, whom authorities tried to scapegoat, and Cleveland Sellers, the only person jailed in connection with the Orangeburg Massacre, actually for the February 6 bowling alley incident, who later became president of Voorhees College. Mosnier thanks Williams, and the session ends with the standard Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC sign-off.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Growing Up in Hawaii
clips.summary: Cecilia Suyat Marshall recalls having no awareness of American racial hierarchy growing up in Hawaii, where she attended school alongside Japanese, Filipino, and Chinese classmates and knew of only one Negro family in her section. She says it was not until she went to New York that she found out about the racial problem.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Arriving in New York
clips.summary: Marshall describes loving New York after arriving, enrolling at Columbia University for a stenographic session, and being told she must support herself if she wanted to stay. She recounts going to the employment office, which sent her to the NAACP National Office in New York.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Learning the Branch Department Job
clips.summary: Marshall recounts working for the director of branches of the National Office, where she learned about the racial problems for the first time. Through the job she met local people, attended annual conventions in different cities, and was always there to take the minutes.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Meeting Walter White and Roy Wilkins
clips.summary: Marshall describes meeting executive secretary Walter White, whom she called Pops, and Roy Wilkins, and how White eventually gave her away in marriage to Thurgood Marshall, the legal counsel. She confirms she worked as secretary to Gloster B. Current, director of branches.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Walter White's Marriage Controversy
clips.summary: Marshall describes her day-to-day correspondence work and meeting people who came to the city for help. She recounts how Walter White divorcing his Black wife and marrying a white woman practically broke up the whole organization, which shaped her fear when Thurgood later proposed.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Refusing and Accepting the Proposal
clips.summary: Marshall recalls telling Thurgood no when he proposed, fearing she would be seen as a foreigner and split the organization. She recounts his insistence that he was marrying her and not the country, and how that is how they got married.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: The Local People Were the Heroes
clips.summary: Marshall says she felt blessed to be at the organization and got to know local people in the South who took the lawyers into their homes when hotels would not. She relays Thurgood's view that the lawyers were cowards who argued a case and left, while local people who put their names on briefs stayed to face hostility and were the real heroes.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Hosts in Atlanta and Chattanooga
clips.summary: Marshall recalls she cannot remember all the names of the people who hosted her in Atlanta, Chattanooga, and Oklahoma City, but says they treated her as a daughter. She mentions having recently reviewed NAACP papers at the Library of Congress about the annual conventions.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Groveland Four and Emmett Till
clips.summary: Marshall recalls being present for the cases of the Groveland Four, indicted over allegations involving a white woman, and remembers the Emmett Till case. She explains that branches were responsible for handling cases locally and got advice from the office's lawyers and from her boss, who headed all the branches in the country.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Hearing Strategy and Seeing Change
clips.summary: Marshall says taking notes let her hear a great deal of strategy and learn about her country and what was being done. Looking back, she reflects that the movement has come a long way but still has a way to go, citing being able to enter restaurants and hotels without being turned away.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: The Bible and the Gun
clips.summary: Marshall recounts being fed by local people at conventions when restaurants would not serve them, then riding in a car with Thurgood when a branch member opened the glove compartment to show a Bible and a gun. The member said they would use the Bible first, and if that did not work, the gun, illustrating how people lived in those times.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Helping on Brown v. Board
clips.summary: Marshall explains that she worked in the branch department and not in the legal work, but when the team ran short of help on Brown v. Board of Education they needed stenographic help and she stepped in, since they were in the same office. She recalls reading about a meeting in Atlanta after the decision and the anticipation that change would come quickly.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision (1954)

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Our Work Has Just Begun
clips.summary: Marshall recounts that after celebrating the victory, Thurgood said their work had just begun, marking him as a realist about the resistance that followed. She places the moment at the office about an hour into the celebration following Brown v. Board of Education or one of the big cases.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision (1954)

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Known to the Branches as Family
clips.summary: Marshall explains that she did not want to break up the organization by marrying, but the people received her warmly because they had seen her around the branches and conventions recording the minutes. She describes being the first person local people reached in the branch department and helping them without bothering the director.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: A Lesson From Hawaii
clips.summary: Marshall reflects that her experience was a lesson and a blessing, contrasting growing up in Hawaii among a mix of people and languages with the divisions she encountered on the mainland. She recalls beginning to understand racial differences only after coming to New York.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Testing the Segregated Dining Car
clips.summary: Marshall recalls that about a week after the Henderson v. United States dining-car ruling she boarded a train to Chattanooga with attorney Franklin H. Williams, who she notes later became a Negro ambassador to Ghana. Her boss, whom colleagues called Bubo, and the membership secretary both declined to go to the dining car, so she went with Williams.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Seated in the Center of the Car
clips.summary: Marshall describes the head waiter unable to classify either Williams or herself, with her long hair and a flower, and seating them next to the partitioned section. Williams insisted in French until they were moved to the center of the dining car, and Marshall says she never felt nervous because she was new and did not know the rules.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Stopping Work and the Sit-Ins
clips.summary: Marshall says she stopped working at the NAACP in 1955 but kept in close contact through Thurgood's legal work. She recalls segregation vividly and wishes Thurgood and his lawyers were present to see later progress, naming Robert L. Carter as one of his first assistants and Mildred Bond Roxborough as a secretary under her boss.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Lingering Differences and Shelby County
clips.summary: Marshall observes that she still gets nasty looks at times and can feel the difference even when served. She comments on Shelby County v. Holder, noting that while much progress has been made, the court has gone back and invalidated some protections, but that progress continues.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Moving to Washington and Memorable Cases
clips.summary: Marshall describes the move from New York City to Washington, D.C., saying she had gotten used to moving. Pressed on memorable cases, she names Brown v. Board of Education as the most important and notes that at her age she does not want to pull out and rehash the cases of the 1950s.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Family in Hawaii and a First Phone Call
clips.summary: Marshall says her siblings did not follow her to work in New York and most are now deceased except one sister, and that she rarely returns to Hawaii because of the long trip. She recalls her first long-distance phone call home around 1948 or 1949, which she could barely afford on thirty-five dollars a week, and how she and her mother cried.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Inside the National Office
clips.summary: Marshall describes the layout of the NAACP National Office: a stenographic pool where she began, a branch department overseeing roughly 1500 branches, a legal department supporting branch litigation, and Roy Wilkins and Walter White upstairs. She recalls the office felt like a family that would sit together and go to dinner after work.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Portraits of the Leadership
clips.summary: Marshall sketches the people she worked with: Walter White as a wonderful administrator who was sometimes above everyone, Roy Wilkins as the one who did the work and met people locally, and Thurgood as her boss. She says it was like a family, especially at conferences and conventions.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Conventions Across the Country
clips.summary: Marshall lists the convention cities she traveled to, beginning with her first in Boston in 1950, then Atlanta, Oklahoma City, Chicago, and Dallas, Texas. She explains that she got to know delegates because they came to her boss with branch problems while she was there.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Credit to the People
clips.summary: Marshall reflects that the delegates deserve real credit for what they did in those times and that they treated the national staff like kings and queens though they did not have to. She frames herself as someone they could call on and work with while they struggled where they were.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Advice for Young People
clips.summary: Marshall says young people should not take anything for granted, because people in earlier days went through sacrifices and indignities to build what exists today. She remarks that younger generations take much for granted and praises the older generations in the South as wonderful people.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Faith in God as Foundation
clips.summary: Marshall notes she traveled in the South until 1955, then turns to faith, saying her deep faith in God was what carried them through. She describes talking to God in private to get everything out of her system and credits the church as sustaining structure.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Edgecombe Avenue to Morningside Gardens
clips.summary: Marshall recalls first living at 409 Edgecombe Avenue, considered the high society of the Negro world, before moving into Morningside Gardens, the first middle-income cooperative in New York. She describes it as wonderful, with friends coming to them because the family had two young boys.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Neighbors in the Community
clips.summary: Marshall describes the Morningside Gardens community and its neighbors, including Edward R. Dudley, who became ambassador to Liberia, attorney Robert L. Carter, Richard Parrish of the urban league, dentist George Dockery, and artist Louis Jefferson. She recalls the Dockery and Parrish wives competing to leave food at the door for her boys.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Moves Within Washington
clips.summary: Marshall recounts the family's Washington residences, beginning at 64 G Street within walking distance of the church, then a three-story cooperative whose steps proved too much for Thurgood. After a year they looked toward Virginia.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Integrating Lake Barcroft
clips.summary: Marshall describes moving to Lake Barcroft, Virginia after Attorney General Ramsey Clark told Thurgood the family had to come and integrate the place. She reports the family remained, as far as she knew, the only Black household there and that neighborhood kids welcomed her boys when they first moved in.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Board Service and Organizations
clips.summary: Marshall lists organizations she was involved in, including the National Capitol Auxiliary of Supreme Court wives drawn from the district, Maryland, and Virginia, Travelers Aid, and the Legal Defense Fund board. She explains that on the boards they decided on policies and which cases to take, and again names Brown v. Board of Education as the case that stands out.
related_events: NAACP Legal Defense Fund board service

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: An Ordinary Person, Blessed
clips.summary: Marshall describes herself as just an ordinary person doing ordinary things, blessed to have gone through what she did coming from Hawaii. Responding to a quote about ordinary people doing extraordinary things, she repeats her modest self-assessment and says she feels blessed and did what she had to do, staying home with the children while Thurgood was away.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: A Charmed Life and Her Son
clips.summary: Marshall closes by saying it has been a blessing and that she has lived a charmed life, joking about her sons' ages. She speaks of her son John W. Marshall, who began at the Marshals and became director of the United States Marshals Service and was then with the Transportation Department, as the interview ends.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Interview Opening in Albany, Georgia
clips.summary: Interviewer Joe Mosnier introduces the June 4, 2011 session for the Civil Rights History Project, recorded at Albany State University during the 53rd Reunion of the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement, and welcomes Charles McDew.
related_events: 53rd Reunion of the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Born in Massillon, a Steel and Football Town
clips.summary: McDew recounts his birth on June 23, 1938 in Massillon, Ohio, a community of about 38,000 known for steel and football. His father and grandfather played for Paul Brown's Massillon Tigers, the Pro Football Hall of Fame in Canton sits eight miles away, and his father worked for Republic Steel.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Union Heritage in the Steel Mills
clips.summary: McDew describes growing up active in United Steel Workers Local 1124, where his grandfather, uncles, and father all worked the mills. The teams he played for, the parks, and the fields were all produced by the unions.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Integrated Neighborhood Schools Up North
clips.summary: McDew recalls Massillon's good, highly integrated neighborhood schools, where he went twelve years with the same children. His sister was one of the first Black teachers in Massillon public schools, and his father trained as a teacher but could not be hired in the North before the 1954 Brown decision. Of 38,000 residents, roughly 3,500 were Black.
related_events: 1954 Brown v. Board of Education decision

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Supporting Bobby Grier at the Bowl Game
clips.topic: Bobby Grier and the Bowl Game
clips.summary: McDew describes early civil rights awareness from football: traveling as a child from Massillon to Pittsburgh, about 120 miles, to support Bobby Grier of the University of Pittsburgh after bowl organizers refused to play a team with a Black player. His parents said Grier was one of us and they had to help.
related_events: Bowl game segregation controversy over Bobby Grier

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Writing Letters to James Lawson
clips.summary: McDew recalls writing letters of support to James Lawson, an advisor to Dr. King and minister at his mother's St. James A.M.E. Church, while Lawson was jailed as a conscientious objector. He also names his uncle Horace Gillom, an end and punter for the Cleveland Browns, and reflects on how football carried civil rights issues into Massillon.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Massillon as a Town of Worker Immigrants
clips.summary: McDew frames Massillon as an immigrant culture and an expression of the American dream, with people drawn from Greece, Spain, Yugoslavia, Czechoslovakia, and the American South. He argues Southerners were as much immigrants as the Czechs, coming to enjoy the fruits of citizenship and decent jobs, schools, and health care for the first time.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Coxey's Army and First Black Nurses
clips.summary: McDew describes learning history from old men at the barbershop, including General Jacob Coxey himself and grandfathers who had marched in Coxey's Army of the poor. He recounts how his aunts and mother became some of the first Black nurses, his mother studying at Homer G. Phillips in St. Louis before nursing work opened in Massillon.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Learning Supply and Demand From Steel
clips.summary: McDew explains how he learned economics from the rhythms of Mesabi iron ore arriving in Massillon, becoming steel, going to Chicago and Detroit, then cycling back. Full employment and overtime produced stockpiles, followed by layoffs, teaching him supply and demand and the swing between times of plenty and scarcity when families gathered coal off trains.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: A Football Player's Plan for Life
clips.summary: McDew recalls that Massillon produced steel and football players, and he was a football player. By ninth grade he had his life mapped out: play ball at Michigan, turn pro, marry, have smart kids, make money, and retire to a business like other players did.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: His Father's Plan to Send Him South
clips.summary: McDew describes his father's insistence that each child attend a historically Black college for at least one year, sending him to South Carolina State College in Orangeburg, where his father had graduated. The aim was to expose him to a professional class of Black people beyond the athletes and preachers who were the only visible role models up North.
related_events: McDew's matriculation at South Carolina State, fall 1959

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: The Drive South and First Segregation
clips.summary: McDew recounts driving Route 21 from Massillon to Orangeburg in fall 1959, having never been South. His father warned him about segregated bathrooms and food once they crossed into West Virginia, his mother packed lunches because no places would feed Black travelers, and the world grew stranger the further they went.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Paradise at South Carolina State
clips.summary: McDew describes arriving at Orangeburg at sixteen and feeling he was in heaven, surrounded by hundreds of Black girls he was not related to, unlike Massillon where he seemed kin to every Black girl in town. He thought his father brilliant for sending him to experience his culture.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Thanksgiving in Sumter and the Beating
clips.summary: McDew narrates being radicalized over Thanksgiving 1959 in Sumter, South Carolina, where roommate Charles Gatson took him home. As designated driver he was pulled over, struck for not saying yes sir to a white officer, and when he fought back, coming from a tough steel town, two officers beat him bloody, breaking his arm and jaw.
related_events: McDew's Thanksgiving 1959 arrests in Sumter, SC

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Three Arrests in Two Days
clips.summary: McDew recounts his first arrest for disturbing the peace and disobeying an officer, a second arrest six hours later for refusing the segregated baggage car and taking a seat in the white car of the train, and a third for cutting through Orangeburg's Edisto Gardens park on a day Black people were barred. He quickly learned how much was off-limits under segregation law.
related_events: McDew's Thanksgiving 1959 arrests in Sumter, SC

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Greensboro Sit-In and Becoming Spokesman
clips.summary: McDew describes how, after the February 1, 1960 sit-in by North Carolina A&T students in Greensboro, South Carolina State students asked him to be their spokesman because he spoke to white people as equals. He initially refused, expecting to leave for Ohio within weeks and never come further south than Columbus for the Michigan-Ohio State game.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Leading the Orangeburg Movement and Mass Arrests
clips.summary: McDew recounts becoming head of the Orangeburg Movement for Civic Improvement, leading sit-ins and wade-ins. He was arrested in Columbia delivering a petition to Governor Ernest Fritz Hollings against segregation of public places, and arrested with about 800 others in Orangeburg demanding desegregation.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Refusing Lifelong Nonviolence at Shaw
clips.summary: McDew narrates the April 1960 Shaw University conference convened by Dr. King and SCLC. He refused King's proposal that students join SCLC and accept nonviolence as a way of life, arguing that for him nonviolence was strictly a tactic, citing Gandhi to insist that a moral appeal could not work in the midst of an amoral society.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Down the Hall: Naming SNCC
clips.summary: McDew describes how those who would not join Dr. King met down the hall at Shaw and formed what became the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee. First considered the student coordinating committee, the group kept the word nonviolent at the urging of Nashville students taught by James Lawson, who deeply believed in its viability.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Electing Marion Barry and the Conventions
clips.summary: McDew recalls the summer plan to take a student statement to the Democratic and Republican conventions, seeking a civil rights plank, and the election of Marion Barry as SNCC's first chairman to carry those concerns. That fall SCLC provided money, office space, and material as the new organization set itself up.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Becoming Chair and the Name Snick
clips.summary: McDew confirms replacing Marion Barry as formal chair at the fall Atlanta meeting and describes SNCC's developing organizational personality. He recounts how a reporter coined the nickname Snick from the initials SNCC, and how the group's identity was shaped by what it did.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Jail-No-Bail at Rock Hill
clips.summary: McDew explains the jail-no-bail program developed as the group asked how long it could continue if it kept getting arrested and bailing out. The tactic was tried in Rock Hill, South Carolina, supported by Rev. C.A. Ivory and state NAACP head I. DeQuincey Newman, who had a strong presence in the state.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: To McComb for Voter Registration
clips.summary: McDew describes SNCC going to McComb, Mississippi in Southwest Mississippi to register voters once the freedom rides had taken place. By then SNCC had its own identity, separate from CORE, and was hammering out two programs: direct action and voter registration.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: A Five-Year Lifespan by Design
clips.summary: McDew explains that SNCC planned its own death at its founding, giving itself a finite span of about five years. The number satisfied parents who would not accept their college students simply finding themselves, and matched the group's sense that members would burn out, be jailed, or be killed within that time.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Power Sits in the House and Senate
clips.summary: McDew lays out SNCC's analysis of American political structure: real power lay not in the presidency but in the House and Senate, held through committee chairmanships won by longevity. Those seats belonged to white Southern men from Black Belt areas with heavy Black populations where Black people did not vote, out of disinterest and fear.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Targeting the Worst Black Belt Counties
clips.summary: McDew names the strategy of going to the worst counties for retaliation: Dougherty, Terrell, and Baker counties in Southwest Georgia, and Amite, Pike, and Walthall counties in Southwest Mississippi. He notes counties 60 to 65 percent Black where no one was registered, the home ground of senators like John Stennis, James Eastland, Robert Byrd, and Herman Talmadge.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Disrupting the Balance of Power
clips.summary: McDew explains the simple logic of unseating an entrenched senator like John Stennis: register enough Black voters that even a freshman replacement, however hostile, would arrive with no power and no time to act on threats. Doing this in enough places could change the country in a short period.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: The Pledge to Pay the Blood Price
clips.summary: McDew describes the central SNCC pledge that the real battle was the vote, that violence was part of the game, and that beatings, bombings, or killings would not turn them away as it had scared off earlier generations. They accepted there would be a blood price to pay, and committed to pay it to change the country.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Education and the Posse Foundation
clips.summary: McDew names education as the key to the future and recounts speaking to the Posse Foundation at Colby College in Maine, the first school outside the South in 45 years where his audience was all Black American students, attending top universities. He notes President Obama gave a considerable amount of his Nobel Peace Prize money to the Posse Foundation.
related_events: Obama Nobel Peace Prize donation to the Posse Foundation

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Correcting the Miseducation of Children
clips.summary: McDew argues for broader education, more Black teachers, and a civil rights course like Mississippi's requirement in every state, noting he had to come South to learn his own role in Black history. He says the nation's soul will be strengthened by understanding the personality of SNCC and the civil rights struggle, which is not over.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Democracy, the New Museum, and Closing
clips.summary: McDew reflects that freedom is not free and democracy takes constant struggle, tying the American dream of the immigrants he knew as a child to that work. He welcomes the new Smithsonian museum as important to defining citizenship, gives thanks for waging the struggle, and closes the interview with the interviewer.
related_events: Construction of the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Born in Jackson on Christmas Day
clips.summary: McLaurin recalls being born in Jackson on Christmas Day 1939 to John and Connie McLaurin, domestics who finished high school, his father landing a prized job with Coca-Cola, and his own place as the first in his immediate family to reach college.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Grandmother's Books and Booker T. Washington
clips.summary: He describes living with his Tuskegee-educated schoolteacher grandmother, who passed him the books she had bought for his uncle and filled his head with her heroes, especially Booker T. Washington, the self-educated former slave who came to speak to presidents.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Train-Station Restroom
clips.summary: At the segregated Jackson train station a tall white man accused the small McLaurin of peeping at white women in the restroom while he stood waiting for his grandmother, a crowd gathering around the frightened boy.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: This Is My Boy
clips.summary: His light-skinned grandmother emerged from the restroom, declared the boy was hers, and led him out, leaving the white man stunned that the woman he took for white had claimed the child.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Learning to Stay Away
clips.summary: On the way home his grandmother taught him the rules of segregation, to stay away from white people and never be seen acting friendly toward white women, lessons he absorbed in a self-contained Black world on Farish Street.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Never Answer the Phone
clips.summary: Working at a white-owned store as a boy, McLaurin was told never to answer the phone or get near the white men who drank there, until the manager fell ill and a group of white men demanded he pick up a ringing phone and take an order.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Note That Broke the Rules
clips.topic: A Note to a White Woman
clips.summary: When he handed the order he had written to the white woman in charge, a white man seized the note in fury that a Black boy had written it and passed it to a white woman, insisting he should never have held a pencil, and McLaurin ran home to a lesson from his mother about the Klan.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Furniture Auction in Rankin County
clips.summary: He recounts being hired with friends by a furniture-store owner to set out, sell, and deliver furniture at an auction over in Rankin County, work that put the near-seventeen-year-old in an old Army shirt left from his reserve training.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Army Shirt and the Constable
clips.summary: An armed constable confronted him over the stripe on his Army shirt, demanding to know whether he had been over white men and threatening him, after which the store owner sent him home and would not use him again for fear of trouble.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Signing Up for the Reserve
clips.summary: He tells how, tagging along while friends tried to enlist, he was the one who passed the test, and how the chance to get out of Jackson drew him toward six months of active-duty training and a reserve unit despite his mother's wish that he stay in school.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: His Father's Signature
clips.summary: Needing a parent's signature his mother refused, McLaurin turned to his father, who had been kept out of the war by a deformed foot and resented it, and who signed the papers that let his son go.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Dumped From the Bunk at Fort Jackson
clips.summary: At Fort Jackson in 1955 he met a newly desegregated army still in transition, getting dumped from his top bunk for an unmade bed and struggling to keep the thirty-inch step on marches behind the long-legged men up front.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Letter to the Congressman
clips.summary: Wanting out, he wrote his Mississippi congressman John Bell Williams, only to have the reply read aloud by a sergeant: a little Black boy from Mississippi ought to be proud to wear the uniform, and his exit was denied.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Seeing Beyond Jackson
clips.summary: Back in Jackson he returned to high school with new eyes, the Army having shown him how unprepared he was for the world beyond his community and how much he had not been exposed to, just as the Freedom Riders were coming to town.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Ride From Medgar Evers
clips.summary: He recalls a tall handsome stranger giving him and his friends a ride to the Farish Street theater, learning days later from the newspaper that the man was the NAACP leader Medgar Evers, which drew him to seek out the organization.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Rock-Throwing Nights
clips.summary: He describes the basketball rivalries between Lanier and Jim Hill highs, and the night white teenagers threw rocks through the Black community, prompting the young men to load up to retaliate before the Freedom Riders arrived.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bevel's Challenge at the Pool Hall
clips.summary: As the young men gathered weapons for a fight, the stranger James Bevel hopped onto a pool table and asked whether any of them were brave enough not to fight each other but to buy a ticket on the white side of the bus station.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Buying a Ticket Instead of a Fight
clips.summary: Several of his friends, James Jones and Ralph Floyd among them, took Bevel's challenge and the money, called off the gang fight, and were arrested buying tickets, though they had not understood they would go to jail.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: No Fear, Only Distance
clips.summary: McLaurin explains that he and his friends were not afraid of white people, only taught to stay away, and that the photographs of Emmett Till in Jet and Ebony left them angry enough to talk of going to the Delta to find the killers.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Learning the Whole Picture
clips.summary: As his jailed friends got out, Bevel kept coming back, explaining CORE, SNCC, and the NAACP and the wider civil rights picture, and the young men began thinking about developing a movement of their own in Jackson.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Jesse Harris and the Parchman Strap
clips.summary: He retells, in his friend Jesse Harris's own grim and almost comic telling, the Parchman penitentiary where Freedom Riders were sent, the sign that read they would break your spirit and your behind, and the strapping Jesse took for refusing to count off as a number.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Building the Jackson Movement
clips.summary: With Medgar Evers, Diane Nash, and others drawn in, the group set about building a Jackson movement, McLaurin moving toward a high-school equivalency exam while helping the effort through the summer of 1961.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The State Fair
clips.summary: He describes the white state fair that came to Jackson with the burlesque show and attractions the Black fair never had, and his determination to get in, by slipping in if need be, because he was never a fearful person.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Arrested for Wanting In
clips.summary: Turned away three times from the white fair he was told was a state fair for everyone, McLaurin went back until the police jailed him, and in that cell a pattern came clear: every time he stepped outside the perimeter set for Black folk, he got cut off.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Medgar's Bail and the Edwards Workshop
clips.summary: Medgar Evers posted his bond and told him about a workshop at Mount Beulah near Edwards on voter registration and civil rights, run by the white lawyer William Higgs and lawyers brought from the North, a surprise in a state with only three Black lawyers.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Hearing Martin Luther King
clips.summary: He recalls the thrill of hearing Martin Luther King speak at the Masonic Temple, admiring the oratory he had only known from Medgar, and even the green double-breasted suit King wore that McLaurin coveted.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Murders in the Free Press
clips.summary: Helping Medgar distribute Hazel Brannon Smith's Mississippi Free Press, McLaurin read of Aaron Henry in Clarksdale and the murder of Reverend George W. Lee in Belzoni, names and places that began to map the violence for him.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Map of the Delta
clips.summary: When McLaurin asked how they could stop the lynchings, Medgar Evers spread a map of Mississippi, drew a circle around the Delta, and explained how registering its Black majority could send representatives to the legislature.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Lawrence Guyot's Pitch
clips.summary: On the Jackson State campus the big red-complexioned Lawrence Guyot pressed a voter-registration application into his hands, promised the vote would free them, and told him a man named Bob Moses was coming to set up a Delta project.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Diane Nash and the Old Hands
clips.summary: At the Freedom House he met Guyot and others and absorbed how Bevel and Amzie Moore had already opened Ruleville through citizenship training, and how outspoken Diane Nash stood up front debating among men.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Little Gangster Wants Out of the Box
clips.topic: Why the Vote Mattered to Him
clips.summary: McLaurin reflects on his old self-image as a little gangster admiring movie tough guys who feared no police, and how the promise that the vote would let him meet a white man as an equal spoke to his hatred of always stepping aside.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Waiting for the Giant Bob Moses
clips.summary: Expecting a Paul Bunyan of a man from all the talk, McLaurin watched a small, quiet figure step from the background and say his name was Bob Moses, and found himself caught by the weight of what the unassuming man said before they set off for the Delta.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Sam Block's Greenwood
clips.summary: Arriving near midnight in Greenwood, he learned of Sam Block, who slept in junkyard cars while organizing, set up a SNCC office above Barnes's photography studio, and had just been run out of it by the White Citizens Council.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Anonymous Thousands
clips.summary: He passes on Amzie Moore's observation that the census could never count all the Black folk packed into Greenwood, Greenville, and Clarksdale, people who lived so close and so guarded that they were functionally anonymous.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Midnight Through Ruleville
clips.summary: He describes creeping through Ruleville after midnight in a convertible with Bob Moses, certain at any moment the police would spring out, and the sweet relief of finally seeing the town's sign behind them.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Amzie Moore's Brick House
clips.summary: Reaching Amzie Moore's brick house in Cleveland surprised the Jackson boy who had seen no middle-class Black homes, and Moore told them of his NAACP organizing in overalls on the plantations and signing up members while his choir sang.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Sunday at Mount Galilee
clips.summary: Bob Moses woke them for church at Mount Galilee Baptist, where the local leadership that had requested SNCC organizers, Joe McDonald, Mary Tucker, and others, gathered to begin their voter-registration drive.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Leadership of Sunflower County
clips.summary: He lays out Sunflower County, seventy percent Black yet white in every office, where intimidation and the murder of Emmett Till had run the NAACP out, and the local people who wanted SNCC's help to register without fear.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Arrested on the First Canvass
clips.summary: On his first walk through the Ruleville community, McLaurin and his fellow organizers were arrested by an armed constable and warned by the mayor to go back to New York, and McLaurin insisted on being driven back so the watching Black community would see they had survived.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The First Trip to the Courthouse
clips.summary: Charged with leading two elderly women into the Indianola courthouse to register, McLaurin found them already out of the car and walking ahead while he hung back looking for the Klan, only for the registrar's office to be closed.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: I Got to Grow Up
clips.summary: Standing in the courthouse beneath the colored-water-fountain sign with the jailhouse bars visible across the street, McLaurin thought of George W. Lee and Emmett Till and told himself he had to grow up and stop being frightened if he was going to stay.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Why They Went
clips.summary: He reflects that the elderly people who walked up to register, born under the plantation's power, did it out of a hope that their children would not have to live as they had, and out of a wanting to get out of the box.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Fannie Lou Hamer Raises Her Hand
clips.summary: At a mass meeting Mary Tucker brought in plantation folk, and among the next group to volunteer to go register was a woman McLaurin did not yet know, Fannie Lou Hamer, who raised her hand to board the bus to Indianola.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Bus That Was Too Yellow
clips.summary: He recounts the August 31 bus of eighteen people to register in Indianola, the police arresting the driver for the bus being too yellow, and the truckloads of armed white men who trailed them home with threats and insults.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Off the Marlow Plantation
clips.summary: He tells how the plantation owner ordered Fannie Lou Hamer to withdraw her registration or leave, and her answer that she had not gone down to register for him but for herself, words that left McLaurin marveling at her courage.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Mayor Takes Joe's Gun
clips.summary: Ten nights later night riders shot up the Ruleville community, into the McDonald, Sisson, and Tucker homes where Hamer was staying, and the next day the mayor went to Joe McDonald's house and took his gun for safekeeping.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Second Amendment, Folded Down
clips.summary: McLaurin showed Joe McDonald the Second Amendment in his son's history book and folded the page down, and McDonald walked into city hall, looked the white man in the face for the first time, and walked out with his gun, becoming the next mass-meeting speaker.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Ella Baker, and Deciding to Stay
clips.summary: Living Ella Baker's teaching to lift up the least and then get out of their way, McLaurin found in Fannie Lou Hamer's refusal to back down, even after her home community was shot up, the reason he decided to stay in the Delta.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Lost in the Hills for Fannie Lou Hamer
clips.summary: Assigned by Bob Moses to find Fannie Lou Hamer, now living with relatives in Tallahatchie County, the Jackson-bred McLaurin got hopelessly lost on gravel roads, stuck in a ditch and pulled out by a white farmer he told he was visiting kin.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Have a Seat, I'll Be Right With You
clips.summary: Finding the hilltop plantation shack at last in the pouring rain, McLaurin told Fannie Lou Hamer that Bob Moses wanted her at Tougaloo, and she, not knowing him from Adam, simply told him to have a seat and packed her bag, talking of her life all the way.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Road to Nashville
clips.summary: Tougaloo turned out to be a rendezvous for a SNCC conference at Fisk in Nashville, and McLaurin loaded into cars with Hollis Watkins, the Ladner sisters, Curtis Hayes, Victoria Gray, and Fannie Lou Hamer, surviving a tire blowout and a run off the road.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Voice Discovered at Fisk
clips.summary: At the Nashville conference Fannie Lou Hamer was discovered, weaving song and the stories of Shadrach, Daniel, and Paul and Silas into a testimony that held a room full of SNCC leaders spellbound and launched her tour of Northeastern campuses.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Writing the Movement's Reports
clips.summary: With Charlie Cobb gone, McLaurin took over the reports and found himself in the middle of a thriving project, writing up local people's needs and grievances, from a veteran's discharge to plantation evictions, and empowering folk to overcome their fear.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Against Freedom Summer
clips.summary: McLaurin admits he opposed Freedom Summer at the Biloxi meeting, since few voters had actually registered, even as he recognized that the work had built local organizations and pushed Mr. Charlie back off people's lives.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Volunteers and Tracy Sugarman
clips.summary: He recalls the volunteers coming through Oxford training after a man was killed, and meeting the older artist Tracy Sugarman, whom he had earlier stayed with in Connecticut and now welcomed onto his project to draw the movement.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Yellow Cars and Conspicuous Help
clips.summary: McLaurin describes placing well-off Northern volunteers, a tax accountant, a congressman's son, in sharecropper homes without running water, his unease at their conspicuous canary-yellow cars, and his fear that they would dominate the people they came to serve.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Surprise of Reciprocal Respect
clips.summary: To his astonishment the volunteers and the local families built reciprocal respect, Tracy Sugarman shopping with the Williams family, freedom schools filling with elders aged fifty-six to seventy-seven, resources flowing into the homes, and McLaurin's view of white people changed.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Nonviolence as Technique
clips.summary: He reflects that the vote was always his single objective, that nonviolence was a technique rather than a creed to keep the state from wiping the movement out, and that Fannie Lou Hamer, more than any minister, made him feel close to God.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Freedom Schools and Mock Elections
clips.summary: Blocked by the reading-and-writing and constitution-interpretation tests, the movement set up its own mock elections and the freedom schools Charlie Cobb devised, supplementing the four-month plantation schooling the state had denied Black children.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Travis Shot, the Food Cut Off
clips.summary: He recounts the shooting of SNCC's Jimmy Travis near Greenwood that drew workers from across the South, John Doar coming for the Justice Department, and the Leflore County board cutting off federal surplus food to starve out the registration drive, answered by relief from Belafonte, Poitier, and Gregory.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Freedom Ballot, and Why Women Ran
clips.summary: For the Freedom ballot they ran Aaron Henry for governor and Ed King for lieutenant governor, and chose women, Hamer, Victoria Gray, Annie Devine, for Congress because in those communities the women were the brave and active ones not dependent on white employers.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Qualifying Hamer for Congress
clips.topic: The Day at the Secretary of State
clips.summary: Told to take the sixth-grade-educated Fannie Lou Hamer to legally qualify for Congress, McLaurin marveled at her readiness, and they faced a roomful of white women gawking at the two who wanted to run, then the five-hundred-dollar fee that a sharecropper might not clear in a year.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Campaign Manager by Default
clips.summary: As the office neared closing on the last day to qualify, with no campaign manager arriving, Fannie Lou Hamer told McLaurin to put his own name on the papers, saying he knew as much about being a campaign manager as she did about running for Congress.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Greenville Courtroom
clips.summary: McLaurin reflects that SNCC made people do what they never imagined, then recounts the segregated Greenville city courtroom where, with Black spectators kept standing outside, he took a seat in the empty white section and was arrested for inciting a riot.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Three Times to the Supreme Court
clips.topic: A Case That Would Not Die
clips.summary: He describes how his Greenville case, defended by R. Jess Brown, went to the United States Supreme Court three times, only to be remanded as the Warren Court gave way to Nixon's, the city of Greenville bent on putting his Black behind in jail.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Presenting Himself at the Jail
clips.summary: With Stokely Carmichael having warned Greenville officials that SNCC would break him out, McLaurin surprised the waiting police by walking straight into the jail to present himself, then quietly arranged with the decent chief Burnley to serve a couple of nights and be released to go to college.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Redistricting MS-2
clips.summary: He explains how the Second Congressional District, once Black-majority and held by Reconstruction's John R. Lynch, was gerrymandered diagonally to dilute the Black vote, and how he presented a redistricting plan while Henry Kirksey drew the maps that would put Black officials in office.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Atlantic City Compromise
clips.summary: McLaurin recounts the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's rejection of the two-seat compromise at Atlantic City, Lyndon Johnson's fear of losing the South, and how the Democratic rebuff drove the white South into a now-solid Republican column.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Isaiah Montgomery's Shadow
clips.summary: Noting that in 1962 only Mound Bayou held Black elected officials, McLaurin tells of learning that Isaiah Montgomery, the last Black legislator in the 1890s, voted with the whites to take away the vote, a history he found hard to reconcile against Fannie Lou Hamer's stand.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Promise in the Hospital
clips.summary: He recounts visiting the dying Fannie Lou Hamer in the Mound Bayou hospital, where she made him promise she would not be buried back on a plantation, a promise he made believing she would outlive him, only to learn days later that she had passed.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Funeral Home
clips.summary: Depressed by the run-down funeral home preparing to bury Mrs. Hamer simply, McLaurin was approached by the undertaker Henry Espy, who offered a burial fit for a president and the assurance not to worry about the money, and the body was turned over to him.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Place Not on a Plantation
clips.summary: Determined to keep his promise that she not be buried on a plantation, McLaurin found that Mrs. Hamer's own Freedom Farm land in town could serve, and attorney Cleve McDowell cleared the legal hurdle of burying within the city limits.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Funeral No One Could Afford
clips.summary: With the funeral nearing ten thousand dollars, Owen Brooks took over the planning and the Delta Foundation through MACE paid the bill, and Mrs. Hamer was buried on her own land, McLaurin hoping someone would one day pick up the bare grave site and run with it.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Headstone and the Statue
clips.summary: He traces how Head Start workers first raised money for a headstone, how Pat Thompson and others slowly built up the site, and how a hundred-thousand-dollar effort joined by supporters in New Jersey finally erected a statue of Mrs. Hamer surveying the Delta.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The March Against Fear
clips.summary: McLaurin recalls James Meredith's 1966 March Against Fear, shot from ambush soon after stepping off, continued by King, McKissick, and Carmichael, and how the Hamer v. Campbell suit led the movement to route the march through the Delta to encourage registration.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Securing Greenwood
clips.summary: He describes scouting Greenwood for the marchers' campsite, being run off the Mississippi Valley State campus by state troopers, finding Stokely Carmichael jailed for raising a tent at a school, and the plan to fill a Black park in the heart of the community.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Night Black Power Was Born
clips.summary: With the park overflowing, a freed Stokely Carmichael took the stage and called for Black power, a phrase Willie Ricks had been voicing, and McLaurin, named one of Carmichael's Mississippi comrades, reflects that Black power only ever meant Black people having a say in their own destiny through the vote.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Interview Opening and Introductions
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill opens the recording on 4 June 2011 in Albany, Georgia, with cameraman John Bishop at the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion. He introduces the Civil Rights History Project as a joint undertaking of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture and welcomes Rev. Charles Sherrod.
related_events: Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion 2011

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Arrival in Albany From Rock Hill
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts coming to Albany around October 1961 with the experience of Rock Hill, South Carolina behind him, where he had been jailed on the chain gang. He describes the eighth chapter of Romans as the conviction that carried him into Southwest Georgia.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Nothing Can Separate Me
clips.summary: Sherrod explains the meaning the Rock Hill jail and chain gang gave to Romans 8, the simple verse that nothing can separate him from the love of God and Christ Jesus. He describes carrying that conviction to Albany as the belief that nothing but death could stop the mission of developing his people.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Indignities of Daily Jim Crow
clips.summary: Sherrod describes how long it took to understand how to reach the elder who would not raise his eyes to a white man or woman. He recounts the indignity of Black women required to pull a stocking cap over their heads before trying on a hat, then paying with deference.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Existing Leadership Before SNCC
clips.summary: Sherrod frames the challenge of breaking people away from fearing the sheriff and going down to the courthouse. He credits the leadership already present in Albany, including voter registration drives and the Criterion Club's petition to the City Commission for paved streets and better lighting.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Permission From the Adults
clips.summary: Sherrod describes how he, Charles A. Jones, and Cordell Reagon obtained permission from the adults and the community to bring the movement into Albany. As organizers aged 19, 20, and 21, they turned first to young people, gathering small numbers in the churches that would have them.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Churches Open Under Threat
clips.summary: Sherrod recalls young people coming to meetings in small churches, with the Presbyterian church in the city the first to open its doors. Other churches hesitated because of threats that any church hosting meetings would be burned to the ground, and some were.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Children Without the Fear
clips.summary: Sherrod reflects on the problem of removing the fear that kept adults from standing up to the sheriff, police, and chief. The answer was the children, who had not been persecuted under the old order and could be taught nonviolence week after week against opponents who were looking for violence.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Recruiting the Four Gangs
clips.summary: Sherrod describes confronting opponents with love and expecting peace, while still finding at least one switchblade knife whenever marchers lined up in the church. He recounts recruiting the east, west, north, and south side gangs to march nonviolently, drawing high school and state college students together for the campaign.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Testing the Bus Station
clips.summary: Sherrod recalls that the first project was integrating the local bus station. He, Charles A. Jones, and Cordell tried it out first to confirm that arrests would follow, and found that they would.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: First March Fills Two Churches
clips.summary: Sherrod describes the strength people felt after their children were arrested, when the first march of about 255 people filled Shiloh Baptist Church and Mount Zion Baptist Church, facing each other, with thousands milling in the streets. He estimates two to three thousand people at that meeting.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Filling the County Jails
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts the plan to keep demonstrating until the whole jail system was filled. When the Albany jail filled, authorities moved arrestees to Americus, Georgia and to Terrell, Baker, and Mitchell counties, the same counties the movement would later return to organize.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Heads Up, Still Loving
clips.summary: Sherrod describes the joy of finding the same elders who once bent over with their heads down now speaking to white people without fear, trying on hats and picketing stores that would not change. He frames it as a great day for Albany, marching together while still loving in the face of hostility.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Decisions Through the Steering Committee
clips.summary: Asked how he charted a course as the first field secretary, against Laurie Pritchett's tactic of dispersing prisoners across many county jails, Sherrod explains that he and the SNCC organizers did not make decisions alone. The Albany Movement steering committee made decisions together, and most strategy came out of that committee.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Picketing to Desegregate Stores
clips.summary: Sherrod describes the first effort to desegregate all public accommodations by asking store owners to change their policies and picketing those who refused. He notes that incidents at a store, and stores with mostly Black customers but no Black workers on the register, drew the movement's attention.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Smith Grocery and the City Commission
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts picketing Smith Grocery for two days until it closed, after which other stores opened their doors and began hiring against the wishes of the City Commission. He explains that the Commission wanted to keep segregation while business owners, feeling the loss, wanted to stop segregating their customers.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: The Bus Boycott and City Transit
clips.summary: Sherrod describes talking to the bus company, which was willing to integrate and hire Black drivers while the city blocked it. After a girl was thrown off a bus, no one rode, the movement started its own transportation system, and the city eventually began operating its own transit.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Baker County Voter Registration March
clips.summary: Asked about a night of fear, Sherrod turns to Baker County, Georgia, where mass meetings and strategy came from the Baker County Movement. He describes a pleasant day with about 150 people lined up from the church toward the courthouse to demonstrate for the voting process before national news.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Beaten With Ax Handles
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts seeing in his peripheral vision white young men with ax handles approaching the marchers as they walked toward the courthouse. He kept walking until blows struck his head, knocking him into the street and leaving him bleeding profusely as they beat him to the ground.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: An Elder Shields His Body
clips.summary: Sherrod credits an old woman trained in his nonviolent direct-action workshops, where he taught participants to throw their bodies across one another to share the blows. She screamed for the attackers to stop before they killed him, and that action saved his life until he was taken to the hospital.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Reflecting on the Reunion Week
clips.summary: Asked how the weekend felt, Sherrod describes the joy of seeing people he had not seen in 50, 15, or 10 years. He shares the week's good news: signing contracts, securing a license for a radio station, and a New Communities Inc. land contract, blessings he says are too much goodness to bear.
related_events: Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion 2011

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Closing Thanks and Archive Tag
clips.summary: Mosnier thanks Reverend Sherrod for sitting down, calling it a privilege and honor. The recording closes with the standard tag identifying it as a presentation of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: A House Full of Readers and a Pencil
clips.summary: Siler describes the Baton Rouge family he was born into, a beautician mother who kept everyone's confidences, a piano-teacher grandmother, and a grandfather who, finding the toddler drawing stick figures on the wall, set him up with paper and pencil and abetted his creativity.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: A Family of Independent Men
clips.summary: He recalls a family of readers and independent businessmen with sharp senses of humor, and his first protest around age eleven, quitting the Boy Scouts and starting an explorer post rather than march behind the horses in the segregated Baton Rouge parade.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: McKinley High and the Paper-Cup Protest
clips.summary: At McKinley High, known for academics, athletics, and fighting, Siler and his classmates were locked on campus during the March 1960 Southern University demonstration to keep them from joining, and he recalls small protests like pouring a paper cup over the whites-only water fountain.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Uncles Who Shot Back
clips.summary: He tells of an uncle who sat in his Lafayette cleaners at night with a gun and a reputation for shooting back, a father who played ball with Satchel Paige and started the Compton baseball league, and uncles, mostly veterans, who tended to work for themselves.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: The Blues, and a Library He Couldn't Use
clips.summary: Siler remembers his grandmother's gospel career and the yellow dog blues she once played him, the radio shows with his grandfather, and the senior-year picket of the state library he was barred from using, alongside an artist uncle who had to leave town.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: An Inside Agitator
clips.summary: He explains targeting the library because he had read everything in the Black branch, the lesson that you had to be several times as good if you were Black, and why the students waited until after final exams so the school could not use absences against them.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: The Southern Demonstration of 1960
clips.summary: Siler frames Southern University's 1960 demonstration as the largest mass protest to hit the South that year, led by Marvin Robinson, Donald Moss, and Major Johns, and recalls shaking Martin Luther King's hand at the 1962 NAACP convention in Atlanta.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Daddy Rode His Bike to Work
clips.summary: He describes a grandfather who rode a bicycle to work rather than pay to be told where to sit on the bus, the lesson that he could speak up if he was right, and the teachers like Mr. Hall and the shell-shocked Mr. Altamous who quietly supported the students.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Foot Soldier at Southern
clips.summary: Siler recounts becoming a foot soldier at Southern in 1961, an art major drafted to make protest signs, hitchhiking to campus with his junior-high friend H. Rap Brown, and the nonviolence training led by CORE's Dave Dennis and Jerome Smith.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: The Uncle Who Made Him Fight
clips.summary: He tells of the uncle who fled Baton Rouge for fighting back and changed his name, and who once made a young Siler stay in a losing fight for half an hour, teaching him that it was not about winning but that bullies do not want people to fight back.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Voices That Stood Up
clips.summary: Siler recalls the uncles who shaped him, Gene with a hidden gun never far away, Earl who became a minister, and Ralph who beat a man taunting him at prayer and then knelt back down to finish, voices of standing up that he carried into his first sit-in against the huge men sent to intimidate.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Christmas in the Baton Rouge Jail
clips.summary: He describes being arrested in December 1961 picketing the Bon Marche center, his friend Willie Bradford asking the cop what he would do with the other five thousand, and spending the Christmas holidays in the East Baton Rouge Parish jail.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: When the Police Rioted
clips.summary: Going back out the next day on a dare, Siler watched thousands assemble to protest the arrests, and from the jail roof heard the police riot, tear-gassing peaceful marchers whose singing of We Shall Overcome set them off, while 340 prisoners were packed into a space built for forty-two.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Moses Parting the Waters
clips.summary: He recalls three weeks in jail, the fearless little lawyer Lolis Elie parting a crowd of giant deputies to free them, the charges thrown out as unconstitutional, and being banned from campus and expelled along with many who never returned to school.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Walking the Vote, Door to Door
clips.summary: Hired by the NAACP through Reverends Jemison and Walker, Siler walked much of South Baton Rouge door to door teaching people to pass the so-called voter-registration test, meeting the fearful who would not get involved and a grandmother who backed his standing up.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: A Klan Picket and the Crystal Sit-In
clips.summary: At the 1962 NAACP convention in Atlanta he nearly fought a Klansman on the picket line and joined a sit-in at the Crystal that the owners tried to steam out, running afoul of a no-protest agreement the youth knew nothing about, until Clara Luper, Daisy Bates, and Ella Baker took them aside.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Three Days Before Medgar Died
clips.topic: Medgar Evers and a Legal Threat
clips.summary: Siler recalls meeting Medgar and Charles Evers at a Baton Rouge church three days before Medgar was murdered, his return to Southern cartooning for the paper, and a trumped-up federal-injunction threat of ten years that his well-known grandmother's reputation helped dissolve.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Drafted, From Nuclear Weapons to the Newsroom
clips.summary: After theater work in California with Frank Silvera, Siler was drafted in 1967 into a strange military odyssey from nuclear-weapons school toward truck driving, and was on charge of quarters at Fort Huachuca the night Dr. King was killed, talking soldiers out of fights.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: A Voice for the Black Soldier
clips.summary: In Vietnam his typing and art degree landed him in an information office, where he cartooned, learned etching from the mimeograph, and made sure the Black soldiers got the hometown news releases and stories that had gone largely untold.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Race Riots Nobody Talks About
clips.summary: Siler recalls the orphanage and maimed children, the things a returning soldier cannot explain to people back home, and the unspoken race fights when field soldiers came into the cities and white soldiers told Black ones they could not enter.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Judging the Heart, Not the Color
clips.summary: Out of the military as an independent cartoonist who picks at wrong on all sides, Siler reflects on white friends who once meant to take him to a Klan rally, his friend Sam Caster's line that blood matters more than skin, and his work as a sports information director and at the first Black sports network.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Culture and Politics Are Not Separate
clips.summary: He describes returning to Louisiana to run the country's first state division of Black culture under Governor Edwin Edwards, his Black 365 campaign to the radio stations, and his argument that politics shapes culture, those in power can harm a culture simply by overlooking it.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: An American African
clips.summary: As a Louisiana State Museum curator Siler fell in love with the Mardi Gras Indians, navigated the inter-office politics of doing their exhibit, and offers his self-definition as an American African, holding that, since humanity began in Africa, everyone is in a sense African.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Teaching the Humanity in the Joke
clips.summary: He recounts representing the museum on Jazz Fest's African heritage stage, telling schoolchildren the truth about being in jail, calling himself a foot soldier who made his difference one to one, and the comedy clubs where he and the comics argued perception and humanity.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: The Power of the Pen and Humor
clips.summary: Siler reflects on his editorial cartooning for the Louisiana Weekly and beyond, the way making people laugh makes them remember what he said, the charismatic teacher Huel Perkins reciting Cyrano, and his wish to leave something his grandson could one day call up in the museum.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Be Chuck Siler
clips.summary: He celebrates the folk art around him and the mentors, Hubbard, Hayden, and Cureau, who taught him not to imitate them but to be Chuck Siler, and remembers the friends he went to jail with, including Willie Bradford, who once yanked his hand from a closing door.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Miss Williams Gets Her Vote
clips.summary: Siler tells of an eighty-four-year-old woman, the daughter of slaves, turned away twice but determined to register before she died, whom he escorted with a gun hidden under his jacket, and who walked through the intimidating deputies in charge and got her vote.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Friends Across Every Line, and the Quakers
clips.summary: He closes on learning from his government-teacher wife and from friends across the political and religious spectrum, his contempt for a minister too afraid to host voter-registration workshops, and his gratitude to the Quakers and the American Friends Service Committee who gave the movement places to meet.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Born to Domestic Servants
clips.summary: Jones, who asks to be recorded as Clarence B. Jones for his mother Benjamin, was born in 1931 in Philadelphia to domestic-servant parents, a maid-and-cook mother and a chauffeur-gardener father who worked for the Lippincott publishing family, and was placed with foster families before his mother decided on a boarding school.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Boarding School for Colored Boys
clips.summary: From about six to fourteen he lived at a Catholic boarding school for indigent colored and Native American boys run by the Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament, the only child there with living parents, who visited and left, prompting the other boys to ask why they did not take him home.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Stuttering and the Nuns' Grammar
clips.summary: He recalls developing a serious stutter he later overcame, and the rigorous grammar the nuns drilled into him with a knuckle-rapping pointer, an education in Latin and diagrammed sentences so thorough that decades later an editor marveled at his flawless prose.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Not Anti-White, and Most Likely to Succeed
clips.summary: Jones explains to Dr. King years later that the unconditional love and discipline he received as a Negro boy from white Irish nuns left him able to see racism as the ideology of white supremacy without hating white people, and recounts excelling at Palmyra High, voted most likely to succeed.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Navy, Columbia, and Two Nobel Teachers
clips.summary: After he tried to enlist underage in the Navy's college program, his principal and teachers paid his application fees, and at Columbia, on a financial-aid package that demanded a B-minus average, he faced his academic Waterloo in physics under Nobel laureates Linus Pauling and Polykarp Kusch.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Working His Way Through, and the Clarinet
clips.summary: Jones describes waiting tables for food and money, his mother surprising him at the Nedick's counter, and the confidence his music gave him as a first-chair clarinetist in the New Jersey all-state orchestra who studied at Juilliard and turned toward jazz.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Sitting In With Charlie Parker
clips.summary: At Manny's Music shop the owner sent the shy young clarinetist upstairs to Charlie Parker, who made him assemble his horn and play along through Indiana and On the Sunny Side of the Street, then told him he was on his way, a profound encounter with a genius who improvised within the melodic line.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: An Emergency Call on His Birthday
clips.summary: Taking midterms on his birthday, Jones was called to the dean's office and learned that his mother had been operated on for rectal cancer, given a colostomy and three or four months to live, leaving his father in inconsolable grief.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Desk at the Foot of Her Bed
clips.summary: Refusing to drop out, he arranged with the dean to keep up his classes and set a desk at the foot of his mother's bed to do his homework, leaving periodically for New York, while she wept and insisted he return to school, not believing he truly had permission to study.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Letter Under the Mattress
clips.summary: After his mother died, wasted to under a hundred pounds, the movers found the letter she had hidden under the mattress: an apology for leaving him, a recipe for the crullers and floating island she could no longer make, and her certainty that he would one day be a great man.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Made Fearless, and Drafted
clips.summary: Jones marks his mother's death as the apex of pain that made him fearless, a tempering by the hottest fire, just as the loss of his student deferment after her death led his draft board to take him in August 1953.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Paul Robeson's Touchdown and Defiance
clips.summary: He recounts the two encounters with Paul Robeson that shaped him: Robeson telling him one touchdown by a Negro before fifty thousand at Baker Field would do more for equality than any leaflet, and Robeson defying the House committee chaired by James Eastland as unconstitutionally seated.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Statement He Wrote the Night Before
clips.summary: Angry and politicized, and convinced his Mount Holly board had drafted him out of prejudice, Jones turned down a friend's offer to help him flee to Eastern Europe and instead wrote a handwritten statement that he would report for induction but not serve a country that denied him first-class citizenship.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Reporting Without Raising His Hand
