clips.summary: At Whitehall Street he refused to raise his hand or sign anything, handed officers his statement, and was sent to Fort Dix, where he laughed at being slotted for special-forces training to parachute into the Soviet bloc, wondering how he would explain his Black face in the Russian countryside.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Left-Wing Friends and Lorraine Hansberry
clips.summary: Jones traces the New York leftist circle that shaped him, the poet Robert Nemiroff, whose family ran a restaurant where Jones picked up conversational Yiddish and who married the playwright Lorraine Hansberry, and the D'Lugoff brothers of the Village Gate.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Informant Who Confessed
clips.summary: In a newly integrated army he became a target after refusing to sign the loyalty oath, was warned he might be taught a lesson, and was betrayed by a close friend named Hinton who broke down drunk and confessed that his job in the platoon was to write reports on Jones, then vanished.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Soldier of the Month, Tried as a Security Risk
clips.summary: Though named soldier of the month and given a good-conduct medal, Jones was charged after twenty-one months as a security risk on absurd allegations, membership in the Communist Party since age seven among them, and was found an undesirable, discharged under armed guard.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Appeal and the Honorable Discharge
clips.summary: With the ACLU lawyer Stanley Faulkner he appealed to the Armed Services Discharge Review Board, won a general then, through a writ of mandamus, an honorable discharge with back pay and GI Bill eligibility, an ordeal he frames as proof that his mother's death had made him fearless.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Meeting Ann Norton at the Library
clips.summary: At the New York Public Library Jones ran into Ann Norton, a progressive white woman he had known from Sarah Lawrence parties, who helped type his appeal transcript, became romantically involved with him, and married him in a major turning point of his life.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Banker Father-in-Law and the Clarinet
clips.summary: Jones describes Ann's family behind the Norton publishing house and her stepfather Daniel Crena de Iongh, a Dutch banker and early World Bank treasurer whose closest friend was the apartheid architect Hendrik Verwoerd, and how playing the Mozart clarinet concerto won over his classical-musician mother-in-law.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Idealism, Class, and Apartheid at the Table
clips.summary: He paints Ann's deep idealism alongside an upper-class certainty that could look down on others, her friendship with the Dole pineapple heiress Damon, and her rebellion against a stepfather she could not reconcile with what he did, which she felt Jones treated too gently.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Boston Apartment and the Irish Superintendent
clips.summary: After apartments fell through once landlords learned they were an interracial couple, Ann secured a Beacon Street place sight-unseen for her husband, and Jones won over the astonished Irish superintendent with his boarding-school memories of Irish nuns and Danny Boy, gaining a devoted friend.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Copyright Law and Entertainment in California
clips.summary: Jones recounts his prize-winning copyright paper at Boston University, his professor Donald Shapiro's counsel that the real power in entertainment is behind the camera, and his move to California to work in entertainment law until his mentor at Revue Productions died of a heart attack.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Judge Delany's Call and a Refusal
clips.summary: Judge Hubert Delany asked Jones to come south as a law clerk on Dr. King's Alabama tax-perjury defense, and when Jones declined for practical reasons, King himself flew to California and came to his Altadena home to ask for the help of a young Negro lawyer, which Jones again put off.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Sermon That Changed His Mind
clips.summary: Invited to King's guest sermon, Jones heard the preacher describe him from the pulpit and recast Langston Hughes's Mother to Son with Jones's own mother as the actor, moving him to tears and to walk up afterward and ask, Dr. King, when do you want me to go to Montgomery.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Birmingham's Spark and Lending King the House
clips.summary: Jones frames Birmingham as the spark that lit thirteen hundred demonstrations across the country, and recounts how, with King exhausted, Stanley Levison and Bayard Rustin arranged for the Joneses to vacate their new home so King could vacation there and stay reachable for the March.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Pressure on Levison and the Speaker Fight
clips.summary: He recounts President Kennedy pressing King to cut ties with Stanley Levison and Jack O'Dell over alleged Communist ties, and the March committee meeting where conservative clergy maneuvered to limit each speaker and bury King in the middle of the program.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Opening Act or Major Act
clips.summary: Standing in for the absent King, Jones drew on his concert-business knowledge to ask the committee whether they really wanted to follow Dr. King, an opening act before the major act, and with A. Philip Randolph's backing, King was made the final speaker.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Promissory Note, and Mahalia's Shout
clips.summary: Jones describes drafting the speech's opening, the promissory note and the bank of justice with insufficient funds, which King delivered verbatim, until Mahalia Jackson called out to tell them about the dream and King set the text aside to speak extemporaneously.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Cosmic Force, and the Genius of Nonviolence
clips.summary: Jones testifies that he had never heard King speak that way before or after, as if a cosmic force took over his body, and lays out the political genius of summoning the conscience of the 88 percent rather than imposing the case of the 12 percent by force.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Moral Compass for America
clips.summary: He argues that King made segregation a moral rather than a legal question, planting a moral compass in Birmingham and taking an addicted America by the hand toward a redemptive, nonviolent recovery, insisting that one day the descendants of slaves and slaveholders would sit at the table of brotherhood.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: From the Dream to Today's Violence
clips.summary: Jones turns King's legacy on the present, defending LGBT people against the conservative Black church, tying the 1963 images of Birmingham's fire hoses to the children killed at Newtown, and refusing to commemorate the March without confronting the ubiquitous violence in the country.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Broken Family and the Omega Boys Club
clips.summary: He points to the destruction of family structure and the 1980 arrival of crack cocaine as fundamental changes, and to Dr. Joseph Marshall's Omega Boys Club as a substitute parental discipline that keeps young people alive and free, with King's reminder that a teacher must tell a story.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Wealth, War, and What King Would Say
clips.summary: Jones closes on the parts of King's legacy he says must be honored, the fight against wealth disparity behind the Memphis sanitation strike and echoed by Occupy Wall Street, the moral and practical opposition to the Vietnam War, and his reading that King would meet even same-sex marriage with love, the lion telling his own story so the hunters do not get all the credit.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Born in Marion County, Oldest of Ten
clips.summary: Magee was born in Marion County, Columbia, Mississippi in 1932, the oldest of ten children of Glessie and O'Reilly Leggett Magee, a sharecropper father and a mother who worked the house and the fields.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: A Father Who Vowed His Children Would Read
clips.summary: He describes a father who, before he ever married, vowed his children would go to school, who cleared new ground with an axe to buy land, and a self-sufficient farm with no electricity or plumbing where the family raised everything they ate and hauled water up the hill.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: The Grandfather's Broken Promise, and Tragedy at Alcorn
clips.summary: Magee tells the story of his father's father breaking a promise to let the children attend school, which fixed his father's vow, and his own first great setback when his mother died from childbirth thirty days into his time at Alcorn, leaving him to choose whether to go back.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: A Diabetic Coma and a Grandmother's House
clips.summary: He recounts his father's diabetic coma during that same school year, the doctor's order to keep working and go on a diet that bought him decades, and how his grandmother across the lane took in and raised four of the children after his mother's death.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Plowing, Picking Cotton, and Beating His Father
clips.summary: Magee describes his father the trainer, who paid each child a penny a pound to teach them weighing and arithmetic, and the morning he came in late, was scolded, and out of anger beat his own father picking cotton, then beat him every day after.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: The Homemade Bus and Hand-Me-Down Books
clips.summary: He recalls walking a mile and a half in any weather to catch the homemade bus a relative ran while white children rode a real bus past the house, the books worn five years with other children's names in them, and a community that helped keep the education-minded family on the right path.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Choosing Alcorn, and the Nickname KT
clips.summary: Magee explains choosing the rural agricultural Alcorn over a city school because he feared the nightlife, working jobs to pay his room and board, and learning, from a garbage-detail nickname, how not to react to what was said to get a rise out of him.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Drafted to Germany, and a Marriage
clips.summary: Deferred through college by an arrangement with the draft board, Magee was drafted after graduating, married a woman he met in Marion County during a furlough, and shipped to Germany for two years while a home was purchased for them back in Hattiesburg.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: A Teaching Career, and the Federal Government
clips.summary: He traces his career teaching across Mississippi, at Wiggins, Prentiss Institute, Eureka, Lillie Burney, and Rowan, before moving to Mobile in 1974 for a federal food-stamp job he held until retirement, working a compressed week to be home weekends.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Two Houses, and the Back Porch
clips.summary: Magee describes how the bond between his sharecropping family and the white family they worked for could feel close, sharing the warmer house in winter, yet at that family's table the Magees ate on the back porch, a line that vanished only inside their own home.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: What You Learn by Watching
clips.summary: He recalls being hurried out the back of a pool hall, a car that nearly hit him on the street, and learning from his parents' silent actions which places not to go and which things not to say, following good leaders.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: What Do You Want, Boy
clips.summary: After buying a home in 1956 and paying the two-dollar poll tax, Magee went to register, met a deputy clerk who said the registrar was out, then a registrar who demanded who sent him, called him boy, and told him to wait, all without explanation.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: The Cat-and-Mouse Test, and the Lawsuit
clips.summary: He recounts the literacy test and constitution interpretation he treated as a cat-and-mouse game, his wife's repeated attempts, and the federal Fifth Circuit case in which her application was set beside that of a white truck driver who could not read yet was passed, helping hold the registrar in contempt.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: J.C. Fairley and a Church That Turned Them Away
clips.summary: Magee traces his NAACP connection through branch president J.C. Fairley, a fellow churchgoer, and the army experience that sensitized him, a white soldier turned away with him from a Virginia church, gross to him in a house of worship, which led most of his small congregation to join.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Sensitivity Training for Freedom Summer
clips.summary: He describes the work he did most during Freedom Summer, sensitivity training for Northern youngsters who did not know the local culture, warning them what words and gestures would set people off and where the danger spots were, while churches housed and fed the volunteers.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: The Biracial Education Committee
clips.summary: Magee recounts the biracial committee working on a school-desegregation strategy, how the Black members met first to role-play the questions, and the volatile meetings with Superintendent Spinks and a finger-pointing principal whom the committee chair met point for point.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Harassment and a Pickup-Truck Chase
clips.summary: He describes the harassment and terror that the word terrorist only later named, a threatening call to his wife during the trial, a man trying to shoulder him in the courthouse hallway, and the night a pickup truck chased him through Hattiesburg until he outmaneuvered it.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Smuggling Dr. King, and the False Notion of Freedom
clips.summary: Magee notes he missed the night Dr. King was smuggled into town, frames his desegregation hopes as better supplies rather than rubbing shoulders, and presses his recurring theme that a freedom you do not struggle for is easily lost and carries a huge responsibility.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Poverty Has No Eyes
clips.summary: Drawing on his federal work visiting homes that received benefits, Magee reflects that poverty has no eyes and that comfort can dull the incentive to do better, illustrated with the cell phone his wife left at a gas station, a parable of unfamiliar technology.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: N.R. Burger's Buffer and the Rowan Walkout
clips.summary: He recalls how city teachers kept their jobs while county teachers known to be in the NAACP were dismissed, the principal N.R. Burger acting as a buffer, the 1969 student walkout and march from Rowan he monitored, and why he took a non-teaching job since teachers had no tenure.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Witness for Clyde Kennard
clips.summary: Magee and his wife were subpoenaed to Aberdeen in the bitter cold to witness that Clyde Kennard, dying at Parchman, was still alive, both going despite the risk, brought in by the Black attorney R. Jess Brown to satisfy a court order to prove him living.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Vernon Dahmer, a Burning Cross, and Sam Bowers
clips.summary: He remembers Vernon Dahmer through sons who were his classmates, standing watch near the highway after Dahmer's house was burned, the chemical cross sprayed on his own lawn while he kept a rifle by the door, and the eventual conviction of Sam Bowers.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Education, Education, Education
clips.summary: Magee says the legacy to remember is the courage to keep on, that three words, education, education, and education, would solve the country's ills, and that the stories must be told because they were left out of the history books even though the people were history.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Building Housing and a Head Start Agency
clips.summary: He describes the Bread Association for civic improvement that built community housing later lost to Katrina, and how a biracial committee he helped form outmaneuvered a senator's paper plan to win Forrest County its own community-action agency and Head Start.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: The University, and Preventing a Ferguson
clips.summary: Magee recounts his relationship with the once-segregated University of Southern Mississippi and its Black president, mediating campus conflicts, and the NAACP's proactive work to keep the city from becoming a Ferguson, since here both an officer and the victim bled into the same red soil.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: A Great-Uncle Lynched, and Restorative Justice
clips.summary: Without bitterness, Magee closes on a great-uncle lynched on Richburg Road in 1929 with no one ever brought to trial, his interest in the restorative-justice efforts a few communities are attempting, and his sense that it could just as easily have been him, honored simply to be alive and still contributing.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: A Full Circle to Voorhees College
clips.summary: Sellers describes the full circle of his life in Denmark, South Carolina, from college mascot at three to president of Voorhees in 2008, growing up in a rigidly segregated town that was long majority Black yet held every official office in white hands.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: An Education That Reached the World
clips.summary: He recalls the Booker T. Washington model of the Voorhees-affiliated school, where teachers instilled education, faith, and service, where students openly discussed Emmett Till from the cover of Jet and projected his memory into a resolve to find remedies, and where African independence and figures like Nkrumah were common talk.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Family, Faith, and a Father in France
clips.summary: Sellers traces his entrepreneur father, a World War II veteran of France who built rental property and a motel, his educator mother who ran a recreation center and mentored young women, and a churchgoing family that moved through AME, Baptist, and the Episcopal Church he has kept since.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The Sit-Ins Come to Denmark
clips.summary: A high schooler working the student center, Sellers watched the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins fill the room each night, and recounts the Voorhees students marching downtown in their Sunday best after the president quietly withdrew the bus, being arrested and retrieved by the college priest.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: A Father's Objection, a School's Rejection
clips.summary: Sellers describes organizing an NAACP youth chapter and his father forbidding him to attend its rally, his frustration, and his rejected application to a Virginia military academy that turned him away once he answered that he was Negro, deepening his search for a place to be his own person in the movement.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Jail-No-Bail at Rock Hill
clips.summary: He recounts taking carloads of Denmark students to Rock Hill, where Charles Jones, Diane Nash, and Ruby Doris Smith had come from Atlanta and gone to jail in the jail-no-bail stand, his first link to SNCC, amid a growing wave of South Carolina activity from Charleston to Clemson.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: A Bourgeois Campus, and Jeans
clips.summary: Sellers arrived at Howard in 1962 expecting a hotbed of activity and instead found a professional, status-seeking campus where his Southern jeans and longer hair drew friction, and where he came to feel his role was to push the whole process open rather than become a token.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: NAG, and Bringing Malcolm to Howard
clips.summary: He describes finding his niche in the Nonviolent Action Group with Mike Thelwell and Charlie Cobb, picketing the Justice Department, FBI, and White House and taking up DC home rule, placing members in the campus newspaper and government, and pushing the administration to host first Bayard Rustin and then Malcolm X.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The March on Washington and the 16th Street Bombing
clips.summary: Sellers recounts working the March on Washington logistics under Bayard Rustin, the controversy over John Lewis's speech and A. Philip Randolph's personal appeal to soften it, the euphoria of the day, and how the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing shattered it and pushed the Emmett Till generation toward full-time work.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Recruiting for Freedom Summer
clips.summary: Assigned by Bob Moses to generate volunteers for Freedom Summer, Sellers and NAG recruited from the Maryland and DC campuses, leveraging the largest contingent of African American volunteers to get into the plantation black belt of Mississippi's second congressional district.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The Cambridge Movement and George Wallace
clips.summary: He describes the violent Cambridge, Maryland movement under martial law, Gloria Richardson's radicalism, and the night George Wallace came to town when the National Guard used a nauseating tear gas, a SNCC photographer was bayoneted, and Sellers was arrested with Stokely Carmichael and held in the armory.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The Three Who Did Not Return
clips.summary: At the Ohio orientation Sellers learned a church had been burned in Philadelphia and saw Chaney, Schwerner, and Goodman off to investigate, and recounts Bob Moses gathering everyone to say the three were missing and presumed dead, and the volunteers' choice, after a song, of whether to still go.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Searching the Barns by Night
clips.summary: He describes the collective who drove into Mississippi from four directions to search for the bodies, hiding by day in barn lofts and going out at night with maps and no lights, watching for the fire towers, until after two nights they pulled the plug to spare the farmers who sheltered them.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The Holly Springs Project
clips.summary: Sellers describes the large Holly Springs project spanning several counties, with master teachers, a nurse, a freedom school, and a community center, the freedom days that took people to register at the cost of their names in the paper, and the young people like Roy DeBerry it sent off to college.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Working His Way Out of a Job
clips.summary: He explains taking over as project director from Ivanhoe Donaldson and the philosophy of organizing communities so local people like Rita could take over, working their way out of a job, and the records the group archived at Rust College that turned Holly Springs into a progressive city.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The Burned Station Wagon on Display
clips.summary: Sellers recounts documenting the summer in WATS logs and the disloyal, openly discriminatory regular Democratic Party, and hauling the bell from the burned Philadelphia church and the murdered workers' burned-out station wagon to Atlantic City to show the level of violence used to defend segregation.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The Compromise and Fannie Lou Hamer
clips.summary: He describes the Johnson administration handing the convention to Humphrey and Mondale, the two-seats-at-large compromise, the parade of coalition figures sent to sell it as a victory, and how Johnson called a press conference to bump Fannie Lou Hamer's I-question-America testimony off the air.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Devastated by Power Politics
clips.summary: Sellers recalls being proud of the delegation's refusal yet devastated that morality was not the basis of decisions inside the Democratic Party, a turn that pushed SNCC to set the party aside and look outward toward self-determination and movements in Africa, Asia, and Cuba.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: John Lewis and Bloody Sunday
clips.summary: He explains SNCC's strategic difference with SCLC, organizing versus mobilizing, and how the group reluctantly let chairman John Lewis march in Selma on his own conscience, then rushed in after he suffered a concussion on Bloody Sunday, an operational principle of never leaving one of their own unsupported.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: On Different Pages in Selma
clips.summary: Sellers describes taking up the march logistics with Hosea Williams, the anger when King crossed the bridge and turned back without telling SNCC the plan, Forman opening another front in Montgomery, and the underlying tension that SNCC, two years into building Selma, knew a march would dissipate without organization.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Building the Lowndes County Freedom Organization
clips.summary: He recounts the turn to independent political organizing in Lowndes County, Jack Minnis's discovery of a Civil War-era Alabama law allowing a separate county party, and how Sellers marshaled the declining organization's resources and NAG veterans to build the Lowndes County Freedom Organization around empowerment.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: How the Black Panther Got Its Name
clips.summary: Sellers explains how the media labeled the Lowndes County Freedom Organization the Black Panther Party, how the young poll watchers who came to help carried the symbol back to California, Chicago, and New York where Bobby Seale's Oakland party adopted the model, and how the county's voters, defeated the first time, later won office.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Black Power and the Distorted Position Paper
clips.summary: He describes SNCC's 1967 transformation, the killing of Sammy Younge that prompted its anti-war statement, success scattering people, the anti-war movement drawing off white students, and how the Atlanta Project's never-adopted position paper distorted Black Power into a narrow color analysis SNCC had actually fought.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: COINTELPRO and Three Months in Prison
clips.summary: Sellers underscores how badly people underestimate COINTELPRO's effect, and recounts being held in prison for three months in the summer of 1967 over his draft induction, denied bail as a risk until Justice Black ordered his release, time he says he gets credit for nowhere.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The Truth About King and Black Power
clips.summary: Sellers corrects the myth of antagonism between SNCC and Dr. King, recounting the long basement evening when King asked Stokely Carmichael and him to explain Black Power, their handshake never to condemn one another, King inviting them to his Vietnam announcement, and Stokely's help at Resurrection City.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: The University of the Movement
clips.summary: With the Orangeburg massacre and prison left for a later session, Sellers reflects on the movement as a university where he learned from King, Malcolm X, Ella Baker, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Ruby Doris Robinson, his master's at Harvard and doctorate at UNC, and points to his autobiography The River of No Return.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Born in Terrell County
clips.summary: Browner was born in 1946 in Terrell County, Georgia, to Clifford and Johnny Lou Browner, recounting the story of how his parents met at school after his mother prayed for someone to love her, and how his father left sharecropping for a job at a fertilizer plant in Albany.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: A Father's Protection From Jim Crow
clips.summary: He describes a father who shielded the children from segregation, keeping them home and looked after rather than working out where he could not watch them, so that growing up in an all-Black community of dirt streets and three-room houses, Browner never had the direct confrontations others did.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Dunbar Elementary and the Coal Stove
clips.summary: Browner recalls Dunbar Elementary, a faded former-white building heated by a potbelly stove the children fed with coal they dug from the crawl space, with outdoor toilets and no running water, and the bus that carried him seven miles to Carver High, and a mother who cooked in the white school and then the new Black one.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Mass Meetings at Mount Olive
clips.summary: With his father and older sister already involved, Browner came to the movement through the mass meetings moved to his home church, Mount Olive Baptist in Sasser, exciting gatherings where he first saw Black and white intermingling, scriptures and singing mixed with reports of beatings, arrests, and the voter-registration drives across Terrell and Lee counties.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: A Letter for New Books
clips.summary: He recounts how he and Clovis Jones, the only two of five to sign, wrote the superintendent asking for new books instead of the marked-up hand-me-downs from the white school, and were called to the all-Black school's principal, threatened with expulsion, and treated as troublemakers afterward.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: The Night the Church Burned
clips.summary: Browner describes being forced to resign as senior class president over the letter, the night Sheriff Z.T. Matthews and armed white men walked through a mass meeting intimidating them, and waking to find Mount Olive burned to the ground, sitting before the smoldering ruins wondering who would burn a church.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Fear for the Family
clips.summary: He recalls the hurt in his father and the community, helping deacons rebuild, the rumors that their house was spared only because uninvolved elders lived nearby, and the white woman who tried to run his father off the road, after which the family grew more vigilant and the children more restricted.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Joining the Army, and the March on Washington
clips.summary: Unable to afford college, Browner joined the army to save for it, and recounts that the rebuilt church gave him his first hourly wage and the spending money to take his first train ride to the 1963 March on Washington, arriving without a jacket in the unexpected cold.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: A Day on the Mall
clips.summary: He describes a day mostly walking and listening among more people than he had ever seen, hearing the bands, Peter, Paul and Mary, John Lewis, and Dr. King's speech, with no idea of the day's importance, and recalls King having come to his own church before.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Basic Training and Germany
clips.summary: Browner recounts basic training that he found rigorous but fun and that redefined his timid personality, communications and armor training, and twenty months in Kitzingen, Germany, where older people looked after the young soldier and his letters home asked mostly for cake and pecans.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Changed by the World
clips.summary: Returning after two and a half years, Browner found the economics of home little changed but himself transformed, wanting more out of life, introduced to jazz and to men from other cities, which convinced him he needed an education to reach what he now wanted.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Albany State, Working Two Jobs
clips.summary: He describes choosing to come home and enroll at Albany State rather than wait or follow relatives to Detroit and Flint, majoring in sociology while toying with law school, and working two part-time jobs so hard that he missed nearly every homecoming and basketball game.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Reidsville, Flint, and a Job in Five Hours
clips.summary: Browner recounts being the first Black counselor at Reidsville State Prison until a warden forced him out, driving to Flint with eighty dollars after his friend Doug urged him north, the General Motors plant and the computer room he quit over the gossip, and being hired to sell insurance five hours after walking off the job.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Twenty Years at General Motors
clips.summary: He describes transferring back to the General Motors plant in Doraville near Atlanta, where he worked twenty years until a collision tied him up, living in DeKalb County and coming home to Terrell County every chance he got.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: What Changed and What Didn't
clips.summary: Back home for good, Browner finds the economics still depressed, sharecroppers replaced by business farmers who ran Black families off the land for trying to register, a few Black officials but, in his brother's words, little that matters about who drives the wagon when it always goes the same place.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: The Difference Between North and South
clips.summary: He closes on the surface gains that hide how little changed behind the scenes, and his parable of North and South: in the North a white host might break the plate he used, while in the South he already knows where he stands, a perceived freedom against an honest one, and an economic separation he says has replaced the racial line.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: From Harlem to Trinidad and Back
clips.summary: Cox describes being born in Harlem in 1941, raised in Trinidad from 1945 until his grandmother died in 1952, and returning to a New York where his mother sent him to Catholic school, the Trinidad family's deep expectation of college setting him apart from a peer group decimated by drugs.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Two Worlds in the Bronx
clips.summary: He recalls hiding his books coming home to live in both the world of school and the world of the projects, the respect his peers gave his difference, an absent father, and an awareness of segregation and a jazz-scene culture absorbed from older men, though no organized politics yet.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Arriving at Howard
clips.summary: Following a cousin to Howard, Cox arrived without a clear plan but with a sense of right and wrong sharpened by Emmett Till and Montgomery, into a Washington as segregated as any in housing, the police, and the department stores in a way New York had not been.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Founding the Nonviolent Action Group
clips.summary: Cox describes forming the Nonviolent Action Group, picketing Glen Echo Park, RFK Stadium, and segregated Route 40 as Julius Hobson's shock troops, and creating Project Awareness, whose first events were the Malcolm X and Bayard Rustin debate, a Norman Thomas debate, and a Baldwin symposium that drew Sidney Poitier.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: The Genius of Why Not
clips.summary: He recalls NAG run by three large egos, himself, Stokely Carmichael, and Ed Brown, the trips to coordinate with SNCC, and what he now sees as the genius of those seventeen-to-twenty-two-year-olds: moving from asking why the situation existed to asking why not change it, without waiting on those who created it.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Malcolm X and Bayard Rustin at Cramton
clips.summary: Cox recounts the segregation-versus-integration debate in Cramton Auditorium, Professor Dorsey moderating, Malcolm X giving Bayard Rustin's spare minutes back to himself and delivering a message-to-the-grassroots speech with phenomenally disciplined followers, an event that gave NAG a new cachet at Howard.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Nonviolence as a Tactic, Not a Faith
clips.summary: He lays out the early tension between the national group that held nonviolence as a philosophy and a way of life and the Howard group that held it as a tactic, his own view that you might as well appeal to an oppressor's liver as his heart, since the other side knew exactly what it was doing.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: What to Expect From the Federal Government
clips.summary: Cox recalls expecting little of the federal government beyond individuals like John Doar and Burke Marshall, and the FBI agent in bombed McComb who told them they were there to protect the evidence, not the workers, and admitted his own two guns left him scared to death.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Going Limp in Robert Kennedy's Office
clips.summary: He recounts the 1962 sit-in with Stokely Carmichael and Tom Kahn in Robert Kennedy's office, Kahn calling the press from the office, and being dragged out limp until Stokely got up mid-drag to retrieve something and returned to his exact spot, deadly serious work he now recalls with a smile.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Changing John Lewis's Speech
clips.summary: As SNCC's steering-committee representative Cox describes the March on Washington fight when Archbishop O'Boyle threatened to pull out over John Lewis's speech, their first impulse to tell him to go to hell, and how A. Philip Randolph's appeal to hold the coalition led them to soften the speech behind the Lincoln Memorial.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Playing by the Rules at Atlantic City
clips.summary: Cox recounts going to Atlantic City believing that presenting the facts would matter, Johnson's diversionary press conference over Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, the suited SNCC workers lobbying the credentials committee, and the congressman who took their list of supporters and handed it to the Johnson forces to pick them off.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: When Power Trumps the Rules
clips.summary: He describes the two-seats compromise and the whole liberal establishment, from the unions to Martin King to Joseph Rauh, urging the delegation to accept it, the refusal, and the lesson that when it comes to power the rules do not obtain, a moment that pushed many to disengage from the electoral process.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: From Protest to Power
clips.summary: Cox calls Lowndes County the work he is proudest of, where an 80 percent Black county had four registered voters, and explains the turn after Atlantic City from asking others to follow the rules to manipulating an Alabama law that let them build their own county party and seize power.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Comic Books and Mr. Black Man
clips.summary: He describes Jack Minnis's brilliant research into the county offices, the comic books that taught the roles and responsibilities and showed the candidates' faces, and the Mr. Black Man piece he made with Jennifer Lawson around a Mississippi woman's line that Black people had used their mouths to eat and say yes, and it was now time to say no.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Mr. Jackson's Gun and Taking the County
clips.summary: Cox recounts the landowner Mr. Jackson who gave them his house and stayed up nights with his gun, the argument that the answer to a brutal sheriff is a new sheriff, and convincing John Hulett and others to run until they could take every office, asking what it profits a man to gain the vote and not control it.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Living With Fear in Lowndes
clips.summary: Coming in after Jonathan Daniels was killed and Ruby Sales nearly so, Cox describes SNCC's trademark of living where they worked in a house with a hole in the roof, a high-speed chase down dirt roads, being the only person of color at the Wilkins trial for Viola Liuzzo's killing, and telling himself not to be paralyzed by fear.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Carmichael, Lewis, and Black Power
clips.summary: He traces SNCC growing more militant after Atlantic City, Stokely Carmichael replacing John Lewis as reflecting the mood of younger members, and how Black Power, raised on the Meredith march, drew condemning editorials and the false framing of a good early SNCC and a bad later one.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Organize Your Own Community
clips.summary: Cox explains the much-misunderstood decision at the Peg Leg Bates meeting, framed by Jim Forman as sending white organizers to work in the white community where the problem lay, his own remark that racism was necessary but not sufficient, the hurt it caused white members, and the funding it cost.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: The Russell Tribunal in Stockholm
clips.summary: Cox recounts SNCC's early anti-war stand and the argument that civil rights people had no right to speak on Vietnam, and being sent to the Bertrand Russell War Crimes Tribunal, the London dinner with Sartre, Simone de Beauvoir, and the European intellectual heavyweights deferring to Sartre like a pope.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: The War of Genocide, and the Watch Lists
clips.summary: He describes pressing the Vietnamese Colonel Ha Van Lau until he agreed that a war against guerrillas, who offer no infrastructure to attack but the people themselves, becomes by definition a war of genocide, the cluster bombs, and the FBI and CIA lists that had him searched, detained at Heathrow, and treated as an enemy of the state.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: The Sixth Pan-African Congress
clips.summary: Cox explains how Atlantic City convinced him that economic power was fundamental and that Black communities had no economic infrastructure, leading with C.L.R. James to convene the Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania to link the diaspora's skills with Africa's land and resources and support the liberation movements.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Secretary-General at Thirty-Two
clips.summary: Named Secretary-General at thirty-two, Cox recounts the tension between state governments and insurgents that kept some Caribbean and Eritrean groups out, persuading heads of state like Sékou Touré, and the cover that C.L.R. James and Julius Nyerere's involvement gave him, though the gathering lacked the infrastructure to sustain its vision.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Drum and Spear
clips.summary: Settling in Washington, Cox describes founding the Drum and Spear bookstore and press with Charlie Cobb, Judy Richardson, and others to organize information on African and African American history, Palestine, and Vietnam, and the Center for Black Education, a great political venture if never a great business.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: The Night King Was Killed
clips.summary: He recounts hearing of Dr. King's assassination in a struggling Volkswagen, the disbelief, the DC rebellion that was hostile outward yet calm and polite within, Mayor Walter Washington's tanks on 14th Street as another lesson in what power protects, and the further shock of Robert Kennedy's killing.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: A Violent Country
clips.summary: Cox reflects on King's and Kennedy's deaths revealing a violent country, H. Rap Brown's line that violence is as American as cherry pie, and his own distance, physical and emotional, from the Chicago convention, which he saw as more about Vietnam and a government that would not listen.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: What Was Radical Was Who We Included
clips.summary: Cox closes with the onion he and Stokely discussed, the economic core beneath the political era he dates from 1955 to Obama in 2008, and his argument that nothing they did was radical except who they tried to include, and that the work now is to broaden the economy to all those at the bottom, Black, poor white, and Native American alike.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: A Cross Burning and the Mill Lawsuits
clips.summary: Fletcher Anderson recalls the Klan cross burning that got Bogalusa's attention and the rigidly segregated Crown Zellerbach paper mill where he hired on in 1962, where he and Robert Hicks filed lawsuits that opened jobs, supervisor posts, and employment for Black women that had never existed before.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Marching for Jobs and a Voice
clips.summary: He describes carrying the fight from the plant into a city that hired no Black workers and seated no Black officials, the many marches to the courthouse, to Franklinton, and to Baton Rouge, the federal injunction against police brutality, naming the first Black policemen, and the boycotts that finally opened employment.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Finding the Hicks Family
clips.summary: Cynthia Anderson recounts the cross burning drawing her in, and Fletcher coming home to say he wanted to meet the man he was about to die for, Robert Hicks, leading them to the Hicks home where, after she handed over her driver's license, the families became a team.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Fletcher's Bogalusa Roots
clips.summary: Fletcher traces his grandparents George and Maggie Anderson, who came from Mississippi for the paper mill, his grandfather a taxi driver and one of the first Black men to register and vote, his grandmother a boarding-house keeper, both college-educated property owners, and his own marriage and three children.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Cynthia, Raised a Hunter
clips.summary: Cynthia describes growing up in the middle quarters among the poorer Black families as outdoors people who hunted and fished, the basketball her mother let her play as long as she made the honor roll, and the hurt of learning how people felt about her that made her want to fight back at desegregation.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Working Up the Mill Line
clips.summary: Fletcher recounts climbing the mill's job line from loader to trucker to slitter operator to overhead crane operator, having to train himself on jobs white workers held, the dangerous crane work that injured him into retirement, and the strange tension of picketing the company he then had to clock in for.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Protecting the Community
clips.summary: Fletcher describes the Deacons protecting the Meredith March and their own neighborhoods with walkie-talkies, block captains, and the ability to block streets against drive-by shootings, since the police were there to protect the Klan, and how the model came from a charter meeting with the Jonesboro deacons.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Hunters, Not Aggressors
clips.summary: He explains that the men were lifelong hunters who knew weapons but had never shot at a person, raised by elders to stay out of trouble, and recounts being attacked leaving a cafe with three coworkers and then charged, absurdly, with the attempted murder of the restaurant's waitresses.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: A Wife Who Believed in Self-Defense
clips.summary: Cynthia recounts teaching in Covington, coming home to her husband's swollen head and a beaten car, climbing onto the car to wrench open the fire-station door to retrieve it, Mrs. Hicks calming her fury, and answering the warnings against the Deacons with her conviction that if you do not defend yourself, no one will.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: A.Z. Young, Bob Hicks, and Gayle Jenkins
clips.summary: Fletcher reflects on the fear in a Black community dependent on white employers, and the leaders who pressed on, A.Z. Young taking over the Voters League, Robert Hicks, and Gayle Jenkins, who came to the school board through appointment after Edwin Edwards was elected governor.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Appointed, Then Redistricted
clips.summary: He describes how the first Black officials held appointed seats until lawsuits forced redistricting that let the Black community elect Gayle Jenkins, R.T. Young, and the first city councilmen, and the districting trick that split neighbors on the same street into different districts to dilute their power.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: A Movement Where Women Had a Voice
clips.summary: Cynthia describes Bogalusa as a rare community where Black women had a voice and men turned to ask their wives' judgment, and remembers Gayle Jenkins as a fireball, and the women who desegregated the vocational college, kept enrolled four years without being allowed to graduate, and became nurses anyway.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Charles Sims and the Deacon Leadership
clips.summary: Fletcher recounts the leadership elected from that first meeting, Charles Sims as president, Robert Hicks as vice president, himself as lieutenant, Sims a fiery, intimidating man who could rouse a crowd, and the key members like Sam Barnes, Reese Perkins, and Willie Jane Preston.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: The Informant, and Arming Themselves
clips.summary: He reflects on the informant whose presence put the Deacons' names on the governor's desk within days, the difficulty of voicing such suspicions, and how they armed themselves with carbines, thirty-thirties, and handguns bought with donations from people like Louis Lomax, trained by veterans and by secret helpers too afraid to be named.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Determined Through the Harassment
clips.summary: Fletcher describes the summer of 1965 at its height, the endless marching while still holding a job, the police harassment on the highways with tickets that cost them their insurance, and a determination not to be stopped that won black state troopers and opened doors in the surrounding towns.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: A Klansman Shot in Self-Defense
clips.summary: He recounts the march when a Klansman attacked the marchers and had to be shot in self-defense, the attempted-murder charges thrown out by the lawyers Lolis Elie and Robert Collins, and Cynthia's own arrest, jailed for refusing to say sir to the trooper who pulled her over behind a school bus.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Protecting CORE and the Workers
clips.summary: Fletcher describes the Deacons protecting James Farmer and the CORE workers, Canadian Griffith assigned to escort them, and the violence that went unpunished, Griffith beaten on Columbia Street and a soldier home from the military, Captain Seymour, shot in a phone booth while the mayor never came.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: The March to Baton Rouge and the Klan Exposed
clips.summary: He recalls CORE and Dick Gregory's economic message, the march to Baton Rouge with its standoff in a Hammond park where they circled the cars like wagons and built a latrine truck, the Klan thick along the highway, and how, with the Klan exposed, he watched his own supervisor collect money for it at the plant.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: What Integration Did to Education
clips.summary: Cynthia argues that for all the surface change, Bogalusa let its public spaces die rather than integrate, that white flight to private schools followed, and that integration hurt education when teachers who would not correct Black children let them drift into special education.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Education Is the Key
clips.summary: The Andersons reflect that they would change the world to see a person as a person rather than a color, doubt racism will ever end, and hold that education is the key to ending it, since the less educated are where the Klan finds its recruits and an educated person will not drag another behind a car.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Reaching the Young
clips.summary: Asked about young people who have not taken up the ballot, they point to drugs they believe were planted in the community and to teen pregnancy and poverty that trap a generation, and to the successful who will not reach back, insisting that a life is not worth living if you have not made it better for someone else.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: The Deacons Took the Fear Away
clips.summary: They close on whether Bogalusa's movement could have happened without self-defense, and say it could not: the Deacons let people sleep knowing someone would protect them, took the fear out of the Klan by reminding them the same bullet kills anyone, and helped hold down the violence in a city that, even in 2011, was only just merging its one Black and one white YMCA.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Difference Between an Activist and a Radical
clips.summary: Opening the August 2013 interview with David Cline, Bailey draws the distinction that frames his autobiography: an activist works for change within accepted boundaries, while a radical bangs against the outer walls for far more than is institutionally conceived, fighting as if he will win even knowing he is lucky to reach the walls at all.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Why He Resists Labels
clips.summary: He accepts the radical label the California media pinned on the Berkeley council takeover but explains why he avoids labels and even hesitated to join CORE in Baton Rouge: a definable group makes you an easier target for those who want to discount what you are trying to do.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Movement Was Never One Leader
clips.summary: Asked how he defines the civil rights movement, Bailey rejects the King-centered frame, recalling the museum's long arc from slavery and his own first-hand experience of the movement's intensity: sitting with Fannie Lou Hamer on her Ruleville porch, the SNCC Freedom Singers behind the museum's music exhibit, and mentors like Will D. Campbell, Anne and Carl Braden, and Bob Zellner met at a 1961 Wisconsin seminar.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Emmett Till and Little Rock
clips.summary: As the first generation of his family to reach college, Bailey describes the movement happening accidentally in his freshman year, and traces his real awakening to seeing Emmett Till's body in Jet and watching the Little Rock students his own age, the reason the museum credits Brown v. Board as pivotal but treats Till's photograph as the spark that ignited Black America.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Riding the Front of the Bus
clips.summary: He recounts riding the front section of the interstate bus from Memphis to Baton Rouge as the only Black passenger up front, whites refusing the seats behind him, and traveling alone through Georgia where he could easily have ended up missing, sustained by a refusal to let fear overcome his dignity even while shaking in his boots.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Pullman Porter's Pass
clips.summary: Bailey describes parents who married in high school without finishing, the entrepreneur grandfather he was named for, his mother the respected housekeeper and receptionist at Dr. Seagerson's clinic, and his father who rose from mail handler to porter on the Illinois Central's New Orleans-to-Chicago line, the railroad pass letting the family travel free to ballgames in St. Louis and Chicago.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Raised to Be Independent
clips.summary: He recalls the latitude his mother gave him and his brother Walter, her own path from a GED to one of Memphis's first Black female barbers to a licensed practical nurse at St. Joseph's, parents who voted but did not organize, a cautious father, the segregated train station, and the calm both parents showed when he was expelled from Southern.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Crump Machine and the Black Vote
clips.summary: Bailey explains that Memphis Blacks could vote early because Boss Crump's machine, which controlled the city and much of Tennessee, drew strength from a Black vote delivered through lieutenants who could broker jobs and favors, with the cautious NAACP the only real civil rights organization as the machine declined in the mid-1950s.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Shelby County Democratic Club
clips.summary: He describes working the sound trucks as a teenager for the Shelby County Democratic Club, the strongest Black political organization of his youth, supporting white moderates and trailblazing Black candidates like J.E. Walker and Russell Sugarmon, whose near-win drove the white community to collapse its field to stop him, an early lesson in the inseparability of politics and race.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: No Youth Wing, and the Bootlegger Job
clips.summary: With no NAACP youth wing, Bailey and his brother sat among seasoned strategists rather than apart from them, and he recounts the summer the Democratic Club paid him through the sheriff's office to hunt bootleggers, delivering a half-pint of corn whiskey when he collected his check, the give-and-take that got things done.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Freshman Class President
clips.summary: Elected freshman class president at Southern in 1959 with no civil rights agenda, Bailey describes an early awakening to a massacre of Black activists in South Africa, his campus newspaper column, and bringing rhythm-and-blues acts like Gene Bowlegs Miller's band to Baton Rouge, a student leader enjoying Louisiana before the movement reached him.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Greensboro Reaches Southern
clips.summary: A month or two after Greensboro, Southern students sat in at downtown Baton Rouge lunch counters and were arrested, then expelled by their own administration; their case became Garner v. Louisiana, the 1961 Supreme Court decision that one cannot be convicted of breach of peace for peaceful conduct that merely provokes others to react.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Tear Gas and Cox v. Louisiana
clips.summary: Bailey recounts the December 1961 march into downtown Baton Rouge met with tear gas and dogs, standing at the head of the line with his brother beside Reverend B. Elton Cox, whose arrest produced Cox v. Louisiana; the divided Court reversed the conviction by reasoning the police had given permission, dancing around the courthouse-demonstration statute it would not strike down.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The National Student Association
clips.summary: At the 1960 National Student Association convention at the University of Minnesota, Bailey watched a national debate over endorsing the sit-ins and met Tom Hayden, Allard Lowenstein, and Barney Frank; he saw a movement of white and Black students alike, an NSA with a Washington lobbyist and a CIA-tainted international wing, against the backdrop of the HUAC fight and a film branding the activists communist.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Port Huron and the Boycott
clips.summary: He keeps an original copy of the 1962 Port Huron Statement that founded Students for a Democratic Society, whose people he knew, and describes how, after the Cox arrest in December 1961, protest broke out again at Southern and the university president closed the school and ordered students home for the holidays.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Rule 18 and a School Shut Down
clips.summary: Warned he would be expelled if he spoke, Bailey spoke and was severed under Rule 18, a catch-all titled lack of university adjustment also used against gay students, while President Felton Clark's campus assembly ordered every student off by five o'clock; three of the expelled took a taxi back to register and were driven off campus ahead of the state troopers, the protest crushed.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Nonviolence as Strategy
clips.summary: Bailey frames nonviolence as a strategic necessity rather than a belief: with three thousand marchers facing dogs and tear gas and nowhere to fight back, there was no other choice, and they stayed well-dressed and orderly even as they ran from the gas.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Dion Diamond and the SNCC Circle
clips.summary: With the CORE national office paying their hotel bill, Bailey describes SNCC organizer Dion Diamond jailed at Southern and field secretaries Chuck McDew and Bob Zellner arrested for criminal anarchy when they came to visit, an involvement that drew Bailey to SNCC staff meetings in Atlanta where he came to know John Lewis, Julian Bond, and director Jim Forman.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Northern Student Movement, and a Lawsuit
clips.summary: Wary of being pinned to any one group, Bailey formed his own Student Action Committee, then connected with Peter Countryman's Northern Student Movement at Yale and started a Worcester chapter; the CORE-backed lawsuit by the New Orleans firm Collins, Douglas and Elie to reverse his Southern expulsion settled by readmitting everyone except him and Ronnie Moore.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Scholarship to Clark
clips.summary: A referral from Walter Williams, a Jackson State student expelled like him, brought a scholarship to Clark University in Worcester, raised by students through car washes and bake sales; Bailey took the SAT at what is now Rhodes College and was pulled aside to lunch separately in 1962, the Clark effort noted in the Senate by Jacob Javits.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Different World in Worcester
clips.summary: Bailey describes the long railroad route north on his father's pass through upstate New York to Worcester, rooming with Fred Jealous, father of future NAACP head Ben Jealous, and the fifth of Jack Daniels two friendly students gave him for his twenty-first birthday in an unfamiliar white world.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Wyman-Gordon and the John Birch Owner
clips.summary: Tiring of being asked only about the South, Bailey turned the question on Worcester's own segregation and took on Wyman-Gordon, run by John Birch Society national secretary Robert Stoddard and employing eighteen Black workers, all janitors; with Abbie Hoffman attached as publicist through his newsletter The Drum, the group picketed and brought the plant to a stop.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Forcing a Corporation to Change
clips.summary: Bailey describes Stoddard's grip on the Worcester newspaper, WTAG radio, and the Clark board, and how a multifaceted strategy, the picket plus complaints to the Massachusetts Commission Against Discrimination and the Defense Department, forced the corporation to change, proving a small force with the right research can move a giant; a one-is-enough editorial drew a page of answering letters, and Clark later gave him an honorary doctorate.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Police and the Trucks
clips.summary: He recalls little fear in Worcester, student counter-picketers, and a police sergeant who disliked the protest but needed the driveway cleared for trucks; Bailey left the choice to the students and they relented at activist priest Father Bernard Gilgun's plea, the standoff as much a union issue as a civil rights one.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Refined Northern Opposition
clips.summary: Bailey contrasts the refined, quiet opposition of the Northeast with Southern threats, picketing the Worcester Area Council of Churches after it refused him a hearing and answering a local NAACP head's charge of recklessness by packing the chapter with students to take a position of strength from within.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bringing Malcolm X to Worcester
clips.summary: Bailey recounts calling Malcolm X at his New York home to invite him to Clark, board ministers demanding an opposing voice, and Malcolm offering to debate Dr. King but refusing the substitute Wyatt Tee Walker; he spent the day with Malcolm at a WTAG interview, agreeing with much of what he said while declining, as ever, nonviolence as a creed.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Deacons for Defense
clips.summary: Crossing the Lake Pontchartrain bridge to a Bogalusa protest in the summer of 1965, Bailey was met and escorted by the armed Deacons for Defense and Justice, the shotguns alongside the march reassuring rather than troubling him; he draws his own line between such self-defense and the proactive violence of groups like the Weathermen.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Title VII at Crown Zellerbach
clips.summary: Working that summer from Ernest Dutch Morial's New Orleans office, Bailey was one of the law students the NAACP Legal Defense Fund sent to file the first Title VII complaints after the provision took effect in 1965, getting Crown Zellerbach workers in Bogalusa to file some of the earliest and most effective complaints under the new law.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Law Students Civil Rights Research Council
clips.summary: Bailey describes his first law year at Boston University and the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council, funded by the Taconic Foundation, Rockefeller Brothers Fund, and others to send top law students South for thirty dollars a week to assist overwhelmed movement lawyers, its headquarters housed in the national ACLU office.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Stop at Yale That Changed Everything
clips.summary: Rejected by Yale, Columbia, and Harvard, Bailey stopped by chance at Yale on a drive back to Clark and met Dean of Admissions Jack Tate, a man from near Memphis who took to him; the waitlist Tate arranged became a transfer admission the next summer, an accident of stopping by that redirected his life.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: National Director, and the Draft
clips.summary: Elected national director of the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council from the ACLU's New York office, Bailey won a draft deferment from his Memphis board as work in the national interest; convinced the war was wrong, he believed he would rather have gone to jail than to Vietnam, his conviction sharpened by Howard Zinn and Norman Thomas at the teach-ins.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Night King Was Killed
clips.summary: Bailey recounts raising grant money to send ten law students to the Memphis sanitation strike, one of them, Alex Hurder, briefly the first suspect in King's assassination as a white man near the Lorraine Motel, and the night his secretary brought word from the radio that King had been shot, then a minute later that he was dead.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A New Life in San Francisco
clips.summary: With his Law Students Council year ending in 1968, Bailey turned down a Cummins Engine Foundation offer from Philip Sorensen for a San Francisco legal-services job arranged by Steve Antler, taking two months first to travel Europe on five dollars a day before beginning test-case practice.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Out on a Limb
clips.summary: Doing central-office consumer-fraud litigation, Bailey admits he is not built to work under others; he challenged the leadership over unfair treatment of staff, told the board the director should be fired, and was fired himself, then sued with a white colleague's help, taking from it the lesson that those who agree with you quietly will leave you out on the limb alone.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Money Makes Change
clips.topic: The Untold Story of Foundation Money
clips.summary: A guilt-ridden young friend with inherited wealth asked Bailey what to do with it, and Bailey answered, give me a million dollars; his conviction that change takes money frames the untold story of foundation money sustaining the movement, the foundations Congress later intimidated, and Leslie Dunbar of the Field Foundation backing Marian Wright Edelman and choosing John Lewis over Bailey for the Voter Education Project.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Foundations That Funded the Movement
clips.summary: Consulting for the Field Foundation under chair Ruth Field, Bailey got five thousand dollars from Dunbar after two students were killed at Southern University in 1972 to convene a Black people's community inquiry with John Lewis, Owusu Sadaukai, and W. Haywood Burns, and describes the consortium of foundations, Norman, Taconic, Rockefeller Brothers, New York, Cummins, Stern, that could pool money for movement work from Bailey's projects to Cesar Chavez's farm workers.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Bread Limited Trust
clips.summary: The friend countered with a million-dollar trust at Bank of America whose dividends Bailey could give away freely, with a salary and access to the corpus, mandated to fund the non-tax-exempt projects the foundations would not touch; he named it the Bread Limited Trust, the independent income that floated him after the legal-services firing as he moved into Berkeley politics.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Deciding to Run
clips.summary: Ron Dellums's election to Congress opened a Berkeley council vacancy, and over an evening at Ira Simmons's home the two decided to run; the white left's April Coalition had a deal to split a slate with the Berkeley Black Caucus, and both the caucus and Dellums pressed the newcomers not to run.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Black Community First
clips.summary: Refusing to drop his running mate, Bailey forced the caucus to accept them both and got on the slate with Loni Hancock, registering to vote only when he signed up as a candidate, having never attended a council meeting; he announced his first priority as the interest of the Black community, a pure Southern instinct carried to Northern California.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Radical Takeover
clips.summary: Bailey opened a separate campaign office in the Black community rather than running joint with the white candidates, establishing legitimacy with a suspicious base before winning two of three seats with Hancock; Julian Bond cut him a radio spot, a strong campaign manager steadied the effort, and the coalition held despite his insistence on his own agenda, the press dubbing it a radical takeover.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Panthers and the Recall Threat
clips.summary: Bailey met Huey Newton at his penthouse and supported Panther programs like free breakfast and daycare without joining or giving the twenty-five thousand dollars Newton asked for; the conservatives' plan to recall all three council members narrowed when Black councilman Wilmot Sweeney objected to sparing the white woman, then focused on Bailey alone.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Mike Wallace and 60 Minutes
clips.summary: Bailey describes filibustering council meetings until the Black mayor cut his microphone and he stood to keep talking, the twenty-five thousand dollars he listed as anonymous fueling a carpetbagger charge, and Mike Wallace turning from charming to hostile the moment the 60 Minutes camera rolled, demanding to know where his money came from while Bailey refused to say.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Spy and the Money Question
clips.summary: Warned by Wallace that a funded group meant to expose his backers, Bailey learned from Dunbar it was the Fund for Investigative Journalism on a Stern family grant; a self-revealed government spy who had infiltrated his campaign and later wrote The Glass House Tapes falsely claimed on Los Angeles radio that he had paid Bailey ten thousand dollars to infiltrate the Angela Davis defense, a charge KPFA declined to air.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: How a Recall Works
clips.summary: After an extortion plot that masqueraded as a contract on his life, Bailey explains the mechanics of recall, twenty-five percent of the last election's voters forcing a yes-or-no ballot, and the bind of needing a majority to survive when he had only ever won a plurality, with ACLU founder Roger Baldwin warning he could not beat it without revealing his money.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Money Runs Out
clips.summary: As the questions mounted, Bailey's friends closed the trust, leaving him a hundred thousand dollars they paid taxes on; a staff of thirteen, several on work-study, armed him with thick research books that drove change at the council, but most of what remained went to fighting the recall, the money running out regardless after two and a half years.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: What Lasted in Berkeley
clips.summary: Bailey points to the integrated city hall and the police and fire opportunities that outlasted him, and recounts breaking a garbage workers' strike by taking the workers aside to show them they were carrying the other municipal unions on their backs, then winning them an accelerated raise to bring them level with the rest.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: "We Shall Overcome"
clips.summary: After noting the gentrification that later shrank Berkeley's Black population, Bailey turns to the Civil Rights and Voting Rights Acts, recalling Lyndon Johnson putting the weight of the presidency behind them and saying we shall overcome after Selma and the deaths of Viola Liuzzo and James Reeb, even as Bailey knew from Memphis that the vote without money or power changes little.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Voting Is Only a Tool
clips.summary: Bailey insists he ran for office as opportunity, not belief in the system the media said radicals had joined, and that the riots in Watts, Detroit, and Newark were what truly forced change; strategies must shift before they are neutralized, and a political system designed to be controlled by money, with unions decimated and no labor voice to offset it, will not itself deliver liberation.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Judge and the Death-Penalty Lawyer
clips.summary: Nineteen years a trial judge and fourteen a lawyer who tried more than a dozen capital cases, Bailey describes the working truth that lawyers seek victory rather than justice and that the legal system orders disputes short of violence rather than delivers them; he recalls William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy at the 1991 museum opening urging activists toward the state courts as the federal courts closed.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Citizens United and Bush v. Gore
clips.summary: Bailey describes a federal judiciary grown unsympathetic, Citizens United handing corporations unlimited electoral money, Bush v. Gore as a judicial coup against the majority, and Justice Antonin Scalia telling the public to get over it, leaving activists to ask where they can now turn to fight.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Hegel's Dialectic
clips.summary: Watching an educational and material decline for a generation no longer sure of out-pacing its parents, Bailey reaches for Hegel's dialectic as his frame, thesis and antithesis and synthesis, oppression and rebellion and partial progress cycling on, the Civil Rights and Voting Rights Acts real landmarks but never the game-changers people hoped, Title VII never ending employment racism.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Pathology This Country Created
clips.summary: Bailey reads the explosion of Black imprisonment as a symptom of a country that destroyed minority self-worth across three centuries, impossible to erase in fifty years, and points to the psychological toll of everyday racism documented even in a Columbia University study of its effect on Black health.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Cycle of Prison
clips.summary: Bailey describes the pathology that sends people through prison and back unchanged, the senseless gang violence that gives the young a sense of bigness and worth, and a society that condemns the end result while denying its complicity; reversal, he argues, takes resources and a different attitude, and the claim of not seeing color is simply a lie.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Beyond Race: The Obama Election
clips.summary: Bailey calls the beyond-race talk around the Obama election nonsense, recalling that he voted early and spent election night on a Gulf cruise, glad to be spared the exhilaration ashore because he knew the same battles would be waiting the next morning, only now with people patting themselves on the back.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Return to Memphis
clips.summary: Bailey describes returning to Memphis in 1974 to practice law beside his brother, writing a weekly newspaper column for seven years to soften his California-radical image, running for mayor in 1983 as a platform for his ideas, and being elected judge in 1990, and back in 1982 running into Walter Bailey.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Walter Bailey and the Lorraine Motel
clips.summary: Bailey recounts Walter Bailey, the Lorraine Motel's owner who happened to share a name with his father, struggling to keep the doors open after his wife died days after the assassination; the motel had decayed to a seedy strip with prostitutes in its rooms, save the small hallowed shrine Bailey kept of King's last room, his dishes, and his wife's effects, open to the public for donations.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Foreclosure
clips.summary: Bailey incorporated the Lorraine Motel King Shrine Foundation that drew no support, watched Bailey carry his mortgage payment in a paper bag to the lender, and failed to assemble private buyers through a local contractor; the foreclosure and Bailey's bankruptcy stalled it until WDIA radio launched a campaign to save the motel and called Bailey in to help.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Auction Begins
