clips.summary: Zellner points to Bob Moses on the Atlantic City convention floor certain they would win, with no plan B if they did not, and argues SNCC's true legacy was teaching that a Black person could direct a white one and that changing the context changes behavior; for a few brief years, she says, its people were the only Americans to achieve a non-racial community, bound by the knowledge that any of them might take a bullet, until a profoundly racist country did them in.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Take Care of Each Other
clips.summary: Zellner sees in SNCC a still-living reservoir of creativity willing to support Black liberation, and says she now carries its lessons into her work speaking out as a Jewish person on Israel and Palestine, and into teaching the young to take care of each other and send people away on the rest Forman invented, support a movement today lacks against vicious, hateful opposition.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Brave as a Lion
clips.summary: Zellner remembers the murmur when Ruby Doris Smith Robinson came into the office with a reputation for being brave as a lion, how Ruby respected her as a hard worker without much liking her at first, and how Ruby, in effect second to Forman, did the personnel and financial work, an emotional woman scarred, Zellner believes, by the trauma of breaking rocks at the prison farm.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Then We Became Friends
clips.summary: When Ruby fell ill, Zellner, unafraid of the sick, visited her week after week at a New York hospital where the once-fierce woman grew gentle and the two became friends who laughed and traded stories; Ruby died of a virulent leukemia around twenty-six, and at the funeral that straightened the hair she had always worn natural, Zellner broke down sobbing, later naming her own daughter Katherine Ruby Zellner.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Men Were Petrified of Her
clips.summary: Zellner recalls Ruby demanding to know where the floaters were and the men being petrified of her, and her own terror going into Freedom Summer, vetting people for a place she dreaded and half-hoped an accident would spare her; in Mississippi she worked the WATS line in eight-hour shifts as a contact for parents and the press, and helped with the Belafonte and Poitier tour, leaving for a New Orleans that felt, by comparison, like Paris.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: They Wanted SNCC Out of the Way
clips.summary: Zellner closes on Forman's blunt, gameless manners, the day Kennedy was shot when he called her in a panic that the assassin might have been one of theirs, and the winter of 1964 to 1965 when she inadvertently learned that a coalition of more acceptable civil rights groups, through Allard Lowenstein and his aide Barney Frank, was planning to make sure the next summer would not be controlled by a SNCC they found too far out.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: A Bangor Grandmother
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier on the Georgia State campus for the project's inaugural year, Doris Derby opens with the family atmosphere that shaped her, the close gatherings of her mother's family of eleven children, and her maternal grandmother, born in Haiti and the Virgin Islands, who settled with a Virginia-born husband in Bangor, Maine.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Work Hard to Overcome
clips.summary: Derby learned only later that her grandmother and eldest uncle were charter members of a 1920s Bangor NAACP chapter, and recalls her grandfather's beloved store, her Boy Scout-leader paternal grandfather, and the values both sides instilled, hard work, determination, and achievement for self, family, and community within a climate of racism, that led her to the Southern struggle.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Two Churches Every Sunday
clips.summary: Derby describes growing up in the Williamsbridge section of the Bronx, an Episcopalian who also sang in the youth choir at her girlfriends' Baptist church down the block, in a varied neighborhood of Caribbean and Southern families, a Jewish delicatessen, an Italian liquor store, and Chinese and Black businesses.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Where Are Our Images
clips.summary: From an early age Derby searched for positive Black images she could not find beyond Amos and Andy or Tarzan, and was drawn to the continent her great-aunt Jesse, a missionary in Liberia, wrote home about, beginning a lifelong mission to learn about and document African and African American culture.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: The Schomburg and the Dunham Class
clips.summary: Raised in a musical, dance-studying family, Derby won an audition for a Katherine Dunham dance scholarship that took her weekly to Harlem, where she also stopped at the Schomburg Library's collection of African and Caribbean heritage, and, inspired by her father's slide photography of family gatherings, resolved to create the images she could not find.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: The Church Was the Hub
clips.summary: Derby recalls meeting Katherine Dunham, joining the NAACP youth chapter that met at her church, the community hub for scouts, Bible school, and fashion shows, so that mass meetings later felt familiar, and the semi-rural edge of the Bronx where her family raised chickens and ducks, kept a vegetable garden, and made juice and jelly from a grape arbor.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: He Left His Awards Behind
clips.summary: Derby describes a politicized household where the children sat in on the conversations, and the dinner-table stories of discrimination, her father the only African American in his University of Pennsylvania civil engineering class, who left his gymnastics and fencing awards uncollected rather than enter by the service elevator, and of riding the rails north for work.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: A Dentist Turned Skycap
clips.summary: She recounts an uncle trained as a dentist at Tufts who, unable to find a partner or office, became a skycap at the airport with his intelligence intact, and her aunts who finished high school and played instruments, the family weighing their disparities and their children's goals at every holiday gathering.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Recruited to Washington
clips.summary: Derby tells of her Aunt Julia, an executive secretary recruited as one of a handful of Black women to integrate the federal workforce in Washington, and how the roundtable family discussions, in a household that assumed college, shaped her own student conversations about Africa, the government, and where African Americans stood.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: A Navajo Reservation
clips.summary: Derby traces the traveling spirit she inherited, the church and Girl Scout trips of her youth and her interest in the Cherokee heritage her family claimed, to the summer she spent on a Navajo reservation in New Mexico as a budding cultural anthropologist who believed in living within a community to understand its life.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: A Summer in Africa
clips.summary: Derby recounts a friend's news that he was bound for Nigeria leading her to win an NAACP scholarship to Africa in the summer of 1960, before she ever went South, and her work with the African Scholarship Committee raising funds for the students brought over on the Kennedy airlift from newly independent countries.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: What Could We Do to Support Them
clips.summary: Derby describes the interracial Christian Human Relations Club at Hunter College, which sent a small group to talk with Southern student and community leaders about segregation, the sit-ins, and the freedom rides to learn how to support them, and the speakers it and the Northern Student Movement brought to campus, among them Ethel Kennedy and Malcolm X.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: My Eyes Were Being Opened
clips.summary: Derby reflects on how her widening view, Nigeria moving toward independence, stops in Paris and Rome, the economic inequality she saw among the Navajo and in Mexico, fit the struggles she had heard in her relatives' oral history, and how the American Society of African Culture drew her toward the cultural connections between Africa and African Americans.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: There Is Not a Void
clips.summary: Studying African dance, listening to African music, and bringing back African art to show at meetings, Derby made it her work to share the culture, later in Head Start programs and churches, against an American void she saw as purposely kept, and resolved to document the everyday and the artistic so others would have the visual images she had to search for.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Go to Atlanta First
clips.summary: Derby recounts how a 1962 plan to pursue a master's in Mexico became a bus trip South to visit a sick, jailed Hunter colleague in Albany, Georgia, and how, because she had helped start a New York SNCC office, she was told she must first go to Atlanta to meet up with the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: An Integrated Car to Albany
clips.summary: At the buzzing Atlanta SNCC office Derby met the key players, James Forman, Bob Zellner, Ruby Doris Smith, before riding in a then-dangerous integrated car with Forman and Zellner to Albany, where a family put her up and she was canvassing and reporting to the Albany Movement office by the next day, slipping at once into her lifelong community-helping mode.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: All Hands on Deck
clips.summary: Derby describes learning that her friend Peggy was jailed in Americus or Terrell County, the county people called Terrible Terrell, and a movement that needed all hands on deck as members were arrested with no help from the police; deploying her many skills, she let the master's plan go when Charles Sherrod asked her to return to New York to raise funds and goods for people thrown off their jobs.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Whatever We Need to Do
clips.summary: Reflecting on the Albany summer of excitement, mass meetings, and nonviolence training, Derby credits the self-sufficiency and adaptability of her upbringing, a family that grew its own food, built and painted and sewed, and a father who taught her cabinetmaking and recycling, for letting her simply do what was needed rather than dwell on the danger.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: A School Bus Full of Goods
clips.summary: Back teaching in New York, Derby raised money and goods for the movement, partnering with a white woman who used a wheelchair, Rae Brandstein, to fill a school bus with canned food, clothing, and books, while expanding the network of people who could support the New York SNCC office she helped staff in the evenings.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: New York Artists for SNCC
clips.summary: Derby describes helping organize the March on Washington through Bayard Rustin's committee and co-founding New York Artists for SNCC, drawing on friendships with the visual artists, writers, and actors she had known since the Harlem Writers Guild and the American Society of African Culture, to donate work and stage benefit concerts for SNCC.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Bob Moses Calls
clips.summary: Derby recounts inviting Bob Moses to speak at a successful New York fundraiser, after which he asked her to come to Mississippi to help build an adult literacy project tied to SNCC's voter registration, developing materials to meet the outrageous requirements placed on would-be voters; happy with her New York work and set on a master's, she at first said no.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: The Least I Can Do
clips.summary: Watching the news of the Birmingham violence that spring, the dogs and fire hoses turned on demonstrating children and adults, Derby resolved that the least she, a committed teacher, could do was join the literacy project, and called Bob Moses to say she would come to Mississippi after the March on Washington.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Full of Hope
clips.summary: Derby remembers the March on Washington for the people of all walks of life and backgrounds gathered without the violence so many had predicted, the SNCC and community people she knew, and the sense that the day, full of hope, would make a difference.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Ten Dollars a Week
clips.summary: Derby describes arriving in Jackson in the fall of 1963 as a SNCC field secretary paid ten dollars a week, housed with local families, working out of the buzzing COFO office on voter registration, demonstrations, and a mock governor's election, in an attitude that nothing was scripted in advance but it was time to do something, day by day, valuing the community people.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: The Cracks in the Dam
clips.summary: Unable to rely on the police or the FBI, Derby and her colleagues relied on themselves and the community, working to bring in the media and to insist the networks send Black crew members, noticing how white families' children drew more coverage, and looking always for the little cracks in a dam stacked against them, joined by some white Southerners who took real roles.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: A Circle of Trust
clips.summary: Derby explains that the divide between her Northern background and rural Black Mississippi was not so great for someone raised camping and handy in an extended-family way, and describes SNCC as a band of sisters and brothers in a circle of trust, humbled by local people whose bravery and deep spirituality, knowing full well what they risked, sustained the work.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: The Women Were Strong
clips.summary: Asked about her status as a woman, Derby says the question never faced her because she always did what she wanted, driven from within, and that with all hands on deck the women were strong, from organizers to the teenage girls who were willing to be arrested and jailed in rural counties with no facilities for them.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: He Showed Me the Gun
clips.summary: Derby recalls the constant danger of an integrated car with an out-of-state plate, and a gas-station moment in the early seventies when a man slid his jacket aside to show her a big gun, a single look that said everything; they left, because they had a goal and that was not the time, never knowing when such a situation would come.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: We Need to Start a Theatre
clips.summary: Derby describes how she, John O'Neal, and Gilbert Moses, all working on the literacy project and based at Tougaloo College, drew on her art and dance, their theater backgrounds, and her New York exposure to Black theater to conceive the Free Southern Theatre, with a sympathetic Tougaloo professor, Bill Hutchinson, drawn in.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Out to the Cotton Fields
clips.summary: Derby explains that they imagined the theater as the vehicle and housing for the other arts, music, dance, and visual art, and called it free because they meant to take plays about voting and the movement out to the rural areas, performing in cotton fields, churches, and grocery stores wherever people could gather, an effort that lasted nineteen years.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Purlie Victorious
clips.summary: Derby recalls the company's first production, Purlie Victorious, staged on campus in the spring of 1964, and the decision point as the year-long literacy project ran low on money: she went back to New York to try to renew the grant and continue the work with South Carolina migrants while the theater's base moved to Tulane under Richard Schechner, a move she disagreed with.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: A Study of Achievement
clips.summary: When the literacy grant did not come through, Derby joined a University of Michigan research project in the spring of 1965 studying Black students' achievement in predominantly white versus predominantly Black schools, conducted at Tougaloo, Talladega, and Tuskegee, before being asked to take up the Head Start work.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Teachers Afraid to Join
clips.summary: Derby describes the very different push of Head Start, where whites opposed it and Black teachers with regular jobs were afraid to be associated with it, so teachers had to be recruited and trained from among college-attenders, high school graduates, and capable mothers; as a resource teacher near Holly Springs she made toys from recycled tires and wood, drawing on her father's craft.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: We Made Our Own Books
clips.summary: Lacking materials, Derby had the children react to pictures and turned their words into homemade books, brought in local musicians, and as a troubleshooting trainer reached the child development center in Durant, a beautiful building the Black community had built itself, where she was lead teacher for a documentary about the program called Chance for a Change.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Cooperatives and Liberty House
clips.summary: Derby describes the Poor People's Corporation as an umbrella for economic development that promoted handcraft and farmer cooperatives across Mississippi, Louisiana, and Georgia, with Liberty House to market their goods; working through SNCC offices, she would go out to groups wanting to start a co-op and explain the minimum requirements of members, seed money, and a place.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: The Black Rag Dolls
clips.summary: Derby recounts recruiting Northern volunteer craftspeople and herself learning and teaching skills like candlemaking and leatherwork, and the black rag dolls of the Una sewing cooperative that became a big seller at a time such dolls were scarce; some of her work, including her SNCC blue-jean jumper dress, later traveled in the America I Am exhibit.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Train Us to Make Our Own
clips.summary: Derby explains that Southern Media, begun in 1967 by two filmmakers, Bill Peltz and Geer Morton, to train young Black people to document their own lives and movement, came to the Poor People's Corporation needing someone with a stable base, and that she, always the experimenter, took it on, helping crew and make films across the state.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: The Family Historian
clips.summary: Splitting her time with Liberty House marketing travel across the country, Derby brought her family-historian instinct for keeping records to the camera, accumulating photographs and later, through the Black women's photography collective Cistography, finding the help to exhibit them, mindful that other movement photographers' images had been lost.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: What the Photographs Show
clips.summary: Derby describes what stands out when she looks at her photographs, women in all walks of life, the faces of children, and Black men doing constructive things against the stereotypes, alongside the flourishing cooperatives and the Tufts partnership with Mound Bayou on healthcare reform; she insists on showing the arts because creativity, more than mind alone, always finds a way, and has started a campus theater and filmmakers group.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: A Black Womanist
clips.summary: Derby closes on the black womanist philosophy she takes from Alice Walker, a Black woman working alongside her family and community, across all ages, to bring her people forward rather than apart from the men, and traces it to the open dinner-table discussions of issues, never hidden from the children, that she now carries in her love of exposing the young from an early age.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: A Dollar a Day
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the University of Southern Mississippi in December 2015, Eddie Holloway, born in Hattiesburg in 1952, introduces his parents, his mother Beatrice, a beautician who loved to fish, and his father Willie, who delivered fertilizer by the barrel at the Meridian Fertilizer Company for a dollar a day plus a dollar bet that he could not last.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: That's My Word
clips.summary: Holloway describes his father's more than sixty years at the one company, rising to office custodian and then watchman, a man who would not sign a contract because his word was his bond and whom Holloway still calls the best person he ever met, remembered across Hattiesburg for that character.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: A Self-Contained Street
clips.summary: Holloway paints Mobile Street, where the family lived at 904 with his mother's Bea's Beauty Salon attached, as a self-contained Black community of restaurants, salons and barbershops, a physician, a dentist, a pharmacist, churches, cleaners, and motels, where the professionals of Black Hattiesburg lived and where nearly every need was met.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: A Thirty-Mile Catchment
clips.summary: He recalls sitting Saturdays with Miss Thelma Williams at the cleaners, and a Mobile Street so busy on Saturdays, drawing people from a thirty-mile radius around towns like McLain and Wiggins, that you had to step into the street to pass, in an era when nearby Camp Shelby was largely a summer-training facility rather than the wartime base of a hundred thousand men.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Schools Built to Stall Integration
clips.summary: Holloway traces his schooling from the Maddie Robinson kindergarten through W.H. Jones Elementary, which opened in 1958, to Rowan High, and recalls overhearing educators, including the Mississippi Teachers Association leader across the street, explain that schools like Jones were built across the state to stall full integration, with separate associations and meeting days for Black and white teachers.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: The Same Faces in Different Places
clips.summary: After his mother died when he was eleven, Holloway grew even closer to the teachers who were also members of his church and his Sunday school superintendents, the same faces in church and classroom who knew how he was doing in both, an anchored community that filled whatever educational voids existed at home.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: I Wanted to Be Informed
clips.summary: Holloway describes a hunger less for schooling than to be informed, knowing there were important things he did not know, and watching the way the teachers and principals dressed, walked, and carried themselves, deciding the path to that life was to become like them.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: The Cardboard Table
clips.summary: Holloway recalls his father, schooled only in what he called the Little Red Building and calling a zero an ought, setting up a cardboard table on the porch each day so his son did his lessons before going out, a struggle that made the boy think him old-fashioned, yet never lessened the love he still feels for the best person he knew.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: He's on a Bicycle
clips.summary: Holloway treasures his father's quiet wisdom, recounting how, when a man on a bicycle mocked his new Chevrolet as a poor man's car, his father gently shamed him by noting the critic was on a bicycle, the kind of lesson in silence, never a gruff or profane word, that he still draws on in any hard moment.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: They Lived and Breathed Civil Rights
clips.summary: Holloway describes how, after his mother's death, an aunt from Alabama took him to live with her family near Birmingham, wise but unschooled people who lived and breathed civil rights, loved Martin Luther King, and instilled in him a heaping dose of pride, taking him to the steel mills on payday to see the workers and the waiting mothers.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Iron the Paper Bags
clips.summary: Holloway recounts the relatives' charge that he uphold their standards, save, work, and never beg, and their extreme meticulousness, multiple baths a day, ironed handkerchiefs and even ironed paper lunch bags so no one would think a bag reused, a strictness that wore on the boy but whose intentions he never lost sight of.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Red Socks
clips.summary: Holloway smiles over the bright-colored clothes the relatives mailed him from catalogs, the red and yellow socks he refused to wear, and the steady stream of letters, packages, and calls of love that assured him he had everlasting kin; fiery about civil rights, they gave money and bonded people out of jail though they did not march.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: The Sixteenth Street Church
clips.summary: Holloway remembers being taken to the Sixteenth Street Baptist Church and told of its bombing, and to Willie Mays's Say Hey restaurant and the Black Barons' stadium, too young then to fully grasp it but absorbing the relatives' daily charge to ask the church for wisdom and knowledge because he would not have them once he returned to Hattiesburg.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Never Let Anyone Talk Against Me
clips.summary: Holloway senses, from his father's repeated plea never to let anyone turn him against him, that the Alabama relatives may have wished to take him, a thing he could never confirm and prayed his father would never have to carry, even as he honors a father who treated him, he says, like the best person ever made.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Get Your Lesson
clips.summary: Holloway describes believing, because the teachers, his Alabama kin, his father, and the porch people who called out as he passed all told him so, that education was the path to economic and personal freedom, and recalls an elder telling him there was a reason people were killed trying to get an education: something in it would set him free.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Equal Footing, Not Integration
clips.summary: Holloway came to understand that Mississippi funded new Black schools as a bargaining point to forestall desegregation, and that the old stalwarts of Black education sought not integration but equal footing, equal attendance money, new books, and lab equipment to match the white schools, recalling a lone new geometry textbook that finally arrived in late 1968.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: A Visionary Before His Time
clips.summary: Holloway calls the educator N.R. Burger a visionary who deliberately did graduate work at Cornell to stand on par with the white superintendent and built a separate but stellar Hattiesburg school system, in one of the few places in the state offering a twelfth grade for Black students, drawing families from fifty miles to feed graduates on to Alcorn State.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: I'm Paving a Way for You
clips.summary: Holloway recounts that his father never voted and could not be moved to, valuing his relationships with whites of power and saying he was paving a way for his son, a way Holloway felt downtown, where his father's accounts and standing spared him the layers of screening other Black customers faced at the stores and pharmacies.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Count Them One by One
clips.summary: Holloway describes picking up snippets in his mother's beauty shop, where civil rights workers courted beauticians, and learning of voting only in the ninth or tenth grade from teachers like N.R. Burger; the registrar Theron Lynd was talked of all over the city, the subject of the book Count Them One by One, after a group of more than fifteen had tried and failed to register.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: The Air-Conditioned Room
clips.summary: Holloway accepted that Black people could not vote and that facilities were separate, but never that it was permanent, and locates an early consciousness of race at a student-council meeting at the university where he was the only African American, in a cool air-conditioned room he vowed one day to have, drawing on a lifelong gift for reframing any situation toward possibility.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: My Mother's Whistle
clips.summary: Holloway names the marches and the nearly nightly mass meetings, moving from True Light to St. Paul to Mount Zion to Palmer's Crossing, as the first civil rights activity he remembers, attending more freely only after his mother, whose whistle from the back porch had once carried three blocks to call him home, had died, and when relatives visited from Muskegon.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: They Came to Set Us Free
clips.summary: Holloway describes his first integrated meetings as reflecting hope and freedom, likening conditioned acceptance to a chained elephant that comes to be held by a string, and electrified that the freedom riders had come to set them free; his father, for all his hesitancy, never set limits on him, and once asked the white office manager whether his son should transfer to Hattiesburg High.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: The Right Decision for Eddie
clips.summary: Told to stay where he was, Holloway came to see Rowan High as pride itself, the place that gave him the teach-a-man-to-fish wisdom he would not have gotten at Hattiesburg High, where many who transferred under freedom of choice returned or struggled, and which, for whatever reason, those transfers still attend his Rowan reunions.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: I Never Experienced That
clips.summary: Holloway reflects that parents who sent children to the white school thought the grass greener and the resources better, even amid harassment he heard of but never himself experienced, and notes he became the first student of color as a resident assistant and officer assistant and the first to staff the upscale Elam Arms residence.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: To Be Near My Dad
clips.summary: Holloway recalls washing dishes on the campus as a high schooler and, though his mentors wanted him at Jackson State or Alcorn, choosing the University of Southern Mississippi in 1970 to stay near his widowed, remarried father; independent, he took a bus to apply and sit the ACT, and helped move in some of the first Black women students.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: An All-White Floor
clips.summary: Holloway describes being moved to an all-white floor as a test of whether he could relate to white students before being made, without pay that first year, a resident assistant, the men there becoming his best friends and the next year bringing the offer to be head resident of the whole hall, a stepping stone he knew his day would come.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Go Get Me a Couple of Books
clips.summary: Holloway recalls the Freedom Library, called the Black Folks Library, and the Freedom School efforts at True Light Baptist Church, which he used to borrow books and attend meetings without fully joining, because his Alabama kin had instilled a pride that he buy his own belongings and not appear to be among the impoverished children driven there for food.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: When I Sensed Danger I Would Leave
clips.summary: Holloway describes marching but leaving when he sensed danger, as on a night uptown when he saw Klan-like men with guns and others with broken bottles, calling himself not an outlier but a lifelong independent thinker, who canvassed door to door asking people whether they had registered to vote.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: I'd Like the Front Door
clips.summary: Holloway recounts challenging the back-door treatment at the office of the physician Dr. A.T. Tatum by calling the federal Office of Civil Rights, which led Tatum, who had held his dying grandmother and known his father, to bar him; he became a quiet civil rights informant for twenty-five years, troubled that his own father and teachers still went to Tatum, and once told the doctor he shared a class and the same door with his daughter Nancy.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: You Accommodated Me
clips.summary: Holloway recalls keeping master keys at the residence-hall desk and sleeping in different rooms in fear, and a scholarship interview in Jackson where, asked his opinion of Senator James Eastland, he praised the senator's service, and the interviewer denied him the scholarship for accommodating rather than telling the truth about Eastland's farm subsidies, a lesson that taught him to frame and reframe and still say his piece.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: A Delicate Walk
clips.summary: Holloway describes N.R. Burger's delicate walk, required to stay on the inside while conscious of what mattered outside, and the unresolved debate over whether Burger had a role in the effort around Clyde Kennard, including a theory he has chased that a junior college was financed to keep Kennard and integration out, leaving a stellar separate system as Burger's accomplishment.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: One Vote From Closing
clips.summary: Holloway relays what the registrar Aubrey Lucas told him over thirty years, that Burger spoke directly with legislators and the wealthy, and that USM president William D. McCain held Mississippi higher education should be desegregated but starting at Ole Miss, since Southern stood one vote from being closed, so a disturbance over Kennard could have been the excuse to shut it down.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Mrs. Jean and the NAACP
clips.summary: Holloway remembers Clyde Kennard talked of in the beauty parlor as the man who tried to attend Southern, when the only Black presence on campus he knew was a peanut seller, and credits Mobile Street figures like Mrs. Jean and the AME church's NAACP lead, and his teachers Daisy Harris Conner and Clarence McGee, while gently noting that some elders' devotion to Alcorn above all could limit growth.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Mississippi's Fourth HBCU
clips.summary: Holloway shares the inside joke that USM is Mississippi's fourth historically Black university, not for open admission but for the work it does to ensure access and to serve students where they are, drawing first-generation students from a roughly hundred-twenty-mile catchment rather than the out-of-state recruits other schools chase.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: You Got Them Here
clips.summary: Holloway recounts a career of building services so students could get from here to there, an office of inclusion, civility and at-risk training, and round-the-clock student support, with a detour running a high-risk students program at Cumberland and four addiction-treatment clinics before returning in 1979; the African American share at USM now approaches forty percent.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Cutting the Sharp Edges
clips.summary: Holloway credits a different commitment at Southern, made five years after its first two Black students, to people who cut the sharp edges off, N.R. Burger, William Carey's president J. Ralph Noonkester, who integrated the first private college in the state, and the murder of NAACP leader Vernon Dahmer, whose firebombing he sees as a wake-up call that made even the white community feel things had gone too far.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Build a Bridge
clips.summary: Holloway describes serving on the Mississippi Civil Rights Commission that builds civil rights into the school curriculum with the William Winter Institute and Susan Glisson, and on the coalition against sexual assault, and bringing home the Leadership Mississippi model to found Leadership Hattiesburg, working always to build bridges that may bear someone else across tomorrow.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Exit With the Dream
clips.summary: Holloway explains his hope, having seen the early plans and the hired architects for the Mississippi history and civil rights museums, that visitors exit with the dream rather than the agony, so marked by the future and not the negatives that they leave inspired, the challenge he set himself in how the story is told.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: A Voice of Freedom
clips.summary: Holloway calls the civil rights movement a breathtaking chance to see tomorrow from where they stood, the local strength linked to outside voices of freedom that, coming from a wealth of places, let the work move faster, and that forced Mississippi and its neighbors to see how far behind they were in equality and the educational and economic advancement the least of their people needed.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: The Spirit
clips.summary: Holloway describes the spirit, the unknown enforcers who would visit at night anyone who broke a boycott line, and frames it as economic steering, recalling ordering hair clippers by mail from National Bellas Hess at eleven because he could not buy them in person, and arguing that wealth is a motivator that, like the University of Missouri football team's refusal to play, can become a powerful demotivator.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Work From the Inside
clips.summary: Holloway distinguishes the spirit from the Deacons of Defense, who guarded dignitaries like King, C.T. Vivian, and Shuttlesworth, and offers his own creed: that those who worked the inside of the movement, less heralded than those on the television screens, often negotiated and accomplished far more, and that he has tried to work from the inside, building bridges and open lines rather than being a silent observer.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: They Got Them a Piece of Land
clips.summary: Holloway recalls the Ladner sisters of Palmer's Crossing and Kelly Settlement, landowning Black communities whose strength was that they owned their property, and their movement work out of Jackson State and Tougaloo, where the white Freedom Rider Joan Trumpauer Mulholland also studied, and how Hattiesburg's teachers linked students to those who had succeeded from right there.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Three Animals
clips.summary: Holloway says he was sensitive to race but never a slave to it or focused on it, seeing instead what he might share with others, and illustrates with a child who named three animals as the boogeyman, Godzilla, and a creature from the one movie at the local theater, a story he uses to show that understanding a person's world, not judging the answer, is the work.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Ask the Right Questions
clips.summary: Holloway closes with a student athlete disciplined for outbursts after his grandmother, who had sent him to Southern over loftier programs, fell ill, arguing that had the authorities asked the right questions they would have understood; his life's work, he says, has been to give people the insight to ask those questions, so that USM has earned a reputation for opening its doors both ways to students from extreme circumstances.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Here Is Son
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at his home in Pelham, Massachusetts, in August 2013, Ekwueme Michael Thelwell, a self-described humble servant of the truth, opens with his legendary father, a six-foot-four Jamaican who in 1930s and 1940s America was a footballer, motorcycle racer, and trick rider, known in Harlem as Son.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: An Imperious Mother
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts his father returning to Jamaica, winning election, and dying at forty-four amid rumors of political conspiracy, and the imperious mother who, left with tangled litigation, raised three children and once flew to the University of Michigan with a folder of clippings to secure her athlete son's scholarship herself.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: He Told Them to Kiss His Behind
clips.summary: He tells how his brother, advised to major in physical education, instead gave back the scholarship, worked his way through Michigan, and earned three engineering degrees, and how that brother, born three and a half pounds and fed by eyedropper, grew to six-foot-five and built Jamaica's department of natural resources before dying prematurely.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Dread of the South
clips.summary: Thelwell describes the deep dread of the South he had absorbed from John Oliver Killens's Youngblood, Frank Yerby's romances, and Faulkner, the petrifying first sight of Spanish moss in Louisiana, and his arrival at Howard in September 1959, which, he jokes, touched off the Southern student movement that spring.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The Best Decision for the Wrong Reasons
clips.summary: He gives his official answer for choosing Howard, immersion in Black culture, and his real one, an elegant economist's tip that the campus had nine women to every man, and calls it the best decision anyone ever made for the wrong reasons.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: An Organization That Did Not Exist
clips.summary: Thelwell explains that the Nonviolent Action Group was never a recognized student organization, and only long afterward grasped that President Mordecai Johnson was protecting them, able to tell the Dixiecrats who controlled Howard's budget that no such group existed, while the annual fight for recognition gave the students a ready-made issue to organize around.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Moral Obligation
clips.summary: He recounts how NAG, sensing a moral obligation to support the South from the nation's capital, ran sit-ins in segregated Maryland and Virginia and picketed the White House, drew on socially focused professors and contemporary political readings, and on deep-South students like Cleveland Sellers, sent to Howard for safety only to plunge straight into the movement.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: He Educated and Terrified Me
clips.summary: Thelwell remembers Hank Thomas, who gave up a football scholarship for the movement, and Lavaughn of Birmingham, a veteran in the next room who first told the freshman just off the plane about the rural South, the Tuskegee professors who drove in chauffeurs' caps to avoid the patrol, and a police chief named Bull Connor, a name he would understand in 1963.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The Stick of Alabama
clips.summary: He describes the cafeteria sticker shock for Caribbean students, his own poverty on thirty dollars a month from his mother, cutting hair in the dorm, and Lavaughn buying him a steak with the out-of-state tuition stipend Alabama paid Black students to study elsewhere, gifts from what he wryly called the stick of Alabama.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Hired Thinker
clips.summary: Thelwell honors the professors segregation kept at Howard, Eugene Clay Holmes, who told HUAC he was a hired thinker, Sterling Brown, E. Franklin Frazier, whose reading and discussion programs instilled social responsibility, and the NAG cohort they shaped, Courtland Cox, Charlie Cobb, Stokely Carmichael, Muriel Tillinghast, and the brilliant student-government head Tim Jenkins.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Lion Met a Lion
clips.summary: He evokes the heady international ambience of Howard in the capital, the African and Caribbean embassies the students could visit, a school that had trained much of the Black diaspora's professional class, and visiting figures like the brother of Tom Mboya and Kenneth Kaunda of Zambia, introduced with the line that a lion met a lion in the forest and the lion blinked.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Forty People
clips.summary: Thelwell recalls busing a hundred Howard students to White House and CORE pickets and the demonstration over the new men's gymnasium, and his surprise on researching Carmichael's book to learn that NAG's membership across four years never exceeded forty people, who out of a student body of eight thousand shaped history in no small way.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: An Area of Jurisdiction
clips.summary: Thelwell describes the women of NAG as faithful, committed, and hard-working, never shy in policy debates or on the picket line but never pushed into formal public leadership, in a division he likens to African spheres of authority, and the extraordinary price they paid that he recognized only in retrospect.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: She Would Not Straighten Her Hair
clips.summary: He recounts Mary Lovelace, who wore her hair natural and was nearly thrown out of the dorm until Dean Patricia Roberts Harris interceded against housemothers who thought a natural a disgrace, and Bill Mahoney's NAG essay that at Howard one was neither Black nor white but merely object, the administration struggling publicly while never expelling them.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Two Different Prices
clips.summary: Thelwell contrasts the price the men paid, mostly in upwardly mobile careers, a resume-building contemporary who went to Harvard Law against Bill Mahoney dropping out to organize the South, with the subtler and wider price the women paid, a struggle against the very concept of what a Black woman should be.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: He Walked Me Through It
clips.summary: He relays Muriel Tillinghast's account of Stokely Carmichael intervening to win her a place on an NAACP trip the male officers had taken for themselves, and of being walked through her first action step by step, the way a generation with no radical training acquired political skills very fast.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Strange-Looking White Boy
clips.summary: Thelwell remembers Tom Kahn, a white socialist activist, gay, and a roommate of Bayard Rustin, whom he first met over a conversation about Faulkner in the dining hall, and his impression that Kahn had come to Howard on a deliberate socialist mission to guide the movement toward Rustin's politics of nonviolence and labor alliance.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Your Dress Has to Be Nonviolent
clips.summary: He tells of returning from a factory job in a mature, posturing denim outfit and Tom Kahn warning that his very dress would invite violence at a demonstration, and of the pale blonde Cynthia Jones who kept walking beside him on Virginia picket lines, to his terror, while children jeered that they would marry and have polka-dot kids.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: You Could Stage a Debate
clips.summary: Thelwell explains that because the administration could not give controversial figures a platform before its Dixiecrat budget committee, NAG's Project Awareness presented them in debates with an opposing voice, so the chief Communist Party theoretician Herbert Aptheker debated a socialist, and Malcolm X had to be set against Bayard Rustin.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Before You Were an American
clips.summary: He recalls Rustin speaking briefly for integration and labor alliance before yielding his time, and Malcolm launching into a nationalist argument that before they were Americans or Republicans they were Black, to an audience of young Black people from across the diaspora, including Caribbean and African students who supported the movement but could not demonstrate for fear of deportation.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The Tried Red
clips.summary: As the student editor sent to interview Malcolm, Thelwell waited at the girls' dorm with Courtland Cox, embarrassed when white co-eds from the demonstrations ran up calling out that Malcolm was here, and indignantly declared that no minister was going to tell him whom to associate with, mispronouncing the name, until he turned to find Malcolm X himself standing behind them.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Kittens in an Oven
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts lobbing softball questions and Malcolm's answers in parable, that a cat having kittens in an oven does not make them muffins, that he would not fight for the wolves against the bears, an African manner of speaking in fables like Aesop, whom Malcolm knew to have been African, and a later demonstration carrying a coffin to Robert Kennedy's office where Malcolm watched from a lamppost.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: As the Honorable Elijah Muhammad Teaches Us
clips.summary: Thelwell describes how NAG liked Malcolm's militance and sheer dominating presence, so unlike King's, but perceived him trapped by the Nation of Islam's ideology, distancing himself by prefacing its strange racial theology with the phrase that the Honorable Elijah Muhammad teaches us, and looking wistful by the roadside as their integrated demonstration marched past.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The White Lady, She Had a Job
clips.summary: He recalls inviting Malcolm to Friends World College after he left the Nation, Malcolm slipping into a Project Awareness event to hear James Baldwin, whom he respected, and a drunken older hustler on a Washington street corner who agreed with everything Malcolm said about the white man being the devil but would not give up his pork chops and insisted, to Malcolm's laughter, that the white woman had a job.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Interpose Your Body
clips.summary: Thelwell explains that the men of NAG, mostly athletes raised on codes of honor, saw nonviolence as a counterintuitive strategy rather than a creed, and how the sticking point of what to do if a sister were attacked was resolved by the pacifist Tom Kahn's answer, that one should nonviolently interpose one's own body and take the blows.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Nonviolence Is Theatre
clips.summary: He frames nonviolence as theatre and psychological warfare that controls the discourse and the image where there are witnesses and cameras, as in the demonstrations before the White House and Capitol, but argues there is no technique of nonviolence that protects against a sniper on a dark country road or a bomb thrown at a sleeping house.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The Master Race
clips.summary: Thelwell recalls the American Nazi Party under George Lincoln Rockwell appearing at demonstrations in swastikas, the police forcing them their own picket line, and his irritation that these out-of-shape representatives of the master race taunted fit, clean-cut students, fueling a naive machismo wish to meet them on the walk back to campus.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Whether You Take a Weapon
clips.summary: He recounts the long SNCC discussion sparked when a host farmer in Mississippi, defending his home against a feared Klan raid, offered Charlie Cobb and Mendy Samstein a weapon, and the resolution that struck him as eminently wise: the organization is nonviolent, but whether an individual takes up a weapon to help defend the home that shelters him is a personal decision SNCC has no business policing.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: If You're Free, Act Free
clips.summary: Thelwell tells of his first drive south with NAG, the feast at Cleveland Sellers's family home, and how, taunting the others to stop at the roadside restaurants they passed, he was called to make good on it, walking alone in the dead of night into a bright restaurant of sunburned Southerners and sitting petrified at a booth, served by no one, until he was grateful to flee back to the car.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: My Grandmother's Beds
clips.summary: He describes reaching the Black community of Atlanta and an immense rush of nostalgia at the little houses with raised flower beds edged in shells, the very flowers and arrangements of his grandmother's home in the hills of Westmoreland, Jamaica, a memory unvisited for decades that came rushing back.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: They Needed a Gestetner
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts James Forman flying him to Atlanta to turn a taped history of SNCC into a speech, the moving narrative of building the organization from nothing, the early project that needed only a duplicating machine no one could fund, and the poverty of a national office with one telephone paid for by Connie Curry and one room given by SCLC.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: May the Circle Be Unbroken
clips.summary: He describes shaping Forman's speech, which Forman wanted to counter Frank Smith's bid for the chairmanship, around the image of an unbroken circle, and quietly priming the audience the night before by going table to table among the singing Southern staff to ask them to sing May the Circle Be Unbroken.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Band by, Band by
clips.summary: Thelwell recalls the UAW-discounted cars the staff loved drawn into the speech, Forman delivering it and turning from the microphone as King would, and the hall spontaneously rising in song, so moving that Thelwell, who had engineered it, had to run from the room in tears.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: You Done Just Treated Me
clips.summary: He tells how, fleeing the room in tears, he found the insurgent Frank Smith outside crying too, conceding he had been treated, and recalls another fracturing meeting saved when Jean Wheeler led the haunting Freedom Is a Constant Sorrow.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Presence in Washington
clips.summary: Graduated and at loose ends, Thelwell took the job NAG suggested as SNCC's Washington representative, the polite word for lobbyist, joined by Bill Mahoney, who arrived with a wooden box of philosophy books and a cardboard box holding his entire wardrobe, to run a conference teaching field and local people how to access federal programs.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Mrs. Hamer Began to Sing
clips.summary: He remembers the federal-programs conference flagging until Bob called up Fannie Lou Hamer, who told how she went to register and then began to sing, the whole room joining in, Thelwell's first real encounter with the Southern spirit of the movement and the best of Southern Black culture, an occasion he never forgot.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Something to Trade
clips.summary: Thelwell describes learning Black power in concrete terms while begging resources for the conference: that the movement could no longer be a supplicant but must bring something to trade, and Forman's lesson when the persona-non-grata Teamsters gave a thousand dollars, that you cannot sleep with someone at night and refuse to walk down the street with them in the morning, so the Teamsters topped the list of sponsors.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: To Break the Logjam
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts Bob Moses coming to Washington in the spring of 1964 with the decision, reached after the murder of Louis Allen, to bring Northern volunteers into Mississippi to break a movement stalemate, on the grim logic that the state suppressed the vote by killing the local leaders who could vote while sparing the outsiders who could not.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Joe Said We Could Be Seated
clips.summary: He describes taking Bob Moses to progressive Washington circles where Moses unveiled the plan for a Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party to challenge the regular delegation in Atlantic City, met with admiring doubt until Moses revealed that the powerful Democratic insider Joseph Rauh thought they could be seated, at which the room turned and commitments of support began.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The High School Friends of SNCC
clips.summary: Thelwell recalls the office's work raising food and clothes for people thrown off the land, the suburban High School Friends of SNCC who collected and packed it, and the Hazard, Kentucky miners brought by a shared-office organizer, whose Elizabethan speech of high sheriffs and hollers he loved.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The Best Mess in Two Years
clips.summary: He tells of taking the Hazard miners to a Washington Black restaurant where one wept over a Salisbury-steak dinner he called the best mess he had had in two years, and of loading their van with as much canned food as could be spared for Kentucky, a SNCC project there that never quite took hold.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: My People and My People Struggle
clips.summary: Thelwell describes a schoolteacher who came in and organized the rascally High School Friends, Ralph Featherstone, who soon decided to leave his marriage and teaching job for Mississippi, accepting Thelwell's warning that those who went down might not come back, and the well-dressed Jewish mothers who took over the office after Schwerner, Goodman, and Chaney vanished, getting through to congressmen on the phone.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Make You a Get Killed
clips.summary: He recounts a mother kissing her son and sending him off to Mississippi with pride, his own mother threatening from Jamaica to set the FBI on him if he went, and how, when he finally reached a Mississippi meeting wanting to be a field organizer, Forman and the lawyers Kunstler and Kinoy turned him right around to go start the MFDP office in Washington.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Run Like Hell for the Door
clips.summary: Thelwell tells of Featherstone bursting in proud as assistant director of the Freedom Schools, of meeting his best friend, a red-haired white Yale philosophy student who loved Mississippi, and of a celebratory dinner where, unable to cover a forty-five-dollar bill, the three-hundred-pound Lawrence Guyot solemnly proposed they leave the money and run before producing a churchman's wad of two-dollar bills, with Jan Goodman the seasoned political hand among them.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: They Talked About the Victims
clips.summary: Thelwell describes the propaganda that swept the Washington beltway once the summer project was known, that it was quixotic, cynical, and really a scheme to get young white Americans killed, a perverse framing that named the potential victims rather than the terrorists who would do the killing, pushed even by syndicated columnists like the Alsop brothers.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Disease, Castro, and Tear Gas
clips.summary: He recounts the austere columnist Joseph Alsop claiming proof that SNCC meant to create martyrs, and the parallel campaign inside Mississippi casting the volunteers as disease-ridden, Castro-financed invaders, with special legislation passed, tanks and tear gas bought, and the Jackson papers' incendiary coverage stoking an atmosphere in which a young white Mississippian might think it his patriotic duty to stop them.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Get the Right Witnesses
clips.summary: Thelwell explains his strategy of countering the suppressed story by getting liberal congressmen like William Fitts Ryan and Robert Kastenmeier to hold hearings the media could not ignore, and describes laying out for Kastenmeier, point by point, the gathering evidence of a coming bloodbath until the congressman's head sank to his desk.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: If I Live, I'm Already Registered
clips.summary: With Congress refusing official hearings, Thelwell rented the National Theatre and staged one with Mississippi witnesses, among them E.W. Steptoe, who, asked by a looming sheriff to state his business, answered that if he lived he was already registered to vote, and Hartman Turnbow; Norman Mailer graciously declined as a liability, and Thelwell's own Jamaican accent kept him from the convention floor.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: I Would So Like to Handle You
clips.summary: Sidelined from Atlantic City, Thelwell tells of a New York laundromat encounter with a character actress of many accents, and of literary patrons who took him up, an agent who had represented Richard Wright offering to handle his award-winning short fiction.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: If You Were Less Full of Hatred
clips.summary: He recounts the agent showing him a paternalistic letter Faulkner had written Richard Wright, that he might one day become an artist if only he were less full of hatred, a message of admission to the high table of letters on condition of surrender, and the patrons' plan to set the Jamaican newcomer up as a Black American novelist in a borrowed Italian apartment.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: James Baldwin Is Finished
clips.summary: Thelwell tells how the agent dismissed Baldwin's Blues for Mister Charlie and declared Baldwin finished and the field ready for a new Black writer, and how, repelled, he left for Washington and SNCC, recalling Forman relaying Baldwin's vow never to betray them if they never accepted the degraded definitions the republic had prepared.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Congress Is the Only Judge
clips.summary: Sent back from a SNCC meeting to open the MFDP office, Thelwell explains the congressional-challenge strategy reaching back to Reconstruction: that Congress alone judges its seats, that only a defeated candidate may challenge, and that with the Democrats holding a huge majority the aim could not be to win the seats but to force the question.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Freedom Election
clips.summary: He lays out the protocol by which a congressman rising as the chamber is sworn in could challenge a Mississippi seat and trigger an investigation with subpoena power, and the problem that with no Black person able to reach the official ballot, the only candidates had to come from a freedom election with no official status, an uphill struggle made harder by the recriminations heaped on SNCC after Atlantic City.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Undying Loyalty to the Ticket
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts Lawrence Guyot's subversive stroke after Atlantic City: running Fannie Lou Hamer, Victoria Gray, and Annie Devine in a freedom election and proclaiming undying loyalty to the Johnson-Humphrey ticket, so that the only Mississippians forthrightly supporting the Democrats were the disfranchised MFDP, even soliciting Lady Bird Johnson to campaign.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: You Prepared It
clips.summary: He describes Joseph Rauh's belief that only his patronage had gotten the MFDP into Atlantic City, when in truth the murders of Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney had given them currency, and his own first conversation with Rauh, refusing a thousand-dollar bill for a brief the MFDP never ordered, which ended in an expletive and a slammed phone.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: No Situation That Can't Be Fixed
clips.summary: Thelwell tells of a Bill Cosby benefit at Howard that SNCC badly needed, threatened by an official who said the unregistered group would go to jail if it held the concert, and how, on advice, he called Joseph Rauh, who said he never saw a situation that could not be fixed and made the threat vanish, a favor Thelwell later suspected Rauh had staged to make him grateful.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The Establishment Group
clips.summary: Representing both SNCC and the MFDP at the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights, where Clarence Mitchell and Joseph Rauh presided, Thelwell endured cold abuse, with only Episcopal Bishop Paul Moore greeting him warmly, until Robert Spike of the National Council of Churches, who had felt betrayed over Atlantic City, publicly reconsidered and became an ally again.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: We Are Not Asking for the Seats
clips.summary: He explains the MFDP's counter to the charge that Congress would never seat three people elected in an unofficial freedom vote: that they were not asking for the seats but for the seats to be vacated and new federally supervised elections held, given a mountain of evidence that forty percent of the state had been disfranchised.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Crib in the Eaves
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts the conventional wisdom that no congressman would raise the challenge, and finding William Fitts Ryan, a maverick liberal of such low standing his office was a crib in the eaves of the House building, while Speaker John McCormack, capable of not seeing a giant rise from the floor, posed the real danger of refusing to recognize Ryan.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: You Got Me
clips.summary: He describes the scheme to reach the forty-odd freshman Democrats swept in on Johnson's landslide, renting a room beside their orientation and handing out invitations, where an upstate New Yorker holding a seat no Democrat had won in decades figured he might as well do good, Patsy Mink saw no way to do otherwise, and John Conyers, weighing a promised Judiciary Committee seat, got Thelwell's blessing to follow his conscience.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: One Worker Is Worth a Thousand Students
clips.summary: Thelwell tells of writing the powerful Republican Thomas Curtis a long letter that won a noncommittal opening, and of two ill-fitting-suited electricians' union men who, cutting through the academic rhetoric of a strategy meeting, handed him a hundred-thousand-dollar check and named a Pennsylvania congressman they would deliver, teaching him that one class-conscious worker was worth a thousand students.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Line of Silent People
clips.summary: Thelwell describes lining the poor Black Mississippians the MFDP had brought to lobby along the platforms of the tunnels between the House office buildings and the Capitol, silent in their Sunday best, so that congressmen rounding the corner stopped short and grew self-conscious under their gaze, a powerful moment he believed moved some votes.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Sixty People Stood Up
clips.summary: He recounts the vote day: Ryan rising to challenge the seats and, behind him, a wall of some sixty congressmen standing, too many for McCormack to overlook, forcing the Mississippians to stand aside and triggering a roll call in which a hundred forty-nine members voted to vacate the seats, a result beyond anything SNCC had expected, achieved without the established liberal hierarchy.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Across the Tracks
clips.summary: Thelwell savors Joseph Rauh and his aide hurrying in afterward, unable to make the unexpected tally add up, and describes how the vote's momentum opened the deposition process, sending hundreds of volunteer lawyers to subpoena white Mississippi officials who, for once, had to come across the tracks into packed Black churches to be questioned by local people.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Trial Balloon
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts pressing for the voting provision left out of the Civil Rights Act, and the administration floating, on a folded envelope, a trial-balloon voting rights bill that would let the president send registrars only to counties where fewer than ten percent of Black citizens were registered, which Clarence Mitchell, relaying King's word, moved to accept as the discussion ended.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: That Means Ninety Percent
clips.summary: Thelwell objected that a ten-percent threshold meant tolerating ninety percent disfranchisement and gave the president a toothless stick rather than an obligation, and that the movement should take to the streets to create a climate for real legislation, a memorandum he sent, fatefully, on MFDP letterhead carrying the signatures of SNCC, CORE, King through Andrew Young, and the MFDP.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Then Selma Happened
clips.summary: He describes the Leadership Conference falling silent for weeks after the memorandum, Clarence Mitchell suffering a heart attack over being called a traitor, Selma erupting by sheer coincidence during the hiatus, and Walter Reuther seizing the vacuum with a telegram whose first paragraph dangled a million-dollar fund to endow the movement's martyrs.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: The Widow of Malcolm X
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts Courtland Cox deflating Reuther's martyrs-endowment maneuver by asking whether the widow and daughters of Malcolm X would be beneficiaries and how it would be explained to the press, and the stiff dinner Reuther then sought at a Black restaurant, an offer of a job Thelwell did not want, noting that the Voting Rights Act of 1965 was rewritten after Selma to real effect, to which he credits his memorandum a slight contribution.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Captain, I Don't Believe I Will
clips.summary: Thelwell tells the story he made an epitaph in his book: the Parchman criminals who refused, even for liquor and easier time, to attack the arriving Freedom Riders, and the local seventeen-year-old Jesse Harris who walked into the white waiting room beside them, was sent alone to a county jail, and when whipped for not saying sir lay back down and dared the jailer to beat him more, later a SNCC project director who ran security for King before disappearing into addiction.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: By the Good Offices of the FBI
clips.summary: He recounts reuniting with Jesse Harris at a book launch and showing him the printed passage that vindicated his story, and how he came to UMass by accident and the good offices of the FBI, two agents warning the foreign student to be in school by September or be personally deported, the SNCC lawyer Len Holt needling them about their favorite girlfriend J. Edgar Hoover, and a rescued fellowship letter from Sidney Kaplan.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: A Statement With Holes in It
clips.summary: Thelwell closes on a phone call from Andrew Young about King's memorandum that instantly warmed a skeptical department, the freshman-football-team class he was assigned, and the Leadership Conference's first meeting back, where Bayard Rustin read a King statement that pointedly never said King's name had been forged, its last lines scratched out until there were holes in the paper, a principled vindication under pressure from the columnists who insisted King could never have signed so radical a document.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Big Man of the Black Panther Party
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Santa Rosa, California, in June 2016, with John Bishop on camera and Guha Shankar of the Library of Congress present, Elbert "Big Man" Howard introduces himself as a native of Chattanooga, Tennessee, born in 1938, who settled in Northern California after four years in the Air Force and made his way to Oakland and Merritt College.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Raised by Women in a Segregated South
clips.summary: Howard describes a fatherless childhood in Tennessee, raised as an only son by his mother and her sisters with his cousins as brothers and sisters, attending all-segregated schools, hearing talk of integration around 1954, and crediting teachers who tried to prepare him to survive in a hostile world before he left after the eleventh grade.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Stay Away From the Army
clips.summary: To support his single mother and develop himself, Howard joined the Air Force in 1956, a few years after the Korean War, after picking the brains of friends and relatives who warned him away from the Army as the cattle chute into combat, choosing instead a service that worked like a job with meals, lodging, and freedom to explore.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: A Revelation in Paris and Stockholm
clips.summary: Howard recalls being sent to Europe, getting to know Paris, Stockholm, and Copenhagen, meeting people of other cultures, and realizing the white people he met were nothing like the whites he had feared in the South, an awakening into wine, art museums, and conversation that he counts as a turning point in his maturity.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Looked Over and Screwed Over
clips.summary: Howard reflects on the nuances of segregation inside the Air Force, even in France, where Black servicemen stuck together for cultural kinship and got passed over for promotions and economic advancement, weighing whether to make a career of it, and traces his own musical progression from spirituals and blues to R&B and bebop while jazz musicians who had fled the United States played the clubs of Europe.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Taking the Discharge in Oakland
clips.summary: Stationed at an air base near Oakland and drawn to the city's blues, jazz, soul food, and friendly people who took him under their wing, Howard chose to take his discharge in Northern California, found jobs scarce, and used his GI Bill to enroll at the city college in Oakland to sustain himself while he looked for work.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Rap Sessions at Merritt College
clips.summary: As he studied Black history and tried to develop his writing, Howard met Bobby Seale and Huey Newton at Merritt College, and in rap sessions after class they discussed Oakland police repression and people being killed by cops, searching for a new approach beyond civil rights and nonviolence, a progressive and revolutionary path embodied in the ten-point platform of a new organization.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Cherry-Picking the Revolutionaries
clips.summary: Howard explains that the party's first demand was the power to determine their own destiny, that they saw their community occupied by police doing the bidding of rich politicians, and that they studied Che Guevara, Mao Zedong, Kwame Nkrumah, and Malcolm X, cherry-picking philosophies to build their own approach, knowing they were not in China or Cuba.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: A Listener Who Filed It Away
clips.summary: Howard describes hanging more with Bobby Seale than Huey Newton, going to Seale for advice on the justice system and his own run-ins with police, admiring Seale's skill for organizing and his far-out ideas, and recalling that Newton had grown up after his family's time in a relocation camp and came to live among the poor and oppressed Black people of Oakland.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: A Brand New Red Pickup Truck
clips.summary: Howard tells of leaving a club run by former basketball player Don Barksdale on a drizzly night, pulling his new red pickup to the front to collect his date, and being singled out for a ticket while the cops skipped the cars behind him, protesting, cursing, driving off, and being boxed in and hauled to jail, the incident that pushed him to get involved.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: A Legal Shotgun in the Rack
clips.summary: Howard recounts going to court with Huey Newton's advice, the judge dismissing the ticket while saying he was probably guilty as the cops laughed in the courtroom, and how, left highly angry, he mounted a rack in his pickup with a legal twelve-gauge shotgun, having confirmed with Newton on a ride home that a weapon openly carried in the car was perfectly legal.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Observing the Cops on Patrol
clips.summary: Howard describes how, as membership and firearms grew and Newton confirmed the law, the party patrolled their community at night and on weekends, observing officers performing their duty so they would not abuse people, advising those arrested not to resist but to let the Panthers follow to the courthouse and bail them out, which drew curious recruits into political education classes rather than shootouts.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: He Couldn't Possibly Jump That Fence
clips.summary: Howard recounts the Denzil Dowell case in Richmond, where authorities told the family they were justified in shooting Dowell in the back as he climbed a fence, his mother insisting an injury made that impossible, and how the party exposed the lie, held a demonstration at the Richmond courthouse, and produced a mimeographed sheet explaining what really happened.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: The Symbolic Power of the Gun
clips.summary: Howard reflects on showing up armed at the Richmond rally so the community saw the party was ready to defend itself and them, a demonstrated determination that drew people without fear, and the growing flood of requests for support as bunches of people showed up with enthusiasm whenever the party called a rally.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: We Need Our Own Organ
clips.summary: Howard explains the need to rent an office so the party had a focus in the community rather than being a roaming group of young men with guns, and tells how, after Bobby Seale said the party needed its own news organ, the first mimeographed sheet with Howard writing the text and Seale drawing the panther graphic became their news outlet, with Seale naming him the paper's first editor.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Minister of Information
clips.summary: Howard recounts that when Eldridge Cleaver, best-selling author of Soul on Ice, got out of prison and became editor, Howard was made deputy minister of information, charged with putting out a steady stream of public information through pamphlets, and notes he gave little weight to titles, wanting only to do the work and put a fork in the system.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Combating the Oakland Tribune
clips.summary: Howard frames the newspaper as vital to combating the negative coverage in the Oakland Tribune and the San Francisco Chronicle by telling the community the truth about incidents and issues, and describes requiring chapters across the country to send in news and pictures to Oakland headquarters, which he edited and printed for nationwide distribution.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Two Hundred Fifty Thousand Copies a Week
