clips.summary: Howard recalls the paper reaching over 250,000 copies a week, members staying up day and night to lay it out, the fifteen-cents-per-paper income that kept members going, the artist Emory Douglas whose relevant artwork people loved, and the difficulty finding printers after the system warned them off.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: A Little Hippie Guy in Berkeley
clips.summary: Howard tells how Max Scherr, who ran a Berkeley paper, let the Panthers print on his presses, a solidarity across groups, until larger printers, seeing the money and fearing bad publicity, also took the work despite FBI pressure, and how the government leaned on airlines to make shipping the paper costly even as distribution spread.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Organize the Asian Community
clips.summary: Howard follows up on Richard Aoki, who helped with the ten-point program and was asked to organize the Asian community, working with the Red Guard Party in Berkeley, while the party built coalitions with the Filipino, Mexican American, Latino, and Puerto Rican communities, the Young Lords, and the American Indian Movement, drawing the FBI's anger; Aoki remained an ally to the end, giving Howard a pack of slides before his health failed.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: An Ally to the End
clips.summary: Howard praises Richard Aoki as a great member, ally, and writer he loved, and recounts writing a blistering critique when an author published a book calling Aoki an FBI informant, after which the author decided he would gain no benefit and dropped the claim.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Going to the Penitentiaries
clips.summary: Howard explains that as Eldridge Cleaver became a popular spokesman, he handed off the paper and was selected to crisscross the country visiting penitentiaries and jails to check on jailed party members, assess legal situations, help lawyers, and organize defense committees, traveling from Denver to New Orleans and on to New Haven for the framing of Bobby Seale and Ericka Huggins.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Engineering Survival Programs
clips.summary: Howard describes relying on community friends who contributed bail money, setting up free breakfast and free clothing programs to build support for incarcerated members, and dodging entrapment during the New Haven case, when all-night phone calls offered him cash, weapons, and dynamite, which he recognized as traps and refused, hanging up to get a few hours of sleep before strategizing.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: The Invaders Become a Chapter
clips.summary: Asked about lesser-known chapters, Howard recalls a group of Memphis college students called the Invaders whom the party advised and inducted as a chapter, building solidarity with Latino communities and the antiwar movement, using jail time as a recruiting tool, and remembering George Jackson, murdered as a nationwide prison organizer and still considered a hero by prisoners.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: We Changed Their Attitude
clips.summary: Howard argues the party's overlooked achievement was changing the perspective of angry young people, taking those with major issues who had been to prison and politically educating them, recruiting kids who would have caused trouble and teaching them to cook breakfast for schoolchildren and gather donations for clothing programs, channeling anger into work that benefited the community.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Why Can't the Government Do It
clips.summary: Howard contends the party threatened the powers that be by exposing them, feeding 250,000 children free food daily, testing people for sickle-cell anemia, establishing free health clinics, and running the liberation school for children written off by the system, teaching relevant education while recruiting teachers fed up with educational bureaucracy.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: You're Going, Take Roberta
clips.summary: Howard recounts being sent with Roberta Alexander to Japan at the persistent invitation of Japanese students during a period of massive student rebellions, speaking at an enormous antiwar rally against the Vietnam War to students breaking out of a repressive system, and being followed by the Japanese version of the FBI as they traveled the provinces.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Not as Slick as the Americans
clips.summary: Howard describes finding the surveillance comical compared to the Americans, hooking up with filmmaker Shinsuke Ogawa who sponsored their travel, spending about a month in the country and appearing in all the papers, before being told to go on to Sweden and Denmark to work with support committees rather than returning home.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: The Base Commander Cut the Power
clips.summary: Howard tells of working with support committees in Denmark and Sweden, visiting Paris and Germany to speak with GIs who had the Panther paper, and a base where the commander blacked out all the power to stop a reception, so the soldiers took them to a local village to talk most of the night about America, the party, and why Black GIs had no business fighting the enemy's war.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Come Home and Save Bobby
clips.summary: Howard recounts speaking at colleges across Sweden, Denmark, and Finland until a message reached him that Bobby Seale had been extradited to New Haven on a murder charge in the death of Alex Rackley, urging him to come home at once to work on the paper and help organize Seale's defense, which he was anxious to do.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: New Haven Could Be Ashes
clips.summary: Howard describes the fourteen or eighteen months Bobby Seale and Ericka Huggins were imprisoned in New Haven, generating daily support with rallies drawing Daniel Berrigan and Dr. Benjamin Spock, the fear in New England that the city would explode, and his belief that Yale president Kingman Brewster and the courts, under FBI pressure, wanted the case gone to avoid mayhem near pristine New Haven.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Rockefeller Wasn't Having It
clips.summary: Howard recounts a call after the New Haven trial that the Attica prison had exploded and inmates had asked the Panthers to come negotiate, traveling there with Bobby Seale to talk with attorneys while the warden hoped they could quell it, and how Governor Nelson Rockefeller, eyeing reelection on a tough-on-crime platform, refused to come or make concessions, costing lives needlessly.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Shooting Chickens in a Barrel
clips.summary: Howard describes walking into the occupied Attica yard where inmates greeted him by name, the pitiful families of the guards begging him to help, the captured guards made to change clothes and sitting defeated by a fire, and the order the next day to retake the prison, when authorities opened fire and killed people like shooting chickens in a barrel, with the guards later proven not to have killed anyone.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Keeping My Sanity
clips.summary: Howard reflects on the relentless calls sending him from one crisis to the next, never knowing what to expect, and crediting something in his personality that let him deal with it and keep his goal in mind, as he sets up the next blow from the FBI's COINTELPRO operation, which they did not yet know by name.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Sam Napier Is in the Morgue
clips.summary: Howard recounts a call that Sam Napier, the party's beloved distribution manager who had no role in any military arm, had been killed and the New York distribution office set on fire, and how he went to a hotel where women and a group of terrified children were holed up, insisting they first find a safe place for the women and children before anything else.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Driven Around in a Paddy Wagon
clips.summary: Howard tells how, stepping out of the hotel after arranging safety for the women and children, police swarmed with guns drawn, drove him around in a paddy wagon for hours, and booked him into the Tombs, until a sympathetic African American assistant and attorney William Kunstler got him released on the condition that he leave New York and never come back, which he accepted.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Then the Killing Began
clips.summary: Howard describes how the press-fueled East Coast versus West Coast conflict escalated, how New York Panthers believed Sam Napier had been killed by East Coast members, and how Robert Webb, a West Coast Panther working the East Coast, was gunned down on the streets of New York in supposed retaliation, while Howard mourns Napier as a phenomenal organizer who built a dynamite distribution crew.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: A Work-Study Program at Merritt
clips.summary: Back from the East Coast, Howard recounts being selected by Merritt College president Dr. Young Park as an ombudsman running a work-study program with its own budget, objecting to a parole system that required a job to get released, and persuading the California adult authority to release parolees into the college, enrolling those without diplomas in GED and work-study tracks and others toward four-year degrees.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: They Knocked Him on His Behind
clips.summary: Howard describes how parolees in the program received stipends, campus meal tickets, and light library duty in exchange for keeping up their studies and attendance, until an instructor critical of the party provoked Panthers in his class to knock him down, an assault on a county employee that ended the program after a successful run.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Ramps and Rent Strikes
clips.summary: Howard recalls attending endless meetings, including one with people in wheelchairs who threatened to block traffic until sidewalk curb cuts and ramps were installed on city buildings, a Panther involvement few credit, and organizing rent strikes that forced landlords to remove lead-based paint that was sickening children.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Close Down the Chapters
clips.summary: Howard explains his decision to leave the party amid political differences, criticizing Huey Newton's order to disband the chapters nationwide and pull organizers into Oakland under the guise of the Bobby Seale for Mayor campaign, which Howard saw as a disservice to people who had worked their tails off to build community programs, after which members began to leave and even Bobby Seale departed without explanation.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: I Fulfilled My Mission
clips.summary: Howard describes the party limping along under last-minute hangers-on using its name and resources after the top-down dissolution, deciding it was time to go, and turning down a small group of organizers who offered to follow him, telling them instead to leave Oakland and use their talents wherever they wanted, with the promise he would help if ever needed.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: A Job at Kmart
clips.summary: Howard recounts going into retail without anyone knowing his Panther background, taking a job as a Kmart manager for about thirteen years, training in a Virginia town and being moved around, and using the role to mentor young workers, telling them to hang on to their jobs and not fly off the handle while he helped them where he could.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Sidestep the Snakes
clips.summary: Howard tells how, tired of a Kmart assignment in North Carolina, he moved to the gift-oriented retailer Service Merchandise, rising from trainee to running his own stores, before management practices that fired well-paid people to do more with less while bosses took big bonuses led him to give it up as his Social Security drew near.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: I Thought You Were Dead
clips.summary: Howard describes how Billy X Jennings kept after him to attend reunions until he finally went to one in Washington, where old comrades thought he had died, beginning to realize he had a legacy, and recalls FBI agents who questioned his elderly aunt and handed him a Freedom of Information Act packet of accumulated surveillance with names blacked out.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: I Kept Her Under Wraps
clips.summary: Howard explains that he kept his Panther past hidden even from his retail coworkers and from his daughter, born in 1980, because he believed the government would harass her to get to him, telling her nothing until she was sixteen or seventeen, when he relented at a Washington reunion and began sharing old stories so no one could harm her over what he had done.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: The Legacy Follows Me
clips.summary: Howard describes the tremendous feedback he now receives, how the legacy draws him back into the same issues he addressed before, returning to California where he reconnected with Carol, a longtime nurse, was treated for prostate cancer, and found that combined state, county, and local forces were killing people, pulling him into the work once again.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: A Help Line Called PACH
clips.summary: Howard recounts holding big meetings on police violence and the ICE immigration problem, and out of those workshops creating a help line called PACH, a twenty-four-hour telephone line where people abused by police could file reports logged into a database that could later supply information for lawsuits against the police.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: They Shot Andy Lopez in the Back
clips.summary: Howard describes the killing of thirteen-year-old Andy Lopez, shot in the back about seven or eight times by a sheriff's deputy who claimed his toy weapon looked like an assault rifle while the boy walked his own neighborhood in daylight, an outrage that sparked demonstrations, made worse when the deputy was not indicted and was returned to the street.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: I Got to Keep Fighting
clips.summary: Howard closes by vowing to keep challenging the district attorney and county prosecutor and giving voice to the people through an endless list of killings, observing that some in the civil rights movement could quit and find peace while he is not so blessed and must keep fighting, before David Cline thanks him and ends the interview.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Old as the Dean Himself
clips.summary: Ellie Dahmer recalls two years at Alcorn before transferring to Tennessee A&I, where a former Alcorn dean threw out her credits and held her an extra quarter, saying her Alcorn education was as old as he was, then earning a home economics degree in 1947 and teaching for better salary across Jasper and Forrest counties in schools starved of new books and resources.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: They Stayed for Dinner
clips.topic: Meeting Vernon Dahmer in Kelly Settlement
clips.summary: She describes coming to teach home economics in Kelly Settlement and first meeting Mr. Dahmer when he and several others came about the teaching job and stayed for the dinner she was cooking, and how after schools consolidated all Forrest County's Black students into Earl Travillion she was not rehired and took a job thirty miles away.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: He Put Christianity Into Practice
clips.topic: The Farm, the Sawmill, and the Store
clips.summary: Ellie Dahmer remembers Vernon as a deeply Christian man concerned for his fellow man who built a comfortable living from the farm, sawmill, and grocery store, worked his own and his siblings' land with tractors and hired help, had the children driving and doing farm work very young, and raised six boys plus a daughter, Betty, who worked alongside them while Ellie cooked for everyone and kept the sales-tax paperwork.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Read That and Tell Me What It Means
clips.topic: Registrar Lynd and the Constitution Test
clips.summary: She recounts trying to register before the registrar Lynd, who handed her a card with part of the Constitution and told her she failed no matter how well she explained it, the requirement that teachers notarize which organizations they funded since giving money to the NAACP could get them fired, and Vernon's voter registration card arriving only in time to be buried with him.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Something They Had to Die For
clips.summary: Ellie Dahmer describes serving three terms as election commissioner for District 2 some ten or fifteen years after Vernon was killed, gathering more than enough qualifying signatures with her daughter's help when another qualified woman declined to run against a white opponent, and the wonderful feeling of helping with something people had died for while making sure no one else was denied.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Meetings on the Same Spot
clips.summary: She reflects that there were always some good white people though many were nasty, declining to name the harassing families, and tells of learning the NAACP was active after moving to the area, the meetings held in her own living room with a lookout posted, the day the chief of police drove into the yard, and the Beard family, the young Ladner sisters, and others active through the same church, some of whom had relatives passing for white in Chicago and California.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: We Stopped Sleeping in Shifts
clips.summary: Ellie Dahmer describes the threats and fear during the registration years, the strength she and Vernon drew from each other, his refusal to drive alone or stop for police he could not trust, always carrying a gun, keeping the porch light off, and the family sleeping in shifts and keeping watch while she replaced the curtains with blackout ones so no one could see in.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: KKK Nailed to the Poles
clips.summary: She recalls drive-by shootings at the mailbox, Ku Klux Klan signs nailed to the poles that Vernon would tear down, and her belief that the harassment came out of Jones County, where the sheriff and his relatives were all in it together, so calling the law was useless.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: They Thought They Were White
clips.summary: Ellie Dahmer tells how the case against registrar Lynd broke open when two light-skinned Black neighbors, including Erlene Beard's family, were let register without the Constitution test because officials assumed they were white, giving the proof needed, and how she was one of the eighteen plaintiffs Lynd was forced to register, attending court every day and meeting John Doar and a young photographer the three-judge panel threatened with jail.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Two Young Men in a Suitcase
clips.topic: Hollis Watkins, Curtis Hayes, and Freedom Summer
clips.summary: She recounts Vernon returning from a Jackson meeting with two young SNCC and COFO organizers out of McComb, Hollis Watkins and Curtis Hayes, who lived and worked with the family like their own children and canvassed for voter registration, and her own Freedom Summer role cooking for the volunteers, including a picnic and a watermelon run when a young man drove a tractor through the white community, while her older sons were drafted as soon as they turned eighteen.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: A Booth Where They Sold Gas
clips.topic: The Voting Rights Act and Federal Registrars
clips.summary: Ellie Dahmer describes the wonderful feeling when the Voting Rights Act passed, and how Forrest County still kept Black people out of the courthouse, setting up federal registrars in a rented booth where gas had been sold, after which people began registering quickly.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: From Chicago to Boeing
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Seattle on February 28, 2013, Elmer Dixon traces his parents from Chicago, a Kentucky-born father raised there and a mother whose people came up from Mississippi in the great migration, and the illustrator job at Boeing that brought the family to the Northwest in 1957.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: From Revolutionary to Interculturalist
clips.summary: Dixon describes his decades of diversity and cross-cultural work, how his Black Panther past stayed hidden from corporate clients, and the 2010 SIETAR Europa congress in Lyon, France, where the theme of human rights and cultural diversity let him give a talk titled From Revolutionary to Interculturalist.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Landing in the Melting Pot
clips.summary: He contrasts the segregated South Side of Chicago with the Madrona home his mother still occupies, moved into in 1960, a mixed neighborhood of Black, Chinese, Japanese, Filipino, and Latino families where the playground across the street was the gathering place and a redlining boundary ran two blocks away.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Greeted by Boos, Not Confetti
clips.summary: Dixon recounts his father and friends forming a group called the Four Feathers and volunteering for the draft after Pearl Harbor, only to be sent to a segregated camp in Mississippi, where the town that should have cheered departing soldiers instead booed, spat, and hurled slurs at the Black men preparing to defend America.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: No One Was Going to Attack His Kid
clips.summary: He explains that such incidents never soured his parents on other people, who taught their children to judge by the content of character, and tells of throwing eggs at passing cars as a boy, getting grabbed by a driver, and his father seizing a hammer and charging out the door to defend his son.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Wax the Floor Days
clips.summary: Dixon remembers a house full of music, Ellington and Count Basie for Black guests and opera like La Boheme played while the boys waxed the hardwood floors for the Scandinavian friends who came to dance, plus matzo balls at a Jewish family's Passover, Russian Orthodox Christmas foods, and the Chinese shopkeeper who called him bad boy for years after a stolen candy bar.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Outgoing and Adventurous
clips.topic: What He Took From His Father
clips.summary: He reflects on the traits he absorbed from his charismatic, easy-to-make-friends father at Boeing, who had progressive friends and possibly contacts in civil rights circles, and the spirit of adventure, fed by the TV show Adventures in Paradise and the Kon-Tiki, that became his own can-do attitude.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: A Series of Micro-Abrasions
clips.summary: Dixon describes the family's dinner-table routine and watching the nightly news, the assassination of Medgar Evers and a church bombing, Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge with demonstrators beaten and hosed, and a colleague's metaphor of racism as a series of micro-abrasions, a wound repeatedly picked at that never heals.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Go Hear This Speaker at Garfield
clips.summary: He recalls the Black awareness movement reaching Seattle around 1965 while he still skateboarded with his white friends down at Crater Lake, his brother Aaron awakening before him, and Aaron telling him in 1967 that he needed to go hear a speaker named Stokely Carmichael at Garfield High School.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: The Good Knight on the White Horse
clips.summary: Dixon relays Carmichael's argument that Black people had been programmed to hate themselves, illustrated by the good knight always on a white horse and the bad on a black, white-hatted cowboys against Dirty Bart, angel food cake against devil's food, claims the teenager first dismissed but began turning over while sitting on the tennis courts across from his home.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: They Thought We Were the Help
clips.summary: He recounts his Madrona tennis team, coached by a volunteer named Miss Sullivan, winning the right to play the city championship at the all-white Seattle Tennis Club across the red line, where a Black kitchen worker mistook the players for hired help, the club barred Black members, and officials left before presenting the trophies the team had won, alongside his mother being told a job was filled until she called back unrecognized.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Co-Chair of Seattle SNCC
clips.summary: Dixon describes knowing he and his brother had to get involved, Aaron entering the Black Student Union at the University of Washington alongside Larry Gossett, and himself, a high school senior, joining SNCC as co-chair of the Seattle chapter, where the organizing centered on voter registration.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Bing Bing Bang Bang, Black Power
clips.summary: He tells how he and a few friends pushed for a Black Student Union at Garfield over the principal's refusal, agitated by sitting through the Pledge and the anthem and leading a Black Power chant that erupted the whole student body, then called a boycott that filled the halls until the principal relented, making it the first high school Black Student Union on the West Coast.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Charged With Inciting a Riot
clips.summary: Dixon recounts landing in the middle of a disturbance outside rival Franklin High School, objecting as two officers pinned a Black girl named Deborah Rodriguez to the ground, being handcuffed and charged with inciting a riot, and a judge who, rather than jailing him, asked him to write an essay on why he defended her.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: We Took Over the Principal's Office
clips.summary: He describes the grievances at Franklin, two Black students expelled over a fight while white students were sent back to class and a girl expelled for wearing an afro, and how he, Larry Gossett, his brother Aaron, and others occupied the principal's office overnight, answering the phones until the administration agreed to address the demands and grant a BSU.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Arrested the Day King Was Killed
clips.summary: Dixon tells of being pulled from his geometry class about a week later, read his rights and arrested for unlawful assembly, while Aaron, Larry Gossett, and other leaders were arrested almost simultaneously and, within the hour, Martin Luther King was assassinated, leaving him in juvenile watching cities burn and his parents driving him home in silence with both sons jailed.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: They Knew Something Was Happening
clips.summary: He reflects that his cross-cultural upbringing was mostly unspoken and that he never sat down to discuss his politicization with his parents, who nonetheless saw the signs, a newspaper photo of the seventeen-year-old in an afro and SNCC jacket telling reporters why he thought Vietnam, unlike his father's patriotic World War II service, was not a war worth fighting.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Like Landing in a Black Army
clips.summary: Dixon recounts traveling to the West Coast Black Student Union conference at San Francisco State in spring 1968, learning that the young Panther Bobby Hutton had been shot by Oakland police the day before King died, and crossing into West Oakland for the funeral, where Panthers in leather jackets and berets stood in platoon formation like a Black army.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: That Could Be Me Lying There
clips.summary: He describes the overwhelming pride and defiance of seeing an organized force so unlike the singing demonstrators on TV, spotting Marlon Brando talking with Bobby Seale, and stepping into a church lined with armed young Panthers where Bobby Hutton lay in an open casket draped in a Panther flag, seventeen years old like Dixon, the moment he decided there was no turning back and felt no fear of giving his life.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Fifty Panthers in My Parents' Home
clips.summary: Dixon recalls Bobby Seale's fiery speech that night at the conference, cornering him with Aaron and a few others to ask to start a Seattle chapter, and Seale arriving two weeks later to about fifty would-be Panthers spread across his parents' living room floor, organizing the chapter with Aaron as defense captain and Dixon as field lieutenant.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Dead Before Twenty-Five
clips.summary: He explains the lifelong commitment Seale laid out, how most serious Panthers, himself seventeen and Aaron nineteen, expected to be dead before twenty-five and those unwilling to accept that left within months, and how in 1968 J. Edgar Hoover named the Black Panther Party the number one threat to the internal security of the United States, painting a bull's-eye on them.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: A Defensive Animal
clips.summary: Dixon describes the party organizing not around civil rights but around God-given rights to humanity and dignity, taking the panther symbol from the Lowndes County Freedom Organization because the black panther is a defensive animal, becoming the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense, whose first survival program was the police alert patrol against routine racist brutality.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Armed With the Law
clips.topic: The Oakland Standoff and Huey's Mystique
clips.summary: He recounts how Huey Newton and Bobby Seale armed themselves with the Constitution and the California Penal Code and studied Mao's Red Book, then faced down Oakland police with shotguns on a street corner, Huey jacking a round into his chamber and declaring his right to the weapon, a moment that birthed the Panther mystique while leaving communities mistaking the party for their personal defense force.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Two Guns and a Thousand Rounds
clips.summary: Dixon summarizes the Ten-Point Program, demands for an end to police brutality, true education, healthcare, food, land, housing, justice, exemption from military service, and juries of one's peers, and the discipline it required, every Panther owning two guns and a thousand rounds and reading two hours a day to become a full revolutionary.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: It Exploded Overnight
clips.topic: From a Flood to a Hard Core
clips.summary: He describes how Bobby Hutton's death drove a flood of recruits, four to five hundred Panthers in Seattle within weeks drawn by pride and belonging, most used to spontaneous rebellion rather than the patient work of building political power, and the political education classes and readings like The Wretched of the Earth that the movement demanded.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Boiled Down to Twenty
clips.summary: Dixon recalls the chapter shrinking within months to a hardcore group of about twenty, the funerals of Seattle Panthers that drew thousands, the macho pride of the uniform balanced against the discipline required, and the infighting and expulsions that he saw as a natural part of forging a close-knit, family-like organization out of people putting their lives on the line.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: A Bodyguard on the Porch
clips.topic: Threats and the First Breakfast Program
clips.summary: He tells of moving into his own apartment after graduating Garfield in June 1968 with two bodyguards at seventeen, a bodyguard sleeping on his parents' porch, midnight death-threat calls his mother answered with go ahead and try, the FBI visiting his father at Boeing, and a white pastor opening his church for the chapter's first free breakfast program when Black churches had said no.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Prevent the Rise of a Black Messiah
clips.summary: Dixon recounts J. Edgar Hoover's memo to destroy, disrupt, and discredit the party and prevent the rise of a Black messiah, naming Los Angeles, Chicago, and Seattle as offices to shut down, with Huey already jailed, Bunchy Carter organizing thousands of former Slausons gang members in Los Angeles, and Fred Hampton uniting Chicago's gangs and young whites and Latinos.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: All Power to the People
clips.summary: He describes assassination attempts on himself and Aaron, including a shotgun whose doctored ammunition exploded and nearly took Aaron's arm, and Fred Hampton coining the phrase rainbow coalition before Jesse Jackson, the party's revolutionary intercommunalism and international branches, and Seattle's coalitions with the Peace and Freedom Party, the Young Lords, the Red Guard, and the American Indian Movement.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Five Breakfast Programs, Two Thousand Kids
clips.summary: Dixon ties his multicultural upbringing back to the party's intercultural character and describes five breakfast programs feeding over two thousand children a hot meal, sited in housing projects like Yesler Terrace and Holly Park and at the Atlantic Street Center, run by Ike Ikeda who had known the Dixon boys as children, sustained by a coalition of supporters including wealthy white liberals.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Sandbags and Gun Bunkers
clips.summary: He recounts Dr. John Green, a University of Washington neurologist who ran a medical detail at anti-war marches, helping the party open a free medical clinic in their second headquarters at 20th and Spruce, a fortified building with the People's Wall mural, sandbags on the walls, gun bunkers, and steel-and-plywood window covers, moved there in late 1969 as offices were attacked nationwide.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: The Clinic Still Open Today
clips.summary: Dixon describes the clinic's first focus, a well-baby clinic addressing high Black infant mortality, mothers walking past the armed officer of the day and sandbags yet feeling secure, the clinic surviving as the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center named for a young woman who died before thirty, and a roster of over thirty programs including free busing to prisons, free food, and a summer liberation school.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: A Subpoena to Washington
clips.summary: He recounts the Seattle chapter being transferred to Oakland in 1971 while he served fourteen months as a political prisoner in Salem, Oregon over an allegedly stolen coat after speaking at the University of Oregon, his family arranging a clemency hearing with Governor Tom McCall, and a House Un-American Activities Committee subpoena where, after giving only his name, residence, and occupation, he took the Fifth on whether he belonged to the Black Panther Party.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Only an Unconditional Surrender
clips.summary: Dixon describes the prison captain warning him and another Panther after the Attica uprising and the murder of George Jackson that they would be shot first if anything broke out, telling the governor's investigator he would accept nothing from the governor but an unconditional surrender and refusing release on those terms, and keeping up low-key organizing and political education behind bars, unlike the volatile California prisons.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: The Sandbags Were Gone
clips.topic: A Changed Era and the Split
clips.summary: Paroled in late 1972, he finds the old sandbagged office replaced and a quieter era, no more guns under pillows, and addresses the famous Panther split between continuing armed militancy and focusing on survival programs, recalling Seattle's own early ideological rift around 1969, including a brother-in-law inclined toward sensationalism and others who went to prison for a bank robbery, that the chapter weathered to keep building the structure Huey and Bobby intended.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Perpetuated by COINTELPRO
clips.summary: Dixon argues the national ideological split was driven by FBI informants through COINTELPRO, defines the counterintelligence program and its long history of union busting in the 1940s, McCarthy-era blacklisting, and the wiretapping of Martin Luther King, and explains how it turned its energies against the Panthers, whom Hoover had declared a greater threat even than King.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: My Father Danced on the Dining Room Table
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program, with biographer John Bishop, opens the July 21, 2011 interview at the Grahamsville, New York home of Dr. Emmett and Mrs. Priscilla Bassett, and Priscilla traces her politics from a Plainfield, New Jersey school of Harold Rugg textbooks through a Republican household, a classmate whose father danced on the table when Franklin Roosevelt died in 1944, a visit to Senator Clifford P. Case, an eye-opening union member at Life Magazine, and joining the Progressive Party back at Smith.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Sixteen Miles From the Carolina Line
clips.summary: Emmett Bassett describes growing up in Henry County, Virginia about sixteen miles from the North Carolina border, losing his father in 1935, a mother who spent half her insurance to send a half-brother to Hampton Institute and St. Paul in Lawrenceville, and half-brothers and Cleveland sisters urging him toward Lincoln University and high school in the North, despite an elementary school that rarely kept a teacher.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: The Only One Who Could Run the Farm
clips.summary: Emmett tells how his father judged him the only child able to run the farm, recalls working his parents' gardens and a brother who hated farming, and describes a cousin named Ida Dotson, schooled at Chestnut Knob near Ridgeway, who skipped him from fifth to seventh grade into a hostile class of much older boys.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: He Knew More History Than Their Sons
clips.summary: Emmett sketches the white Bassett family of the furniture industry and his grandfather, a Turner raised among the Bassett sons who slept in their house but was barred from their schools, taught math and history at home until he reportedly outknew them, made boss of a sawmill shipping lumber to Grand Rapids, Michigan, and overseeing white workers until a six-million-dollar Continental Bank loan to build a furniture factory required state approval.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Virginia Wanted No Blacks in Charge
clips.summary: Emmett recounts that around 1900 Virginia, unwilling to let a Black man supervise the operation, forced his grandfather William Henry out, after which he became a preacher marrying couples nearly weekly, that his father was likewise pushed out at the Tillesta furniture company, and that returning from the University of Massachusetts he learned of a white woman, a cousin of his father by the Tillester wife, whom he declined to visit.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: We Are Cousins
clips.summary: Emmett recalls hearing as a high school freshman about the Scottsboro defendants, boys his own age, and a swimming hole the family dammed near the Morris place where neighbors of both races swam without suits, and tells of a white Morris neighbor, his mother's first cousin, who brought two girls to swim, was reassured the children were all first cousins with nothing to fear, and afterward thanked Emmett for letting the girls swim.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: A Roadhouse That Refused to Serve Blacks
clips.summary: Priscilla recounts the road between Smith College and Amherst and a notorious roadhouse that refused Black patrons, a picket line Smith women joined, and a lawsuit after arrests, with the couple's meeting tied to that protest, and describes serving as a witness rather than a defendant at the trial, a Progressive Party lawyer careful to put no words in her mouth.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: It Was Our Right and Privilege
clips.summary: Priscilla recalls a small enclave of Black families in white Plainfield, an eighth-grade cantata in which a Black student sang Paul Robeson's part in Ballad for Americans and Robeson himself performing at the high school, and the couple reflects on being an interracial pair, Emmett noting little hostility at the University of Massachusetts and a house mother who took it in stride, Priscilla affirming it their right to proceed.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Up at Four to Milk the Cows
clips.summary: Emmett describes entering Tuskegee on a work-study program of some twenty-four hours a week, rising at four to milk thirteen Guernsey cows by hand to a supervisor's astonishment, earning fifteen to twenty cents an hour, and taking weekend barbecue work, including a man who cooked meat all night in a wooded trench for Tuskegee's yearly community feast and said white people had been good to him.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: The Farm Back Home
clips.summary: Emmett tells how he earned high marks, an A in biochemistry, and a standing offer of an easier job and a biochemistry major, but kept thinking of the farm his family still depended on him to run, and how he brought a Black worker off a white farm by paying two dollars a day where the man had earned seventy-five cents.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Just Like a Little Spring Chicken
clips.topic: Ralph Ellison and George Washington Carver
clips.summary: After a recording break, Emmett describes a National Youth Administration scholarship of about twenty-five dollars a month that freed him to study and guide visitors at the George Washington Carver place, a Labor Department writer he later realized was Ralph Ellison, and Dr. Carver, who kept no distance from his scholarship students, ran a hand over the growing boy's chin calling him a spring chicken, and died, about seventy, before Emmett could get his autograph.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Nobody Who Volunteered Came Home
clips.summary: Emmett describes the options as the war intervened, volunteering for the Tuskegee Army Air Force at about eighteen or training as an officer at Fort Valley State or in Texas, and writing his mother, who replied that no one in their family who volunteered ever came home alive, naming a great-uncle, Willie Travis, said to look more white than Black and killed at the Alamo, a story Emmett himself holds uncertainly.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: I Never Wanted to Go to Prison
clips.summary: Emmett explains that conscientious objectors he knew went to prison, which he would not do, and recounts his Martinsville, Virginia draft board, including a judge named Whittle and dairy farmers, telling him that with such a poor physical turnout among Black men they wanted to draft him, and his retort that they deferred men who worked their own farms.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Go Sit in the Back Seat
clips.summary: Emmett recounts entering the quartermaster corps, completing both the soldier and quartermaster courses with high grades, and assignment to Company E under Colonel Snowden, the highest-ranking Black officer in a unit largely of professors, and after a break describes being summoned to a meeting of about a thousand white people, taking a front seat, a woman asking him to move back, and his reply that they could simply send him outside, which a young white officer soon did without explanation.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Everybody Wants to Be Known
clips.summary: Emmett says segregated service kept him at Camp Lee, Virginia in Company E among professors and college presidents, and recounts attorney Robert Ming, who argued a case before the Supreme Court and traveled with him to Fort A.P. Hill, being commissioned and sent to Europe to address mistreatment of Black soldiers in France, telling Emmett everyone there sought recognition, and a hostile second lieutenant who threatened to bust the sergeant back to private.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: I Could Never Train a Sharecropper
clips.summary: After the war Emmett taught agriculture in the county at about ninety dollars a month, then confronted a plan along Route 58 toward Danville where bankers who owned thousands of acres would run a plantation school training returning Black veterans for seventy-five dollars a month and federal money, only to leave them stuck as sharecroppers after three years, which he refused to teach, a program a white professor on a Julian Bond tour later recognized.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Library, Nursing, School Teacher
clips.summary: Asked about Columbus and any early stirrings on women's status, Priscilla says they first spent a couple of years in New York where, with law school out of reach and no money for graduate school, she took a New York Public Library internship, an excellent program that nonetheless channeled her into women's work, library, nursing, and teaching, which she recalls without great regret.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: I Could Do Something About It
clips.topic: A Woman on the Production Line
clips.summary: In Columbus Priscilla left library work for far better pay at General Motors, women's work on the production line where the skilled trades were almost exclusively white men, and tells of recognizing a Black co-worker in the washroom despite a wig, learning that this woman with real Curtiss-Wright factory experience was kept on ninety days probation in the washroom while Priscilla was placed directly, an injustice that drove her to become a shop steward and convinced her she could do something about it.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: A Meeting at the Baptist Church
clips.summary: Priscilla recalls being in Columbus in 1954 when the Brown decree came down and in the summer of 1955 when Emmett Till was murdered, and a Baptist-church meeting and a collection at the state capitol that raised about three thousand dollars for Till's mother, covered in The Crisis, then Emmett's own required teaching of a course before his Ohio State qualifying exam and a talented colleague, Kenneth Fox, who later went to Uppsala, Sweden.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: The Whole Top Would Go Off the Building
clips.summary: Emmett recounts Chicago companies flying him in and housing him at the Palmer House for two weeks of job hunting, each saying that hiring him would blow the top off the building, and a man at Beatrice Foods who said he had nearly lost his job over hiring a Chinese technician, since the dairy and food technology jobs in the heart of the Black community were all white.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Seventeen Years of Being Illegal
clips.summary: Emmett notes his doctorate likely made him the first person of African descent to take a Ph.D. in dairy science, that Black colleges in Florida and Georgia recruited him sight unseen but the couple could not move South under miscegenation laws until Loving v. Virginia in 1967, and describes joining Columbia University's microbiology department after Sheffield Farms, banking the organic chemistry he had taken at Ohio State while declining a chemistry Ph.D. that demanded more math.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: I Don't Want That Job
clips.topic: The FBI and a Lost Position
clips.summary: After lunch the interview turns to a position Emmett was considered for at Ohio State; his department head Dr. Gould relayed concern not about him but about friends of Priscilla's tied to a place called Iowa House, and when Emmett refused to speak with the FBI or CIA about his associations he was told plainly he could not have the job, which he did not want, while the family faced closed segregated pools, found refuge at Lake Hope, and Priscilla turned away an FBI agent while hanging laundry.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: You Had to Bring In Grants
clips.summary: Emmett describes his Columbia salary coming off grants, requiring him, a senior colleague, and the technician to fund themselves through grant money, and a productive run of five major publications on the biosynthesis of patulin and the formation of aromatic amino acids.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: That Is Their Field
clips.summary: Emmett recounts wanting to publish full findings that tyrosine and phenylalanine came from a common precursor, his radioactive-carbon results showing tyrosine could not derive from phenylalanine, and his colleague warning that Rockefeller Institute researchers controlled the field and would block a full paper, so they published only a guarded note.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: They Republished It Their Way
clips.summary: Emmett tells how, weeks later, prize-winning Rockefeller Institute researchers put out the same common-precursor finding he had been forced to soften, publishing it as their own across three better-funded groups that had cornered the field and dismissed his lab's claims as insufficiently researched, a matter he attributes to academic rather than racial politics, noting he was only a postdoctoral researcher then.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: No Dilemma, Mae Mallory Said
clips.summary: Asked how their activism unfolded in mid-1950s New York around Little Rock and Greensboro, Priscilla turns to their daughter's schooling, the chance at the Fieldston ethical-culture school in Riverdale, and her reluctance to leave the public system against Emmett's memory of a one-room segregated school, resolved when Mae Mallory, a colleague of Robert F. Williams, told her to send the child wherever she would get the best education and keep on fighting.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: They Filled Our Car With Sugar
clips.summary: Priscilla and Emmett describe trying to integrate three nearby Washington Heights junior high schools by balancing enrollments so no student would travel far, a walkout, a Freedom School in a Methodist church, the Ocean Hill-Brownsville fight to keep a community-controlled school open, and Emmett's front-page Daily News appearance for picketing, after which angry people filled their car's tank with sugar and their supporters, including the principal, were fired.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: In Friendship and Stanley Levison
clips.summary: Emmett recounts being recruited by his neighbor Bob Lewis into the In Friendship group planning the March on Washington, encountering Stanley Levison who advised Martin Luther King, Bayard Rustin's absence in Africa to stop the French bomb, planning handed to A. Philip Randolph at the Apollo Theater, recruiting buses toward Randolph's hundred-thousand threshold, and the couple attending both the March and an earlier Prayer Pilgrimage for Freedom where Priscilla first heard Dr. King.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: The Locked Apartment Door
clips.summary: Emmett and Priscilla recount returning around 1956, after the bus boycott, to an apartment they had claimed only to find the lock changed, the landlord admitting he had been told not to rent to them, and Priscilla turns to her work on the Council on Interracial Books for Children and a library-science thesis on how rejecting a child's first language alienates them, which she declined to publish, feeling it improper to take the white privilege of doing so.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: They Voted Not to Allow the Meetings
clips.summary: Priscilla recalls reconnecting in the early 1960s with the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom and a talk on war toys, resistance to meetings near Fort George Avenue, and their Mitchell-Lama cooperative, a state-subsidized middle-income development where she sat on the board that voted by majority not to allow the Upper Manhattan Committee for Equal Rights to meet there, prompting the couple to move out around 1964 before going to Sweden.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: A Three-Way Conversation
clips.summary: Priscilla recalls regularly going to hear Malcolm X and a sidewalk exchange that became a three-way conversation with Michael Harrington, while Emmett says he went three or four times to Malcolm's Harlem speeches before the break with the Nation, the couple living not far from where Malcolm was assassinated and out for a walk that morning, before the talk turns to the war, the 1967 Newark rebellion during which they were in Sweden, and Emmett's two years at Ortho Pharmaceutical.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: They Bought Blood Through a Front
clips.topic: Leaving Johnson and Johnson for Newark
clips.summary: Emmett explains he left Ortho Pharmaceutical, part of Johnson and Johnson, troubled by product testing in poor communities and blood bought through a front group rather than under the company's name, and that the New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry, standing in a Newark just torn by rebellion with no African Americans on its faculty, recruited him for admissions, offered free tuition for his daughter and a raise, though Black doctors meant to cover his clinical duties never showed up.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: The Stationmaster Took Us In
clips.summary: Asked about their year in Sweden, the couple describes crossing by ocean liner to Norway, visiting a Rockefeller Institute friend whose wife had been Emmett's technician, traveling by train from Trondheim to Uppsala, a stationmaster who took them home when they found no lodging, and Emmett's easy entry to a lab through Dr. Arne Tiselius, who never came in but spoke with him often.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: He Blended All the Issues
clips.summary: Priscilla recounts how Dr. King's Riverside Church speech drew her by blending civil rights, economics, and the war, citing Muhammad Ali's refusal to fight in Vietnam and the poverty draft, calling King an activist philosopher she respects alongside Malcolm X, whose memorial by author Manning Marable they attended, and arguing the missing element today is the mass movement that creates such leaders; the couple mark nearly sixty-two years of marriage, recall W.E.B. Du Bois's NAACP statement that it neither opposed nor supported interracial marriage but believed those who fall in love should be free, after Loving v. Virginia struck down miscegenation laws in seventeen states, their elder daughter crediting a shared moral compass, the family's interracial history including the Alamo ancestor Paul and Ned, and Emmett's New York City Human Rights Commission work monitoring neighborhood housing groups like Reena.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: The Recording Resumes the Same Ground
clips.summary: After a pause the recording resumes and the couple re-tell, nearly verbatim, the preceding reflection: Dr. King's Riverside Church speech blending racial, economic, and antiwar concerns, Muhammad Ali on Vietnam and the poverty draft, King as an activist philosopher, Malcolm X and Manning Marable's book, the need for a mass movement, their long marriage, Du Bois and the NAACP on interracial marriage after Loving v. Virginia, the moral-compass remark, the Paul and Ned and Alamo ancestry, and Emmett's Human Rights Commission work with neighborhood housing groups.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: They Go to Who Has the Inside Information
clips.topic: Jobs, the Commission, and Beatrice Foods
clips.summary: Resuming with new material, Emmett sums up the Human Rights Commission, noting jobs go to those with inside information or political-club ties, that he never needed a city job, but that dairy technology plants set in the heart of the Black community would not hire even a Black porter, naming Beatrice Foods around 1955, where a professor's brother, both Purdue men, thought Emmett should have disclosed his race before applying, to which he replied they would see his color when he arrived.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: You Can See That I Am
clips.summary: The interviewer closes by invoking Frederick Douglass's line that without struggle there is no progress, and Emmett tells his final story of registering to vote just before entering the Army, sent to a field station where the registrar handed him a blank sheet of paper, advice from a well-off Black man named Jim Mullins to write his identifying facts, his omitting his race so the registrar pointed it out, his reply that one could see he was Black, and registering without problem around 1942 or 1943.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Reared by His Father and His Aunts
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at his Columbia, South Carolina home on June 8, 2011, Justice Ernest Finney Jr. opens with his upbringing after his mother died when he was ten days old, raised jointly by his father and his aunts, his father a Virginia State University graduate and schoolteacher in Smithfield, Virginia, where Finney was born, a strict man who ruled the roost fairly.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Bags Packed Within 24 Hours
clips.summary: Finney describes a childhood of constant moves across Virginia whenever his father got a better teaching offer, his father's wartime work at the Pentagon, then Morgan State University in Baltimore, and finally the call from Claflin College in Orangeburg to be its dean, which put him on a train to South Carolina amid friends' warnings he would be lynched.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: They Treated Us as Family
clips.summary: He recalls the warm welcome that disproved his friends' exaggerated stories, his job as a soda jerk at the college soda shop owned by the Weber family on the edge of Claflin and South Carolina State, the active Black business community, a Ku Klux Klan parade down the street, and membership in Trinity United Methodist Church.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: A Path to Hitch His Star To
clips.summary: Finney recounts enrolling at Claflin around 1948 without a formalized ambition, and how observing the standing of Black lawyers in Washington's Black community during the Morgan State years, among them the immaculately dressed fraternity president and national leader Belford V. Lawson Jr., showed him the path he wanted to follow.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: About Four Students in the Class
clips.topic: Law School and the Brown Decision
clips.summary: He describes beginning law studies at the South Carolina State College of Law in the fall of 1952, the dedicated faculty and tiny classes that gave individual attention, the lawsuit by a South Carolina State student that helped desegregate the University of South Carolina, and the ecstatic reaction on campus to the Brown decision weeks before he took the bar in 1954.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Walking in the Front Door of the Courthouse
clips.topic: A Young Black Attorney in the 1950s
clips.summary: Finney explains the challenges facing a young Black attorney in mid-1950s South Carolina, taking clients to the courthouse to show them a Black lawyer could pull down the books and walk in the front door, and his lasting disappointment that the state closed the South Carolina State law school while North Carolina kept the comparable Central law school open and integrated.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: My First Meeting at the Bar Was as a Waiter
clips.topic: Teaching in Conway and the Bar Banquet
clips.summary: He recalls teaching school in Conway for about six years and working a second job at the Ocean Forest Hotel, where, because Blacks were barred from the bar association's social events, he attended his first South Carolina Bar Association meeting around 1954 as a waiter, a story he later told as Chief Justice when he sat at the head table.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: A Decision to Go Full-Time
clips.summary: Finney describes staying abreast of South Carolina's Black legal community and how a chance encounter with Matthew J. Perry Jr. and I. DeQuincey Newman, on their way to work on desegregating a state park, prompted his decision to move his family to Sumter and practice law full-time, helped by a part-time post teaching South Carolina history at Morris College, and remembers Newman, the NAACP field secretary, as active in everything in the civil rights community.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Jail Without Bail
clips.summary: He recounts being called from Rock Hill to represent students from Friendship Junior College connected with the Congress of Racial Equality, whose field secretary James T. McCain was a fellow Sumter citizen, his car breaking down on the first trip, and his shock when, after the conviction, the students announced the new jail-no-bail tactic, with SNCC also active and one athlete student withdrawing for fear of losing his scholarship.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: A Cadre of Good Lawyers
clips.topic: The Volume of Civil Rights Work
clips.summary: Finney explains that a lawyer articulating the Black community's concerns had to be everywhere at once, that he identified most closely with the active Sumter NAACP chapter, and that he and a cadre of lawyers mostly trained at South Carolina State took on financially insignificant cases to make a living, joking with Matthew Perry that even a Greyhound bus wreck out front would send the lucrative cases to established firms.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Beat Three Times but Never Lost Faith
clips.topic: Civil Rights Commission and Running for Office
clips.summary: He describes joining the South Carolina Advisory Committee to the US Commission on Civil Rights in 1963 as another avenue into the struggle, naming the active White Citizens' Council and the white ally James McBride Dabbs of the human rights effort, and notes that he ran for political office three times and lost three times before finally winning.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: It Did Not Reach the Most Needy
clips.summary: Asked about evolving race relations through the 1960s, Finney voices disappointment that the progress made did not benefit the neediest of the Black community, a current rather than ancient problem, pointing to the gap between Spartanburg County schools and the so-called Corridor of Shame, and the continuing need to give poor children a quality education.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: I Resigned in Protest
clips.summary: He recalls the 1968 Orangeburg Massacre shootings and how, when the next administration refused to reopen an investigation, he and Ben Payton and several others resigned, feeling the federal government had not used all its powers, noting he was chairman of the South Carolina committee of the US Commission on Civil Rights at the time.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Twenty-Five Percent Was All I Needed
clips.topic: Winning the State House and the Black Caucus
clips.summary: Finney describes putting together his 1972 State House race on little money by talking to anyone who would have him, the journalist Jack Bass noting in a 1975 interview that he drew 20 to 25 percent of the white vote, and his founding of the legislative Black Caucus on the conviction that the caucus first had to learn the rules to be a mechanism for change.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: A Miracle Still Happened
clips.summary: He recounts becoming a force in the legislature, then his 1985 election to the State Supreme Court with an inside track in the 124-member House against an opponent who worked the Senate, and his 1994 election as Chief Justice by the legislature, which left him shocked and pleased in the belief that hard work earns its share of the vote.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: My Most Famous Run
clips.summary: Prompted by his wife, Finney recalls the most famous race he lost, a run for Congress for an open seat against a sitting congressman he believes was Albert W. Watson, drawing perhaps 1,800 votes, and tells of declining overtures from Robert F. Kennedy to join the Justice Department in Washington, preferring to stay and work in South Carolina, which had been good to him.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Almost as Bad as Separate but Equal
clips.summary: Finney discusses Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina, a case in which poor school districts sought more financial support, and his effort to keep the case alive so legislative leaders might resolve the tax-base disparity between wealthy and poor counties, the ruling he caught the most flack for likening to separate but equal, and his later work with Richard W. Riley against school voucher proposals.
related_events: Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: I Had Taken This Oath
clips.summary: Finney closes on the hardest decision of his career: though unalterably opposed to the death penalty as a legislator, he was assigned as a circuit judge to try a man who had robbed and raped, and after a Horry County jury fixed the sentence at death by electrocution he had to impose it, calling it a hard day's work under the oath he had taken, before thanking his hosts.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: The Youngest of Twelve
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at Bennett College in Greensboro on July 6, 2011, Provost Esther Terry opens by describing her childhood as the youngest of twelve on a farm, surrounded by older siblings, and begins the story of her mother, who entered normal school to become a teacher but was called home when her own mother died.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Two Widowed by the Flu
clips.summary: Terry recounts how her mother's first husband died in the flu epidemic of 1917, leaving her with three surviving children in Richmond, Virginia, while her father lost his first wife and three children to the same epidemic; widowed, her mother returned to Warren County, met her father, and he raised all the children as his own, with nine more born to the union.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: The Land in Wise, North Carolina
clips.summary: She describes how her father, a sharecropper, worked with the owner to buy the land around 1945, land the family has kept ever since; her father died in 1970 and her mother in 1985, and now the last of her siblings, she holds the farm in Wise, North Carolina for the nieces and nephews who are her many children.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: A Sermon Rather Than a Preaching
clips.summary: Terry explains her churchgoing mother valued the teachings of the church over its preachings, favoring Sunday school and the saying that she would rather see a sermon than hear one, and recalls being asked each week what she had learned and her mother's refrain, how would you feel if that happened to you.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Run, Run to the Field
clips.summary: She recalls loving school and going because her mother thought it important, though farm work kept the children out when the crops needed saving; her mother picked cotton to make up for their absence, and the children were sent straight from school to change clothes and run to the field while their mother went to make supper.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: The Church and the School as Centers
clips.summary: Terry describes how the church and the school served as centers of the spread-out Black farming community around Wise, with parents deeply involved in Easter and evening programs, and notes that whether the family could attend often depended on whether a brother was home to drive, since her father never learned to drive.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: The Luckiest, Not the Smartest
clips.summary: As the first in her family to attend a four-year college, Terry reflects that she was not the smartest but the luckiest of her siblings, since there was no money and few scholarships; her brothers and sisters left for factory work, two at Ford Motors, one at a Newport News shipyard, and one in the New York school system, taking pride in their jobs and buying their homes.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: The Black Community Was the Bible
clips.summary: Terry explains that farmers gained Social Security in 1957, retroactive to age sixty-five, and how she helped neighbors born at home with midwives prove their ages through family Bibles where births and deaths were recorded, and through catastrophic events like floods and tornadoes that served as landmarks for dating their lives.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: This One Will Go to College
clips.topic: A Mother's Hope and a We
clips.summary: Terry recalls that when her father got a Social Security check her mother declared this one would go to college, revealing she had wanted every child to go to school; she describes evening spelling contests and lessons as a family, and how at seventeen her ambitions were shaped less by independent plans than by a collective we centered on owning the land.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: The Childhood Story Retold
clips.summary: After a pause the recording restarts and Terry re-tells the childhood material covered earlier, again describing being the youngest of twelve, her parents widowed by the 1917 flu, the family's purchase of the land around 1945, her churchgoing mother's teachings, picking cotton, her siblings' factory jobs, Social Security in 1957 and proving neighbors' ages by the family Bible, and her mother's resolve that she would go to college.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Born Up by the Community
clips.summary: Asked about Theaoseus Tobias Clayton of Warren County, whom she later knew, Terry turns to her arrival at Bennett College fifty-seven years earlier, describing how the whole community prayed for her and gave her a few pennies, how she had earned a small scholarship on the SAT, and how her parents drove her to a campus she had never seen.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: The Mothers Who Exchanged Children
clips.summary: Terry recalls the lonely feeling when parents said goodbye, and how, as they settled her in, her mother met the mother of Roslyn Smith from West Virginia; because the Smiths could not afford trips home, the two mothers bonded and exchanged children, with Terry's family hosting Roslyn for holidays across all four years, beginning a friendship that endures to this day.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Miss Alexander, What Am I Holding
clips.summary: Studying English and theater in the late 1950s, Terry honors her professors, the brilliant and exacting Hobart Jarrett, who quizzed her by holding up a book and demanding its title and convinced his arrogant students they could write and debate anything, and the less polished grammar terror Dr. Crawford, who drilled them in parsing and diagramming sentences.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: We Sent Him the Flowers
clips.summary: Terry tells how the theater director Fred Eady came from Howard and how the students, deciding he should marry, secretly bought flowers for a woman on campus and signed his name until the two did marry; she recalls living in Honors Dorm and being taught daily that Bennett girls were as good as anyone and bound to give back to their communities.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Happiness Was Being Safe
clips.summary: Terry reflects that her childhood happiness was bound up with feeling safe, even as the Southern terrain was dotted with white-only signs, and describes Bennett as a cocoon where students were taught they were special; she frames the contradiction of F.W. Woolworth, where a Bennett lady could buy anything but could not sit down at the lunch counter for a cherry coke.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Aware of the Storm
clips.summary: Asked how much attention she gave to wider events like Little Rock, early Greensboro school desegregation, and the lawsuits of figures like Dr. Simkins, Terry says Bennett's purpose was always to prepare students to contribute to the world, that her classmates came with prior experience of racial conflict, and that they felt safe inside even as a storm raged around them.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: She Erased the T and the Y
clips.summary: Terry recounts her mother's rule against speaking of all white people as they, demonstrated by writing THEY on a board and erasing letters until only he and she remained, insisting wrongs be named to individuals; she repeats her mother's lesson to her son that white people were as good as they knew how to be.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: What If We Sat Down
clips.summary: Terry describes how by the fall of 1959 her sociology-studying classmates, among them Roslyn Smith, Gwen Mackel, Shirley Dismute, and Gloria Brown, were intensely engaged and brought their discussions back to the dorms, asking how barriers could be torn down, what would happen if they boycotted or picketed, and finally what would happen if they sat down at the lunch counter, talking through nonviolence all the while.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: It Was Not Spontaneous
clips.summary: Terry argues the Bennett women are not sufficiently credited, recounting a fiftieth-anniversary retrospective where Gwen Mackel, Shirley Dismute, Dolores Finger, Roslyn Smith, and Linda Brown returned and maintained that, while the four young men at North Carolina A&T sat down first, the action grew from a plan deliberated on Bennett's campus, with Dr. Jarrett urging the girls to invite the A&T men so they would be protected.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Wait Until After Christmas
clips.summary: Terry recounts that President Willa B. Player would not forbid her girls from acting but cautioned that starting before Christmas vacation would scatter the momentum, so they waited; she recalls Gwen Mackel standing ready in Woolworth's on February 1, having bought something first as trained, when the young men sat down, the culmination of a plan made on campus.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: We Were Proud
clips.summary: Terry confirms she sat at the lunch counter alongside Linda Brown, recalling how proud she felt while her mother was likely terrified, and how unnerved she was by the unexpected hatred glaring at them; she notes her quiet mother said little about it, perhaps regretting the world that made it necessary but never telling her how she felt.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: A Big Woman in Stature
clips.summary: Terry portrays Willa B. Player as a dignified, quietly powerful woman profoundly committed to making Bennett students into strong women grounded in social justice, who believed education was no good unless used to correct wrongs and who lived what she believed without fiery speeches.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Turn In Your Grades
clips.summary: Terry recalls that Player let her girls march and sit at the counter but required them to keep up their grades with no faculty excuses, a real-stakes stance she contrasts with students elsewhere asking professors to forgive their absences; she says she knew little of the A&T president but believed he must have wanted the Greensboro sit-ins to succeed.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: These Are My Girls
clips.summary: Terry tells how, when the girls were arrested in a later wave and jailed, Willa B. Player drove herself downtown alone, a dangerous act for a Black woman in Greensboro, to declare these are my girls and see after them, and notes that a cross had been burned at the college president's house.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: I Wanted to Study English
clips.summary: Turning to her 1961 graduation, Terry describes how studying with Dr. Jarrett and Dr. Crawford turned her ambition from teaching back home toward becoming an English teacher and going to graduate school, and explains the old practice by which a Black applicant accepted at a Northern university could have the state pay rather than desegregate its own classrooms.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: You Can't Be Esther Alexander
clips.summary: Terry recounts her parents urging her to UNC-Chapel Hill after the sit-ins, a stint as a waitress in Hartford where her sister kept her wages as enforced savings, and her arrival to collect a dorm key, where a woman, stunned that Esther Alexander was Black, realized she had paired her with a white roommate against a UNC ruling and did not know what to do.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: It Is the Law, Not the Girls
clips.summary: Terry describes being given a room alone, prepared to be furious until white girls short of housing offered to room with her, and her mother's lesson that it was the ruling she should hate, not the girls; she earned her master's quietly, taking a class with C. Hugh Holman among gentleman professors, then taught at a college for a couple of years before pursuing the doctorate at UMass Amherst.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Easy in Massachusetts
clips.summary: Terry turns to the 1969 creation of the Afro-American Studies program at UMass Amherst, which she would later direct, and explains that what happened in Massachusetts could not have happened elsewhere because of a sympathetic administration and an incredible assembly of faculty and friends gathered there.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Sterling's Old Gang
