clips.summary: Terry recounts how the Massachusetts Review, launched in 1959 and dedicated to the four young men who sat down at North Carolina A&T, became her circle, and how she, Michael Thelwell, Bernard Bell, and her future husband Eugene Terry came to Amherst because Sterling Brown of Howard sent his students to study with Sidney Kaplan, who knew Black American literature like no one else and now directed the English graduate program.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Embellishing the American Story
clips.summary: Terry describes the program they built under the English department and the conviction that animated it, never to erase American literature but to embellish it with the African American contribution as a uniquely American narrative; Michael Thelwell had come from the movement and the work of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, and the talk turned from a program to a department whose case went to the trustees in Boston.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Nobody Blew Anything Up
clips.summary: Terry recalls that, unlike the fights at Cornell and elsewhere, the independent Afro-American Studies department was granted quietly with no protest, leaving her almost let down for want of celebratory noise; she reflects that UMass Amherst was a special place where she crossed thresholds others thought she could not, joining Black and white colleagues to build a strong program.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Run the Lap You're In
clips.summary: Returning as provost in 2009, Terry reflects on Bennett and the young women there, warning that knocking down the old signs can fool people into thinking the work is done; she tells students that one runs only the lap one is in before passing it on, that the world is different but not yet perfect, and that they must find its remaining problems.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Big Women in the Making
clips.summary: Terry closes by charging students to make social justice and giving back to the community a partner to whatever skills they pursue, reflecting on a younger generation's technology she resists, and saying she hopes Bennett can be a haven that turns hundreds of little girls into big women, work she counts an honor on the days she does it.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: The Youngest of Seven in Itta Bena
clips.topic: Birth and Family in the Delta
clips.summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer in Jackson, Mississippi, in March 2013, Euvester Simpson opens on being born the youngest of seven children in Itta Bena, a butcher father who became a sharecropper, and a mother raised on her grandfather's farm in Mound Bayou, a great-grandfather who went to college after emancipation and became a teacher, minister, and landowner.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: The Car That Would Not Crank
clips.summary: Simpson tells how her mother met her father during a revival at her great-grandfather's church, asked God for a sign before eloping, took it as that sign when the brand-new Model T would not crank, ignored it, and married him the next night when the car started, after which the couple farmed rented land until both her father's friend and her great-grandfather lost their land and the family fell into sharecropping.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: A School Built So Black Kids Could Pick Cotton
clips.summary: She recounts her schooling at Ellis Rogers on the campus of Mississippi Valley State, the seventh-through-ninth grades in Itta Bena, the consolidated Amanda Elzy High School that bused Black children from across the county, and the split session that sent students to class in summer so they would be free to pick cotton in the fall, a system that helped push her parents to send her to Racine at fourteen.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Everybody Knew Their Place
clips.topic: Segregation and Daily Life in the Delta
clips.summary: Simpson describes the totally segregated, economically depressed Delta of her childhood, a self-sufficient Black community of sharecroppers, day workers, teachers, preachers, and a few shop owners, families that had owned land until the 1920s or 1930s before losing it, and her own poor showing at chopping and picking cotton as a bug-fearing country girl.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: A Smokehouse Full of Hams
clips.topic: Father the Butcher and the Migration North
clips.summary: She remembers her butcher father killing neighbors' hogs in the fall for cuts of meat, the smokehouse hung with hams, her mother's homemade sausage and canned gardens, her much older siblings and an eighty-one-year-old brother in Detroit, and how the older boys, after working for the city of Greenwood, joined the Great Migration north to jobs in the auto industry.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: I Played the Rabbit in Winnie the Pooh
clips.summary: She recounts her childhood passion to become an actor, landing leading roles in plays in Mississippi, and the cultural shock of Racine, where a school of roughly ten percent Black students never cast her in the dramatic club's productions except as the rabbit in Winnie the Pooh, behind makeup that hid who she was.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: I Threw the Jet Magazine Across the Room
clips.topic: Emmett Till and Reverend George Lee
clips.summary: Simpson, about nine when Emmett Till was lynched in 1955, remembers screaming and throwing the Jet magazine across the room at the photograph of his body, the fear that gripped Black children, and the killing of the Reverend George Lee, a friend of her NAACP-member father, who appeared on the cover of a funeral newsletter the family later lost.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: No Immediate Impact From Brown
clips.summary: She reflects that the Brown decision had no immediate impact in her area beyond new separate-but-equal buildings rising without libraries or laboratories, and turns to the church as the center of family and community life, with Sunday services, BTU, and Wednesday Bible study, her father a Sunday school teacher whose lessons her mother drew out on butcher-block paper.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: A Beautiful Library and a Culture Shock
clips.summary: Simpson explains that her aging parents sent her at fourteen to live with her sister in Racine for a solid education, always meaning it as temporary, and describes the culture shock of Washington Park High School, where she interacted with white people for the first time, marveled at the library and athletic equipment, and played field hockey, soccer, and tennis.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Embarrassed to Be From Mississippi
clips.summary: She recalls being teased and embarrassed about being from Mississippi, called Bigfoot country, a stepsister named Ruth Days who became her protector and best friend, that Racine did not have the segregation she had known, and that the few summer jobs went to white kids, so the only work she could get was babysitting.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: I Found Exactly What I Was Looking For
clips.topic: The Mass Meeting and Her Father's Bargain
clips.summary: Simpson tells why she came back for her last semester at Amanda Elzy rather than graduate in Wisconsin, attending a mass meeting in Greenwood where freedom songs and young leaders amazed her, naming Hollis Watkins, Lawrence Guyot, John O'Neal, and Mary Lane and meeting her lifelong friend June Johnson, and how her reluctant father agreed to let her join full time only if she named someone in charge, then telephoned Lawrence Guyot himself, so that Guyot told her in the SNCC office her daddy had put him in charge of her.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Selected for Citizenship School
clips.topic: Annelle Ponder and the Trip South
clips.summary: Simpson describes coming to know Fannie Lou Hamer when Annelle Ponder of SCLC selected a group, including Hamer and June Johnson, to attend a two-week citizenship school in Charleston, South Carolina, and Ponder telling the group she would use the white waiting rooms on the way because the Interstate Commerce Commission had outlawed separate facilities, leaving it to each of them whether to follow.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: What It Meant to Be a Citizen
clips.summary: She recalls Myles Horton of Highlander, Septima Clark, Esau Jenkins, and Guy and Candie Carawan teaching the basics of citizenship and the right to vote, the duty to return and run workshops registering others, and how she carried that into door-to-door canvassing in Greenwood and her hometown of Itta Bena, where even her own parents, who had never registered, finally did because of her.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Car Loads of Highway Patrolmen
clips.summary: On the bus trip back from South Carolina, Simpson describes the group using the white waiting rooms without incident until they stopped at Winona, Mississippi, where highway patrolmen and local police grabbed those who got off to use the restroom, piled them into a car as Annelle Ponder wrote down license numbers, and arrested Fannie Lou Hamer when she got off the bus to ask what was happening.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: I Still Wasn't Afraid
clips.summary: She recounts Annelle Ponder, college-educated and soft-spoken, asking why they had been arrested and citing the Interstate Commerce Commission, the jailer ordering her to shut up and striking her when she refused to say sir, and the teenaged Simpson speaking up without fear until the jailer jabbed her with his ring of keys and stamped her foot, with Ponder urging her to say nothing more.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: You Are Black Like Me
clips.summary: Simpson describes the row of cells, how the jailer made two drunken trustees beat the prisoners one by one with a strap, hearing Annelle Ponder moan and return bloody, then June Johnson, who at fourteen looked older, and Fannie Lou Hamer screaming and asking the men why they would beat her when they were Black like her, before Simpson's own turn was cut short after a single blow and she later learned Lawrence Guyot had been arrested and badly beaten nearby.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Walk With Me, Lord
clips.topic: A Washcloth and a Gospel Song
clips.summary: She describes nursing the feverish, swollen, bruised Fannie Lou Hamer through the night in their shared cell with cold water on a washcloth, and how, when Hamer's pain eased, the two quietly sang for themselves, Hamer wanting her gospel songs rather than her signature This Little Light of Mine, settling on Walk With Me, which calmed her and which moves Simpson to tears in the telling.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: I've Got Some Sad News
clips.topic: Refusing to Be Moved, and Medgar Evers
clips.summary: Simpson recounts SNCC's lawyers reaching the jail so the beatings stopped, the group refusing to be moved at night for fear of being taken into the dark to disappear, and being held until around Wednesday when Andrew Young came to free them and first told them the sad news that Medgar Evers had been assassinated the night before, ambushed on his way home from a Jackson meeting, so the joy of release was overshadowed.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: A Watershed for Mrs. Hamer
clips.topic: A Turning Point and the Oxford Trial
clips.summary: She frames Winona as a watershed for Hamer, after which Hamer came into her own as a national figure who told the story at the next year's Democratic National Convention, and recounts the trial in Oxford where, barred from hearing other testimony, Simpson was asked only whether she was arrested and beaten and answered no because she took just one lash, given no chance to describe what she had heard and seen.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: A Freedom Vote to Prove a Point
clips.summary: Simpson explains the COFO Freedom Vote meant to disprove the claim that Black people did not want to vote, her work running mock voting workshops in Clarksdale and elsewhere drawing on her citizenship-school training, and Aaron Henry, the candidate for governor on the ticket, arranging for her to hold workshops at his church while she stayed at a local woman's home.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Bring Down the Children of Affluent People
clips.summary: She recounts the national attention paid to the white Yale and Stanford students who helped with the Freedom Vote, the heated SNCC and COFO discussions about bringing hundreds of Northern volunteers into Mississippi so the country would pay attention, the Oxford, Ohio orientation she left early when her mother took sick, and the workshops dramatizing the violence and nonviolent self-protection volunteers might need.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Manning the WATS Line
clips.topic: The Three Disappear and the COFO Office
clips.summary: Simpson recalls word arriving at the orientation that James Chaney, Michael Schwerner, and Andrew Goodman had disappeared and were understood to be dead, and her move to Jackson rather than college to staff the COFO office shared by SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and SCLC, learning to work the WATS line that had to be manned around the clock so volunteers in trouble anywhere in the state could call in and get word out.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Everyone Came Through Jackson
clips.summary: Simpson tells how everyone passed through the Jackson office that summer, including the man she would marry, Jesse Moore, father of her five children, with Bob Moses and Dave Dennis sharing leadership and friends like Gwen Gillon, Mary King, Casey Hayden, and Dona Richards, and remembers Worth Long, an oral historian who later helped build the Delta Blues Festival, amid the violence of the summer including the McGhee brothers beaten in Greenwood for integrating theaters.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: We Rode a Bus and Couldn't Go In
clips.summary: Simpson explains the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, formed because the state's all-white delegation excluded Black voters, and her own trip by bus to the Atlantic City convention not as an official delegate, where the group spent most of its time outside the convention center, sharing the few badges to let people slip in for a few minutes at a time.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: One of Us Is Tired
clips.summary: She describes hearing of Fannie Lou Hamer's televised testimony and President Johnson calling a press conference to get her off the air, the singing of freedom songs on the boardwalk, and the rift that opened over the offered two-seats-at-large compromise, which Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray rejected as unfair while many wanted to accept, leaving Simpson eager to get back to the field.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: A Man Who Spoke French
clips.summary: Simpson recounts deciding in September 1964 to enroll in college on a work-study program alongside Glenda Gillon, Hollis Watkins, MacArthur Cotton, and others, first hearing Tougaloo praised by a French-speaking recruiter at a career day at Amanda Elzy, and only later recalling that her great-grandfather, whom her mother called Professor Jeff Davis Pearson, had himself attended Tougaloo after Reconstruction.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: An Oasis for the Movement
clips.topic: Tougaloo's Reputation and a Hard Transition
clips.summary: She describes Tougaloo as a private school that was an oasis for the movement, known for turning out lawyers and doctors, which she reached when someone arranged scholarships for about ten movement veterans, and how hard it was for veterans who had been to the March on Washington and met Martin Luther King and Stokely Carmichael to settle into campus life among students chasing sororities, especially after she married early in 1965 and began having children.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Slacks and an Afro in Dr. Townsend's Class
clips.summary: She recalls the strict Dr. Naomi Townsend, in whose English class girls could not wear pants, calling Simpson out for arriving in slacks and questioning her afro, one of the few on campus in 1964, the little white Honda motorcycle that added to her movement notoriety, and Dr. Ernst Borinski, who taught her German and whose legendary forums brought internationally known visitors to the campus.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Trying to Go Back to School
clips.summary: Simpson recounts raising five children while repeatedly returning to school, Jackson State for nursing courses she abandoned, and finally Millsaps College around 1986, a predominantly white liberal arts school she found the most difficult she had attended yet where she did her best work, going full time while working full time and raising children before she graduated and held her own among younger students.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: A Legal Secretary for Civil Rights
clips.topic: Jobs and Grant Work After the Movement
clips.summary: She describes her early career, training a year in stenography through an MDTA Great Society program at Hinds Community College to become a legal secretary for the Lawyers Committee for Civil Rights Under Law, then Head Start through Friends of Children of Mississippi, a health clinic in Mendenhall, AmeriCorps programs at Delta State, her marriage to Les, and their work as community coaches for the Kellogg Foundation's Mid-South Delta Initiative across Mississippi, Louisiana, and Arkansas.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: From the Delta to Ghana
clips.summary: Simpson describes a side proposal that funded trips to Africa for community participants, including a mixed-age group to Ghana and a later one for younger people, among them Mrs. Ayers, the seventy-five-year-old widow of activist Jake Ayers of the Ayers v. Fordice case, and her last work doing social work for about three years helping older people displaced by Hurricane Katrina who had moved to the Jackson area navigate the system for health care, food, and other needs.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: You Have to Walk Into the Storm
clips.summary: Asked about the major changes the movement brought, Simpson names Black people gaining a sense of their own worth and reclaiming power they had given away, invokes a Bernice Reagon song about walking into the storm to reach change, and notes she now lives in a once-off-limits white neighborhood though Jackson's schools, drained by white flight, are nearly all Black again.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: The Power Structure Hasn't Changed
clips.summary: She argues that Black elected officials, including a Black mayor, school board, and city council in Jackson, have not visibly benefited the masses while the economic power structure stayed white-owned and businesses fled to Madison and Rankin counties, eroding the tax base and the schools, and laments the crime and the black-on-black killing that was almost unknown when she was a child, which she ties to drugs and gangs imported from the North.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Earl, Orlando, Omar, T, and Jessica
clips.summary: In the closing exchange Simpson describes her five children: Earl, who owns a construction business; Orlando, a fireman; Omar, an IT specialist returning to Jackson State for an engineering degree; T, who holds a doctorate and teaches at Ohio State with a book coming; and Jessica, a Michigan-trained attorney, before thanking the interviewer as the session ends.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The One-Floor Colored Hospital
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Richmond, Virginia, Evans Derrell Hopkins describes being born in 1954 at Winslow Hospital, a one-floor facility reserved for Danville's colored citizenry, his mother Margaret Swanson Hopkins and his father, who worked for Dan River Mills as its first black supervisor of the mail room and janitorial crew before starting one of the town's few black businesses, a landscaping operation run with a tractor from the family farm.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Old Jail by the A&P
clips.summary: Hopkins credits his older sister with leading him toward the Black Panther Party and the church with drawing him toward the movement, then reads from his book Life After Life, recalling himself as a small child at the segregated A&P beside the old city jail, where in 1963 police locked away hundreds of demonstrators while firemen turned hoses on the crowds and dogs were set on protesters.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Lighter Than My Father
clips.summary: Hopkins reflects on the inevitable confusion of a black child in 1963, taught that everything white was superior, and how colorism reached into his own family, his mother lighter than his darker father, himself darker than his sister, until he resolved to develop his mind and prove he was as good as his sister and, later, as good as the white students.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The House Moved Brick by Brick
clips.summary: Hopkins describes his maternal grandfather, perhaps the most prominent African American in Danville, who built the houses of the Bruce Street neighborhood with his sons around the turn of the century and, denied a brick house except on the white West Main Street, bought one there and reassembled it brick by brick in Bruce Street, giving the family a big house and a sense of privilege bounded by streets they could not safely walk.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: A Pistol Beneath the Robe
clips.summary: Calling Danville's civil rights history rugged, Hopkins reads further from his book about the 1963 mass demonstrations against segregated buses, stores, and the city library, the bloody protesters who staggered into High Street church for first aid, Judge Archibald Aiken who placed a pistol on the bench before each session, and the library that integrated only after the courts forced it.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Either Integrate or Not Go at All
clips.summary: Picking up after a short break to cover 1963 to 1969, Hopkins explains that with the nearest black county high school thirty-five miles away and no buses to reach it, his family decided the children would integrate the recently desegregated town high school, and recounts hating it there, harassed, cursed, jostled, and once attacked from behind and given a black eye, a racial bullying he later judged among the most important forces of his young life, feeding a need to resist that led him toward the movement.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The First Panther Paper
clips.summary: Hopkins describes a 1970 family trip to Washington where a cousin was selling Black Panther Party newspapers, his earlier involvement in cultural nationalism with dashikis and afros, and how the party offered not only racial but class solidarity, set against a backdrop of fear of Klan terror, closed drapes, and white men who might shoot through a window out of spite.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: We Wanted Our Own Identity
clips.summary: Hopkins recounts planning a walkout at the integrated high school after John M. Langston High School, named for a Reconstruction-era congressman, was downgraded to a middle school, the students wanting to shed the name of George Washington, whom they saw as a slaveowner, and to win black studies classes, organized through a community-worker group that sold Black Panther newspapers from Winston-Salem.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: A Career Begun on a Comical Footing
clips.summary: Hopkins tells how the planned walkout was leaked early, how about 150 students including some whites walked out, how he negotiated with the principal and was kicked out of school for three days with the other organizers, and how the police did not attack them, in an atmosphere charged by the Kent State killings spilling from college campuses into the high schools.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Budding Writer Reads
clips.summary: Hopkins describes feeling he had an inordinate gift for leadership and loving the philosophy of the party, dialectical materialism as a textbook, and the Panthers' sense of a kernel of truth about the world, while reading the autobiography of Malcolm X, H. Rap Brown, Eldridge Cleaver, and the prison letters of George Jackson, and recognizing the budding writer in himself.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Shotgun Apartments
clips.topic: The Authentic Culture of the Cadre
clips.summary: Hopkins recalls being a shy young man drawn to the Panthers' fervor and culture, learning to blow on the masses, and finding the Winston-Salem members not the bereted figures of the photographs but impoverished and dedicated people sleeping on mattresses in shotgun apartments, so named because a shotgun fired through the front door would pass straight out the back.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Survival Till Revolution
clips.summary: Hopkins tells of leaving Danville at seventeen to live with the party in Winston-Salem, recounts the party's 1966 founding by Huey Newton and Bobby Seale and its ten-point platform written in an anti-poverty office, and remembers serving in the free breakfast program, still feeling the heat of the griddles, in what he calls one of the happiest years of his life before Oakland.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Repression Breeds Resistance
clips.summary: Hopkins frames the police brutality and Hoover's COINTELPRO campaign that had killed nearly thirty Panthers by 1971, the year he joined just after Huey Newton returned from prison, and the split when Eldridge Cleaver and the New York chapter pushed armed struggle and attacks on the police to heighten the contradiction with the oppressor.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Put Away the Gun
clips.topic: Survival Programs and the Oakland Turn
clips.summary: Hopkins recounts Newton's argument to put away the gun and keep it only for self-defense, his own lingering romance with the ideal of armed struggle, the survival programs of free groceries, busing to prisons, and medical clinics offered as survival till revolution, and the decision to work within the system as Bobby Seale ran for mayor of Oakland, a campaign that drew most chapters there and left them dismantled.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Revolutionary War Correspondent
clips.summary: Hopkins describes the party's dismantling and the migration to Oakland that carried him there in August 1972 to become the revolutionary war correspondent he had dreamed of being since reading of Clark Kent, joining within months the editorial cadre of five or six writers on the party newspaper alongside layout artists led by Emory Douglas.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Son of Du Bois
clips.summary: Hopkins remembers David Du Bois, stepson of W.E.B. Du Bois and son of Shirley Graham Du Bois, who had edited an English-language daily in Cairo for twelve years, became editor-in-chief of the paper and Hopkins's mentor and friend, taught him the fundamentals of journalism and the sensibilities of a literary artist, and wrote a blurb for his book before dying; Hopkins appeals to researchers to help locate Du Bois's papers on the party.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: On the Bus With Bobby Seale
clips.summary: Hopkins describes serving as writer and sound man on a reel-to-reel recorder, covering Bobby Seale's 1972-73 run for mayor of Oakland and Elaine Brown's city council bid, appearing barely visible in a newspaper photo holding a microphone near Seale and in a photographer's book at eighteen on a bus with Seale, an image that ran in Jet, and feeling he could reach the masses through a paper read by thousands.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Feet of Clay
clips.summary: Hopkins reflects on forming his ideal of the heroic male and female through the party's strong women leaders, the disillusionment when the party fell apart and many heroes proved to have feet of clay, and his later reading on how the young mind keeps developing into the twenties, lending those years a bond reality in which they truly believed they could change the world.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: You Can Never Leave
clips.summary: Hopkins recalls a filmmaker shooting a documentary on the Seale campaign with the inventor of the Steadicam behind the camera, his first marijuana in California and the line from Hotel California about never being able to leave, and the positive illusion that let them believe they would take over Oakland until the campaign forced a runoff and they conceded the incumbent was too far ahead.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: He Drew the Best Out of Me
clips.topic: Du Bois and the Rhetoric of the Movement
clips.summary: Hopkins describes David Du Bois as a learned man with a British accent who had lived in China and Cairo, inspired him toward social science, and edited his words to draw out a sense of style, color, and a cinematic eye for the humanity in detail, and reflects on the story, allegory, and poetic phrasing of the movement's preaching, observing that listening to Martin Luther King is almost like hearing a Shakespearean use of rhetoric.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Physical Methods of Correction
clips.summary: Hopkins describes the party after the spring 1973 campaign as the Berlin Wall coming down, the loss of money for programs once the political donations stopped, Newton's inner core and security detail trying to hold power, and the physical methods of correction including the flogging of members that bred a totalitarian fear and began to unravel the fabric of the party.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Whose Ancestors Were Whipped
clips.topic: A Stolen Car and a Mass Exodus
clips.summary: Hopkins recounts the painful irony of an African American whose ancestors were whipped enduring the same inside the party, after he stole a car and fell in with an armed robber to raise money to bring his pregnant fiancee from Winston-Salem to California, then describes the central committee's authority being usurped, factions forming, and a mass exodus in which he happened to leave during the same weekend Huey Newton fled to Cuba after a 1977 pistol-whipping of his tailor and the shooting of a woman who later died.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Sisterhood Is Powerful
clips.summary: Picking up after a break, Hopkins explains the party's philosophical ideal that women were equal to men and that gender inequality was part of the white patriarchal system, recalls that women may have made up nearly half the party and held many leadership positions though men kept most authority, and observes that women in the civil rights movement carried a greater role than women in the larger society.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Brought It Home to Their Kids
clips.summary: Hopkins argues that black women were better educated and could withstand an educational system biased against black males, and that women who worked in white households gained cultural enrichment they brought home to develop their children and husbands, citing his own mother's college degree against his father's lack of secondary schooling as a source of equality of the sexes in the movement.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Comedian and the Theoretician
clips.summary: Hopkins gives his perspective on Bobby Seale, the highest leader he worked with directly, a stand-up comedian, jazz drummer, sheet-metal mechanic, and military veteran who brought organizational skills and drive to the party, contrasted with Huey Newton as the philosophical force and chief theoretician beloved for his brilliance and physical beauty.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Life of an Outlaw
clips.summary: Asked about the state's response to the Panthers and the expansion of incarceration, Hopkins recounts leaving Oakland so disillusioned and anti-everything that he wanted to live the life of an outlaw, robbed a bank and was sentenced to ten years, and after two years was returned to prison on a life sentence from an all-white Danville jury for a robbery in which no one was injured.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Standing Watch With a Shotgun
clips.summary: Hopkins recalls joining the party in Winston-Salem while it was under attack by police and white supremacists, the previous headquarters firebombed, shootouts in Winston-Salem and an actual gun battle in High Point because members were told to defend their headquarters, and how all Panthers stood guard duty at night with a shotgun, an attitude he is unsure how to connect to his later turn toward criminality.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: All Prisoners Are Political
clips.topic: The Drug War and Mass Incarceration
clips.summary: Hopkins describes a plank of the party platform holding that no black man got a fair trial so all should be freed as political prisoners, a position he does not fully share, and analyzes the rise of incarceration as driven by many causes, cultural, racial, and chiefly the drug war waged against people of color.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Bastards of the Party
clips.summary: Hopkins contends that in the absence of the Black Panther Party, as a filmmaker argued in a documentary called The Bastards of the Party, the attitude of resistance fractured into gang activity of Bloods against Crips, and that young people without a philosophy or an understanding of their history turn toward the hedonistic and tribal, glorifying guns with no understanding of what the guns were for.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The New Jim Crow
clips.topic: A History People Do Not Know
clips.summary: Hopkins frames incarceration as a degeneration the party never foresaw met by a larger community wanting to lock people up and throw away the key, cites a former New York official on white-collar criminals who steal more than common thieves, and laments that people do not know what the Black Panther Party, SNCC, Kwame Ture, or even the SDS were really about, mistaking them for hate-whitey movements.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: An Idol or a Hero
clips.summary: Hopkins distinguishes idols from heroes and laments a whitewashed history in which young people know no civil rights hero but Martin Luther King, recalls a Virginia native who helped found SNCC whose name he cannot remember, and argues for heritage enrichment and a sense of cultural justice beyond mere legal justice, teaching young people how to live rather than only training them for jobs.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Educate the Emotions
clips.summary: Hopkins calls for understanding the harmony of interests among peoples and the contributions of Africans, Chinese, Germans, and Russians to culture, criticizes a politics that frames the future as a race against China rather than cooperation, and warns that stories now titillate rather than educate the emotions while children are taught to believe the metaphysical and supernatural they see on screens.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Sons of Malcolm
clips.summary: Hopkins explains that the party was founded just after Malcolm X's assassination and was nearly called the Sons of Malcolm, that Malcolm was developing a more ecumenical, worldly view of working within the system, and that the Black Panther Party should be seen as an extension of his philosophy, taking up the intercommunal idea that the world was more a group of communities than nations.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Mind Is the Highest Form of Matter
clips.summary: Hopkins describes the party's legacy as political but also one of pride and courage that debunked the notion of black inferiority, teaching that power is the ability to define a phenomenon and make it act in a desired fashion and that the mind is the highest form of organized matter, a continuation of Malcolm combined with Martin whose spirit of activism he sees in the election of Barack Obama and in Egypt.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: The Power of Love
clips.summary: Hopkins names the power of love and an ideal of service to something larger than oneself as part of the party's legacy against the self-hate in the black community, ties the idea of human progress to economic as well as cultural justice, and argues that the attacks on the union and service-worker movements show how far the country has drifted from the hard-fought gains of the labor movement.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Maggie L. Walker's School
clips.summary: Hopkins argues that the Tea Party leads people to vote for tax cuts for the rich against their own interests in an almost idolatrous relationship rooted in race, then closes with the irony of a governor's school moved into the formerly segregated Maggie L. Walker High School, named for the turn-of-the-century black woman who founded a bank and a hundred-thousand-member benefit society, where a young white Republican spoke on television apparently unaware of Walker's history.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Poison Pen Letters
clips.summary: Hopkins describes the FBI counterintelligence program launched against a so-called new black messiah, used against Martin Luther King and against the party through poison-pen letters that split Cleaver from Seale and helped imprison Elmer Geronimo Pratt, recounts learning that an agent told Winston-Salem State he was a Panther sent to infiltrate, costing him a scholarship and pushing him toward Oakland, and closes on the sense that they were never given a chance, George Jackson's line that revolution is against the law, and a comparison of the state's reaction to peaceful protest before the session ends.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Born Into Sharecroppers in Pocahontas
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in Jackson, Mississippi, in December 2015 with John Bishop recording, Frankye Adams-Johnson opens by placing her birth in the small town of Pocahontas about fifteen minutes north of Jackson, the daughter of a separated couple, raised in a sharecropping family by her mother, grandparents, and great-grandparents until she was about twelve.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: From the Country to a Cramped Apartment
clips.summary: Adams-Johnson recounts her mother leaving sharecropping for domestic work in Jackson, eventually buying a home and running a mom-and-pop store, and her own depression at trading the freedom of roaming the woods as a self-described nature girl for a cramped apartment in the city.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Out of School for the Crops
clips.summary: She describes attending church schools in Pocahontas through fifth grade, then being bused to a district school in Clinton, and how being kept home to work the crops during planting and harvest seasons became her first realization of what it meant to be Black and poor and a sharecropper.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Walking Through the White Strip
clips.summary: She recalls walking through town and past a strip of white houses on the way to her church school, wondering as a child raised in a religious home whether God was white and could possibly like a Black girl, and dates her racial consciousness to 1954 and the murder of Emmett Till.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Stories Under the Quilting Frame
clips.summary: She explains how education was stressed in Black families, her blind great-grandmother having taught her to count before she started school, and how, being nosy, she absorbed the realities of race from women's talk under the raised quilting frame and from visiting her mother at the back door of a white household where food was passed out, a scene she likens to the film The Help.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: The Newest of the Black High Schools
clips.summary: Adams-Johnson describes transferring from junior high to Brinkley High, the newest of Jackson's Black high schools with top-of-the-line equipment and rich activities, and how her life in Jackson shifted with the arrival of the Freedom Rides about a year after the family moved there, around 1959.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: A Word I Was Clueless About
clips.topic: From Communism Lessons to the Mass Meetings
clips.summary: She recounts a government course warning students against communism to deter involvement, her ignorance of the word leading her to a god brother whose father was active in the NAACP, and the meetings at his house where she met Medgar Evers and, as a starry-eyed high school student around 1962 and 1963, was inspired by three women, Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: My First Act of Courage
clips.summary: She tells how her first act of courage came in 1963 through college students she was meeting, among them Pearlena Lewis, a North Jackson Youth Council leader who lived in her neighborhood and sat in at Woolworth's, and how her god brother Cleveland and an organizer helped mobilize high school students after the brutal attack on the sit-in.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: A Quarter Mile From the School
clips.summary: She recounts marching about three days after the Woolworth's attack in response to the beating of Anne Moody and John Salter, students walking out of the three Black high schools including Jim Hill to converge and march downtown, only to be surrounded within a quarter mile by trucks, garbage trucks, and police with guns in a scene she likens to the Twilight Zone.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Penned Up Like Livestock
clips.summary: She describes being held on the concrete floor of livestock pens at the fairgrounds, a place she and Medgar Evers likened to a concentration camp, surrounded at night by armed men while the boys formed a protective circle around the girls, and identifies her god brother as Cleveland Donald Jr., a youth leader and the second Black student after James Meredith to attend the University of Mississippi, and her most important connection to the movement.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: My Summer Job Was Marching
clips.summary: She recalls the summer of 1963 becoming her summer job of marching in protest, reporting daily to the Masonic Temple for her marching orders, and how Medgar Evers's assassination in June deepened her sense of the seriousness and danger of being a freedom fighter, a word that always stirred her lifelong love of freedom.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Hit Across the Back in the Paddy Wagon
clips.summary: She describes the emotionally charged march around Medgar Evers's funeral when she was jailed and struck across the back with a billy club after climbing down from a hot paddy wagon to retrieve her dropped writing pad, her fury at being hit by the first man ever to strike her, a white officer, and her struggle to suppress that anger and hold to nonviolent discipline, having once defied even her tyrant grandfather.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Medgar Was Like a Father
clips.topic: Jesse Harris, a Void, and the Dogs
clips.summary: She describes the nonviolence training the young people received with Jesse Harris set up as a leader after Medgar Evers's assassination left a void, COFO and SNCC working mostly in the Delta, how Medgar had been like a father always pleading that the young be gotten out of jail, and being in the middle of the funeral march where the conflict with police brought out the dogs and hoses she had seen on the news from Alabama.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: You Need to Stay Out of That Mess
clips.summary: She recounts slipping out to march while her mother worked, her mother and younger siblings among those swept up at the fairgrounds, and her mother's white employer driving her to retrieve Frankye's brother and threatening to personally beat him if he marched again, an incident that left brother and sister angry that their parents would not stand up.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: A Music Scholarship to Tougaloo
clips.topic: Arriving in 1964 With the SNCC Workers
clips.summary: She remembers the tear gas she recalls especially on Lynch Street, going to Tougaloo College in 1964 on a music scholarship as a pre-freshman during Freedom Summer, and meeting the full-time SNCC activists there, the challenge that older organizers withheld respect and kept young students off dangerous trips, the bond among six young women including Annie Devine's daughter, and Stokely Carmichael taking them to Greenwood for registration work.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Bringing White Friends to the House
clips.topic: Kicked Out and a Mother's Refusal
clips.summary: She tells how her immersion in the movement got her dismissed from the conservatively run pre-freshman program, the Brown University tutors who deepened her involvement, and how, taking her newfound white friends home under her mother's open-door tradition, her mother refused out of fear the whole family could be killed, while Frankye, in her first close intimacy with white people, refused to judge them all by the hatred of the man who hit her.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: The Quiet One of the Three
clips.summary: She portrays Annie Devine as the quietest and least known of the three fearless women, alongside Victoria Gray and Fannie Lou Hamer, who were always together organizing, and recalls trying to get Devine's daughter Barbara to talk and write about her mother.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Not Everyone at Tougaloo Was Progressive
clips.summary: She complicates Tougaloo's reputation as a civil rights hotbed by recalling the conservative pre-freshman director who expelled her for spending too much time with activists, the choir director who made her straighten her hair to perform, a student protest on the lawn of conservative president George A. Owens, and a later chance to process Owens's papers and find his rich collection of files on the movement she had lived as an activist.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: The Only Trip My Mother Allowed
clips.summary: She describes wanting to look like the SNCC women who traveled to Atlanta and Atlantic City, and the one trip her mother permitted, the March on Washington, riding one of about six buses out of Jackson with ministers and chaperones, her first trip outside Mississippi, where she sat by the monument with her feet in the water amid a sea of people.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: We Came From Washington, We're Not Taking This
clips.summary: She recounts that on the way home, fired up from Washington, the group integrated the white restroom and lunch counter at the Meridian bus stop, where young white men on motorcycles attacked and a whole-town mob, dressed in black and white though not in Klan uniforms, threw things, until the ministers got between them and the crowd and pleaded with them back onto the bus.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: A Different Kind of Anger
clips.summary: She describes the anger that built after Medgar Evers's assassination and the march on Washington, and the killing of Benjamin Brown around 1967 on Lynch Street, where the National Guard had been brought in, the training that the Guard would not shoot unless fired upon, young and angry marchers throwing things, the Guard opening fire as they ran into an alley, and Brown being killed that evening.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Longing for the Connection
clips.summary: She tells how her alarmed mother sent her to live with her older sister in suburban New York around 1967, where, disconnected from the movement, she began longing for it and attended meetings of a cultural nationalist women's group around Amiri Baraka's wife in White Plains, finding them too intellectual and semantic for her grassroots taste.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Black Leather on 125th Street
clips.summary: She describes meeting people in black leather on 125th Street and Seventh Avenue in Harlem around the spring of 1968 whose street-level language and presence drew her in, helping set up a Black Panther Party branch in White Plains with her sister, teaching at a daycare center, and starting a liberation school as she came to believe everyone was becoming politically conscious.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: They Called It Urban Renewal
clips.summary: She contrasts Mississippi segregation with the deep poverty she found in New York, the urban renewal that moved Black families from their homes into tenements and high-rises, including her sister's home, the conflict over starting a daycare center in their building, and her reassignment by the central committee to the South Bronx as officer of the day.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: You Can't Put the Panthers in One Bag
clips.summary: Asked about being a woman in the Panthers, she says New York men gave more respect to women's leadership and key positions while California men expected what they called socialistic sex, stresses it was never full equality given the chauvinism, and reflects that the party forgot its own families were part of the people, recounting marrying a Panther, the confrontations with law enforcement, and her four years underground in which she gave birth to her youngest daughter.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Free Breakfast and Roaches in Ears
clips.summary: She credits the Panthers with addressing real urban problems beyond civil rights, learning that children went hungry and got roaches in their ears, and names the free breakfast in schools program the Panthers first initiated, rounding up children without breakfast on the premise that hungry children cannot learn.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: A Guerrilla Woman and Mother Too
clips.summary: She describes the burden on Panther women raising children alone, the dangerous expectation, modeled on the Battle of Algiers, that a woman be a guerrilla and a mother at once, going on the lam with babies, and a male Panther's complaint that she was always bougie because she put her children first.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: A Decision Against My Better Judgment
clips.summary: She calls going underground a turning point and one of her greatest mistakes, made against her better judgment that she had children, set in the context of the 1971 split in the Black Panther Party that left women especially exposed, coming home to Mississippi to regroup and bringing COINTELPRO danger to her family, returning to the radical wing of the split party, and giving birth to her baby daughter underground in a safe area among white women.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Always Looking Over My Shoulder
clips.summary: She describes the life-threatening danger as people around her were killed or jailed, separating herself to survive, networking to build her own support system, getting her two older children back, and after four years exploring her options with a lawyer to clear the situation with a slap on the hand, living always afraid each day might be the day she was caught.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly
clips.summary: She tells how her husband's troubles extended her probation to five years, then turns to her message for young people: stand up for something and make a difference as the organized Panthers once gave her purpose, and study the movement without romanticism, learning the good, the bad, and the ugly and finding what works for themselves rather than blindly following, as the interview ends.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Born in the Heart of the Delta
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Library of Congress in December 2015, Freddie Greene Biddle opens with her birth in Greenwood, Mississippi, on February 15, 1945, a large Black family, a father who ran a house-painting business and had been involved with the NAACP, and a mother who taught school until marrying at twenty-nine and found her father's farm too quiet and dark after a Greenwood girlhood.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: A Horse, a Wagon, and a Lost Brother
clips.summary: She recounts her mother's only sibling killed at five when a tethered horse kicked him in the head in front of the house, leaving her mother an over-protected only child who went to Wesley Methodist Church and to Tougaloo for high school and college, and the rare standing of a Black family in Leflore County that owned its inherited home.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: He Did Not Want to Farm
clips.summary: Biddle describes her father, born in 1900, going to Alcorn and then a year at Tuskegee Institute to study shoemaking because he wanted to work for himself rather than farm, and the small pockets of land-owning Black families around his rural home where people lived off the land and helped one another.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: An Act of Faith in Social Security
clips.summary: She tells of grandparents dying around 1951 and 1952, an uncle who was one of two Black postmen in Greenwood, and her father's conviction, shared with friend Mr. Hilton, that paying into Social Security when no one around him had ever experienced it was an act of faith that would support him in old age.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Retired by the Movement
clips.summary: Biddle explains that the Social Security her father trusted since the 1930s proved its worth when the family's movement involvement dried up his painting work, forcing him to retire on it in 1964 at the very age he became eligible, an advantage she credits to having older parents who could withstand the economic pressure.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: On the Edge of the Black Community
clips.summary: She maps the 600 block of Howard Street where she grew up, separated from downtown Greenwood by a railroad track and bordering the white neighborhood close enough to hear white children at the park, and her father's grievance that his tax dollars bused county white children to better schools.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Hand-Me-Down Textbooks
clips.summary: Biddle recalls schools wholly segregated from 1951 through her 1963 graduation, textbooks shipped over from the white school once white children got new ones, and how after Brown Mississippi built high schools in small communities like Greenwood and later a county school, Amanda Elzy, for rural children.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: I Could Not Name a White Person
clips.summary: She describes a childhood of window shopping downtown only with parents, a corner store her father forbade, white people across the street picking up domestic workers and laundry, the white superintendent opening commencement, and the white football team brought to special bleachers, with so little contact that she could not name a single white person she grew up alongside.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: A Body Brought to Greenwood
clips.summary: She recalls a body brought to a Greenwood funeral home that none wanted to handle, her mother's stories of a person burned alive, and her father's warnings about a dry state's bootleggers who, to stay in business, fed information to the white downtown and would make up stories on people.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: North Greenwood and Shotgun Houses
clips.summary: Biddle contrasts wealthy old white families in North Greenwood tracing roots to the plantations, a sizable Jewish community, and Chinese families who ran small grocery stores in the Black community while their children attended the white school, against the shotgun houses on brick stacks with planks laid over open sewers and her own older frame home on Howard Street.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Antebellum Homes and GI Houses
clips.summary: Biddle contrasts the antebellum houses of North Greenwood with the ordinary white community's frame homes, and a small early-1960s housing development across from the high school where returning veterans could finally finance a four-room house, an improvement over crowded shotgun rentals whose wood deteriorated unmaintained.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Take Back the Application
clips.summary: She tells how her father had long worked with the NAACP and her parents registered to vote in the late 1950s without voting, and how in 1962 her brother Dwight, back from the Navy and wanting more than the education major Mississippi allowed Black students, applied to be the second Black student at Ole Miss, drawing the mayor's demand that he withdraw it.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: The Mississippi Free Press
clips.summary: Biddle recounts Dwight going to work for the Mississippi Free Press in Jackson and through it meeting Robert Parris Moses, Sam Block, Willie Peacock, Reggie Robinson, Medgar Evers, and Amzie Moore of Cleveland, and how Willie Peacock and Sam Block came into Greenwood in fall 1962 to hold mass meetings that opened with Hollis Watkins singing to move people toward registering to vote.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Dick Gregory Sent a Plane
clips.summary: Biddle explains that as cotton-picking machines arrived in 1962 and Leflore County cut off the surplus food, powdered milk and peanut butter that struggling people needed, her father saw it as a deliberate effort to starve people for registering to vote, and recalls Dick Gregory renting a plane and Ivanhoe Donaldson driving a truck to bring food, distributed from Wesley Methodist Church where she helped bag and box it.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: An Outside World
clips.summary: She describes the SNCC workers as an exposure to a world beyond Mississippi, Stokely Carmichael telling her of New York and Trinidad, in a town where the public library was closed to Black residents, and meeting Eleanor Holmes and Tim Jenkins as a high school senior, while naming local supporters like June Johnson's mother, the McGee family, and Aaron Johnson, whose First Christian Church the white community forced him to rename.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Shotgun Blast Through the Front Door
clips.summary: Biddle describes teachers too terrified of losing their jobs to take part, a March 1963 shotgun blast through her family's front door after George attended a SNCC meeting, schoolmates' parents who kept their children away for fear, and her own departure that September for Dillard University in New Orleans to study nursing, alongside cultural events like Pete Seeger and the Freedom Singers performing in Mississippi.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: My Brother in Parchman Farm
clips.summary: Biddle describes the Free Southern Theater touring Mississippi, and how hard it was to leave for college just after big Greenwood demonstrations where police dogs she could hear from her house arrested George and others on trespassing charges and sent them to Parchman Farm, and James Forman, a mentor she loved, urging her to go because school would let her do more when she returned.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Nobody at Dillard Cared
clips.summary: She recalls being at Dillard when the Birmingham church bombing killed the four girls, and after Medgar Evers and the shots into her own house it seemed nothing would stop, hard to study amid a campus with no student movement, until she connected with Casey Hayden and a Tulane group through the Newman Center under New Orleans's strict girls' rules.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: George Talks Dad Into McComb
clips.summary: Biddle tells how, hearing of the 1964 summer project from George and wanting to go somewhere other than Greenwood, she got her parents to agree she could work in McComb where George had a project, went to the Ohio orientation and on to Washington with the Southwest Mississippi group to ask congressional representatives for protection, and was there when word came that Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman had been kidnapped.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Half the House Was Taken Away
clips.summary: Biddle describes eleven people sharing a McComb freedom house split between freedom schools and canvassing, a staff meeting the night before in which everyone but George voted to bring down more volunteers, and the dynamite set at the garage door that shattered windows two blocks away and embedded glass in Curtis, sleeping nearby, as they woke to shouts of get down and crawled toward the half-gone house.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: A Minister Visits the Heffners
clips.summary: Biddle tells how George drove Curtis to Jackson to see a doctor and how walking the McComb streets to keep the community calm they saw people closing their doors in fear, and recounts a minister volunteer arranging a visit to the Heffner family, sending in the blonde Stanford student Dennis Sweeney to pass as kin, after which the Heffners were driven from town, while no white person spoke out though nine Black churches were burned.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: We Showed It Was Possible
clips.summary: She describes canvassing and holding meetings to select Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party delegates that short summer, going with them to the Atlantic City convention, her father's pride that they showed the country it was possible whether or not they were seated, Fannie Lou Hamer's speech reaching the whole world, and her own realization when the seats were refused that Mississippi acted as it did because the rest of the country did not care.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Education Was Not Enough
clips.summary: Biddle recounts her hardest year back at Dillard as classmates urged her to finish school and get a good job, her argument that people lacked education because the system was structured to deny it, and her decision at the end of her second year that she could not return to Dillard or to nursing because real change demanded decent housing, healthcare, and opportunity, not education alone.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: The Fairground Penitentiary
clips.summary: Biddle describes leaving Dillard to work on voter registration in Jackson, where 1965 demonstrations led authorities to turn the fairground into a penitentiary, jailing Black demonstrators on a concrete floor under a tent while white women went to the actual jail, and her role as a later arrival, going in with Janet Moses, to lift the morale of people held for weeks not knowing when they would be released.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Ruby Doris Said Learn Everything
clips.summary: Biddle tells of moving to the Atlanta SNCC office after jail, drawn partly by James Forman who mixed field and office staff and put her on the switchboard, and of working closely with Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, a Spelman graduate, Freedom Rider, and one of her heroes who could outwork anyone and urged her to learn as many things as she could, drawing her into finance though a Mississippi girl had never imagined a business career.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: When Black Power Came Out
clips.topic: Finance and the Money Drying Up
clips.summary: Biddle describes helping SNCC bookkeeper Chessie Johnson with finances and donations while George stayed in the field, canvassing in Lowndes County, Alabama, and recounts being in the Atlanta office as Stokely Carmichael raised the cry of Black Power on the Meredith March Against Fear in 1966, after which donations sharply fell and the media pressed SNCC about whether it meant a separate nation.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Problems Better Solved by Black Staff
clips.summary: Biddle gives the staff's reasoning that problems in the Black community would be better solved by Black staff while white staff could do more in the white community, the press inflating it, and notes she stayed on staff until June 1968 after King was killed in April, by which time the money was gone.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: You're Going to Be Sorry
clips.summary: She describes Stokely Carmichael, tall, dark, and personable, brought home around 1962 and a regular guest at her family's house whenever he was in Greenwood, telling her of his Howard students in NAG and teasing that she was making a mistake going to McComb because he would flood the Fourth Congressional District with his people, and how none of the college students could persuade her to leave the state.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: They Said They Were My Sisters
clips.summary: Biddle recalls meeting Joyce and Dorie Ann Ladner, also Mississippians, and a night when Stokely Carmichael and Dorie Ann Ladner, down for Mardi Gras, talked the Dillard housemother into letting her come down by claiming to be her sisters and brothers, leaving her overjoyed at the way movement people made connections.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Not for Personal Advancement
clips.summary: She closes on the movement's power to draw people from all walks of life, recalling that Robert Parris Moses, James Forman, and everyone she encountered tried to help others and to expose people to making a difference, none of them seeking personal advancement but setting aside their own possibilities to help somebody else.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: As Fortunate as a Child Could Be
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County in July 2011, Freeman A. Hrabowski III opens on being born in Birmingham in 1950 to older, educated parents who had met at Alabama State in Montgomery and married in 1940, with Sixth Avenue Baptist Church, sister church to the one later bombed, at the center of everything, and a father whose family had lived on plantation land where the first Freeman was the first born after slavery.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Three Jobs Each
clips.summary: He describes his father leaving teaching for the steel mill and the railroad, where he secretly did the reading and writing for white supervisors who had no high school education, and a household where both parents worked three jobs, his mother teaching by day, teaching GED at night, selling insurance, tutoring, and selling snowballs, in the Titusville middle-class neighborhood alongside Smithfield.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: You'd Hear Bach in Church
clips.summary: Hrabowski recounts a childhood spent always in church, in youth fellowship, choir, and Bible school, and the arrival of Pastor John T. Porter at thirty-two when he was twelve, an Alabama State and Morehouse man whose speaking he credits for his own, succeeding Reverend John Goodgame, with a pastor's wife who held graduate music degrees from NYU so that one heard Bach and piano lessons in a Southern Black church.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Polished by the Best of Whites
clips.summary: He explains the class range within the middle class, from a few lawyers and physicians to people who worked for wealthy whites and traveled to Europe with their employers, mostly teachers, and his parents who constantly filled out financial-aid forms, pushed families to send children to college, and served as marriage counselors, in a city where his Polish surname Hrabowski was locally pronounced robust.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: A Wall of Family Names
clips.summary: Hrabowski tells how families put up their mortgages to build the new Sixth Avenue church when the congregation moved to Titusville to make way for the University of Alabama at Birmingham, leaving a wall of their names, and how the Black newspaper, the Birmingham World, ran a front-page story that the city had more Negroes owning their homes than any city in the country, the home being the great symbol of status.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: You Don't Have Time to Be a Victim
clips.summary: He recalls never having met anyone white as a child and only learning how distinctly hierarchical the two groups were when he peeled back the brown-paper cover of a second-grade book and found the name of a white school on it, and the teacher's answer, the same one his parents gave, that the book may be second rate but he was first rate and had no time to be a victim.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Fired for Organizing Teachers
clips.summary: After a short break, Hrabowski describes a household where race and politics were discussed constantly, and his mother's pride at having been fired from a school system outside Birmingham in the late 1940s for trying to organize teachers to demand equal salaries, then hired into the plum Birmingham City system by Dr. C.R. Hayes, the director of Colored Schools.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: You Must Be Twice as Good
clips.summary: He recounts his father tutoring steel-mill men toward their GEDs and writing the Negro section of the company bulletin with advice on hard work and supporting families, and the central lesson both parents drove home, common among Black children in the Deep South, that the world is not fair so you must be twice as good, a philosophy of self-empowerment he still teaches his students.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Kiddieland We Could Not Enter
clips.summary: Hrabowski notes his parents were proud members of the NAACP and the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights, and describes his first impressions of segregation, the Kiddieland amusement park with its Ferris wheel that Black children, including his friend Condi, had to pass by, the movie-house balcony, the side doors of restaurants, and the filthy separate water fountain that told them in subtle and unsubtle ways they were a caste system.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Almost Born in the Classroom
clips.topic: A Precocious Boy and a Hidden Pregnancy
clips.summary: He describes being a precocious only child of older parents, reading and starting school very young and nearly finishing first grade at four, hidden in the girls' restroom whenever the VIP superintendent Dr. Hayes, his godfather, visited, and his mother, who had hidden her pregnancy with loose clothing because pregnant women could not keep teaching, joking he was almost born in the classroom and born to teach.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: The Best Liability You Ever Had
clips.summary: Hrabowski recalls being kept busy teaching slow kids at five, and how, knowing police were not fair, his light-skinned father who could nearly pass and drove and talked too fast would be addressed politely by officers as Mr. Freeman A. Hrabowski until they saw the boy's kinky hair and the son's name on the license, after which the courtesy turned to free when you're a color boy, prompting his father's joke that he was the best liability he ever had.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Bombs Next Door
clips.summary: He describes the February 1960 sit-ins as frightening because the targeted families, the Shuttlesworths and attorney Arthur Shores, were people his community knew in nearby Smithfield, so a bomb felt like it was next door, and how his church discussed ideas and books in youth fellowship led partly by Condoleezza Rice's father, developing critical thinking, until a principal forced to suspend demonstrating students had them read Thoreau's Civil Disobedience.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: What's This Guy's Name? King
clips.summary: Hrabowski tells how, sitting in the back of church doing math to be left alone, he was caught by a visiting man proposing that involving children in the march could let them attend the best schools in the city, which got his attention because he wanted to test how smart white children were, and how he asked the man's name and learned it was Reverend Martin Luther King, who was changing the vision from this is the way of the world to it does not have to be this way.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Mama, Dad, I've Got to Go
clips.summary: He recalls wanting to believe King without being convinced change would come, referencing a current Smithsonian exhibition by his UMBC colleague Maurice Berger on the visual culture that let America see the fire hoses and dogs on television, and how after seeing children abused he told his parents he had to go, called them hypocrites when they refused, and was sent to his room before they relented after a sleepless night.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: You're Our Treasure
clips.topic: A Frightened Volunteer Chosen to Lead
clips.summary: Hrabowski describes his parents praying with red eyes and explaining that they did not distrust him but the white people in the jail who did not care about Black children, telling him he was their treasure, his two-year-older cousin laughing that no dogs would bite him, and how, frightened but committed, the older boy was chosen to lead a group of children and keep them from reacting to police trained to provoke them.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Spat On at the Steps
clips.summary: He recounts the training meetings at Sixth Avenue and being bused to the other church, practicing songs like Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around that gave the marching children strength, and how, knees shaking, he led them to kneel and pray on the steps for first-class citizenship until a red-faced man asked what he wanted, spat in his face, and threw the children into the paddy wagon bound for City Hall.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Reading the Bible to the Bad Boys
clips.summary: Hrabowski describes five days in a juvenile facility among hardened boys, the teachers warned they would lose their jobs and families their homes if their children went to jail, so most of his fellow marchers came from the projects rather than the middle class, and how, a self-described fat math nerd unable to fight, he kept the bad boys back by reading aloud from the twenty-third Psalm, aided by one boy who had been in his mother's class.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: What You Do This Day
clips.topic: King at the Bars and Release
clips.summary: He reflects that even children can make decisions that shape their lives and know right from wrong, and recounts the gloom of the dark cells, Dr. King coming with the parents who sang outside while he told the children through the bars that what they did that day would have an impact on children for generations, and the rush of appreciating freedom when he was released on the fifth day.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Mr. Bell Tied His Tie
clips.summary: Back at Ullman High within a day, suspended and embarrassed, Hrabowski credits principal George C. Bell, the small mathematician with a booming voice who made him a mathematician and was the uncle of Alma Powell, and his counselor John W. Rice, father of Condoleezza Rice, recounting how Bell quietly arranged for suspended students to get homework at home until the federal courts ordered the children back to school.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Decency Among Whites
clips.topic: The Court Order and a Fragile Hope
clips.summary: He remembers the joy in church when news came, by a judge he believes was named Black, that the courts had ordered the children back to school, the relief of knowing some white person in power thought them not wrong, and the cautious hope of that September as the city fathers compromised with Dr. King, even though he had never seen a single Black person in any position of power downtown.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: That Hand and That Ring
clips.summary: Hrabowski recounts the Sunday in September when the pastor got a note at the pulpit, stopped preaching, and told them their sister church had been bombed, the first time anyone had seen church stop in the middle, and how he had said goodbye on Friday to fourteen-year-old Cynthia Wesley, who was killed, recalling the ring a father had given his daughter that morning, recognized on a severed hand, and nightmares that lasted years.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Life Is as Hard as Steel
clips.summary: He describes the three coffins, the smallest holding eleven-year-old Denise McNair, and Dr. King's eulogy answering Black voices who blamed the jailed children for the bombing by taking responsibility and telling the grieving parents that life is as hard as steel, and the hope Hrabowski drew from seeing, for the first time, rabbis, priests, and Baptist ministers among the white mourners.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: He Was Somebody's Child
clips.summary: After a break, Hrabowski reflects, citing David Brooks's The Social Animal, that we are products of our childhood experiences, and how his parents worked to move the children past bitterness with the lesson that hatred eats on the inside and you hate the act, not the person, recalling that when Bull Connor died his mother wept and said he was somebody's child, a product of how he was raised, an enlightening and cathartic moment.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Content of Character
clips.topic: A Worldview Built From the Pain
clips.summary: He explains that the experience taught him King's lesson to judge people by the content of their character rather than race, a view reinforced by working with students from a hundred and fifty countries, and frames his life's work as helping people think well and act with one another, on a campus that lets people say what they really think, arguing that exposure to all kinds of people produces tolerance and isolation breeds stereotyping.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Invisible in an All-White Class
clips.topic: A Summer in Massachusetts and Hampton
clips.summary: Hrabowski recounts a summer in Massachusetts at thirteen in an all-white eleventh-grade class whose rich, rigorous education he loved but where no teacher ever called on him, a feeling of being invisible he likens to Ralph Ellison's Invisible Man, and his enrollment in 1966 at Hampton Institute, chosen for its tie to Booker T. Washington, where caring white teachers in math and science first showed him many decent white people.
