clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: I'm Going to Marry Her One Day
clips.summary: He describes taking calculus at the University of Alabama at Birmingham after his freshman year and discovering that, though the best student in his segregated school, he was less prepared than Black students from Northern prep and integrated schools, including the better-prepared young woman with Latin whom he vowed to marry and did four years later, and learning that the white Alabama students were poorly prepared too, so everyone had suffered from segregation.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: A PhD Scientist for Mayor
clips.summary: Hrabowski describes watching Birmingham change through periodic visits and his home church, friends going to formerly white universities, and his pride when Richard Arrington Jr., a PhD scientist, became mayor, even as the influx of educated people drawn by the University of Alabama at Birmingham was matched by the arrival of the same Black-on-black violence he had assumed belonged only to Northern cities.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Teachers Who Did Not Want You There
clips.summary: He recounts the demographic shift as families moved to the suburbs and Birmingham city schools became increasingly Black, and explains that his mother kept him out of integrated schools like Ramsay and Phillips in the tenth grade because she did not trust teachers who did not want him there, recalling the violence and thrown objects that met his friends during desegregation and her conviction that unwelcoming teachers cannot give a child the best education.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: Why the Adverb?
clips.topic: From a Topology Grade to a Mission
clips.summary: Hrabowski tells how at the University of Illinois graduate school, with no Black faculty, a professor wrote you did surprisingly well on his A-minus topology test, prompting the English-minded student to ask why the adverb and to refuse, as in second grade, to be defined as a victim, and how, finding no predominantly white university that produced even ten Black science doctorates a year, he met the Jewish philanthropist Robert E. Meyerhoff and married their concerns into a program.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: It Does Not Have to Be This Way
clips.summary: He describes defining excelling as earning PhDs and MD-PhDs from the best universities, and the program since 1989 producing hundreds of such graduates now on the medical faculties of Harvard, Stanford, Duke, Hopkins, and Michigan, moving beyond the lone exception of a Tuskegee or George Washington Carver, and connects the achievement to the childhood lesson that just because it is this way does not mean it has to be, the same boy who could not sit with whites becoming president of a largely white university.

clips.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
clips.title: What the Movement Was Really About
clips.summary: Closing, Hrabowski contrasts the roughly two percent of African Americans who had completed college in 1963 with the seventeen to twenty percent today, and argues the civil rights movement was about opportunity but also about families and communities that focus on education, values, and dreams, warning that without that focus the underclass persists, that race still matters alongside poor white children, and that teaching every child to read well decides whether the future is bright.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: In the Home of the Jenkins Family
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier opens the Civil Rights History Project interview in Bogalusa, Louisiana, on May 28, 2011, with curator Elaine Nichols, filmmaker John Bishop, and Dr. Rickey Hill present, and after a recording restart introduces the participants: Gayle Jenkins's son Willie Elliott Jenkins known as Chuck, her sister Geraldine Crawford Bennett, and her daughter Willie Exposé Breaux known as Toni.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: My Sister Was Born a Go-Getter
clips.summary: Geraldine Bennett describes segregated Bogalusa and the white clinic where she worked as maid and her sister Gayle cooked, a born leader whose drive to send their two daughters to college pushed them into the movement, and Toni adds how Gayle refused a politician's money and was shaped by the story of her own grandmother's white father, Mr. Smith, who would not give the light-skinned girl a nickel in Poplas Quarters.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Cooking Breakfast for CORE
clips.summary: Toni remembers the saddest part of the 1960s movement, being sent to relatives like her Aunt Sis because CORE workers and the Klan kept people guarding the house, going by day to cook grits for CORE, and her quiet stepfather Monroe Jenkins, a mill worker who joked he could not fetch Gayle from jail because he was watching the game.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: We Want Our Freedom
clips.summary: Toni describes her 1965 senior year at the all-black Central Memorial High School, when the students marched off campus singing We Shall Overcome to the Freedom Hall on Sullivan Drive against Principal J.C. Crump's warning, and her brother Willie E. Jenkins becoming the named plaintiff in Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: The Backbone of the Movement
clips.topic: A Domineering Mother and Forgotten Hero
clips.summary: Toni recalls the white and black proms, her domineering mother overruling Grambling to send her to Dillard for nursing though she became a teacher, pressing hair for home customers and earning a diamond ring for never needing to be asked, and calls her mother the forgotten hero and backbone of the movement who founded the Voters League with A.Z. Young, R.T. Young, and Bob Hicks but, unlike the men who won promotions, took nothing for herself.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: You're Going to Get Your Freedom
clips.summary: Chuck remembers his sister pulling him from seventh grade for his first march and bar counting, his mother whom he thought mean ordering him to file the school-integration suit, and telling her he hated all white folks until she answered that her own mother was white, while he caught hell from the black community for attending the white school.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Certified but Not Hired
clips.topic: Her Nursing Denied and Her Businesses
clips.summary: Geraldine explains that Gayle, repeatedly rejected and jailed, finally earned her nursing degree in New Orleans only to be passed over for a Bogalusa job and to commute her whole career to Covington twenty-eight miles away, and the family lists her enterprises, the hair work, rent houses, a store, and a snowball stand the children had to run, contrasting the chance-taking mother with calm Monroe, who met the dark house and unpaid light bill with the line, try paying the bill.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: That's What Brought Gayle In
clips.summary: Geraldine and Toni tell how Monroe, brave after a Friday-night nip, put his pocketknife to a Klansman's neck to stop CORE workers being killed on Fourth Street, drawing death threats that brought the Deacons for Defense to escort him to the mill and led Gayle to take over once Monroe sobered up and grew nervous.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Sitting Outside Crying to Get Out of Jail
clips.summary: Asked to situate the houses, Geraldine recalls a jail incident with a hosepipe hooked to hot water and Chuck crying outside because they had put him out of jail, then maps black Bogalusa, Poplas Quarters, Richardson Town, the Bogue projects and River Terrace, against the white neighborhoods divided by the railroad tracks, and the bare white Freedom House on Sullivan Drive.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: A Man Ought to Be Up Front
clips.summary: Toni recalls the January 1965 leadership change as Andrew Moses left the Voters League amid charges he was an Uncle Tom and Gayle asked A.Z. Young to take over, and explains her mother's belief, rooted in scripture and a church upbringing, that a man should lead, alongside her sense that the white power structure would always promote a black woman before a black man.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Name the Dog Deacon
clips.summary: Chuck remembers the Deacons for Defense, Charles and Roy Sims, Sam Barnes, and his grandfather Bill Crawford, sleeping armed atop the houses and ringing the women in the middle of marches, a night car chase when his father shot back through the mirror, and how fear of the Deacons stopped white vendors, one watermelon seller having his load burst melon by melon.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: You're Going to Meet Your Mammy Today
clips.summary: Chuck notes Monroe bought the store partly to move out a white neighbor who fancied Toni, and Toni describes her mother as secretary-treasurer of the Voters League quietly directing presidents A.Z. Young and Bob Hicks and serving twenty-seven years on the school board, where she once met a Genco family confrontation by pulling a .38 from her purse and laying it on the table, a measure of the blunt, profane fearlessness she brought to board meetings.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: The Reason I Don't Tell You Is I Show You
clips.summary: Toni and Geraldine describe Gayle's kindness toward poor people of any race, taking in her stepchildren, Chuck's friend, and Rickey Hill's sister Gilda, and the long ache that her mother never said she loved her until, answering Toni's letter, she explained that she showed it every day, a love Geraldine likened to fighting a bear for her children.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: You Develop a Tunnel Vision
clips.summary: Chuck recalls a march down Columbia Road when a white man named Applewhite swung a crowbar and the police ran him down, the crowd briefly fleeing back to Freedom Hall before marching out again through a thousand whites, Hattie Mae Hill's dress sprayed up by a hosepipe, and the tunnel vision of a thirteen-year-old who feared his mother more than the street.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Have You Ever Heard That Klansman
clips.summary: Geraldine and the family recount the march toward Baton Rouge that left Bogalusa with twenty-five or thirty people, harassed every step while John Doar was sent down, and swelled to five thousand by the city, and the day outside Satsuma when troopers could not protect a march of about eighty-six from thousands of whites; the night before, Don Duane Exposé and others had taken razors from barbershops, and as whites jumped the cars the marchers slashed them, astonishing the Klan who had thought them unarmed.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: The Bigger You Are, the Harder You Fall
clips.summary: Toni and Chuck describe Don as humbuggish and a lover of trouble who led the first school walkout, recalling Gayle taking his gun from a confrontation, his refusal to let anyone speak ill of his mother and sister, ordering Gayle out of his house, and the time he shot the antenna off a car of whites chasing him on Columbia Street and told his mother he had only fired in the air.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Niggers Invading the Schools
clips.summary: Chuck recounts integrating the school after Principal Flemings called it an invasion on the radio, being struck with a crutch in the hallway, returning with a borrowed pocketknife to beat the boy who hit him, and the principal warning him only to keep quiet about the knife, on the day the Klansmen surrounded the school and pulled a gun on A.Z. Young.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Figuring Out How to Survive
clips.summary: Chuck describes the constant racial taunting from coaches and classmates, Coach Volk proposing they let the boys fight it out, beating Bob Thomas so badly the offer was withdrawn, and a high school spent in survival mode, never studying, only working out how to make it through the next day after a real education in the all-black grade school.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Why Is Your Name Exposé
clips.topic: Barred From a Job at Home
clips.summary: Toni explains the cost of loving Bogalusa yet being kept from working there, teaching in New Orleans and Picayune, Superintendent M.J. Israel demanding to know why her name was Exposé and her mother's Jenkins, and her years of denied principalships while less-qualified people received emergency certifications to keep the jobs from her.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Don't You Ever Tell Me to Sit Down
clips.summary: Toni recounts Applewhite, from a Klan family, vowing she would have nothing, her defense of the first black superintendent he degraded, her demand that he never again tell her to sit down at a board meeting, the blackballing that followed, and how her mother's voting influence finally won her a behavior-discipline job as Applewhite lost his last race.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: We Can Go to Any Restaurant Now
clips.summary: Toni and Geraldine weigh what changed, integrated communities like Founders Drive, open restaurants, friendships across race, against Geraldine's own story of working twenty years for twenty dollars a week, raising six children, earning her RN, being barred from every Bogalusa hospital, and commuting twenty-eight miles to Covington for her whole career.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: A Common Goal and a Common Enemy
clips.summary: The family remembers being raised as one household with Aunt Jack, whose movement-bought Cadillac and house in the white community marked real change, the State and Ritz theaters that seated blacks upstairs, and Chuck's reflection that a common enemy once kept black neighbors from killing one another, though the churches stayed segregated then as now.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: He Was at the House All the Time
clips.summary: Asked about the police force, still brutal after a federal judge enjoined it in Hicks v. Knight, Toni recalls attorney Dick Sobol counseling the leaders at the house, the book Crossing Border Street about Gayle and Bob Hicks, and the young black New Orleans law firm of Robert Collins, Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie tied to CORE, one of whom offered Chuck a place to stay and a Tulane scholarship he turned down.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Taken Out in a Casket
clips.summary: The family recalls national figures passing through Bogalusa, and the night James Farmer, threatened with death, was smuggled out of town from Plaquemine in a casket through Richmond's funeral home, and how bringing celebrities drew the Klan from Poplarville and Franklinton, making the marches worse, while Gayle warned City Hall they would march permit or no permit.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Worry About Your Own Lights
clips.summary: The family describes lining up to be loaded onto the paddy wagons and buses that hauled marchers to jail, and Dick Gregory speaking at the park by Sweetville Baptist Church when the lights went out, telling the crowd to worry about their own lights and telling the surrounding police they were as oppressed as the marchers and needed freedom too.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: My Mama Told Me Don't Play With No Nigger Doll
clips.summary: Toni shares her hope that people will look past skin color, the kindergarten white girl who refused a black doll and insisted Toni was white like her, and the story of Chuck's son Cal marrying a white girl over Gayle's furious objection and Corvette bribe, until a cousin reminded Gayle she had marched for equal rights, drawing the retort that she had not marched for that.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Opportunity Was the Name of the Game
clips.summary: Toni frames education and opportunity as her mother's true goal, contrasts her own rich all-black high school years as school queen with Chuck's lonely integrated experience, and notes that whites now bus their children to Franklinton or private schools while the once all-white band and Paper Dolls dance team have become almost entirely black.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: I Love You Because You're a Racist
clips.summary: As the interview winds down the family disagrees over a street to be named for Gayle, Chuck preferring roses given while one lives, and Toni tells of Senator Ben Nevers, a racist board colleague Gayle walked the streets to defeat who returned on his knees to win her support then demanded a federal investigation of Chuck, of candidates coming through her chair, and of Gayle telling the openly racist principal Mr. Creel she loved him for letting her know where he stood, insisting that without the backbone, integration would never have come to Bogalusa.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: What About My Sisters
clips.summary: The family tells how Governor John McKeithen offered Gayle a fifty-thousand-dollar job to leave the movement, and how she turned it into a demand that every black woman in Bogalusa be given work, refusing the money, and Toni gives thanks for the chance to reflect on a mother she had feared was forgotten.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: It Was the Last Thing in Her Will
clips.summary: Answering Elaine Nichols, the family says Gayle was proudest of her children, who learned of her love only from her will, and Chuck recalls her freeing him from the racist deputy Vertrees Adams over a motorcycle, while Toni recounts her mother loving her people and wanting everyone equal, still political as she retired from the school board before dying that December.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: She Burned It All in the Backyard
clips.summary: The family closes with Gayle's indifference to appearance, the day her wig sat backwards at the board office, her wish only to help people live in harmony with education first, her pride in Toni's daughter as the family's first college graduate, and the day near the end when she burned all her movement papers, court orders, and photographs in the backyard while Richard Sobol was recording a book on her life.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: The Place of the Willow
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in El Monte, California, on June 26, 2016, with Guha Shankar and John Bishop present, Gloria Arellanes introduces herself as born in 1946 and a lifelong resident, and identifies herself as a Tongva elder whose ancestors held this land, the village of Houtgna, the place of the Willow, before development erased what was natural there.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: We Have to Take This House Back
clips.summary: Arellanes recounts how her fair-skinned father bought this El Monte house on his GI benefits, and how the sellers, having dealt only with him, tried to rescind the sale on the closing day when her dark, native-looking Tongva mother arrived to sign, until her father threatened to sue and kept the home, a refusal she learned decades later stemmed from rules barring sales to Mexicans and Asians.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: No, Dad, I'm American
clips.summary: Arellanes describes the racism she met in an Anglo El Monte, the high school race riots where only Chicanos were arrested and students were mocked for their bean burritos, and the confusion of a father who insisted she was Chicano against a mother who, shown a cousin's genealogy of Indian vaqueros, told her never to call herself Indian and to say only that she was Mexican.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: History Said We No Longer Exist
clips.summary: Arellanes explains the cultural trauma her mother carried, how the Spaniards forced Tongva women and children into the missions, stripped away their language, dress, dances, and ceremonies, and how mass disease left thousands buried in unmarked graves, and counters the claim that the people went extinct by describing a growing tribe reclaiming regalia and recovering language from John Peabody Harrington's recordings.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: He Wouldn't Let Us Speak Spanish
clips.topic: Her Father and the Musician Family
clips.summary: Arellanes contrasts her conservative, hard-to-reach father, a businessman and Mason who clashed with her ideologically yet labeled her Chicano, with her opposite, best-friend mother, and recalls his refusal to let his children speak Spanish for fear of an accent, alongside his family of traveling musicians from Chihuahua and the pipe organ in her grandmother's East LA house that she would sneak in to play.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: It Just Makes You a Fighter
clips.topic: High School Organizing and Becoming Tough
clips.summary: Arellanes traces her first activism to high school, where racism drew students together for protection, a parent offered her home for a youth council, and counselor Jack Barton let them hold meetings and defuse the race riots by talking with the surfers and reaching younger kids at the junior highs, and she describes how her size, near three hundred pounds at five foot eight, and years of bullying made her a fighter ready for the movement.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: A Coffee House in the Dark
clips.summary: After the recording resumes, Arellanes lists her early jobs, a youth employment project, a satin-ornament factory, and the East LA anti-poverty program NAPP, and tells how, while cruising Whittier Boulevard, she was invited to the La Piranya Coffee House on Olympic Boulevard, where people sat in the dark as a sheriff's floodlights swept the windows, a young community action group then forming the Brown Berets.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: The Birthplace of the Chicano Movement
clips.summary: Arellanes describes the passions she developed hearing the Brown Berets speak of police brutality, barred college access, poor jobs, bad housing, and unmet health care, her decision to join after three visits and leave East LA College for life experience, her view of La Piranya as the birthplace of the Chicano movement, and the training to never argue, recalling a man who crumpled her flyer and threw it at her feet.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: They Welcomed Us With Open Arms
clips.topic: Allied Movements and the Free Clinic
clips.summary: Arellanes recalls watching other movements and groups form, UMAS in the colleges and outfits like Lucha and La Junta, the infighting she tried to rise above by getting along with everyone, and the cross-racial ties of the Brown Berets, attending the US Organization's Sunday gatherings of Swahili and regalia, links to the Black Panthers, a Watts-era non-aggression pact, and the free clinic David Sanchez opened with Anglo and Jewish physicians for community action.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: They Didn't Hire Black Teachers in Philly
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline for the Southern Oral History Program on April 14, 2013, Gloria Claudette Grinnell opens with her grandparents, teachers who moved from Virginia to Philadelphia where no one would hire black teachers, her grandfather losing five hundred dollars in the stock-market crash, and her own birth in Washington, D.C. before her parents divorced and her mother took her to San Francisco.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: I Hope the Nun Doesn't Slap Me
clips.summary: Grinnell recalls growing up in the Western Addition and the Presidio, attending St. Dominic's at Pine and Steiner as the only black child, where a nun slapped her during morning prayers and marked all her papers with F's until her mother confronted the teacher, and remembers a white boy refusing to hold her hand and the subtler injuries around her: a teacher reduced to substituting after a dishonorable discharge for refusing to fight in World War II, and her mother caught in a union-and-job catch-22.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: I Couldn't Get a Banana Split
clips.summary: Grinnell tells of arriving in Virginia around 1959 and sitting down at a soda fountain to order an elaborate banana split, only to be refused service because she was black, contrasts the overt Southern segregation with the covert California kind, and explains her mother sent her to Virginia Union though she felt she should have gone to Howard in a city.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Charles Sherrod and the Student Meetings
clips.summary: She remembers her favorite instructor Dr. Bob Johnson, who held two doctorates and told nervous jokes about being killed driving a white woman home, and recalls student leaders organizing meetings, naming Charles Sherrod, though she cannot recall the substance of the meetings.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Her Father Was Fired and They Moved
clips.summary: Grinnell describes meeting a fellow sit-in participant at the fiftieth reunion who wept telling how her father was fired and the family had to leave the state, making Grinnell realize how naive she had been to resent Virginia students who did not join, since a sit-in could cost their parents everything.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Am I in Hell
clips.summary: She recounts being arrested and put in a smelly cell, then seeing the courtroom itself segregated and a drunken man brought before the judge, feeling she had landed in hell, and reflects that laws can change but people change only individually, as she committed to nonviolence though she might have retaliated had she been a man.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Thirty-Four Students at Thalhimer's
clips.summary: Grinnell describes the thirty-four students sitting in at Thalhimer's department store, where she stood out at over six feet tall in high heels and was refused service in a relatively calm protest, and recalls the jovial mood of walking from school downtown, learning black songs and the black national anthem, and being frightened at an Adam Clayton Powell meeting by a clapping, foot-stamping congregation.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: I Was Getting the Hell Out of There
clips.summary: She explains how her understanding deepened, that black residents had to live there while whites went along with the status quo, but admits she disliked Virginia and was too immature to appreciate it, then recounts leaving for D.C. and a boyfriend before her mother coaxed her back to California, where she spent two restless years partying before moving to Los Angeles.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: It Did Hurt Me Job-Wise
clips.summary: Grinnell describes her career as a California expulsion counselor under Assembly Bill 922 and teaching career psychology to graduate students, then recounts how her sit-in arrest record cost her jobs, a D.C. employer hinting she would not be happy and Los Angeles Unified repeatedly failing her on training and experience until a contract reached her by what she believes was a mistake or a guardian angel.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: It Hurts to This Day
clips.summary: She explains she rarely discusses the sit-ins because she suppressed how uncomfortable they were and it still hurts, partly because attitudes have not changed and black children today are not going to school or voting, and notes that Obama's presidency brought ugly feelings to the surface, recalling her brother who worked behind Henry Kissinger insisting the racial fights were nonsense beside real world problems.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Because His Parents Had Money
clips.summary: Grinnell recounts writing a three-page letter of protest after a white student deeply involved in drugs was spared expulsion because his parents had money and he was college-bound, while inner-city students who did the same or less were expelled, and reaffirms her elation at Obama's election because he was intelligent and would serve everyone.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Why Are You Only Busing the Black Kids
clips.summary: She describes serving on a committee during the busing era and being silenced when she asked why only black children were bused, recalls her View Park neighborhood with the top-ranked elementary school full of educators' and professionals' children whose parents refused to bus them, and notes the district pulled every other grade out anyway.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: A Black Person at the End of the Chapter
clips.summary: Grinnell tells how she sent her children to the French school and later public schools like Palisades Charter, declined to prejudice them as her own mother had declined to prejudice her, and recounts serving on a Los Angeles Unified book committee where she objected that a black person was tacked onto the end of a chapter only because the page was torn out for books sent south.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: What Is the Civil Rights Movement to You
clips.summary: Asked what the civil rights movement means to her, Grinnell says young people in Los Angeles care only about brand names, cars, and sports rather than civil rights, and recounts keeping her Los Angeles house in case anyone burned a cross on her Ohio lawn, prompted by a report of a black girl's locker defaced with a slur in Ohio, an overt act that did not surprise her.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: We Have Code Words Now
clips.summary: She describes life in her small town with its expensive boutiques, first-run theaters, horse trails, and private rather than ghetto helicopters, and how counselor home visits taught her to read neighborhoods, then argues the more things change the more they stay the same, racism now covert with code words like a neighbor twice noting she was from a southern state, and that her generation of black professionals, now in their seventies, were funneled into education, social work, ministry, and the post office because those were the only jobs open.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: They Weren't Backwards, I Was Backwards
clips.summary: She recalls a fine graduate experience at USC with few black students and no felt racism, then reflects that her Virginia experience taught her to respect and appreciate different cultures, admitting that at the time she judged Richmond people as backwards when the backwardness was her own, and that the city looked unchanged at the reunion.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: You Need to March for Better Jobs
clips.summary: Grinnell recounts an older black woman at the time asking why the students marched for lunch counters instead of better jobs, a question she dismissed as silly then but came to see differently, notes she graduated in 1962, and recalls being present in Washington for the March on Washington and following the movement while her brother kept her informed of White House strategy meetings.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Let's Take It All the Way Up
clips.summary: She says she was extremely happy about the civil rights and voting rights acts, arguing that education must come first so people change their thinking and so they vote despite stumbling blocks put in the way, and reacts to the recent challenge to the Voting Rights Act by saying to take it all the way up and see what happens, like her uncle and aunt who loved a good legal fight.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: My Uncle Called Me Screwball
clips.summary: Grinnell recounts how her uncle, who called her screwball, directed her to attorney Martin A. Martin who handled the case, and how her aunt called approvingly from New York, with no one in the family fearful for her, and reflects that her family prepared her without knowing it, passing on values covertly and overtly as her mother's saying held that the squeaky wheel gets attention.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: He Was My Boyfriend
clips.summary: She tells how she reconnected with Charles Sherrod, her college boyfriend and a Baptist minister, after seeing him on a black history broadcast, recounts white parishioners laughing and moving away from her in a Richmond Catholic church until she stopped attending, and remembers going with Sherrod when he interned preaching at emotional country churches that sang with a beat.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: I'm Not Too Sure We're That Far From the Past
clips.summary: Grinnell explains the project moved her to tears because she is unsure how far the present is from the past, having gone online to read the many insults hurled at Obama, weeping over black and white children alike not voting or attending school amid rampant drugs, and admitting she avoids reflecting because thinking of her uncle and aunt's struggles keeps her awake at night.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Call Hugo, I'm Going to Get Arrested
clips.topic: Her Aunt Integrates the Bus Line
clips.summary: She recounts how her aunt integrated a Greyhound bus line in Virginia despite the courts having already ruled, insisting on taking the bus, sitting in the white section, getting off to be arrested, and telling her sister to call uncle Hugo and Adam Clayton Powell, illustrating how much testing the law still required even after it was on the books.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: The Negro Motorist Green Book
clips.topic: Navigating Where Black People Could Go
clips.summary: Grinnell says she still knows which California cities to avoid and trusts her gut over intellect, recalls the Negro Motorist Green Book and how her parents stopped at black homes to find lodging traveling south, where someone would direct them to a Mrs. Jones with a room, and notes that integration shuttered many black businesses, including Peg Leg Bates's upstate New York resort.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Capitalize the Word Black
clips.summary: Grinnell describes what the black community lost to integration, including children who do not know who they are, and recounts writing the Los Angeles Times and other papers arguing that black should be capitalized because, unlike generic white, it names specifically an American of African descent, and protesting a local paper that called two black women fraudulent with no description, endangering her downtown.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: I Guess It Was a Big Deal
clips.summary: She tells of two white men helping her after she fell from her bicycle and hit her head, taking it as a sign she moves on from slights, then closes by saying she only later understood the Virginia Union sit-in as historic, realizing it when a young librarian asked Dr. Grinnell whether she knew about the civil rights movement and she answered that she had been in it, before insisting the interviewers eat the pound cake she baked.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Too Shy for the Press
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the Civil Rights History Project interview in New York City with Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge), who recalls enrolling at Howard around age sixteen where cousins had gone before her, being thought shy her first year away from a small town, and studying under radical minds like the sociologist E. Franklin Frazier and Rayford W. Logan.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: One Voice to Speak Through
clips.summary: Richardson describes her first protests at Howard, against the university's curfew rules for women and picketing Woolworths around her junior year, and how, the self-described shy student, she ended up at a press conference only because the others wanted one voice through which to speak, crediting Frazier's attacks on the Black bourgeoisie for shaping her view.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: The Third Black Man on the Council
clips.summary: She situates her family as in effect the first Black family in Cambridge, Maryland, with her grandfather the third Black man on the City Council, reputedly wealthy, who gave money and land to the church, while her grandmother distributed Red Cross food and clothing to poor and seasonal workers from their house, and most relatives lived within a block and were raised to come back and help the town.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: They Brought the Meal Out to Him
clips.summary: Richardson describes a totally segregated Cambridge with one reserved maternity room in the hospital and a single Black ward, her grandfather consulted on credit and on getting people out of the Maryland penitentiary yet served his council banquet meal apart because he could not join the white members, and her late realization, reading The Help, of how deep such separations ran.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Doctors Without Privileges
clips.topic: Health Care and Her Father's Store
clips.summary: She recounts that Black doctors, including her cousin Carroll, lacked hospital privileges so the family traveled to Provident Hospital in Baltimore, distrusting Johns Hopkins as a place that used Black patients as guinea pigs, tells of her father, a druggist who worked until midnight before a heart attack in his fifties took him, and describes taking over the drugstore after his death while struggling to find a Black pharmacist and sending her then husband to Howard's pharmacy school.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Cookies on Saturdays
clips.summary: She tells how SNCC field workers and Freedom Riders had been meeting at her uncle's house for nearly a month in late 1961 before she knew, how she let her daughters and other young people gather there on Saturdays to make cookies and guide the organizers around the community, and how she began as an observer watching over the youth on picket lines.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Put Off the Picket Line
clips.summary: Richardson recounts her cousin Frederick St. Clair telling Freedom Riders that Cambridge needed fixing, organizers Bill Hansen and Reginald Robinson arriving, her daughter Donna organizing students who nearly stopped the town, her own daughter putting her off the picket line for confronting people who spat at the marchers, and the mass meeting that asked her to lead the new adult group because she would not be financially jeopardized, after which she organized through laundromats, churches, and bars.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: White Gloves Meet SNCC
clips.topic: From Voter Registration to a Survey
clips.summary: Richardson recalls going to Atlanta in white gloves to meet the SNCC crew, her doubts about voter registration since Cambridge Black people had voted since the eighteen-hundreds yet still faced segregated hospitals, housing, and jobs, and how Junior CNAC of grammar school children carried a survey through the community that Swarthmore volunteers wrote up and correlated for their professors.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Public Accommodations at the Bottom
clips.summary: She explains that the survey, built on the graduate methods she learned from E. Franklin Frazier, showed public accommodations ranked at the bottom of community priorities behind housing, jobs, and health, that Robert Kennedy grew sympathetic partly through the Irish experience of poverty, sending her to his housing and other agency commissions, and that secretary Weaver would put a shovel in the ground once they left the streets.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Chicken Coops and the Packing Plant
clips.summary: Richardson describes people living in actual chicken coops stacked one on another with rent collected on them, roughly sixty percent unemployment, the Phillips Packing fuel magnate ordered not to send his trucks into the Black community who vowed to send them under Maryland State Police guard, and how Cambridge eventually ignored the middle-class NAACP whose lawyers would not represent ordinary people.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: The Heart of CNAC
clips.summary: She and the interviewer note that Cambridge's nearness to Washington drew heavy national press and a stream of job offers meant to deflect her leadership, including a state senate candidacy and a Carnegie Hall event with King and a Johnson aide, and she describes the ten to fourteen core CNAC members who, on her grandfather's model, were the recognized leaders in every neighborhood across the county.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: If You Can Beat Us, Defend Us
clips.summary: Richardson reflects on leading as a woman, attributing her freedom to being a long-awaited grandchild in a family where women were never told they could not act, and describes a division of labor in which men worked by day while women conducted movement business and stood guard, recalling women on the street challenging men who fought on Saturday night to instead stop the police from coming through their community.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Secret Whistles in the Field
clips.summary: She recounts her daughter Donna crossing the field paths back home guided by the men's secret whistles in hunting country, a night when guardsmen or fired men drove through shooting up the Black community and were fired back upon, a machine gun someone had brought home from the war, and the press hiding under tables in her uncle's house as the smoke of gun ozone filled Pine Street by morning.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Read at Fort Belvoir
clips.summary: Richardson describes National Guard commander General George Gelston, whom Black Cambridge came to respect as fair though willing to arrest, and the irony that both of them knew their phones were tapped, which is why he sent his second-in-command back and forth with messages rather than trusting lines that were routed all the way to Fort Belvoir to be read before being sent on as if normal.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Born Here, Not Voting on It
clips.summary: She lays out her stand against the referendum on public accommodations, arguing that people whose Cambridge roots ran three hundred years through slavery and freedom should not have to vote on whether they could enter a restaurant, a position she found normal but that the NAACP, the Baltimore ministers, and others outside Cambridge opposed, perhaps thinking it made more sense against the Deep South.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: The Word Went Out We'd Been Killed
clips.summary: Richardson recounts a near clash of hundreds of Black and white people on Race Street that frightened the authorities, being summoned to the White House through General Gelston and family friend Maceo Hubbard of the civil rights division, police cars stationed along every crossing from Cambridge to Washington for safety, a possible shot mistaken for a backfire, and returning to find press in her living room because word had spread she was killed.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Like a Hog Against the Wall
clips.summary: She reflects on managing fear, saying she was not fearless but trusted that if anyone got her the people of Cambridge would get them, and that she would recite a paraphrase of Claude McKay, that if pinned against the wall like a hog she would not die like one, repeating it as she walked through the valley of the shadow.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: A One-Cent Fine
clips.summary: Richardson tells of her mother arrested for trying to be served at a Coca-Cola counter, her daughter Donna and young people arrested then beaten when they changed into jeans and lay in at the theaters, the whole town pouring out by midnight in nightgowns until the jails filled and surrounding towns refused the overflow, and a conflicted Judge Lord Henry handing down a one-cent fine while lecturing her about her grandfather.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: The First Civilian Tear Gas
clips.summary: She recalls the Citizens Council bringing George Wallace to speak at the segregated fire department that Black residents had helped fund yet could not enter, a march that drew people of every background, and the use of CS gas reported as the first civilian use of that gas, after which an elderly man and an infant died, people crawled on the ground, and the arrestees still carried the gas in their clothes to Baltimore County.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: It Turned Into a Big Party
clips.summary: Richardson describes being asked weeks before the March on Washington to add women to the program, told not to wear jeans so she searched the Eastern Shore for a denim skirt with straps, her sense that the march was turning into a party while Cambridge people were threatened in the streets, and arriving as a SNCC coordinator for the Eastern Shore to find the women being held in a holding tent.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: They Took Your Chair
clips.summary: She tells how Bayard Rustin found her waiting near a satellite broadcast and brought her through the crowd to the stage where Lena Horne and Josephine Baker sat, how she realized the whole thing was planned when she was told her chair had been removed, slipped to the back to talk with attorney Robert Ming about a Cambridge legal matter, and helped present Rosa Parks to the international media.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Powell Got the Food In
clips.summary: Richardson names Adam Clayton Powell Jr., Robert Kennedy, and Malcolm X among those she still cannot watch on television, and describes Powell arranging surplus food for Cambridge over the City Council and governor who denied anyone was starving, the Guard distributing it, and Black men loading vans to carry food to white families threatened off welfare for showing up at the distribution sites.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: A Union at the Rod and Gun Club
clips.summary: She recounts an effort, in the middle of all the racial tension, to organize a garment workers union at Phillips Packing Company where lower wages were paid for the same work, Black and white workers meeting at a Black rod and gun club after being blocked from white facilities, and Black men being asked to voice the complaints to the New York organizers, as had happened when SNCC went to Appalachia.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: At Reverend Cleage's Church
clips.summary: Richardson describes going to Detroit for Reverend C.L. Franklin's SCLC event, being told she was in the wrong place and that Malcolm X was speaking at Reverend Albert Cleage's church, hearing him invoke the Honorable Elijah Muhammad in a tone that made her sense he would not stay in the Nation of Islam long, and watching the Cambridge clinic owner Bob Pannett turn completely around over Malcolm.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: He Should Make It Clear
clips.summary: She recalls inviting Malcolm X to Cambridge, urging that before the election with a candidate named Goldwater running it should be the ballot rather than the bullet, to which he agreed, being up at the Audubon Ballroom and meant to be there the day he was killed, and her mother having offered Malcolm's family a third-floor parsonage in New Jersey after his house was bombed.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: His Biggest Mistake
clips.summary: Richardson disputes Manning Marable's biography, which she finds patronizing and has not finished past 1962, reportedly quoting a review that Malcolm's biggest mistake was appearing on stage with her, rejecting the word reinvent in favor of grew, and reflecting that she was glad the press came to Cambridge because they eventually learned and carried the community's issues.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Keep Us In or Protect Us
clips.summary: She and the interviewer frame her as a uniquely independent national movement leader, and she credits her family for teaching her to judge people not by titles so she felt no awe of important people, her independence drawn from the determination of the people she represented and the creative chaos sustained for months, and recalls the shifting belief about whether the Guard was there to keep them in or to protect them.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: They Don't Have the People in the Street
clips.summary: Richardson recounts that SNCC let local people decide and did not interfere, that James Forman could not order her to drop Malcolm X and let one phone call settle it, and that when the national organizations debated Malcolm she had to tell them they had no standing because they did not have the people in the street, recalling Roy Wilkins, who had known her family, trying to talk her out of her position.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Tell the Kennedys to Go to Hell
clips.summary: She recalls sending the Kennedy brothers a defiant message through Maceo Hubbard, sitting on a step at her uncle's with the telephone, crediting General Gelston over his harsher superior with keeping things from getting bloodier, and describing Gelston walking unbidden into a City Council meeting and calmly offering to leave when challenged, proving he held real power with someone.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: An FBI Informant in the House
clips.summary: Richardson identifies a figure who moved through CNAC, the NAACP, and a Swarthmore-linked group as an FBI informant exposed in documents from the Media, Pennsylvania FBI office break-in, and tells of Stokely Carmichael ringing the bell at three in the morning on his way through, the informant grabbing a gun kept in the house against intruders, a moment unsettling only in hindsight.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Six or Seven Lost Months
clips.summary: She judges that the movement accomplished what it wanted yet puzzles over a gap after she left when SNCC people went to Atlantic City and the schools were desegregated and a housing project begun, wondering whether incompetence cost six or seven months, notes white Cambridge electing a racist county figure before steps toward desegregation, and marvels that the town now has a Black woman mayor whose father sat on her executive board.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: This Was Not a Paid Position
clips.summary: Richardson explains moving to New York City in 1965 because the man she married lived there, the agreement had been signed, leadership remained in Cambridge, and she had two daughters to raise, stressing that hers was never a salaried position with offices and secretaries and that she had not meant to spend the rest of her life at it, hoping the people had at least learned they could fight back.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: The Whole Town Was Involved
clips.summary: She recalls a recent Cambridge tribute that overwhelmed her, the whole town involved in a service and an Elks home banquet before a mixed audience, awards from government she had not expected and the newly elected mayor, and an earlier honor at Washington College whose president had learned of Cambridge through Malcolm X's Message to the Grassroots, where she found scholarships given to local Kent County students.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Going to Hell in a Handbasket
clips.summary: Richardson closes by saying the country is going to hell in a handbasket, pointing to a far-right Supreme Court she weighs even against slavery, warning her citizenship class students that what was done to Black people is spreading to everyone, faulting a movement that let the Tea Party get too far out with no opposing entity outside academia, before the interviewer thanks her and closes the recording.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: The Baby of Six in Baker County
clips.summary: Interviewed by Will Griffin at Albany State University in March 2013, Grace Hall Miller gives her name and her July 1932 birth as the sixth and youngest child of farmer Joe Nathan Hall and housewife Nanny Hall in lower Baker County, and tells of marrying Hosie Miller Sr. secretly in 1946 while still in school.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Whatever She Does Is Fine With Me
clips.summary: She recalls her parents' upset that their baby daughter, who could not yet cook or wash, had married, Hosie reassuring them he would do the cooking, and the family they built of five girls and one boy with a father so devoted the daughters claimed him.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: If You Want Something, Ask for It
clips.summary: Miller remembers her parents as church workers, her father a deacon who taught her never to steal and to treat others as she would be treated, and her early walk school two miles from home where she begged her father for firewood while bused white children threw spitballs at the walkers.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Newton Colored High and the Teachers Who Lasted
clips.summary: She describes going to Newton Colored High School while her husband's family drove their children to Mitchell County for schooling, names dedicated teachers like Miss Granberry and Miss Smith and a principal named Bradley, and laments that schools no longer teach the homemaking, Bible verses, and life skills that stayed with her.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Married at Fourteen at the Probate
clips.summary: Miller tells how Hosie courted her at church and BTU, married her at the probate office at fourteen without her parents' knowledge until his brother revealed it, and teased her gently through her early cooking failures while they cleared every disagreement before bed.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: He Painted the Room Blue
clips.summary: She recounts their eighteen years together, Hosie's wish that no boy be good enough for his daughters, and how after he was killed she waited and at her dying mother's urging tried once more for the boy he had so badly wanted, a son born that June whom she was allowed to name only Hosie.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: He Shot Him in the Back Over a Cow
clips.summary: Miller describes the March morning Hosie went with a man to retrieve a cow, the dispute when the man claimed Hosie's cow as his own, and how the man, George Williamson, shot Hosie in the back with buckshot, after which he lingered a week before dying at six in the morning as she dressed to visit him.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: They Wouldn't Even Indict Him
clips.summary: Left with the children and a baby three weeks old when the movement began, Miller tells how the whole community sat on her lawn to help her heal, and how the all-white jury of Baker County men who knew the killer would not indict him across three attempts, the killer being her own father's brother whom she grew up around.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: The First Two to Register
clips.summary: Miller recalls how she and Hosie became the first two people to register to vote in Baker County while paying taxes, her sister Josephine vowing to follow, the registrar too nervous to manage it, and how the county switched the short citizenship card for a long literacy sheet until the Justice Department forced a change.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: They Bombed the Old Men's Houses
clips.summary: She names elders who tried to register and were attacked the same night, including her uncle Joshua Williams in his eighties, Mr. Cunningham framed with planted moonshine whiskey and jailed two weeks, and others whose cars were bombed, recalling the feared local figure they called Gator Johnson.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Pumping the Gun on the Porch
clips.summary: After Hosie's death the movement made her new house a meeting place and Charles Sherrod came in, drawing threats that she was violating her farm-home loan and a cross burned in her yard, and Miller tells how she handed guns to her daughters and pumped a shotgun on the porch until neighbors and her brothers drove the carloads off under gunfire, after which the movement men, summoned by an ordinary phone, watched the house nightly for two months so her frightened children could sleep.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: They Really Caught It in School
clips.summary: She describes her children among the first to integrate the local school, blocked at first by state patrol, taunted by teachers and classmates who twisted the word so they could still say it, and an incident where her daughter was hauled to the superintendent's office over a white girl's claim, after which Miller confronted two white officials and the harassment stopped.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Sherrod Married My Oldest Daughter
clips.topic: SNCC's Stay and a Run for Office
clips.summary: Miller says SNCC stayed in Baker County about two years after Sherrod arrived, that he sent her oldest daughter to college and married her, and that her daughter's treatment at the superintendent's office moved her to ask a mass meeting in Springfield for support and pay the thirty-five-dollar fee to run for the board of education.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: I Picked Peas to Get the Vote
clips.summary: She recounts discovering she had filed in the wrong district yet refusing her money back, winning a runoff against two white men, campaigning door to door by shelling peas and picking butter beans alongside voters on a platform of quality education, and the one man who called her a name yet voted for her, and Roy Kelly, whose lost billfold her father had once returned.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: I Had to Listen and to Learn
clips.topic: Working the Board and Its Fights
clips.summary: Miller describes earning the respect of the white board members by listening and refusing to be bullied while keeping her focus on Baker County's children, and her present alarm that an all-white board is pushing out a capable Black superintendent before his June contract ends and the CRCT testing, ignoring his agenda in a school district that is ninety percent Black.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Just Like the Night They Killed My Husband
clips.summary: Miller recalls hearing of her daughter Shirley Sherrod's forced resignation from the Department of Agriculture, comparing the relatives shielding her from the news to the night Hosie was killed, driving over crying, and her certainty that she had raised no fool when officials stopped Shirley on the highway and would not listen to the full tape.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: If You Don't Know Your History
clips.topic: Teaching the Young and a Legacy
clips.summary: She worries that younger people do not know the segregation she lived through, the colored water fountains and separate doctors' rooms, and urges that children be taught their history, then reflects on turning eighty and on the school road named Grace Miller Circle and the road by her house named Grace Miller Road, honors given while she is alive to smell the flowers.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: They Own That Farm Now
clips.summary: Miller closes on Shirley as Georgia's first Black state director of rural development, her belief the episode was a setup sprung from an NAACP speech, and the story Shirley told of forgiving her own father's killing and helping the Spooner family, who reached CNN to clear her and who, with help found at the last minute, kept and still own their farm.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Five Generations on Finley Land
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine on September 13, 2011, Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan, born in Lincolnville in 1956, traces her fifth-generation roots to great-great-grandfather Thomas A. Finley, who came from the Carolinas with thirteen children and settled near Moultrie, and describes a segregated but self-sufficient Lincolnville of Black-owned grocery stores, beauty parlors, and the St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church her family helped found.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Don't Look Back
clips.summary: Duncan recalls being seven and a half when Dr. King came to St. Augustine, the Ku Klux Klan marching through Lincolnville at night while Black residents lined the streets, her mother holding her hand and telling her not to look back, classmates' homes being firebombed, her childhood confusion over Badcock Furniture as bad cops, and Dr. King crossing from Mr. Proctor's house to ask her mother if she would join the marches.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: The Colored Sign Comes Down
clips.summary: Venturing downtown around age ten to twelve, Duncan remembers being watched as a suspected thief in stores, the colored sign on the wall of the doctor's building near Flagler Hospital, and how, after the sign came down, she walked her younger sister straight down the once-forbidden front hallway only to be marched back through the office to the Black entrance at the rear.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Fighting Just to Eat Lunch
clips.summary: Duncan describes her school closing after fifth grade at Excelsior Elementary and forced integration in sixth grade, teachers who would not teach her and children who would not sit by her, a junior high with no cafeteria where white students formed lines to block Black children from walking to Orange Street for lunch, and the violence she says made the schools feel anything but nonviolent.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Put On Your Thinking Cap
clips.summary: Duncan tells how she learned the movement's history later in life through Catherine Twine and Cora Tyson Johnson, and how after Twine's death local historian David Nolan, with J.T. Johnson, Jane Billings, and others, asked her to help plan the fortieth anniversary; she proposed modeling it on a family reunion, recalling her own 2001 reunion and a cousin found in Madagascar who had been a Clinton-era ambassador.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Time to Say Thank You
clips.summary: Duncan recounts the strategic meeting at the Galimore Center where older residents shared heart-wrenching stories, the decision to bring movement participants back to a history hidden by the city's power structures, a doubter who urged them to let sleeping dogs lie, J.T. Johnson's reply that it was time to say thank you, and her own grief that despite all Dr. Hayling and Dr. King did, much in St. Augustine, the first free Black settlement at Fort Mose nearby, had not changed.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: The City He Loved
clips.topic: A Son Who Could Not Stay
clips.summary: Duncan speaks of stressing education with her five children and of her son, who earned an industrial and systems engineering degree from the University of Florida and a second degree in building construction yet could not find work in St. Augustine, and who wrote that the city he loved would never be the city he would return to, a pattern she says holds for many Black people there.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Angels Who Walked Among Us
clips.summary: Duncan describes the all-volunteer 40th ACCORD, long unfunded until Northrop Grumman backed thirty historic civil rights markers placed with the permission of city officials and private citizens, and her wish to bring recognition to Dr. Robert Hayling, the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers, Audrey Nell Hamilton, and Rena Ayers, who at over a hundred had once housed and fed demonstrators and Northern attorneys, people she calls angels.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: One Hand Wouldn't Cover It
clips.summary: Asked what still has not changed, Duncan points to the scarcity of jobs and Black-owned businesses, so few one hand would cover them, the near-absence of Black people in St. Augustine and St. Johns County government and administration, and the flight of young people like her son, now in Jacksonville, and a daughter with a doctorate in physical therapy who is looking to leave.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Because It's the Money Maker
clips.summary: Duncan attributes the city's new embrace of its civil rights history to tourist demand and the revenue it generates rather than moral transformation, crediting Andrew Young's film and the previewing of his movie for national exposure, noting the Foot Soldiers monument was a private venture the city credited only after the money was there, and that Northrop Grumman's funding contrasted with the unfriendly Fertile Hill company that preceded it.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: A Paternalistic Little Town
clips.summary: After a brief pause to change recording tracks, Duncan reflects that St. Augustine is a quiet, peaceful place to raise a family but, as Hank Thomas said, paternalistic, and argues that while the city can stay old and historic, the county must open to industry so that Black families, who have taken flight by the thousands, can come back and raise children as she once did in Lincolnville.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Toward a Civil Rights Museum
clips.summary: Duncan outlines the 40th ACCORD's program: keynote speakers Dorothy Cotton and the coming Taylor Branch, freedom trail tours, and two Northrop Grumman-funded teacher workshops for St. Johns County built around a civil rights workbook, and the long-term goal, led by chair Richard Burton and co-chair J.T. Johnson, of opening a civil rights museum by 2014 to mark the fiftieth anniversary of the Civil Rights Act, before the closing thanks.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: No Doors on the Main Street
clips.summary: Interviewed in Montgomery, Alabama on June 1, 2011 by Joe Mosnier and filmmaker John Bishop, Gwendolyn M. Patton paints her arrival at Tuskegee in the fall of 1961: a large ornate campus where the buildings on Campus Avenue had no front entrances facing the main thoroughfare, and the Lincoln Gate named for Abraham Lincoln.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: He Built the Doors Facing the Campus
clips.summary: Patton recalls the National Guard greeting them, called up because the city schools were being integrated, and learning that the doorless thoroughfare was Booker T. Washington's deliberate choice, turning every entrance inward to the campus so Black and white passersby would not feel welcomed, which proved to her he was a race man despite being called an uncle Tom.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: The Father of Black Power
clips.summary: She describes her childhood paper on George Washington Carver written to dispel the peanut-man myth, her father Clarence Robert Patton Sr. as a race man, and the story of Robert Russa Moton insisting on Black professional staff at the new VA hospital for World War I veterans against Woodrow Wilson's directive that only menial roles go to Black workers.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: The Veterans Lined the Highway
clips.summary: Patton tells how Moton called out the Black World War I veterans who lined the highway to the VA hospital when the Klan threatened violence, and the Klan never showed, and connects this to a 1967 piece she wrote in the hospital calling Booker T. Washington the father of Black power and the on-campus Tuskegee Black Power Conference organized by SNCC and underwritten by SHARP.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: The Mother's Day Massacre
clips.summary: Patton recounts how, as a race-conscious young student in 1961, she stayed connected to Montgomery when the Freedom Riders came through, what she calls the Mother's Day Massacre, and how the movement infrastructure including a secretary to Dr. King and the Montgomery Improvement Association jumped into gear to rescue and shelter the riders.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: They Came and Got Me
clips.summary: She describes the family divisions over involvement, status-conscious schoolteacher aunts versus the broader-minded Aunt Mary, her grandparents the Pattons who worked at the Greyhound station coming to take her to the all-night mass meeting, the teenagers feeling invincible amid white men she took for newspaper people, and a youth named John Lacy crawling through the basement window to make phone calls.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: They Are Not to Enter My Front Door
clips.topic: Aunt Chick and the White Boy
clips.summary: Patton recalls hosting an integrated group of Freedom Riders at her Aunt Chick's home the next day, and her aunt taking her to the kitchen to declare that as long as she had to use the back door of white folks' houses, no white person would enter the front door of her home, a distinction between houses and home Patton had to take under consideration even as a race person.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Up the Rickety Back Steps
clips.summary: Patton describes entering the segregated Benson Sanatorium in Lafayette, Alabama in January 1963 to treat tuberculosis in her lungs, arriving weak by ambulance from Tuskegee's John Andrew Memorial Hospital, made to use the rickety back steps and a back vestibule that served as the intake room, traumatized by a disease she had known only as a deadly plague.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: The Wards Were Horrifying
clips.summary: She maps the Black women's section: a three-bed room she was first placed in, two huge wards of eight to ten patients each, a tiny black-and-white TV, and the daily shots over six months as she fought to regain strength, finding the crowded wards horrifying and being made to clean their own areas, which she took at first for part of the therapy.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: The Movement Is Wherever You Are
clips.summary: Patton tells of befriending a deathly-ill mother and her little girl, watching the March on Washington in August 1963 on the tiny TV the state could cut off at will, then the Birmingham church bombing that killed the four little girls in September, and resolving that the movement is wherever you are, making the sanatorium her movement base.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Chopin in the Sitting Room
clips.summary: She recounts seeking the sanatorium's library, taking the little girl by the hand into the white section's game room where a baby grand piano stood, and playing Chopin's C-sharp minor to the stunned and angry white patients, then stopping when she realized she risked being a mammy performing for a white child at the expense of her own.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: I Would Like to See the Menu
clips.topic: Demanding the Library and the Food
clips.summary: Patton describes negotiating with the head nurse, who reminded her of the matron in One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest, to arrange Jim Crow library visits for herself and the child, then challenging the monotonous boiled food by asking to see the kitchen menu, until the staff began posting it and she could choose perch and encourage fellow patients to do likewise.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Way Over Yonder Where Jesus Is
clips.summary: She remembers the patient-led outdoor chapel services under a pecan tree with sorrowful death-themed hymns like Way Over Yonder Where Jesus Is, and reflects that the white staff tolerated and even respected her because they assumed Black people ignorant, were impressed by her intelligence and determination, knew she was temporary, and feared reprisal from her well-connected family.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: One Big Fat Disease
clips.summary: Patton describes returning to Tuskegee in the fall of 1964, where a roommate terrified of tuberculosis refused to share a room, so the school moved the roommate and left Patton alone in Travis Hall, the honor dorm, and how the Greensquare community embraced her, her family pointedly drinking from her glass, and her grandmother Flora and Aunt Jix supporting her.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Never for Integration, Ever
clips.summary: She found the visiting SNCC women tough, committed, and resolute, but worried she thought too deeply because she had never believed in integration, only desegregation, equal opportunity, and justice, recalling that her Montgomery teachers had pushed her toward the University of Alabama assuming her Northern background, and she had refused, wanting only to be the world's best cheerleader.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: It Was About Sitting Anywhere You Please
clips.summary: Patton recounts how her grandmother, a block captain in the bus boycott, always sat in the back of the bus while young Gwendolyn sat up front to aggravate white folks, and how her grandmother explained that the boycott was not about sitting next to white people but about sitting anywhere you please, and she was pleased to sit there, a lesson that altered Patton's whole thought process.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: It Ain't About Integration
clips.topic: The Class Problem in the Caste
clips.summary: She explains how watching the desegregation of Macon County schools, where middle-class professional children were bused to the white school downtown while poor Black families already lived nearby, and seeing the Negro swimming pool reserve professional children's days separate from working-class children's days, showed her the class problem within the caste and deepened her skepticism of integration for its own sake.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: You Should Not Submerge the People
clips.summary: Patton describes the founding of the Tuskegee Institute Advancement League (TIAL) in early 1965 and her conviction, drawn from the Montgomery Improvement Association's refusal to become an SCLC chapter and surrender the identity that sustained the 381-day boycott, that an organizer must lift up a grassroots group's own identity and structure rather than subsume it.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Two Christmas Parties
clips.summary: She recalls Tuskegee as a bourgeois near-prep school with a campus laundry, where President Dr. Foster held one Christmas party for the maintenance and help and another for the professional and VA staff, and how, becoming close to Foster, she argued the cost-effectiveness of a single party until the businessman PhD began to reason with her and they nearly became peers.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Tuskegee Had Become a Liberated Zone
clips.summary: Patton describes Tuskegee by early 1965 as a liberated zone where SNCC members attended classes and ate in the dining hall and the administration had given up, with faculty support from progressive white professors Dr. Foster had hired and Black professors turning nationalist, as students went to Lowndes and Dallas counties to organize before the Selma to Montgomery march.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Education Has to Be Relevant
clips.summary: She explains the conviction that education must serve the community or a degree means nothing, fed by compulsory Negro history and assigned reading of W.E.B. Du Bois and Booker T. Washington that was meant to change behavior toward the revolutionary, and recounts writing her sophomore paper on homosexuality after the card catalog offered only deviant behavior, prompted by her cousin Bobby's love for a neighborhood friend.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: You Ain't Deviant to Me
clips.summary: Patton tells how a friend named Junior, married after fathering a son, confided he was homosexual and pointed her to the Mattachine Press and San Francisco and a book she likened to a Bible of the gay world, leading her to learn the terms Lesbos and lesbian, and reflects that SNCC people in the heat of the day did not do this kind of contemplation.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Who Are You, Where Do You Come From
clips.summary: She names the TIAL circle of enrolled Tuskegee students including George Ware of Buffalo, George Davis of Rhode Island, Sammy Younge Jr., Ruby Taylor of the Alabama Black Belt, and Kathleen Neal Cleaver, a group of broad perspective that held philosophical discussions about identity and lifestyle, contrasting them with SNCC, caught up in tactical, day-to-day grassroots drudgery.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: How Dare You Got Your Degree
clips.summary: Patton recounts Stokely Carmichael, impressed by Tuskegee students yet wanting everyone to drop out and join SNCC, and her retort that he had gone back to Howard for his own degree, and her practical argument, made also to Bettina Aptheker who was horrified by the campus ROTC, that many Tuskegee students needed ROTC for scholarships and the school ran strong draft and conscientious-objector counseling.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: We Thought It Would Take Three Days
