clips.summary: Patton describes meeting as TIAL direct-action chair with James Forman, Hosea Williams, and Dr. King at Birmingham's A.G. Gaston Motel, the democratic decision to march from Selma until they reached Montgomery, and returning to organize the East Black Belt across Macon and Bullock counties with Sammy Younge taking the lead stopping cars on Campus Avenue to raise money.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Every Hour on the Hour
clips.summary: She explains TIAL's meticulous logistics, gridding cars so students could picket downtown every hour without missing class, which astonished SNCC and made James Forman the campus mentor while Stokely went to Lowndes County, and recounts watching Bloody Sunday on television, horrified, then gearing up to meet the marchers in Montgomery on what they expected to be the third day.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: He Promised the Children
clips.summary: Patton tells of her fury when Dr. King prayed and turned the march around on the Tuesday, a breach of the collective decision that felt to the youngsters like a broken promise, then Judge Frank M. Johnson Jr.'s injunction, and Dr. Foster calling her in, thinking her malleable, to show her the injunction paper, which she pocketed without reading because the caravan and buses were already organized.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: It Said Nothing About Macon County
clips.summary: She recounts deposing the New York student-body president who would not serve the movement and installing George Ware, mobilizing thirty or forty cars and paid buses with cafeteria workers ready to strike, and how George Ware and George Davis read that the injunction barred only the Selma-to-Montgomery march and said nothing about a march coming from Macon County, so they could play the legal angle.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: The Window From Italy
clips.summary: Patton digresses into Montgomery church history: First Baptist that sheltered the Freedom Riders in 1961 with its Italian windows and pulpit installed by the race man Reverend Stokes, whose likeness sits above John the Baptist in the glass, and the offshoot Dexter Avenue Baptist Church, divided by class feuds between laborers her grandfather housed and status-conscious schoolteachers.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Fifteen Hundred to Montgomery
clips.topic: Taking Refuge in the Cold Church
clips.summary: She tells how, of Tuskegee's roughly 2,300 students, about 1,500 were mobilized democratically by stomping feet in the dining hall to call a mass meeting, and how, after the governor refused them and the cold rainy night set in, they took refuge in Dr. King's Dexter Avenue Baptist Church, finding the heat off, the lights dark, and the toilets unable to flush.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: We're Going to Take Over This
clips.summary: Patton recounts learning the deacons had cut the church's utilities, deciding to occupy and hold it as Alabama State students arrived to buffer them from the police, and a heated debate in which the older graduate student George Ware grew protective and called Forman a manipulator of the undergraduates, while Patton insisted the students were grown scholars who knew the risks, a stance that helped get her elected student-body president.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: The March That Wouldn't Turn Around
clips.summary: She explains that to stay true to their word that it was a march, students led by Sammy Younge Jr. ran a relay in which people got out of the caravan to walk and the cars behind picked them up, and that the name the march that wouldn't turn around was coined years later by a New Mexico student who researched her papers in the state archives and won a history award.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Selma to Lowndes to Montgomery
clips.summary: Patton argues the Selma-to-Montgomery story overlooks Lowndes County, the longest stretch, and recounts her concern as an organizer for how the marchers would eat and relieve themselves, persuading Dr. Foster, who fed 2,300 students daily, to provide barbecue chicken for a campsite where she had tasted the poor food, taking it as a sign Foster grasped the depth of their mission.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Now What Do We Do With This Vote
clips.summary: She describes the rich movement history of proud Lowndes County and the discussions with TIAL and James Forman that drew the distinction between integration and desegregation leading to self-determination, and how, with the Voting Rights Act won, the Lowndes County people led by John Hulett chose to seek offices closest to the people rather than mayor or city council.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Closest to the People
clips.summary: Patton explains why Lowndes County people wanted the sheriff who came unrobed into the Black community, the board of education for decent schools, the tax assessor who had over-assessed Black landowners while white plantations were taxed nearly nothing, and the coroner who ruled Black deaths in jail and ditches suicide, and why, knowing the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, they refused to replicate that model or chase delegates to distant Atlantic City.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: No Clouds in the Sky
clips.summary: She recounts the November 1966 election the people of Lowndes County saw stolen through white terror, tenant farmers hauled to the polls by plantation owners and reprisals against people who had only their lives to lose, described in her piece No Clouds in the Sky, contrasted with neighboring Macon County, which elected Black sheriff Lucius Amerson to the consternation of the Black middle class.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: We Didn't Hit Anything
clips.summary: Patton describes the eve of the first Black Power Conference underwritten by SHARP, which Dr. Foster moved off campus until Dean Phillips arranged a Boy Scout camp site, and the mysterious February 10, 1967 car accident as she drove a young man toward her grandmother's home to lay over before flying to Canada to avoid the draft, an accident in which nothing struck them yet she woke at John Andrew Memorial Hospital among the SNCC people.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Have You Talked to the Lord About It
clips.summary: She tells how Dr. Hume, a bourgeois doctor who hated the movement, wanted to amputate her leg, and how, motivated partly by a young woman's thoughts of intimacy, she asked whether he had sought a second opinion or talked to the Lord, then summoned her devout paternal grandparents, who dispatched her cousin to take her from the hospital because the doctor was a heathen.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: I Will Work for SNCC
clips.summary: Patton closes on calling Bettina Aptheker to find a doctor and her move to New York, where SNCC and CORE people, James Forman, her fiance, Julia Wright from Paris, and the Communist, Socialist Workers, and Progressive Labor parties supported her, and where she met Mary Kochiyama and Puerto Ricans for the first time, deciding on furlough from long hospital stays that she would work for SNCC.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: A Beehive of Activity
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program on the University of Florida campus in Gainesville on September 14, 2011, Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons recalls her first impressions of the Atlanta SNCC office as a young Spelman student: a chaotic, untidy beehive with James Forman at its center, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson a fixture, and Black, white, and Japanese American staff she met for the first time.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Breaking Spelman's Prohibition
clips.summary: Simmons describes Ella Baker's gentle, unobtrusive teaching at SNCC gatherings, and the terrible tension of slipping to the office while violating Spelman's prohibition on student political activity, knowing that as the first in her family to reach college on a full scholarship she could be sent home, with her parents refusing to back her up if she got involved.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Pulled Asunder
clips.summary: She recounts the figures who pulled her toward engagement, Staughton Lynd teaching the long Black freedom struggle, Howard Zinn as a radical force, and Vincent Harding and his wife at the Mennonite House Sunday dinners, set against her own folks' warnings, leaving her feeling pulled asunder, with James Forman as an early mentor and Ruby Doris a Spelman dropout she could relate to but feared becoming.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: The Worst of the Worst
clips.topic: Heroes and the Fear of Mississippi
clips.summary: Simmons remembers the Nashville staff and the Mississippi field workers, including women, whose stories of being jailed and beaten blew her away and struck home, John Lewis among the dropouts she befriended, and how her grandmother's warning that Mississippi was the worst of the worst for Black people made that state loom larger than Albany as she wrestled with taking the further plunge.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: They Will Kill Whites Too
clips.summary: She describes the emotional struggle of choosing to go to Mississippi in 1964, helping plan Freedom Summer through Staughton Lynd's Freedom School curriculum, and her hope that the thousand white students would offer protection, a hope shattered when, during the second week of orientation, James Chaney and two white workers she had just met disappeared and Bob Moses said they were probably dead.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Are We There Yet
clips.summary: Assigned to Laurel with James Garrett and Lester McKinney because it was too dangerous to send whites, Simmons recounts believing they would never get out alive but feeling she could not turn back having severed ties with her family, and waking from sleeping the whole drive from Ohio to find that Mississippi, where she half expected monsters in the trees, looked no different from the rest of the country.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: I've Been Waiting on You All My Life
clips.summary: Because Laurel had no structure, the team based in well-organized Hattiesburg and drove thirty miles north knocking on doors, and Simmons tells how Mrs. Eberhart Spinks, hearing she was a Freedom Rider, said come in, I've been waiting on you all my life, taking her in and arranging for neighbor Mrs. Carrie Clayton to house the two men, the beginning of the Laurel Project.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: She Was the Driving Force
clips.topic: Two Women Who Took Them In
clips.summary: Simmons contrasts the two hosts, the older widowed Mrs. Clayton with her lovely home who said little, and the fighter Mrs. Eberhart Spinks who declared she was sick and tired and ready for change, whose husband worked a union job at the Masonite plant and worried for their teenage son, and how once Jackson and Atlanta were alerted some twenty-three volunteers were sent in.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: You've Got to Step Up
clips.summary: She recounts how Lester McKinney, the seasoned worker, vanished and was found jailed on an old Jones County charge, forced to sign an affidavit not to return for five years, leaving only Simmons and the Californian James Garrett, so that James Forman called and told her to become project director, promising relief that never came as she wound up staying sixteen months.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Those Skills Just Came to the Floor
clips.summary: Simmons reflects on how she took charge despite feeling unprepared, drawing on a childhood of leadership as editor of her school paper, president of her high school sorority and Girl Scout troop, and on watching her grandparents in Memphis register voters and Black churches host campaigns, with Mrs. Spinks, active in her church and the NAACP, reminding her of her grandmother.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Like a Barn Raising
clips.summary: She describes the volunteers rolling up their sleeves, the first office on Mrs. Clayton's back porch, and renting a wealthy Black man's shuttered nightclub that the community fixed up like a barn raising, only for it to be firebombed; there they ran a Freedom School for grades one through twelve, adult literacy, a Freedom Nursery School, mock voter registration, and organized the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party chapter.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Thrown Under the Bus
clips.summary: Simmons recalls preparing the Laurel group for the state convention in Jackson where two local delegates were chosen, and the disappointment of Atlantic City, where naive about the political system they believed they would be seated until President Johnson threw them under the bus to keep the regular Democrats, a profound blow that jaded the seasoned SNCC workers with the lesson that politics was elevated over dying people.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: We're Just Getting Started
clips.summary: She describes deciding not to leave for Antioch College, where Staughton Lynd had helped her enroll with a recommendation from Coretta Scott King, because she could not abandon Mrs. Spinks, Mrs. Clayton, and Mr. Richardson when the work was just getting started; Antioch graciously held her scholarship until January and two other women, Lynelle Barrett and Marion Davidson, stayed on sharing a bedroom at Mrs. Spinks's home.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: A Feminist Way of Organizing
clips.topic: Consensus and the Fight for Resources
clips.summary: Simmons reflects on becoming a leader, recognizing in hindsight a feminist, bottom-up style modeled on SNCC's ultra-democratic consensus, where the project discussed everything for hours because all their lives were on the line, and on the struggle as one of only three women project directors in Mississippi that summer to be taken seriously and to fight Jackson for good cars and typewriters the men got first.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: An Absolute No No
clips.summary: Having grown up under total segregation in Memphis until she met white professors like Staughton Lynd, Simmons describes standing up to white male volunteers her age from Stanford and Yale amid swirling class, race, and gender tensions, and taking an absolute stand against the sexual harassment of women on the project, having herself been sexually assaulted by someone she trusted at the orientation.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: I Hope They Would Say I Was Fair
clips.summary: Simmons recounts reconnecting with a SNCC volunteer from Stanford who sent her a fragile letter she had written from Laurel, treasured because she has no documents from the period, and says she hopes the volunteers would remember her as fair and dedicated to keeping them and the community people alive and on their property.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: It Blew the Gas Line Away
clips.summary: She describes the period after most volunteers left, when the project shrank to six including two expelled high school students, Ulysses Everett and Ben Hartfield from Hattiesburg, and the illiterate local John Handy, and how their second office, half of a shotgun duplex secured without disclosing who they were, was firebombed, the Molotov cocktail blowing the gas line away from the house in what neighbors called an act of God that let the family next door escape.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: This Is Our Church
clips.summary: Simmons recounts expanding into desegregating Laurel's restaurants in Klan-heavy Jones County, leading a group of high school students past a sheriff who drew his gun and cursed her, singing freedom songs outside the jail when Ulysses and Ben were arrested, and Mrs. Spinks fighting battles in the Black community, threatening to boycott churches whose scared, displaced ministers wanted to shut the movement out.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Something Wrong With You
clips.topic: Trauma and a Move to New York
clips.summary: Simmons tells how a large demonstration at the Jackson Capitol led to roughly a thousand arrests and fifteen brutal days held in livestock houses at the state fairgrounds, after which she caused two head-on car accidents, prompting James Forman to insist something was wrong and bring her to New York to work with the Friends of SNCC, where over three months she discovered the Nation of Islam, black nationalist street preachers in Harlem, and LeRoi Jones's Black Arts Theatre.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: A Whole Nother Song
clips.summary: After Julian Bond was elected but barred from his seat in the Georgia legislature, James Forman asked Simmons to come to Atlanta to organize his re-election, and she brought her three months of black-nationalist thinking into the SNCC offices, recruiting her future husband Michael Simmons, whose brother had been the Nation's national secretary and roomed with Malcolm X, along with Dwight Williams and an older military veteran, forming the nucleus of the Atlanta Project.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Will We Be Accepted Ever
clips.summary: Simmons describes setting up Bond's campaign in Vine City, Atlanta's poorest and most rundown area of shacks beset by poor housing, unemployment, addiction, and bad schools, around the corner from Nation of Islam Mosque Number 15, and how winning his re-election only to face a renewed fight for his seat pushed the group to question whether integration was possible or whether race ran too deep for Black people ever to be accepted as full Americans.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: A Wollensak Tape Recorder
clips.summary: Simmons explains that the Atlanta Project's discussions of where to go next, recorded on a Wollensak tape recorder and typed up by herself and two other women, became the group-authored position paper on Black Power later credited to Stokely Carmichael, and how Freedom Summer had so changed SNCC's racial ratio that justifying resources to the many white staff at the Atlanta headquarters fed the argument that whites should organize in their own communities.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: This Catches Fire
clips.topic: Black Power and Division in SNCC
clips.summary: She recalls the project's innovative organizing with a loudspeaker truck and hot dogs, noticing that playing a Malcolm X record drew people out, and the questions spreading through SNCC as Bob Moses's group worked Lowndes County with Stokely and others looked to Africa, until Stokely stood up in Mississippi and said black power, catching fire while Forman lost control and the deeply religious John Lewis opposed it, opening a real division in the organization.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: SNCC Was Home
clips.topic: The Pain of the White Volunteers
clips.summary: Simmons reflects on the pain of the longtime white volunteers, meeting Bob Zellner years later to discuss the break before a Chicago class and learning that his own Klan family had threatened to kill him so that SNCC was his only home, and recalls the statements against the Vietnam War, which she helped draft, and on Israel and Palestine, drafted by a woman she hopes to interview at the Chicago SNCC reunion, which created a firestorm.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Wondering What We Were Questioning
clips.summary: Asked about the new SNCC's vision for the future, Simmons admits no vision had coalesced, that she was questioning what she had believed and wondering whether older strands of Black thought from slavery forward pointed away from integration, recognizing a two-pronged tradition that fought to integrate into the country and that sought instead to leave it or carve out a separate space for African Americans.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: A Totally Disruptive Element
clips.summary: Simmons describes the shock of the Atlanta Project being expelled from SNCC, ostensibly over a three-thousand-dollar check sent to their address that Forman insisted belonged to SNCC and they refused to turn over, leading to a lawyer and a fight, but underneath because Forman saw the project as a disruptive element pulling the organization off course, and her lasting regret at never reconciling with the mentor she loved before he died.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Never Heard the Term Oral History
clips.summary: Simmons recounts going to work for the American Friends Service Committee's project on government surveillance and civil liberties, traveling the country to interview members of the American Indian Movement, Brown Berets, Black Panther Party, and Corky Gonzales's group about COINTELPRO, gathering their FBI files under the Freedom of Information Act, with staff like Ken Lawrence documenting the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission, the Quakers having launched the project after finding infiltrators in their meetings.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: The People We Thought Would Protect Us
clips.summary: She describes learning that Mississippi FBI agents fed information to the Sovereignty Commission and possibly the Klan, that Fred Hampton's bodyguard had drugged the apartment so no shots were fired in what was an assassination by Chicago police and the FBI, and that California Panthers and members of US were pitted against one another by agents provocateurs, a shock given how openly and trustingly the Laurel project had once talked to the agents who came around.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: A Mountain of Skulls
clips.summary: Asked about the long-term effects of trauma, Simmons says she stayed too busy moving from movement to movement to feel damaged, but that working with students who cannot believe such things happened, and being among the first Americans flown into Cambodia after the fall of Pol Pot to see a mountain of skulls and hear survivors' oral histories in Phnom Penh, taught her that cruelty is a human problem requiring change in people's hearts as well as in law.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: The Only Way That Leaves People
clips.summary: Simmons explains how her movement into Sufism, the mystical stream in Islam, brought her full circle to understand and re-embrace Dr. King's message of nonviolence, which she had once rejected, as the only real way to bring about deep change that does not leave corpses piled mountains high or wounded spirits and souls, however difficult that path may be.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: When People Ran, I Ran
clips.summary: She recalls the dire despair of Dr. King's assassination, learning of it at a Manhattan hotel during a National Council of Negro Women meeting, dropping her bags and taking the subway up to Harlem where she joined a riot, running when others ran amid fires, needing to vent, and carrying for years afterward an anger and a belief that the government had probably had him killed.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: A School Where We Learn
clips.topic: Seventeen Years With a Sufi Teacher
clips.summary: Simmons closes on meeting a Sufi mystic from Sri Lanka in 1971 and spending seventeen years studying with the teacher Bawa Muhaiyaddeen, gradually embracing the teaching that life is a school for learning to choose good and reject bad, which led her to reflect anew on Dr. King and her Christian upbringing and to re-embrace nonviolence as the only way to bring about lasting social change, grateful her story will outlast her.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Skipped Grades and a Regents Scholarship
clips.summary: Interviewed in Brooklyn on March 16, 2013, by John Dittmer, Dr. H. Jack Geiger opens on his Upper West Side upbringing as the son of a doctor and a microbiologist mother, being skipped four or five grades, and graduating at fourteen from Townsend Harris, a magnet high school taught by City College faculty who taught him about the colonial rape of Africa before being fired under the Rapp-Coudert Act.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Billie Holiday on 52nd Street
clips.summary: Too young at fourteen for any college to admit him, Geiger describes hanging out on 52nd Street listening to Billie Holiday and Lester Young, working as a copyboy for the New York Times, and being so moved by Canada Lee in Orson Welles's Native Son that he talked his way backstage and began visiting the actor.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Couch on Edgecombe Avenue
clips.summary: Geiger recounts the worsening conflict with his European-born immigrant parents, packing a suitcase one Sunday night and going to Canada Lee's penthouse at 155th and Edgecombe in Harlem, where Lee let him sleep on the couch and gave him an education sitting in the corner as Paul Robeson, Langston Hughes, and Adam Clayton Powell Jr. came through.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: This Was Not a Race Riot
clips.topic: The Harlem Uprising and Canada Lee
clips.summary: Geiger describes a Harlem ablaze in 1941 with stories of black soldiers abused at southern camps and the 1943 uprising sparked by a rumor that military police shot a black soldier, a selective trashing of white-owned 125th Street stores rude to black customers, and how the FBI threatened Canada Lee with blacklisting if he would not denounce Paul Robeson, which Lee refused.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: From Jockey to Actor
clips.summary: Geiger recounts Canada Lee's blacklisting after he refused to denounce Robeson, and Lee's life story: a Jamaican family, a violin prodigy turned jockey turned light-heavyweight contender ended by a retinal detachment, who wandered into Welles's Mercury Theatre in Harlem and became a gifted actor, starring in the Broadway Native Son.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: An Evening With Langston Hughes
clips.summary: Geiger recalls his middle-class white parents bravely attending a black party at Lee's penthouse, his mother spending two hours cooking in the kitchen with a man she never recognized as Langston Hughes, and the era's ritual of alerting the doorman so a black dinner guest would be let in, a transformative window into Harlem and its daily struggles.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Picketing the Raytheon Plant
clips.summary: Admitted at fifteen to the University of Wisconsin in 1941, Geiger describes getting a 1942 letter from Bayard Rustin and A. Philip Randolph to organize for the first threatened March on Washington, picketing a Madison defense plant that would not hire blacks, an effort that pressured Franklin Roosevelt into an executive order barring discrimination in defense hiring and triggering a new wave of the Great Migration.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Off the University's Housing List
clips.summary: Geiger recounts his first structured campaign at Wisconsin, forcing the university to drop approved off-campus landlords who refused Jews, blacks, and Asians, then meeting James Farmer in Chicago, helping start one of the earliest CORE chapters in Madison, and working a police beat at sixteen as the only reporter who needed a special pass to be on the street after the wartime youth curfew.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The Only Integrated Service
clips.summary: Geiger explains enlisting in the merchant marine because the National Maritime Union had desegregated the ships and rank mattered less than the job, the opposite of the segregated Navy where black sailors could only be cooks or waiters, then training as a radio radar officer and shipping out on a Pacific tanker through the battles at Ulithi and Leyte Gulf.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The SS Booker T. Washington
clips.summary: Geiger describes grabbing an opening on the SS Booker T. Washington, the only ship in the American fleet with a black captain, Hugh Mulzac, who had held a master's license for years but never a command, and its integrated crew of black and white mates and engineers, on which he made several trips, some of them to Europe.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Integrate the White Waiting Room
clips.summary: Geiger recounts how the integrated crew, sent to segregated Norfolk for repairs, would put on their dress uniforms and integrate the white waiting room of the railroad station, daring the police to arrest them, and would enrage ticket clerks by asking for tickets to the United States, his first nonviolent direct-action confrontation in the real world.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: You Don't Become a Doctor
clips.summary: Geiger tells how, floating around on the water, he decided he was interested in biology and medicine despite his father's repeated wish that he not become a doctor, and enrolled as a pre-med at the University of Chicago because he wanted what he saw as a real education.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Sit Down and Refuse to Leave
clips.topic: CORE and the AVC in Chicago
clips.summary: Geiger describes his Chicago activism with CORE, walking interracial crews into restaurants that would not serve blacks and inviting the other diners to refuse service too, and his role as civil-liberties chairman of the campus chapter of the American Veterans Committee, all while working night shifts at the International News Service.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Robbed Their Files Blind
clips.summary: Geiger recounts the best-documented campaign of his life, after a faculty member's black servant was refused care: discovering the University of Chicago's lying-in hospital barred black patients while others diverted them to Provident, gathering sixty pages of documentation from veterans' secretary wives, finding admissions minutes that a qualified black applicant would not be admitted at this time, and chancellor Robert Maynard Hutchins calling it appalling but doing nothing.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Follow the Money
clips.topic: The Howard Plan and the Strike
clips.summary: Geiger tells how he arranged with Dr. W. Montague Cobb of Howard for strong black applicants to also apply to Chicago, each secretly paired with a white veteran of matching grades and MCAT scores, culminating in a student-faculty strike on December 7, 1947, and how, with the university still refusing, he took the documentation to a Pittsburgh foundation, after which it called to ask what they wanted, a lesson in following the money before Watergate.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The Kiss of Death Letter
clips.summary: Geiger describes being called to Howard by Dr. W. Montague Cobb, who showed him an American Medical Association letter sent to every dean flagging his extracurricular activities, the kiss of death, and offering him a place at Howard, which Geiger declined, unwilling to take another student's place and unsure medicine was what he wanted.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Science Editor at the Wire Service
clips.summary: Geiger recounts becoming science and medicine editor at the International News Service for about four years, interviewing Nobel laureates and covering major research, an education in medicine from a different vantage point, while he and his first wife Mary informally adopted her preteen brother and sister out of a broken home.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Class of Bent Arrows
clips.summary: Geiger describes assigning himself to cover the deans' meeting on the Willie Sutton principle and being recruited to Western Reserve's radical new integrated curriculum by one-man admissions dean Jack McCoy, who each year admitted a dozen bent arrows, deviants like Geiger, into his preceptor group alongside women and African American students unusual for the time.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: An Older Student in Cleveland
clips.summary: Geiger reflects on the advantage of starting medical school at almost thirty after a life as an activist, merchant seaman, and journalist, unlike classmates who knew little beyond school, and on the usual wonders and uncertainties of the immersive early years and whether medicine was really what he wanted.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Medicine Is Politics Writ Large
clips.summary: Geiger recounts the second-year epiphany on the medical-school steps, looking out at Cleveland and realizing that who got sick was a social phenomenon, thinking he had invented social medicine only to find the Germans and British had written it a century earlier, and embracing Virchow's idea that medicine is simply politics writ large.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Flying to New York for Freelance Work
clips.summary: Geiger describes mentors Margaret Mead and Barry Commoner, a committee on the social responsibility of science at the AAAS where he met Rockefeller Foundation vice president Warren Weaver, and using Western Reserve's free Tuesdays to fly to New York for freelance writing assignments to support his family before flying back for Wednesday classes.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Letter to Warren Weaver
clips.summary: Geiger tells how he turned down Warren Weaver's offer to be the Rockefeller Foundation's science editor, explaining his social-medicine epiphany in a letter, and how Weaver, knowing the foundation funded Sidney and Emily Kark's pioneering community health centers in apartheid South Africa, sent him reprints showing a place that was actually practicing what he wanted to do.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Three Places Had to Say Yes
clips.summary: Geiger recounts his first exercise in grantsmanship, needing Western Reserve, Sidney Kark's University of Natal Medical School in Durban, and the Rockefeller Foundation each to say yes, and writing each that the other two already had, securing funding to go before 1960 and the full-scale ideological apartheid of Verwoerd.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Window of Opportunity
clips.summary: Geiger describes the window before full apartheid when South Africa flirted with a national health service, the Karks and other medical students taking over a clinic in overcrowded Alexandra Township to build arguably the first community health center, and Sidney Kark's systematic study documenting appalling morbidity and mortality among African children.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Pholela and Lamontville
clips.summary: Geiger recounts his four and a half months in 1958 at the rural Pholela health center and the Zulu housing project at Lamontville near Durban, meeting people from the African National Congress and the Indian National Congress, and learning what it meant to assume total responsibility for a community's health, housing, food, water, and disease.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The Social Pathology of Syphilis
clips.summary: Geiger describes Sidney Kark's paper The Social Pathology of Syphilis, tracing the disease at Pholela to apartheid's migrant-labor system that sent working-age men away on eleven-month contracts, and the center's use of indigenous community health workers, a community vegetable garden, and skim-milk distribution that the government called communism.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Teaching Hospital for Poor People
clips.summary: Geiger recounts returning intending to do international health, doing his medical residency on the Harvard service at Boston City Hospital because it cared for poor people, and taking more than two years off for an NIH fellowship in social sciences in medicine at Harvard, then organizing in Boston for the 1963 March on Washington.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The Medical Committee for Human Rights
clips.summary: Geiger notes Truman's executive order integrating the armed forces under the pressure of a second threatened march, then describes finishing his residency, taking a junior faculty post at the Harvard School of Public Health in 1964, and almost immediately taking a leave of absence because that summer brought the formation of the Medical Committee for Human Rights.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Because White Lives Were Valuable
clips.summary: Geiger describes being asked to be the August field coordinator for the Medical Committee for Human Rights in Jackson, his misgiving at the irony that the summer project would work because the nation valued the white volunteers' lives, and his sense that the murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner had registered in the national consciousness.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: How Can You Be Sleeping
clips.summary: Geiger recounts flying to Jackson to a full-scale MCHR operation, a rotating crew fanning out to support the freedom schools and voter registration under COFO, meeting Aaron Henry of the NAACP, and contrasting volunteers who documented brutal conditions reminiscent of South Africa with a smaller for-show group, recalling a doctor in a motel room scolding his wife for sleeping while he was down there freedom fighting.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The First Protest Against Tuskegee
clips.summary: Geiger describes realizing in Hinds County that he need not go abroad to find such conditions, and recruiting Dr. Count D. Gibson Jr., chairman of preventive medicine at Tufts and a Georgia native, relaying that historian Susan Reverby later found the first protest letter against the Tuskegee syphilis study was written by Gibson, a study in which black sharecroppers were denied penicillin to study a natural history already published in Scandinavia.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The COFO Creep
clips.summary: Geiger recounts how Count Gibson's southern accent deepened the moment he returned to Mississippi, and the COFO creep, the trick of barely slowing at railroad crossings so as not to reveal you were an outsider by actually stopping, with Gibson's head shoved out the car window to answer the police in his accent whenever they were stopped.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: What Do We Do Now
clips.summary: Geiger describes returning to his Harvard job while a national MCHR was organized, becoming national field coordinator, going back to Mississippi on weekends, the autumn malaise of what to do after Freedom Summer, and the December meeting the Delta Ministry organized in Greenville of leftover summer people, indigenous and from outside.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The Headache That Went Away
clips.summary: Geiger recounts starting the small free Winnick Clinic in Holmes County under MCHR with money from Luke and Ruth Wilson and support from Dr. Robert Smith and psychiatrist Dr. Alvin Poussaint, and the Greenville meeting floundering until his headache lifted as he remembered Pholela, proposing that a northern medical school build a comprehensive community health center, an idea others enriched with community involvement and community control though it stayed abstract.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Let's Talk About the Deal
clips.summary: Geiger recounts being grounded by fog in Atlanta with Count Gibson, who proposed that if Geiger found the money Tufts would sponsor the project, turning it into a real plan aimed at the Office of Economic Opportunity and its principle of maximum feasible participation, and approaching William Kissick of the administration's Appalachian initiative.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Take 300,000 and Do It Now
clips.summary: Geiger describes Kissick passing him to Elizabeth Shore at OEO as a wild man with a crazy idea, a January 1965 meeting where he spent two and a half hours laying out the community health center concept and its South African models to Sanford Kravitz, and his timid request for a thirty-thousand-dollar feasibility study met with Kravitz telling him to take three hundred thousand and do it now, against a background of devastating unattended illness found in Head Start and the Job Corps.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Budget for 1.2 Million
clips.summary: Geiger describes writing the first grant application for two community health centers, a southern rural one and one at the Columbia Point housing project in House Speaker John McCormack's district, returning with a budget grown to one-point-two million dollars, and his strategy against the southern governors' veto: listing the project as Southern rural across ten states so the site stayed secret and out of any single delegation's reach.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Don't Steal Our Patients
clips.summary: Geiger describes Shriver's reservations about entering health and Mississippi, the public-health establishment's resistance embodied by Boston's George White Health Centers whose slogan was no diagnoses made and no prescriptions written, the opening of Columbia Point, scouting near Sparta, Georgia, and a call from Dr. John Hatch of the Boston Housing Authority, who had seen the grant and asked whether the southern positions were filled.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Half the Class Sat Beside Him
clips.summary: Geiger calls John Hatch the most brilliant community organizer he ever met, recounting how Hatch sued a Kentucky law school that admitted only whites and was told to sit by himself until half the white class sat beside him, then took a social-work degree at Atlanta University and, hired instantly, became the first of the group to scout Sparta, where he stood at the country club's back door to get food.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Physician Beside Dr. King
clips.summary: Geiger recounts flying to Selma after the Edmund Pettus Bridge, an MCHR physician needing to be beside Dr. King at all times because someone would try to kill him, flying with King to Montgomery and riding with him to find a place to stay, and sitting in the corner that night for the long heated discussion among King, the young James Forman, and SNCC.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Alabama's Good Samaritan Law
clips.topic: The March Argument and an Arrest
clips.summary: Geiger describes the tense night over whether to defy the injunction against marching to Montgomery and SNCC's resentment of King, the national outpouring stirred further by the murder of Reverend James Reeb, and being arrested with Dr. Richard Hausknecht and hauled before the Alabama medical society, citing the state's Good Samaritan law, then meeting John Lewis and arranging to send him and Ivanhoe Donaldson to Boston for the skull fracture from the bridge.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Concrete Shells in an All-Black Town
clips.summary: Geiger describes the OEO ban on bricks and mortar that it would relax for renovation, hearing of unfinished concrete buildings in Mound Bayou meant for the JC Campbell Junior College, and discovering an all-black town with its own government where people could vote, sheltering an integrated crew, with a forty-bed hospital run by the Knights and Daughters of Tabor that had grown from selling burial insurance.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The Dean Sitting In
clips.summary: Geiger recounts the JC Campbell shells tied up in a Jackson receivership, proposing Mound Bayou to OEO while keeping the grant out of Mississippi amid Senator Stennis's attack on the Child Development Group of Mississippi Head Start, the opening of Columbia Point on December 11, 1965, and timing a day Shriver testified on the Hill to walk past the secretary and sit in his office, an observer noting it was the first time in the sixties that the dean was sitting in.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: I Type a Hundred Words a Minute
clips.summary: Geiger recounts Shriver handing them to Julius Richmond, the deputy director, with whom he worked out an agreement by evening and, rather than wait for secretaries, typed it himself at a hundred words a minute, after which Richmond took it up to Shriver, who signed it and sent it back with a congratulatory note and a gift of raspberries, leaving them free for Mississippi.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Decoy in Batesville
clips.summary: Geiger recounts convincing OEO that prefab modules built by the same firm working in Watts would count as renovation under a lease-purchase, then conspicuously scouting Batesville as a decoy while giving the grant to Joe Disparti, who passed it through a Batesville doctor so it landed on the governor's desk, recapitulating Pholela and Lamontville in the Delta.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Three Oranges Came Up
clips.summary: Geiger describes the governor and Stennis screaming at Shriver, a roar from the state medical society and health commissioner Archie Gray, and his realization that nothing barred a grant to a Massachusetts institution to act in Mississippi, leaving the project veto-proof, then meeting the Mississippi State Medical Society at an AMA meeting where, after their insistence that care was not needed in Batesville, he named Mound Bayou and watched three oranges come up as they urged him toward the town he had wanted all along.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: I Picked Cotton for Three Weeks
clips.summary: Geiger describes John Hatch disappearing for three weeks scouting Bolivar County, returning to say he had picked cotton and lived in sharecropper shacks, knowing the leaders and the needs, and the profound destitution of the Delta as mechanized agriculture collapsed the sharecropper system, one cotton-picking machine replacing a hundred croppers.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Thirty Doctors in the Men's Room
clips.summary: Geiger recounts months addressing the Delta and Bolivar County medical societies, speaking as a doctor rather than accusing, a fifty-to-one vote to disapprove at the Greenwood country club with fifty abstentions, and thirty doctors following him into the men's room at the dinner afterward only to quietly tell him to go ahead, his first lesson that white professionals of goodwill would help if never publicly exposed, with Shriver's spy later feeding lies about him.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The Prisoner's Dock
clips.summary: Geiger recounts being placed in the prisoner's dock at the Bolivar County Medical Society meeting, the difficulty of recruiting to a village of two thousand where two failing black fraternal-order hospitals could not meet payroll, renting the old three-story mansion once occupied by Dr. T.R.M. Howard to cover their payroll, and writing a grant with consultant George Allen to merge the Knights and Daughters of Tabor and Sarah Brown hospitals into a community hospital for all the poor of Bolivar County.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Community Participation, Not Control
clips.summary: Geiger describes the merged hospital never becoming a true community hospital because the fraternal orders kept control and continued selling insurance amid conflicts of interest, while John Hatch patiently organized ten local health associations across the five hundred square miles of northern Bolivar County, home to twelve to fourteen thousand black people, using the black Baptist church as an organizational model.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Shield Against Opposition
clips.summary: Geiger recounts recruiting a remarkable staff including Catholic nurse-midwife Sister Mary Stella, nurse Chris Hansen, Joe Disparti, and Phyllis Cunningham, and going to the Bolivar County sheriff to warn that as a former journalist he knew every newspaper in the country, so any harm to the integrated crew would make headlines like Selma, and the sheriff assured him there would be no difficulty.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Wells, Privies, and Outreach
clips.summary: Geiger describes national press from the New York Times to Time Magazine attracting recruits and shielding the project, the community organization, surveys, and prenatal care underway before any clinic was built, and John Hatch's community health action division recruiting black sanitarian Andrew James from Ohio to dig wells and sanitary privies for people drinking from drainage ditches.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Fifteen, Then Twenty-Five, Then a Hundred
clips.summary: Geiger recounts renting a vacant church parsonage in Mound Bayou with the living room as a waiting room and bedrooms as exam rooms, recruiting mostly pediatricians like Leon Kruger and Roy Brown for a population whose median age was fifteen, patient numbers jumping from fifteen the first day to a hundred by the third, families renting wide-body trailers to live in, and Dr. Aaron Shirley and Dr. Robert Smith driving up from Jackson two days a week to help.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Ted Kennedy's Fifty Million
clips.summary: Geiger recounts Senator Edward Kennedy visiting Columbia Point in August 1966, taking up health as his issue, and writing legislation creating an office of health affairs within OEO with a fifty-million-dollar budget to build health centers, ending the research-and-demonstration phase and launching community health centers in a major way as congresspeople realized they could bring one home.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Pearl Bailey With an MD
clips.summary: Geiger describes how their care differed from the white practice of writing a prescription across a desk without examining the patient, and the recruitment of surgeon Harvey Sanders, physician Charles Humphrey, and Dr. Helen Barnes, a Mississippi-native obstetrician he chased for two years, along with clinical director David Weeks from Aramco, with the ten health associations sending delegates to the North Bolivar County Health Council.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Thousands Raised Their Hands
clips.summary: Geiger recounts organizing as a charitable trust when the governor would not charter a nonprofit, John Hatch turning a plan for a hundred vegetable gardens into the North Bolivar County Farm Cooperative when thousands of families volunteered, L.C. Dorsey rising from a ninth-grade dropout to deputy director and earning her GED at the center, and the office of education sending local people to nursing, medical, and pharmacy schools, including the first two sent to Tufts who returned to work there.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: The Jolly Black Giant
clips.summary: Geiger recounts training the first ten registered black sanitarians in Mississippi history and a college-prep course feeding Stony Brook, and a Ford Foundation grant that grew the farm co-op into a five-hundred-acre partly mechanized operation involving thousands of Bolivar County people who traded labor for income and crop shares, raising tons of vegetables instead of cotton.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Palliative, Not Capital
clips.summary: Geiger describes dreams of a cannery to grow and can soul food for northern markets with backing from the Jolly Green Giant company, the government refusing to grant them a defunct white-run cannery thirty miles away, making clear it favored palliative programs for black communities over capital programs and real entrepreneurship, and the health council shifting independently funded programs under its own control.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Bus Network Across the Delta
clips.summary: Geiger recounts the health council running an emergency food program, a library and cultural center, early-childhood intervention, a bus network across the ten towns that also became a route to jobs, and Mrs. Robinson in Round Lake organizing meals and company for isolated elderly people, framing it all as intervening in the social determinants of health, futile otherwise, like rehydrating an infant only to send the child back to drink from the same drainage ditch.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: A Map With Measles
clips.summary: Geiger reflects that the work, involving hundreds of thousands of professionals, grew into more than twelve hundred community health centers at over eight thousand sites that look like measles on a map of the United States, serving more than twenty million low-income people, urban and rural, in housing projects, schools, and the migrant streams.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Fifty-One Percent Patients
clips.summary: Geiger explains that since 1975 every federally qualified community health center must by law be a nonprofit with an elected board at least fifty-one percent current patients, a patient voice unique in American health care, and describes his 1971 move to Stony Brook, where the health council made what he believes are the only reverse site visits by a community group, choosing it for its scholarships, sending nineteen Bolivar County people, while John Hatch became the first African American to hold an endowed chair at the University of North Carolina School of Public Health.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Training the Next Generation
clips.summary: Geiger recounts leaving Stony Brook for the new City University of New York Medical School devoted to training minority and low-income students for primary care in underserved areas, admitting them out of high school into a combined program with eight community health centers as the clinical campus, where he founded a department and worked from 1978 until nominally retiring around 1996.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Still Trying to Disturb the Peace
clips.summary: Geiger describes continuing work through national medical fellowships and a new Medical-Legal Partnerships organization that puts lawyers in health centers to address the housing and exploitation making patients sick, his hope at eighty-seven to help create a next generation with this orientation, and the interview closing with the Institute of Medicine's honor for creating the model of the contemporary community health center.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: The Son of Mrs. Emma Brown
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in San Diego in June 2016, Harold K. Brown, known as Hal, opens with his birth in York, Pennsylvania, in 1934 as one of seven children of Mrs. Emma Brown, and describes growing up poor in a segregated colored community in a county that was barely one percent Black, attending segregated schools until the seventh grade.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: The Men in the Factory Windows
clips.summary: Brown recounts the five-mile walk past white schools to his Black elementary school, where men leaned out the upper windows of a chain works factory to hurl racial insults at six-year-old children every day, and jokes that he did not know he was Black until his mother carried him home from the white hospital into the colored community.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: No Blacks in Positions of Importance
clips.summary: He reflects on the mental toll of seeing only white males in positions of importance, of being the only Black student in an integrated junior high, and links the constant message that he was less than the others to the psychological tests in which Black children chose white dolls as the good and smart ones.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: You Want to Think About College, Harold
clips.summary: Brown credits the contradictions that pushed back against the racism: his white homeroom and math teacher Cassandra Shea, who adopted him and introduced him to the idea of college and how she had worked through it cleaning houses, and his football coach who told him he ought to be a dentist, a profession he had never seen a Black person hold.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Old Black Joe Every Day
clips.summary: He remembers the only interracial images at school being slaves picking cotton, which made Black students sink down in their seats, and a music teacher who sat at the piano and made the class sing Old Black Joe seemingly every day, learning nothing of Black history until after he graduated from college.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: All-State, in His Brother's Footsteps
clips.summary: Brown describes becoming a popular high-school athlete, named all-state basketball player his senior year after his brother had been the first in their school's history to earn that status, attending Penn State on a basketball scholarship that paid only tuition, and leaving to take a tryout with the St. Louis Browns in Thomasville, Georgia.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Drafted Between Two Schools
clips.summary: He recounts being declared ineligible as a transfer at San Diego State and steered to a junior college that would have cost him his eligibility, the lack of any guiding adult in his life, and a baseball tryout where, unprepared and out of shape from a Pennsylvania winter, his arm gave out before he was drafted into the Army.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Friendships the Sports Allowed
clips.summary: Brown reflects on athletics as one of the few avenues for cross-racial friendship, noting that the relationships he built with white teammates rarely extended beyond the field or the school, that Black players socialized together at the Crispus Attucks community center, and that even in college the teams left much to be desired.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: No Jews or Niggers Allowed
clips.summary: He tells of inquiring about his draft status on a visit to Pennsylvania in 1953, being inducted at the end of the Korean War, and doing basic training at Camp Gordon, Georgia, where life on post was tolerable but the town displayed signs reading no dogs, Jews, or niggers allowed, so the Black soldiers stayed on base.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: I'm Not Going Down There
clips.summary: Stationed at Fort Monmouth, New Jersey, Brown describes the one trouble there: an assignment to Operation Sagebrush in Louisiana, which he refused by walking in to tell the post commander an enlisted man should not be sent where he was not wanted, only to be sent to the outskirts of Shreveport with everyone else.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: I Couldn't Approve That
clips.summary: Back at San Diego State after the Army, Brown recounts winning a student-council seat and chairing the Constitution Committee, where he found the white sororities and fraternities had charters restricting membership to white males or white females and refused in good conscience to approve them, drawing a meeting with the dean of students.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: For Me It Was Anger
clips.summary: He describes being one of about twenty-five Black students in an enrollment near seven thousand, the loneliness of always being the only Black person in the classroom, and how being called a slur on a San Diego street fed an anger that, as he matured, led him to decide on graduation to join the civil rights movement.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: They Don't Hire Blacks to Coach
clips.summary: Brown did postgraduate work in speech pathology and aimed to be a physical-education teacher and coach, only to realize that high schools did not hire Black coaches, and when he applied to teach in La Mesa the superintendent said the only way to hire him was to poll all the district's teachers about accepting a Negro.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: A Chapter of CORE in San Diego
clips.summary: He recounts joining the all-white El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee, being approached around 1960 by a Black man about forming a San Diego chapter of CORE, and landing a teaching job through a San Diego State schoolmate, a post he held for five years before the Peace Corps, while at first he knew nothing about CORE.
keywords: El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Cool It the First Year
clips.summary: Brown explains deciding to prove himself as a teacher before becoming active, being named one of San Diego's five outstanding new teachers, and recalls that during the 1955 Montgomery bus boycott he was still in the Army at Fort Monmouth, following the civil rights news on television though he never saw a newspaper on base.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: The Afro-American Association
clips.summary: He describes how the chapter wrote its bylaws and held elections, the tension over his prior membership in the Afro-American Association (labeled a Black nationalist group by the media) that taught Black history and the plight of Black people, and his first reading of Lerone Bennett Jr., which began filling his library.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Nineteen Segregated Schools
clips.summary: As chairman Brown found deplorable conditions: nineteen segregated schools, but employment discrimination as the top priority, with Black people unable to be hired even as grocery baggers or downtown workers, recalling earlier demonstrations against Woolworth's led by the Black dentist Dr. Jack Kimbrough.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: We Don't Rent to Negroes
clips.summary: Brown turns to housing, where Black people could not rent or buy outside Logan Heights, and tells how the woman he married in 1967, a math major recruited from Columbus, Ohio by General Dynamics as a computer programmer, was turned away from apartment after apartment and had to stay at the Y, which is how he met her while she was buying a car.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: A Hundred Friends in the Pool
clips.summary: He recounts helping his future wife find an apartment with a pool whose manager refused, blaming an owner in Canada, and how Brown first proposed knocking on every tenant's door to ask if they objected to a Negro neighbor, then threatened to bring a hundred Black friends to swim in the pool, at which the manager suddenly found a vacancy.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: My Jail Sentences Came From the Bank
clips.summary: Brown describes covenants and demonstrations against the California Real Estate Association, and the biggest project: a statewide campaign as Western Region chairman of CORE against Bank of America and San Diego Gas and Electric to open employment to Black workers, which earned him his jail sentences, and his friendships with national CORE leaders James Farmer and Floyd McKissick.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Counting Pennies at the Teller Window
clips.summary: He details the tactics: marches and sit-ins, mail-ins that sent bank payments over or under the balance as a nuisance, and coin-ins where members brought sacks of pennies to the teller window and counted them out one at a time while others sat on the floor singing, tactics he calls very effective in forcing the companies to start hiring.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: We Laid Our Jobs on the Line
clips.summary: Brown recalls being summoned to Sacramento before the state board to explain why his teaching credential should not be revoked over his CORE leadership, going with a CORE lawyer to defend himself, keeping the credential, and serving his jail time on weekends so he could keep teaching, calling it an intimidating, scary time.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: More White Members Than Black
clips.summary: He reflects that the San Diego chapter was about two-thirds white, that recruiting Black members was hard work because many Black people were afraid of reprisals and unwilling to risk what little they had, and that some friends would not even admit to their white acquaintances that they knew him.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: The NAACP I Couldn't Find
clips.summary: Brown discusses the Black community's origins through the Navy and migration from Texas, a settlement of Negroes in Ramona, the small Latino population then in Logan Heights, and the other organizations: an NAACP so invisible he tried and failed to find its office, an Urban League present but uninvolved, and the fading Afro-American Association.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Fighting on Two Fronts
clips.summary: He describes a less-known struggle: fighting white racism outside while battling infiltration inside, with the peace movement seeking influence he refused, and accusations of communism and Trotskyism he could not even identify, all part of a campaign to oust him and take over the chapter's leadership.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: More Painful Than the Companies
clips.summary: Brown calls the internal fighting more painful than the fight against the discriminating companies because it came from people inside the organization, both Black and white, and recounts that after his jail sentences he resigned around 1967, after which CORE faded and the conflict between the Black Panthers and the US Organization that followed in San Diego was only hearsay to him.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: A Room Above the Airport
clips.summary: Brown turns to the Peace Corps, where he served as deputy director in Lesotho, a country ringed by South Africa, and tells of being held with his wife in a room above the Johannesburg airport reserved for Africans under apartheid, deciding to test it by walking the airport until an embassy man quietly checked that he was all right.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: The Peace Corps Was Black to Them
clips.summary: He recounts landing in Lesotho under Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan to a joyous welcome, the shock of the Basotho when the white Peace Corps volunteers later arrived, his speaking engagements in Maseru where people asked how Black Americans were treated, and the Charge d'Affaires displeasure that he told the truth and exposed America.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: The King Walked In
clips.summary: Brown describes how the assassination of Martin Luther King was too much to bear from outside the country, leading him to organize a memorial service at the AME church in Lesotho, where the house-arrested King Moshoeshoe II, who had opposed apartheid, walked in to ululation and sat through the whole service as Brown gave the remarks and his wife sang.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: McKissick Enterprises and the Bank
clips.summary: He recounts returning to the States, being unable to find a San Diego position, joining Floyd McKissick at McKissick Enterprises in Harlem, then working as a loan officer at Marine Midland Bank on Wall Street, until an old San Diego acquaintance called offering a post as assistant to the president at San Diego State to help start a Black studies program.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: The First Black Administrator
clips.summary: Brown describes starting at San Diego State in September 1971 as the university's first Black administrator, hiring newly minted PhDs, establishing a major and minor and winning the major coup of getting the courses to satisfy general-education requirements, then leaving after two years for the directorship of computer services, with the program now the full-fledged Africana Studies department.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: The Black Economic Development Task Force
clips.summary: He traces his later path: associate dean in the College of Business Administration until retiring in 2004 after twenty-six years, then real estate development, and the 1987 founding of the Black Economic Development Task Force and a community economic development certificate program, which the university kept running because of him until he retired.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Ninety Percent of the Work Is You
clips.summary: Brown reflects that his programs could not pay for themselves the way Columbia or NYU programs could, that a Leadership Training Program he founded after CORE drew praise even from the Labor Department before fading when he left for the Peace Corps, and that in nonprofit work he founded, ninety percent of the work fell to him.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Community Leaders Undoing Biases
clips.summary: He describes forming an organization at the business school, the acronym CLUB for Community Leaders Undoing Biases, that brought prominent San Diego multimillionaires together monthly over lunch to discuss race, and how after ten years he tired and no one took his place, defending himself against the charge that he failed to delegate by asking, delegate to whom.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: How Do You Get to That Point
clips.summary: Asked what should happen now, Brown says we must learn the lessons of the past, including the old fight within CORE over whether integration belonged in its constitution, and frames the question driving his planned book: how a mild, shy athlete became willing to sacrifice his life and career, militant though not insane.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: How We Fit Into Capitalism
clips.summary: Brown argues the movement, busy kicking doors open for jobs and education, never addressed how Black Americans fit into capitalism, and that even first-class citizenship is not enough; he began years ago thinking about building wealth, which is why he founded the economic-development efforts, insisting that the word capital matters and that people must learn to save.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: They Couldn't Put Those Two Words Together
clips.summary: He recalls that in 1987 Black people could barely join the words Black and economics, that many were taught wealth is bad, and that a chief obstacle is never having learned to work together outside the church, urging a real focus on saving and on understanding the capital that real estate development and other ventures require.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: We Hit a Wall
clips.summary: Brown observes that after the civil rights movement Black people hit a wall, that some got good jobs like his own while poverty, lack of education, and the criminal justice system persist, and that Black Americans became great spenders rather than generators of jobs, tying this to a Georgia activist's definition of Black power as economic power and self-determination.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: They Have to See It Happen
clips.summary: He explains that self-determination motivated him to refuse the white-led peace movement and Trotskyites who wanted to impose their will on CORE, and that after retiring he went into real estate and land development to make an example, so children could see wealth built not by an athlete or movie star but by a man like himself.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: How Much Did Your Shoes Cost
clips.summary: Brown relates how his son, who played basketball for Stanford, found kids asking only about the cost of his shoes and his car, and laments that even his accomplished fraternity brothers, doctors and lawyers and educators, had no grounding in business or economics, which he had urged through Black economic summits since the 1980s as the movement's next step.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: CORE Had a Voice
clips.summary: He observes that in the CORE days the city fathers heeded the organization but now the Black community has no voice, recounts meeting the San Diego Urban League's CEO and assembling a think tank of men to advise him, and notes the same need across the Latino, Filipino, and Asian communities.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Forced Through the Schools
clips.summary: Asked whether Black Lives Matter or the Bernie Sanders movement give him hope, Brown answers no, says change requires anger and commotion, hoped Black studies programs would force the lessons but sees no sign of it, and notes that San Diegans his own age do not even know there was a civil rights movement in San Diego.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Stuck in Its Corner
clips.topic: Black Studies for the Whole University
clips.summary: Brown recalls speaking to a 1972 San Diego State class that knew nothing of the movement, his goal of marketing the African American Studies program to the whole university rather than only Black students, his being ousted over internal matters, and his belief that figures like Jesse Jackson and John Lewis could tell of the infighting that limited the movement's progress.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: My Nervous System Was Shot
clips.summary: Brown discloses that when he left CORE his nervous system was completely shot, close to a nervous breakdown, likens it to the post-traumatic stress of soldiers returning from war, notes activists who were torn apart and ended up strung out on drugs, and returns to his conviction that the answer is to get economic-minded.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Not a Word About Economics
clips.topic: A Closing Plea for Economic Leadership
clips.summary: Brown praises the broadcaster Tony Brown, whose KPBS program preached Black business and wealth and showed how little Black people spent in their own communities, laments that leaders like John Lewis and Jesse Jackson never spoke of economics, and closes wishing he could press the point on Hillary Clinton, before the project's standard sign-off.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: He Ran Up Two Flights to Enroll
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Midtown Manhattan in July 2011, Jack Greenberg recalls starting at Columbia Law School with no thought of civil rights work, since the label did not yet exist, until he saw Walter Gellhorn's seminar called Legal Survey on the bulletin board and ran up two flights of stairs to enroll in what amounted to an early form of clinical legal education on real cases.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: A Course Named to Hide From the Faculty
clips.summary: Greenberg explains that Gellhorn called it Legal Survey because naming it Civil Rights risked the charge of being a Communist, a charge that twice summoned Gellhorn before Congress, and recounts taking the course four semesters in a country with only a handful of civil rights lawyers, until the departure of LDF attorney Marian Wynne Perry opened a slot he was recommended to fill.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Thurgood Marshall's Small Band
clips.summary: Greenberg describes the tiny Legal Defense and Educational Fund he joined in 1949 under an already nationally known Thurgood Marshall, fresh from the restrictive covenant and voting rights cases, alongside first assistant Robert L. Carter, Franklin H. Williams (later ambassador to Ghana), a full-time staff sociologist, and himself, a unit that spun off from the NAACP over tax exemption in 1957.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: A Cosmic Decision
clips.summary: Greenberg recounts trying the Topeka, Kansas school case with Robert L. Carter on two tracks, the measurable inequality of black schools and the argument that segregation itself is inequality, and the stunned silence when Brown v. Board of Education came down in May 1954, a cosmic decision that swept away all state-imposed segregation and provoked vehement Southern resistance because schools meant mass numbers and the taboo of mixing the sexes.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Thirty-Five Hundred Acquitted
clips.summary: Becoming director-counsel in 1961 as the sit-in movement spread like wildfire and students were arrested across the South, Greenberg recalls that there were no ready defenses because no one had needed them before, and that the LDF, armed with constitutional arguments, ended up representing some thirty-five hundred people and getting them all acquitted.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: A Pro and Anti Review
clips.summary: Greenberg describes writing Race Relations in American Law, published in 1959 as the first book on the subject since about 1919, and its curious reception: the Columbia Law Review ran both a pro and an anti review as if the field were controversial, set against Herbert Wechsler's Toward Neutral Principles and Edmond Cahn's attack on Brown for its use of social science, while a reviewer he approached begged off because he was white and married to a black woman.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: An Ice Cream Cone at the Airport
clips.summary: Greenberg recalls bringing cases to desegregate segregated airport terminals, including one in Atlanta and one in New Orleans where co-counsel A.P. Tureaud, long the only black lawyer in the city, brought his granddaughter to buy an ice cream cone she had never been allowed before, and the 1963 program he launched giving black lawyers ten thousand dollars to open practices where there were none, seeding figures like Julius Chambers, Mel Watt, and Marian Wright Edelman.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: The Market Did Not Take Care of It
clips.summary: As the Civil Rights Act of 1964 took shape after Kennedy's bill and assassination, Greenberg describes crafting a litigation program: public accommodations proved easy since few owners truly resisted, hotels dodged the law with private clubs handing white guests membership cards, and Title VII employment brought the bitterest fights against labor unions that were pro civil rights nationally but kept segregated white and black locals.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: My Left Flank Is in Retreat, I Attack
