clips.summary: Greenberg traces the school fight into the late 1960s, the real estate stakes and Nixon's Southern Strategy of resisting integration for political advantage, and the maneuver in Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education when Senator John C. Stennis held up the armed services appropriation and HEW Secretary Robert Finch asked the court of appeals not to decide, forcing the LDF to reach the Supreme Court through the ancient common-law writ of certiorari, a gambit Greenberg likened to Marshal Foch's line at the Marne.
related_events: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: There He Stood Naked
clips.summary: Greenberg recounts Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education, where the outcome was uncertain and a preliminary vote favored upholding the lower court, until Warren Burger found himself outvoted in conference and, as retired Chief Justice Earl Warren told Greenberg, stood naked, so Burger switched his vote to control the opinion, assigned it to himself, and wrote an opinion calling for quotas, busing, and integrated teachers, while burying a last paragraph warning that one day this would have to end.
related_events: Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Fifty Yards Across the Line
clips.summary: Greenberg assesses Milliken v. Bradley in 1974 and San Antonio v. Rodriguez, judging Milliken significant for drawing the line at the political boundary between city and suburb, so that two families living fifty yards apart fell under separate school systems, which he calls a political decision rather than a worked-out legal one, before the recording breaks off.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: He Wanted All Ten of Us in Heaven
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Austin, Texas, on May 26, 2011, James Oscar Jones opens with his parents, Ernest and Curtis Jones of rural Arkansas, a father who raised all ten children (five from the mother's first marriage after that husband was killed cutting logs) on two goals: that all ten be educated and that all ten reach heaven, through church, hard work, and never missing a day of school.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: He Bought His First Acre at Thirteen
clips.summary: Jones describes the four-o'clock mornings milking cows before the bus, his father buying his first acre at age thirteen for a dollar and a quarter, adding to land from the McKinney side, and farming cotton and corn funded by cash from peach orchards and produce because the Farmers Home Administration would not lend to him.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: A Few Bucks to Go to the Fair
clips.summary: He recounts how his father gathered school dropouts who wandered after being kept out for farm work, paid them at the end of the week, and gave late-evening work so they would have money for the fair, escaping the plantation debt system where laborers were put on the books and never earned enough to pay off.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Everybody Knew Her as Vic
clips.summary: Jones tells how his mother, formally Curtis Jones but known everywhere as Vic, drew hundreds to her funeral, attended NAACP meetings in Magnolia, and brought back to church people the message that they must improve their own homes and stop depending on outsiders, loading neighbors into a pickup truck without asking permission.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Stand Still at Twelve Noon
clips.topic: The Brown Decision on the Radio
clips.summary: He remembers his father's powerful radio that pulled in St. Louis baseball, Thurgood Marshall asking every Negro to stand still at noon for the Supreme Court announcement, his father alerting the grocery store and the little Sweet Home school so everyone would hear Brown, and how he watched white farmers to acquire broken plows the vocational students rewelded for the family.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Take This Quarter Up to the Poor Pit
clips.summary: Jones describes being sent with his brothers to seal and chink the leaky houses of families who would be tossed off the land if they repaired them, at no charge, and his father making a brother return a quarter he had accepted by dropping it in the church offering, because the work was something they were to do for folks.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: I'm Saving for Hard Time
clips.topic: Pushed at School and Paid Nothing
clips.summary: He recalls learning from older siblings that his parents told teachers to push him hard because he was smart, being handed every school assignment from MC to agriculture leader, his father refusing to pay his own children because he was saving for hard time, and the day his father gave him twenty-five dollars at a district basketball tournament that made other kids call him rich.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Go to the Head of the Line
clips.summary: Jones describes how his parents followed Little Rock's Central High closely, and his mother telling him that since the family had already integrated a local bus line by riding in the front seat, he should never be afraid to act for other people, never go to the back of the line, but go to the head and be brave about it.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: We Weighed Our Own Cotton
clips.summary: He tells how, while white gin operators shorted other Black sharecroppers by tens of pounds per bag, his mother, father, uncle Bluford, and aunt sat at the gin with double-barreled shotguns and shells between their fingers so the Jones family could weigh their own cotton, with the children watching the theft done to everyone else.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: They Came to Our House to Kill Him
clips.summary: Jones recounts his brother Troy, working a railroad job, refusing to leave the train at a small Arkansas town that barred Negroes, stabbing and cutting white men before being shot and escaping through the woods, and the night a white mob came down the dirt road to the farm only to be shot back and disarmed by his father and relatives.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: You Look Just Like Ernest Jones
clips.summary: He describes reaching the city of Pine Bluff and its college, carrying a letter from his brother in Milwaukee to a vocational professor who recognized him on sight as Ernest Jones's son, walked him around campus calling him one of his boys, and told the faculty to keep their eyes on the good student from a small-town high school that had taught algebra, trigonometry, physics, and chemistry.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Built With Dormitories for the Quakers
clips.summary: Jones explains that his hometown high school, one of two all-Black school districts able to float bonds, was built around 1929 with dormitories by a New York group he thought were Quakers, drew handpicked teachers who lived on campus, and gave students real books rather than white schools' castoffs, an unusually strong start.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: You've Already Done What We Tried to Do
clips.summary: He recounts his professor housing him and introducing him to organizers trying to start a civil rights effort, and Worth Long and Bill Hansen arriving to tell him that the upbringing he described, integrating buses and refusing back doors, meant he had already done what they had been trying to do, and that the college town's heavy segregation needed organizing.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: I'm a White Boy and I Don't Need to Be Leading
clips.topic: Why They Wanted a Black Leader
clips.summary: Jones describes Worth Long's labor-union background and Bill Hansen's view that as a white man he should not lead, that they needed a Black student to challenge segregated lunch counters, and Hansen testing whether Jones's parents would object, to which Jones answered that they had raised him on going through front doors and would support him, settling that his mother was already civil rights.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: They Want to Be Thugs
clips.summary: He tells of recruiting students with the rule that anyone whose parents would object should stay home, and a principal steering him to brave, rough teenagers at Townsend Park High School, whom Jones won over by telling his own story and explaining that this would be a nonviolent group even when they were hit in the face and knocked down.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Diane Nash Said It's Open
clips.summary: Jones recounts the first day in February 1963 finding Woolworth locked, then Diane Nash, a very light-skinned veteran of the Freedom Rides, going in and being served as white before telling them it was open, and her strategy of crowding the counter at eleven o'clock and falling toward the white patrons when the beating started.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: The President Suspended Us
clips.summary: He describes the college president, Dr. Lawrence A. Davis Sr., calling the students in and parents arriving to snatch their children home, leaving only three or four including Jones and a young woman, Joanne, who later became a professor there, with the president declaring anyone who went downtown the next day suspended.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: They Locked Us In and Turned the Gas On
clips.summary: Jones calls the Magnolia sit-in the most violent, where the owner locked the students inside and turned on a stinging fog gas while an armed crowd waited outside, and how he survived by recalling his brother Troy's advice about water against gas, turning on the sink and getting everyone to put their heads in the water.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: I'll Bond Everybody Out of Jail
clips.summary: He recounts the Black business community coming down hard, with only two backers: Mr. Brown the funeral home owner, who quietly promised to bond everyone out of jail and whose son agreed to help, and a prominent dentist who offered to fix free of charge anyone hit in the mouth.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: My Church Door Is Open Day or Night
clips.summary: Jones tells of Reverend B.E. Allen, pastor of a large Methodist church, appearing in a blue suit to vouch for the students as future leaders, his Methodist standing seeming to calm the police, his offer of his church day or night, and the Townsend Park boys agreeing to stay nonviolent through the first arrest.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: A Real Fear Moment
clips.summary: He describes the first successful restaurant integration in Pine Bluff in March and the looming reality of mass arrests, the college crowd's nervousness against the young toughs who had already done jail time, and the quiet reassurance of seeing supporters like Mr. Coleman watching from across the street.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: The Old Black People Came Out
clips.summary: Jones recounts the jailings drawing out the older Black population, who packed Reverend B.E. Allen's church with rolls of money to bond the students, brought fried chicken the jailers eventually let them eat, and shifted the city so that white reaction turned to thrown objects and slurs rather than knockdowns, even as a campus rumor that demonstrations would cost the football team its scholarships frightened the players.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: I'll Handle the Case
clips.topic: A Young Lawyer and the Hotel
clips.summary: He describes the small Black law firm whose lawyer, later a Bill Clinton federal-judge appointee, walked into the church to say he would handle the case before leaving, and returning to Woolworth and a hotel that quietly desegregated by asking that Black guests come after dark to avoid white retaliation.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: My Wife Never Went Back
clips.summary: Jones recounts that the first suspension wave left three or four who later returned to school, that he never did, and that the woman he married on May 16, 1964 was in the first Woolworth group though not arrested at the hotel, and like seven or eight others never went back to college.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: They Made Jim Jones the Head
clips.summary: He reflects on how the interracial environment hardened resentment because whites like Bill Hansen were perceived as leading, how the group deliberately had Jones launch mass meetings so Black audiences would respond, how Bill and Ruthie's interracial marriage inflamed Pine Bluff, and how Jones was formally made head of the effort to refocus attention off the white organizers.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: They Going to Shoot at You
clips.summary: As the project-director title shifted to him in 1964, Jones describes shutting down the aggressive Hansen and choosing voter-registration targets in the hard, conservative plantation country of West Helena, Forrest City, and Marianna, insisting that he and Worth Long go because white reaction there would key on Hansen's skin, and Mrs. Clay of Forrest City opening her house to him.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: A Direct Connection to the Community
clips.summary: Jones describes traveling to Atlanta SNCC coordinating meetings where the educated big-city organizers showed off with big words and theory while he spoke concretely about the rural people he served, and James Forman telling him his direct connection to the community was a wonderful thing.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Put Your Shoes On and Knock on Doors
clips.summary: He recounts asking Atlanta to send a great singing group rather than organizers because his church-folk crowds would love it, getting people to knock on doors for voter registration, and persuading a respected plant manager in Forrest City to run and win a local office, alongside Mrs. Clay's help and the SNCC drive toward the 1966 governor's race.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Some Folks Were Stuck
clips.summary: Jones argues that some activists saw only public accommodation and, once restaurants and hotels opened, wanted to chase distant fights like protesting in Chicago, while he pushed the new piece, getting this population access to voting and running candidates, faulting those who could not move past the one idea they had.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Go Talk to Mrs. Clay
clips.summary: He describes a voter-registration week in the Delta following advice to find, in nearly every community, the woman who knew the politically active people, going first to the Black barbershop in Forrest City where the men pointed him to Mrs. Clay, who offered him a choice of her houses to stay in and, with her principal sister, packed a church that night to launch a candidate.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: By Then a Lot More Were Elected
clips.summary: Jones recalls another woman in Forrest City who had run two years earlier without votes and joined him eagerly, her college-educated son, and returning later to find many more Black officials elected and a man he had worked with become an attorney and, by Bill Clinton's appointment, a federal judge, with a health clinic among the gains in East Arkansas.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: I Don't Get Mad With Folks
clips.summary: Jones reflects on his personality of incredible tolerance, never seeing low turnout as failure, preferring a small high-energy group to seventy-five reluctant people, the way some folks do in church, and crediting his ability to recruit and identify people who then took off and did a lot of things.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: I See So Many Babies
clips.summary: He tells of a Georgia town gutted by a factory closing, where residents in a meeting dreamed of building department stores and high-rises until Jones pointed out that with so many children no one could go to work, and a prominent schoolteacher revealed her brother, a hustler and gambler, owned the big empty warehouse standing there.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Are You Jesus Christ?
clips.summary: Jones describes persuading the gambler brother to give the group his warehouse for a nursery so the mothers could take jobs at a nearby military base hiring heavy labor, and the women tearing out walls and hauling lumber to the street themselves, astonishing the owner and a passing lumberman who asked if Jones was God.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Stack Them In Like Sardines
clips.summary: He recounts a lumber company donating used but good wood, the women completing the nursery, and his advice to refuse federal money because square-footage-per-child rules would price out most families, where privately owning it let them keep many children and put non-working mothers to work caring for them, a self-running model he credits to believing folks can do it themselves.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: It Was Not a Lifetime Situation
clips.summary: Turning to his 1965 departure for Atlanta, Jones describes the SNCC staff in Arkansas of about eight, evenly Black and white, with racial-leadership tensions sharpening amid the hot summer of 1965 and the first riots, and his sense that resources and the people with early energy were drifting toward making a living, while white volunteers wanted a lifetime situation the work could not be.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: SNCC Went As Far As It Could
clips.summary: He recalls telling Bill Hansen and the men that they had done a good job but were stretching the work thin with no income, that as the Black Panthers and Stokely Carmichael tried to rebuild something new this phase was ending, and that with aging organizers needing jobs and Black officials being elected, SNCC had reached the limit of its capabilities and could not be a lifetime instrument.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: They Wanted the Top Gun
clips.summary: Asked about Worth Long's challenge to abandoning integration and the rise of Bobby Brown and the Black United in Little Rock, Jones says he heard of it from afar and saw it as a small faction satisfying Brown and Stokely Carmichael, who he believed always wanted to be the top gun and a next Malcolm X for the media rather than focusing on benefits to people.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Closer, Fresher, a Lot Cheaper
clips.summary: Working with sharecroppers, Jones describes his assignment identifying small farmers across Virginia, Georgia, Florida, and South Carolina and connecting them to national chain stores eager for closer, fresher, cheaper Black-farmer produce, against others fixated on the big farmers' supposed money and high-volume shipping that overlooked five acres of tomatoes harvested four times and sold to a nearby Kroger.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: I Always Saw Them As Contacts
clips.summary: Asked whether slow economic progress made him doubt the integrationist model, Jones says he never thought it was the only way, that he used well-connected white volunteers not for their money but as contacts for outlets and college scholarships in the Northeast and Ohio, advising farmers' children that with no one to run the land they should hold property that would later be worth fortunes in condos.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Trees of Dollars Falling Out of the Sky
clips.summary: Jones credits his farm background with keeping him grounded against movement people whose theories depended on government action the people were not ready for, and recounts goals that did not happen, recruiting nurses and doctors to small-town hospitals across East Arkansas, while finding satisfaction in the growth of young people who excelled and moved on rather than reading their departure as disappointment.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Ninety-Five Percent Were Dead and Gone
clips.topic: On Compensation and a Life's Work
clips.summary: Jones closes by wishing the fifty thousand Black farmers denied loans could have been paid, but noting that by the time recognition came most were dead and their children scattered to Chicago and Detroit, doubting that compensating a grandchild who never knew the farming grandfather honors the hard work, and saying every job kept him close to agriculture and small towns working on behalf of people, the joy of his life, before the interviewer's thanks and the closing tag.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: My Sister and I Rode the Bus Line
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Atlanta in April 2011, Jamila Jones opens with her Montgomery upbringing by her mother and grandmother alongside her sister Doris and a younger brother, and how she and Doris, riding the Montgomery bus line to school, experienced the driver's daily harassment that drew them toward the bus boycott.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: She Taught Us We Were Important
clips.topic: Rosa Parks and the Youth Council
clips.summary: Jones recalls her sister introducing her to Rosa Parks through the NAACP Youth Council, where Parks read from National Office papers and taught the young people that they mattered, that they should learn their rights, that voting was vital, and that they should carry themselves responsibly, then served them Kool-Aid afterward.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: The 381 Days, We Walked
clips.summary: Jones describes a youth movement that organized itself to cross town once the buses were abandoned, her sister refusing rides so as not to take an adult's space, walking the full 381 days, and the joy at the boycott's end when riders no longer had to take the abuse she had endured on the bus since age six.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: The White Lady With the Red Hair
clips.summary: Riding from the first stop, Jones tested sitting at the front and had to give up her seat for one red-haired white woman she never forgot, and recounts reaching up to touch the woman's hair as if by accident to learn what made her different, concluding the hair was no different and resolving to walk however long it took.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: We Practiced Freedom Songs
clips.summary: Jones tells how she and other girls at the Alabama State Laboratory High School formed a group to compete in a Friday talent show and, though about Diana Ross's age, chose freedom songs, so that when the boycott came they were ready and were invited to sing at the teas held all over Montgomery to raise money for the movement, naming Gladys Carter and Minnie Hendrix among the four.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Reverend Seay Each Sunday
clips.topic: Churches and a High School Influence
clips.summary: Jones explains the girls sang mostly at school at first because they attended different churches, hers being Mount Zion AME Zion, and names Reverend Solomon Seay, who held the first meeting for the bus boycott and spoke each Sunday about the community's needs, as a formative influence on her commitment to the freedom struggle through her high school years to 1962.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: The Montgomery Gospel Trio
clips.topic: Highlander, New York, and a Name
clips.summary: Jones recounts being selected for a cultural exchange at the Highlander Folk School, where she met people from around the world including Africa, and traveling to New York with Reverend Sheldon's help to record one of the first albums of civil rights fundraising songs, an occasion that finally forced the unnamed group, once just called the girls, to choose the name Montgomery Gospel Trio.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: That Day We Stole a Car
clips.summary: Jones recalls that the adults' wounds from the movement left the youth to act, so when James Bevel and Bernard LaFayette arrived with the Freedom Riders and Juanita Abernathy had no one to fetch them, the young people borrowed Antoinette Carson's grandfather's car, chalk-marked its tires to return it exactly, and went to pick up the riders, witnessing how they had been beaten getting off the buses.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: We're Going to Do Like the Doodlebug
clips.summary: Jones reflects that youthful fear never outweighed the need to participate, and describes the night the Freedom Riders reached the mass meeting and the church was surrounded by armed white men on horses, where Reverend Solomon Seay, who called her doodlebug, answered her fear with the children's doodlebug song about a house on fire, a meaning she grasped only as an adult, and they spent the night inside.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Let's Continue to Sing
clips.summary: Jones clarifies she was not in the original Freedom Singers, keeping the Montgomery Gospel Trio going through Alabama State while singing voter-registration meetings for James Bevel's group in rural areas, then coming to Atlanta to work with Bernice Johnson Reagon on the Pinpoint Festival, after which she and Mattie Casey of the Harambee Singers knocked on Reagon's door asking to keep singing and started the Harambee Singers.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: This Little Light of Mine
clips.summary: Jones explains that what they sang depended on the moment and the message, that they learned at Highlander a song could carry the movement's message anywhere, and names This Little Light of Mine, We Are Soldiers in the Army, and Old Freedom among the songs they performed at eleven and twelve years old that later became the basis of the movement's repertoire, with Jones singing melody and much of the lead.
keywords: We Are Soldiers in the Army

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Ain't Gonna Ride No Buses No More
clips.summary: Jones credits a professor at Alabama State with writing the first song for the Montgomery movement, describes the group as traditional singers who at first only added a word or line, and tells how, after seeing others rework songs at Highlander with Guy Carawan, they began writing their own, including a reworked traditional song whose pointed line asked whether the white folks knew they would not ride the buses anymore.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Do You Have to Sing So Loud
clips.topic: The We Are Not Afraid Verse
clips.summary: Jones recounts traveling to Highlander by night at fourteen on Reverend Seay's advice, and the night police cut the town's power to plunge the workshop into darkness, when someone began the We Are Not Afraid verse of We Shall Overcome and the whole room joined, growing louder until a visibly shaken policeman asked her not to sing so loud, the moment she understood the power of the music as the voices blended in the dark.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: That Verse Steadied My Knees
clips.summary: Jones describes a later march to the Montgomery capitol, leaving from King's church when he had returned for a campaign, and facing bare-chested white men with rifles on horses at the top of the steps, where with her knees shaking she sang the We Are Not Afraid verse again and it steadied her.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Twenty-Seven Page Questionnaires
clips.summary: Jones recounts her college years working voter registration and sit-ins with James Bevel and James Orange of SCLC, venturing beyond a tightly watched Montgomery, learning nonviolence training to protect oneself when spat upon, and going back to age twelve when Rosa Parks and Mrs. Williams taught the youth in a hairdresser's shop to fill out long questionnaires and recite the Preamble so they could teach adults to register.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Hands Off Nkrumah
clips.summary: Jones explains that by the founding of the Harambee Singers around 1966 she had turned her attention to Black Power and the Pan-African movement, so the group wore Afros and sang radical international songs that no longer included the Montgomery trio's repertoire, among them Hands Off Nkrumah, which named the CIA amid the upheaval in Ghana.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Sitting Under the Table
clips.summary: After a break, Jones turns to the community that shaped her, recalling Flora Grant of Atlanta Life Insurance who kept close to movement news and talked with her mother as Jones listened from under the card table, the woman who wrote See How They Run, the early Black jockey Sullivan Rogers, and her storytelling aunt she called Besidee.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Pry, Boy
clips.topic: A Sharecropper's Tale and a Song
clips.summary: Jones retells her aunt Besidee's story of a sharecropper called John who, unable to feed his family despite working from sun up to sun down, taught his young son to pry up the white man's chicken coop so he could take a single chicken, the boy trembling as he held it, a tale that so unsettled her she later wrote a song from it, and she closes that such stories and songs taught the young what their choices would make of their lives and keep her, at sixty-seven, still fighting for freedom.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Birmingham Man on One Gallon of Gas
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in her Washington home in December 2015, Jennifer Lawson opens on her birth in Fairfield, Alabama, in June 1946, and on a father born in 1899 in Bullock County, the fourteenth of fifteen children, who ran away from the farm at fifteen with a third-grade education to find work in the Birmingham coal mines.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Lawson's Repair
clips.summary: Lawson describes how her father taught himself to fix tractors and cars by taking them apart and reassembling them, opened a shop he called Lawson's Repair that mended washing machines and refrigerators, and took her along on calls to mining camps and company towns like Powderly and Bessemer that served Tennessee Coal and Iron.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Learning to Wind a Motor
clips.summary: She recalls her father teaching her and her brothers basic repairs, insisting she learn to rewire a motor from coils of copper wire, and an unfound article, said to be in Life magazine, about a Birmingham man who reached Canada on one gallon of gas by running his converted engine on kerosene, the backyard taken up by his repair shop with cars and appliances always under repair.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: A Modest Bungalow on a Mixed Block
clips.summary: Lawson sketches the economic range of her block, from the doctor and pharmacist a few streets over to the children in the alley without indoor plumbing, her family's modest bungalow in between, her father sometimes paid in barter when the mines struck, and his idea of a good evening reading the encyclopedia at random.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Moving the Barrier Up
clips.summary: She describes a totally segregated Fairfield of white-only signs on laundromats and gas stations, the bus that stopped in front of her house with its movable color barrier, and how the neighborhood children, boarding the empty bus, would slide the barrier forward so whites had nowhere to sit, drawing yells from the driver but no serious trouble.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: A Mother Who Missed the World
clips.summary: Lawson turns to her mother, a former schoolteacher who had attended college and stopped working after the children were born, doing occasional substitute teaching, a woman she calls in retrospect a lonely, depressed figure, in a time when a wife who did not work signaled that the man was a good provider.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: If He Had a Real Education
clips.summary: She explains that travel let her family escape the constricted segregated world, her father leading excursions to Washington and New York and narrating the Museum of Natural History and Hayden Planetarium with such detail the children groaned, the family joking that with a real education he would have been dangerous, and New York showing race to be arbitrary.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: The Library Above the Drugstore
clips.summary: Lawson recalls Birmingham removing seats and relegating Black patrons to segregated libraries of worn books, and the women of Fairfield starting their own community library in a room above Dr. Parham's drugstore, where she had her first paying job selling ice cream and helped maintain the shelves Mrs. Parham kept.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Talking Across the Street
clips.summary: She remembers her first consciousness of race around age five, a best friend named Teresa Ragusa from the Italian American family that ran the grocery across the street, and the day both sets of relatives told the girls they could no longer play together as elementary school began, leaving them to call across the street from their separate sides.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Kerosene on the Wound
clips.summary: Lawson describes adults who did not hide their talk of lynchings, a widely reported case of a Black man kidnapped and castrated in Birmingham whose attackers poured kerosene on the wound and inadvertently saved his life, and her childhood awareness of the Ku Klux Klan and the threatening things whites did to Black people.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: A Screening at Miles College
clips.summary: She recalls Miles College, the small Black college near her home, hosting a community screening of the banned film Imitation of Life that the segregated Carver Theater would not show, the front-porch discussions of race it sparked across generations, and the theme of passing, with families known to have a relative who had crossed over and disappeared.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: The Bombed House on Center Street
clips.summary: Lawson describes the wealthier Black families on Birmingham's Center Street, among them Angela Davis's family and an attorney whose ranch-style house was repeatedly bombed, and how urban renewal and a freeway later destroyed that traditionally Black community of nice homes.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: The Best Peach Cobbler
clips.summary: She recounts Robinson Elementary and the segregated Fairfield schools, poor in resources yet staffed by a mix of barely trained teachers and brilliant ones who in another world might have taught at an Ivy League school, and the lunchroom ladies who, using government commodities, cared enough to make food the neighborhood children would praise.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Dare to Struggle, Dare to Win
clips.summary: Lawson describes Fairfield Industrial High, named for the Booker T. Washington vision of industrial training mixed with college prep, its hand-me-down band uniforms and books from the white Fairfield High, and the tough principal E.J. Oliver, who ran it almost as a private school with homilies like dare to struggle, dare to win and inspired alumni reunions that outlasted the school.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Just These Little Cotton Pickers
clips.summary: She tells of a nighttime stop by police in Mississippi on a trip west, the officer shining his flashlight into the car and calling her father boy, her father defusing it by saying he was just looking for work with these little cotton pickers in the back, and the children's resentment at watching him be subservient to whites who were also his customers.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: A Partner of A.G. Gaston
clips.summary: Lawson recalls her father's pride in once partnering with A.G. Gaston, who became one of the first Black millionaires, visits to the gracious Gastons to swim in their pool, the Black papers the men read, the Pittsburgh Courier and Chicago Defender, and her parents voting as Republicans because Alabama's Democratic Party was tied to the Dixiecrats and white citizens' councils.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: My First Awareness Was the Boycott
clips.summary: She places her first real awareness of the movement at the Montgomery bus boycott when she was about nine, knowing who Rosa Parks was, and describes the roughly hundred-twenty-mile drive to Montgomery on pre-freeway roads through Alabaster, a speed trap where Black travelers feared being stopped by police.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: This Is Our Land
clips.topic: Summers on the Bullock County Farm
clips.summary: Lawson describes summers on her father's family land in Bullock County, where he showed the children the family's acres and a cold spring, taught her to use a rifle on tin cans, birds, and squirrels, and how she came to dislike shooting and grew fearful of guns after stories of children losing eyes to Christmas BB guns.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Glued to the Radio for the Freedom Rides
clips.summary: She recalls following the Freedom Rides as they neared Anniston and Birmingham, then Martin Luther King and the Birmingham demonstrations and the call for students to join, and how Principal Oliver locked the school doors to keep his students from leaving for the protests.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: We Disobeyed and Went Downtown
clips.topic: Kelly Ingram Park and Mass Meetings
clips.summary: Lawson tells how she and classmates defied the principal, left school, and joined the Kelly Ingram Park demonstrations where some were arrested or hit by firehoses, how her mother had by then died and her father, first upset at the disobedience, came around as a suspension turned to praise once the campaign won, and how mass meetings she attended with him gave her the positive feeling of a community gathered in a crowded church around a serious issue.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: My Sisters at Hayden Planetarium
clips.topic: A Summer Internship in New York
clips.summary: Lawson explains that in the summer of 1963 she followed the March on Washington from New York rather than attending, holding an internship at Hayden Planetarium arranged by her two older half-sisters, lab technicians there in cancer research, products of a father who, like a proverbial Jewish mother, wanted a doctor in the family.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: A Cosmopolitan Set
clips.topic: Falling in Love With New York
clips.summary: She describes loving New York at sixteen, commuting from her sister's Queens home to Manhattan, befriending a Swedish nurse and her roommates, a flight attendant and a UN worker, going to plays and experimental theater, and meeting doctors at work, one a Cornell dean who offered to help her into Cornell and its medical school.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Like Trying to Eat the Moon
clips.summary: Lawson recounts believing she could be a good doctor yet realizing she did not want to work with the dying, doing computations on a slide rule she had thrilled to master and handing results to doctors who would say a patient need not be done anymore, meaning the person had died, and returning in the summer of 1964 to try cancer research with lab animals and traced tumors, struck when a respected doctor said it had taken twenty years to isolate one small thing, like trying to eat the moon.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Why the Whites Broke Up the County
clips.summary: Lawson recalls arriving at Tuskegee, declining the sorority pitch for students talking about desegregating downtown Tuskegee, and curiously seeking out Dr. Gomillion, whose focus on the gerrymandering of Macon County, the whites breaking up the county so Black people could never form a voting majority, intrigued her though it had nothing to do with her schoolwork.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: You Don't Practice on People
clips.topic: A Scholarship and a Hospital Job
clips.summary: She explains she could attend college only on a full scholarship, got little money from home, and needed to work, taking a job at the Tuskegee hospital on the strength of her Hayden Planetarium experience, then quitting when staff pushed her, untrained, to draw blood from a young woman in labor.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Sound Waves in a Pool of Water
clips.summary: Lawson recounts winning her college scholarship, a statewide science-fair prize of full tuition and board to a Black Alabama college, with a project demonstrating sound-wave frequencies vibrating in a pool of water, assembled from parts at a Tandy hobby shop she loved as a still-gadget-oriented tinkerer.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: A Copied Monopoly Set
clips.summary: She describes her father encouraging the children to design a house expansion with an elevated platform for electric trains and ill-advised skylights, her painting stars and clouds on the playroom walls like a small Sistine Chapel, and copying a friend's Monopoly set by hand down to the box when he refused to buy one, creativity she calls a lasting strength.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Beyond the Classroom
clips.topic: A Photo Studio and an Honors Circle
clips.summary: Lawson tells of another Tuskegee job hand-tinting rosy cheeks and lipstick onto black-and-white portraits at what she later learned was a renowned studio documenting the African American community, and of an honors program that let her skip conventional classes, take calculus one-on-one and graduate-level vet-school courses in parasitology and poisonous plants, and through them mingle with professors like the veterinarian Dr. Doris Mitchell, a friend of Odetta with an imported German sports car, in a circle where serious talk of ideas meant talk of race and segregation.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: This Is Our Group
clips.summary: Lawson recounts beginning to register voters in rural Macon County, meeting Gwen Patton and a chemistry graduate student, and a cohesive Tuskegee student group that later divided, not over strategy but when Mukasa Ricks arrived as a SNCC representative and the group bristled at being told what to do.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Engaging the Larger Issues
clips.summary: She explains how, as the campus group insisted on keeping its work parochial and local to downtown Tuskegee, she found that counterproductive and leaned toward SNCC, whose dialogue ranged over the whole country and whose strategy of organizing Black Belt counties promised real change, and reflects that she was predisposed to such work by growing up in Birmingham, joining the high-school demonstrations and living through the church bombing, and by reading in a union town that spoke of John L. Lewis, planting the seed that organized people, not left alone, could have a voice that makes a difference.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: I Will Go
clips.summary: She tells how, back at Tuskegee after New York in 1964, she answered a call for student volunteers to register voters in Jackson, Mississippi for the November elections, having just missed Freedom Summer, and felt it a conscious decision she made alone, needing no parental or peer validation.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: A Little Soldier in the Unit
clips.summary: Lawson describes door-to-door canvassing in a part of Jackson where she felt at ease with the work but, shy and introverted, awkward on the social side, mistaking a couple for unmarried and finding a man's flirting off-putting after the tamer New York parties, and feeling like a little soldier in a bigger unit, not privy to the larger strategy but proud to have gone when others called Mississippi too dangerous, reasoning that from the middle of Alabama the state line hardly made it worse, even after Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Still in School When Sammy Was Killed
clips.summary: Lawson describes moving away from the campus group to work with SNCC in Wilcox and Lowndes Counties while still enrolled, the killing of her classmate Samuel Younge Jr., and a group of Tuskegee students, among them Gwen Patton and the Paris brothers, traveling to work in Lowndes and Selma, where she met Phyllis Cunningham of the medical group.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Where Do You Stand
clips.summary: Lawson explains how shuttling between Tuskegee, Selma, and Wilcox became untenable and she had to choose whether to give up her paid-for college education for full-time SNCC work with no means of support and nowhere to go between terms, not wanting to return to Fairfield or to her aging father whom she suspected was slipping into dementia, and how, torn between a professor expecting papers and a movement needing her to canvass, she grew depressed with no one to confide in, her brother a supportive but party-loving figure, until she decided to leave for SNCC and convinced herself she could return to medicine later.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: In Charge of the County
clips.summary: She describes the Tuskegee student group meeting on campus but focusing on Macon County, then joining SNCC and other groups for the Selma to Montgomery march by demonstrating in Montgomery, and the strategy of registering enough Black voters to win pluralities in Black Belt counties like Macon, Wilcox, and Lowndes and put Black people in charge of county government.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: A Different Kind of Victory
clips.summary: Lawson reflects that the MFDP's Atlantic City challenge was seen as having worked only to a point, that transforming the government of a Black Belt county like Lowndes offered a different path to victory, that Dr. Gomillion was viewed as cautious in pressing the franchise after the Voting Rights Act, and that she still wonders why Wilcox got less effort than Lowndes.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Everybody Was a Pettway
clips.summary: She describes Gee's Bend in Wilcox County as a Black supermajority enclave cut off after whites severed the bridge, a planned New Deal community of houses and a community center where everyone, descended from the Pettway plantation, carried the name Pettway, a culturally distinct and isolated place she contrasts with Macon.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Billy Clubs on Their Thighs
clips.summary: Lawson recounts James Forman and other SNCC workers at Tuskegee asking students to demonstrate in Montgomery, the group of about forty or fifty marching with picket signs in an oval before the state capitol, the former White House of the Confederacy, state troopers slapping billy clubs against their thighs, as a caravan of unrobed white men and women said to be the Klan drove up the street.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Arrest Them All
clips.summary: She describes an officer ordering arrest them all just before the caravan reached the top of the hill, the marchers going limp as their training had taught and being carried to the wagon, and the relief of being arrested rather than left between the troopers' billy clubs and the approaching vigilantes with nowhere to go.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Singing Freedom Songs Inside
clips.topic: Five Days in the Montgomery Jail
clips.summary: Lawson recalls about five days in the Montgomery County jail, unafraid because there were so many of them singing freedom songs, the camaraderie hardening as overflowing bathrooms, filth, and inedible food prompted a hunger strike, while more and increasingly diverse people including whites from the North were brought in and local women jailed for other things found the demonstrators a curiosity, then being bailed out without a court appearance, the absurd charge of resisting arrest with no underlying charge dismissed with the help of a movement lawyer she credits with creative strategies.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Afros on Auburn Avenue
clips.summary: Lawson tells of a later arrest during the Christmas holidays while working in the Atlanta SNCC office with Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Karen Edmonds Spellman, and Fay Bellamy Powell, the women closing the office and walking down Auburn Avenue for music and dinner, distinctive in their afros, as Black policemen slowed to a crawl and drove alongside them.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: To Control the Black Community
clips.summary: She recounts Fay's exasperated retort with an expletive prompting the officers, two of them Vietnam veterans, to pull over and arrest them, and reads the harassment as Black police, intended in part to control the Black community and threatened by civil rights and the rising talk of Black power, seizing an easy chance to show who held authority, dating the incident to about 1967 before turning to organizing in Lowndes County, where she first lived in Wilcox and met with Stokely Carmichael, Ralph Featherstone, Courtland Cox, and Charlie Cobb.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: You Could See the Stars Looking Up
clips.summary: She describes moving into the Lowndes Freedom House, a shack once belonging to the Jackson family that served as office by day and lodging by night, sleeping in sleeping bags in a building you could see the stars through, warming around a potbelly stove, and being valued as a key worker rather than treated as merely a girl.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: What Does Freedom Mean
clips.summary: Lawson recalls evenings around the stove reading Frantz Fanon's Black Skin, White Masks and debating freedom, revolution, and power, Ralph Featherstone's conviction that economic empowerment was essential, and later projects toward it: catfish farming in Mississippi, Ed Brown's leadership of Mississippi Action for Community Education, and the Fine Vines blue-jeans garment work.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: The Only Place That Would Rent to Jews
clips.summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer in Arlington, Virginia, on St. Patrick's Day 2013, Joan Trumpauer Mulholland describes being born in Washington and raised in the Buckingham apartments, known as the only place in northern Virginia that would rent to Jews, among New York families chasing government jobs, with a father from southwest Iowa at the Department of Labor and a mother from rural Georgia.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Hypocrites as Soon as We Left Church
clips.topic: A Literalist in a Segregated Church
clips.summary: She recalls memorizing Bible verses for gold stars at the Presbyterian church, singing that Jesus loves the children red and yellow black and white while the church itself was segregated by law, and the secret Sunday-evening youth meetings where Black students from the YWCA came to share spaghetti dinners, kept quiet because the American Nazi Party headquarters was around the corner.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Lynching Was Not Breaking News
clips.summary: Mulholland says she was barely aware of Emmett Till's 1955 murder, contrasting it with the Leo Frank lynching debated at her grandmother's in Oconee, Georgia, where she spent two weeks each summer, and recalls walking with another girl through the Black part of town and seeing with her own eyes the gap between the new brick white school and the Black school.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Those Yankees Looked Down Their Nose at Us
clips.summary: Asked why white Southerners like her, Bob Zellner, and Casey Hayden joined SNCC and CORE before Northerners, she answers that Northerners looked down on them while Southerners were working on what they saw as their own community's problems, and recounts choosing Duke University because her status-minded mother wanted a name school that was safely segregated.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: We Didn't Want to Rush
clips.summary: Mulholland describes Duke in 1959 as dripping ivy and all about status, and how she and her New York roommate Lucia refused to rush a sorority, choosing a graduate-student potluck instead, prompting counselors to check whether they were unhappy because it had supposedly never happened in the university's history.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: We Would All Be Others
clips.summary: As a freshman she joined the Durham sit-ins, picketing and sitting at lunch counters at a Kress or Woolworth and going to jail twice, and tells how the Black students, finding the white students had a harder time inside, hatched a plan to exploit a North Carolina law providing separate cells for white, colored, and others by all claiming to be others.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: She Put the Key in Her Pocket
clips.summary: Mulholland recounts the dean locking her and Lucia in a dark administrative building and pocketing the key until they phoned their parents, the administration's wish to expel them blocked by the professors' organization, an English teacher offering to bring a pop quiz to the jail, her mother's view that she had joined a cult, and her father's fear his daughter would be killed.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Go Up to Howard and Get Something Going
clips.summary: After dropping out and moving to Washington to work menial Senate office jobs, she joined the Howard-based Nonviolent Action Group on the advice of North Carolina College students who had not heard from Howard since SNCC's founding, wandering the campus asking after the sit-in students until she was welcomed into a group then planning to desegregate Arlington.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Waiting to Have Their Hand Forced
clips.summary: Mulholland describes sitting in at Cherrydale and other Arlington lunch counters with about thirteen NAG members from Howard and the community, the American Nazi Party busing high schoolers up to confront them, and the locally owned chains like Drug Fair and Peoples, run by liberal New York Jewish owners, who after a week of demonstrations quietly opened to everyone.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Arrested on the Merry-Go-Round
clips.summary: With the lunch counters won, NAG turned to Glen Echo Amusement Park, the segregated Disney of its day, where she bought tickets and handed them to Black students who rode the merry-go-round to be arrested, sparking a summer-long picket backed by the Jewish co-op community of Bannockburn against the American Nazi Party, whose swastikas unnerved World War II camp survivors.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Come Down and Join Them
clips.summary: Mulholland tells of NAG as an on-call movement answering the call to Rock Hill, South Carolina, the farthest south she had been, where she was in contact with the adult contact Reverend C.A. Ivory, visited the jail-in, and was briefly taken into what she believes was protective custody rather than a real arrest.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: The Whole Idea of the Freedom Ride Was Formed
clips.topic: A Plan on a Greyhound Bus
clips.summary: She traces how Diane Nash stayed her jail-in sentence and Tom Gaither of CORE, leaving Rock Hill, began on a Greyhound bus the idea of testing the Supreme Court's interstate-commerce ruling, how SNCC's April meeting in Charlotte called for students to test it, and how when the bus burned in Anniston three NAG men left at once while the Montgomery church came under siege.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Send More People
clips.topic: The Call From the Besieged Church
clips.summary: Mulholland recounts Paul calling from the basement of the surrounded Montgomery church to say send more people, NAG filtering riders in two or three at a time, the Kennedys' bargain with Mississippi that there would be no Alabama-style violence but riders could be arrested for breach of peace, and her own routine flight to New Orleans and train into Jackson.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: He Jerked His Hand Back
clips.summary: She describes the Jackson police captain instinctively reaching to help her down from the paddy wagon as a sweet young white thing, then jerking his hand back as if struck by lightning, a moment that gave her faith things would end well, and her famous calm mug shot, taken because unlike the Northern women she was at home in her own culture.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Less Than Three Square Feet Each
clips.topic: Hinds County Jail and Women Power
clips.summary: Mulholland recalls the overcrowded white women's cell in the county jail with people sleeping under bunks and in the dripping shower stall, feeling more in common with the Southern Black women in the next cell than the Yankee girls, and the support of Women Power Unlimited, the network of church women who supplied shower shoes, stamps, and toiletries.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Coming Out of the Woodwork Years Later
clips.summary: She explains the thirty-nine-day window to bail before the six-month sentence sent people to Parchman, the homes that offered showers and clean clothes on release, the Women Power founder Clarie Collins Harvey who ran a funeral home, and meeting on a later cruise elderly Jackson churchwomen who had quietly insisted the riders were not violent communists.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: The Full Horror Had Not Reached Virginia
clips.summary: Mulholland describes the scripted two-month sentence and rising fines, the truck ride to the isolated cotton-plantation prison near where Emmett Till was murdered, the terror when the driver turned off the two-lane road toward a house, and arrival at Parchman where they were stripped, given striped skirts, and subjected to a vaginal exam one cellmate blocked out for decades.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: They Cleaned Out Death Row
clips.summary: She explains the segregated cells alternating white and Black down one cell block on death row, which was emptied for them and was cleaner with better food, how she knew people by voice without seeing faces, meeting Helen Singleton years later who remembered her voice, and the psychological cruelty of isolation where, unlike Hinds County, no reporters could hear them.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Lectures in Swahili Through the Walls
clips.summary: Mulholland recounts the unremarkable Southern food, fried chicken and lemonade for a Fourth of July Mississippi barely celebrated since Vicksburg fell, taking up coffee, making playing cards from censored mail now in the American History Museum, and college-level lectures in Greek classics, Swahili, and French delivered cell to cell by women they could hear but not see.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Those Yankees Couldn't Sing
clips.summary: She describes the cells smaller than Nelson Mandela's, mattresses taken away for singing, the Southern spiritual singing and clapping the Northern girls could not match, the once-weekly showers, and the local Black lawyers Jack H. Young Sr. and Carsie Hall who worked the cell block because outside counsel like William Kunstler was not recognized by Mississippi.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: He Slipped In Little Bits of News
clips.summary: Mulholland calls Rabbi Perry Nussbaum a hero who drove up faithfully each week from a Jackson Jewish community unwelcome to the power structure, praying in Hebrew to lull the wardens while slipping in world news and individual family messages, and notes that years later the synagogue and his home were bombed by the Klan.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Deserting the Rabbi for the Catholics
clips.summary: She tells of choosing to pray with visiting Catholic seminarians over the rabbi and being scolded for deserting him, the lack of physical violence against the women though the men fared worse, her choice to serve the full sentence rather than bail since she had been accepted at Tougaloo, and getting out the week the rabbi left for a holy day.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: School Integration Is a Two-Way Street
clips.summary: Mulholland recounts coming out of Parchman to the Freedom House on Rose Street, then enrolling at Tougaloo College, which she had applied to in reaction to seeing Charlayne Hunter clutch her Madonna under riots at Georgia, believing integration had to run both ways, the school's pre-segregation charter and Northern funding making her presence possible.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: A Ghost in a Flowery Shortie
clips.summary: She describes being confused with the other white student Charlotte Phillips, a roommate startled to find a white roommate asleep on the bed, a girl screaming in the hall thinking she was a ghost in a pink flowery nightgown, and the social science forums and Ernst Borinski's basement lab that meant white visitors were not new, though a full-time white dorm resident was.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Students First and Foremost
clips.summary: Mulholland explains that Tougaloo sat six miles north of downtown Jackson, that she did secretarial work at the SNCC and CORE office on Lynch Street near Medgar Evers but as a white woman did not go into the Delta, and that she ended up majoring in history rather than sociology to avoid three hours of statistics.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: The Back Bedroom Was the Movement Room
clips.summary: She recalls the campus NAACP youth chapter, Reverend Edwin King's back bedroom where Jeannette King kept coffee brewing as a movement room and where she typed legal documents as William Kunstler paced in his boxer shorts, and political science professor John R. Salter Jr., whose house was a headquarters for stuffing boycott envelopes.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Field Reports From the Delta
clips.summary: Mulholland describes sitting next to Marlon Brando at Dean Branch's breakfast during the 1966 Meredith March, co-chairing the campus NAACP agitation group with Joyce Ladner, and compiling field reports mailed from Delta organizers into a stapled Mississippi newsletter, originals of which, including early references to Fannie Lou Hamer, she gave to the new Smithsonian museum.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: I Was Going to Be a Spotter
clips.summary: Mulholland recalls visiting Hodding Carter's office with Edwin King, then the spring 1963 escalation of the Jackson boycott, and a planned sit-in in which she and dorm mother Lois Chaffe were assigned as spotters for a picket-line diversion meant to draw police while demonstrators reached Woolworth, with dimes ready to phone Medgar Evers's office.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: The Police Could Not Go In
clips.topic: A Plan Unravels at the Counter
clips.summary: She explains that the picket line was arrested at once instead of drawing police away, that a Supreme Court ruling the week before, in a consolidated case bearing her own name from Durham, barred police from entering a store uninvited, and that as a crowd of white Central High students gathered, Memphis Norman was pulled from his stool and stomped beside Anne Moody and Pearlena Lewis.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Blood Mixing With the Mustard
clips.summary: Mulholland describes Edwin King suggesting she sit down at the counter for safety as someone brandished a knife, the police laughing outside while citing the Supreme Court ruling, John R. Salter Jr. taking the brunt with brass knuckles and being dubbed the mustard man, and Fred Blackwell capturing the iconic photograph from atop the counter.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Why Doesn't Salter Ever Turn Black
clips.summary: She reflects that the Woolworth photograph, blown up at the Kennedy Library, has eclipsed the Greensboro image because violence feels imminent, and puzzles over children's-book illustrations that always render her figure as Black and female rather than John R. Salter Jr., suspecting it touches the old taboo of protecting white women from Black men.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Like a Guardian Angel Above Me
clips.summary: Mulholland recounts the roughly three-hour ordeal that reporters called the most frightening of the movement, keeping conversation light by joking about Salter's hard exams, an out-of-body sense that the real her hovered above like a guardian angel, and the college president negotiating safe passage and even sitting at the counter once the store closed.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Where Else Was I Going to Go
clips.summary: She describes returning to the counter rather than the forming mob outside, being driven to the Black community near Medgar Evers's office and cleaned up at a beauty parlor, the packed mass meeting that night, Roy Wilkins and the national NAACP arriving, and Medgar Evers's assassination by Byron De La Beckwith with John Doar keeping the funeral from erupting into a riot.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: It Had Been an Awful Year
clips.summary: Mulholland recalls working the press section at the March on Washington headquarters amid fears the federal government would stop the buses, the awful year of Birmingham, the Letter From Birmingham Jail and King's dream speech, camping with Anne Moody on the way back, and the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing two weeks later that killed four girls.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: If You Can Make It to the Catholics
clips.summary: She describes the church-visit campaign of integrated groups appealing to white congregations' conscience and often being arrested or turned away, knowing the downtown Catholic church offered sanctuary, Galloway Memorial Methodist as a major target where Joyce Ladner was arrested on the steps, and ministers like W.B. Selah who resigned and the signers of a statement of conscience forced from their pulpits.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Do We Have the Right
clips.summary: Mulholland recalls Bob Moses at the Freedom House on Rose Street agonizing over whether they had the right to bring students down knowing someone would be killed, and the night a carload of white activists drove to a mass meeting in Canton, a town off limits to whites under curfew, letting Hamid Kizilbash drive and finding themselves boxed in by men with crowbars on the new road.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: He's From India, Don't Hit Him
clips.topic: Almost Killed on the Canton Road
clips.summary: She describes Edwin King shouting that the dazed and bleeding Hamid Kizilbash was a foreigner from India until the apparent leader, seemingly tipped off through diplomatic channels after an Indian opposition figure's arrest, pulled the attackers back, and her understanding that they were meant to die that night to stop Freedom Summer, so three others, Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney, died instead.
related_events: Murders of Chaney Goodman and Schwerner

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Not a Bone in His Body Unbroken
clips.summary: Working at the Smithsonian, Mulholland heard from Black workers that Chaney's body was brought in after dark with not a bone unbroken, a horror later confirmed when Dr. David Spain of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, at Fannie Lee Chaney's request, examined it and compared the injuries to an airplane crash, part of how Freedom Summer drew the world's attention.
related_events: Murders of Chaney Goodman and Schwerner

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: The First Printed Black Power
clips.topic: Black Power at the Meredith March
clips.summary: Mulholland recounts joining the 1966 Meredith March from Tougaloo carrying flyers folded into her New Testament that she believes were among the first printed references to Black Power, issued in Canton a week after the cry arose in Greenwood, soon echoed in a Washington Post political cartoon as the movement turned in a new direction.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: He Knelt on the Floor for the Kids
clips.summary: She describes bringing her freckled blonde twins, then about four, to meet Stokely Carmichael at a mid-1970s Smithsonian program despite friends' warnings, and how the towering and most feared man in white America knelt to talk eyeball to eyeball with them, a kindness she says counted more than the raised fist and that Joan Browning recalled too.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: I Had to Do Something Right Raising Him
clips.topic: Fifty Years and a Son's Film
clips.summary: Mulholland closes on working menial jobs, marrying and raising five boys, three decades as a classroom teaching assistant bringing civil rights perspective, her continued speaking, and her son Loki Mulholland's documentary An Ordinary Hero about her life, as the interviewers thank her and the recording ends.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: That of God in Every Person
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline at their home in August 2013, with John Bishop behind the camera, Jean Rosenberg opens by tracing her start: born in Philadelphia and adopted by a transplanted New York couple whose father left a Presbyterian community for the Quakers, leading the family into the Abington meeting, intense social action, decision by consensus, and the Philadelphia yearly meeting's work on racial and peace concerns that guided her to Earlham College.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Sit in Front of the Plate Glass Window
clips.summary: Jean recounts refusing to take part in air-raid drills at Abington High School as a psychology of fear, being called a Communist dupe by the principal and told to sit in front of the plate glass window to be killed first, and arranging to screen the film Operation Correction so students could see another side, protected somewhat by her standing as an honor student.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Have You Ever Thought About Joining Me
clips.summary: Jean describes her awareness of the Vietnam War and her witness against it, and how her Earlham friend Dorothy, daughter of an internal revenue lawyer, had interned with John Doar indexing FBI reports on Klan members; when Dorothy had Jean introduce Doar at a convocation, he invited Jean over lunch to join the Civil Rights Division, and she was accepted as a research analyst at the Department of Justice in June 1965.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A School for Jewish Children in Magdeburg
clips.summary: John Rosenberg introduces himself as Jean's husband of forty-six years, born in Magdeburg, Germany in 1931, where his father, a teacher, set up a school for Jewish children after Hitler required the schools be segregated, also working for the Jewish welfare agency and assisting the rabbi, the family living in a building next to a synagogue.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: The Last Ship
clips.topic: Escape and Settling in the Carolinas
clips.summary: John tells of Kristallnacht and the bombing of the synagogue, his father's eleven days in a concentration camp where he taught by the Socratic method, the family's year in Holland, and their arrival in the United States on February 22, 1940 on nearly the last ship, while a brother, sisters with five children in Rotterdam, and his grandmother were lost; in America his father swept mill floors while writing sermons for Jewish communities in Spartanburg and Gastonia before John grew up an Eagle Scout in Gastonia and went off to Duke.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: They Took the Binoculars
clips.summary: John remembers Treasury agents confiscating his immigrant family's binoculars and shortwave radio during the war, embarrassed to find Franklin Roosevelt's picture on the patriotic family's wall, and describes Gastonia as a segregated society with Black schools and a comfortable Jewish merchant community that respected his learned father but, grateful simply to be there, took no initiative against segregation.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Everybody Knows Where the Lines Are
clips.summary: John reflects on Gastonia's totally segregated schools and his mother, a friendly saleswoman who offered Black and white customers alike something to eat, recalls a county voting rights case that reached the Supreme Court and a much later joint class reunion of the 1948 Black and white graduates, and explains how a Gastonia trustee secured him a Duke tuition scholarship that ended when the man died after his first year.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: He Went to the Back of the Train
clips.summary: John tells of becoming a chemistry major and ROTC cadet at Duke, then an Air Force navigator flying Cold War exercises near the Russian border, and the life-changing moment when he and a radar operator from Charleston named Abe Jenkins flew a plane back from Europe, only for Jenkins, in uniform in Washington, to head for the back of the train where the Black passengers rode, leaving John thinking this was really wrong and that someday he might do something about it.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: You Depend on Each Other
clips.summary: John describes a relatively integrated Air Force still carrying a fair amount of racism, joking, and a Confederate sticker among navigation-school roommates from Texas and New Orleans, and how flying dangerous crewed missions where survival depended on one another taught the Black and white officers, in everyone's first real experience of it, that the color of one's skin made no difference at all.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A Jewish Quota at Duke
clips.summary: John recalls that Duke was still segregated with a Jewish quota, his two assigned Jewish roommates Norman and Marty (one a lifelong friend who became a lawyer in Jacksonville), and joining the then all-Jewish fraternity; Jean interjects that her own Quaker college in 1961 intentionally assigned her a Black roommate from the Bronx who became a lifelong friend, and John notes serving as a national delegate when the fraternity's charter was changed to admit non-Jewish members.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A Place I Could Make a Contribution
clips.summary: John describes going to law school at Chapel Hill, befriending the brilliant student who became number one in their class, and his determination to join the Civil Rights Division, explaining that being born in Germany and seeing the Holocaust, then serving alongside Black servicemen, convinced him this country's caste system was wrong and that, perhaps as a southerner with an easier line of communication, he could make a contribution representing the United States in these cases.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Go Find Out What the Facts Are
clips.summary: John recounts the Civil Rights Division, created in 1957 with little authority, where lawyers reviewed papers until John Doar arrived in 1960 with six months left in the Eisenhower administration and insisted on going south, working a Louisiana case where ginners refused to gin a registered voter's cotton, which led Robert Kennedy to keep Doar on to run the trial work; he explains the division's 1962 mission to challenge voting discrimination while protecting James Meredith's entry to Ole Miss took considerable time, and recalls a photograph of Burke Marshall and Doar with Meredith confronted by the lieutenant governor.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Chased Out of Town With a Pistol
clips.summary: John describes being assigned first to Georgia amid a series of church burnings, working with a liberal lawyer who had lost his business in Fort Smith, Arkansas, and one of their very first cases against a deputy sheriff in Sasser, near Americus, who chased three or four Black students with a pistol after they went door to door registering voters, leading to a criminal information and a federal trial.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Calling Them Mister and Missus
clips.summary: John recalls his first federal criminal prosecution where, the lead lawyer's plane delayed, he examined the Black student witnesses himself and addressed them by their last names as Mister and Missus, which southerners found unusual, while the lead lawyer still believed southerners could be persuaded to comply; he was then transferred to Mississippi at the end of 1962, and reflects on time spent in Albany, Georgia trying to gather information for the division with no success on the nighttime church burnings whose witnesses would have required a way into the white community or the Klan.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Getting Bob Moses Out of Jail
clips.summary: John recounts how SNCC and the SCLC, primarily SNCC, launched a major registration effort around 1961 with Bob Moses given charge of Mississippi, how the division brought one of its first cases to stop a prosecution of Moses for resisting arrest, and how, after Moses and seven or eight SNCC members were jailed in Leflore County for encouraging people to register, the division sued the county officials and John helped negotiate getting Moses and the group out of the Greenwood jail.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: If They Lived and Breathed They Could Vote
clips.summary: John explains the field method of locating community leaders and Black residents who had tried to register, how registrars stalled them while whites could register over the phone or, as he used to say, if they lived and breathed they could vote, and how those who got in faced a literacy test on a section of the Constitution; he describes having the FBI photograph all the voting records to show in Washington that illiterate whites were passed while Black applicants were failed, with whites giving incomprehensible interpretations of provisions like the ban on imprisonment for debt.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: The Freezing Principle
clips.summary: John contrasts Judge Frank Johnson of Montgomery, a beacon who ruled in the Macon County case that the same standards frozen for whites had to apply to Black applicants and required protection for his strong orders, with Judge Cox of Jackson, an avowed segregationist and former roommate of Senator Eastland who threw up roadblocks, taking two or three years to even hear a motion to photograph records.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Assigned by Counties
clips.summary: John explains the division's geographic assignments under section chiefs (one nearly blind lawyer from California, another over Mississippi from Texas, another over Louisiana), how he worked Leflore County and Greenwood plus Jackson and several other counties, and the running joke about Robert Kennedy asking the plan early on, when Burke Marshall said they needed just four more lawyers because Eastland chaired the judiciary committee and getting thirty was unthinkable.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Fannie Lou Hamer Off the Bus
