clips.summary: John and Jean describe how, until the Civil Rights Act, almost everything revolved around the registration effort, that the big expansion came after the 1964 act, and the incident with Fannie Lou Hamer of Ruleville, who with several other voter-registration workers returning from a workshop got off the bus at a lunch counter in Montgomery County and was arrested, taken to jail, and beaten for no reason.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: I Mopped the Blood Off the Floor
clips.topic: The Hamer Case and an Acquittal
clips.summary: John tells how the division filed a criminal case against the officers who beat Mrs. Hamer and the workers, how the FBI claimed it could not find the jail trustee, and how John, working in Mississippi the day Ruby shot Oswald, found the trustee, who said he had mopped the blood off the jail floor and would testify; the all-white jury acquitted in about two hours, and John drove to Sunflower County to tell Mrs. Hamer they had lost, noting Black leaders still saw a difference between the FBI and the Justice lawyers and welcomed them, though what they wanted was federal protection John said the system would not provide.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: He Shot Back at the Klan
clips.summary: John describes Hartman Turnbow of Holmes County, who led people to register near a community of Black landowners outside Lexington, whose home was firebombed and shot into and who fired back at the Klansmen, only to be arrested for setting his own house afire, prompting a division case to free him that the courts again deferred to state proceedings, and notes the local editor Hazel Brannon Smith and a book chapter written about Turnbow.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: My Name Is John Doar
clips.topic: From the Evers Funeral to the Lynd Trial
clips.summary: John recalls 1963 heating up as President Kennedy and Medgar Evers were assassinated, and how after the Evers funeral in Jackson, with mourners and police about to clash, John Doar walked into the street with his hand out declaring his name was John Doar of the Justice Department who stood for what was right, calming the crowd; he then returns to categorizing photographed records and an entire morning of articulate Black teachers turned down by a registrar near Hattiesburg held in contempt in the Lynd case, testimony that appalled the three appellate judges.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: The Floor Theater in Greenwood
clips.summary: John explains that the 1964 Civil Rights Act, promoted by Lyndon Johnson after Kennedy's death, made the division responsible for school desegregation, employment, and public accommodations, and tells how in Greenwood a national-chain theater complied and admitted Black patrons who were then beaten while police stood by, while the local Paramount refused outright, leading the division to sue both the police officers and the theater, cases eventually resolved in the division's favor.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: This Was the Culture of the FBI
clips.summary: Answering the interviewer on jurisdiction, John explains that the FBI was the Department of Justice's investigative agency under the attorney general, that until the civil rights statutes its work was bank robberies, kidnappings, and interstate crimes, and that enforcing civil rights was difficult for local white southern agents who believed in segregation, a problem the murder of the three civil rights workers brought into focus; he recounts the need for agents from elsewhere, his own jury analysis of an eighty-person pool, and the few courageous white editors such as Hazel Brannon Smith and Hodding Carter whose papers were bombed.
related_events: Murder of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: He Came Down From New York
clips.summary: John describes the change toward non-southern agents, a key agent in the Meridian civil rights case who worked with informants, and the New York agent who led the investigation, recounting the long process of a grand jury that heard evidence of local police brutality but produced no indictment until two confessions led to the conviction of seven men while a Klan minister's jury hung, and how, when these federal cases were brought local law enforcement might as well not have been there, so that after the murders the division lawyers, who had driven alone, began driving in twos.
related_events: Murder of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: He Shot a .38 Into the Floor
clips.summary: John tells the one real incident of trouble: in Yazoo County, with the FBI photographing voting records in the next room, the segregationist registrar nicknamed Foot wanted to chat, boasted of the lynchings he had witnessed, then pulled a .38 from his drawer and fired it into the floor in front of John's foot as a statement, bringing the FBI running, the only time anything like that happened to him in all those years.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: If You Signed Your Name You Could Vote
clips.topic: The Act Turns It Upside Down
clips.summary: John explains that the Voting Rights Act of 1965 turned the whole voting episode right side up by abolishing literacy tests and allowing federal examiners into southern counties under a formula so that people could register simply by signing their names, and turns to Alabama and Sheriff Jim Clark of Selma, a poster child for segregationists who for over a year obstructed registration drives, used a cattle prod on students, and was challenged in a federal case John presented in December 1965.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: They Impounded the Black Precincts
clips.summary: John recounts the first 1966 Alabama primary under the Voting Rights Act, when registration surged from about seven thousand to fifteen thousand voters in Sheriff Jim Clark's county, and how on a seventy-three-item paper ballot the white election officials finished and impounded the boxes from five Black precincts that came in at four in the morning, then decided to exclude them as fraudulent, prompting the Civil Rights Division to sue to require the ballots be counted.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Counting Every One of Seventeen Thousand
clips.summary: John describes filing the federal case, getting Judge Johnson to order the seventeen thousand ballots kept safe before the case was assigned to Judge Thomas, who had long resisted desegregating the Mobile schools, while Jean and Dorothy spent a week watching the ballot boxes in the sheriff's office, observed by deputies who tried to romance them and gave Dorothy a segregationist Never button.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: The Right to Vote Was Too Important
clips.summary: John recounts the several-day trial before Judge Thomas with hundreds of federal observers who had watched the count, his thought of putting Jean on the stand, and how Judge Thomas, despite his record of keeping schools segregated, found only minor irregularities and ruled that a voter should not be deprived of a counted vote, ordering the ballots counted, a big first win on the voting rights case.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Viola Liuzzo on the Road
clips.topic: From the March to the Supreme Court
clips.summary: John recalls the Viola Liuzzo killing after the Selma to Montgomery march, when a woman of conscience from near Detroit was shot by the Klan while driving a young Black man, and reflects that the principles of the 1965 act came out of those earlier cases; asked about the recent Supreme Court ruling, he and Jean say Justice Ginsburg and the minority were right, note Hillary Clinton at the American Bar Association urging it be turned around as states rolled out voter ID laws, and that, like Bush v. Gore, you work to change it rather than going into the streets.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Doused by the Local Barber
clips.summary: John recalls roughly a month in Bogalusa, Louisiana, helping build a case around incidents against local Black residents testing restaurants and public accommodations, much of it FBI work, prompted in part by threats and leaflets that scared off a liberal senator from Arkansas from speaking, and references photographs of a barber dousing a protester and CORE marches led by James Farmer that the division lawyers, including John, observed alongside another attorney.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A Judge in His Undershirt
clips.summary: John explains the history of equity, that one could see the chancellor even in his nightshirt for relief, and tells how, to get a preliminary injunction against the Klan in the Bogalusa case, he found a Fifth Circuit judge in his back office on a sweltering Saturday in New Orleans, sitting in his undershirt, who signed the order, so that John likes to say he got an injunction from the judge in his undershirt.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Your Feet Feel Like Mud
clips.summary: Jean, not yet married to John in those Alabama years, recounts the real fear of the work, water spit in their drinks and never knowing if it would escalate, and a night on the Meredith March when, serving as the traveling connector between the field attorneys and the attorney general, she and an attorney from Mississippi named George took a late walk in Yazoo City and a car screeched up with men jumping out to follow them, George telling her to look straight ahead and keep walking though her feet felt like mud.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A Field Office in Selma
clips.summary: John and Jean recall the division opening its first field offices, the first in Selma led by a lawyer who later went to a Minneapolis firm and then divinity school, and mention the office's first Black secretary in Selma, leading Jean to a hard memory from preparing a massive Alabama school desegregation case with that secretary as her roommate during three days and nights around the clock.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: They Saw She Was Black and Wouldn't Take Her
clips.topic: A Seizure and a Refused Ambulance
clips.summary: Jean tells of her roommate, the Black secretary from Selma, who after the three-day school case had run out of epilepsy medication and suffered a grand mal seizure in their Montgomery motel, how the first ambulance crew saw she was Black and refused to take her until John helped get another, how the hospital sent her home only for another seizure to follow, a hard lesson in how unequal things were that, John adds, made him figure they might as well get married.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: I Think I'm Going to Ask Jean to Marry Me
clips.summary: Jean and John recount that after that trip she flew home to Philadelphia while John was ordered to Charlotte to investigate the Klan bombing of attorney Julius Chambers's house, near John's family in Gastonia, and how, standing in a lunch line with the colleague helping him, John said he was going to call Jean and ask her to marry him, then proposed over the phone, after which her parents drove to meet John's parents the next day.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: You Knew You Were Living History
clips.summary: Asked how they kept going, John and Jean say everyone in the division, from John Doar to the secretaries, did the very best they could because they knew this was history, that it was hard on the wives at home and brought some divorces, and that against segregationist judges like Cox the work meant making a record so the next court would reverse them; John reflects the cases were the first attack and first law, that Bob Moses knew he could call any hour, and that knowing local people like Fannie Lou Hamer chopping cotton or a father who only wanted his child in school, putting their lives on the line, was enough to keep them going.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: From Fifteen to Eighty Years Old
clips.topic: A Church Group Jailed in Itta Bena
clips.summary: John recalls that in rural counties people took the Justice Department lawyers in suits for insurance salesmen, and tells of a case in the Black section of Itta Bena outside Greenwood where, after a smoke bomb was thrown into a church the group had filled night after night, the whole group from age fifteen to eighty went to the deputy sheriff's house, were arrested for disturbing the peace, tried in groups of ten, fined and sentenced to thirty days at the county farm, where John interviewed them daily for two weeks until church bond money freed them.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: It Was Really a Work Day
clips.topic: The March on Washington and Resurrection City
clips.summary: Jean recalls being in the Department of Justice on the first floor during the March on Washington, stepping outside to watch though it was a work day while she handled the Hamer case, and she and John remember a later observer role around Resurrection City and the Poor People's Campaign, when Marian Wright Edelman negotiated with John Doar over how long the encampment could stay to press its demands on voting.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Extradition Papers for James Earl Ray
clips.summary: John recounts the Washington riots after Martin Luther King's assassination in 1968 and the Nixon inauguration, and how he and a senior Justice Department official went to Memphis to prepare the extradition papers to bring James Earl Ray back from England, gathering affidavits from police and fingerprint experts and examining the gun, the case assembled in Washington but the information gathered by the division, and recalls just visiting the civil rights museum there.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A Brochure With Wallace's Picture
clips.summary: John explains the poll tax as another Reconstruction-era device, cumulative and only in the South, which the Voting Rights Act required the attorney general to contest forthwith, so that on the Monday after the act was signed he filed the Mississippi poll tax case before preparing the Alabama case argued by a colleague, and how that record captured a brochure Governor Wallace had the highway patrol distribute through the white schools and a legislative history of frank remarks from 1900 about stopping Black citizens from voting, leading the court to invalidate the poll tax.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A Blessing to Have Them on the Court
clips.topic: Fred Gray and the Fifth Circuit Judges
clips.summary: John recounts a Montgomery election case brought by the prominent Black attorney Fred Gray, in which Judge Johnson ordered the United States made a party to keep the division's lawyers in his court, a case of no particularly good outcome beyond some election-process reforms, and praises the Fifth Circuit judges, naming Wisdom, Brown, and Bell, a blessing on the court when so many southern district judges made litigation slow and difficult, the trial records they built ultimately making the record for the Voting Rights Act.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: The Briefcases Lined Up on Friday
clips.summary: John describes living in Washington while gone much of the time, the briefcases lined up outside the division offices on Fridays as lawyers caught DC-3 flights to Memphis or Birmingham for two weeks to photograph four or five counties of voting records, including thousands in the Jackson and Rankin courthouses, and how in larger cities some Black residents had registered but not by carte blanche, while the line lawyers grew up under a disciplined complaint-drafting system.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Dot Maps of Houston
clips.summary: John and Jean recall moving to Houston for six months for the long-running school case under Judge Connally, color-coding dot maps of every student residence (Black in red, white in green) to show how children were bused past schools, an antitrust-scale effort with foot lockers of records seeking busing to remedy past discrimination amid a Black core ringed by Spanish-speaking and white neighborhoods, which the judge addressed by pairing schools before the appeals court later required some busing.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: They Threw the School Board Out
clips.summary: John reflects that the best outcome in Houston was voters throwing out the school board and the lawyer who had made a living resisting desegregation, as in Little Rock and other districts, and turns to employment cases brought as chief of the Western section, suing the electrical workers in Las Vegas, the steam fitters in Los Angeles, and Roadway Express, and the division's expansion into schools in Indianapolis and Oklahoma City because segregation, often de facto, was not only southern.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Which Congressional District
clips.summary: John explains leaving the division because the administration changed and which congressional district a case fell in seemed to matter more than its merits, prompting some lawyer revolts over weak enforcement, and the difficult question of whether a committed person should stay and fight or whether staying in government slowly priced one out, after which he and Jean, deciding money was not all that important, took a three-month camping trip to Canada and the national parks with a six-month-old before formally leaving.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Night Comes to the Cumberlands
clips.summary: John recounts how a former colleague directing legal services for the Office of Economic Opportunity offered to support public-interest work in Appalachia, how they camped at a Kentucky state park on their way to visit his parents in Florida, and how they interviewed Harry Caudill of Whitesburg, author of Night Comes to the Cumberlands, about the broad form deed, strip mining, black lung, and the poverty issues facing a region with few lawyers free of the coal companies.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A First-Class Law Firm for Poor People
clips.summary: John describes coming to Prestonsburg to build the Appalachian legal services program covering the eastern Kentucky coal counties, the strip-mining and black lung environmental issues, and the poverty issues of food stamps and Medicaid where local welfare offices treated applicants badly, his aim being a first-class law firm for poor people, the way John Doar had assembled good lawyers to represent the government.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: They Thought We Were Communists
clips.summary: John and Jean recount the opposition when federal legal-services funding arrived: the local bar fearing lost money, the community suspecting them of being communists or outside agitators, a Supreme Court case involving the Appalachian Volunteers who had lived with local families and encouraged them to seek benefits, and hospitals turning away people without money, including pregnant mothers, so that a lawyer could make a big difference for people with disability, consumer, and foreclosure problems.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Eleven Offices Across the Mountains
clips.summary: John describes expanding the program to eleven offices, this one the main administrative office, and starting a separate nonprofit, the Appalachian Citizens Law Center, on the coal issues when he retired in 2002, with each office covering several counties and aiming for four lawyers to handle day-to-day divorces and custody alongside larger cases such as suing the state park system for discriminating against Black applicants, hoping to build a first-class law firm for poor people in remote eastern Kentucky.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: You Can Mine Coal Responsibly
clips.summary: John and Jean reflect that unlike the South, where they prepared cases and went home to Washington, here they became part of the local community, caring about its schools and environment, and discuss the difficulty of arguing for environmental regulation when coal mining is people's livelihood, that coal can be mined responsibly, and that for years out-of-state interests owned the coal and paid no taxes on it until that was changed.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: A Planetarium in the Mountains
clips.topic: A Science Center and a Closing Reflection
clips.summary: John and Jean describe the present hardship as coal miners lose jobs with no replacement industries, and their effort, inspired by the natural history museum in John's hometown of Gastonia, to start a local science center with a planetarium and classroom space to lift low math and science scores, secured politically over a couple of legislatures because the governor and speaker were from neighboring counties; closing the interview, they reflect that you feel very fortunate when your values are congruent with what you do day to day, and that they have been able to do that.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Born on Lenox Avenue
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Brooklyn in August 2013, John Carlos opens with his parents, a father born in Camden, South Carolina about sixty years after slavery and a mother who migrated from Cuba at seventeen, who married in New York City and raised their children on Lenox Avenue in Harlem.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A Good Show for the Money
clips.summary: Carlos describes growing up between the Cotton Club and the Savoy Ballroom, hustling tips with a little song and dance and collecting silver dollars, a meeting with Fred Astaire who praised them for always giving a good show for the money, and his first sight of the white flight that emptied Harlem.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: King Kong and Heroin
clips.summary: He recalls the bootleg liquor called King Kong that sent drinkers leaping from rooftops, the arrival of heroin that disintegrated Harlem families and left mothers raising children alone, and how his own parents held together, his father a World War I veteran and shoemaker, his mother a domestic who cleaned doctors' offices through Harlem.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A Harlem Robin Hood
clips.summary: He explains modeling himself on Robin Hood and the distinction between God's law and man's law, leading his friends to raid the frozen freight trains coming through the Yankee Stadium yards not for fat pockets but to stock the houses of neighbors who had nothing, while keeping it from his law-abiding parents.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: To Swim the English Channel
clips.summary: Carlos recounts hearing on the radio of a man swimming the English Channel and peppering his father with questions about sharks and tides, then learning of the Olympic Games and declaring he would be the first Black swimmer to represent the United States.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Because of the Color of Your Skin
clips.summary: He relates the painful talk in which his father explained he could not train as a swimmer, not the river where friends drowned, not the crowded public pool, not a club, and finally the real reason, the color of his skin, recalling the white mothers at the High Bridge pool who called their children out of the water the moment he jumped in.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: You Need to Talk to Him
clips.summary: Carlos describes two Black detectives from the 32nd precinct, friends of his shoemaker father from weekend poker games attended by Jackie Robinson and other ballplayers, who suspected him in the freight-yard break-ins, and how his father told them to speak to the boy directly.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Johnny, You're Special
clips.summary: He tells how the detectives caught him taking other boys' sneakers at a track meet, smacked him for smirking, but told him he had a talent as a runner because he had outrun the police, recalling that his mother too was a runner who fought to keep her purse, and gave him a number for the New York Pioneer Club.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Total Fatigue at the Armory
clips.topic: First Race at the Pioneer Club
clips.summary: Carlos recounts arriving at the New York Pioneer Club at thirteen or fourteen with no track gear, telling Joe Yancey he ran distance, and being thrown into 660s against older runners, leading the first laps before total fatigue dropped him, and overhearing on the cold walk home that everyone would soon know his name.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: J C on My Buttocks
clips.summary: He describes writing a big J and C on his sweatpants so crowds would chant his name, building a reputation though his high school had no track and the team trained running hallways and the East River walkways, all while he still wanted to be a swimmer.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A Pool That Was Not There
clips.summary: Carlos tells how, as a self-described gangster kid barred from his school's track program, he was steered to Haaren High on a promise of a swim program that did not exist, was kept off its track team as the wrong sort of student, and finally transferred to the School of Machine and Metal Trades.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: They're Singing About Us
clips.summary: He recalls running at Machine and Metal Trades as a novice who had never won, breaking the record in his heat and finding the crowd had made up a song about the team, then the serious racing that happened outdoors after the meet at the 168th Street Armory, where he gave handicaps and raced the winners for crowds as large as those inside.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A Springboard for a Voice
clips.summary: He describes coming to see track and field not as a way to collect medals but as a springboard, realizing the better he perfected his game the better he could speak and carry a message, never forgetting the drugs, the domestic work, the white flight, and the racism he had witnessed.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: They Gave Us a Basketball Court
clips.summary: Carlos argues that decades ago Black neighborhoods were given basketball courts where white areas had swimming pools, producing stars like Bill Russell and Elgin Baylor and the Rucker Park players who resisted letting pro money control their game, and laments that modern athletes care only about the dollar while leagues tolerate doping for bigger television contracts.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Shame on the Olympic Committee
clips.summary: Carlos condemns Vladimir Putin barring gay athletes from the Sochi Games and faults the International Olympic Committee, recalling that in 1936 it let Adolf Hitler force young Jewish athletes off the German team, and says athletes today lack the courage to make statements because they fear repercussions more than they value what is right.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: An Embargo on the Games
clips.summary: Carlos explains he no longer agrees with boycotting the Olympics, equating it with an embargo that spares the government and hurts the people, since the officials still make their money while the spectators and athletes, the sacrificial lambs, are the ones hurt, recalling that Jimmy Carter was attacked for the 1980 boycott though he held life more valuable than Olympic medals.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A Grain of Sand
clips.summary: Carlos calls Mexico City a spiritual thing, the first Games televised worldwide and in color and held in a developing nation, and recounts a vision from age seven or eight in which he stood on a podium and his upraised left hand changed the crowd's joy into venom and hatred, exactly as it would on the victory stand.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Stop the Train
clips.summary: He frames the boycott debate as a train ride, building consensus among athletes then pulling the red lever to hang banners that let the world know, after which the crowd singing God bless America turns to firebombs hurled at the burning train, tells the latecomers who now want a brick at San Jose State to show him their burns, and says people stay silent only because they are afraid to offend the oppressor.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: That's His Son
clips.summary: Seeking religion to answer the voids in his life, Carlos found Abyssinian Baptist Church, where the elder Adam Clayton Powell resembled his father and one day handed the sermon to his fair-skinned son Adam Clayton Powell Junior, whose preaching was about the content of his heart, and reflects on his father's lessons about light-skinned people passing for a better standard of life.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Get on the Ships
clips.summary: Carlos describes absorbing his heroes early, Marcus Garvey whose parades came up through Harlem and whose ships invited Black people back to Africa to find themselves, and Paul Robeson, the baritone, scholar, and movie star who had everything and risked it all rather than zip his lip, using his fame as a springboard to be a voice for those who could not speak.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Which One Is Malcolm X
clips.summary: Carlos recalls hearing Malcolm X on the radio speaking the truth he had only heard from his father, going to the mosque on 116th Street, asking to walk with him from one location to the next to learn, and how he was supposed to be at the Audubon Ballroom the Sunday Malcolm was shot but had taken a road trip past West Point, racing back to the hospital only to find him gone.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: God's First Lieutenant
clips.summary: Carlos describes meeting Martin Luther King and being awestruck, raised to see King as God's first lieutenant, and learning King wanted to be not the number one figure for the Olympic boycott but second in command after Memphis, naming Harry Edwards and Ken Noel as organizers of the potential boycott of the 1968 Games sparked by a statement Tommie Smith made in Japan, while Carlos was off at East Texas State facing his own racism.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: If You Go, We Go
clips.summary: He recounts how a runner he met at a New Jersey meet helped get him a track scholarship to East Texas State, the coach promising no prejudice and a good life for his young daughter, and the choice between that scholarship and a New York housing-project apartment, settled when his wife told him that if he went, the family went together.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: My Name Changed to Boy
clips.summary: Carlos describes traveling south with a Jamaican-born running friend who insisted he was not Black, the pristine whites-only restroom beside the filthy colored one at the Dallas Fort Worth airport, the whites-only water fountain, and the coach who renamed him from John Carlos to boy and came close to a racial slur in front of his wife and daughter.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Above Being a Slave
clips.summary: He describes a recently integrated school still ruled by an old slave-master attitude, a neighbor who screamed a slur at his small daughter for playing with a white child, and Carlos confronting the man knowing that if he killed him everything he stood for would go out the window, yet refusing to call his parents for money to leave.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: As Great as Bobby Morrow
clips.summary: Carlos tells how the coach warned him not to talk to white girls, how he reminded a coach's wife that his running paid the bills, and how a statement that he was as great as the Texas sprinter Bobby Morrow so offended people that a retraction was printed the next day.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A White Man on His Own Plane
clips.topic: The Coach Picks Up a Hammer
clips.summary: He remembers a white teammate he placed in the class of John Brown and Peter Norman, then the day before the Lone Star conference when the coach ordered him to run timed trials, Carlos refused and walked the track, and the coach came at him with a hammer and a slur before Carlos stripped off his uniform and ripped his name from the locker.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: We Made a Bond
clips.summary: Carlos describes calling his wife to pack while the coaches formed a delegation to take him to lunch, his insistence that they feed the whole team and not him alone, and his white teammate reminding him of the bond they had made to win the Lone Star championship, persuading him to stay.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A Ring or a Shoe With a Flake
clips.summary: He recounts winning the conference and being given a tiny shoe charm while the mostly white football team got big rings, demanding equal rings for the track team, a bar in Austin that refused to serve him in the state capital he was representing, and deciding, with the hammer fresh in mind, that he had to leave East Texas.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Drop the Rock in the Lake
clips.summary: Carlos tells how Harry Edwards phoned him to invite him to a meeting, his walk into the Americana Hotel lobby past the chandeliers to the SCLC suite where Andrew Young welcomed him, and King answering why he joined the Olympic Project for Human Rights with a parable of dropping a rock into a still lake, the waves rousing everything on the shore, a way to wake the world up nonviolently.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Stand for Those Who Cannot
clips.summary: Carlos describes pushing his shades down to look for fear in King's eyes and asking why he would return to Memphis after threats on his life, King answering that he had to stand for those who would not and could not stand for themselves, a phrase that made Carlos's whole life come full circle and that he ties to the God-given icons people still talk about every day.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Not for the Medal
clips.summary: He explains that, unlike Kareem Abdul-Jabbar who could stay home for his studies unquestioned, if Carlos skipped the Games someone else would take his place, so he went not for a medal but to be represented on the victory stand, crediting God for placing Peter Norman, who alone among white athletes had the audacity to stand with them, on the podium.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Do You Believe in Human Rights
clips.summary: Carlos recounts that when the boycott dissolved he and Tommie Smith resolved to make a statement, and with about twenty-five minutes before the ceremony he asked Peter Norman whether he believed in human rights, pinned on him an Olympic Project for Human Rights button thrown down by a white Harvard rower, and stresses that beyond the button Norman had the conviction never to flinch or denounce them through years of trials.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Take Their Picture in My Spot
clips.summary: He tells how, learning that the San Jose State statue left Peter Norman out, he drove from Los Angeles to refuse his own likeness and phoned Norman in Sydney, who said he loved the idea but proposed his place be left empty so visitors from around the world could stand in his spot to support what they did.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: From Hero to Neighborhood Bum
clips.summary: Carlos recalls breaking the world record at the New York trials and owning the city, then thirty days after the demonstration the same paper calling him a neighborhood bum, his hospitalized father weeping over the headlines until Carlos reminded him that nobody knew John Carlos better than his own father, and the word trouble maker he came to wear proudly beside Gandhi, King, Robeson, and Mandela.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Beads for the Lynched
clips.summary: Carlos walks through the symbols he and Smith chose, a black shirt over his USA jersey for shame at America, Smith's scarf for Black sacrifice, beads for those lynched in the South and thrown overboard on the Middle Passage, the shoes and stockinged feet for poverty, and the glove for five continents of people of color becoming a powerful force, then his thoughts on the victory stand that they could never shackle him again because he was a free man.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Bodies in the Square
clips.summary: Carlos recounts the killing of close to two thousand students in a Mexico City square days before the Games, bodies burned and dumped at sea so the Games could go on, says his family and his God were his only support, and that the right-wing press tried to pit him against George Foreman, who waved the American flag yet became one of his dearest friends.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A Beacon for the World
clips.summary: He contends the Republicans are rolling back the gains of the sixties and faults critics of Barack Obama, then describes the demonstration becoming a beacon that resonated in Cuba, Africa, Asia, and Italy because the button said human rights for all humanity, and how an eighty-foot picture of him at the Sydney stadium draws visitors from around the world.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: A Thousand No Christmases
clips.summary: Carlos says his only regret was being too young to better protect his family, naming the hurt of his first wife taking her own life, his children sacrificing at school because of their father, and the Christmases he could not give them, yet concluding that what he did in Mexico City was far greater than any of it because it was about all of us.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Become Part of the Kid
clips.summary: He describes teaching by melting himself down to become part of a kid, like two candles blending, and the New York reunion of track athletes the night before where former students told him he had come to them not as someone over them but as part of them, confirming that the vision God showed him was coming true.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: That's Me, the Hummingbird
clips.summary: Carlos says the biggest sacrifice is losing a loved one and that personal sacrifice is irrelevant beside giving his all for those who come after, recalling two fellow Olympians at an airport telling him he had screwed up his life and would never work in America, and answering that like a hummingbird or a man who falls off a bridge and treads water, he did not go down, he stopped, and he would rise again.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: I Can't Change History
clips.topic: Those Who Want a Brick Now
clips.summary: He recounts the people who tried to put him down now calling to be in the picture and wanting a brick, telling them he cannot rewrite history to add those who were not there, and closing on what he refuses to dwell on, the welfare and hungry house a friend quietly relieved with groceries, choosing instead to talk about coming together for the children, since everyone who did something for society, from John Brown to King, knew the sacrifice and was not deterred.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Born at a Very Early Age
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project in Philadelphia on July 15, 2011, John Churchville opens by recalling that he was born with an extreme case of myopia and attended a sight-saving program, where in the fifth grade he first faced discrimination not for being Black but for being a sight-saver.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Site-Savers Had Their Own Lunchroom
clips.summary: Churchville describes the internal segregation at T.M. Pierce School in North Philadelphia, where sight-savers had their own lunchroom and safety patrol and could only direct other sight-savers, and how a teacher urged him to run for student council to raise the issue.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Gee Whiz, Was I Bullied
clips.topic: The Bully and a Ukrainian Friend
clips.summary: He recounts forgetting his identity once elected student council president, learning the lesson only later, and then being beaten daily by a bully in seventh grade until a friend referred him to a Ukrainian classmate, a self-described Nazi who could quote Mein Kampf yet whose favorite teacher and best friend were not who one would expect.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: He Brought Me a Zip Gun
clips.topic: The Day He Almost Killed His Tormentor
clips.summary: Churchville tells how the Ukrainian classmate built him a zip gun with twenty-two caliber bullets, how he carried it to school intending to end his tormentor's life, and how the thought of disappointing his mother stopped him at the last moment; the bully beat him lightly that day and never bothered him again, and was killed in a gang six months later.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: How's Your Mommy
clips.summary: He reflects that the bully was Black, recalls a German teacher's loaded question about his mother that his own mother taught him to answer in kind, and contrasts supportive teachers like Mrs. Maines and Mrs. Corn with counselors who told Black students to go to trade school and forget college.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: He Taught Us the Stock Market
clips.summary: Churchville credits a ninth-grade social studies teacher who, though blunt that Black students should not expect good jobs, made them follow stocks daily and taught that wealth in the United States comes not from a nine-to-five job but from investing, a lesson he still appreciates.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Always Examine Your Motives
clips.summary: He describes his mother, a well-read musician with a non-traditional mind who never made excuses for injustice, who became his best friend and taught him to always examine his motives and know why he was doing whatever he did.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Village Concert Choir
clips.topic: His Mother's Music in North Philadelphia
clips.summary: Churchville recounts his mother turning down an operatic opportunity to raise her children, running the Village Concert Choir that sang Bach, Mozart, and Negro spirituals on summer nights in North Philadelphia, and how he learned to fake his way through her piano students' voice lessons by memorizing the right chords and notes.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Won a Music Scholarship
clips.summary: He describes directing the Simon Gratz High School choir when its director Joy Going Smith ran late, staging Gilbert and Sullivan's Trial by Jury with teenagers in Germantown, and winning a music scholarship to Temple University on the strength of a great ear despite being too near-sighted to read the notes.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Kwituated From Temple
clips.topic: Moving to the Lower East Side
clips.summary: Churchville tells how at nineteen he left Temple after a year and a half and moved to New York's Lower East Side, an address on East Houston Street, where he encountered Marxists, poets, musicians, artists, and Black nationalists, listened to poetry at the Pink Pussycat, and played some jazz piano.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Whatever Gets You to Take a Bath
clips.summary: He recalls debating his mother that the church was gutless and irrelevant, her steadfast love of Jesus, and how at seventeen or eighteen he became an Ahmadiyya Muslim after meeting a sharp young man who taught him Black history and Islamic history, including that the Moors who built the culture of Spain were Black; his Muslim name was Yahya Abdul Hamid.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Sure, I'll Take a Ride
clips.summary: Churchville describes working as a janitor at Columbia Presbyterian Medical Center while five non-paying friends crowded his rent-controlled apartment, and how, jobless and at loose ends, he agreed to ride a bus that needed delivering to a SNCC retreat in Georgia purely as a way to take a ride.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Under Sherrod's Wing
clips.topic: The Albany Project and Malcolm X
clips.summary: He recounts being impressed by the young people in Georgia talking about freedom and justice, hooking up with Reverend Charles Sherrod who ran the Albany Project, working in Lee County, and an earlier New York encounter outside Micheaux's bookstore where Malcolm X told him his own life was irrelevant and what mattered was Black people getting their act together.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Need a Black Partner
clips.summary: Churchville explains that as a Black nationalist he found it intolerable that Black people deferred to his white partner as the power symbol, so he asked Sherrod for a Black partner and accepted Sherrod's deal that he could keep one only if he registered more voters, then went to work in Americus.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Stop Fighting Each Other
clips.summary: He tells how he walked into the middle of a gang fight in Americus, Georgia and got two teenage Black gangs to stop and negotiate a peace treaty, framing voting not as integration but as building a power base to elect people who look like you, the beginning of what he called Black power.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Fear Will Get You Up
clips.topic: Violence and Faith in Southwest Georgia
clips.summary: Churchville describes the terrible violence of Southwest Georgia, blaming his high blood pressure on the fear, and how, surrounded by frightened committed people, everyone started singing; though a Muslim, he prayed to Jesus and once castigated a deacon for an insincere prayer while the Klan threatened to burn the church.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Cleaning Up After His Mess
clips.summary: He admits that, picking up SNCC's resentment, he thought Martin Luther King was too light and not on point, blamed him for leaving Albany when the cameras left while SNCC stayed, and held issues over King's role with the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, realizing only at King's death how much he respected him.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around
clips.summary: Churchville recalls the Monday-night mass meetings in Albany and a preacher who came bearing a state travel letter to tell them to cease and desist, how the crowd, led by Charlie Sherrod's watching, grew suspicious and drowned him out singing Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around until he sat down.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Older Folks Had the Guts
clips.topic: A Sheriff and a Family's Helplessness
clips.summary: He observes that older grandmothers, not the young people, took the risks for change, and recounts a wrenching case where a notorious sheriff held a gun to a boy's head, threatening to kill him or to claim he molested a white girl after a bakery driver had raped the boy's sister, leaving Churchville with rage he had nowhere to put.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: A Yellow Streak All the Way Down
clips.topic: Why He Stayed Despite the Fear
clips.summary: Churchville confesses he was scared enough to run the first day but stayed because the local people had far more to lose and could not leave, recalling the FBI agents who came down, did nothing, and reported back to the sheriff whatever was said, and the daily atrocities that never came to light.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Chose the Voter Registration Camp
clips.summary: He explains the two strains in SNCC, direct action and voter registration, and why he chose voter registration as a way out he could believe in, refusing to be beaten over a lunch counter, appalled that Black citizens had to pass a constitutional test, and resolving simply to do what was in front of him.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Privilege of the Moment
clips.summary: Churchville describes moving to Mississippi to teach literacy at the COFO Freedom School in Greenwood rather than getting people to the polls, calling it the first time in his life he lived fully in the moment, an experience that later drew him toward teaching when he returned to Philadelphia.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Sweetheart of the Movement
clips.summary: He names the people who stand out in memory from fifty years before: Charles Sherrod, Diane Nash, Bernice Johnson Reagon who brought song and more to the movement, Faith Holsaert and Bob Mants in his camp, community folk like Eddie, and Dorie Ann Ladner.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Justice, Not Integration
clips.topic: Respect and Disagreement With John Lewis
clips.summary: Churchville voices deep respect for John Lewis, who said the same thing asleep, awake, or before cameras and never came back at a critic, while disagreeing with his vision of integration; Churchville drew a category line between integration as a personal choice and economic justice as a necessity.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Buttermilk Bottom
clips.summary: He recounts leaving SNCC, joining the Nation of Islam in Atlanta under Minister Jeremiah Pugh, trying to recruit SNCC people to the mosque, and organizing the very poor unpaved community of Buttermilk Bottom on the theory that Black people should develop themselves rather than be nice to white folks.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Talented Tenth
clips.summary: Churchville describes being turned down by the Nation's Chicago headquarters when he sought permission to organize young people to sell papers, his W.E.B. Du Bois-inspired effort to recruit a talented tenth of Atlanta University professionals including a mathematician and an economist, and Chicago shooting down their proposal too, which first made him feel the Nation was not what it advertised.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: He Got Sent to Philadelphia
clips.summary: He tells how, losing interest in the Black Muslim movement, he followed Jeremiah Pugh back to Philadelphia on a promise to be made secretary of the mosque, a promise Elijah Muhammad overruled, and recalls Pugh's honesty in once telling him to get a grip about Elijah Muhammad being just a guy.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Freedom Library
clips.topic: Founding the Northern Student Movement Library
clips.summary: Churchville recounts joining the Northern Student Movement and opening the Freedom Library on Ridge Avenue, a storefront of books by and about Black people built on his belief that low self-esteem and ignorance of history held Black people back, which survived a North Philadelphia riot untouched and won a Dolfinger-McMahon Foundation grant.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Don't Touch Him
clips.summary: He describes meeting Malcolm X in New York after Malcolm left the Nation, agreeing to help start a Philadelphia chapter, joining the Organization of Afro-American Unity, and how putting Malcolm's picture in the Freedom Library window led Jeremiah Pugh to order the Fruit of Islam not to touch him and to beg his mother to intervene.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Just Preached Jesus
clips.topic: Conversion and a Debate With Pugh
clips.summary: Churchville recounts converting to Christianity around 1966 through a personal encounter in his own house, then debating Minister Jeremiah Pugh publicly with the Fruit of Islam standing by, praising what he had learned from Elijah Muhammad while testifying that as a Muslim he had lacked an internal witness that God was with him, and Pugh's joke that this first Muslim convert to Christianity would be the last and his private admission that, had he known Jesus as Churchville described him, he never would have trashed him.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Was Devastated
clips.summary: Churchville describes being devastated by Malcolm X's killing in 1965, reflecting on how people brought leaders criticism instead of help, and how the same people who said they would follow Malcolm if only he left the Nation still would not take a risk once he did, teaching him about human nature and fear.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Was In It to Win It
clips.summary: He explains his conviction that taking a principled stand, win or lose, lets him sleep at night because history eventually comes back around, then turns to the FBI coming to him at the Freedom Library seeking information about James Forman's reparations work, which he refused.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: On the Pink List
clips.summary: Churchville recounts being told he was on the pink list as both a Muslim and a communist when seeking work, learning the FBI had warned Southern contacts he was coming to foment violence, and his amusement that he never owned or advocated a gun, framing his view instead as creative versus destructive violence.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: A Faulty but Energizing Analysis
clips.topic: Why a Library Became a School
clips.summary: He describes seeing in SNCC and the Atlanta mosque how Black history energized people who felt stuck, and building the Freedom Library on Ridge Avenue near high-rise projects on the analysis, which he now calls fallacious, that Black people's central problem was not knowing their history.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Everybody Knows Your Name
clips.summary: Churchville recounts Wednesday-night history classes where patrons from the bar next door would chime in and correct his dates, teaching him before Cheers ever aired why people love bars, and reshaping his sense of his role from solving problems to eliciting and empowering what people already knew.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Three Steps Back
clips.summary: He tells how stranded tutors left him teaching children who could not grasp their homework because they had missed fundamentals three steps back, leading him to start with preschoolers not yet corrupted by the education system, and how a teacher using the look-say method soon gave up.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Found a Miracle
clips.summary: Churchville describes recalling first-grade phonetics with Mrs. Freed, discovering the J.B. Lippincott reading series that had children reading two years ahead, and using his own sons as guinea pigs, with his youngest son David reading at about a year and a half.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Like the Pied Piper
clips.topic: Opening the School for Ten Dollars a Week
clips.summary: He recounts going through the neighborhood like the Pied Piper recruiting children two and a half to five years old, incorporating Freedom Library Day School, charging ten dollars a week with lunch included, praying for ten students and starting with three, and using the individual prescriptive method to let each child move at their own pace.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Enjoyed It and I Hated It
clips.summary: Churchville describes grouping children so they pushed each other, finding his methods worked though teachers called them horrendous, and the exhausting nightly labor of grading forty-five separate homework books and preparing each child's next individual lesson, work he hated even as he loved watching them learn.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Skills and Blackness Both
clips.summary: He insists the work was simply what the children needed, not radical posturing, and contrasts his emphasis on skills, character, and history with other nationalist schools heavy on rhetoric and drums, naming his disagreement with Amiri Baraka and Maulana Karenga for building an ideological base without giving children usable skills.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: How to Make a Blackboard
clips.summary: Churchville recounts helping form CIBI in the early 1970s and running a two-week teacher-training institute at the Freedom Library Day School that taught teachers in independent Black schools how to build blackboards and room dividers, recruit children door to door, and demand the same work from a child labeled learning disabled as from any other, with lasting results.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Room to Voice Our Nationalism
clips.topic: Father Paul Washington and the Gangs
clips.summary: He describes coming to know Father Paul M. Washington, the North Philadelphia priest who gave them room to voice their nationalism when other churches found them tainted, and taking a small salary from a donor to work with gangs, where he began to see gang members as potential community leaders.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Gang Warring Is My Life
clips.summary: Churchville recounts a gang member telling him that gang warring was all he had, sending him back to imagine community protection as an alternative role, and his confrontation with the donor and with Father Washington when the funder cut the money for steering the work toward the gang members' real interests.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: We Tried to Make It Inclusive
clips.summary: He explains that the Black People's Unity Movement differed from others by being inclusive of gang members, elders, professionals, and artists like Arthur Hall and John E. Allen Jr. and future state representative David P. Richardson, tolerating fourteen points of view among seven people and seeking only what all could agree on.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Infamous Dynamite Case
clips.topic: A Police Plant and a Setup
clips.summary: Churchville recalls the founding principles that white supremacy was constitutive of United States culture and that Black progress depended on intraracial resources, then turns to the SNCC Dynamite case, describing Casamiro, a police plant who proposed bringing M-16s to the school, and his belief that the man arrested was set up with planted dynamite.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: They Broke Down the Door at One A.M.
clips.topic: Rizzo's Raid on the Freedom Library
clips.summary: He describes police commissioner Rizzo's officers breaking down the Freedom Library door around one in the morning in 1966 in bulletproof vests with sawed-off shotguns while his pregnant wife was horrified, how he calmly asked them to search the bedroom first so he could shelter her, and refused to give them the resisting-arrest pretext they sought.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: They Had a Warrant
clips.summary: Churchville recounts that the terror only struck after the police left and he could not sleep for nights, that they had been looking for dynamite, and that his lawyer and Black People's Unity Movement member William Lee Akers told him they had a warrant and nothing could be done, a hard moment given that he had never owned a gun or preached killing.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Don't Get It Confused
clips.summary: He describes dispersing a crowd outside that was ready to confront the police, lest they be mowed down, and deciding not to let his violated pride derail his work, framing the August 13, 1966 raid by FBI and Philadelphia police as a massive show of force by the white police establishment against the SNCC office and Freedom Library.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Function With Blinders
clips.summary: Churchville explains that he disciplined himself to stay in his lane and not get involved in the 1967 high school student organizing or the violence that befell those children, declining to chase press or causes outside his chosen work because his mother taught him to examine his motives.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: How Do We Get Rid of You
clips.topic: Voted Out as a Jesus Chairman
clips.summary: He recounts continuing the school until 1978, telling the Black People's Unity Movement around 1966 that as chairman he would use Jesus's leadership principles, being voted out when a member asked how to remove him, and feeling vindicated as friends called him a Jesus freak while he came to see Jesus as a revolutionary.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Was Very Anti-Politics
clips.topic: Refusing the War on Poverty Money
clips.summary: Churchville describes staying the course as people dropped away, turning history nights into Bible study, and his deep aversion to electoral politics and to the war-on-poverty funding streams that he felt turned self-styled community leaders into people chasing money, choosing instead to do his work without asking anyone for funds.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: When Things Got Frustrating, I Did National Stuff
clips.topic: CIBI Curriculum and a CBS Documentary
clips.summary: He recounts turning to national work when local efforts frustrated him, developing CIBI curriculum and trainings, and how a CBS documentary aired around 1969 led to speaking at colleges and demonstration teaching with children in Chicago, where he insisted no one should heed people who talk about teaching children but do not teach children.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Revolutionary Versus Cultural Nationalism
clips.summary: Churchville frames a strong period of struggle between revolutionary nationalism and the cultural nationalism of California and Newark, arguing for training community-trusted people and electing them outside the corrupt political machine, and describes letting a stalled project sit while he worked another so as never to be discouraged.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: I Was Supposed to Die
clips.topic: A Car Accident and a Turn to Theology
clips.summary: He describes a serious car accident around 1995 that he survived against expectation, which refocused him and drew him to study theology, and his realization that practicing law was not fun, beginning as a farm-worker attorney in Cumberland County, New Jersey, work that recalled the danger of Mississippi and Georgia.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Practical Sound Advice
clips.topic: From Criminal Law to a Leadership Institute
clips.summary: Churchville recounts moving to Philadelphia to practice estate planning and criminal law, growing frustrated trying to redirect the young people he defended, and after the accident developing the Churchville Leadership Institute to translate the practical wisdom of Jesus, Moses, and the prophets into sound advice without naming it religious.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Just Get My Piece of Paper
clips.summary: He describes attending Lutheran Theological Seminary at Philadelphia and being disturbed by fellow students aiming to be noticed by a bishop rather than serving a community, then pursuing African American studies at the Union Institute and University to deepen his theology and history.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: The Gospel to Empower or to Keep Down
clips.topic: A Thesis on Religion and Liberation
clips.summary: Churchville explains his thesis on whether religious thought makes people quiet or self-liberative, finding cases on both sides, the gospel used as in SNCC to empower people to fight and used in mining towns to keep miners from revolting, and his discouraging year teaching at the seminary that taught him people in a mill just want their paper.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: A Non-Religious Church
clips.summary: He recounts starting Liberation Fellowship Church of Jesus around 2005 in Germantown, a church framed around practical leadership lessons drawn from the Bible with a question-and-answer period and open Bible study, drawing Muslims, atheists, and others, out of which the small congregation built a community development corporation everyone helped write.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: What Are We Going to Do With This Thing
clips.topic: HIV Education and a Business Association
clips.summary: Churchville describes the CDC's HIV/AIDS community education program, for which he and his wife Nancy became certified testers and counselors and ran a health fair in Vernon Park, and his founding of the Greater Germantown Business Association after an earlier association deteriorated under real estate disputes.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Make Everybody Feel at Home
clips.topic: A Diverse Association and Town Hall
clips.summary: He recounts going store to store to build a diverse business association reflecting Germantown's Arab, Muslim, African, Asian, and Korean communities, and the Liberation Fellowship CDC taking on the redevelopment of the historic Germantown Town Hall at 5928 Germantown Avenue, near the birthplace of the antislavery movement.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: You Got Thirty-Five Million Dollars
clips.topic: A Vision for the Town Hall
clips.summary: Churchville describes the building lying in disrepair since the mid-1990s, a councilwoman laughing off the cost when he proposed acquiring it around 2008, and his vision, now taken seriously by the mayor's office of sustainability, of a green building using geothermal and solar power that houses dual education.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: A Rival to MIT in Germantown
clips.summary: He lays out the plan for the Town Hall as a rival to MIT teaching STEM subjects, green job training in plumbing and welding across from Germantown High School, and a green business incubator, ties it to the 2011 Brownfields Conference, and closes the interview with thanks from the museum and the library.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: A Mortician's Son in Lincoln City
clips.summary: Charles Jarmon opens the round of introductions, describing growing up in an extended family in Lincoln City, Kinston, North Carolina, his father a mortician and his mother a tobacco-factory worker, raised by his grandmother, aunt, and uncle after both parents died before he was twelve, until he left for the military in 1957.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: The Tuxedo Club and a Country School
clips.summary: Mrs. Stewart recalls her father Isaac Darden, a Kinston businessman who ran a poolroom, a soda shop, and a private club called the Tuxedo Club, her mother the minister of music at First Baptist Church, and being driven by Reverend and Mrs. Moses to a small country school with a potbellied stove until she entered Kinston's public schools as a fourth grader and the new Samson Elementary as a sixth grader.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Breakfast, Lunch, and Dinner
clips.summary: John Dudley describes growing up one of eleven children in Mitchell Wooten Court, his father the handyman there and his mother a seasonal tobacco-factory worker who labored alongside German prisoners of war after World War II, and how he put himself through college by working summers in Atlantic City and delivering groceries.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Watching His Stories at Lunch
clips.summary: Mrs. Suggs (Frances) remembers a family of five or six on Shine Street, her father a shirt-factory worker who biked home for lunch to watch his stories, her mother working in the tobacco factory, in domestic work, and at Piney Chemical Company, and schooling at Lincoln City Elementary and Tower Hill Road before Adkin.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: A Schwinn Bike and a Paper Route
clips.summary: Mr. Suggs tells of being an adopted only child from Snow Hill in Greene County, meeting his future wife in the fifth grade, and the Schwinn bike from his biological father that launched him as a boy entrepreneur: a 120-customer paper route, Afro-American subscriptions, Saturday grocery deliveries from the A&P, Sunday shoeshining, and mowing lawns to escape the green-tobacco fields, going to college only to honor his late foster mother.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: He Gave This to the Boys
clips.summary: Mr. Dove (Jackie) recalls growing up in Mitchell Wooten Court across from Harold, his parents divorcing when he was five, his father handing him fourteen cents to split among the boys, learning to read at four on his mother's lap, lasting one day in kindergarten, his mother taking in laundry and making apple jacks and potato jacks, and his own caddying years at the Kinston country club until he jacked a white golfer up on a fence for calling him boy.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: I Didn't Know I Was Poor
clips.summary: Dove continues, describing a mother who would not let him play football after he hurt his knees, teaching himself basketball and tennis by watching players like John Dudley and Calvin Thompson, learning to serve with a beat-up racquet, traveling north to Camden and Brooklyn in summers, and going on to play basketball and tennis at Fayetteville State University without ever taking a lesson.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: You Knew Where Your Limits Were
clips.summary: Asked about race relations, the men describe caddying at the Kinston country club to stay out of the tobacco field, allowed to play only on Wednesdays at a caddy house run by an African American, earning sixty-five cents for two rounds or more by hunting lost balls, and Dove recounts again the day Larry Beck, who had beaten Jack Nicklaus in the national juniors, called him boy, leading to a fight after which Dove left the club for good.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: That Wet Tobacco Slapping in Your Face
clips.summary: Jarmon describes tobacco work as a hand, picked up at dawn, the primers cropping leaves brought by mule-drawn wagon to the barn where women handed and looped the wet tobacco, and Dudley adds the skill of driving the mule-pulled truck between field and barn without overturning it, the croppers passing the time with folklore, jokes, and song as the Golden Leaf was cured.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Nanny, Aren't You Going to Get Up?
clips.topic: Dudley, the Stadiems, and a Bus
clips.summary: After a tobacco-field joke about seeing the Empire State Building, Dudley recounts working for H. Stadiem, a Jewish merchant ostracized for marrying a white woman, who let him drive and found him lodging on sales trips, and a bus incident when he was nine, when a white man told his mother to give up her seat and the bus driver, overhearing, told the man to sit elsewhere among the many empty seats.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: And So Is Toilet Paper
clips.topic: Dove's Bus and the Hannibal Suit
clips.summary: Dove tells of a bus ride at twelve when a white woman demanded his seat saying I'm white and he replied and so is toilet paper, the driver moving him to the rear until black men there said leave that boy alone, and the group recalls that within a year Dr. Hannibal's wife, an activist, challenged the bus company's license before the ICC over segregated seating, after which Kinston's bus system disappeared.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: We Don't Want Any of It
clips.summary: Mrs. Stewart recounts getting tobacco poison handing in the barn and seeing Dr. Hannibal, then trying cotton-picking for one day, and working years as a waitress at Standard Drug Store where Afro-Americans could not sit but ordered at the end of the counter, and a five-and-dime called Rose's where a group of them ordered armfuls of candy then said we don't want any of it and walked out.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Camaraderie in the Field
clips.summary: The group weighs working tobacco among a larger group against the isolation of caddying, agreeing the field was integrated with white croppers, even the farmer or his son, sharing a mason jar of water and a degree of camaraderie absent outside it, the owner's wife preparing lunch eaten at the table, and detailing the wage structure of primers at a dollar an hour against barn workers at four dollars a day.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: A Spread Just Like the Ponderosa
clips.summary: They describe where croppers stayed, sleeping under the barn and cooking corn in the ashes, nipping the suckers off growing plants, the curing process, and the end-of-season barbecue with pigs and watermelon that made you want to stay with a farmer, the radio station WFTC (World's Foremost Tobacco Center), and the North Carolina State Wolfpack golfers who took over the Kinston club on Wednesdays.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: K-Town and the Chitlin' Circuit
clips.summary: The Beaches sponsored Chitlin' Circuit performers like James Brown, Ruth Brown, and Arthur Prysock, and Mr. Suggs describes Kinston as K-Town, a hub among Camp Lejeune, Cherry Point, Fort Bragg, and Seymour Johnson, where he scalped dance tickets bought at a dollar fifty and sold at two dollars, where the young men had to defend their sisters from servicemen, and weekend accidents killed liquored-up soldiers heading back to base, a scene that lasted into the fifties.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: The Line of Demarcation
clips.summary: Returning to the buses, the group explains there were no separate white and black lines but a single transit system where whites sat up front and blacks in the back, the line of demarcation at the side back door, blacks forced to move back as the bus filled, and Dove and Mr. Suggs recount being told to shut a child up and the white prerogative that let a man sit beside Dove while a woman behind him demanded only the black boy's seat.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: All He Needed to Say Was Mgawa
clips.summary: Mr. Suggs describes a scheme two weeks after the Greensboro sit-ins in February 1960 to integrate Standard Drug Number Two: dressing his friend Curtis Henderson as a newly free Ghanaian diplomat who spoke only Tarzan's Mgawa while Suggs interpreted in French, convincing a confused waitress and a young white manager who feared a diplomatic problem, a friend from the projects refusing to serve them before the food came, and the three of them running back to East Kinston after the meal.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Little Things We Could Get Away With
clips.summary: The group shares quiet acts of defiance: a sister who dropped a prescription after a long wait, tasting the white water fountain to find the difference, and Mr. Suggs and his brother stealing Budweiser from supply cars by the Kinston Indians stadium railroad track to cool in the stream behind Mitchell Wooten Court, escaping the court case that caught the other boys.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: They Threw Rocks Every Saturday
clips.summary: Asked about interactions with whites, Mr. Suggs describes living beside the poor whites of the Simon Bright apartments, swimming with a boy named Bo and shining shoes to the bus station on Sundays, while Jarmon recalls the white kids who lay in wait throwing rocks as he walked past the white theater to the black one, and Mrs. Suggs tells of white boys who knocked her off her bicycle, took it, and spat on her.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Is There a School in Our Area Like This?
clips.summary: Dudley, of the class of 1952, explains the walkout began in a Wednesday civics class with the motivational teacher B.C. Davis, when the weekly reader's topic was what an ideal school has, and the students asked whether such a school existed nearby; told it was Grainger High up on the hill, they asked Mrs. Davis to leave the room so they could discuss it without involving her.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Petition the School Board
clips.summary: The students drew up eight items they needed, kept their teacher out so she could not be blamed, and won the principal Stewart's permission to meet without teachers in the auditorium and then the old barn, where, trusted as responsible kids, they petitioned the school board for a Monday hearing and resolved that if the board would not respond they would walk out.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Carolyn Has Lost Her Red Pocketbook
clips.topic: The Board Says No and the Signal
clips.summary: With all 720 students sworn to tell no parent or teacher and a coded signal that Carolyn had lost a red pocketbook, a committee of five met the board, who demanded who sent you and said the request was not in the budget for ten years; the next morning Dudley made the announcement and 720 students rose orderly, picked up pre-made placards, and marched down Queen Street.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: So Be It, We'll Take Our Chances
clips.summary: Still gathered around the school to strategize, the committee was warned by the board that seniors would not benefit and would not graduate if any of the 720 students caused trouble, and answered that a quality education mattered for their brothers and sisters; the students stayed out until the Monday after Thanksgiving, congregating at the Carver Theater, with Dr. Hannibal's activist wife as a next-door neighbor.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: What Am I Going to Say to My Parents?
clips.summary: Others describe their reactions, fear of facing their parents and going along with the crowd, parents who proved kind and asked only whether they had thought it through, and the deeper reason the students kept silent: their parents and teachers could lose their jobs, so even the principal's son, Mrs. Stewart's late husband, walked out without telling his father.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: There Was No Talking, We Just Rose Up
clips.summary: Participants recall the eerie quiet as 720 students rose when the announcement was made and walked out, the Quality Education placards hidden off campus and gathered at the Carver Theater, teachers amazed, and a seventh-grade girl whose religious teacher barred the classroom door so her class never joined the march but waited at the flagpole, the five leaders expressing no fear because the action was simply needed.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Ten Years Became Eighteen Months
clips.summary: Dudley reflects that the significance struck him only at the 2010 reenactment, that the eight petitioned items were built in eighteen months rather than ten years, and explains the committee of five decided to bring the 720 students back the Monday after Thanksgiving by calling designated key people once they felt they had made their point.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Leave Those Kids Alone
clips.summary: Dudley describes six seniors guarding Dr. Alice Hannibal's house for a week after bomb threats over her letters, no threats against the students' own families, and the Free Press editor Jake Strother probing Dove's laundress mother, who knew nothing; the group credits the principal Stewart, who told the panicked teachers to leave those kids alone, and other teachers who quietly let them meet without adults.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: A State-of-the-Art Gym and the Brown Backdrop
clips.topic: The Result and the Legal Context
clips.summary: They recount winning everything they asked for, a state-of-the-art gymnasium, home economics suite, band room, and classrooms, set the November 1951 walkout against the backdrop of Brown v. Board then before the Supreme Court, and note the parallel student cases, including Barbara Johns's Prince Edward County walkout at Moton High in 1951, which they had not known about when they organized.
related_events: Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
