clips.title: Halloween Helmets and a State Title
clips.summary: The group recalls the hand-me-down athletic equipment from Grainger High, brought by a recreation worker named Busby, the Halloween-lantern helmets they wore, and how, despite it, the 1952-53 Adkin team won the North Carolina state championship, beating the Little Blues of Raleigh and Dudley High, forced to play the title game at Grainger's stadium because they had none of their own.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: The Gym the Students Built
clips.topic: Recognition, the Gym, and Althea Gibson
clips.summary: They describe holding fish fries to buy their own championship jackets with no community receptions, the tiny gymnasium built by students in vocational class with a potbellied stove and no seats, an incident when a pin stuck in an opposing player was wrongly blamed on Dove until B.C. Davis intervened, and the great Althea Gibson of Williston High playing in that gym and beating their team almost by herself.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: And the Gym Burned Down
clips.summary: The group recalls football helmets thin as tams and how the student-built gym burned down after ashes from a warming fire caught the floor while they played, which got them the new state-of-the-art facility, and that the school itself closed with desegregation in 1970, recounting the indignity of having no mail delivered, so Dudley biked to the Queen Street post office for the school's mail in exchange for a free lunch.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: A Creek That Shared the School's Name
clips.summary: They describe the creek beside the campus that flooded the Mitchell Wooten Court apartments and drowned a first cousin, the only swimming they got, and identify Adkin High as a Rosenwald school, now gone with its original building, the gym the only survivor, and explain Rosenwald schools were built across the South for black children by the Sears head Julius Rosenwald at Booker T. Washington's urging, requiring community money and industrial courses.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: A Doctor's Wife Who Could Not Be Touched
clips.summary: The group portrays Alice Hannibal, born in New Jersey with a Jamaican husband trained at Fisk, the first black person on Kinston's city council, in whose yard the Klan burned crosses, who could act for education because her husband's medical practice made the family independent; her children went on to be doctors and a dentist, one working under Floyd McKissick at Soul City, and Dove recalls her bathing his back after he was shot on prom night, with a municipal building now named for her.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: If Mr. Charlie Said Do This
clips.summary: Asked about their parents' influence, the group reflects that class reunions keep the walkout alive, that most of their parents had not finished high school and could not grasp its impact in a world where one did what Mr. Charlie said, and Mr. Suggs recalls black adults on Federal Street in Durham telling student marchers under Floyd McKissick and CORE to go back to school, a generational gulf he imagines was wider still in 1952.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: I Never Looked at Myself as a Leader
clips.summary: Dudley reflects that his high-school leadership followed him into college as class and student-body president though he credited it to innate drive and dedicated teachers rather than his parents, and Mrs. Stewart describes how she and her late husband Reggie, longtime alumni-association president, reconstructed the walkout story from a letter by committee member Frederick Thompson to post in the gymnasium.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: You Never Told Me How We Got This Gym
clips.summary: Mrs. Stewart proposes a PowerPoint presentation for the alumni banquet so the story reaches members who cannot hear it over the gym's poor sound system, recalling a niece who never knew how they got the gymnasium because the cafeteria-head mother had stayed silent, and the group agrees they appreciate the walkout more every year; they then explain the application for National Register of Historic Places recognition of the gym was withdrawn because the original school is gone and twenty-three apartments now modify the structure, while the editor who penned the 2010 story won national editor of the year and Mrs. Stewart's husband commissioned an artist's drawing to keep the vanished school alive.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Intelligence for the Freedom Rides
clips.summary: Mr. Suggs, who brought the walkout to the Smithsonian, describes the interview as his own step into the Civil Rights Movement, recounting his first actions against Jim Crow on the bus and at a Kinston lunch counter, joining CORE after a December 1960 ride James Farmer assigned him to chart where black travelers could stop between Baltimore and Washington, work that turned out to be intelligence for the Freedom Rides, then organizing campus to campus as a college athlete and sitting in at Fayetteville State.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: The Blood Is on Your Hands
clips.summary: Mr. Suggs recounts a Fayetteville sit-in where a police sergeant harassed him over his draft card, white soldiers from Fort Bragg angrily telling the officer off, his being taken into custody at Pope Air Force Base's FBI office where he warned the agent the blood would be on his hands before producing the card, and the police chief's parting threat that if he ever got him in jail he might not come out alive.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: I Was the Guy Who Wrote the Position Paper
clips.summary: After an injunction bearing his name, Mr. Suggs left Fayetteville State for New York but kept organizing in every southern state except Mississippi, planning to retire at fifty-five after seeing too many colleagues die young, and describes his role in SNCC until it ended as writing position papers by assignment for James Forman, sometimes funded by Harry Belafonte, and later chairing the D.C. Coalition for Youth that built Marion Barry's summer youth program.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Keep Them Thinking on a Larger Plane
clips.summary: At Mr. Suggs's urging, Mrs. Stewart (Eleanor) describes the boys' choir she formed before retiring from D.C. public schools in 1993, now marking its twentieth anniversary with tours to Austria, Switzerland, Paris, England, and China and an invitation to Italy, her passion to open boys' eyes to a larger world, with a partnership with the Morehouse glee club, before the interview closes with thanks and a note that John Bishop filmed and Elaine Nichols of the Smithsonian took part.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Born in Sasser in 1940
clips.summary: Interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries at Albany State University in March 2013, Johnnie Ruth Brawner McCullar opens with her birth in Sasser, in Terrell County, Georgia, on February 4, 1940, to Clifford and Johnnie Lou Brawner, and traces her father's people from Worth County and her mother's roots back to Alabama.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: I Didn't Like the Farm
clips.summary: McCullar describes her hardworking father, who farmed, worked at the Marine base, and retired after about 28 years at the Swift fertilizer plant in Albany, and her own dislike of farm life near Doublegate on Old Dawson Road, the animals, heat, gnats, and snakes she feared, and the move off the farm to Sasser when she was twelve or thirteen.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: A One-Room School Called Piney Grove
clips.summary: She recalls a school year that ran October to April so children could work the rest of the time, the one-room Piney Grove school of seven grades, one teacher, and about forty students, her first teacher Vanny Wakefield, and her later moves to Dunbar in Sasser and to Carver High School in Dawson.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Books With the Pages Torn Out
clips.summary: McCullar describes Carver High School in the mid-1950s, cold rooms with one heater and no lunchroom, a partition between two classrooms doubling as an auditorium, and the hand-me-down books from white schools with pages torn out, sometimes one spelling book per class, conditions her parents knew left Black children improperly educated.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Waiting a Year for Her Sister
clips.summary: After graduating in 1959, McCullar stayed home a year keeping house and babysitting while her older sister finished nursing school in Bibb County near Macon, then chose Albany State so her sickly self would not be far from home, with her parents, who wanted her to teach or nurse, deciding her path.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Hepatitis From the Food
clips.summary: Enrolling at Albany State in 1960 with a vague plan to study science, McCullar enjoyed living on campus but found the food so badly prepared that she and other students complained to an unresponsive administration, and she came to believe the food made her ill.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: A March Downtown With Bertha Gober
clips.summary: McCullar recounts her first and only march at Albany State, a downtown demonstration she connects to a young woman named Bertha Gober, the fear of not knowing what would happen, and how her parents, learning of it from a white head cook who told her mother the girl should have known better, were not angry because things were opening up.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Becoming Secretary of the Terrell Movement
clips.summary: Falling ill in spring 1962 and returning home to recuperate, McCullar found her parents already going to meetings of the Terrell County movement, which had begun around 1960, held first at a Baptist church in Dawson and then in the family home, and somehow she was drawn in as secretary under its president, Lucius Holloway.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: My Mother Lost Her Job for Registering
clips.summary: McCullar describes the family's efforts to register at the courthouse basement, where the registrar, who also worked at the post office, would leave to avoid them and made applicants read a Bible scripture, and how after they registered her mother was fired from the white school and a later employer in Albany refused to fire her.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Sheriff Mathews at the Mass Meetings
clips.summary: As secretary she took notes as people described what they faced on their jobs and SNCC workers like Charles Sherrod, Don Stewart, Prathia Hall, and Ralph Allen spoke, while Sheriff Z.T. Mathews and his deputies would come to the Mount Olive meetings, put their feet on the seats, and insist outsiders were stirring up contented Black people.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Always Around the County
clips.summary: McCullar recalls meeting Charles Sherrod, whom she found a unique, soft-spoken, self-assured person, the SNCC workers' constant visibility in the county, and where they stayed, a woman named Carolyn Daniels housing some and Lucius Holloway housing others, while her mother made cakes to feed them.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: They Called Us Freedom Riders
clips.summary: With high school students Gladys Clark and her sister Anadine Clark, McCullar canvassed Dawson after school, met people who ran inside or were too afraid of being fired or evicted to talk, walked or hitchhiked home until dark, and always went in pairs or threes for safety as cars swerved at them on the road.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Just Sit at the Counter
clips.summary: McCullar describes occasional sit-ins with Reverend Samuel Wells at a restaurant in Dawson, where he instructed them only to sit at the counter and leave quietly if confronted so no one would be hurt, and how patrons and owners would call them names and refuse service while they simply got up and left.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Teaching the Doves to Sign Their Names
clips.summary: After the church burning, McCullar attended an adult literacy school that taught people who could not read or write to sign their names, recalling students like Catherine Mallory rejoicing at learning their alphabet, and she served as a poll watcher in local and national elections, helping voters who needed it choose their candidates.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Like a Death in the Family
clips.summary: McCullar describes the August 1962 burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church, the family church where mass meetings were held, as feeling like the death of someone loved, a blow that only made the people more determined, after which meetings moved briefly to a Methodist church and then to a tent set up behind a foundation her brother helped build.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Taking Turns to Watch the House
clips.summary: With the word out that movement participants' homes might be burned, McCullar's family took turns staying up at night watching for suspicious or strange cars, ready to get everyone out before a fire could be set, and she notes that one uncle, her father's brother, was also active while most relatives and returning classmates were not.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: They Took My Little Book to Washington
clips.summary: McCullar contrasts the Georgia Bureau of Investigation, which she felt came to cover for the perpetrators, with the U.S. Department of Justice, whose investigators took statements and carried off the little notebook of meeting notes she never got back, leading to federal court in Americus over church burnings and the shooting at SNCC workers by a local police officer.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: It Opened My Eyes
clips.summary: Reflecting on Terrell County, McCullar says her parents protected her from working in white homes so she met little prejudice as a child, knowing only that white students had buses and new books, but working in the movement opened her eyes to ordering a hamburger through a hole in the wall and made her determined to make things better for everyone, poor whites included.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Then I Got Married and Moved Away
clips.summary: McCullar recalls the canvassing slowing through 1963 and 1964 as people registered, work concentrating around campaigns to turn out voters, and her own departure to care for her sister's baby before marrying in 1967 and moving to Topeka, Kansas.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: The Signs Were Down
clips.topic: Coming Back to a Changed Area
clips.summary: After Kansas and four years in Atlanta, McCullar returned to Albany to work as a CNA at Phoebe Putney hospital, finding the black-and-white signs and segregated water fountains gone, restaurants and theaters open, and Terrell County's schools integrated with everyone now riding the bus.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: A Deputy Registrar for Seventeen Years
clips.summary: McCullar resumed movement work with the NAACP and served as a deputy registrar from about 1979 until 1996, spending four hours each Saturday morning registering voters because people thought her dependable, and recalls the Terrell County NAACP's strength and the 1994 murder of branch president JL Barnes.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Register, and Then Vote
clips.summary: McCullar reflects that things look better but much is hidden beneath smiles and handshakes, citing opposition she sees to the Black president, and closes by urging people to register and vote, to support and hold accountable the officials they put in office because if they do not, no one else will.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: Warren Street and the NAACP
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at Cascade United Methodist Church in Atlanta in June 2011, Joseph Lowery opens on coming to Warren Street Church in Mobile in 1952, where the NAACP held its Sunday afternoon meetings, and how he jumped in on voter registration and education before SCLC existed.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: A Racism Less Toxic
clips.summary: Lowery describes John LeFlore, the Mobile NAACP leader whose lawsuit-and-complaint style differed from his own activism and whose friendship later cooled, and a city he calls a little New Orleans, racist but not with the toxic racism of Birmingham or Montgomery, where the Catholic commissioner Joe Langan offered a warmer hand.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: A Paper Sack Full of Money
clips.summary: As president of the Interdenominational Ministerial Alliance, Lowery recounts carrying the first thousand dollars of outside support from a Mobile church to the Montgomery Improvement Association, the crowd going wild at the paper sack of money, and then launching Mobile's own bus movement with Fellowship of Reconciliation workshops on the dangerous Prichard route.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: Please Sit Down, the Bus Is Moving
clips.summary: Lowery tells of riding the front seat to Prichard with his Baptist partner, a drunk man with a bottle who ordered the driver to make them move, and how he defused it nonviolently by warning the man it was dangerous to stand on a moving bus, the man finally storming off, and a Black rider at the end of the line unsure how she felt about the change.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: The NAACP Is Outlawed
clips.summary: After a pause, Lowery describes the 1955 to 1956 boycott months, Governor Patterson outlawing the NAACP and demanding its membership roll while the group refused and was held in contempt, the Montgomery Improvement Association rising in its place under King and Abernathy, and King telling the crowd after his house was bombed to seek no eye for an eye.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: Born in New Orleans
clips.summary: Lowery recalls leaders including Fred Shuttlesworth, C.K. Steele, Kelly Miller Smith, and T.J. Jemison gathering in Atlanta to build a South-wide movement, the meeting cut short when Abernathy's church was bombed and reconvened in New Orleans as SCLC's official birthplace, a Klan-seized Montgomery night spent hiding in the Ben Moore Hotel, and King elected president with Lowery an officer.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: He Didn't Charge Us Anything
clips.summary: Lowery remembers Bayard Rustin coming to Montgomery to help draft the documents that formed the basis of SCLC without charging a fee, and King's emergence leading the first boycott a city of fifty thousand Black people sustained for over a year, accepted by many out of confidence in his leadership and making him the natural president when SCLC organized.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: The First Woman to Lead
clips.summary: Lowery credits Ella Baker as SCLC's first full-time executive director after John Tilley, a strong woman whom history may have overlooked as the first to give that executive leadership to the movement, who did not always get along with the preachers but whose inspiration helped organize the students into SNCC.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: We Were Outmanned
clips.summary: Lowery lays out the two roots of nonviolence: the practical reality of a small army with no weapons or uniforms that could never have won a violent confrontation against the National Guard and troopers, and the theological reality that the most independent leader was the preacher, accountable to his congregation, drawing on the example of Jesus forgiving from the cross.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: We Became New Creatures
clips.summary: Lowery reflects that nonviolence worked inwardly as well as outwardly, freeing them from hatred, recalling George Wallace astounded by their spirit, the citizens' council unable to understand a courage they tried to call cowardice, and a Nashville white woman who opposed the direct action movement until she watched their conduct and said the spirit of the Lord was upon them.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: The Lord Spoke and I Said Madison
clips.summary: Returning after a break, Lowery describes the late 1950s libel campaign and the seizure of his car under a judgment, his home safe because he lived in the church parsonage, and how, having warned his lawyer not to mention the Huntsville property he inherited from his father, he answered on the stand that he was born in Madison rather than Huntsville, sparing the land taken from Abernathy.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: Sitting With Reinhold Niebuhr
clips.topic: The Libel Suit and Its Reversal
clips.summary: Lowery recounts the libel suit argued to the Supreme Court with the New York Times and four ministers, Abernathy, Shuttlesworth, Seay, and himself, the Times refusing to shelter them under its bond, the city's lawyer Roland Nachman invoking Sammy Davis Jr. and his marriage to a white actress to inflame the jury, and the eventual reversal that returned the car his Methodist members had bought back at auction for his wife.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: Over My Dead Body
clips.summary: Lowery turns to the students entering in 1960 and the movement growing from buses and voter registration to segregation as a whole, the 1964 ruling against segregation in public accommodations, and demonstrating at Morrison's cafeterias in Nashville until the chain's president vowed to desegregate over his dead body, then desegregated in July and died a month later.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: The Era of Self-Determination
clips.summary: Lowery names the Montgomery boycott as the beginning of an era of self-determination, the first time an entire community refused the back of the bus regardless of court or legislature, and the sit-ins as another expression of the same spirit, refusing to buy toothpaste where they could not eat lunch, for which he later received an award from Greensboro.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: We Did Not Hate Bull Connor
clips.summary: Lowery describes how the spirit of nonviolence spared them from hating even Bull Connor or King's killer James Earl Ray, and how after Birmingham they retreated to the A.G. Gaston Motel and chose Selma for a voting campaign over President Johnson's plea to slow down after the 1964 act, picking the black belt county where SNCC had already been persecuted.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: How Long, Not Long
clips.summary: Lowery recounts Dallas County with its handful of Black registered voters, the march conceived after Jimmie Lee Jackson was killed in Marion and mourners marched to his grave, his wife's memorials to Jackson and James Reeb, Bloody Sunday halted at the bridge, and the eventual arrival in Montgomery where King declared how long, not long.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: The Blue Sea Parted
clips.summary: Named by King to chair the committee carrying the march's demands to Governor Wallace, Lowery tells of state troopers lining the Capitol steps like a blue sea until the National Guard general ordered them aside with bayonets, his refusal to hand the petition to a secretary, the later ninety-minute meeting where Wallace shredded scraps of paper, and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 that changed the nation's voting pattern.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: There'd Be a Black President
clips.summary: Lowery reflects on hearing Bobby Kennedy in the Freedom Riders film predict a Black president none of them thought they would live to see, and how, feeling God moving in history, he became an early supporter of Barack Obama when few civil rights leaders were, unlike the purely symbolic Jesse Jackson campaign of 1984.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: Good Crazy
clips.summary: Lowery describes going to Brown Chapel AME in Selma in 2007 to hear Obama, his good crazy speech that YouTube spread across the country, Obama naming himself the Joshua generation to Lowery and John Lewis's Moses generation, marching the bridge together with his wife, and later praying at a Georgia Tech rally and campaigning as far as a chilly Iowa where strangers held yes we can signs.

clips.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
clips.title: The Last Word at the Inauguration
clips.summary: Lowery recalls Obama calling his cell phone to ask him to give the inaugural benediction, choosing the benediction for its last word, and standing on the steps with hazed-over eyes near eighty hearing King's summons of forty years before answered in the election, weeping as the anthem sounded better though nothing had changed but the country, before closing with thanks and the Joseph Lowery Institute.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: A College President's Daughter
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline for the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian NMAAHC on December 13, 2015, Joseph and Embry Howell introduce themselves, and Embry tells of being born in 1945 in Bethesda, Maryland, raised first in Bristol, Virginia, then on the campus of Davidson College in North Carolina, where her father was treasurer and ultimately president.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: Across the Tracks in Davidson
clips.summary: Embry describes parents born and raised in Georgia and unusually well-educated grandparents, a mother who attended communist meetings in 1930s New York, a father whose presidency helped integrate Davidson with its first students from Africa around 1963, and her own segregated schooling alongside friends she crossed the tracks to play with, learning that all people have dignity.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: Belle Meade, Banking, and Polio
clips.summary: Joe recounts being born in 1942 in Nashville and raised in the elite Belle Meade neighborhood, the son and grandson of bank presidents, expected to become a banker and sent to Montgomery Bell Academy, and contracting polio in 1952 and convalescing at Warm Springs, an experience that left him moderately paralyzed, cost him two years of school, and gave him a lasting empathy for people who were suffering.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: The Controversial Student
clips.summary: Joe tells of arriving at Davidson, where he met the much-younger Embry and became a campus leader as president of the YMCA and ODK, while growing aware of the early sit-in movement planned in Nashville, and recalls the childhood playmate Frederick, the nephew of his family's housekeeper, whom he was told he could no longer play with, an injustice he found a no-brainer even as a boy.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: You Can't Be Neutral
clips.summary: Joe describes a 1964 conference at Bryn Mawr called the Third Great American Revolution, where lieutenants of CORE, SNCC, and SCLC urged students that they could not be neutral, and how, recalling the Freedom Riders who had passed through Davidson, he and a handful of students drove home and resolved to mount a march on Charlotte gathering signatures for the Civil Rights Act of 1964 to mail to President Johnson, partnering with schools like Barber-Scotia and Johnson C. Smith.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: A Hint of a Wink
clips.summary: Joe recounts being summoned at night to the home of Davidson's president, D. Grier Martin, who, under pressure from the Board of Trustees and the mayor of Charlotte, asked him to abandon the march while stressing he could not force him, all with a hint of a wink that signaled private support, the president working behind the scenes to advance integration at Davidson.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: Banker's Son Leads Rights March
clips.summary: Joe tells how the march drew wire-service coverage and a conservative Nashville headline reading Banker's Son Leads Rights March, naming him as the son of a city bank president, how his parents proved supportive amid the elite cocktail-party set, and how he became a pariah, turned away at a friend's door as a discredit to his race, a woman he came to pity as a product of her time.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: A Pied Piper Named Al
clips.summary: Joe describes his friendship with Allard Lowenstein, who taught at North Carolina State and sent him notes scribbled on the backs of tests by special delivery, a former National Student Association president and Yale Law graduate close to Eleanor Roosevelt and the Kennedy family, a charismatic pied piper of the dump-Johnson movement later assassinated by a mentally ill former student.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: Into the Big Leagues
clips.summary: Joe recounts a Rockefeller Fellowship taking him to Union Theological Seminary in New York, where Paul Tillich and Reinhold Niebuhr had taught, a year out working in an urban service-training program, and a path through city planning, and Embry tells her side of their courtship, marrying after her sophomore year, transferring to Barnard while they lived at Hastings Hall, then both heading to graduate school in Chapel Hill.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: I Need You Down in Southwest Georgia
clips.summary: The Howells describe being recruited at Union Theological Seminary by Charles Sherrod, a charismatic SNCC founder working in Southwest Georgia who looked them in the eyes and asked for their help, and recall a pre-summer training conference near Wake Forest, a radical Christian ecumenical institute they found to be thought control, where civil rights workers advised them to back their car in for a quick getaway.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: Go Home, Whitey
clips.summary: Arriving in Southwest Georgia after driving all night to the SNCC office in the Albany neighborhood called Harlem, the Howells were met by workers who told them panthers eat roosters and to go home, an unsettling welcome they came to understand through the 1966 struggle between John Lewis and Stokely Carmichael over whether white Southerners still had a role, the summer Carmichael and Willie Ricks raised the call that confused where the couple could fit.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: I Couldn't See the Black Doctor
clips.summary: Embry reflects on being treated with kid gloves as a white Southern woman whose Georgia upbringing let her relate easily to local Black people, while the color line still cut hard: a white woman who refused her a ride, a serious infection that forced her to a white doctor rather than the Black one, and a reluctant Black barbershop that gave Joe a haircut, all leading to the central question of where a married couple could live.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Born on Section 7
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Library of Congress in December 2015, Juadine Henderson opens with her birth in Batesville, Panola County, Mississippi, to farmers Leora Morris and Hugo Henderson, her father's death when she was four or five, her mother's remarriage when she was ten, and the family's move to eighty acres of land owned by groups of brothers and cousins that everyone called Section 7.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Walk the Glasses Out Front
clips.summary: Henderson recalls washing dishes at age eight in Brown's Cafe where her mother worked, being told to carry a tray of glasses out front to see the white diners, her sisters and a brother who has since died, and starting school at age three because her grandmother taught at the church-run Olive Ray School where she learned to read and write early.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: I Cried for His Parents
clips.topic: Emmett Till and the Color Line
clips.summary: She describes riding a farm truck to school while white children passed on a bus, playing with poor white neighbors who had to watch for their father's car, and at age eight reading the magazine coverage of the Emmett Till murder her mother gave her, weeping for his parents and knowing something was seriously wrong.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: You Must Never Say That Again
clips.topic: The NAACP Card and the Church
clips.summary: Henderson tells of finding her mother's NAACP card and being warned never to say the name again, the hushed meetings in their two-room former store, and Sunday sermons at West Camp Missionary Baptist Church where Reverend Middleton taught Black history and the congregation discussed Little Rock and voter registration.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Walking Down the Road With Their Guns
clips.summary: She recounts the NAACP-active men of the county, C.J. Williams, Robert Miles, and Willie Kirkendall, and how when a sheriff ordered Kirkendall to beat his daughter falsely accused of stealing and Kirkendall refused, the men of the neighborhood walked down the road with guns on their shoulders to climb into the trees and trucks and protect the family, who then moved to Indiana.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: There Was a Stranger There
clips.summary: Henderson describes meeting SNCC's Frank Smith at her cousin's house at Thanksgiving 1962 when she was thirteen, riding with him to point out the house of a relative of one of Emmett Till's killers, telling her mother that Black people in Batesville could change the whole system if they voted, and beginning to organize her friends.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Freedom in the Air
clips.summary: She tells how the young people met secretly at Concord Missionary Baptist Church with a record player, learned freedom songs from the album Freedom in the Air featuring Bernice Johnson Reagon and the Albany Movement, putting the word freedom where Jesus had been, and how she once played Oh Freedom instead of Amazing Grace and survived the elders' shock.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Headed to Greenwood
clips.summary: Henderson recounts hiding a packed bag under the bed to attend a voter registration workshop in Greenwood, her mother consulting an uncle who let her go because her older cousin Jimmy was going, the June 1963 trip with cousins and friends including John Hardy, and arriving at the hushed, serious Greenwood SNCC office to stay with a woman called Mama Hackman.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Medgar Evers Was Killed Last Night
clips.summary: She describes her first mass meeting, learning the next morning that Medgar Evers had been killed in Jackson and the boy called Freedom delivering the news, canvassing the neighborhood with leaflets, the largest gathering she had ever seen, and meeting John O'Neal, Willie Peacock, and James Peacock at voter registration workshops.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Connecting the Dots
clips.summary: Henderson tells of practicing the long voter registration forms and interpreting a section of the state constitution, learning only two Black people in the county were registered, and describing Panola County where Northern landowners held their own land with little sharecropping apart from the Macmillan plantation, in a town that was fifty-four percent Black with no Black voters.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: We Found Out Later
clips.summary: She recalls that while she was in Greenwood, June Johnson, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Euvester Simpson came up missing and Lawrence Guyot was sent to look for them, that she eavesdropped on the discussions but only learned later from another group of Batesville kids what had happened, and that the news did not register as fear because the women were still strangers to her.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: I Think Those People Hypnotized You
clips.summary: Henderson describes returning to harass her mother and stepfather about registering to vote, her mother saying the movement people had hypnotized her, the small neighborhood meetings, and her resentment that when the 1964 Summer Project brought crowds of outsiders to town after she had been begging people to act for a year, though it broadened the movement countywide.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: She Hit Me With the Fly Swatter
clips.summary: She recounts being driven home late by Jeff Cowan, a young white Harvard volunteer, and her mother hitting her with a fly swatter for riding around with white children, then describes her stepfather memorizing and reciting Section 30 of the Mississippi Constitution all night, with a little drink to help, so that both parents passed the registration test in 1964.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Everybody Was From West Camp
clips.summary: Henderson names the older Panola movement people, Robert Miles, C.J. and Jasper Williams who had attended Piney Woods boarding school, and the Robinson family, most of them West Camp landowners who traced their ancestry to Guinea, and notes that her mother spent three years in eighth grade because there were no high schools for Black children until the 1950s.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: I'm Coming Out of the Kitchen With a Knife
clips.summary: She describes the young people moving to direct action in 1964, her mother quitting her restaurant job and vowing to come out with a knife if anyone touched her daughter, and a sit-in at that same restaurant where the waitress Miss Lil nervously refused to serve them and they sat in the booths after practicing nonviolent workshops.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Slinging Us Across the Floor
clips.summary: Henderson recounts white farmers in brown cotton gloves throwing the demonstrators into the street and banging heads together, a second restaurant locking its doors as pool-hall men came out with cues, and the deputies finally marching the singing students to jail where the sheriff booked them but refused to lock the cell, releasing those under eighteen at five o'clock.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Take Some Ice Water for the Kids
clips.summary: She tells of being picked up again by the sheriff while carrying ice water to jailed friends, the whole community turning out for the over-eighteen trials, Robert Miles decking a man who tried to hit white volunteer Chris Williams, and the juvenile court judge warning that continued protest would leave them with a criminal record when freedom came.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: You're Moving Out of That Room
clips.summary: Henderson describes a deputy stepping into their house one Saturday morning, saying wrong house and leaving, her stepfather concluding the man had come to locate her room for a night attack and immediately moving her and loading a shotgun, and her high school principal quietly offering help after a gun was fired near the jail.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Almost Every Woman Petted Her Purse
clips.summary: She recounts a voter registration meeting at West Camp where, told strangers were inquiring outside, almost every woman patted the pistol in her purse and the response was carry on, that the women including her mother were good shots who carried even to church without the men knowing, and that her grandfather tried but failed to teach her to shoot after she turned eighteen.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: That's Where I Have to Be
clips.summary: Henderson describes winning an academic scholarship to Mississippi Industrial College, then hearing that a thousand farm workers had walked off Delta plantations and deciding at eighteen to work in the movement full time, telling her disbelieving mother, taking ten dollars slipped by her older sister, and catching a ride to Jackson with project head Lou Grant.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Stay With Us
clips.topic: Mount Beulah and a Jackson March
clips.summary: She tells of arriving at the Mount Beulah conference center, fleeing a room with no locks when a man got into the next bed and finding three older women from Batesville who took her in, spending her last money on a dress, and going to the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party office in Jackson to canvass for a demonstration while pretending she knew the city.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Passing the Trench Coat Around
clips.summary: Henderson recounts being arrested within an hour at the Jackson demonstration and held two weeks at the Fairgrounds with about a thousand women, sharing a single trench coat to bathe, washing clothes with ivory soap, stacking mattresses at five in the morning, the boiling wash tanks, and the authorities separating the white women into the jail.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: She Steps Out and Starts Praying
clips.summary: She describes a Fairgrounds standoff when a woman refused to give up her mattress and took an officer's billy club, the police facing the cursing women until Mrs. Thelma Glover of Batesville walked into the line praying and swinging her arms until they backed out, and her bail arriving with a change of clothes sent by a woman whose sister was a Black police officer.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Just What I Needed
clips.summary: Henderson tells of reuniting with Hollis Watkins, going to the scary town of McComb, meeting Curtis Hayes who was assembling Mississippi young people to organize the Delta, traveling to Cleveland near the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union work, and joining the mostly male Freedom Corps paid ten dollars a week by the Delta Ministry through the National Council of Churches.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Owen Was Like a Mother Hen
clips.summary: She recounts Owen Brooks of the Delta Ministry threatening to call her mother when she stayed out all night at a juke joint and placing her with the Washington family, then her move to Rosedale to live with local leader Frank Davis alongside Morton Thomas of Harvard and Billy Kochiyama, and names the local Freedom Corps members including Isaac Foster and Joe Harrison.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: I'm Playing First Base
clips.topic: Holding Her Own and the Chase
clips.summary: Henderson describes telling Benjamin Brown she would play first base whether or not he liked it, recruiting women like Helen Williams who could hang and friends who could not, and the dangerous canvassing on plantations where a lookout in the van beeped the horn when the owner's dust appeared so everyone could run, sometimes returning to find families had fled overnight.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Three Dollars a Day
clips.summary: She explains the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union effort to organize farm workers earning thirty cents an hour for ten-hour days to walk off for higher pay, the company-store debts that trapped them, her tiny freedom school teaching dropouts to write their names, and Billy Kochiyama, whose family had been interned in World War II, telling her she was African American.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: It Was My First Plane Ride
clips.summary: Henderson recounts the Freedom Labor Union walkouts that left people without support and the tent camp at the Greenville Air Force Base, then being sent on her first plane ride, terrified, to speak at white church groups in Minnesota, Pennsylvania, and Illinois with Reverend Robert Beach in late 1965, where congregations stared at her natural hair.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: A Wonder We Don't See You Hung
clips.summary: She tells of a Pittsburgh woman warning that Black people did not go out to Mount Lebanon after dark, her sister in Chicago straightening her hair and dressing her in a suit and heels for a church talk, and her decision to keep her hair natural after seeing Gwen Gillian and an Odetta album cover, against her sister's wishes.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: It's Time to Move
clips.summary: Henderson describes the Freedom Corps breaking up as the politics of the movement shifted in early 1966, leaving a Bolivar County family's home where a mouse ran across her pillow, and moving to Jackson to live in the Patio Apartments with movement women including Gracie Hartthorn, Joyce Brown, and Marjorie Hyatt who worked for the MFDP.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: You Need to Go See Dr. King
clips.topic: Strike City and a Push to College
clips.summary: She recounts working in Frank Smith's neighborhood development office making bricks for Strike City, the tent town where people went after being kicked off plantations, meeting Jean Wheeler who was married to Frank and became a big sister, and a recruiter from Bishop College in Texas drawing the movement young people who had finished high school.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Look, She Washed Her Hair
clips.topic: An Afro at a Baptist College
clips.summary: Henderson describes the Bishop College recruiter's repent-and-be-saved car, students staring at her short afro as though she had simply washed her hair, being assigned to the laundry room because she would touch no one's hair, being fired after a hairdryer roller burned and smoked, and a librarian comparing her to an African woman.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Can You Do My Hair Like That
clips.topic: A Hair Movement and Sunday Visits
clips.summary: She tells how classmates soon asked her to cut their hair natural, how Ebony's 1966 all-natural cover pleased her mother and made afros acceptable, and how at the strict Baptist campus she skipped chapel to play guitar and spent Sundays visiting Lafayette, a federal prisoner at Seagoville she reached by signing out for a Quaker meeting.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Put Your Clothes On, We're Going to See Dr. King
clips.topic: King's Visit and a New Push
clips.summary: Henderson recounts leaving Bishop in 1968, her depression and her uncle marching her out to see Dr. King speak in Batesville before his death, her surprise that young people were with him, and her friend Tate arriving to organize the Poor People's Campaign as King was assassinated shortly after.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: And Then You Can Go Home
clips.summary: She describes going to the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, helping get people on buses to Washington while insisting she would not go, then being passed from Selma to Montgomery to Birmingham each time told she could go home afterward, ending up in Washington with the Mule Train, and meeting reporters Gerald Fraser, Thomas Johnson, Robert Maynard, and Earl Caldwell whose work fascinated her.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Living Out Here in Mud
clips.summary: Henderson contrasts the poor people living in the muddy tents of Resurrection City on the National Mall with the SCLC staff who had warm rooms at the Pitts Motor Inn, recounts spotting Charlie Cobb on 14th Street after the riots, and how Frank Smith finally told her to get her bags and pulled her out of the camp and the campaign.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Every Book in the World About Black People
clips.summary: She tells of becoming a student of Frank Smith's at the Institute for Policy Studies among fellows like Ivanhoe Donaldson, then taking a job at the Drum and Spear Bookstore founded by Charlie Cobb and Afro-American Resources, the largest Black bookstore she had seen, its link to the Center for Black Education, and the Sunday radio program Sauti ya Watoto.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: They Tear Gassed the Bookstore
clips.summary: Henderson recounts the police tear-gassing the store while historian Lerone Bennett Jr. signed copies of Before the Mayflower, Ralph Featherstone becoming manager and making the store more political, and the store moving to a bigger location only for Featherstone to be killed by a car bomb with Che Payne on March 10, 1970, with no counseling offered.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: You Actually Put On Clothes
clips.topic: Office Work and the West Indies
clips.summary: She describes leaving the bookstore for a receptionist job at the Black Child Development Institute where she hid that she could not type by working at night, meeting Florence Tate who became a big sister, becoming a program analyst, and a recuperative trip with Judy Richardson and Mimi through Puerto Rico, Haiti, and Trinidad, including a Haitian guide who took her to a vodou ceremony whose drumming terrified her into promising never to return.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: A Big Boom on the Third
clips.topic: The July Picnic and the Drums
clips.summary: She describes the two-day picnic every July third and fourth in northern Panola County where old men heated drumheads over a fire and only the men danced, including her embarrassing stepfather, her later discovery online that the Smithsonian had recorded the drummers in the 1940s and traced their rhythms to Mali, the customs of saluting the drums and serving goat that Alan Lomax noted elsewhere but her community did not follow, and Othar Turner, who came after Sid Hemphill, and the wish to recover the recordings for younger people.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Not to Bring Peace But a Sword
clips.summary: She returns to playing Oh Freedom in church and reflects that she stopped playing piano and going to church after she turned eighteen, the deacon who locked the church and went house to house warning parents yet later sheltered volunteers, and West Camp opening its doors when Concord locked hers.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: A Movement Town
clips.summary: Henderson contrasts active Greenwood with quieter Batesville, recalls meeting Freddie Greene and reconnecting over the years, and describes Robert Miles as a strong farmer and community man who once checked on the children playing church, alongside the outspoken teacher Mrs. Glover who stood praying between the police and the demonstrators.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: They Watched Them
clips.summary: She describes the active Panola women like Pearl and Virgil Robinson who took in civil rights workers and got people to the polls without speaking publicly, the Thomas Sundry where people gathered, the shock and curiosity at white volunteers like Penny Patch and Claire O'Connor walking the neighborhood, and her mother keeping her separate from the volunteers who would leave.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: He Left His Car in Town
clips.summary: Henderson recounts serious family trouble before the 1960s when an uncle jumped by white men in Pope led to a shooting that left one dead and one paralyzed, how one uncle took responsibility by leaving his car as a decoy and turning himself in when the family was threatened, his conviction and electrocution around 1952, and her father's mysterious death eleven months later.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: It Was Performance Art
clips.summary: She describes learning of her father's death from her uncle while pretending not to already know, treating the funeral as performance art and watching her older cousin to know when to cry, and recalls relatives suspecting she joined the movement because of the family trouble and asking where her daddy was without knowing they had executed the wrong Henderson.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Only I Know, and I Will Never Tell
clips.summary: Henderson tells of her jailed relative refusing to let her bring ice cream so she would not remember him that way, moving to California in 1979 partly to learn from the surviving uncle what happened that night, his answer that only he knew and would never tell, and his widow later revealing that he had actually done the killing while his brother took the blame.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: He Owned Thirteen People
clips.summary: She recounts learning that her great-great-grandfather Duncan C. Henderson was a slaveholder who lived with his Black family and founded the Henderson school in Panola County, finding him on the slave schedule owning thirteen unnamed people, and a cousin helping recover the name of an enslaved ancestor, Classy.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: I Decided I Wanted to Be a Doctor
clips.summary: Henderson describes returning from the West Indies and Florence Tate keeping her working, brief stints studying premed at George Washington University and jobs at the Children's Defense Fund and the Interreligious Foundation, then the National Urban Coalition as a public relations writer, before reading of Columbia's Michele Clark Fellowship for minority journalists and being accepted in 1974.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: A Sneaky Shy
clips.summary: She recounts being too shy to ask a gubernatorial candidate a single question, Earl Caldwell drilling her on note-taking at the weekly paper Deadline, writing a story she liked about an escaped North Carolina prisoner, covering the Newport Jazz Festival, and starting her first newspaper job at the Daily Argus in Mount Vernon with a front-page story she did for twenty-eight years in journalism.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: The Movement Is Us
clips.summary: Henderson reflects that the movement and her strong family shaped her, recalls her grandmother teaching and her grandfather the farmer-healer who wanted her to be a lawyer, says she stays in touch with Charlie Cobb and Frank Smith, and stresses that young people once stood up to power as they do now, and that she left the church at eighteen.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Marched Us Off to Parchman
clips.summary: She recounts a church-steps rally in Rosedale when fearful churches would not open their doors, men jumping from the bushes with rifles to arrest about ten of them and take them overnight to Parchman, the strip search, a jar of liquid that forced everyone to the single toilet, and Morton Thomas of Boston beaten so fiercely by other prisoners that his father sent for him.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Jumping on the Burning Rag
clips.topic: A Detroit Doctor and a Gun
clips.summary: Henderson tells of slipping freedom buttons onto her young sister's clothes, doing little on the Meredith March but stopping the car to jump on a burning mosquito rag after a Detroit doctor disparaged the poor people of Mississippi, and her sister handing her a thirty-eight when a car followed them, with instructions to shoot out the back window if it got too close.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Right on the Money
clips.summary: She recalls hearing Willie Ricks and Stokely Carmichael in Greenwood after having been at the Nashville meeting where Carmichael was elected, thinking black power necessary and being surprised it turned so many people off, the Deacons for Defense walking armed on the Meredith March, and learning while in school that Benjamin Brown had been killed by police, which briefly broke her.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: We Had Better Mobilize Again
clips.summary: Henderson grieves Benjamin Brown killed for fetching a sandwich near Jackson State, reflects on the pain of watching the present and the loss of the once close-knit community now scattered, mentions a nephew who is both a Batesville deputy and a preacher and her habit of giving children books so they know where they came from, and closes urging young people to mobilize again because you cannot let it stop you.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Closing the Curtains on the Union Man
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in Silver Spring, Maryland, on December 9, 2015, Judy Richardson opens with her 1944 birth in Tarrytown, New York, her seamstress and jazz-pianist mother Mellie Louise Richardson, and her father William King Richardson, who helped organize the United Auto Workers local at the Fisher Body Chevrolet plant, the family drawing the curtains around the national organizer and finding grocery bags left on the porch by neighbors too afraid to support him openly, until he died of a heart attack on the assembly line around 1951 when she was seven.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: A Town Steeped in Sleepy Hollow
clips.summary: Richardson sketches Tarrytown's roughly ten percent Black population, the working-class Italian, Polish, and Irish families under the hill near the plant, the Washington Irving and Sleepy Hollow schools, and her later return from SNCC to protest segregated housing alongside a Black doctor and architect couple who became the first to move onto Broadway.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Billy's House and the Blackball
clips.summary: She recounts the picket line her group threw up at the all-white volunteer fire department, explaining the blackball that kept Black residents out, and a fireman who, when the family house caught fire on Thanksgiving, stopped another from taking a hatchet to the door because it was Billy's house.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Stokely in Patriots Park
clips.summary: Back in Tarrytown around 1964 raising money for SNCC, Richardson brought Stokely Carmichael and the SNCC Freedom Singers, including Bernice Johnson Reagon, Chuck Neblett, Rutha Mae Harris, and Cordell Reagon, to Patriots Park, drawing a red-baiting letter from a Mrs. Kenney charging that Communists were singing in the park.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: My Little Mommy's Blue Chords
clips.summary: Richardson reads her mother's worried letters from the 1964 Mississippi period, written in beautiful hand despite an eighth-grade education, recalls returning to find her mother playing We Shall Overcome with gorgeous blue jazz chords she had heard rather than read, and describes her mother's earlier years playing piano with the Teddy Whiteley Orchestra in Westchester County until her father stopped her, and her lifelong love of Oscar Peterson and Art Tatum.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Booking the Numbers
clips.topic: The Numbers and a Fur Coat
clips.summary: She explains how her mother booked numbers, the lottery based on the day's horse races, for the ethnic neighborhood with Trippi as the runner, how her father was once arrested with number slips on him, and how her mother, after a win, drove to the Bronx to buy fur coats with her sister Helen.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: She Paid Cash for the Valiant
clips.summary: After her father died, Richardson recounts the UAW pension that gave her mother a modicum of security, one of the only two times she saw her mother address race, when a white salesman implied she could not afford a Valiant and she returned to pay cash outright, and how her mother kept scoring at the top of the county exam yet was hired only after the druggist Mr. Slaven told her to switch her registration to Republican, becoming a secretary in White Plains.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: I Couldn't Lift My Head
clips.topic: A Mother and Birth of a Nation
clips.summary: Richardson recounts the second time her mother spoke of race, describing seeing Birth of a Nation around 1915 under President Wilson and being so shamed by its portrayal of Black people that she could only look down at the pavement leaving the theater, a film that valorized the Klan as protectors of white womanhood after Reconstruction.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: The Same Illustration in My Textbook
clips.topic: A Caricatured Reconstruction and Little Rock
clips.summary: She describes the lone illustration of Black Reconstruction in her AP history textbook at Sleepy Hollow High School, drawn from Birth of a Nation, the white classmates glancing back at her, learning later from historian John Dittmer during eyes on the prize that it was a nationally prescribed text, and her own high-school letter to the Tarrytown Daily News questioning why the Little Rock students were trying to enter a white school.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Four Black Men, Four Black Women
clips.summary: Richardson describes entering Swarthmore in 1962 as one of eight Black freshmen, four men and four women, after Bennington offered admission without money while Swarthmore offered full freight for four years, all of them housed with Quaker children on the assumption the Quakers would be more accepting.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: The Boy Who Knew Lenin and Trotsky
clips.summary: She contrasts her own background, one plane ride to Buffalo with the all-state youth orchestra, with classmates who had wintered in Paris and come from schools like Sidwell Friends, and recalls respecting a Poli Sci classmate who knew Lenin and Trotsky yet would later say the Black students should stick to their education.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Six Weeks in Parchman
clips.summary: Richardson recounts her first SDS meeting through the Swarthmore Political Action Committee, SPAC, co-chaired by Mimi Feingold and Carl Wittman, learning only decades later that Feingold had done six weeks in Parchman on the Freedom Rides as a CORE staffer before standing up at that meeting, later going into deadly Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: H2O Backwards on My Thigh
clips.summary: She lays out SPAC's three programs, organizing the all-Black cafeteria staff, tutorials in Chester, Pennsylvania, and supporting Gloria Richardson Dandridge in Cambridge, and tells how, as a work-study waitress, she was branded with H2O backwards on her thigh by the uncovered hot water pipe the staff had complained of, an injury Mimi Feingold turned at once toward opening conditions for the strong all-Black women's weight staff.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: I Just Get on the Bus
clips.summary: Richardson describes sampling everything as a freshman away from her mother, signing up for the weekend bus to Cambridge, Maryland that left Saturday and returned Sunday night for Monday classes, joining students from Morgan State and other colleges to picket the Choptank Inn at the Old Barn Grill and the skating rink.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Little White Gloves at the National Office
clips.topic: Gloria Richardson and the Roller Rink
clips.summary: She recounts the dank Old Barn Grill she wondered why she was trying to integrate, and how Gloria Richardson Dandridge and a relative, wearing little white gloves and heels, walked into SNCC's overalls-and-jeans national office seeking help to organize the roller-rink campaign their young people had started, where Reggie Robinson became the first SNCC field secretary Richardson met.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: I Tripped One of Them
clips.summary: Richardson explains that Gloria Richardson was never philosophically or even tactically nonviolent off the picket line, recounting how Gloria tripped a white man she had grown up with who called her names, and how Race Street divided white and Black Cambridge, where the community kept and used guns to defend itself.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: The Camaraderie Was So Amazing
clips.summary: She describes the energy of being part of a group of young Black and white people her age doing something about what she had sublimated all her life in Tarrytown, a town with no Black middle class until Cab Calloway's daughter Chris briefly enrolled, contrasted with the Black-owned businesses she saw in Cambridge.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: You Lift Up to Another Place
clips.topic: Mass Meetings and a Bible Camp
clips.summary: Richardson recalls the freedom songs at Black-church mass meetings lifting her up though she was not religious, her mother who read the Bible but hated organized religion, and the white Baptist Bible camp she quit after overhearing members say they had to be careful not to let too many Black children come in and get overrun.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Go By Ruby Doris
clips.topic: Penny Patch and Taking a Year Off
clips.summary: Richardson tells how Penny Patch, just back from the Albany, Georgia SNCC project, came to her dorm room and urged her to take her sophomore fall off to work full-time with SNCC in Cambridge, warning that as a Northerner she would have to apply and get past Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, the administrator in the Atlanta national office.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Kita Screaming on the Hall Phone
clips.summary: She recounts calling her mother from the dorm hall phone to say she would keep her full scholarship while working with SNCC, her mother amazingly calm, then being called back to find her older sister Carita, who worked in the A&R division at Columbia Records, screaming that she could not sacrifice her education, until Richardson convinced her too.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: My Sister Could Talk to Anybody
clips.summary: Richardson remembers her mother and sister Carita driving her to Swarthmore the first time, her mother sensing the prestige of the rolling hills though she had never seen Bennington, being allowed to drive through the tunnel and changing lanes, and her shy self eased by a sister who could talk to anyone and paved the way in the dorm before her mother returned to an empty house.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: A Princess in a Crown of Roses
clips.topic: The May Queen and a Divided Campus
clips.summary: She describes SPAC running a block-voting slate for the May Queen ceremony that usually went to a fraternity sweetheart, electing Mimi Feingold as May Queen and Richardson as a princess in a crown of roses, her proud mother coming down unaware that fraternity boys were boycotting and that the Cambridge demonstrations had split the campus, with the student paper quoting normally liberal students that the Negro students should concentrate on their education.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Same Campus, Different Organizations
clips.summary: She reflects on how she came into the movement through Cambridge while Mimi Feingold was already CORE from the Freedom Rides and Brooklyn CORE, the two ending up in different organizations, then finished her summer job at a foster-kids halfway house and headed into Cambridge in October to continue the work.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Maid of Honor for a Stranger
clips.summary: Richardson recounts Reggie Robinson inviting her on the train to Cincinnati for the wedding of field secretary Bill Hansen, who worked in Arkansas, and Ruthie, the interracial couple pouring her into a little black dress and heels to stand as maid of honor for people she had never met, with a stop planned at the national office on the way back.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Hey, Captain
clips.summary: She describes expecting an Urban League office with rugs and suits and instead finding a man in overalls sweeping the national office stairs on Raymond Street, greeting Reggie like a long-lost brother, who turned out to be executive secretary James Forman, who swept so everyone would see no job was too lowly for anyone in SNCC.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Ninety Words a Minute
clips.summary: Richardson tells how Forman, learning she could take shorthand and type ninety words a minute, skills forced on her when Washington Irving Junior High shifted its top Black student into the secretarial track, made her his secretary and moved her into the Freedom House, and recalls her first staff meeting in November 1963 just as Kennedy was shot, with Dinky Romilly the one she remembers crying as the staff worked out a strategic response.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Up the Stairs and to the Left
clips.summary: Richardson walks through the Atlanta office, the reception area where staff took turns and Jimmy Bolton worked, Julian Bond as communications director with Mary King his assistant, Bobbi Yancy heading the campus travelers to the Black colleges, and a colleague doing related work with the Black campuses.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Research From a Stone
clips.topic: Jack Minnis and the WATS Line
clips.summary: She introduces Jack Minnis, the research-department head who could pull research from a stone, and the Mississippi chronology of violence and intimidation since 1961 he compiled from WATS-line reports, explaining the person-to-person collect-call trick by which the office learned who was in trouble before calling back free, until white Mississippi operators caught on and cut the calls off.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: An Occupying Army
clips.summary: Richardson traces how Jack Minnis's chronology lesson shaped her later career, building a pattern of police-brutality cases for a Los Angeles film company and as information director of the United Church of Christ Commission for Racial Justice, citing Eleanor Bumpurs and Michael Stewart, and using the pattern in eyes on the prize on the Fred Hampton and Mark Clark assassinations in the Nation of Law episode.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: I Was Trained by the Movement
clips.summary: She recounts the Detroit uprising over a closed blind pig, persuading writer Steve Fayer and producer Orlando Bagwell to keep the narration that police are an occupying army in Malcolm X: Make It Plain, and a New York Newsday reporter, surprised by the chronology in her press packet, asking if she was a reporter, to which she answered that she had been trained by the movement.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: The Movement Bypassed St. Louis
clips.summary: Richardson tells of screening her Orangeburg massacre film at Washington University after Ferguson and meeting young people from the Organization for Black Struggle, who said the movement had bypassed St. Louis and described the fur-coated Black glitterati who appear each King Day, and of urging on them the value of statistics, how many police are charged or go to trial, as evidence that cannot be denied.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Our Own Printing Press
clips.topic: The Student Voice and Friends of SNCC
clips.summary: Richardson describes the printing press in the national-office basement run by Wilson Brown so SNCC never depended on others, the Student Voice mailed to the offices, and the Friends of SNCC network coordinated by Dinky Romilly and then Betty Garman, like Mrs. Shaw in Chicago, who would phone a Mississippi sheriff so white supremacists knew outsiders were watching, the field staff calling in once a day to the national office and twice a day into Jackson by the summer of 1964.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Get Me Your Field Reports
clips.topic: Ruby Doris and the Monthly Reports
clips.summary: Richardson recounts Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's insistence on monthly field reports as the condition for the ten-dollar weekly pay that came to $9.64 after taxes, and Ivanhoe Donaldson's account that while people respected Forman's administrative sense, they knew Ruby truly cared, sending him a letter every day he sat in a Clarksdale jail.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: She Did Not Suffer Fools
clips.topic: Ruby's Presence and a Lean Week
clips.summary: She describes Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's history, Parchman and thirty days no bail in Rock Hill, South Carolina, and her presence akin to Ella Baker, a baby shower for the pregnant Ruby where the SNCC women fell into long silences because they did not do small talk, and a week in Cordele, Georgia when her own $9.64 did not come and she asked an unfriendly NAACP man for credit on a hamburger while playing Dionne Warwick's Walk On By.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: A Bird's-Eye View
clips.summary: Richardson explains how Forman gave her as much responsibility as he thought she could take, a bird's-eye view of the whole organization, taking executive-meeting minutes so she knew who was in trouble, sitting at a tiny desk by his office near Dinky Romilly and the bookkeeper, and writing donor letters she signed with Forman's name.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Prathia Hall Lifted You Up
clips.summary: She describes transcribing a Prathia Hall speech, probably from Selma, off a reel-to-reel onto an IBM Selectric with tears streaming, recalling that even Dr. King disliked following Prathia Hall, who came out of a Pentecostal Black church in Philadelphia, and how Hall got on the case of SCLC field staff who told local people after Bloody Sunday they could not demonstrate unless they were philosophically nonviolent, calling it blackmail.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Singing on the Assembly Line
clips.summary: She describes the workroom assembly line stapling and collating press releases off the mimeograph, everyone singing freedom songs because the work went quicker, and the executive committee meetings of field staff held in Frasier's basement or other spaces when the office was too small, sometimes fed by the host when money was short.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Thirty-Three Pages of Minutes
clips.topic: In Awe of the SNCC People
clips.summary: Richardson recalls her first executive-committee minutes running thirty-three pages because she was in awe of the SNCC people, the Howard NAG members who cited Nietzsche and the Southern young people who knew Black history she did not, typing onto long green stencils corrected with finicky green fluid, then steeling herself to run them off alone late at night in a workroom full of flying water bugs, her diary noting the birds singing as she finished and mailed them to the field.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: I Want to Go to the Field
clips.topic: Who Staffed the Office and Why
clips.summary: Richardson tells how she kept asking Forman to send her to the field in Mississippi while he resisted losing a good secretary, and explains that white staff, especially white women whose presence could get Black men killed, were placed in the national office, with Charles Sherrod's integrated Southwest Georgia staff the deliberate exception.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Birmingham the Day the Girls Died
clips.summary: She describes the national office's photo department with Danny Lyon, and the arrival of Tamio Wakayama, who reached Birmingham the day the four girls were killed in the church bombing, was steered to safety by a Black resident, found Forman, Danny Lyon, and Julian Bond, and drove them to Atlanta before joining the photo department.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: No Minutes Until Freedom Comes
clips.summary: Richardson recounts the staged protest while Forman was away at an anti-apartheid meeting in London, when she, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Mildred Forman, Mary King, and Bobbi Yancy sat with little placards reading no minutes until freedom comes to the Atlanta office, photographed by Danny Lyon when Forman returned.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: No, I Have the Facts
clips.summary: She describes Forman's second edition of his memoir claiming he had suggested the women do the sit-in, her exclamation-pointed disagreement, confronting him alone at a book party, and at the Duke SNCC fortieth anniversary panel, where he said Judy had another interpretation and she replied that she had the facts but he was the one telling the story.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Limitless Within the Agenda
clips.summary: Richardson reflects on feeling limitless because the organization let her act, proposing a residential freedom school around 1965 to join the energy of Southern young people with Northern Friends-of-SNCC youth in Chicago and Cordele, which Ivanhoe Donaldson helped fund and Stokely Carmichael and Charlie Cobb offered to support, no one telling her she had never done it before.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: A Diplomat at the Peachtree Manor
clips.topic: The Toddle House Sit-In and the Cold War
clips.summary: Richardson closes on the Atlanta sit-ins where she was arrested several times, and the day at the Toddle House when Forman learned the Kenyan official Oginga Odinga, serving under Jomo Kenyatta, was staying at the recently integrated Peachtree Manor Hotel, set against the Cold War embarrassment of African diplomats driving Route 40 between New York and Washington unable to eat once past Delaware, a segregation the United States could not afford as it courted newly independent African nations against Russia.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: A Hundred Twenty-Three Acres in Humphreys County
clips.topic: Born in Greenwood, Raised on FSA Land
clips.summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer in Tchula, Mississippi, in March 2013, Julia Matilda Burns recounts being born in Greenwood to Margaret and Trevenu Griffin, and her father moving the family to Humphreys County in the early 1940s to buy roughly 123 acres of his own through the Farm Security Administration, a place with cotton, corn, cane, and livestock that she calls a stepping stone to progress.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Grew Up Overnight
clips.topic: A Mother's Death and an Early Start in School
clips.summary: Burns describes her brother leaving for New York to work for an auto maker and send money home for her education, her mother passing when Burns was thirteen so that she became head of the household as a girl, part farmer and part housekeeper, and how she had started school at four and a half partly to keep her brother out of fights.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Two Teachers, Grades One Through Eight
clips.summary: She remembers a school heated by a pot-bellied stove where two teachers taught all eight grades in one open room, learning from the classes ahead, then the consolidation that created Womack and Hodge, later Womack High School, before students went on to Humphreys County Training School in Louise, Mississippi.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: News Is News
clips.topic: Mississippi Valley and the Brown Decision
clips.summary: Burns recounts finishing high school in 1954 and going on to Mississippi Valley State, and how the Brown v. Board of Education decision that same year did not register with her at the time, with nothing changing in Mississippi schools, no new buildings, materials, or textbooks brought in to make segregation look equal.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: The Picture in Jet
clips.summary: Burns recalls reading about the Emmett Till case in 1955 in Jet magazine, which her parents had delivered to the home, the horrifying picture of the body, her father warning her brother not to whistle at or bother white girls, the Belzoni boys who openly had white girlfriends, and the trial that set the killers free.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Baptist, Methodist, and the Sanctified Church
clips.summary: Burns describes growing up between a Baptist and a Methodist church with a sanctified church further down the road, attending the nearer Baptist church every Sunday though Methodist by faith, getting rides from friends to the sanctified church, and remaining Methodist by her own choice.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: She Saw What I Could Not See in Myself
