clips.summary: Burns credits her tenth-grade English teacher as her major influence, a very smart woman who urged her to go to college and major in English, in an era when teachers visited families and took an interest in children's futures, and recounts telling her father, who agreed to help her become an English teacher.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: I Rode the Bus to College
clips.summary: Burns recounts being the only sibling to go to college at the time, riding the bus three miles from home because there was no money to live on campus, having access to library books she could take home, and her love of Alfred Lord Tennyson and poetry in tune with nature, alongside her reaction to reading Eldridge Cleaver's Soul on Ice.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Teachers for the Delta
clips.summary: Burns describes Mississippi Valley State, built around 1950 with only a few buildings and everything taught in one classroom building, a small student body and an education-focused curriculum, founded by Dr. James Herbert White out of Tennessee with the goal of developing teachers for the Delta, where she graduated in 1959.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Student Teaching and a Marriage
clips.topic: First Job, Marriage, and Moving North
clips.summary: Burns recounts doing her student teaching in Belzoni where she grew up, naming productive former students like Clara Reed, getting her first teaching job and marrying in her second year, then moving with her husband to Chicago, where he worked as a salesman at Wieboldt's and she found the city and the cold not for her.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: One Bale of Cotton
clips.topic: Back to the Farm and Belzoni's Reputation
clips.summary: Burns describes returning from Chicago so her husband could farm his family's Farm Security Administration land, getting loans through the FSA, living off the land and a community grist mill, and Belzoni's reputation as a town so tough that people picked up their goods and hurried out, a place civil rights workers later called dangerous.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: You'll Lose Your Job
clips.summary: Burns recounts coming back to teach at Marshall High School under principal S.B. Marshall, her husband farming while working with SNCC and later the Child Development Group of Mississippi, and a man named John Ball out of Greenwood warning her at the school that she would lose her job if she did not leave the freedom workers alone.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Lemonade and Tea Cakes
clips.summary: Burns describes freedom workers parking behind her house so no one would see, her cooking refreshments for them as they passed, being active behind the scenes while keeping a low key for fear of losing her job, and the arrival of Abe Osheroff to build the Mileston Community Center where the community began to meet.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: You Understand Fear
clips.topic: Henry Lorenzi and the Voter Workshops
clips.summary: Burns recalls the work sessions preparing people to register and vote, the organizers Henry and Sue Lorenzi, Henry telling her that people thought she understood fear, and her husband and others, including Reverend Whitaker and several women teachers, teaching registration classes that helped launch the Child Development Group's Head Start program.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: They Burned the School Down
clips.summary: Burns recounts the 1965 federal court order to desegregate Holmes County schools, her young son being one of about sixteen Black first graders sent to integrate the white Tchula Consolidated School, whites burning down part of the school to scare them off, and the children and parents refusing to leave.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: All the Whites Left
clips.summary: Burns describes her son's classmate Christopher Epps, who later directed corrections, the white students and teachers all leaving once Black children arrived so the school turned entirely Black, and the whites founding the segregated Cruger-Tchula Academy rather than attend integrated schools.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Going to School for the Right Reason
clips.topic: Returning to Teach and College Aspirations
clips.summary: Burns recounts coming back to teach in 1967 after two years out, her students going on to historically Black colleges like Mississippi Valley State and Jackson State, the later arrival of white-college recruiters and Ole Miss admission, and her concern that some students now chase grant money or earn online degrees rather than going to school for a real purpose.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: He Died Before the Election
clips.summary: Burns explains running for the school board because as a parent and teacher she knew education's problems and wanted to fix them, campaigning through churches and communities rather than door to door, winning, and the white incumbent Martin Smith dying the night or two before the election after a Black person opposed him for the first time.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: She Saw Things the Way White Folks Saw Things
clips.topic: Dr. Arenia C. Mallory and Board Issues
clips.summary: Burns recalls serving as one of two Black members on a five-person board with three whites, alongside Dr. Arenia C. Mallory of the Saints Industrial School, who warned Burns against asking for too much for Black folks while Burns carried teachers' and parents' demands to the board, dealing with staffing, funding, buildings, and teacher shortages.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: The Academies Phase Out
clips.topic: White Flight, Durant, and a Black Superintendent
clips.summary: Burns describes the white academies phasing out so white teachers had nowhere to go, a separate white school district set up in Durant for those who could not afford the academies until it too went majority Black, hiring younger white teachers who worked out well, and the first Black superintendent, William Dean, arriving in the late 1980s.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: A Child of Six With Hypertension
clips.topic: The Shimkin Study and Community Health
clips.summary: Burns recounts her role in the research behind Dr. Demitri Shimkin's book The Extended Family in Black Societies, growing out of the Mileston Cooperative and the Holmes County Health Research Program, going house to house and finding widespread hypertension even in a six-year-old child, while serving as secretary and go-between with the University of Illinois.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: We Didn't Know We Were Poor
clips.topic: School Lunches, a Clinic, and Migration
clips.summary: Burns describes recommendations from the study, the Robert F. Kennedy economic development effort, starting breakfast and lunch programs for schoolchildren, developing a health clinic, studying population shift as plantation families moved into city apartments and migrated to Chicago, Peoria, and Wisconsin, and how civil rights workers showed people they were poor.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: I Teach French
clips.topic: Thirty-Five Years and a Job She Loves
clips.summary: Burns closes on her career after leaving the school board in 1976, teaching English and literature at Mississippi Valley and then in the Holmes County public schools, going back to school at Mississippi Valley to learn French after the state mandated a foreign language, and at retirement teaching French three hours a day part time in a job she loves.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: A Janitor's Son on the Main Line
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline of Virginia Tech and the Southern Oral History Program in Washington on December 15, 2015, Lieutenant General Julius Wesley Becton Jr. opens with his birth in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania, where his father was a janitor and the family lived in a basement a block and a half from Bryn Mawr College, and his schooling at Lower Merion High, whose graduates he reels off from James Billington and Alexander Haig to Kobe Bryant.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: In Front of the Mason-Dixon Line
clips.summary: Becton describes the segregation he grew up under in the Ardmore area in the 1920s and 1930s, schools that were not segregated but restaurants and theaters that were, having to sit in the front rows, the swimming program he could not join, and a high school class he led as president alongside a friend he had known since kindergarten.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Silver Wings and Gold Bars
clips.summary: He recounts a half-colonel coming to Lower Merion to recruit for the Army Air Corps with the promise of silver wings and gold bars, half the football team going to take the tests, and six passing and being inducted on December 28, 1943, then flunking the flight-school eye test at Keesler Field because of an astigmatism he had hidden by memorizing the standard chart.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: A Pickup Truck on the Pavement
clips.summary: Becton tells of being sent to Keesler Field while his five buddies went elsewhere, a weekend in Biloxi when a pickup truck chased him and a friend onto the sidewalk, and a return decades later as a lieutenant general flown in on his own aircraft, greeted by the Mississippi adjutant general with a state trooper escort, when he told the assembled Guard how things had changed.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: I Need a Volunteer Who Can Type
clips.summary: He describes the segregated barracks and white officers over Black cadre at McDill Field, becoming a first sergeant's clerk on a wet day because he could type, discovering the sergeant could not read and called the roll from memory, and the lieutenant pointing him toward an Officer Candidate School application board.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Integrated to a Point
clips.topic: Officer Candidate School at Fort Benning
clips.summary: Becton recounts the OCS board president turning out to be from Lower Merion, going to infantry Officer Candidate School at Fort Benning in January 1945, graduating August 16 just after the atomic bombs, and the way the regiment trained and lived fully integrated while a step outside it returned candidates to the segregated South, separate bus lines, theaters, and service clubs.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Why Put Those Two Together
clips.summary: Shipping to the Pacific to join the all-Black 93rd Infantry Division, Becton tells of West Pointers on the voyage who urged him never to attend West Point and taught him to shoot craps, landing on Morotai north of New Guinea where the 93rd and the Confederate-flag-flying Dixie Division fought each other when there were no Japanese to fight, riding patrols to keep the peace, and a postwar patrol into the hills where his platoon was shot at by holdouts and ordered not to engage.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: My Lieutenant Climbs the Pole
clips.summary: With the 93rd deactivating in January 1946 and no points to go home, Becton transfers to a Manila signal unit, a heavy construction company stringing overhead cable, where he learns to climb poles from his soldiers and earns their pride by going up a hundred-foot pole himself to hang wire, something the other lieutenants would not do, though he hated heights.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: An Excellent Pre-Med Program
clips.summary: Separated in 1946 but staying in the reserves as a signal officer, Becton describes a call from his old football coach steering him from Lincoln to Muhlenberg College for its pre-med program in February 1947, being the first Black American to go there on a football scholarship, and dropping the scholarship after deliberately ill-fitted equipment gave him a shoulder separation that ended his playing.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: My Mother Came on the Honeymoon
clips.summary: He recalls meeting a young nurse, Louise, and marrying her on January 29, 1948, the day after a final exam in analytic geometry he failed by memorizing formulas without understanding, a professor giving him a B for prior A work, and a honeymoon driving his brother Joe to college in North Carolina with his mother at the wheel.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: As Long as I'm Here, No Change
clips.summary: Becton describes hearing President Truman's Executive Order 9981 to integrate the military read aloud at Aberdeen Proving Ground in 1948, and the post commander immediately declaring that as long as he was there nothing would change, keeping officer club number one and number two, every one white and every two Black.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: The Army War College Study
clips.topic: Why the Brass Doubted Black Troops
clips.summary: Asked to walk through his feelings, Becton steps back to history: the 369th fighting under the French in World War I and returning jeered rather than greeted, a 1925 Carlisle Barracks Army War College study holding that Black soldiers could do labor but not think through a project or lead, and how that mentality persisted until Benjamin O. Davis Jr. and the 99th Pursuit Squadron began to break it.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: All Deliberate Speed
clips.summary: Becton explains that the order set no timeline, only all deliberate speed like the later Brown decision, and that a lieutenant would not challenge his post commander, then describes going back on active duty to Fort Bliss as a signal officer applying for infantry and a regular Army commission, and being the junior officer running the mess kitchen on a road convoy from El Paso to Fort Bragg.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Get Away From the Counter
clips.summary: He tells of stopping in Van Horn, Texas, where in uniform he was ordered away from a drugstore fountain by a large armed man, leaving without paying, and his white major backing him to the sheriff, then a sedan crashing the next morning and his soldiers, including the sergeant chased off the night before, carrying two injured women to the hospital, the doctors saying the soldiers saved their lives.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: A Sergeant Named Rangel
clips.summary: Becton describes the soldiers' return to Fort Bragg, transferring to the 2nd Division at Fort Lewis on a competitive tour for a regular Army appointment, the 9th Infantry Regiment with one Black battalion and two white, his assignment to the all-Black third battalion of the 9th Infantry, and one of its sergeants, Charles B. Rangel, who would become a congressman.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: They'll Break Up the Color Pattern
clips.summary: He recounts running the regiment's prize drill team, white soldiers asking to join and a lieutenant colonel refusing because they would break up the color pattern, and the unit's hope when the Korean War broke out that the Army would bring back the demanding PhD-holding battalion commander who had trained them rather than the new white commander it installed.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: I Didn't Know Where Korea Was
clips.summary: Becton describes deploying in June 1950 with the 9th Infantry, the first unit to leave the States for Korea, not knowing where Korea was at age twenty, landing at the Pusan Perimeter, his company first ashore, and being sent ahead with a small platoon and communication gear on a separate mission to MacArthur's headquarters to protect an airfield at Pohang.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: They Took Us for the Enemy
clips.summary: He recounts setting up the command post at Pohang while his battalion was ambushed on the same road his patrol had passed safely, the September breakout from the Pusan Perimeter, his platoon leading the company up Hill 201 into heavy fire while the adjacent battalion and the Air Force both mistakenly fired on them, and a messenger weaving marking panels at the planes until the pilots understood, earning a medal.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Home for Christmas
clips.summary: He recounts being wounded for the first time and recovering at Sendai, Japan, rejoining the regiment as it moved into North Korea near the northern border, leading the battalion again with no winter gear in the cold and mountains, a frozen Thanksgiving turkey, and the Chinese entering the war against MacArthur's promise of home by Christmas.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Are You Satisfied Now, Colonel
clips.summary: Becton explains being repeatedly picked to lead because he was on a competitive tour and good at it, and recounts being sent up a hill twice on the word of a collaborating adjacent battalion commander, taking a wound the second time, a bullet passing cleanly between the tendon and bone of his left ankle without damaging either, and asking the colonel if he was satisfied.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: The Army's First Defeat
clips.summary: He describes being evacuated by air just as the Chinese and North Koreans chased U.S. forces out of North Korea in what history calls the Army's first defeat, the battalion and regiment taking severe casualties with only five officers surviving, and returning to the same Japanese hospital he had rushed out of before.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Learning to Ski With Two Straps
clips.topic: Recovery and Command of L Company
clips.summary: Becton tells of being told to rebuild his strength by learning to ski at Sendai with skis strapped to bare boots, returning to the regiment in March and being put straight into L Company, his old company, which had lost all its officers and needed leadership, over the battalion officer's protest that he had just returned.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Put Them Where They're Needed
clips.summary: He recounts Colonel Sloan ordering replacements, who arrived listed by military specialty but not by race, to be put where they were needed, which integrated the 9th Infantry from below, his own first non-Black soldier a Mexican American from Texas whom he told his platoon to protect, and Black company commanders soon leading white battalions, integration driven by the need for bodies, not the executive order.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Keep Doing What You've Been Doing
clips.topic: Two Purple Hearts and a Silver Star
clips.summary: Becton describes a white captain with no combat experience arriving to command his fully integrated company and telling the first lieutenant to keep running it, finishing the war that way, by which time Becton had earned two Purple Hearts and a Silver Star for the Pusan breakout and Hill 201, before rotating home in May.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Don't You Think I Deserve Better
clips.topic: From Camp Edwards to Indiantown Gap
clips.summary: Home and assigned to Camp Edwards to a Black company doing post police and kitchen work for white soldiers, Becton complains to his branch by phone and within an hour is transferred to Indiantown Gap as a basic-training company commander, where he is promoted to captain, integrated into the regular Army, and sent to a signal communications course.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Heavy Foot on the Turnpike
clips.summary: He fills in the births of his first daughters, Shirley born while he was delayed near Fort Monmouth before Fort Bliss and a second daughter born after the family left Fort Lewis for Korea, and recounts his mother flying in to drive the family, his pregnant company commander's wife, and the dog back to Philadelphia, getting her first speeding ticket on the Pennsylvania Turnpike until he warned that the next one would be her last.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: A Mars Station for the Regiment
clips.summary: He describes joining the 2nd Armored Division in Germany as a captain and communications officer for the 42nd Armored Infantry Battalion, building a military amateur radio (Mars) station no one else had and naming it for Combat Command B, commanding his third company, an armored infantry company with personnel carriers, and the birth of his third child at Fort Benning before Germany.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Don't Talk to My Mother
clips.summary: Assigned to ROTC duty at Prairie View A&M with no degree yet, Becton recounts a friend in the Department of the Army sending him there to earn his degree, and friends from Germany routing him to a white family's home in Mississippi with the warning to speak only to the father, not the mother, who would not understand a Black officer at her table.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: My Mother-in-Law Stayed
clips.summary: He recalls his mother and mother-in-law visiting them in Germany, his mother-in-law staying with the family until she died and always taking his side in disagreements to Louise's irritation, the third daughter born at Fort Benning, and a Columbus, Georgia officer so impressed by how composedly Louise adjusted, drove a block, and parked that he passed her on the spot.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: There's No Way You Can Take a Course
clips.summary: Becton describes being the only combat-experienced and only regular Army officer among a lieutenant colonel and three majors at Prairie View, his professor of military science blowing up at the idea of his taking classes while teaching, and the colonel relenting the next morning with the warning that any sign it interfered with his instruction meant trouble.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: The Five Thousandth Graduate
clips.summary: He recounts the Army paying his final-semester costs, a new professor of military science bringing in a commencement speaker Becton had met in Washington, and crossing the stage in 1960 to be announced, conveniently for the Army and the university, as Prairie View A&M's five thousandth graduate, a distinction he found about as likely as a snowball in hell.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Sixteen Out of Six Hundred
clips.summary: Becton describes entering the Army with his diploma and going to the Command and General Staff College at Fort Leavenworth, drawing on relationships built in Germany and at Indiantown Gap, becoming a major there, and being one of sixteen Black officers in a class of six hundred who studied together, all passing except a standoffish lieutenant colonel who failed.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Why Us and Not Germany
clips.summary: He recounts orders to France out of Leavenworth, six classmates sent to the communications zone rather than the real Army in Germany after a commander there complained of not getting his share of talent, his job in operations as chief of plans preparing the evacuation of families across the border, a fourth daughter born at Prairie View, and Louise earning her degree and serving as acting dean of the college nursing program.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: We Don't Have to Carry a Placard
clips.topic: How the Movement Looked From Inside the Army
clips.summary: Asked about the wider world during the Montgomery bus boycott and the marches, Becton explains that he and his peers believed they did more for race relations by excelling in the Army than by demonstrating, and recounts his daughter Karen writing anti-war letters to campus papers in South Dakota until he reminded her who was paying her tuition and she stopped.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Floating Around the Army
clips.topic: Relationships and the Road to General
clips.summary: Becton reflects on the pride that the military pioneered integration, however bumpily, and how Black officers advanced by being good at their jobs and respected, recounting the lasting relationships from Germany, Leavenworth, and France, including a colonel of his French unit who rose to brigadier general and spoke up for him, that carried him toward his first command at Fort Hood as a brigade commander.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Diversity Is Not Affirmative Action
clips.summary: Becton describes a returning phone call from a retired colonel now in Army personnel, and his service on a congressional Military Leadership Diversity Commission, drawing the line that affirmative action counts heads while diversity asks what the whole population does to show everyone is qualified, a distinction he says many in and out of uniform still miss.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Women in Ranger School
clips.summary: He credits the diversity commission with pushing hard to drop the restriction on women in combat jobs, recounts his delight when women entered Ranger School, and tells of his son, a former Old Guard company commander in Washington who resigned his commission because people kept asking after his father, calling during a Rotary dinner with the news.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: He Stuck With the Four Stars
clips.summary: Becton tells of his son preparing the parade ground at the Pentagon when chief of staff Gordon Sullivan and chairman Colin Powell came out to chat, the young captain pressing to get back to work as his exasperated commander glared, and a later dinner where, after his Ranger son phoned that two women had passed Ranger School, a colonel sneered and Becton erupted across the table.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: You Better Talk About Marquita
clips.summary: Becton describes refusing to speak to a fellow resident who slurred the women Rangers until he apologized, his insistence that the Brooks family, who boast of sons promoted to brigadier and four stars, also talk about their lawyer daughter Marquita, Naomi Brooks deciding the family would move in after a luncheon Louise hosted, and Vincent Brooks commanding in the Pacific, whom Becton lent a four-star pin a former boss had once given him.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Every Station Played for Our Leader
clips.topic: France, Kennedy, and the Staff Colleges
clips.summary: Becton recalls being in France when Kennedy was shot and every French radio station playing music fit for the nation's loss, returning in 1963, attending the Armed Forces Staff College in Norfolk to become a joint staff officer, and his daughter, the only Black child there, becoming the first Black student at a just-integrated school, escorted off the bus by the principal.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Find Out Where the Marchers Are
clips.topic: The Selma Crisis on the Army Staff
clips.summary: Assigned in Washington to the deputy chief of staff for personnel handling National Guard and reserve affairs, Becton describes the Selma march and the Army Operations Center, his job to keep communication with the Guard commander on the ground, and pressing a reluctant Guard general by phone for the location of his crew-served weapons by telling him he would report to the chief of staff that the general refused.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: McNamara's Whiz Kids
clips.summary: Becton recounts reuniting in the Pentagon with the West Pointer who once won his money at craps, then being selected with five Army officers for a systems-analysis graduate program at the Institute for Defense Analyses run through the University of Maryland, created to counter the problems McNamara's whiz kids were making for the Army, where he spoke for the military as the senior officer.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: The Highest School for Soldiers
clips.summary: Becton describes a staff group that rewrote what higher-ups disliked, then being selected for the National War College, the third Black Army officer to attend after Roscoe Robinson Jr., the first West Pointer to reach four stars, and a story of Robinson in Hawaii unable to recognize a sweat suit his wife had bleached.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Why Jump Out of an Airplane
clips.summary: He recounts leaving the War College for the 101st Airborne Division at Fort Campbell to command its only cavalry squadron, getting airborne-qualified at jump school despite hating heights, being the only Black commander at that level, deploying to Vietnam as a squadron and then deputy brigade commander, and Creighton Abrams landing his helicopter to visit the only armor unit in his area, before Becton made brigadier general and went to Fort Dix.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Don't Close Fort Dix
clips.summary: As deputy commander at Fort Dix near Philadelphia in charge of training, Becton recounts being sent to Washington to argue against closing the major Northeastern training center, Mayor Frank Rizzo and worried civilians weighing in, the Army telling him to go do his job, and the politicians keeping Fort Dix open to this day.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Becton Gets the Armored Division
clips.summary: Becton describes being promoted to major general while a new boss at Fort Dix wanted to be sole commander, learning he would command the 1st Cavalry Division at Fort Hood as it converted to armor despite his thin armor experience, Creighton Abrams's written note directing the assignment, and his boss writing him the worst officer evaluation he ever received, praising his civic skills and panning his training.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: One of Two Black Division Commanders
clips.summary: Taking the 1st Cavalry Division at Fort Hood, Becton notes he and Harry Brooks of the 25th Division in Hawaii were the only two Black division commanders and stayed in touch, praises his three brigade commanders and a chief of staff now a fellow resident, and describes two years of hard field training and new concepts as the family enjoyed the post.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Armored Divisions Don't Ride Horses
clips.topic: A Change of Command on Horseback
clips.summary: Becton recounts the corps commander, his predecessor and neighbor, planning a mounted change-of-command ceremony on horseback, Becton's reluctance given his limited riding despite his wife having taught him, and the compromise of his standing and marching on foot, an episode he survived before moving on from the division.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Does It Still Work in a Soldier's Hands
clips.topic: The Operational Test and Evaluation Agency
clips.summary: Becton describes moving to the Operational Test and Evaluation Agency at Bailey's Crossroads, the only one who looked like him in the job, a civilian engineer pledging support on condition Becton never overrule his testimony to Congress without warning, and testing the new M1 Abrams tank whose program manager is also a fellow resident.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Held Up Several Months
clips.summary: Promoted to three stars and given VII Corps, the largest combat corps in the Army with the mission of stopping a Russian push across the border, Becton recounts the announcement being delayed several months while the Army quietly told the outgoing commander and German leaders that a Black American was coming, and his being sent to German language school before the change of command.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: You Were Speaking German
clips.topic: A Change of Command in Two Languages
clips.summary: Becton tells of preparing his change-of-command remarks alternating English and German, drawing on his Muhlenberg German and his presidency of a German-American club, delivering fifteen minutes of it to the press and assembled soldiers, and the press surprising him by saying they understood he could speak English, after which he never tried to speak German in public again.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: He Never Said Sir, Always Boss
clips.topic: George Patton IV at VII Corps
clips.summary: Becton recounts his VII Corps deputy George S. Patton IV, the long relationship from Vietnam where Patton resented the attention Abrams gave Becton, through Becton becoming armored branch chief in charge of all armor officers though he had never attended the Fort Knox school, which irritated Patton, who refused to invite a non-armor graduate to address the branch school.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Just Do Your Job
clips.summary: He describes taking command of VII Corps with Patton as a willing deputy, telling Patton only to do his job, and Patton repeatedly asking to send his family home and transfer back to the States, Becton clearing it with his Heidelberg boss General Blanchard, the same officer who had been his student company commander at Fort Benning.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Hundreds of Tanks on Thawing Ground
clips.summary: Becton recounts the Reforger exercise moving forces from the U.S. to fall in on prepositioned equipment in Germany, a February thaw leaving hundreds of tanks tearing up German property, his decision to terminate it cleared with his boss, and Alexander Haig flying in as Supreme Allied Commander to make the announcement, the two reminiscing instead about Haig being expelled from their shared high school for smoking.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: They Played the Cavalry Song
clips.summary: Becton describes recovering the corps and German roads after the canceled exercise, Patton finally being sent home over Becton's relayed request to General Blanchard, and the band at Patton's farewell in Stuttgart playing the Garry Owen cavalry song Patton had resented as a division commander, Patton driving off furious as Becton, helpless, almost died, before Becton finished his tour and came home.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: The Army's First Inspector for Training
clips.summary: Becton recounts coming home to the Training and Doctrine Command at Fort Monroe as deputy commander, the only Black American at that level, his old division chief of staff Jack Merritt rising to four stars, and his becoming the Army's first inspector for training, charged not only with the training the Army conducted but with inspecting how well units in the field performed it, which irritated some.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Industry Wasn't Interested in Soldiers
clips.summary: Becton describes the three-star landscape with Roscoe Robinson going to four stars while he and Art Gray stayed in three-star billets, retiring from the Army in August 1983, and finding that corporations were not interested in what a retired lieutenant general with a master's and ground command could do.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: One Look at Me and He Left
clips.summary: He recounts becoming director of disaster assistance at the Agency for International Development from January 1984 to summer 1985, the famine in Africa his biggest crisis as he coordinated support across agencies, and the South African ambassador, sent to request assistance, taking one look at a Black American in that position and walking out rather than face Becton's view of his country.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: A Soldier With No Political Background
clips.summary: Becton describes contacts made at AID, Vice President Bush and Senator Al Gore among them, speaking on his behalf so he reached FEMA without political support, and reassuring the political appointees who expected anything but a soldier that he was there to do the job and to listen, winning them over so well that three still send Christmas cards.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Sometimes We Said No
clips.summary: He recounts major floods in Northern Virginia his first week, flying with Senator Warner by Guard helicopter to assess the damage before recommending the president approve a disaster declaration, and explaining that FEMA does not dig ditches itself but coordinates and controls the agencies on the ground, sometimes recommending against a declaration on his experts' advice.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Be My Guest, Governor
clips.summary: Becton tells of New Hampshire governor John Sununu's fury that FEMA would not certify security for his nuclear power plant until his staff was satisfied, Sununu demanding a change and threatening to go to the White House to no effect, then becoming Bush's chief of staff and vowing to act against him, Bush protecting Becton with an ambassadorship Sununu would not block, and his wife taking the Caribbean posting off the table, ending his relationship with the White House.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: I Thought You Said a Board
clips.summary: Becton describes a call to help the District of Columbia school system, assuming he was to join the control board that ran the schools but learning they wanted him as superintendent, his wife's two conditions that he not bring his anger home and that they not move from Springfield, Virginia, and his insistence to the control board that he would stay in Virginia.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Dad, We're Tired of the Press
clips.summary: He recounts being the one who went before Congress for school money rather than the jailed mayor, getting along with the mayor until the mayor meddled in his job, and after eighteen months his family holding a caucus, telling him they were tired of seeing his name beaten up in the press, so the practical man reported to the control board and the media that he was going home to be a private citizen.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: What Choice Did I Have
clips.topic: Reflecting on a Life Led First
clips.summary: Asked to reflect on being so often the first or one of very few, Becton credits help along the way and a supportive family that endured constant moves and modest pay, notes he drew no retirement pay while at AID and FEMA under the federal dual-compensation rule, and recounts joining four corporate boards, beginning with Illinois Tool Works at the urging of former Joint Chiefs chairman Jack Vessey.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: He Paid Money
clips.summary: Becton tells of joining Wackenhut at the call of a Marine friend who noted they had never had a Black board member, the Marine Spill Response Corporation through a retired vice admiral he knew from his testing days, and General Dynamics at the urging of Thomas and Jack Merritt, each board a first for a Black American and each, he jokes, accepted because it paid real money.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: In Everybody's Microscope
clips.summary: Becton reflects that he was the first Black American at each corporation and the first Black corps commander and inspector for training, that the families knew they were under a microscope where every misstep was noticed and reported, and that the most important thing was to demonstrate a Black American could get the job done, asking what choice he had but to do the work.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Maintain a Sense of Humor
clips.topic: Recognition and a Philosophy of Command
clips.summary: Becton closes on a Fairfax radio announcement that friends across the country heard, an Army magazine article crediting him with leading the charge at the Pusan Perimeter breakout and Hill 201 though he says he only led his unit, his thirteen-point philosophy of command from his book that singles out maintaining a sense of humor, and his sense of having done all he could for himself, his family, and the country.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: The First Black Band Director in Richmond
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier on the Rutgers campus in Newark on July 20, 2011, Junius Williams opens on his Virginia roots: born in Suffolk, he moved to Richmond at five, the son of two music teachers, his father the first black band director in Richmond and his mother the first coordinator of music for North Virginia schools, in a house full of music with his brother John.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Armstrong or Walker
clips.summary: Williams describes starting school early at his mother's urging, the insulated middle-class world his parents negotiated, and his father steering him from Maggie Walker, where his friends were headed, to Armstrong High School, one of Richmond's two black high schools.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: A Classic, Not a Game
clips.summary: He recalls the Maggie Walker-Armstrong football classic that filled city stadium with thirty thousand people and ordered black social life, playing tennis with his Richmond contemporary Arthur Ashe, and the Baptist church music that surrounded his family at Ebenezer Baptist Church.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: We're Not Going to March in the Back
clips.summary: Williams remembers the picture of Emmett Till's body in Jet magazine igniting anger in him as a boy near Till's age, and a family civil rights victory when his father and Walker's Joe Kennedy refused to put their black bands at the back of Richmond's segregated Tobacco Festival Parade, the black drummers outplaying the white schools when the parade was integrated.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: He Wouldn't Let Me Play the Pickup Bands
clips.summary: Williams explains that his father, a professional musician who knew the precarious life of bands, forbade him the Richmond pickup bands and discouraged a music career, how he applied to Northwestern for its marching band but took Amherst College's full scholarship instead, and how, with Amherst's music program so poor he set his saxophone down, he later took up the harmonica from a movement visitor.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: The Fire Was Still Burning
clips.summary: He recounts Charles Sherrod leading Virginia Union students in sit-ins at downtown Richmond department stores around 1960 and 1961, his parents using the claim that college students barred high schoolers to keep him out of harm's way, and resolving that he had to go south once he reached Amherst in 1961.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: The Color of His Neck Was Red
clips.summary: Williams describes the household conversations from Emmett Till to the Montgomery bus boycott and Brown, and the day Richmond's buses were desegregated, when he and his brother John, warned by their parents to be careful, sat just behind the red-necked driver and drew quiet smiles from the older black riders who had wanted to do it for years.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: BB Guns Across the Ravine
clips.summary: He tells of white boys shooting BB guns across a wooded ravine by his family's Barton Avenue house and the bamboo weapons the black boys made in return, getting shot in the hand trying to make friends, the off-limits white blocks like Brooklyn Park Boulevard, and a Richmond so insulated he had no white friends his own age.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Why Don't You Be a Lawyer
clips.summary: Williams recounts the idea of law stealing into his consciousness at Amherst while he meant to become a SNCC field secretary, his parents pushing law because they saw his civil rights bent, and his early activism: voter registration on spring break and in Springfield, Massachusetts, and a conference he organized in the winter of 1965.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: If Junius White Says He's Okay
clips.summary: He describes the Richmond civil rights lawyer Oliver Hill as a family friend and neighbor who attended Howard with his mother, tells how Hill once called Williams's grandfather Junius White to vouch for his release after a Suffolk arrest on the way to argue a teacher equal-pay case, and his own high-stakes gamble to apply only to Columbia and Yale, getting in.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Pop Williams on the Front Porch
clips.summary: Williams names his father, known to the neighborhood kids as Pop Williams for speaking to everyone from the front porch, as his closest role model, and describes the best-and-worst-of-times shock of arriving at all-male Amherst in fall 1961, where summers among white people had spared him culture shock but there was now no enclave to retreat to.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: There Was a Quota
clips.summary: He recalls the four black students per class at Amherst and the matching quotas at Smith and Mount Holyoke, the dean who recruited him, the strained dance mixers where he was told no more than yes, and being unwilling to attempt interracial dating in that era, while still finding good roommates at Pratt Dormitory.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: The First at Alpha Delta Phi
clips.summary: Williams describes rushing fraternities as a freshman because fraternity houses were the only social life, choosing the exclusive Alpha Delta Phi, which had never had a black member, over Theta Delta Chi, which he felt wanted him only for being black, and largely being welcomed there as the first.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: I Couldn't Catch on to Calculus
clips.summary: He recounts how unprepared Southern schooling left him in calculus and physics, dragging him to a C average that bruised an ego accustomed to being valedictorian, and the math professor Bailey Brown who restored an answer he had erased and gave him an A he then celebrated across campus, only for the C to follow him after a failed final.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: You Got to Do the Class Analysis
clips.summary: Williams describes finding his footing in political science and economics, and how working in Harlem in 1964 alongside Julian Houston and the daughter of Norman Cousins, who understood Marxist analysis he had been taught to reject, sent him to Professor Colston Warne's course, where he first read Marx and gained the lens of class beyond race.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: President of Students for Racial Equality
clips.topic: An Editorial and a Campus Group
clips.summary: He tells how an anonymous Amherst Student article attacking civil rights drew his rebuttal editorial, praised by a once-harsh English teacher as understated emotion, which made his name synonymous with the cause and led to his leading Students for Racial Equality, raising money for jailed activists and meeting Cordell Reagon when the Freedom Singers sang at Smith.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: The Fat Man With the Camera
clips.summary: Williams recounts his first demonstration on a 1963 Richmond picket line with the NAACP youth Commandos, photographed by a government man sent to intimidate them, his voter registration work, meeting Marian Wright Edelman at Amherst, and her steering him to Operation Crossroads Africa, which sent him on scholarship to spend ten weeks in Uganda's Kigezi District.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Off the Plane Into the March
clips.summary: He describes landing in New York from Uganda on the day of the March on Washington, August 1963, going with his brother John, and being most moved by the SNCC singers performing the songs from Danville, having seen a foreign newspaper photo of that summer's violence and regretted being away.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Reform or Revolution
clips.summary: Williams describes convening a large February 1965 conference titled Civil Rights Movement: Reform or Revolution, drawn to the thrilling, much-vilified Malcolm X he had not yet met, and choosing Ossie Davis for his civil rights record and Michael Harrington as the man said to have discovered poverty for the opening session.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Talking to Malcolm X on a Wall Phone
clips.summary: He recounts getting Malcolm X's home number through the network and, on a public wall phone with onlookers, persuading him to come for a thousand-dollar honorarium, then filling out the program with civil rights luminaries and trench organizers from CORE, SNCC, and the Northern Student Movement.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Down to Ossie Davis
clips.summary: Williams tells how Malcolm X was stranded abroad over a visa and Michael Harrington stopped by an ice storm, leaving only Ossie Davis, who took the stage at Mount Holyoke before a thousand people, voiced the absent speakers' roles plus his own, won a standing ovation, and gave the conference legitimacy, with Williams concluding he was becoming a revolutionary.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Would They Have Killed Him Here
clips.summary: Williams recalls Malcolm X being killed at the Audubon Ballroom the week after the conference, wondering whether someone might have tried it in the lonely hills around South Hadley, and his reaction of anger, sadness, and regret at having had only one phone conversation with a man whose voice and honesty he had liked.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: It Was Time to Go South
clips.summary: He describes how his small group, after the conference, decided in March 1965 to join the Selma to Montgomery march, feeling they could not be revolutionaries without front-line experience, and piling about seven students from UMass, Mount Holyoke, Smith, and Amherst into a Volkswagen bus.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Take My Station Wagon
clips.summary: Williams recounts stopping at his parents' Richmond house, where his father swapped the troubled Volkswagen for his big Buick station wagon, the two black riders hiding under clothes when a South Carolina cop stopped them and quick-thinking Fred Arano claimed they were bound for a Florida spring fling, and a semi-trailer collision in Georgia that a friendly gas station fixed.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: We Need You Here in Montgomery
clips.summary: He describes reaching the Montgomery SNCC office at Jackson and High Street, where Stokely Carmichael redirected them into SNCC's parallel operation born of distrust of King and SCLC, and what Williams later pieced together: that Jimmie Lee Jackson's killing and John Lewis's beaten march predated SCLC's involvement, which came only after Johnson pressed a judge to lift the injunction.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: This May Be the Last Time
clips.summary: Williams recounts SNCC commanding Montgomery so the police had to bottle the demonstrators at Jackson and High Street, where mounted men with long cattle sticks, rumored deputized Klansmen, beat them back each time they marched, until one charge halted as the trapped crowd sang at a church that never opened its door, the horses turning away in a moment that revised his thinking about God.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: He Stopped Us on One Leg
clips.summary: He describes watching Lyndon Johnson say we shall overcome on television while violence raged in the street and judging him a hypocrite, and the Puerto Rican demonstrator who, after a motorcycle cop knelt to taunt then struck him, leapt up on his injured leg to stop the crowd from seizing the scattered officer, sparing them from being branded terrorists.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: King Marched Us Two Blocks
clips.summary: Williams recounts King coming over as the march drew press, the barricades opening only for King to lead them two blocks and turn back while cops grinned and threw peanuts, and the SNCC organizers then leading them out a back way to empty Alabama State University into the streets, the crowd now mostly black, until a young woman was run down by a horse.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Brother, They Got Too Many Guns
clips.topic: Jumping on the Man With the Brick
clips.summary: He tells of jumping on the back of a lineman-sized man who picked up a brick after a horse ran down a young woman he called his sister, talking him out of throwing it over a block until she rose unhurt, an episode Williams turned into a rap his singing group performed, before the marchers sat down downtown and were arrested.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Unless Everybody Had a Blanket
clips.summary: Williams describes being jailed in a Kilby Prison bullpen of seventy-five men with too few dusty blankets the biggest men seized, until the small, bespectacled organizer Worth Long stood on a table and declared he would take no blanket unless everyone had one, shaming the men into sharing and, when the jailers claimed there were no more, winning more blankets through collective refusal.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: My First Leadership Role
clips.summary: He recounts that after Worth Long was put in solitary for trying to integrate the church service, he and the Detroit SNCC member Stu House led the cell for seven days, keeping morale up with freedom songs and refereeing fights over food, and how, money raised in Massachusetts to bail him out forcing his early release, he reluctantly left for the staging ground outside town.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: I Wanted to Be Just Like Him
clips.topic: Ivanhoe Donaldson and the Rear Jeep
clips.summary: Williams describes joining the rainy staging ground with its stars Harry Belafonte and Nina Simone as one of the celebrated Kilby Prison releasees, admiring the calm, focused logistics man Ivanhoe Donaldson in his yellow rain slicker, and riding in the very last first-aid jeep of the march into Montgomery, exposed to a feared shot from behind.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: He Calmed the Waters
clips.summary: In a reflective aside Williams speaks of Worth Long as an ethnomusicologist who sought out old blues singers and made a film about them, too sick to attend the fiftieth anniversary, recalling him as a man he wanted to be like for calming the waters, and mentions Posey Lombard, a white artist from Smith who went to work in Georgia for the movement.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Nobody Told Us, We Just Evolved
clips.summary: Williams reflects on how he and Stu House simply evolved into leading the seventy-five men, crediting his experience running a college civil rights group and Stu's time with SNCC, and recounts hearing on the radio in his father's station wagon, driven by Posey, that Viola Liuzzo had been killed, picking up a screwdriver and vowing his would not be a nonviolent confrontation.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: The Civil Rights Movement Is Over
clips.summary: He recounts Tom Hayden seeking him out in the summer of 1965, telling him the civil rights movement was over with the voting rights law in hand and recruiting him to Newark on the strength of his conference and Alabama work, and Williams deciding he could go to Newark and start law school at Yale, making Newark his home if he liked it.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: The Movement Had Already Come North
clips.summary: Williams explains that the movement had come north through efforts like his Harlem playground work, that SDS and its ERAP projects came to Newark on the theory that class mattered more than race, and how Hayden's SDS group split from the older white Jewish liberals of the Clinton Hill Neighborhood Corporation and moved down into the poorer, mostly black lower Clinton Hill.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: It Was the Working Poor
clips.summary: He describes finding, as Betty Maul explained, that Newark's people had jobs that were merely bad and underpaid, the dawning era of the working poor, while what stirred them was police brutality, gouging landlords, and store credit bait-and-switch, the issues the Newark Community Union Project took on as Williams set out to be an organizer on a frontier SNCC could not match.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Let the People Decide
clips.topic: A Coalition of Radicals and Liberals
clips.summary: Williams describes the wider Newark coalition against the forces keeping people down: CORE, the group Anvil, black labor unions, and preachers outside Mayor Hugh Addonizio's reach, including white rabbis dragged through the mud at the Barringer High School jobs protest, with the NCUP students as the radicals insisting the people decide in meetings that ran all night, led in fact by Tom Hayden alongside Terry Jefferson, Bessie and Thurmond Smith.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Let CORE Deal With That
clips.summary: He recounts that some NCUP organizers found police brutality too volatile because it was nakedly racial, white cops beating black heads, and left it to CORE's weekly demonstrations, and how his very first day in Newark he joined a CORE protest over a man shot by an officer named Hank Martinez, who years later won a City Council seat and became his friend.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Race Was a Bigger Issue Than They Could Deal With
clips.summary: Williams reflects that within weeks he saw race would overwhelm what the white SDS students could handle, that he and Phil were the two black college organizers, and how SNCC's 1966 turn telling whites to organize whites, Carmichael replacing Lewis at a tumultuous meeting, and the coming purge reflected a wider conviction, voiced by Malcolm X and the nationalists, that race was predominant as the integrationists, even King, lost ground.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: I Can Work With You, But Not Her
clips.topic: Organizing a Block for a Stoplight
clips.summary: He describes a SNCC office opening in Newark's Central Ward beside the NCUP hangout Hayden cultivated, taking the black power line under Phil, and Williams being assigned with Corinna to break open a new Central Ward block over the residents' demand for a stoplight, where a man named Billy who knew the Nation of Islam line would work with him but not with his white partner, testing his commitment to an integrated, class-based movement.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Just a Grunt They Still Watched
clips.summary: Williams recounts the evidence he was under surveillance though only a low-level organizer: a 1968 HUAC proceeding where a policeman produced a private letter to Hayden and accused him of living with the SNCC hell-raiser Phil Hutchings, an FBI search of his suitcase after a California trip to see his sister, a Paris automat reunion with a young woman named Jackie he had met in Russia who pressed him about his itinerary and hurried away, and informers' reports he later found in Governor Hughes's files in Trenton.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Up Against the Car
clips.summary: Williams recounts returning from a Philadelphia black power conference as the 1967 Newark Rebellion erupted, slipping into the sealed city by the streets he knew, and being pulled over after curfew with three other men by police with drawn pistols and shotguns, saved when a sergeant saw the law books he had left in the car and overruled officers who wanted to shoot them.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Then the State Police Came In
clips.summary: He describes laying low with Phil at a basement apartment the HUAC report misaddressed, watching people glad to turn the tide, and how things worsened when the state police and National Guard arrived and the police rioted, so that his team of law student VISTAs investigating urban renewal relocation turned instead to interviewing witnesses to the police violence.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Shot Forty-Six Times
clips.summary: Williams recounts a witness account of state police systematically shooting a young looter dozens of times, including in the top of the head, the information he turned over through the Legal Services Project to a grand jury that treated him as the suspect, Amiri Baraka's flyer of the destroyed corpse, and his retort that what was inflammatory was the man being shot forty-six times.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: After the Rebellion, Everybody Wanted to Talk
clips.summary: He describes the homeowner-organizer Louise Epperson, fighting the proposed College of Medicine and Dentistry that would take two hundred acres, finding that after the Newark Rebellion the power structure suddenly listened, and Williams resolving to capture that energy and the image of the black man with the brick to force lasting change.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Nineteen Acres by Going Up
clips.topic: A Smaller Footprint for the Medical School
clips.summary: Williams recounts Amiri Baraka rising to prominence after being beaten in the rebellion and keynoting the black power conference, while Williams and others, after the community lost an earlier lawsuit over the medical school, formed the Newark Area Planning Association and asked Yale planners for the smallest footprint, who offered nineteen acres by building up against the proposed clearance of well over a hundred sixty.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: One Percent Vacancy
clips.topic: The Lawsuit and the United Brothers
clips.summary: He describes his cold candy-store office, bringing in the NAACP Legal Defense Fund under Jack Greenberg to file a housing-insufficiency complaint to HUD and HEW built on his VISTAs' research showing a one percent vacancy rate, and Amiri Baraka asking him in December 1967 to join the United Brothers, formed to unite the community and elect Newark's first black mayor.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: A Nine-Person Negotiating Team
clips.summary: Williams recounts a coalition of NAPA and Louise Epperson's Committee Against Negro and Puerto Rican Removal forcing the state to the table, holding to a nine-person negotiating team backed by two hundred supporters who shut down would-be speakers, and negotiating with officials including Paul Ylvisaker and Ralph Dungan the medical school agreements that yielded sixty acres for community housing.
keywords: Committee Against Negro and Puerto Rican Removal

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: That Invisible Brother With the Brick
clips.summary: He describes winning in the agreements a construction-trades training program for blacks and Latinos, the throwing out of the mayor's Model Cities committee and his own election to its replacement, and a multi-stakeholder body of unions, contractors, and the community to monitor compliance, all backed by the unspoken memory of the man with the brick.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: May the Best Man Win, and We Hope It's Not Williams
clips.summary: Williams recounts becoming Kenneth Gibson's first campaign manager in 1970, the in-community fight over who would get the sixty acres while white onlookers hoped Williams would lose, the housing council that decided the land and produced a thousand units, and the construction-trades corporation that won hundreds of young men union books with backing from Gus Henningburg and others.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Twenty-Six and Making Twenty-Six Thousand
clips.summary: He describes being put in charge of planning most of the federal money coming to Newark at age twenty-six, the friction his salary caused, the street momentum that came from the civil rights and black power groups rather than the Democratic Party, and his distaste, shared by many, for the cultural nationalism of Amiri Baraka's Committee for a Unified Newark that helped elect Gibson.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: They Said I Stole the Money
clips.summary: Williams recounts Gibson, wanting neither him nor Baraka in leadership, eventually firing him over a year-old audit interpretation dispute revived by Congressman Joseph Minish, who implied on the front page of the Star-Ledger that Williams had stolen three hundred thousand dollars, even as Williams had doubled the city's funds and brought in the fifteen-million-dollar Project Rehab.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Bring Me Down What You Have
clips.summary: He describes turning the story around by persuading Star-Ledger editor Mort Pye to run his response on the Wednesday front page, a television host declaring him a good guy to applause, and forcing Congressman Minish into a tie at a hearing, where Williams, a lawyer who knew the material, defended himself though his entire staff stayed away.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: There Is Nothing There
clips.topic: Fired, and Vindicated by an Auditor
clips.summary: Williams recounts Gibson giving him thirty days then firing him, a rally of four hundred where Raymond Brown asked why the mayor was absent and trying to get rid of Williams, and an auditor approaching him later at an airport to reveal he had told the prosecutor there was nothing to indict him on, while Baraka, similarly thwarted by Gibson, abandoned cultural nationalism to become a Marxist.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Plan My Convention
clips.topic: Rising in the National Bar Association
clips.summary: He describes leaving City Hall for law practice and being drawn by O.T. Wells into the National Bar Association, the black analog to the ABA, organizing Wells's 1973 San Francisco convention on his own dime after a Los Angeles fall-through, and building from that the network that became his organizational base.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: A Coalition of Young and Women Lawyers
clips.summary: Williams recounts reviving the Young Lawyers Division as a vehicle, partnering with the newly formed women lawyers division steeped in movement-era democracy against an imperial old guard, and parlaying that coalition through the vice-presidential offices and delegation trade-offs until he ran unopposed for president-elect.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Adopted by the United Nations
clips.summary: Williams describes becoming the youngest president of the National Bar Association at thirty-four in 1978 and introducing the cause of southern African independence, his international affairs committee under Julius Chambers producing a paper exposing the sham of white-controlled constitutions in southern Rhodesia, which a SNCC friend helped carry to the United Nations, where it was adopted as an official document on Zimbabwean independence and translated into seven languages.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Hold On to Sanctions
clips.topic: From the OAU to Jimmy Carter
clips.summary: He recounts the paper opening doors among African diplomats and at the OAU, invitations to Jamaica and Cuba, a thwarted plan to bring black professionals to a newly independent Zimbabwe, and the crowning report that his White House contacts got the document to Jimmy Carter to help hold sanctions, followed by a 1979 Los Angeles convention where Louis Farrakhan, whom he had met in New Haven, mesmerized the lawyers, and a semester at Harvard's Institute of Politics.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Power in the Suite, Not the Street
clips.summary: Williams describes returning from Harvard to find Gibson twelve years in office with little changed, having divorced the power of the people from the institutions, and resolving to build another movement and run for mayor in 1982, a race he lost for lack of money, concluding that being all things to all people to raise funds was not for him, before serving as town attorney in Irvington under Mayor Sara Bost.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Thorough and Efficient
clips.topic: Abbott v. Burke and Organizing Parents
clips.summary: He recounts the Abbott v. Burke school-funding case, brought by the Education Law Center in 1981 under the state constitution's thorough and efficient clause and upheld so cities must get suburban-level funding no governor has fully granted, and Williams's Rutgers-based effort, funded by foundations, to teach parents and young people how to get organized rather than building one more organization.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Your Voice Deserves to Be Heard
clips.summary: Williams closes on teaching parents to use information and strategy and young people that their voices, once amplified through media skills they learn over a summer program, deserve to be heard, lamenting that the concept of organization was lost when the movement put its eggs in electing black politicians, and pointing to his coming book, Unfinished Agenda: Urban Politics in the Era of Black Power.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: 1954 as a Turning Point
clips.summary: Interviewed in Atlanta on September 16, 2011 by Joe Mosnier, with Henry Louis Gates auditing, Kathleen Neal Cleaver opens by naming 1954 as a turning point in her own life at age nine and in the world: the defeat of French imperialism in Vietnam and the Supreme Court's Brown decision throwing out separate but equal.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Horror to the Segregationists
clips.summary: She describes how the black community read Brown as making segregation unconstitutional, sparking spontaneous protests at libraries, pools, and buses, while it inspired viciousness in places like Mississippi and the murder of Emmett Till, and recalls her parents' civil rights activity at Tuskegee Institute before her father took a foreign aid post.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Point Four and a Childhood in India
clips.summary: Cleaver explains her father was hired as a technical director under Truman's Point Four community development program in India, chosen for his rural farm work in Alabama, and how moving from a segregationist Alabama into newly independent India, the land of Nehru and Gandhi, showed her that dark-skinned people could govern themselves and blew apart white supremacist propaganda.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Growing Up Against Colonialism
clips.topic: The Philippines and a Quaker School
clips.summary: She recounts her father's transfer to the formerly colonized Philippines, growing up amid unfurling anti-colonial movements in Africa and Asia with the elimination of segregation and colonialism as her childhood norms, and coming back to attend a Society of Friends boarding school, the George School, in 1961, the same school Julian Bond had attended.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Girls in the Paddy Wagon
clips.summary: As she neared graduation in May 1963 with Birmingham erupting and SNCC students jailed in Albany, Cleaver saw a Philadelphia newspaper photo of high school girls singing in the back of a paddy wagon being carted off to jail, wanted to be like them, posted a note proposing a dining-hall fast in support, and was rebuked by a classmate who urged a more cooperative solution.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Learning to Defend Nonviolence
clips.topic: Fellowship House and an Assembly Speech
clips.summary: Wanting to defend the students, Cleaver went to Fellowship House in Philadelphia seeking SNCC activist Prathia Hall, found her gone to Georgia, and got a resident artist to explain nonviolence as transformation through love and refusal to retaliate, then wrote and delivered a speech at her school's Thursday assembly defending the protesters and asking that they at least be respected.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Four Black Students in 400
clips.summary: She describes the racial dynamics of a 400-student school with only four black students, how her speech upset the white audience of students and teachers who called her a radical, the follow-up discussions she organized, and her thwarted wish that summer of 1963 to attend the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.
related_events: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Their Caution, Their History
clips.summary: Cleaver reflects that her parents barred her from the March citing predicted violence and her father's caution as a foreign service officer, yet both had been activists: her father in the 1930s effort to desegregate the Texas white primary, her mother in the Southern Negro Youth Congress and a beneficiary of a Virginia consent decree paying her graduate tuition at Michigan, before she went off to Oberlin College.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Hotbed at Oberlin
clips.summary: At Oberlin she found an active black-and-white civil rights group, radical faculty, and speakers describing protests like the one in Danville, Virginia, and lived through the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing and President Kennedy's assassination, with friends fearing what the struggle would become under Lyndon Johnson, whom young people never expected to back civil rights.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Reports in Triplicate
clips.summary: Feeling college was not what she had imagined, Cleaver quit and took Washington government jobs, moving from the Peace Corps to the new Community Relations Service set up under the 1964 Civil Rights Act, where she typed in triplicate the reports field teams filed after conflicts like the Cleveland riots; she also recalls Malcolm X's February murder and a night her parents took her to a Baltimore mosque advertising a Malcolm X and James Baldwin debate, where the Muslims at the door nearly refused her as white and it proved to be only a taped film.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Circle of Future Leaders
clips.summary: Cleaver describes her year at the Community Relations Service under deputy director Roger Wilkins, a civil rights attorney who knew her father from his post in Sierra Leone, and reconnecting with James Thomas, a Crossroads Africa student turned law student who had once invited her as a high schooler to his first trial at Yale Law School, where she met fellow students Marian Wright Edelman and Eleanor Holmes Norton, an inspiring circle in which no one close to her opposed the movement.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Meeting George Ware
clips.summary: Wanting New York, Cleaver enrolled at Barnard but quickly found the classes dull, and that summer met George Ware of SNCC, who had come out of the Tuskegee student movement and led a group called TIAL active in the Selma and Tuskegee marches, introduced through a graduate student she was dating whose mother had taught in Tuskegee and knew her mother.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Too Collaborative for George
clips.summary: In a transformative first conversation, George Ware told Cleaver how the Tuskegee Civic Association in 88-percent-black Macon County ran only two candidates rather than contest an all-black council, which he found horrible, and how TIAL deliberately integrated white churches to provoke the armed parents and break up an overly collaborative approach; Ware, a brilliant chemist from rural Alabama who talked only of activism, became her mentor as she enrolled at Barnard while he took a chemistry job at Hoffmann-La Roche and her boyfriend Ernest worked in a New York lab near the Columbia campus.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Job at SNCC
clips.summary: Through Ivanhoe Donaldson, a 1964 Mississippi summer project director she met at her friends' apartment, Cleaver got a thrilling 1966 summer secretary job at the New York SNCC office, a fundraising office where Southern activists came for rest, dental work, or because they had burned out, arriving just about two weeks after the call for black power in Greenwood, Mississippi.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Asleep a Chairman, Awake Not
clips.summary: Cleaver recounts that white staff in leadership had been asked to leave SNCC, the New York director Elizabeth Sutherland being replaced by Ivanhoe Donaldson amid the turmoil as black power supplanted freedom now after the Voting Rights Act, and how a second vote at a midnight 1966 staff meeting replaced John Lewis as chairman with Stokely Carmichael, so Lewis went to sleep chairman and woke to find he was not.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Shot in the Back
clips.topic: The Murder of Sammy Younge Jr.
clips.summary: Cleaver tells of Sammy Younge Jr., a Navy veteran she knew from childhood who returned to Tuskegee and became an extraordinarily active organizer working with George Ware and others on an independent black party in Lowndes County, and who in January was shot in the back and murdered at a gas station by attendant Marvin Segrest while registering black voters, a death devastating to SNCC.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Julian Bond and the War
clips.summary: She recounts how Younge's murder led George Ware to quit his chemistry job and return to the movement to run the Atlanta campus program, and how, because Younge was a veteran, SNCC took a position against the Vietnam War that Julian Bond endorsed, leading Georgia to deny Bond his elected legislative seat in a fight that reached the Supreme Court.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: SNCC in Crisis
clips.summary: At the end of January 1967 Cleaver moved to Atlanta to work for Ware, finding SNCC financially broke with projects eliminated and direct action giving way to black power, and recalls the SNCC newsletter, researched by Jack Minnis and written by Ethel Minor, that criticized Israeli warfare techniques and the cluster bombs that looked like toys, which horrified Jewish donors during the 1967 war.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Three Days Taking Notes
clips.topic: Forman, and Trading Tuition for SNCC
clips.summary: Cleaver describes a 14-hour meeting where she did nothing but take notes, the report that became Black Power: The Politics of Liberation, and her deep admiration for James Forman, whom she remembered from a riveting Jet Magazine photo facing a Mississippi sheriff toe to toe, and tells how she wrote her father that SNCC was a better education than Barnard and asked for the tuition money instead, which funded the rent on a single Atlanta apartment shared by the whole campus program as an unpaid cadre that hitchhiked to work for lack of gas money.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Conference of Black Students
clips.summary: Cleaver recounts organizing a SNCC conference called Liberation Will Come From a Black Thing to bring radical black student groups together at Fisk University, capturing the new black-student consciousness then emerging, and sending invitations to speakers including LeRoi Jones, Margaret Walker, Charles V. Hamilton, and Eldridge Cleaver, who had published in Ramparts.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Stark, Raving Mad
clips.summary: She describes the Nashville press going wild because the SNCC chairman was reviled and threatened with deportation as a native of Trinidad, the Vanderbilt Impact symposium controversy over inviting him alongside Strom Thurmond and others, and Fisk deciding to kick the conference off campus, leaving the organizers with arriving students, no rooms, and an Easter-weekend blizzard shutting the East Coast airports.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Giant in the Doorway
