clips.summary: With speakers stranded by the blizzard, only Eldridge Cleaver made it from California and a local Episcopal pastor, Father Woodruff, offered his church off campus, saving the conference; Cleaver recalls retyping the agenda when this giant out of prison about three months was brought in, masklike and almost intimidating, asking her for a room and a typewriter to get her attention, how she beat him at chess back in Atlanta and the two fell in love, and the huge Nashville riot that erupted around the Easter conference as police drilled in riot control began rioting before Carmichael even arrived.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: All the Preparation for a Revolutionary
clips.summary: She reflects that though she would not have called herself a revolutionary as a Barnard sophomore, she already had all the preparation, needing only a few more pieces of experience, which came when she met Eldridge Cleaver, a Marxist who kept that to himself and held a more radical revolutionary model than SNCC, on parole and confined by his officer to San Francisco's city limits.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Self-Selected Radical Crowd
clips.summary: Cleaver recalls the amazing collection of radical student groups that did reach the conference, including the Cornell black student group later famous for the armed Straight Hall photographs and groups from Berkeley and Roosevelt University, and frames SNCC as a debating society, ideological institute, family, and activist group in transition toward a revolutionary movement it could not sustain as an intact organization.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Integration Is Disintegration
clips.summary: She lays out the first black power argument she absorbed, from Carmichael's Black Power article, that integration deals only with color and not the black community's real problem, the absence of power and institutions, and recounts going out in July to visit Eldridge Cleaver, joking they would send Willie Ricks to retrieve her, while he, forbidden on parole to associate with armed people, drove her to Oakland to meet the Black Panthers in the summer of love of 1967.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: From SNCC to Liberation
clips.topic: A Movement Gravitates to the Panthers
clips.summary: She traces how black power funneled SNCC veterans into the black liberation movement of the late sixties, naming early Black Panther leaders who had been in SNCC, and recounts Eldridge Cleaver's arrest after an Oakland police shootout in which one Panther was killed, his parole revoked, and his unbelievable June 1968 release on a habeas petition, his nomination as the Peace and Freedom Party candidate, and the publication of Soul on Ice.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: We Could See the War
clips.summary: Cleaver describes how the Black Panther world outlook was shaped by a Vietnam War they could watch on television, taken as evidence of a reckless imperial United States, and how the Vietnamese proved an organized people could defeat a richer power, citing the reported remark of General Giap that the Americans won every battle and it did not matter.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Ending Segregation Was Not Integration
clips.summary: Returning after a break, Cleaver argues the movement has been taught wrong, that ending school segregation was about stopping oppressive exclusion rather than merging separate societies, that integration was a government idea not from the black community, and that the real story was a groundswell of unnamed local people after the Montgomery bus boycott she defines as community revolution.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: That Mess Becomes the Movement
clips.topic: How Communities Turned to the Struggle
clips.summary: Cleaver criticizes the academic framing that treats the movement as good until King died and bad afterward because black power was cast as anti-white, recalls that in the late fifties many called the movement that mess, and contrasts community structures thick with churches and colleges against Oakland's loosely organized openness, which drew young people who flocked into the Black Panther Party because being a Panther became the thing to do.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Civil Rights and Black Power Are One
clips.summary: She insists treating civil rights as separate from black power is a mistake when the shared issue was how excluded people empower themselves, surveys the spectrum from SNCC to SCLC to the NAACP, tells how Fred Hampton had led an NAACP youth council and as an elementary student already fed hungry classmates breakfast years before the Panthers' free breakfast program, and describes a world repudiating colonialism with successful revolutions in China and Cuba as proof ordinary people could change their lives, the FBI-planted black propaganda demonizing the Panthers, and the founding of the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense in Oakland in October 1966, named from the Lowndes County Freedom Organization's panther logo.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Panthers Across the Country
clips.summary: She explains how the panther name passed from Alabama to Oakland and became a symbol of black power, with groups forming in urban areas nationwide, including a Kansas City group under Pete O'Neal that split off as the Sons of Malcolm, alongside the Nation of Islam, black elected officials, and white SDS and anti-war activists in a general ferment.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Finding a Counter
clips.summary: Cleaver argues the state used its posture toward genuine communist enemies to paint the radical Panthers, sending James Farmer of CORE to Africa to counter Malcolm X's alignments there, and recounts setting up an international section of the Black Panther Party in Algeria with Eldridge Cleaver, meeting the African and Arab revolutionaries the intelligence agencies most feared they would ally with.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: The Rise of a Black Messiah
clips.summary: She describes the FBI's lists of key extremists and a counterintelligence program against every form of activism, the domestic COINTELPRO begun around 1967 whose objective was to prevent the rise of a black messiah who could unify the black masses, and its early targets: Max Stanford of the Revolutionary Action Movement, Stokely Carmichael, and above all Martin Luther King with his Nobel Prize and worldwide following.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Scare You or Kill You
clips.topic: Bobby Seale and a Frightened State
clips.summary: Cleaver explains the Panthers were not initially on the FBI's list partly because the party was collective, describes the attempt that wounded Bobby Seale in a shootout that left a policeman dead and his resulting murder charge, recounts proposing courthouse demonstrations, starting with about eight people, that grew into an international movement, and reflects that what frightened the government was encountering armed, articulate black people who told authorities what they would not do, an experience drawn from colonial warfare that they faced knowing they risked their lives.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Twentieth-Century Set of Demands
clips.topic: A Young Spokeswoman and the Ten Point Platform
clips.summary: Responding to the interviewer's framing of her young self as part of the apparatus explaining the party amid rapid ideological shifts and law-enforcement pressure in Oakland, Cleaver notes she was actually older than most high-school-age Panthers, then describes how the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense started with a tiny Oakland group but addressed the whole black community, its Ten Point Platform essentially reform analogous to the Atlanta student movement's demands and the colored people's conventions after the Civil War, a twentieth-century version of an oppressed community's hopes in new words.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: I Wouldn't Let Anybody Beat Me
clips.summary: Cleaver recounts that what drove many older activists into the movement was Emmett Till's 1955 murder, while the Panthers drew slightly older youth, many children and grandchildren of Southern migrants in big cities for whom televised beatings in Birmingham were traumatizing rather than mere news, citing Emory Douglas's resolve that no one would beat him like that, which the Panthers' self-defense stance answered.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Generation That Believed It Was Immortal
clips.summary: She characterizes the Panthers as a collective youth movement whose members believed themselves immortal and were undeterred by the real danger of being killed, backed by adults who would not join but would help, and recounts a story from Assata Shakur's autobiography of a friend who sheltered her without surprise as her picture circulated for a bank robbery, illustrating people who quietly helped.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Democracy for the White, Tyranny for the Black
clips.summary: Cleaver describes the flip side, people pressed to inform on activists to alienate their support, the surveillance reaching even her son, whose name she found in the Mississippi sovereignty commission records though she doubts he was ever there, and likens the United States to a South African sociologist's description of a society with democracy for the white and tyranny for the black.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: The Distance of Forty Years
clips.summary: Asked how forty years of distance has changed the telling, Cleaver considers how the immediacy and ugliness of what they confronted reads differently now, reflects on the Black Power Mixtape film and why these images resonate, recalls a 1970 Black Panther newspaper issue on fascism filled with page after page of arrests, citing Mumia Abu-Jamal jailed as a teenager for jaywalking, and frames the everyday police raids, bombed offices, and FBI hostility as low-intensity counterinsurgency warfare targeted at people who understood themselves as revolutionaries accepting the risk of war.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: A Different Country We Won't Be
clips.summary: Cleaver closes on how the intensity has diminished because so many powerful, brilliant figures of that time, from singer Miriam Makeba to Stokely Carmichael, have passed, imagining how different the country would be had Malcolm X, Martin Luther King, and Fred Hampton not been killed and their organizations flourished, and noting that the Black Power Mixtape appeals because it shows young, charming, upbeat people who still made room to live and party.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Born in Paducah, Raised in Metropolis
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Louisville in August 2013, Kay Tillow recounts being born in Paducah, Kentucky in 1942 and raised across the river in Metropolis, Illinois, a town of six thousand, in a family of modest means descended from German immigrants who each held eighty-acre farms, her mother a teacher trained at Southern Illinois and her father a furniture-store owner.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: The Wall Between
clips.summary: Tillow describes a Presbyterian minister, Maynard Elftman, giving her Anne Braden's book The Wall Between in high school, an eye-opening revelation from a family that talked morals and education but never politics, and how the book told of the Bradens buying a Louisville home for a Black family, the bombing that followed, and the sedition charge of trying to overthrow Kentucky.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: We Weren't Ready for That
clips.summary: She recalls the integration of the grade schools in Metropolis, the upset it caused, Black teachers losing their jobs, and a teacher telling her, when she nominated a young African American girl to lead a high-school club, that they were not ready for that, all before she graduated in 1960.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Discrimination Is Stinkin'
clips.summary: At the University of Illinois Tillow joined the NAACP and walked her first picket line against a store that would not hire African Americans, frightened but certain it was right, carrying a sign reading that in the land of Lincoln discrimination is stinkin', and traveled with the local NAACP to SNCC demonstrations and a segregated courtroom in Cairo, Illinois, where she saw Mary McCollum, gashed on the thigh during a demonstration.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: You Don't Know Du Bois?
clips.summary: Tillow recounts her 1962 junior year abroad in Ghana, where W.E.B. Du Bois received an honorary degree to work on his Encyclopedia Africana and where students were astonished she had never heard of him, exposing the holes in American education, and where she learned from chancellor Conor Cruise O'Brien; she returned in 1963 to find Du Bois had died in August and the March on Washington had just happened, and later met John Lewis at Illinois.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: I Couldn't Stay in School
clips.summary: She describes her NAACP chapter sending a delegation to Atlanta over the semester break, where a discussion with Prathia Hall of Philadelphia and James Forman led her to decide she had to leave school and help, and then going to Hattiesburg, Mississippi, an oppressive and violent place where Bob Moses had been jailed and a white movement worker so badly beaten by white prisoners he could hardly be recognized as human, where police marched in military ranks to the courthouse and a mass meeting of hundreds gave each other courage in the area where Schwerner, Goodman, and Chaney would soon be killed.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Cairo Could Have Vied With Hattiesburg
clips.summary: Asked whether she identified as a Southerner, Tillow says she claimed Kentucky as her birthplace, notes that southern Illinois towns like Cairo could rival Hattiesburg for the brutality of their response to the movement, and explains that after a school break visiting these places she resolved to go back, young and without particular skills but determined to be there and learn.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Mentors to the Young People
clips.summary: Tillow describes working with the Appalachian Committee for Full Employment among coal miners and meeting Walter at Highlander Folk School, and how Carl and Anne Braden, along with James Dombrowski, mentored the young people coming through the South, sharing their newspaper The Southern Patriot, having them read works like Eleanor Flexner's Century of Struggle, and, out of Carl's conviction about the corrosive impact of anti-communism drawn from his own sedition case, shaping SNCC's refusal to adopt the liberal anti-communism of Hubert Humphrey.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: March on Frankfort for Jobs
clips.summary: Tillow explains being drawn to the argument that public accommodations alone could not end inequality because it had an economic base, describes a group of coal miners arrested for conspiracy while trying to revive their broken union, her newspaper Kentucky Jobs with Justice, and the March on Frankfort for Jobs and Freedom on March 5, 1964, where Appalachian coalfield contingents joined Louisville organizers.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: From the Movement Into the Unions
clips.summary: She recalls a Highlander workshop on unions during the SNCC era, drawing on Highlander's earlier role educating CIO organizers trying to bring Black and white workers together in the South, with a United Electrical Workers speaker leading a discussion of what role unions should play in justice and equality, and describes how, as people left SNCC by 1965 and 1966 with Walter having worked on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in 1964, she married Walter around 1964, the two went to the electrical workers union and were sent to separate cities, and she spent essentially all of 1964 organizing in East Kentucky.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: A Black Union in White Hospitals
clips.summary: She recounts leaving the United Electrical Workers for hospital workers union 1199 in 1969 and establishing it in Pennsylvania where the workforce was not majority Black, winning the first hospital in Lewistown despite an employer campaign branding it a Black union, organizing registered nurses at Mercy Hospital in Wilkes-Barre, and notes the union's reach into Baltimore and Johns Hopkins, where Coretta Scott King helped, and the hard-fought Charleston, South Carolina campaign that did not win recognition.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: The Grave of Myles Horton
clips.summary: Tillow describes Highlander's locations over the years, burned down and relocated to a big house in Knoxville, and a later return with a single-payer colleague from Tennessee to visit the grave of Myles Horton, with a play about Horton and Don West and a historical marker she photographed with Walter.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: No Law Covered Hospital Workers
clips.summary: She explains that when 1199 began in Pittsburgh in 1969 other unions saw it as a New York intruder on their territory while the United Electrical Workers, David Montgomery, and Tom Quinn formed a support committee, that the National Labor Relations Act did not yet cover hospital workers so recognition had to be forced by movement pressure, and that a 1970 strike at Presbyterian Hospital, though it won no bargaining, led Governor Milton Shapp to add hospital workers to state collective-bargaining coverage.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: A New Kind of Challenge
clips.summary: Tillow notes that Pennsylvania became a state that legally allowed hospital workers to organize and that the National Labor Relations Board did not amend its coverage nationally until about 1975, and describes the Pittsburgh service workforce as only thirty or forty percent African American, unlike New York, making it a new challenge they were proud to meet.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: A Sit-In on Christmas Eve
clips.summary: She recounts spreading the union to Uniontown, where families with coal-mining traditions wanted it and a strike was waged but not won, the tactic of shutting down the enterprises of hospital board members, and the first union victory at the Jewish Home and Hospital for the Aged in Pittsburgh, a majority African American campaign led by Henry Nicholas, who later headed the Philadelphia hospital workers, settled after a solid Christmas Eve 1969 sit-in.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: A Rabbi to Hold the Election
clips.summary: Tillow describes the sit-in holding firm, telling management they would send help only for emergencies, dismissing the laundry worker's demands, and winning by day's end an agreement for a secret-ballot election overseen by a rabbi from Rodef Shalom, which when won established the first 1199 union in Pennsylvania, soon followed under the new state law by Lewistown, Wilkes-Barre, and Washington, many of them majority-white bargaining units that proved people could unite on economic issues with civil rights understanding.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: My Mother Crying on the Phone
clips.summary: Tillow recounts her family's unhappiness that she left school before graduating, not finishing college until 1967, their fear that the work was dangerous, and the FBI visiting her parents over her anti-war activity, her mother crying on the phone believing she was in terrible trouble, a repression meant to make participation in the movement difficult.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: We Didn't Come All This Way for Two Seats
clips.summary: She describes seeing all her work as connected, the civil rights movement as the foundation and catalyst that made other organizing possible, and draws strength from common people of humble origins who shook the nation, recalling seeing Fannie Lou Hamer many times and her line that they did not come all this way for two seats, and Diane Nash telling the Justice Department that the freedom riders had all made out their wills.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: It Wasn't the Right Time on the Agenda
clips.summary: Tillow describes her involvement in pay equity and serving as president of the Pittsburgh Coalition of Labor Union Women, repeatedly denied recognition at the Allegheny Central Labor Council, and the founding of CLUW in 1974, which gave union women an organization to speak on women's, civil rights, and anti-war issues, with a dynamic Pittsburgh chapter often told it was out of line, even as she notes the labor movement's deep problems and losing ground on wages.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Make Health Care a Right
clips.summary: Tillow describes her current work building the nurses union in Louisville and the single-payer movement for universal health care, championing Congressman John Conyers's bill H.R. 676, which would remove the private for-profit insurance industry and for-profit hospitals so no one could profit from denying care, and her hope that health care, touching everyone, could be the place to break through and think big rather than settle for tiny incremental change.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Overworked to Incompetence
clips.summary: She argues that health care, education, and the military should not be run as businesses, voices concern about charter privatization undermining public schools and praises the Chicago teachers who took it on, and recounts learning from nurses in Louisville that the profit motive at Humana-owned hospitals understaffed nursing units to the point that even an excellent nurse could be overworked to incompetence and patient care suffered.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Six Hundred Five Unions
clips.summary: Tillow recounts that H.R. 676 was first introduced in 2003 and that she founded the All Unions Committee for Single Payer Health Care, winning her first endorsements from her CLUW chapter and the Chicago CLUW, then the national CLUW and the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, building to 605 unions, 43 state AFL-CIO federations, 146 central labor councils, and 22 international unions, while arguing against the insurance-company thinking that treats people using care as a loss.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Medicare Desegregated the Hospitals
clips.summary: She connects universal health care to closing the disparity between African Americans and whites, recalling that when Medicare passed in 1965 the law denied money to segregated hospitals, forcing Southern hospitals to integrate, and citing a study associated with David Satcher and Adewale Troutman estimating tens of thousands of excess deaths in the African American community that would not have occurred under equality, much of it tied to lack of access to care.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Go Forward or Go Backward
clips.summary: Tillow speaks of having to go forward or fall backward, citing the threat to voting rights after Shelby County v. Holder, and names her husband Walter as her source of strength, amazing in his persistence and shaped by a different left perspective from her farm background, reflecting that many couples met in the South during a period when a movement lifted everyone, and that she gained more from the movement than she gave.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: The Movement Had No Pension Plan
clips.summary: She observes that the movement offered no pension plan and that many veterans struggle, advocates expanding Social Security by taxing all wages and capital gains rather than capping the tax, and describes a resolution to the upcoming AFL-CIO convention, backed by eight central labor councils, to protect and expand Social Security and Medicare, fold everyone into Medicare as the single-payer plan, and call a March on Washington.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Used to Divide People
clips.summary: Tillow warns that immigrants are being used to divide people much as African Americans once were, notes that the Conyers bill excludes no one while the Affordable Care Act bars the undocumented from even buying insurance though their payroll contributions to Medicare and Social Security go uncollected, and calls the rising number of African Americans in prison deeply troubling, welcoming the judge's ruling against stop and frisk.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: What a Movement Is Made Of
clips.summary: Closing the interview, Tillow appreciates the chance to talk about matters she had not considered in a long time, defers to others who played more prominent roles, and affirms what she says the project has shown strongly, that a movement is made of all the people doing their pieces and how they all connect.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Sitting on the Mall, Opening Question
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier introduces the joint interview with Dorie Ann Ladner and Dr. Joyce Ann Ladner at the Library of Congress and frames an opening question about the ambivalence and complicated feeling they carried about the 1963 March on Washington.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Under Siege on the Front Line
clips.summary: Dorie describes the ambivalence of coming to the March while the movement was under siege: Medgar Evers killed June 12, his funeral June 16, Lawrence Guyot beaten, Fannie Lou Hamer jailed and beaten in Winona, a Jackson store boycott, and arrests in Greenwood. The sisters knew they had to return to those towns.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Ruby Doris Sends Joyce to Harlem
clips.summary: Joyce recounts learning of Medgar Evers's death by phone from Ruby Doris Smith Robinson while at a student conference in Lake Forest, Illinois. Ruby Doris persuaded her to go to New York to work under Bayard Rustin's direction at the Harlem March headquarters, where Joyce raised money, bringing back enough to run another bus each trip.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Bob Dylan and the Statler-Hilton
clips.summary: Joyce remembers late New York nights with Bob Dylan strumming on the sofa and seeing Malcolm X speak on 125th Street, then traveling to Washington to stay at the Statler-Hilton, the March hotel. Early the next morning the group picketed the Justice Department over Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen, jailed for sedition in Americus, Georgia.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Lena Horne Yields the Interview
clips.summary: On the Lincoln Memorial platform, Lena Horne declined a television interview and led nineteen-year-old Joyce by the hand up a scaffold to Nancy Dickerson, telling her to interview the young woman who lived in the South. Joyce recalls Josephine Baker, Marlon Brando, and her mother later reporting that the neighbors in Hattiesburg had seen her on TV.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Look White Men in the Eye
clips.summary: Joyce recalls her mother teaching them at four and five to look white men in the eye and never look down, and the family mantra that they were as good as anybody. She then describes the on-platform crisis over John Lewis's speech, with James Forman and Courtland Cox, and SNCC's anger that an authority figure presumed to change their words.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: De Lawd and Breaking an Area
clips.summary: The sisters explain their ambivalent relationship with Martin Luther King, whom SNCC called De Lawd, contrasting his arrival at the crisis stage with SNCC's work of breaking an area: staying under the toughest conditions until a local community let them in. They left the day after the March, without lasting euphoria.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Bob Moses Drives to Birmingham
clips.summary: Three weeks after the March, Bob Moses drove a school bus of SNCC workers to Birmingham for the funeral of the four girls killed in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing. Joyce reflects that the killing of children struck deeply, raising the fear that if they could do that to a child they could kill her brothers.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Stomach Illness and the Holiday Inn
clips.summary: Dorie describes the stress-induced stomach illness she developed in Natchez, going to bed not expecting to wake up, with neighbors leaving phones off the hook for safety. Frustrated trying to bond out arrested demonstrators, she took a cab to the segregated Holiday Inn and paid the cashier to summon the chief of police.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Rage at the Masonic Temple
clips.summary: The day after Medgar Evers was killed, Dorie ran up to two white police officers on motorcycles outside the Masonic Temple, screaming at them in a rage she cannot fully describe. They sat with heads bowed and never responded, and only late in life has she come to think they practiced non-violence with her.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Beckwith Trials and a Volkswagen Escape
clips.summary: Dorie attended both Beckwith trials, watched Ross Barnett shake the defendant's hand, and saw the Klan give Beckwith a standing ovation. She recounts district attorney William Waller's openly racist jury selection, being ejected by the judge, and escaping a mob with Dave Dennis and Mattie Bivens in a Volkswagen bus offered by a passerby.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Guilt, Courage, and a Black Scarf
clips.summary: Joyce describes the guilt she carried in graduate school while Dorie stayed in dangerous Natchez, and her conviction that courage was acting in spite of fear. She recalls covering her white roommate Joan Trumpauer's blonde hair with a black scarf and putting her on the floor on the unlit Hanging Moss Road to Tougaloo.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The House and Mr. McCarthy's Still
clips.summary: Asked to describe their home, the sisters detail the three-bedroom Palmers Crossing house with its front porch and the fenceless compound shared with grandparents and great aunt and uncle. They recount Mr. Joe McCarthy's bootleg whiskey still and the Friday-evening Mississippi National Guard raid that broke crates of liquor all night.
related_events: Mississippi National Guard raid on bootleg operation

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Camp Shelby, the Crossing, and Miss Bama
clips.summary: The sisters explain how their community served as a recreational outpost for Black soldiers from Camp Shelby, with the Inkwell Club and Club Desire drawing Chitlin' Circuit entertainers. They describe Palmers Crossing itself, the City of New Orleans train that brought the Chicago Defender, the Hudson grocery that became Hudson's salvage stores, and Miss Bama's house.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Mother Met Them on the Porch
clips.summary: The sisters describe how their mother, divorced and for a time a single parent, told all her girls to get an education because no one could take it from them. They watched her meet insurance collectors on the front porch and never let them inside or treat her as inferior, modeling a no-nonsense pride.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Grandpa Ben Said We're As Good As Anybody
clips.summary: The sisters trace their resistance ethos to a Creek Indian woman named Mary and a white man, Ben Willard of Rockingham, North Carolina, who would not enslave his own children. The mantra that they were as good as anybody passed through the generations. Their entrepreneur uncles refused to work for whites, illustrated by an uncle who walked away from a racist customer's broken TV.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Coining the Emmett Till Generation
clips.summary: Joyce describes coining the term Emmett Till generation for post-war Black Southern youth whose parents, aunts, and uncles told them they would change things. She recalls Uncle Archie, a World War I veteran who served in France, telling them their generation would change things, and the Baptist liturgy they translated into the movement's songs and mass meetings.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Dr. McCloud and the Censored World
clips.summary: The sisters recall the herbalist they called Dr. McCloud, a Hattiesburg NAACP member who brought them the Pittsburgh Courier, Chicago Defender, Jet, and Ebony each week. In a censored Mississippi with one TV station that called Black people slurs on screen, this outside reading material fueled their curiosity and showed them how others lived.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Meeting Medgar and the Kennard Frame-Up
clips.summary: The sisters met Medgar Evers in high school when he came to set up the Hattiesburg NAACP youth chapter, with C.E. Clack and Vernon Dahmer as the motivating advisors and Dahmer's sister Hallean Beard, the church pianist, as Dorie's best friend and chaperone. They recall watching the frame-up of Clyde Kennard, jailed on a moonshine charge and held over a year.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Powerless Teenagers Clipping the News
clips.summary: Looking back as fourteen and fifteen year olds who knew things were wrong but felt powerless, the sisters describe running to Hudson's for the Hattiesburg American at 4:15 each day during the Emmett Till case. Joyce clipped the articles; Dorie, wanting to be a lawyer, went to her teacher Mr. Clark and then memorized the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments for her own empowerment.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Why the Elders Followed Teenagers
clips.summary: The sisters marvel that older people followed nineteen-year-olds, crediting it to taking themselves seriously and carrying a message the community had waited to hear. They recall getting waitresses and sharecroppers to leave their jobs and risk their lives at the Indianola courthouse where Fannie Lou Hamer registered in August 1962, and the Jet photograph of Emmett Till as the clarion call for their generation.
related_events: Fannie Lou Hamer voter registration August 1962

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Mack Charles Parker and the Racism Unleashed
clips.summary: Joyce recalls the second lynching they experienced, that of Mack Charles Parker in Poplarville, fifteen minutes from Hattiesburg, when international media came to the city and her mother pulled the boys close before dark. She reflects that such killings emboldened local racists to talk openly, and that the perpetrators lived in the community where everyone knew them.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Founding COFO and the Night Drive
clips.summary: Dorie recounts the August 1962 founding of COFO in Clarksdale, her first time in the Delta. After a curfew violation, Dave Dennis was nearly jailed and the group drove in fear through the night, told that one of Emmett Till's killers was a night watchman in Ruleville, searching for Amzie Moore's house in Cleveland. The terror pushed her to commit to Delta organizing.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: One Foot in College, Dorie Leaves Home
clips.summary: Joyce explains the difference between them: she kept one foot in college while Dorie dropped out, saying she could not stay in school while people suffered. Dorie recounts leaving home with a small orange suitcase, telling her mother on the way out the door that she was going to Jackson to work with Bob Moses for her freedom, and arriving at the Freedom House on Rose Street.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Jackson State and the Tougaloo Nine
clips.summary: The sisters recall James Meredith as a clear-headed strategist at Jackson State before he enrolled at Ole Miss, and demonstrating on behalf of the Tougaloo Nine, where they were tear-gassed. They describe crossing the street every Wednesday to talk with Medgar Evers about their freedom, and his bringing Dorie money sent by their mother.
related_events: James Meredith Ole Miss enrollment September 1962

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Prayer That Got Them Expelled
clips.summary: Advised by Medgar Evers to act on campus, Dorie as dorm-council president called on Joyce to pray, and Joyce spoke of dark days ahead. The dorm matron reported the prayer to Dean Oscar Rogers, the sisters grew combative defending it, and after a march for the Tougaloo students and an early spring break, they and student-government president Walter Williams were expelled.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Freedom House and the Freedom Riders
clips.summary: Joyce describes the Jackson Freedom House, initially rented by the Freedom Riders, where Lawrence Guyot and Cole Ladell cooked for the group. She recalls first meeting the Freedom Riders upstairs at the Masonic Temple by Medgar's office, Tom Hayden, Charles McDew, Marion Barry, John Lewis, and Dion Diamond, and feeling she had been looking for them since she was fourteen.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Judge Cox and the Successor
clips.summary: Dorie recalls being ordered out of a Freedom Rider trial by the judge, and the sisters describe US District Judge William Harold Cox referring to them as slurs in the courtroom. At the 50th Freedom Rider reunion, Joyce met the Black man who had replaced Cox on the bench, a remarkable turn fifty years later.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Cousin Victoria Gray, Teacher and Confidant
clips.summary: The sisters describe their cousin Victoria Gray, beautiful and a model of going away to college at Wilberforce, who lived in Germany and returned exotic. She became active first with SCLC's Citizenship Education Program and then SNCC, working in Hattiesburg with Guyot and Frank Smith. They remember her as a teacher and confidant whose death still pains them.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Building the MFDP With Ella Baker
clips.summary: The sisters explain the two levels of movement projects, strategic voter registration and events pushed to the national stage, and the building of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party through freedom ballots, precinct, district, and state conventions. They credit Ella Baker as the leader who taught them to listen and guided them to consensus with an unseen hand.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Sojourner Fleet to Atlantic City
clips.summary: Joyce recounts driving to Atlantic City in the Sojourner Fleet of cars, named by Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, stopping at Cleve Sellers's parents' home in Denmark, South Carolina. She describes the burned-out station wagon from the Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman murders displayed on the boardwalk and the movement's tactile way of showing people what segregation was like.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The WATS Line Goes Silent
clips.summary: Dorie had seen Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman leaving Miami University in Oxford, Ohio to return to Mississippi after the church burning. Joyce describes the moment word came that they had not checked in on the WATS line and the staff knew they were dead. The sisters blame the federal government for not intervening and reflect on the movement's walking wounded.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Natchez Bombing and Little Richard
clips.summary: Dorie recounts a Klan attempt to bomb the Natchez SNCC office that hit the wrong house, an integrated cafe belonging to Jake Fishman, and how Mr. Archie Curtis the funeral director escorted her through the mob to retrieve the voter-registration cards. She describes quieting a harassing late-night caller by promising her a Little Richard concert ticket.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Greenwood, Police Dogs, and John Doar
clips.summary: Dorie recalls working all day in the Greenwood SNCC office, learning it was bombed that night, and cleaning up the files the next morning before going right back out. At the Leflore County Courthouse, police dogs chased the group and a young Marian Wright, who had interned at the Justice Department, called John Doar from a pay phone.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Terrorism, the Back Seat, and PTSD
clips.summary: Dorie reflects that the word terrorism does not faze her because she lived through churches and homes bombed beyond counting. Unable to drive, she always sat in the back seat so another could take the wheel if the driver was shot, recalling Jimmy Travis shot in 1963. She describes how some memories flash across her mind like PTSD, and how Joyce sublimated trauma into driven work.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Dahmer's Death and Mrs. Turnbow's Pistol
clips.summary: Joyce, who later worked in a Level 1 trauma unit, names her worst movement moment: the 6 a.m. call from her mother in January 1966 that Vernon Dahmer was killed, after years of repression broke through. She recalls lobbying the Oregon delegation with Representative Edith Green and Senator Wayne Morse alongside Mrs. Hartman Turnbow, who carried a pistol in a brown paper bag.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Genealogy After the Records Opened
clips.summary: Dorie describes her genealogy quest after Mississippi opened its slave records to Black researchers in 1992, drawn by her mother's family being visibly different, malato and Indian. Through cross-referenced research, others studying the same great-great-grandfather connected her to white Willard descendants in Georgia, Alabama, and London, leading to reunions in Mobile, Alabama.
related_events: Mississippi archives opened to Black researchers 1992

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Willard and Mary Line
clips.summary: Dorie lays out the traced maternal line: Benjamin F. Willard, born 1805 in Rockingham, North Carolina, and Mary, a Black Creek Indian also born 1805, who came together to the Alabama-Mississippi territory and had thirteen children. The line runs through Frank (1825), Jerry Willard (about 1870), Joseph Collins Willard and Martha Gates, to their mother, born May 14, 1921 and died January 23, 1995.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The White Side Resists
clips.summary: The sisters reflect that they were not surprised by the white side of the family, though those relatives were. They describe the Willard descendants who deny the connection and the large Creole Favre family in Louisiana, which includes Brett Favre and Jimmy Buffett and holds integrated reunions, who decided early to stay in the white race rather than the Black race.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Teach the History, and Closing
clips.summary: Asked what part of the civil rights narrative most needs attention, Joyce answers that the schools must teach the history so that elementary, high school, and college students know what happened, countering revisionists. The interview closes with thanks and the Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Excommunication or Integration
clips.summary: Interviewed by Julian Bond for the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian NMAAHC, Guyot explains how he grew up in Pass Christian, a Gulf Coast town shaped by Ingalls Shipyard, strong labor unions, and a Catholic Church that in 1957 or 1958 desegregated its schools by telling parishioners to choose between excommunication and integration by Friday, teaching him firsthand that institutions can shape social policy.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: This Boy Is a Creole
clips.summary: Guyot recounts being seventeen, having passed the entrance test for Tougaloo, when a local official threatened to jail him over three slot machines he had bought, and how his father hired Bidwell Adams, a former lieutenant governor and Democratic patronage broker, who told the official the boy was a Creole and threatened a federal gambling investigation, after which Guyot never heard of the slot machines again.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: It Gives Me More Time to Think
clips.summary: Guyot describes a deeply political family, a great-uncle who chaired the Republican Party in Hancock County for decades, and a grandfather who taught him the value of listening, letting Baptist ministers believe they had convinced him and repeating a visitor's words back to buy time to think, lessons that ran alongside reading the newspaper together every day.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: My Father Would Have Killed Twenty Men
clips.summary: Guyot recalls reading Paul's letters aloud at age seven in St. Philomena's Catholic Church, learning the interrelationship of religion and politics, and a father who out-argued textbook publishers, held out a jury until a widow got her insurance money, and beat one of the richest men in town with his fists, letting Guyot use the Coast's white restaurants because no one dared trouble him.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Functional Only by Their Absence
clips.summary: At Tougaloo, threatened with extinction by the state legislature for its interracial faculty, Guyot meets Reverend Ed King, Ernst Borinski, a Jew driven out of Germany who taught that some institutions like Mississippi's Democratic Party are functional only by their absence of function, and A.A. Branch, who let students join voting demonstrations so long as they brought their books and took the same exams.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Tougaloo Was an Oasis
clips.summary: Guyot names schoolmates who became movement figures and argues that had he gone to Jackson State instead of Tougaloo he would never have lasted under its rigid conformity, the same conditions that drove the Ladner sisters to leave Jackson State, and reflects on his luck in a town where the Catholic Church desegregated, labor unions and Keesler Air Force Base flourished, and a frightened uncle who in 1964 would not let him phone the mayor about the summer project ran for alderman by 1966.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: 714 Rose Street
clips.summary: Guyot recounts running into SNCC through the Ladner sisters and Bob Moses, meeting Moses, John Doar, and Marion Barry at once at the Freedom House at 714 Rose Street, moving into the Delta to Amzie Moore's house, and at a mass meeting with James Bevel, Charles McLaurin, and James Forman watching Fannie Lou Hamer raise her hand to go register, a religious fundamentalist with a perfect sense of timing.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Lyndon Interrupted Her Speech
clips.summary: Guyot describes Hamer becoming a national figure who spoke at the 1964 Democratic Convention until President Johnson called a press conference to interrupt her, drawing 1,200 telegrams of support, and how Johnson, on his own tapes, demanded the floor fight be stopped, pressuring delegates with offers of judgeships and OEO money, leaving the MFDP a two-seat compromise it courageously refused.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: I Ran Into Chemistry
clips.summary: Guyot explains that he majored in biology and minored in philosophy meaning to become a doctor, but scoring poorly in mathematics and chemistry he abandoned medicine, and reflects that he would rather have been a lawyer, having grasped how Brown and Baker v. Carr opened the political arena to the courts and expanded judicial power.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: They Were About Empowerment
clips.summary: Guyot says SNCC people had a religious commitment to interracialism and cared about empowerment rather than building membership, working with established local leaders like E.W. Steptoe and Hartman Turnbow, and recounts how in Greenwood, despite tremendous fear, demonstrations drew a hundred people into the jail with food and five hundred down to register, a thing not supposed to happen.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: When You Hit Him, You Hit Everyone
clips.summary: Guyot tells how Greenwood forced the Department of Justice into court, with John Doar filing to free the arrested registration workers, and how SNCC took Doar to Walthall County to meet John Hardy, a SNCC worker pistol-whipped by a registrar, prompting Justice to halt the prosecution on the theory that striking Hardy struck every Black person who might register, a stay carried to the Fifth Circuit.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: The Most Beautiful Language Ever Written
clips.summary: Guyot recounts how the MFDP's first private suit under the 1965 Voting Rights Act, joined by Doar and Justice as amicus, won eight to one at the Supreme Court so that anyone aggrieved under Section 5 could sue, and credits the idea to reading Anthony Lewis's Gideon's Trumpet and to a conviction that Johnson, having stolen an election, wrote Section 5's preclearance to stop anyone from doing what he had done.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Come On Over and Find Out
clips.summary: Guyot describes driving to Winona after Fannie Lou Hamer, Annelle Ponder, June Johnson, and others were arrested returning from a South Carolina meeting, being beaten almost to death by twelve people who threatened to burn his testicles, refusing to sign a statement that he had fallen out of a car, then being charged with a murder he had not committed, sensing he would not leave alive amid a knife by his open cell and a fake FBI agent, and crediting survival to calls from Roberta Galler and to Medgar Evers's assassination before Andrew Young, James Bevel, and Frank Smith freed him.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: I'd Rather Lie and Take Control
clips.summary: Guyot recalls another time, driving a slow car with bad tires from Hattiesburg when men with shotguns followed, when he got out and bluffed that the FBI had a tail on them and anyone touched would get ten years, sending the men off, and reflects through Eleanor Holmes Norton, who said he walked right into terror, on a Black southern code that told men to stay out of jail.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: A Beast of a State
clips.summary: Guyot contrasts the organizations, quoting Roy Wilkins that Mississippi was a beast of a state where anyone who tried to organize was crazy and the NAACP would litigate and raise money off its atrocities, and separating Martin Luther King from SCLC, since King personally backed SNCC's Freedom Election, the summer project, the Freedom Schools, and Dorothy Height's Wednesdays in Mississippi.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Bring America to Mississippi
clips.summary: Guyot says King prized the freedom and creativity of Mississippi organizing that produced 80,000 Freedom Election votes and the 1964 logic of bringing America to Mississippi, recounts asking King to co-sign with Guyot and John Lewis a memorandum that the Mississippi delegation should still be unseated even as the Voting Rights Act passed, and credits Ekwueme Michael Thelwell and the congressional challenge that won 149 congressmen with forcing the stronger Section 5 trigger.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: There's America, the South, and Mississippi
clips.summary: Guyot agrees with Bond that theirs was the last generation to engage in sustained mobilization, citing Johnson's line that there is America, the South, and then Mississippi, and points to Operation Understanding, the Children's Defense Fund, and the Young People's Project led by Moses's children as inheritors, urging that the mantra of poor power and the SNCC experience be melded and used now.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: That's Real Political Mobilization
clips.summary: Guyot insists SNCC's distinction was seeing total oppression and turning every impediment into an opening, registering when told they could not, voting anyway, putting officials' names in the newspaper, and warns that if Black voters who turned out in 2008 keep skipping midterms and let Republicans control Congress they must write off the whole mantra, since he sees no equivalence between the two parties.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: We Made Change, Others Wrote About It
clips.summary: Guyot frames the moment as a golden opportunity with everything on the table, vowing to spend the next two years pushing books and the SNCC example and calling on veterans of the Freedom Elections, Freedom Rides, and summer project to act, drawing the line between people who made change and people who wrote about it, and warning Bond that if they fail to act now the loss will be irretrievable.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Ten Dollars a Week
clips.summary: Asked about a typical week in the summer of 1964, Guyot says he stayed in contact with organizers from Hattiesburg, Jackson, or the SNCC summer headquarters in Greenwood, tracking political news and press while sustaining local leadership on ten-dollar-a-week salaries, and praises Harry Bowie, a Black Episcopalian of the Delta Ministry, an organization born when Guyot invited religious groups to Hattiesburg in 1963.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Y'All Do the Litigating
clips.topic: Lawyers, Howard, and the Summer Project
clips.summary: Guyot tells how SNCC kept its lawyers, Kunstler, Arthur Kinoy, and others meeting with Dave Dennis and Bob Moses, on the line that SNCC made the decisions and they did the litigating, and recounts a Howard University conference after Kennedy's assassination, when Johnson sought a moratorium on demonstrations and Theodore H. White's book branded SNCC dangerous, where Bayard Rustin, Baldwin, and Norman Thomas spoke, Thomas saying they could not win unless they sang, and Moses threw his weight behind the summer project after the murder of the Herbert Lee witness Louis Allen.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: A Reconciliation Post
clips.summary: Guyot describes the Freedom House at 714 Rose Street in Jackson as a reconciliation post where Freedom Riders tried in Jackson were briefed before returning home, and how National Council of Churches figures, asked what they could do for the congressional challenge, opened doors to congressmen through their bishops, so that even Gerald Ford agreed to meet James Farmer and Guyot because his bishop called him.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: When You Beat the Best
clips.summary: Guyot recalls the labor unions and a $25,000 AFL-CIO offer with strings that did not last, a post-convention New York meeting with Gloster Current, Roy Wilkins, Courtland Cox, and James Farmer over who would run Mississippi, his insistence that beating the worst made Mississippi a national paradigm, a demographer's 1965 letter warning that the SNCC-MFDP operation would not stay loyal to the national party, and the NEA and AFT sending representatives to capture the Freedom Schools.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Wasting Your Bus Fare to Chicago
clips.summary: Guyot says he moved to Washington in 1968 as a delegate to that year's Democratic Convention, where Bidwell Adams told an all-white delegation it was wasting its bus fare to Chicago, and how the loyalists, sent up to destroy the MFDP, instead joined with it to win half the delegation and be seated, after which Guyot began fighting for a first presidential primary in Washington to spotlight the city's lack of representation.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: The Bible and the Ballot in Fusion
clips.summary: Guyot describes becoming an Advisory Neighborhood Commissioner active in Columbia Heights development and supporting Adrian Fenty for mayor, recalls the 1968 decision to carry the church into the political arena and forswear hate for the ballot, and explains that joining Fenty's defeated campaign and backing same-sex marriage flow from his conviction that Black people separating politics from religion would be finished.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Can I Deny It to Two People in Love
clips.summary: Guyot answers how he reconciles supporting same-sex marriage against Black church opposition led by Walter Fauntroy by telling of his white wife, who came to Mississippi for SNCC, went to jail with him, worked to pay his way through Rutgers Law School, and saved his life, reasoning that he could not deny the same to two people of the same sex in love, and grounds all his power politics in religious belief and Jefferson's call for an informed electorate.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: John Salter and Medgar Evers
clips.summary: Beginning a rapid roll of names for the camera, Guyot identifies John Salter Jr. as a political Tougaloo teacher who led NAACP demonstrations in Jackson and worked with Medgar Evers and Ed King, Medgar Evers as the NAACP's executive director in Mississippi who went disguised to gather information on the Emmett Till case, and Tom Gaither as an early CORE member.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: The World Will Little Note
clips.topic: John Doar, Ford, and the Wednesdays
clips.summary: Guyot characterizes John Doar as a relentless Republican Assistant Attorney General under Robert Kennedy who drove Justice into civil rights and quoted Lincoln while prosecuting the Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman case, recalls Doar's line that the Voting Rights Act was passed in McComb and the streets of Greenwood and SNCC's lone fight for illiterates' right to vote, and revisits Gerald Ford agreeing to meet them and Dorothy Height's Wednesdays in Mississippi.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Another Religious Fanatic
clips.topic: The Ladners, Victoria Gray, Theron Lynd
clips.summary: Guyot says the Ladner sisters came out of Hattiesburg related to Vernon Dahmer, Medgar Evers, and Victoria Gray, the MFDP National Committeewoman and SCLC speaker he calls another religious fanatic who brought in her family and filled the Hattiesburg Freedom Schools, and introduces Theron Lynd, the Forrest County registrar who failed even postgraduates, tried by the Fifth Circuit and chronicled in the book Count Them One by One.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Wait Until We Get to New Orleans
clips.summary: Guyot recounts John Doar introducing him to Judges Brown, Tuttle, and Bell during the Theron Lynd trial, and praises the Fifth Circuit, chronicled in Jack Bass's Unlikely Heroes, as Eisenhower appointees who had filed challenge delegations in their own states, won the appellate seats they sought, and expanded constitutional rights so that a case lost in Jackson could be won in New Orleans.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: They Treated Our Work as Theirs
clips.summary: Guyot describes the Fifth Circuit declaring Jackson's apartheid policy unmatched in the United States, validating Section 5 cases and Arthur Kinoy's removal cases later reversed by the Supreme Court, and repeatedly overturning the racist district judge Cox who called Black registrants chimpanzees, while singling out Griffin Bell as the least praiseworthy of the circuit for keeping Bond out of the Georgia legislature and advising Andrew Young not to testify against Bork.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Find Out What Ball the Governor Is Going To
clips.summary: Guyot explains that he handed Ekwueme Michael Thelwell a check to open the MFDP's Washington office, where Thelwell followed legislation and worked the Leadership Conference, recounts Thelwell's letter protesting that no MFDP people were invited to the inaugural after electing Johnson in Mississippi, and tells how Hartman Turnbow, invited to the White House, asked which ball the governor of Mississippi would attend so he could dance with his wife, Turnbow being the man charged after defending his burning house.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Two Sheriffs Signed His Bond
clips.summary: Guyot tells how, with Justice rigorously defending Turnbow by accepting the state's no-murder position, he himself carried a bond between the sheriffs of Leflore and Holmes counties to have Turnbow released to him, an event he says never happened again in the South, and turns to Bob Moses, a mathematician and philosopher totally committed to social change who believed there was political potential in everyone and got others to behave that way.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Racists Are Never More Exposed Than When They Brag
clips.summary: Guyot says Moses believed those who do the work should make the decisions and that you free the arrested or go to jail with them, recalls Moses filing charges against the man who beat him in McComb before a judge who bragged about treating darkies well, and explains that SNCC could do nothing in Mississippi without the people's approval, won by never promising protection but asking them to come along.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Nothing Was Non-Political in Mississippi
clips.summary: Guyot argues Mississippians needed no lessons that everything was political since the Board of Supervisors decided hospitals, jobs, and pay, traces the 1964 unpledged-elector system that opened the door to the Republican Party as the GOP expelled Black delegates in San Francisco, and recalls 1960, when Jackie Robinson left Nixon crying after Nixon refused to help, and Kennedy's call to Coretta Scott King and the blue leaflet in Black churches made him president.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: The Mantra of SNCC
clips.summary: Asked what he wants the world to know, Guyot says he was a man who carried out his religious beliefs through political activity, never stepped away from a fight, and wants to be remembered for perpetuating the mantra of SNCC, which unlike the short-lived Anti-Saloon League changed the very concept of democracy and citizenship, and closes by urging that organizing become a social necessity and that those who refuse to register their families and churches to vote in the next two years be declared social pariahs, warning that everything fought for will be beyond retrieving unless they fight now.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Born Across From the Health Department
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in her home in Port Gibson, Mississippi, in December 2015, Leesco Guster recalls being born on Main Street across from the Health Department on March 26, 1936, delivered by a midwife, and being raised by a single mother who did domestic work and moved to Vicksburg after separating from her father.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Negro Street Was Busy
clips.summary: She describes the odd grade-shuffling between Vicksburg schools, liking home economics over sports, and moving to Port Gibson in 1953, where the strip then called Negro Street, now Fair Street, was lined with joints and a cafe called Peaches and felt alive.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Colored Fountain and White Fountain
clips.summary: Guster recalls absorbing race by instinct without it bothering her young, then growing to feel things needed to change, naming the colored and white fountains, and remembers living in the house rather than the basement of the white family she worked for before going to Chicago in the 1950s.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: You Worked Yourself to Death
clips.summary: She contrasts the punishing pace of Chicago, hopping the El and buses between jobs and working Sundays for extra pay, with a stint at Montgomery Ward cut short by her nerves, and recalls cleaning tile floors on her hands and owning her own house at sixteen with help from a sister who managed her Social Security check.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: You Could Look Clear Under the House
clips.summary: Guster describes her husband Harry Guster, a lumber contractor about twenty-four years her senior whom everyone in Claiborne County knew, and the run-down house he had bought for around twenty-five hundred dollars, so unfinished you could stand at the front and see clear underneath it to the back.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: I Gave Him a Year to Think About It
clips.summary: Returning on a three-week vacation from Chicago at nineteen, she told Harry she would not come back unless he married her and divorced his first wife, recalls the friction caused by his young son whose mother had expected Harry to take the boy back, and how she pushed Harry to keep paying the twenty-five then thirty dollars a month of support even when lumber work was scarce, until the boy's mother took him to Cleveland, Ohio and stopped collecting.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: I Wanted People Registered to Vote
clips.summary: She tells how Mr. Walcott let her work for his daughter Hillary, who had moved from Adena, Ohio, and explains that though she was not even an NAACP member, she wanted to vote, to register people, and to win a little recognition and a better job, hanging around McFatter's drugstore where Rudy Shields and the federal workers gathered.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Well, Lee, We Don't Need You Anymore
clips.summary: Guster describes canvassing across Claiborne County after the federal registrars arrived, being seen standing out at registration, and her employer, who had come from Ohio and taken on the local whites' attitude, complaining she was tired of all that mess and politely letting Guster go, after which Guster walked home and kept going up to the drugstore every evening.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Brave, Wasn't Scared of Nothing
clips.summary: She recalls meeting Rudy Shields on the street in front of the drugstore, a brave man who spoke up about what he knew and was doing what she wanted to do, and how she volunteered her car and a pistol while keeping the sickly Shields from marching or risking jail, sitting up with a shotgun while he stayed in her house, and insisting she was brave though she does not know where it came from.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: We Lost All Our Work
clips.summary: Guster describes the family living on a hundred-fourteen-dollar check and a small Social Security payment after both she and Harry lost their work, Harry having come into the area with Charles Evers but staying only long enough to sleep, and the patient canvassing she and Rudy did among people who had never voted, some going along and some too afraid.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: He Had His Shotgun on the Truck
clips.summary: She recounts her scariest moment, going out to Rocky Springs where the property owner waited with a shotgun racked across his pickup, telling them he did not want to lose what he had, and Rudy Shields, calling Harry Guster's name and telling her to just drive, sitting in the back with a pistol as they passed the man's truck at a one-way bridge.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: We Had as High as Three
clips.summary: Guster tells of putting up civil rights lawyers and workers in her home, including a sweet woman named Tuck Tate, with Lily Mae Williams as chairperson handling arrangements, doing it without pay though others spread that she was being paid, and explaining the gas donations she did collect and read aloud at meetings.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Count on Both Hands
clips.summary: She reflects that she could never have passed the courthouse registration test herself, and names the handful of Black people registered before the movement, among them Mr. and Mrs. Colton, the Johnsons, Roscoe, and Junior Jones, so few you could almost count them on both hands.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: I Put the Lord Behind
clips.summary: Guster admits she stopped attending choir practice and could not pay her church dues because the meetings met when choir did, a wrong she says she would not repeat, and describes being put over the youth after Rudy organized them, taking the children to sing freedom songs at church.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: They Took the Pulpit for a Play Place
clips.summary: She names freedom songs the youth sang, including Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around and He's Got the Whole World in His Hands, recalls James Miller among the children, and objects to A.C. Garner wanting dances and to youngsters beating on the pulpit, which is what pushed her aside and led Reverend Dawson to step down.
keywords: Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: He Was on the Radical Side
clips.summary: Guster names good speakers like Mrs. Wales and Miss Julia Jones while counting herself and Mr. Nate among the quieter workers, explains that a speaker she found radical pushed a second boycott she opposed for punishing merchants like Claiborne Hardware who had already complied, and lists the first boycott's demands: the right to vote, recognition, and putting Black workers like a daughter or sister on the cash register, recalling Moselle hiring four Black workers.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: He Asked Was I Tired of Living
clips.summary: Guster recounts a man phoning during preparations for a banquet that Charles Evers was to attend, asking for Harry Guster and whether she was tired of living, identifying himself as a member of the KKK, and the later threats to burn her house and the place where Rudy was staying.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: The Only Fatality Was the Mattress
clips.summary: She describes a tense night under the shed when men were thought to be on the food-stand roof and a truck slowed as if to stop, Harry and the others keeping watch with shotguns, and how the next morning she accidentally fired a shotgun aimed down into the mattress, frightening Harry who thought she had killed herself.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: He Was the One Who Brought the Klan
clips.summary: Guster sizes up Charles Evers as a man who got right to the point and mostly talked while Rudy Shields was the main one on the ground, tells of the grocer Hudson saying he would do what was asked because it was for colored people, names a merchant named Jones she believed tried to bring the Klan in and was kept from opening his store for a year, recalls the federal registrar Bobby Gage rudely making them move their cars, and a sabotaged car given the wrong oil that ruined her motor during the boycott.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Don't Shoot, Just Scare
clips.summary: Guster describes the enforcers organized at a green picnic table outside who handled people who broke the boycott, says she opposed their shooting into houses though she did not mind a good scare, recounts confronting a Priscilla Duck store with a pink house and glass showcase and a woman who kept letting the candy man come until Guster told her the business was over, and explains that the first boycott ended when stores added Black workers, floating her own idea of backing a white candidate since neither race can do without the other.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: The Material Was Still on His Desk
clips.topic: The Bank Loan and the House
clips.summary: Guster recounts going to a banker named Cadman and then Drake for a loan to build a school addition onto her house, being refused because of the lawsuit over her property and then finding the loan already written up when the banker returned, overhearing a rumor at the bank that they meant to take her home, meeting the lawyer Bobby Gage with all her receipts to prove she was not behind, and crediting the house's survival to pushing Harry to start paying the twenty-five hundred dollars he still owed Mr. Shields's sister.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: If You Were Black, No Deals
clips.summary: Guster explains the lawsuit grew out of the boycott rather than any deal she made, recalls car salesmen Buster Barber and a man named Davis testifying they had no dealings with her, says the merchants put the names of everyone with property on the suit including old people like John Ellis who only signed bonds, and recounts Eddie Walls proudly answering in court that his race was oatmeal.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: We Need Black and White Together
clips.topic: Dusty Jackson and the Movement's Meaning
clips.summary: She says she testified little, questioning how anyone could be sued for money the merchants would never have gotten anyway, recalls being in town when Nat Dusty Jackson was killed though she did not march for it, and reflects that the movement showed people they could come together for a while and accomplished something even as much was lost, arguing that Black and white need each other rather than doing to one another what was once done to them.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: More for the Young People
clips.summary: She says she worked not so much for herself as for the young people coming along, recalls trying to back Thomas Russell, whom she calls her backbone, for office because he refused to take what was meant for white officials, declines ever running herself, and tells young people to speak up for themselves but to work, not just talk.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: I'm Not Going to Put No Lie Out There
clips.summary: Guster recounts Kenneth Ross asking her for ten names for the Port Gibson mural, going far back among the people she knew, refusing to name women she felt only talked or to claim credit for herself, and praising Ross as smart while contrasting him with a man who collected church money for a museum that never came and turned it over to the NAACP.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Why Couldn't They Get Somebody From Here
clips.summary: She compares the courthouse civil rights exhibit to the meetings, honors workers like George Henry and Reverend Dawson who labored in the heat, and resents that Margot, appointed president of the Walmart NAACP chapter because of who she was and where she came from, was set over local people who had done the struggle, while Guster refused to pay the twenty-five dollars.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: We Were Good Friends in the Movement
clips.summary: Guster says she is proud of her work though she did not do it for show, recalls George urging her to take in the ailing Rudy Shields and her telling him she could not, since she had a husband and they had been good friends only in the movement, and mentions a daughter of a man Rudy stayed with in Yazoo trying to write about him, before the interview closes.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: The Man Never Invited to the Picnic
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop at her home in Americus, Georgia, in May, Linda Fuller Degelmann recounts being born in Birmingham in 1941, growing up in Tuscaloosa where her parents ran Caldwell Electric Company on Broad Street, and the Black man who worked in her father's basement repair shop yet was never invited to the family's annual picnic, an exclusion she wondered about amid her unquestioned segregated schooling.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: An Awareness Picked Up From Millard
clips.topic: Marriage, Montgomery, and the Bus Boycott
clips.summary: She describes marrying Millard Fuller in 1959 and absorbing from him a growing awareness of segregation after his youth-conference travels north, the move to Montgomery where he set up a law practice, attending the all-white Huntingdon College while raising children, and driving into town to fetch the woman who kept her children because, during the bus boycott, the woman would not ride the bus.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Cancel Our Subscription to the Fiery Cross
clips.topic: The Freedom Rides and Breaking With the Klan
clips.summary: She tells how Millard Fuller and Morris Dees went to the Montgomery bus station and were shocked to see Freedom Riders beaten as they stepped off the bus, shifting their minds a hundred and eighty degrees from earlier political work for a lieutenant-governor candidate, and how, never Klan members but on its fringe through the publication called the Fiery Cross, they summoned a Klan leader and with a pistol on the desk demanded to cancel their subscription, after which their Fuller and Dees Marketing offices were broken into.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Bedrolls on the Floor for the Marchers
clips.summary: She recalls pastors Millard knew from Ohio and Indiana coming for the Selma to Montgomery March and sleeping at the Fullers' home while Millard and Morris drove them to the march route each morning, the nasty phone calls from neighbors who photographed the visitors' license plates, and the family's growing conviction that they needed to take a side.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Called to Give It All Away
clips.topic: Marriage Crisis, Tougaloo, and New Jersey
clips.summary: She describes leaving Millard in 1965 as his and Morris Dees's drive to become millionaires by thirty destroyed the marriage, the decision to give away their wealth and put God in control, Dr. Durgan of Broadway United Church of Christ asking Millard to raise money for a Tougaloo College dormitory, and the family settling in the Montclair area of New Jersey for two years to raise that money where the wealth was.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: It Was Just a Political Alliance
clips.summary: Pressed on whether Millard and Morris Dees were sympathetic to the Klan, she explains it was a political alliance like the Lions or Kiwanis Club rather than membership, says she does not know whether they defended white men who attacked the Freedom Riders, and recalls that Bob Zellner graduated from Huntingdon while she, married and living at home, was not active on campus.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Ethics and Philosophy Broadened My Mind
clips.summary: She reflects on Huntingdon College's strong Christian emphasis, the required Old and New Testament, ethics, and philosophy courses that taught her to examine values, her sense that Black students were likely priced out rather than barred, and the later effort to recruit a more diverse student body after she left.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
