clips.title: Feeding the Army of Marchers
clips.summary: Returning after a recording break, she recounts promising her parents she would finish her degree, enrolling at Huntingdon full-time after their first baby, feeling guilty that exams and children left the Selma marchers to fend for themselves at her home, and not grasping the event's significance until later.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Briars in the Cotton Patch
clips.summary: She describes Koinonia Farm's suffering for its sympathy to Black people in the area, the community where everyone ate together and was treated on the same social level, and the Fullers' call after the Tougaloo dormitory to move there and work with Clarence Jordan, pointing to her daughter's documentary that tells the story of the fires in the cotton patch.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Newspapers Tacked to the Walls
clips.summary: She recounts visiting sharecropper shacks with holes in the roofs and floors and newspaper for insulation, the idea to start a partnership housing movement on Koinonia's two thousand acres selling no-profit, no-interest cinder-block houses to families who could not get bank loans, the Johnson family the first to move in, and how the growing neighborhood led her to set up a child development center and start partnership industries in sewing and pottery.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: From an African Trial to a Chicken Barn
clips.summary: She tells how the family moved to Africa for three years to test whether the housing philosophy would work overseas, building about a hundred twenty houses on the same no-profit, no-interest terms funded by churches and a Lilly grant, then returning to find Koinonia's leadership declining the worldwide vision and giving the Fullers part of a chicken barn where, running a law office and Habitat side by side, they built the foundation that grew Habitat for Humanity International into a hundred countries.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: What Have You Got Up Your Sleeve
clips.summary: Describing Koinonia in the summer of 1968 at a low ebb with Clarence Jordan considering leaving, she recounts Millard's note asking what Clarence had up his sleeve, Clarence's reply that maybe God had something up his sleeve for both of them, and the brainstorming weekend that produced the Koinonia Partnership of housing, industry, and farming.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: No Progress in Three Generations
clips.summary: She recalls moving into the apartment Clarence and Florence Jordan had built in the forties, her parents climbing the rickety stairs from their thirteen-room Montgomery house and luxury cars, her mother's dismay at no progress in three generations, and the thrilling sense of a richer life on the cutting edge of something significant.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Like I Was a Leper
clips.summary: She describes the hippie-movement volunteers arriving from the north in long hair and tattered jeans, the shared cars for trips into town, townspeople recoiling when she said she lived at Koinonia, and the earlier story of Conrad Browne arrested for walking down the street sharing a bag of peanuts with a Black resident.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: A Boy Who Wanted to Be a Doctor
clips.topic: The Unadilla Family and the Documentary
clips.summary: She begins the story of a young man in Unadilla who challenged the segregated schools to win a place in the white high school so he could reach medical school, drawing the Fullers to drive over and show support, then turns to her daughter Faith Fuller's hour-long documentary Briars in the Cotton Patch, released in 2003, which Faith researched, funded, and produced to tell Koinonia's story.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Greek Scripture Against Georgia Culture
clips.summary: She recounts Koinonia's founding by Clarence and Florence Jordan with Martin and Mabel England on poor red Georgia clay, Clarence's agriculture degree from the University of Georgia and seminary training in Louisville, his boyhood in Talbotton seeing restrictions on his Black friends, and his conviction as a Greek New Testament scholar that scripture did not match the culture he was raised in.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: You Couldn't Be a Christian Community
clips.summary: She describes the early Koinonia traditions of shared noon and evening meals and worship in simple structures with no church building, the visitor who found Clarence greasy under a tractor and doubted a community with no church could be Christian, and her observation that most Koinonia residents came from outside the South while the Black families were local.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: They Set Her House on Fire
clips.summary: She recounts the Black families threatened with burned houses and killed relatives if they kept coming to Koinonia, some organizing and moving north, and the story of Miss Gussie Jackson of the candy kitchen whose house was set on fire while she ran out and prayed on her knees, with many families drifting away in fear by the time the Fullers arrived.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: What Are You Doing at This House
clips.summary: Resuming the interrupted Unadilla story, she recounts taking Millard and baby Faith with strawberries to visit the family, waiting on the porch as the children came home from school, and a policeman roaring up with his gun, demanding to know their business and citing a city ordinance against whites visiting Black homes, which Millard called not worth the paper it was written on.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: You Got to Arrest Me Too
clips.summary: She tells how she walked down from the porch holding Faith and insisted that if her husband was arrested she must be too, the wait at the city hall jail while the commissioners conferred, her worry about diapers, Faith plunking the piano keys, and the officials letting them go for a first offense while warning they were not responsible once the family left town.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Calling Churches From a Motel Room
clips.summary: She describes checking into a motel and seeing a van full of men pull up, hiding in the bathroom with Faith, Millard phoning churches from the yellow pages while the Episcopal minister begged off for a ladies' tea, and the county sheriff finally coming, contacting the FBI, and calming things down as Clarence drove out to follow them home.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Just Go Right Ahead and Shoot
clips.summary: Asked about self-defense, she says Koinonia kept only one gun for wild dogs and was wholly against returning violence for violence, recalls cars firing into the farm and a bullet that shattered a mirror inches from a Jordan daughter, and the residents taking turns sitting at the road at night to show they were not afraid, all detailed in the Briars documentary.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Crying Over the Schools in Plains
clips.summary: She describes her children attending a county school, then an American school in Kinshasa, and on return public school in Plains where Faith was in fourth grade with Amy Carter under secret service watch while Chris and Kim came home crying over the dilapidated white school that later became the Carter Museum, and her belief that the local schools did not integrate until the late seventies, shortly after the family returned from Africa in 1976.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: The Girls Who Were Jailed
clips.summary: She reflects on learning Americus civil rights history from local historian Alan Anderson who wrote a book on Sumter County, recalls meeting one of the girls famously arrested and jailed in Americus at Koinonia's seventieth anniversary celebration, and begins recounting a 1965 visit to friends who had been pushed out of a Birmingham church.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: We Stayed a Month Instead of Two Hours
clips.summary: She tells how the Fullers, having decided to leave business and give away their money, looked up Allen and Carol Henry at Koinonia in December 1965, met Clarence Jordan, were amazed by the community and its simple life, and stayed a month, during which Carol's father, head of the Georgia Baptist Convention, invited the group to a Christmas service at the all-white First Baptist Church in Americus.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Filing Into First Baptist
clips.summary: She recounts about twenty of them, including Clarence, the Fullers, and Collins McGee the only Black person willing to go, filing single file into the crowded First Baptist Church, a deacon's reaction when a Black hand reached for a program, and the little ladies in the surrounding pews getting up and moving away until the group sat as an island on one pew.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Dark on the Inside
clips.summary: She describes the head deacon, the town fire chief, grabbing Collins McGee and ordering him out, Clarence saying they would all leave and replying that he thought it was God's church, Clarence outside remarking on the floodlit white columns that it was all light outside and dark on the inside, the armed deacons of those years, and the later change as Black worshippers became welcome in nearly any church in town.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Broken Glass on the Driveway
clips.summary: She credits Habitat's stream of visitors from across the country and world with broadening local attitudes, then recounts buying a house on Church Street, neighbors forbidding their children to play with hers, broken glass on the driveway each morning, two house fires the week they moved in, and the slamming car doors that frightened her one night Millard was away.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Blitz Builds and Changed Hearts
clips.summary: She describes Habitat spreading to Immokalee, Appalachia, and Johns Island, the blitz builds raising ten or twenty houses in a week with hundreds of volunteers, volunteers attending local churches and inviting friends, and the work gradually opening hearts as people saw it was about putting God's love into action.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: A Quarter Million Dollars a Day
clips.summary: She recounts the early struggle that raised about seventy thousand dollars the first year before the Carters got involved in 1984, the growth to a quarter million dollars a day and over nine hundred employees by the time the Fullers left in 2005, the board's later move of headquarters to Atlanta, and a teacher noting how children's grades and behavior improved after moving into a Habitat house.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: We Kept Our Focus on Housing
clips.summary: Asked about the co-op movement and War on Poverty programs of the era, she says Habitat kept its focus on housing despite temptations to broaden, traces the international goal to a desire to eliminate poverty housing in the world, and recounts the six-week trip to five African countries, Tanzania, Rhodesia, South Africa, Ghana, and the Congo, where Millard's promise to raise money for a dump truck for a church-run block business led the family to choose that place to build houses.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Appropriate Technologies
clips.summary: She reflects on her adventurous spirit and simple upbringing, the skills learned at Koinonia of making yogurt and bread, reading about each new country's culture, and using appropriate technologies and local materials with manual labor to keep house costs down, often staying with families who had moved into Habitat houses in Sri Lanka, South Korea, and Fiji.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Free to Flood Into the Cities
clips.summary: Asked about anti-colonial movements, she recalls that around their first 1965 visit Belgian businessmen had left Congo after independence as crowds freed from pass laws flooded into the cities, making the housing need overwhelming, and that their hundred twenty houses were a drop in the bucket that nonetheless spread to other parts of Zaire and Africa.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: I Was Bigger Than How I Was Treated
clips.summary: She notes apartheid still strong when Habitat began in South African townships with job training to let people afford payments, and recounts back at Koinonia a farmer churning his horse through a new family's yard in anger and farmer Ethel Dunning's employers cutting off her water for months when she built her own house, then asking her to bake their Christmas cakes, which she did to show she was bigger than how she was treated.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: A Congregational Church in Their Home
clips.summary: She recounts starting a Congregational, later United Church of Christ, congregation in their Montgomery home that grew to about twenty people, met in the Huntingdon College chapel, then built a small building, with Allen Henry among the visiting ministers, and how after the Fullers left the church died and they later joined Maranatha Baptist Church when Jimmy Carter twisted Millard's arm.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: The 1971 Black Boycott
clips.summary: She describes growing aware at Koinonia of serious police brutality against Black people by an all-white force and sheriff, the volatile time of integration, and the 1971 Americus boycott when Black residents refused to do business in town and marched downtown, the Fullers walking with them through streets where frightened white people peered from store windows.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Some of You Black Folks Ought to Step Up
clips.summary: She recounts a riled-up church meeting where Millard calmed the crowd toward focused change and urged Black residents to apply for law-enforcement jobs, the rejections that let them file a legal case and eventually get hired, a woman turned down as too pretty whom Millard got hired, and his growing involvement in death-row cases of Black men accused of murder.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Getting a Jury to Pray With Him
clips.topic: Death Penalty Defense and Leaving the Law
clips.summary: She explains how prosecutors struck Black jurors by asking whether they believed in the death penalty, producing all-white juries, Millard's courtroom tactics in partnership with an Atlanta attorney including getting a jury to bow and pray over a sleeping juror to win life sentences, the Black Atlanta lawyer who worked for the EEOC on death-penalty inequities, and how Millard eventually gave up law as Habitat grew at a phenomenal rate.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Shunned by Our Own Families
clips.topic: Faith, Fear, and the Cost Among Kin
clips.summary: Reflecting on the 1971 march, she says she felt some fear but knew it was right after years at Koinonia, affirms her Christian faith intersected deeply with the civil rights movement, and recounts gaining far more friends than she lost while being shunned by most family members on both sides who thought giving away their money meant a mental institution, her mother avoiding controversy and her sister, who had moved to liberal California, the supportive exception.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: The Trajectory of Morris Dees
clips.topic: Dees and the Southern Poverty Law Center
clips.summary: She traces Morris Dees following a similar path without family, founding the Southern Poverty Law Center, starting by integrating the Alabama state patrol and moving into representing people harmed by the Klan and other groups, and says she thinks his change traced back to the Freedom Rides though contact was sparse as the Fullers worked in Africa and Habitat.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Everybody Had a Piece of the Puzzle
clips.summary: Asked how God's plan, practical work, and the civil rights movement fit together, she answers that everyone contributed a piece of the puzzle to make slow societal change, that the movement spurred other freedom movements for women, minorities, and the mentally ill, and recalls hearing Andrew Young tell of the Birmingham Children's Crusade of May 1963 when demonstrators sat down and the fire hoses were eventually turned off, the most significant thing being people putting their lives on the line.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Getting Down to a Human Level
clips.summary: She describes relating to people worldwide without sharing a language through gestures about children and grandchildren, likens it to the Carters meeting world statesmen on a common human level, and recalls Millard making crowds in Africa go berserk by dancing and making a fool of himself, unlike the pinstripe-suited dignitaries who ordered people around.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: They Never Meet a Stranger
clips.summary: She reflects on how living at Koinonia and three years in Africa shaped her four children, a son whose international upbringing won him a job leading children for Saudi Aramco, their work with campus Habitat chapters and the Fuller Center for Housing the Fullers started in 2005, and a home-builder daughter in Texas who led a Habitat build, each carrying the work forward in their own way.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: The Daughter of a Corporate Lawyer
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop at her Washington, D.C. home in June 2013, Lisa Anderson Todd describes growing up in 1950s suburban Summit, New Jersey, the daughter of corporate lawyer Carl Anderson and a psychiatric social worker mother, with two older brothers and a Republican father who was wary of signing documents during the McCarthy scare.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: The Iron Range and Hibbing High
clips.summary: Todd traces her father's poor upbringing as one of six children in Hibbing, Minnesota, and her mother's benefit from the wealth of the Iron Ore Mines, which gave Hibbing a model high school with a swimming pool, before her mother moved from Hibbing junior college to the University of Minnesota.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: I Don't Want a Vacation
clips.summary: Todd recounts a summer with the Experiment in International Living in northern Sweden, following her older brother's American Friends Service Committee example, and deciding she wanted to do something rather than vacation, signing up for two 1963 work camps including an Ecumenical Voluntary Service project at Tougaloo College.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's Home
clips.summary: At Bob Moses's invitation the group visited the SNCC office in Greenwood on August 1, 1963, and a few of them had dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's home in Ruleville, where she told the story of the beating in Winona, then attended a mass meeting Todd found emotionally overwhelming.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: 803 People Registered in Greensboro
clips.summary: Todd describes moving on to a second American Friends Service Committee project in Greensboro, North Carolina, where eighteen volunteers lived in a church basement and did house-to-house canvassing in a receptive neighborhood with cooperative registrars, registering 803 people, mostly Democrats.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Special in Mississippi, Free in Greensboro
clips.summary: Todd contrasts being a feared, conspicuous white person in Mississippi, seated on stage at a Jackson mass meeting addressed by Spottswood W. Robinson III, with the freedom of Greensboro, where late-night parties let her get to know black peers and concluded that relationships across the color line were no big deal between individuals though historically loaded.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Why Children Marched in Birmingham
clips.summary: Returning to her 1963 Tougaloo experience, Todd recalls CORE doing the Jackson work, asking Dave Dennis why children were used in the Birmingham demonstrations and hearing they should learn to stand up for freedom early, and meeting figures like Memphis Norman and Rev. Ed King, whose scarred face she connected to his sacrifice as a white southerner.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: An Engine on Fire to Jackson
clips.summary: Todd describes reading of Medgar Evers's assassination in the New York Times, then flying to Jackson alone and seeing her plane's engine catch fire and turn back, a moment of truth before she continued, joining a group with black students Ivory Phillips and Thelma Sadberry and hearing evening speakers including Timothy Jenkins and Jane Schutt.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Outsiders Versus the Community
clips.summary: Todd recounts the July to August 1963 lull in Jackson as the federal government pushed the movement from direct action toward less confrontational voter registration, the NAACP halting bail for arrested demonstrators and being criticized as outsiders, Charles Evers taking over, poor nonviolence-workshop turnout from students mistreated in jail, and the split between the local NAACP youth working with John Salter and Ed King and the national organization.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: He Wouldn't Talk to Us
clips.summary: Todd recalls Bob Moses on the Tougaloo campus declining to address the group because he had to leave, her annoyance when he turned up at a Student Center dance instead, and how his invitation to see the SNCC headquarters in Greenwood naturally followed, where the Delta work was being run.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: A Short, Fat Woman with a Captivating Voice
clips.topic: The Power of Fannie Lou Hamer
clips.summary: Todd remembers having no idea who Fannie Lou Hamer was when Hamer cooked them dinner in Ruleville, then being struck by her captivating speech about her water bill rising and her home being shot into, her shade-tree parable, and the SNCC men like Charles McLaurin who came over from Greenwood.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: A Sorority That Pledged Jewish Girls
clips.summary: Todd describes her difficult adjustment from an all-girls day school to Cornell, finding a diverse group when she pledged a sorority that rushed Jewish girls against national policy and cost Kappa Alpha Theta its charter after she left, and being an overachieving American Studies government major who took a race relations sociology course from Robin Williams that she thinks helped steer her toward the two summer work projects.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Choosing Tougaloo Over Brownsville
clips.summary: Todd explains choosing Tougaloo over a Texas project because the civil rights situation was in the news, framing 1963 as learning about the movement rather than activism, her supportive parents who let her go on her own savings, and her father's United Negro College Fund and Lutheran Church involvements.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Back at Cornell a Different Person
clips.summary: Todd describes returning to Cornell a different person, becoming active with Cornell United Religious Work under Paul Gibbons, putting out a newsletter and working for passage of the Civil Rights Act, and recalls meeting Jane Stembridge at Tougaloo working on literacy books, who ensured the group received SNCC literature to start friends-of-SNCC chapters Todd never formed, and who remembered Bob Moses as a saint in the summer of 1960.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: A Letter from Walter Tillow
clips.summary: Todd describes lobbying on the Civil Rights Act during a spring Washington trip, then applying to work for SNCC in fall 1963 and receiving a letter from Walter Tillow describing a planned project of Freedom Schools, legal resources, and voter registration, with a strange note that they might find a way to pay her.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Where's Mother? She's Not Feeling Well
clips.summary: Todd recounts weighing becoming a civil rights worker against law school after taking the LSATs, debating during the summer whether to stay in Mississippi, and coming home by bus to Summit where her father said her mother was upset, prompting her to choose Stanford, after three students recruiting MFDP support told a reporter she planned to stay and her mother read it in the paper, a strain eased by a parents group and the Sheffield family whose daughter Nancy volunteered in Greenville.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: To Be There Would Be Important
clips.summary: Todd describes arriving late to the Oxford orientation after a friend's wedding, riding with Cornell students bound for the Fayette County Tent City project, hearing Bob Moses in his overalls tell volunteers not to expect to accomplish much but to write congressmen and newspapers, and being lectured on nonviolence by Vincent Harding and Bruce Hanson before the role-playing on the lawn where Newsweek's Carl Fleming punched her in the stomach as she played victim.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: The Day I Arrived in Mississippi
clips.summary: Todd describes the disappearance of Chaney, Schwerner, and Goodman the Saturday she arrived, her fear that they would all be victimized, and ending up assigned to voter registration in Greenville, a relatively moderate city, partly because her Greensboro experience suited her for it and the fields were too dangerous for women elsewhere.
related_events: murder of Chaney Schwerner and Goodman

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: I Felt That I Had Status
clips.summary: Todd reflects on feeling privileged in voter registration for the access it gave her to black adults and politics, compared with the freedom school teachers, all white women, working with children, and names canvassing partners including Pam Trotman, a Howard graduate recruited by Stokely Carmichael, and older men Charles Moore and James Williams.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Who Was the Project Director?
clips.summary: Todd describes confusion over the Greenville project director, recalling George Rawick of the University of Chicago for the first month and thinking Charlie Cobb directed it, only to learn through research and from Dottie that Muriel Tillinghast was project director, with Cobb directing Washington, Sharkey, and Issaquena counties while traveling the state.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: I Felt Safe in the Black Community
clips.summary: Todd describes feeling safe and less at risk in Greenville, moving freely in the black community and never going to the white side, canvassing all day in the heat, and the significance of living with a black family after first sleeping on the Freedom House floor, sharing quarters with Valerie and Lynn who worked in research.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Don't Use Too Much Water
clips.summary: Todd recounts a widow who asked them to leave for fear of hosting three white women, moving to the independent Mrs. Harden whose neighbor said Uncle Toms wanted to run her out of church, then to Jerry and Lamar Britton's air-conditioned brick house, an open home where she shared a room with Valerie Horton and Lynn Hamilton.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: A Clipboard and a Freedom Registration Form
clips.summary: Todd describes no fixed routine, dividing the city at the office, the transportation problem that meant walking or catching rides with black taxi drivers, and how her canvassing shifted from neutral responses about courthouse registration to signing people up for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, which required only a signature, not literacy.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: This Form Is Not Going Anywhere
clips.summary: Todd contrasts the deterrent of official registration, with its literacy tests, constitutional interpretation, and two weeks of name publication, against the privacy of freedom registration, explaining people signed when assured the form would stay private, that Pat handled the returned forms, and that black-and-white canvassing teams avoided deference to a white authority figure.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: In Charge of a Precinct
clips.summary: Todd recalls the rumor of a white volunteer who left pregnant with a black volunteer, the six Greenville precincts where she ran one with a workshop at Mr. Williams's house drawing twenty people, and the county convention where local Democrat Mr. Chappell failed to install his chairman but moved that all present precinct delegates become county delegates, mixing traditional leaders with strong MFDP people while women resented that establishment figures stepped in only at the end.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Women More Able to Stand Up
clips.summary: Todd names strong Greenville leaders working with SNCC and the FDP including Dorothy Jones, who worked for the Hodding Carter family, Edna Morton, and Willie Rollins, and reflects, as a matter historians consider in retrospect, that women were more involved because men risked being lynched while women were more able to act for civil rights.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Fannie Lou Hamer Led the Singing
clips.summary: Todd describes the Delta's second congressional district caucus held at Greenville's Friendship Baptist Church, a former movie theater, where Fannie Lou Hamer led the singing and gave Todd a big hug recalling her 1963 home visit, and explains the MFDP was built to parallel the regular Mississippi Democratic Party under matching state-law procedures.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: A Recommended List, Not Yet Delegates
clips.summary: Todd recalls her enthusiasm seeing people participate in politics, explains the district caucus produced a recommended delegate list for the state convention that would elect the 68 national delegates, and that she was pleased Mr. Chappell was not recommended though he got nominated by another district and still failed to be elected to Atlantic City.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Not Hippie Scruffy Kids
clips.topic: Mrs. Britton and the Percy Library Oral Histories
clips.summary: Todd relays Mrs. Britton's recorded view that the volunteers were nice rather than scruffy, her worry for them coming home at night, her husband's death that fall when the project sent notes, and the roughly 1978 oral histories in Greenville's Percy Memorial Library, done she thinks by Daisy Bates, that mostly interviewed schoolteachers.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Finding New Voices
clips.summary: Todd clarifies she was not at the state convention though she long thought she was, then relays Charlie Cobb's instruction to work around established leaders to find new voices, which she later understood as empowerment, and Martin Luther King's idea of the beloved community as the goal of nonviolence and reconciliation, words they never used at the time.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: This Nonviolence Stuff Is Not Working
clips.summary: Todd recalls militant young men on the Greenville project, apparently SNCC staff assigned by COFO Jackson, who spoke of anger and self-defense and disparaged Bob Moses as on his way out, and a first-week demonstration at the federal building, instigated by the local Mississippi Student Union, where she prepared to be arrested but nothing happened.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: That Couldn't Be True
clips.summary: Todd describes Bob Moses in Oxford disbelieving that Greenville had demonstrated despite instructions not to, the two militant men's push to picket the black swimming pool and later again at the federal building for protection, the dilemma of supporting local people who wanted their support, and one man phoning the police chief that they would arrive in fifteen minutes.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: What Did We Do for Fun?
clips.topic: Cafe Talk and a Doctor's Picnic
clips.summary: Todd answers what they did for fun, sharing beer and movement talk in cafes apart from Charlie Cobb, who never joined them, a July 4th barbecue picnic at Dr. Frisbee's home for thirty-five volunteers among Greenville's established black middle class, and the time Dorothy Jones invited them to swim in Hodding Carter's pool while the family was away.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Men on Horseback and a Revolver in the Drawer
clips.summary: Todd describes the whole project swimming in Hodding Carter's pool in their clothes until two or three men rode in on horseback to protect the Carters, the party disbanding, and Barbara and Nancy staying overnight to find the house an arsenal with a revolver in the nightstand, an event everyone including Charlie Cobb remembered.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: A Black Armband at Coleman High
clips.summary: Todd tells how she pieced together that Johnny Frazier, who addressed her 1963 group as head of the Greenville NAACP Youth Chapter, had worn a black armband to Coleman High School over the Brown decision and was suspended, with Hodding Carter writing an editorial, and was readmitted only when his aunt threatened to sue to integrate the white school.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Thank You for What You Did
clips.summary: Todd recounts reuniting with Johnny Frazier at a reunion sponsored by his daughter Nishani Frazier, his bear hug thanking her, and meeting Dorie Ladner around 1994 or 1995 at a Biograph Theater showing of Freedom on My Mind where Heather Booth greeted Senator Paul Simon, with Ladner thanking her for being a 1964 summer volunteer.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Freelance on the Boardwalk
clips.summary: Todd describes deciding to attend the convention because Atlantic City was near Summit, driving down with Pat and missing Fannie Lou Hamer's Saturday testimony, sleeping at the Union Baptist Temple church where the MFDP met, and working freelance walking the boardwalk to urge delegates to vote for the MFDP without seeking an official role.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Ella Baker Ran the Lobbying
clips.summary: Todd explains she later learned Ella Baker organized the state-delegation lobbying from the MFDP Washington office, with help from the woman who wrote Rauh's brief and Charlene Cranston Young arranging transportation at the Gem Hotel, while she and Pat never went there and instead attended open meetings at the church through the Tuesday credentials decision.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: There Was No Compromise
clips.summary: Todd recounts standing in an aisle hearing prominent people urge acceptance of what they called the compromise, arguing there was no compromise because there were not two sides reaching agreement, that she knew the MFDP should be seated, that few delegates spoke in the open discussion, and that the no vote came in a closed room of only the 68 delegates.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: King Was Hushed Out
clips.topic: The Church Meetings and a Confidential Caucus
clips.summary: Todd describes the sequence after the credentials decision: Joseph Rauh recommending the delegates support the offer, Martin Luther King not allowed to speak on Tuesday afternoon, Edith Green and other supporters meeting separately and agreeing to keep discussions confidential, and ultimately filing no minority report because Rauh decided he lacked the numbers.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: To Find Out Why We Lost
clips.topic: Beginning the Book and the Research
clips.summary: Todd explains retiring on April 1, 2006 to write something more interesting, advised by writer friends to produce a fast memoir from her transcribed letters and diary, her real aim being to understand the MFDP convention challenge and why they lost after leaving disillusioned, and how her research moved from Taylor Branch to Anne Romaine's oral interviews at UNC and the LBJ Library, where the Johnson tapes were eye-opening, into a 250-page paper criticized as repetitive that John Dittmer advised needed footnotes.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Johnson Did Not Want a Race Election
clips.summary: Todd lays out her research findings on why Johnson opposed seating the Freedom Democrats: beyond the traditional fear of losing southern electoral votes to Barry Goldwater, the Rochester and Harlem riots had made backlash part of the lexicon, and Johnson, advised by Governor John Connally and worried about his Texas votes after the Civil Rights Act, did not want the election to be about race.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: He Was Not One Hundred Percent for the Freedom Democrats
clips.summary: Todd assesses Joseph Rauh as critically important, having structured the credentials hearing around Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony and won the large ballroom, but serving two roles, the MFDP and his own standing in the Democratic Party, and astoundingly offering to withdraw yet advising against it lest Kunstler and Kinoy take over and he lose control.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: They Did Not Want a Floor Fight
clips.topic: A Vehicle to Reform the Party
clips.summary: Todd explains the National Democratic Party's overriding fear of a floor vote that would expose its northern pro-civil-rights and southern segregationist wings, Rauh's wish to reform the party using the Freedom Democratic Party as a vehicle to force change, and Walter Reuther's early lack of enthusiasm as a Johnson ally who saw the political problem.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: The Names Kept Changing
clips.summary: Todd recounts Courtland Cox's openness about Congressman Charles Diggs requesting the list of credentials committee members supporting the MFDP, Bob Moses's reluctance, the roughly fifteen names handed over, overnight calls threatening supporters, and Johnson aides who pegged every member exactly, with documentation of LBJ calling the governor of Iowa and a Colorado reporter and pressure on a New York delegate who was Mayor Wagner's secretary and a black woman from Maryland, all of which she set up her book to tell day by day to give the Freedom Democrats their due credit.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: It All Happened Because of Reuther
clips.summary: Todd argues Hubert Humphrey acted only on Monday, pressuring state delegations to drop a roll-call vote to please Johnson and secure his vice presidency, but it was Walter Reuther, arriving Tuesday in the night and working the Mondale subcommittee, whose manipulation produced the symbolic at-large two-seat offer by holding two meetings at once so the credentials committee voted while the leadership was tied up, refusing to negotiate, with the contested claim that Aaron Henry and Ed King accepted the offer and Bayard Rustin conducting the leadership meeting attended by King and Andrew Young.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: He Was Ready to Cut It Off
clips.summary: Todd presents her finding that Johnson's press conference was scheduled the day before Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, contradicting the gospel from Rauh via Anne Romaine that he called it hurriedly, so he was ready to cut off the televised testimony, and reflects that she needs to settle her main points before talking publicly about the book.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Six Hundred People at the Church
clips.summary: Todd describes wanting to set the historical record straight, then a fundraiser at the Central Presbyterian Church in Summit where she and a neighbor who volunteered in Drew addressed 600 people to fund the Herbert Lee Memorial Freedom Center, which she helped organize by enlisting the established Greenville leaders she had called Uncle Toms, the center taking shape with Delta Ministry and volunteer lawyers, young doctor Matthew Page as president and Charles Moore, later a longtime city councilman, as vice president.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Across the Country to Stanford
clips.summary: Todd describes driving to Stanford law school, living in graduate housing with Karen Davis whose younger sister Linda taught in a Ruleville Freedom School, joining the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council, and applying to return to Mississippi as a law clerk before being reassigned from the Lawyers' Committee for sounding militant and failing to defer to lawyers she found naive, admitting she disassociated from the movement during law school out of fear of flunking out as her brother had at Minnesota, reading only the San Francisco Chronicle.
keywords: Law Students Civil Rights Research Council

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Lisa, Get the Gun
clips.summary: Todd describes her 1965 reassignment to the National Lawyers Guild, living in Tougaloo with Claudia Shropshire Morcom and clerking for volunteer lawyers evaluating Title III public facilities county by county in places like Madison County, Greenwood, Greenville, and Jackson, and Morcom's later story of yelling for a gun they did not have at a feared peeping Tom.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Red Baiting to Be Dismissed
clips.topic: Returning to Greenville as a Lawyer
clips.summary: Todd recalls Bob Moses's orientation lecture that red-baiting was past and the movement would accept help from anyone, her own dismissal of being called communist during the summer, and how far she had disassociated, returning to Greenville for legal work without looking anyone up or asking about the Herbert Lee Center she had helped found.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: The Atmosphere of the Agency Changed
clips.summary: Todd describes clerking at the Office of Economic Opportunity, choosing Washington over New York, and her first job at the United Planning Organization, a Community Action Agency writing delegate-agency contracts, where within two years she felt the effects of black power and grew less welcome as a white worker under executive director Wiley Branton, whom she admired, and resigned.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: A Board of Contract Appeals Judge
clips.topic: Government Contracts and the Rule of Law
clips.summary: Todd traces entering government contracts law with Trovon Bower and Morgan, Lewis and Bockius, then qualifying under the Contract Disputes Act of 1978 to become an administrative judge on the NASA Board of Contract Appeals with worldwide jurisdiction, hearing contractor disputes in full trial-type proceedings, and reflects that after years discussing nonviolence and civil disobedience her judicial obligation was to apply the rule of law, offering a fair hearing even when a victimized contractor's situation cried out and in the end she had to say no.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: A Whole New World
clips.summary: Todd describes getting the book idea from a friend who saw her large civil rights library, how revisiting the Mississippi summer project through the Veterans of the Mississippi Civil Rights Movement conferences in Jackson opened a whole new world, being meticulously mentored over tea by Mary King, and her view of the legacy: that Freedom Summer did not affect the 1964 Civil Rights Act, done before they arrived, but contributed to the Voting Rights Act and party reform she traces toward the 2008 election of Obama.
keywords: Veterans of the Mississippi Civil Rights Movement

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: We Made a Difference
clips.topic: Legacy and a Message for the Young
clips.summary: Todd insists the work was done by many, the SNCC activists in real danger and the roughly 500 college-age volunteers rather than the often-cited thousand, that things are better and young people should not be cynical about a dysfunctional Congress, and offers what she would tell students: that change comes when a single person finds a passion and reaches out to build a larger group, what might be called community organizing, recalling one high school history class that lit up when she read from her diary.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: I Didn't Think You Asked If I Was Scared
clips.topic: Fear, Safety, and the VFW Knife
clips.summary: Todd answers that she was scared at the beginning but not when she left Oxford, unsettled by cars circling the Freedom House that turned out to be police, and reassured by her Greenville assignment where no Klan or citizens council was allowed to organize, naming her standard worry as pickup trucks with rifle racks at night and her closest brush with violence at a VFW fundraiser dance sponsored by Charles Moore, the only white woman there, when one man pulled a knife after she danced with another, before the interview closes with thanks.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Born Looking Up at the Bottom
clips.topic: Birth and Family in Calhoun County
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop in a Southwest Atlanta library branch in 2013, Lonnie C. King opens with his roots in Arlington, Georgia, in Calhoun County, his mother Bertha Lee Smith King, one of seventeen children, his father L.C. King Sr., their divorce when he was two, and his line that he was born not on the bottom but looking up at it.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Evangelist Who Wrote on My Slate
clips.summary: King credits his grandfather, the evangelist Reverend Joseph P. Smith, a note singer with perfect pitch who raised him until he was eight and taught him to read before school, invoking John Locke on the first six years writing on a child's blank slate, including the lesson that one day they would have to change the system.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: What Is a Nigger, Granddaddy
clips.summary: He recounts picking up a leaflet dropped by a crop duster campaigning for Eugene Talmadge in 1942, asking his grandfather what the slur meant, and the grandfather pulling him onto his knee to explain racism and to say they were still in a form of slavery, only allowed to go home at night, and that the NAACP would one day set them free.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Rattlesnake in the Cornfield
clips.summary: King describes his grandfather as a sharecropper farming ten rented acres in Southwest Georgia and Northeast Alabama, never sure he broke even, tells the anecdote of running from a rattlesnake in the cornfield and not being beaten for it, and the lesson always to tell the truth because a lie has to be remembered.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Joining the Church at Eight
clips.summary: Sent to Atlanta at eight after his grandfather's death, King recalls joining Ebenezer Baptist Church the first night of the spring revival as Martin Luther King Sr. opened the doors, founding the Ebenezer Youth Organization, sharing a paper route with A.D. King, and watching the young Martin Luther King Jr. practice preaching upstairs on Thursday nights.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Look Out for My Son
clips.summary: He describes his mother holding him back a grade and taking him at eight to the Butler Street YMCA, organized in the 1890s, and how, learning from an eighty-five-year-old man named Eugene Thomas while researching his dissertation, he found that his mother had asked director J.D. Winston to keep the boy until six o'clock when she got home from her job as a maid.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: We All Chipped In Fifty Cents
clips.summary: King recalls the trust of an era when parents did not fear for their children, his mother checking secretly on his behavior, and his shock on learning from Eugene Thomas that there was no budget for the fifty cents a day he was paid as a boy: the senior staff had each chipped in fifty cents to pay the ten-year-old.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Kitty Cruise
clips.summary: Unable to afford Morehouse tuition after a partial scholarship, King describes entering the Navy on a short Kitty Cruise just before turning eighteen, the boot-camp role as educational petty officer for his company, and being assigned with the only other low-scoring sailor to the deck force to chip paint and scrub while the nineteen white men got skilled jobs.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Be the Best Ditch Digger Ever Born
clips.summary: He tells of being assigned to clean the head for two hundred deck-force sailors and remembering Dr. Mays's charge to do any job better than anybody but God, so he requisitioned paint and brass polish and made the restroom so bright that a petty officer petitioned to take the easy job from him and put him back on the deck force.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: We Don't Want Any Negroes Down Here
clips.topic: The Fight for the Disbursing Job
clips.summary: King recounts applying to be a disbursing clerk striker, being told by a Southern officer named Klaren that they wanted no Negroes there, and how a lieutenant from Louisiana named Horne listened to his story, took him to that same officer, and ordered him transferred, one white man telling another he was wrong.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Only One Who Passed
clips.summary: He describes the disbursing crew's cold silence until Welty Wolf of Seattle offered to teach him the work, John Bloom of Tempe joining to teach him to type, and his taking the November 1955 exam alongside four white third-class petty officers and being the only one to pass, after which the resentment broke open.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: I'm Going Back to Atlanta
clips.summary: King recalls telling a Navy friend, Everett Render, in 1957 that he was going back to Atlanta to finish Morehouse because the time was coming to throw off segregation and he wanted to be there, and his mother's earlier insistence to the recruiter that her son not become a see-going bus boy, which spared him steward duty but not the deck force.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Joe, Why Don't We Organize
clips.summary: He describes reading of the Greensboro Four sitting down on February 1, 1960, at Yates and Milton drugstore, recruiting his friend Joseph Pierce, and persuading a reluctant Julian Bond, a former Time magazine intern who could write, to sign on so the three of them could organize and tell the story.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Presidents Had Good Spies
clips.summary: King recalls building from the Morehouse football players and Clark's James Felder at a first meeting, then being summoned within days to Harkness Hall, where Rufus E. Clement and Benjamin Mays, leaning on Roy Wilkins and the NAACP, argued the students were sent to get an education, not start a revolution they could do later.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: I Think the Students Are Right
clips.summary: He contrasts the presidents who echoed Clement, Manley of Spelman and Brawley of Clark who said a sit-in would embarrass him, against Harry V. Richardson of the Interdenominational Theological Center, who broke the agreed front to back the students, joined by Frank Cunningham of Morris Brown, splitting the council and leaving King to speak for the students.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Write a Manifesto
clips.summary: King explains Clement's counter, that the students draft an intellectual manifesto for the public, which King read as a chess move to make philosophers of them rather than activists, and his choosing Roslyn Pope to edit it with Julian Bond and Charles A. Black, English majors who could write a great document.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: It's Show Time
clips.summary: He recounts Roslyn Pope calling the night before the deadline to say the committee had done nothing, his telling her it was show time, and her writing An Appeal for Human Rights and typing it at Howard Zinn's house, a document the presidents approved almost wholesale and published in the Atlanta papers, the New York Times, the Nation, and the Harvard Crimson, with Senator Jacob Javits reading it into the Congressional Record and a 1962 Miles College document essentially reprinting it with Birmingham swapped for Atlanta.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Divide and Conquer
clips.summary: King describes a rump group under a mathematics professor at Atlanta University trying to sit in first, and his appeal to Rufus E. Clement that separate sit-ins by Clark, AU, and Morehouse would let the merchants ask whom to negotiate with, arguing the common denominator was that all were African Americans facing the same discrimination downtown.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Dr. Cross, Sit Down
clips.summary: He recounts Clement convening all AU students at Sale Hall, backing a single coalition, and a professor jumping up three times to challenge King's right to lead until Clement told him to sit down, after which the man's contract was not renewed; the schools then formed a policy council of three representatives each who chose King as founding chairman.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Written by Somebody From Moscow
clips.topic: The State Under Siege, the Volcano Erupts
clips.summary: King reads Clement's slow-down as acceptance that events had overtaken the presidents, who feared for their tax status after Governor Ernest Vandiver claimed the appeal could only have come from Moscow and had Attorney General Eugene Cook check the HBCUs' nonprofit standing, and frames segregation as a volcano simmering below a cultural surface that the Greensboro sit-in made erupt, seventy thousand students raising sand across the South in one hundred twenty days, the postwar generation ideal shock troops whose distant parents could not be fired in reprisal.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Go Over to Spelman
clips.summary: King recalls struggling to organize beyond thirty or forty people between March and May 1960 and his mother telling him to get the Spelman women behind him because the Morehouse, Clark, and Morris Brown men would follow the girls, and an invitation from Albert Manley to debate Roy Wilkins at Spelman over whether to return to class.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: If Not Now, When
clips.summary: He describes the packed Spelman hall, Roy Wilkins arguing smoothly for the affirmative, and his own use of a public-speaking teacher's advice to damn with faint praise: lavishing true praise on Wilkins, then pivoting to ask, if not now, when, and if not us, who, winning the crowd and leading to the May 17 march of nearly four thousand.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: One Hundred Sixty-Nine Spelman Women
clips.summary: King says his research identified one hundred sixty-nine Spelman women who went to jail, more than any campus, with Morehouse second, Clark third, Morris Brown fourth, and argues the Spelman women were the backbone of the movement because they took up a job to be done without contesting who led it.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Massive March on the Capitol
clips.summary: King recounts announcing to the state NAACP a march on the Capitol for May 17, 1960, to commemorate the Brown decision, the Atlanta Constitution headline that sent the town wild, Governor Vandiver calling out the State Patrol with billy clubs and dogs, and Benjamin and Sadie Mays warning him through police chief Herbert Jenkins of a coming mob.
related_events: March on the Georgia State Capitol

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Never Sacrifice a Principle for Peace
clips.summary: King describes Mays asking him not to march for fear he would be hurt, his reply quoting Mays's own speech, never sacrifice a principle for peace, and Mays asking where he would end up, to which King said Wheat Street Baptist Church; Mays secretly followed and later wrote in Born to Rebel that he saw King had matured.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Walking With the Chief
clips.summary: King tells of marching on May 17 with thousands as chief Herbert Jenkins fell in beside him warning of danger, his decision to ask Jenkins for a direct order to turn at Whitehall Street so that one student beaten by a billy club could not hand the movement to its enemies, and how about two hundred marchers who got lost reached the Capitol, where Charles A. Black led them through menacing heckling without a beating, with young African American men he later spotted in photographs whom he suspects were a black vigilante group.
related_events: March on the Georgia State Capitol

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Doused With Acid in West End
clips.summary: King describes launching a grocery-store boycott, targeting Mann Brothers in West End where a hostile newspaper editorial had compared him to Hitler, and a white man of about fifty-seven lunging from a crowd of three hundred hecklers to douse him with an acid-like substance that burned his skin and clothes and splashed Charles A. Black and Frank Holloway.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Pool Hall Emptied
clips.summary: He recalls being refused water at one service station and helped at the next after the attack, hearing it reached the news, and how an honest black pool hall in West End emptied of men who ran to see what was happening, leaving him convinced Atlanta came close to a Birmingham-style conflagration that would have gone hard against blacks.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Nonviolence as a Tactic
clips.summary: King explains that his committee, unlike Nashville, ran no nonviolence workshops and held that nonviolence was a tactic, not a way of life, a practical and inexpensive approach, and begins to turn to the institutions of the Atlanta movement.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Slow Dollar and the Fast Dime
clips.summary: King recounts the hostile Atlanta Daily World, hiring Julian Bond to write a publication called the Student Movement and You, and the fall plan to boycott downtown with Rich's department store as the kingpin, applying economics lessons about the fast dime over the slow dollar and a Domino Theory framing, then sitting in at Rich's Magnolia Room in June 1960 with Howard Zinn's family and being taken by chief Jenkins not to jail but to meet Richard Rich, who boasted of his record with Negro customers and told King to take his students back to class, to which King promised to return in the fall with thousands.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Newspaper to Fight Back
clips.topic: M. Kasuth Hill and the Inquirer
clips.summary: King tells of a light-skinned black businessman, M. Kasuth Hill of Hill Office Supplies, coming to his office angry that the Atlanta Daily World was selling out human dignity for advertising, and proposing they start a newspaper using his offset press and Hill's rights to the Atlanta Inquirer name, with Julian Bond and the Gibson brothers forming its core for the first issue in July 1960.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: This Is Your Time to Get Hurt
clips.summary: He recounts boycotting Colonial and A&P stores, C.A. Scott of the Atlanta Daily World summoning him and spitting as he warned he would lose a nine-thousand-dollar Colonial ad if picketing continued, and King answering that in every revolution some people get hurt and this was Scott's time, then steering black retailers to move their ads to the Inquirer.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Letter From the Fulton County Jail
clips.topic: Hill, the Inquirer, and the Jail Letter
clips.summary: King spells out M. Kasuth Hill's name and lineage as son of the city's first African American doctor, credits himself and Julian Bond with writing the Inquirer's articles, and recalls writing a letter from the Fulton County jail during the October 1960 jailing when he and Martin King shared a cell as movement leaders.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Pennington the Cartoonist
clips.summary: King praises Maurice Pennington as an illustrator who, but for his race, would have ranked with Herbert Block, describes a coming AU Library exhibit of Pennington's thirty-odd cartoons and over a hundred movement photographs, and an agreement with Mrs. Pennington to lend the materials for five years.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: You Have to Get Organized
clips.summary: King recalls recommending to his committee that he ask Martin King to convene the student leaders into a region-wide organization, the meeting with Ella Baker, King, and a few students, and his argument, witnessed by Otis Moss Jr., that the South's segregationists were organized from Maryland to Texas passing near-identical laws, so the movement had to organize to beat them.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Battle Between Her and Me
clips.summary: He describes disagreeing with Ella Baker, who wanted a grassroots movement organization and used an Indian expression for her position, while Martin King agreed with King and put up five hundred dollars for Baker to arrange the Easter-weekend conference, held at Shaw University in Raleigh, to which six Atlanta students drove in a rented white Buick provided by Dr. Asa Yancey of the Atlanta Medical Society.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Junior Arm of SCLC
clips.summary: King recalls some three hundred people forming SNCC at Shaw, doubts King intended it as a junior arm of SCLC since the charismatic leader never raised it with him, and recounts the chairmanship battle when Nashville put forward Marion Barry, whom he had never heard of, against King's own nomination, expecting it would have been Diane Nash.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Citadel of Atlanta
clips.summary: He describes the compromise making Marion Barry temporary chairman while moving SNCC's headquarters to Atlanta, where the Atlanta group paid the rent for the first months, and argues Atlanta was the logical home as the citadel of civil rights thinking since W.E.B. Du Bois, a genius the city long failed to honor.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: They Overlooked the Lady
clips.summary: King calls Diane Nash the Nashville movement itself, articulate, committed, and the real leader the others followed, and says he never understood, except perhaps as sexism, how James Bevel, Marion Barry, and John Lewis leapfrogged over her, naming her a true unsung hero who risked her life.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Names From Other Towns
clips.summary: He runs through the leaders he had heard of beyond Atlanta, David Forbes at Shaw, Ed King expelled from Kentucky State, Marvin Robinson at Southern, Chuck McDew at Orangeburg, the students pushed out of Alabama State until Bernard Lee joined them in Atlanta, and later Hank Thomas and Reginald Robinson, while never meeting the four from Greensboro.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Written Out of the Story
clips.summary: King insists on remembering Ed King, expelled from Kentucky State, the second person after Jane Stembridge to staff the SNCC office and effectively run it, articulate, courageous, and close to Marion Wilson, whom he searched for only to find had died in 2012 in Prince George's County, and distinguishes him from the white Ed King of Mississippi.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Wait Until October the Nineteenth
clips.summary: King describes building the fall campaign of sit-ins and boycotts, noting Rich's later loss of ten million dollars, and battling A.D. King and Bernard Lee, who wanted to act immediately, while he insisted on waiting until October 19 to coincide with the presidential election, prevailing because the Spelman delegation under Herschelle Sullivan backed him.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: You Can't Lead From the Back
clips.topic: Persuading Martin King to Sit In
clips.summary: King recounts arranging for Martin Luther King Jr. to join the October 19 sit-in at Rich's, King hesitating over an existing probation from a DeKalb County arrest while his father and Wyatt Walker were on the phone, and King winning him over by invoking the elder King's own sermon that you cannot lead from the back, you have to lead from the front, and citing their long friendship since 1945.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: But for That Arrest
clips.topic: The Kennedy Call and the Election
clips.summary: King ties Martin King's arrest to a firestorm in the black community and the Kennedy intervention, the call to Coretta Scott King that switched votes in Illinois, Michigan, and beyond, and his belief that but for the arrest Nixon would have been president, noting Nixon had prepared but withheld a statement of support through E. Frederic Morrow rather than buck the boss.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Walkie-Talkie Chess Game
clips.summary: King credits a retired master sergeant he calls El Comandante with turning his ideas into a plan, and describes countering the merchants' tactic of merely closing lunch counters by deploying walkie-talkie teams downtown with a central radio post, so spotters could send students right back when a counter reopened, a system built by Ronald Yancey, the first black graduate of Georgia Tech.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Testing Boynton v. Virginia
clips.topic: From the Tests to the Freedom Rides
clips.summary: King recounts that after the December 1960 Boynton v. Virginia ruling against discrimination in interstate commerce, his committee sent teams to test it in Chattanooga, Columbia, Jacksonville, and Birmingham, with Maurice Pennington and Mays Dillard arrested in Chattanooga and Charles A. Black and Otis Moss Jr. hustled out of Athens, and how James Farmer phoned about the tests, recalled his own earlier attempt outside the South in the 1940s, and said he would mount the Freedom Rides in the spring, a plan they discussed at Clark College and that a February 1961 Marvin Rich letter to Roy Wilkins in the NAACP files confirms.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Snookered Into a Meeting
clips.summary: King recounts the white powers and sidelined Negro leaders meeting behind his back to settle the boycott on their terms, and Jesse Hill Jr., trusted by King, luring him to a chamber of commerce meeting billed as the most important of his life, where the merchants he had been battling presented a proposition to desegregate after the schools were integrated.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: I Baptized You, Boy
clips.summary: He describes Martin Luther King Sr. rising to say he was tired of King and had baptized him, and the irony that his defender was Frank H. Neely, chairman of Rich's board, who had been tapping King's leg with his cane yet declared that King alone had a constituency while the leaders had appointed themselves, after King asked to take the proposition back to his group.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: That's a Short Time
clips.summary: King recounts the recess where Rufus Clement told him he was between a rock and a hard place, and John C. Calhoun, a former NAACP head who had gone to jail rather than surrender its membership list, urging him at sixty, after a lifetime of waiting, to accept this first voluntary Southern desegregation as a short wait, leading King to agree on two conditions, that it be in writing and that he present it to his students.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Literal Firestorm
clips.summary: He tells of Ivan Allen Jr. holding a press conference before King could reach campus, announcing the students had agreed to end the boycott, the firestorm that followed, King and Herschelle Sullivan resigning only for the students to refuse the resignation, and a mass meeting of over two thousand at Warren Memorial Methodist Church where a nurse in white silenced Martin Luther King Sr. and the place erupted.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Greatest Speech of His Career
clips.summary: King recalls phoning Martin King to calm the booing crowd, and King delivering what King calls the greatest speech of his career, surpassing the March on Washington, taking the crowd to the mountaintop, warning that the cancerous disease of disunity would defeat them, and quieting a powerful meeting of conflict between students, young Turks, and old guard, with a quiet white observer at the back.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Send Me Your Credit Card
clips.summary: King reflects that the sober realized the town had never been at such a place before, and tells how, at the height of the boycott, he asked people to mail him their Rich's credit cards and three hundred fifty-three did, kept in a safety deposit box and returned afterward, proof that the movement had reached the masses, not the classes, with the fast dime over the slow dollar.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Greatest Movement Since Slavery
clips.summary: King casts his life as a continuation of his grandfather's NAACP recruiting, calls himself one of at least a hundred who played similar roles, and frames the 1960s civil rights movement as the greatest since the end of slavery, a volcano whose eruption, like a giant firecracker reaching its apogee, would eventually settle.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Embers That Reached Everyone
clips.summary: He argues that when African Americans won Title VII with its clause against discrimination by race, color, creed, national origin, and sex, the embers spread to the women's, Latino, and gay movements, the irony being that the masses of blacks remained at the back of the bus economically while others advanced, which he attributes partly to inadequate preparation.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: A Blanket and a Cell Phone Light
clips.summary: King closes with a homeless young woman who studied by cell-phone light under a blanket after lights-out in a shelter and is entering Spelman as a junior on full scholarship to become an oncologist, his lesson that one must work to rise from looking up at the bottom, and his hope to convince more people to write the right things on young people's slates rather than blame the system.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Born in a Blizzard in Newell, PA
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline and Guha Shankar in Pasadena in June 2016, Maria Varela introduces herself as born January 1, 1940, in a blizzard in Newell, Pennsylvania, and describes a family that was not political but socially open on injustice, a scrupulously fair mother, and a father who had known racism after coming from Mexico.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Greaser, Go Home, and the Scholarships
clips.summary: Varela recounts her father being born in Mazapil, Zacatecas, coming to the United States around age nine during the Mexican Revolution into fierce anti-immigrant sentiment, her grandfather choosing to settle where there were few Mexicanos, and her father and his brothers winning football scholarships, playing harder for every epithet thrown at them.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: The Secretary-Until-a-Husband Track
clips.summary: She describes hunting trips with her father and the shotgun she later took south, the Catholic high school's terminal-education track in shorthand and typing aimed at secretarial work until marriage, how a family friend dropping dead and leaving a widow with five children changed her father's mind and led her to Alverno College in Milwaukee, her grandfather's ban on the boys speaking Spanish, and her Irish and German mother whose father raised money for the IRA.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Society Has to Be Restructured for Justice
clips.summary: She describes the era when social engagement was treated as the core of Catholicism, the social inquiry movement and Christian Family Movement rooted in the Belgian worker priest movement and Pope Leo XIII's encyclical, and the see-judge-act training method of finding a problem by asking the people affected, then reflecting and acting.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Out of the Catholic Ghetto
clips.topic: Young Christian Students and the NSA
clips.summary: Varela recalls joining the Young Christian Students in high school and college, an action-oriented method that always demanded a next step, and its thrust to get out of the Catholic ghetto and into the National Student Association, which the nuns suspected of communism in the post-McCarthy era.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: They Wanted the NSA on Record
clips.summary: As student body president working the National Student Association Secretariat with her typing skills, Varela heard SNCC sit-in participants press the NSA to support the sit-ins against a hostile bloc of Southern schools, was struck by how articulately organizers like Charles McDew and Tim Jenkins defeated every argument, and met Tom Hayden building the liberal caucus that became SDS, marveling that relationships drew a girl who had never read C. Wright Mills or Marx into that world.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Rat-Infested Apartments on the West Side
clips.summary: She recounts postponing a Marquette speech therapy fellowship to accept a Young Christian Students national staff offer, moving to Chicago headquarters to live on seven dollars a week in rat-infested West Side apartments, and Tom Hayden reaching her to help put together a manifesto at the Port Huron conference.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Why Do We Need Adult Overseers
clips.summary: Varela explains that student groups were then appendages of adult organizations, making SNCC's independence remarkable, as Diane Nash said they had brought Nashville to its knees, and recalls meeting Casey Hayden at the Port Huron conference, typing the original minutes, and later getting Casey's letter that the SNCC office desperately needed help.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: You're Pretty Hypocritical
clips.summary: Varela describes the semantic and authenticity questions she helped resolve in the Port Huron statement, Casey Hayden coming out of the Young Christian Women, and her months of procrastination before deciding it was hypocritical to exhort Catholic students to support the movement while refusing the SNCC office's request that she come to Atlanta.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: When Lightning Strikes, It Illuminates
clips.topic: A Summer School and the Selma Offer
clips.summary: She recounts the six-week summer school that NSA, Connie Curry, and SNCC ran to train young activists being jailed and beaten in Southern towns, where Robert Coles taught and she first learned Afro-American history, an exploding epiphany she likens to lightning illuminating everything and watched transform how young Black men carried themselves, and how Frank Smith and the badly beaten Bernard Lafayette Jr. then asked her, to her surprise, what she would think about going to Selma.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: A Priest, a Mission, and a Literacy Test
clips.summary: She explains being recruited to support a French Canadian priest at St. Elizabeth Mission, an Afro-American Catholic church served by the Edmundites whose hospital and school employed the Black community, who wanted literacy training so people could take the rigged twenty-one-question voter registration test, with thoughts of a possible class-action lawsuit.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: We Are All American Here
clips.summary: Asked about her own identity, Varela reflects that her family moved too much to grow up in a Mexican community and spoke no Spanish at home though her father was proud of his heritage, and recalls a nun who, hearing her mention being Mexican, walked down the aisle to declare that they were all American here, a wound she carried for years.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Maybe Adults Already Could Read
clips.summary: Varela describes teaching a woman who wanted to read the Bible and observing, through the YCS method of watching and reflecting, that adult non-readers already had varied vocabulary tied to what they wanted to know, and learning that foundations were eager to fund literacy work tied to voter education in the movement.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Four Black Kids and a Thousand White Kids
clips.summary: She recounts Rennie Davis writing her first funded proposal, recruiting four to six Afro-American students to develop literacy materials in Selma for the summer of 1964, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson pointing out the contrast with Bob Moses bringing a thousand white students to Mississippi, a program Varela thought was a horrible idea given how hard it was just to keep her few students safe.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Here's Jim Clark With His Cattle Prod
clips.summary: Varela tells how, the day after the Civil Rights Act was signed on July 2, 1964, her students went to the Thirsty Boy to be served because it was the law of the land, only for Jim Clark to haul them to jail with his cattle prod, leaving her four weeks raising bail and dodging a warrant under a Selma ordinance banning meetings, and reflects that Selma, drawing students mostly from the North, was like a Nazi state with none of the refuge Mississippi offered.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: It's Bigger Than CAT, C-A-T
clips.summary: Varela describes shifting literacy toward what people actually wanted to read, working with Afro-American farmers in Batesville, Panola County, on an okra co-op to make booklets and filmstrips showing Black people in leadership, materials so prized that an organizer begged her not to pass them out before meetings, and how the hunger for education brought out parents who skipped voter registration but filled Freedom Schools that became Head Start, work she likens to a second Reconstruction that SNCC alone had boots on the ground to do.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: A Darkroom in a Shotgun Shack
clips.summary: Varela tells of moving to a shotgun shack near Tougaloo College and building a darkroom with Bobby Fletcher because the books needed photographs of Black people that no files held, being sent to learn from Matt Herron, a New Orleans commercial photographer who taught SNCC people the gray-scale documentary method passed down from a student of Ansel Adams, and the SNCC photographers' duty to cover events as an extra eye that might protect marchers, as she did at the James Meredith March Against Fear.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: To Show the Local Folks, Not the Celebrities
clips.summary: Varela describes the nine SNCC photographers as the only photography department of any civil rights organization, their purpose to show local leaders rather than celebrities, the This Light of Ours exhibit, and how the camera simply expressed her vision since she already lived in the Black community, later shooting Latino participants in the Poor People's Campaign.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Are You Pregnant? It Will Spoil the Hog
