clips.summary: Varela recounts producing Fannie Lou Hamer's first autobiography with Julius Lester and helping her campaign after Atlantic City, the day Hamer met her at the door asking whether she or her sister was pregnant lest it spoil the hog they were cutting, their conversations over chicharrones about the kinship of Mexican and Black flavors, the poor white families Hamer quietly supplied from donation trucks, and Hamer telling the 1964 debate she would not be part of an organization that did not reflect the society they wanted.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: You Could Make Twenty-Five a Day
clips.topic: Filmstrips and the United Farm Workers
clips.summary: Varela explains the filmstrip printer New York supporters built, the cheap storyboarded strips with scripts and canvas mailing bags so organizers anywhere could show them, being asked by the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union to document Cesar Chavez's United Farm Workers, and how Marshall Ganz and George Ballis got the SNCC body to support the union with surplus Plymouth Valiants and CB radios, making SNCC the first Afro-American civil rights organization to back the farm workers.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: We're Not the Only Ones
clips.topic: A Filmstrip in a Tent City
clips.summary: Varela tells of an Afro-American Catholic priest from northern Louisiana who gathered plantation workers thrown off the land for trying to vote, where she showed the Panola County okra filmstrip and the farm workers strip, moving an older man to tears that they were not the only ones the sheriffs beat, and admits the mortal flaw that the strips assumed projectors the poor Black schools did not have.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Keep Us Informed
clips.summary: Varela dismantles the narrative that non-Black people were thrown out of SNCC, noting John Lewis was hurt at not being re-elected chair but the story was blown up in the media, that two months after Stokely Carmichael was elected and SNCC adopted an all-Black executive committee he and Ruby Doris sent her a personalized letter encouraging her work with Mrs. Hamer's campaign, and that Bob Zellner was simply told SNCC lacked resources for white-community work.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: It Was a Strategy, Not a Creed
clips.summary: She explains that those who came in by late 1962 or 1963, herself included, were tactically rather than philosophically nonviolent, that she brought a gun to defend herself, that after Atlantic City and the murder of three civil rights workers a deep anger and the lesson of Black history's effect on identity led many to insist they control their own organization, and that foundations then cut her support because she would not repudiate black power, leaving Elizabeth Betita Martinez running the New York office.
related_events: murder of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: A Bridge to the Land Grant Movement
clips.summary: Varela recounts Julian Bond inviting her in the fall of 1966 to host a New Mexico land grant leader known for a courthouse raid at the National Conference for New Politics, his later letter asking her to come work as a bridge between the civil rights and land grant movements, and her choice of New Mexico over offers in Crystal City and with Cesar Chavez because farmers with their own land could not be thrown off it, reflecting on how much Black land had been lost since Reconstruction.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Protected Only by a Treaty
clips.summary: She describes taking a seven-dollar-a-week job, the land grant movement reaching back to the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo and the only non-Native people protected by treaty, and watching elderly members in neat worn clothes and battered hats slip the charismatic leader hundreds of dollars from selling cattle, prompting her to wonder who was actually organizing it.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: No Room in Resurrection City
clips.topic: Bunk Beds in a Borrowed School
clips.summary: Varela recounts being handed the Poor People's Campaign logistics, going to Washington where there was no room in the swampy Resurrection City, so the women were housed with bunk beds in a borrowed school with communal meals, and wondering how the SCLC people carried on so soon after King's assassination.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Chicanos Don't Put Up With That
clips.summary: She describes the SCLC running roughshod over everyone on its crusade, so the Chicanos staged their own actions at the State, Education, and Justice Departments over the treaty, racism in education, and police brutality, the Puerto Ricans leading everyone across the bridge to Robert Kennedy's grave, the Young Lords and Brown Berets uniting on security, an Appalachian contingent the women called poorer than themselves, and the experienced Rodolfo Gonzales acting as de facto leader.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Thirty-Three Chicano Newspapers
clips.summary: She describes a Department of Education hearing in Washington where people testified about school conditions, returning to work on the Chicano Press Association linking thirty-three papers with a photo and news service, writing for Chicano newspapers, and meeting Anselmo Tijerina, brother of the land grant leader, who believed the people themselves should do the work in Tierra Amarilla.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: A Call at Three in the Morning
clips.summary: Varela tells of Anselmo's plan to work the land with potatoes and a hog farm rather than just talk about it, his determination to buy a clinic after the doctor left for the city because women were giving birth on the highway, and the three a.m. call that the clinic had been torched by Anglo bankers and ranchers fearing it would legitimize the land grant movement.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Mississippi All Over Again
clips.summary: She recounts deciding she had to go after the arson, the Presbyterian Church taking out the mortgage on a clinic still operating since 1969, and framing thirty years of New Mexico work as a translation of what black power and SNCC meant to her, including work with livestock growers and weavers to preserve a nearly extinct breed of sheep the USDA had condemned and to build businesses from its meat and wool, while learning how reluctant the country is to support rural people empowering themselves.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: We Study SNCC, They Told Me
clips.topic: From Land Economics to a New Generation
clips.summary: Varela reflects on the saying that the white people got the money and the brown people got the politics while money in the rural West and black belt South is rooted in land, then turns to her amazement at the undocumented student movement, its nine hundred-strong Houston meeting and California victories on in-state tuition, and students telling her they study SNCC as the closest of the movements to them for its anti-celebrity, team-based values.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: It's Bigger Than a Hamburger
clips.topic: A Legacy Written Out of History
clips.summary: She recounts the undocumented movement widening from individual relief to include families, echoing Fannie Lou Hamer's line that it was bigger than a hamburger, calls these young people heroes carrying the best of SNCC's legacy, and notes she taught SNCC as a case history for sixteen years to students asking why they never learned it in school.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Even If It Takes Fifty Years
clips.summary: Varela recalls Ella Baker urging the movement to reflect after the 1964 convention, the setback when the undocumented movement nearly won DACA much as SNCC lost the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party fight in 1964, and closes that the best of a movement carries into a later generation if the empowerment strategies are solid, as the interview ends with her daughter Sabina Zuniga Varela present.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Walk With Me Through Oklahoma City
clips.topic: Clara Luper and the History Lesson
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Oklahoma City in May 2011, Marilyn Luper Hildreth introduces her mother Clara Luper, an educator at Dunjee High School in Spencer who wrote a play about Martin Luther King Jr. that drew an NAACP invitation, then gave her young people a history lesson on segregated Oklahoma City: stores where you could not try on a hat or shoes, restaurants that passed you a brown bag, and separate phone booths and water fountains.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: A Taste of Freedom
clips.summary: Hildreth recounts the NAACP trip north, where children who had never left Oklahoma sat down to drink a Coke and eat a hamburger and drank from the same water fountain as everyone else for the first time, then returned to the South to face discrimination again, carrying what she calls the dangerous taste of a little freedom.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Just Sit Until They Served Us
clips.summary: At a Monday-night NAACP Youth Council meeting at the family home on NE Park Place, an eight-year-old Marilyn moved that the group go to Katz Drug Store and sit until served; she describes being spat on, kicked, and called names, the youth pressing on without fear, and the lunch counter desegregating within days as the group moved restaurant to restaurant.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: You Could Knock It Down but Not Keep It Down
clips.summary: Hildreth reflects on her mother as a woman leader when local authority was almost entirely male, a dreamer and reader who gave her children books rather than toys, faced threats to her teaching job, and was protected by a colleague who refused to fire her as the price of a principalship, and who drilled the children every morning in Gandhi's nonviolent method.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Where Did My Mom Get That Toughness
clips.summary: She traces her mother's strength to Hoffman, Oklahoma, where Clara was near the top of a class of five with only leftover textbooks missing pages, and to a father denied hospital treatment because he was Black, a wound Hildreth believes, alongside a strong belief in God, gave her mother the resolve to keep fighting injustice.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Run and Knock That Door Down
clips.topic: The One Time Mother Showed Fear
clips.summary: Hildreth recalls the single time she saw her mother afraid, driving home from a freedom rally in a caravan of more than eighty cars when white men in a pickup truck began following them; Clara told the children to run for the Posey family door when she blew the horn, then drove away alone, leaving Marilyn fearing she might never see her mother again.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: The Maids and the Garbage Workers
clips.summary: Members of Fifth Street Baptist, the family found Clara loved, feared, and disliked among the city's Black pastors, with congregations afraid their churches would be bombed; Hildreth notes the Oklahoma City movement rested on the have-nots, the maids and garbage workers, including her own grandmother, who moved into servant quarters to educate Clara and her siblings.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: They Threw a Chimpanzee on Me
clips.summary: Hildreth describes a relatively low-violence Oklahoma City, which she believes consciously chose not to become a national flashpoint, a workable relationship with the police, and her mother being arrested twenty-six times without being beaten, set against a cruel incident in which someone threw a chimpanzee on her, small as she was then, while she was picketing.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: She Read the Congressional Record
clips.summary: Asked where her mother's political skill came from, Hildreth answers all of the above: Clara read the Congressional Record as a hobby, knew everyone in Oklahoma, and became the voice of people whose problems had become struggles; she adds that her parents divorced amid the movement, her fun-loving electrician father unable to handle the pressure.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: A Whirlwind Effect
clips.topic: Years of Protest and Its Legacy
clips.summary: Hildreth remembers Saturdays of her youth spent protesting, with amusement parks closed to them and a closeness among the young participants outsiders cannot grasp; recalling the standing-room 50th-anniversary commemoration of the 1958 sit-in, she says they only wanted social change in Oklahoma City but set off a whirlwind that changed American history.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: You Can't Out-Read Mom
clips.summary: Hildreth recounts joining her mother's 1972 US Senate primary campaign, an unbeatable debater versed in government and education from decades of study who carried the same message to tiny all-white towns across Oklahoma and surprised people of every background; they lost the primary, but with a little more time, Hildreth believes, Clara could have made historic change.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: So Goes One, So Goes All
clips.topic: The Sanitation Strike and a Closing Creed
clips.summary: Hildreth closes on the 1969 Oklahoma City sanitation workers' strike, where Clara was chosen spokesperson as an honest woman of integrity who would fight to the end for people who could not represent themselves, and on the daily creed her mother taught: to believe in the rain when it does not fall, the sun when it does not shine, and a God you have never seen.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: You Look Like a Nice Man
clips.summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer in Baltimore in March 2013, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan opens with her family in Providence: a Saint Vincent-born optometrist father active in Pan-West-Indian unity, a Michigan-born mother descended from a Colored Conventions minister who settled in Buxton, Ontario, both Progressive Party members, and her own first political act at age three, walking up to presidential candidate Henry A. Wallace and landing on the front page of the Providence Journal.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: I'm Black and I'm Proud
clips.summary: She recounts the price of her parents' activism: an FBI or immigration agent threatening to deport her naturalized father, a jewelry-factory owner called before HUAC who committed suicide, the family buying a former funeral parlor in an unwelcoming white neighborhood where stones came through the window and she was beaten going to school, and her mother teaching her at six to answer slurs with the line that she was Black and proud of it.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: The Only Black Student
clips.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan describes being the lone Black child at Lexington Avenue Elementary, then the only Black student in the entire Lincoln School for Girls, a Quaker prep school her mother secured a scholarship to, the neighbor who came for an eye exam as a gesture of welcome and used the same prescription forty years later, and being barred from the Glee Club lest she socialize with boys at the joint prep-school concerts.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: A Member of the Rosa Parks Generation
clips.topic: A Northern Child and the Movement
clips.summary: Her parents kept Emmett Till's murder from her until college, but she identified instead as a child of the Rosa Parks generation, fixing on Daisy Bates, the Little Rock Nine, and Autherine Lucy integrating schools, and crediting the Harriet Tubman biography her mother gave her, the first book she owned, alongside spirituals and an early lesson to distrust the history taught in school.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Take It With a Grain of Salt
clips.summary: She remembers a mother who sang opera and spirituals, played organ, and taught her to question the official history and even the pulpit, citing Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John to note no women wrote the Gospels, and describes the small Winter Street AME Zion congregation where she accompanied her sightless father to trustee meetings and learned how an organization handles its finances and rules of order.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Two Years Apart for a Daughter
clips.topic: Getting to the University of Michigan
clips.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan explains how her mother, a Michigan graduate at seventeen, moved to Detroit and lived apart from her father for two years so her daughter would qualify for in-state tuition at the University of Michigan, the school the family could afford, even as the daughter had wanted Fisk after seeing a white paper on the Nashville movement.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Your People Are in Motion All Over the South
clips.topic: SDS and a Trip to Chapel Hill
clips.summary: She recalls joining the Methodist Students Association and meeting Curtis Hayes from McComb, Mississippi at a Friday brown-bag event, joining SDS as the only group talking about racism and peace, and Tom Hayden, back from a Freedom Ride in Albany, pressing her to leave peace work and inviting her carload to an SDS-SNCC meeting in Chapel Hill in April 1962 that she calls an extraordinary experience.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Representing the Race
clips.summary: She describes her parents' adamant refusal to let her go South at seventeen, rooted in her mother's own law-school experience at Michigan, where she believed Black students were flunked out once the school realized its mistake, and the general expectation among the small group of Black families that their children would become professionals, keep their noses clean, and honor the sacrifices made for their education.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Friends of SNCC in Detroit
clips.topic: Summer Support Work at Wayne State
clips.summary: Instead of going South in 1962, she enrolled at Wayne State and spent afternoons with the Friends of SNCC group built around Bernard Lafayette, fundraiser sent by SNCC, alongside Freedom Rider Elizabeth Hirschfeld and Detroit activists like John Watson and Luke Tripp, who were reading Robert F. Williams and Negroes With Guns and would later take over the Wayne State South End paper, all doing support work for the students in the South.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: She Left Michigan in Labor
clips.summary: She recounts the summer's culminating event, bringing Diane Nash to Detroit while Nash was under indictment in Mississippi and nine months pregnant, her tagline that any Black child in Mississippi was in jail already, and how Bernard Lafayette put Nash forward rather than himself, then setting up a Friends of SNCC group in Ann Arbor with the Freedom Singers until her parents, seeing she barely knew where her classes were, cut off her money and she took on her own education.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: I Thought the Bad Things Were Over
clips.summary: Slipping away South in 1963 to Charles Sherrod's Albany project, she describes orientation in Sumter County and the murder there of a young woman raped by thirteen white men, one a bread-route driver who parked his truck outside her house, a horror that shattered her belief the worst was past and made her fear the same could happen to her, amid still-large mass meetings led by Sherrod with Reverend Samuel Wells and C.B. King.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: I Would Hate to Talk to Your Brother
clips.summary: She recalls taking sanctuary in a church guarded against carloads of armed white men by JoAnn Christian's father and cousin with rifles across their laps, her estranged mother working through the Deltas up to Burke Marshall and Robert Kennedy with a warning she would hate to have to talk to his brother, and the preventive nonviolence she first saw in Albany, where Sherrod tipped off local gangs to position themselves between the white crowd and the nonviolent marchers.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: A Bumblebee Car Into Greenwood
clips.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan tells how Sherrod had tricked them into staying by warning Mississippi was too dangerous after Medgar Evers, so when he left town she and Jean Wheeler bused to Greenwood, where Sam Block and Willie Peacock met them in a yellow-and-black car and drove eighty miles an hour, and she spent the summer canvassing for a voting day that drew two or three hundred people though almost no one was allowed to register.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: The Culture of the Movement Was Black
clips.summary: Comparing Sherrod's Albany project and Greenwood, she notes both had only a handful of white volunteers, who in Mississippi never left the office for safety, and argues that what drew her was a Black-led movement, young Black people in charge of their own destiny as she had first seen in Chapel Hill, with Sherrod in command and Prathia Hall taking over when he left, not a fifty-fifty integration.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Encouraged to Be as Much as I Could Be
clips.summary: Asked about the treatment of women in the movement, Prescod Norman Noonan says she never felt mistreated, only encouraged to do as much as the men, felt herself a novice in what was a war zone to her, and observed that in the minutes of meetings, where no one was put down by gender, you often could not tell which speaker was a woman and which a man.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: A Soldier in the Civil Rights Army
clips.summary: She recalls the 1963 March on Washington feeling too civilized after Mississippi, identifying with the hard-pressed people from Danville and Cambridge, Maryland, sitting up front as honored guests, the censoring of John Lewis's speech, the few women on the program, and her dismay that Roy Wilkins made a disparaging remark about W.E.B. Du Bois, who had died that very day.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: She Faked a Heart Attack
clips.summary: She tells of going back North to reconcile with her parents after the march and her mother, knowing Wayne State had not yet started, faking a heart attack to keep her home, later admitting she would have done anything to keep her out of Mississippi, and returns to John Lewis's censored speech, thought too radical for its line about marching through the South like Sherman and its first-draft charge that Kennedy's civil rights bill was too little and too late.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: I Know What Democracy Is
clips.summary: She explains why she skipped Freedom Summer in 1964, opposing the summer project as much of the Mississippi staff did because Black organizers in the Black community was what worked and they were not recruiting Black students, then recounts Atlantic City that August, proud Michigan's was the first Democratic Party to back the MFDP, where a Council of Churches man defended the two-seat compromise red-faced before Fannie Lou Hamer rose to say the people should choose their own representatives, not an appointed Ed King and Aaron Henry.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: We Blew It Wide Open
clips.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan reflects that in roughly two years of activism with almost no money the movement had made the vote a national issue, that through a small historical opening they forced change wide open, and invokes Mahalia Jackson singing Joshua Fit the Battle of Jericho as her image of leaning on the walls of segregation until they came tumbling down.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Twelve Dollars for a Six-Day Week
clips.summary: Joining the Alabama SNCC project in early 1965, she describes weekend work while studying Black college students' vocational aspirations with Pat Gurin, Doris Derby, and John Bracey at Tuskegee, then being assigned a ward of Selma to do whatever the people wanted, from a stoplight to getting Black women hired at the Dan River Mills against a day-work wage of twelve dollars a week, and helping Pepsi-plant men unionize with an AFL-CIO organizer who came at night.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: We Were Community Organizers
clips.summary: As King and the SCLC came to Selma to dramatize the right to vote, Prescod Norman Noonan voices her suspicion that liberals after the MFDP challenge wanted SCLC to blunt a populist SNCC, citing a New York meeting with Allard Lowenstein, and describes the uneasy coexistence, eating together at Walker's Cafe, a SNCC and an SCLC person assigned to each ward, with SCLC promoting demonstrations while SNCC did local groundwork.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: The U-Turn March
clips.summary: She recounts that SNCC officially opposed supporting the march to Montgomery, though individuals like John Lewis and Bob Mants joined, and that after Bloody Sunday everyone came to Selma for the U-turn march, when SCLC's leadership stopped at the bridge, prayed, and led the marchers back to Brown Chapel under a deal, and relays the account in Hands on the Freedom Plow of Forman and Stokely pleading with King to break the federal injunction.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: The Streets Were Full
clips.summary: Rather than march, Prescod Norman Noonan went to the parallel SNCC demonstrations in Montgomery, where University of Michigan students came down through a Tuskegee tie, left before things got rough as a community organizer rather than a demonstrator, and returned the day the march culminated to feed people at a church by the Capitol amid streets so full of participating community people the cars could not pass.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: People Were Going in Droves
clips.summary: After the march she kept canvassing and tried to organize Selma registrants by ward, work that turned administrative once federal registrars arrived and people went to register in droves without difficulty, even as the movement's energy and Stokely Carmichael shifted out to Lowndes County, Alabama and the independent Black political party that became the Lowndes County Freedom Organization.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: I Wanted a Black Star
clips.topic: A Debate Over the Panther Symbol
clips.summary: She describes visiting Lowndes for an early mass meeting where people already had a practiced arrangement for getting home safely, and a Boy Scout camp discussion of the independent party's symbol, where she favored a black star over the black panther, linking it through Marcus Garvey's Black Star Line and Nkrumah's Ghanaian airline to the African struggle, while admitting this set her apart from her colleagues' thinking on Black institutions.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Across the Street From the Jail
clips.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan recalls that challenging the poverty funds brought a cordial Mayor Smitherman to the table, that the SNCC office sat directly across from the police station so they could see people jailed through the window, that it was state troopers, not Selma police, who beat marchers on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, and that public safety director Wilson Baker, who had set up that office placement, once sent cars to bring them home safely on Halloween.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: I'll Wait for You
clips.summary: She tells of meeting her first husband on the Selma project, marrying in 1967 as he faced orders to Vietnam and won conscientious-objector standing as an officer, her pledge to follow him to Canada, Africa, or wait through jail, and his mother's blunt warning against crossing an international boundary or marrying a felon, then how a visit to Sherrod's New Communities farm near Albany, the house riddled with bullet holes, sent the couple toward medical school and a history PhD instead of risking the baby's life.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Something Old, Not Something New
clips.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan admits she often stayed outside SNCC meetings to talk with people from other projects, found the internal tensions easily resolvable, and saw the changes not as something new but as a return to a SNCC she had always known as a Black organization, with the Mississippi Summer Project as the aberration and the later turn a homecoming.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Organize, Organize, Organize
clips.summary: She recounts her integrationist parents hosting a party after Stokely Carmichael's Black Power speech, her mother telling him she was glad they finally got some sense and to organize, and explains she was never surprised or turned off by the slogan because she had always understood the work as building vehicles of Black power and radicalizing the whole political structure, while regretting a missed chance to unite the Southern movements.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: A New Set of Dangers
clips.summary: Asked about the last forty-five years, she describes studying and teaching history, a PhD left unfinished amid family emergencies, anti-hunger and sickle-cell tutorial projects, post-SNCC retrospective conferences and freeing Curtis Hayes from jail in Liberia, and raising three sons in inner-city Detroit amid police harassment and gun violence that made at least four women in her circle lose sons, the sons now a lawyer and two physicians.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Hands on the Freedom Plow
clips.topic: A Book of SNCC Women's Stories
clips.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan closes describing the collection she helped compile, Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC, published by the University of Illinois Press in 2010, gathering the stories of fifty-two women active in SNCC-connected civil rights work, most of them college students at the time but also middle-aged activists, white and Black women, and two Hispanic women.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Born in Waycross, Raised in Albany
clips.summary: Interviewed by Willie Griffin at Albany State University in March 2013, Mary Frances Jenkins gives her birth in Waycross, Georgia on August 12, 1928, a young childhood in Pittsburgh after her father was killed in an automobile accident on a Georgia highway, and the move to Albany around age eight or nine, where she entered the fourth grade, after her mother remarried.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: You Can't Has Followed My Life
clips.summary: Jenkins recounts growing up in the Jim Crow era, sitting in the balcony of the Albany movie theater and drinking from separate fountains, a white boy squirting a water pistol in her face while her mother held her hand, having to tell her own three-year-old daughter the same word her mother told her, you can't, when the child wanted a whites-only ride, and the homemaker mother who never finished high school but insisted education was the key, married to a stepfather who worked on the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: The Best Teachers in the World
clips.summary: Jenkins remembers Madison High School as an all-Black segregated school with hand-me-down books and outdated equipment but, she insists, the best teachers in the world, naming a strict principal Ruth T. Kimball and English teacher Olive Rogers, the firm non-abusive discipline of that era, serving as editor-in-chief of the high school annual, graduating as salutatorian with her friend Vashti Stubbs steadying her nerves, and her early ambition to be a writer of fiction novels before settling on teaching.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: A Petition on the First Day
clips.summary: Jenkins describes choosing Fisk University in Nashville on a teacher's recommendation and her family's financial sacrifice, being handed a petition to oust a teacher almost her first day on campus, a teacher she signed against but who became the best she ever had, and her involvement in creative writing and the little theater, directing a teacher's play her senior year.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: From the Service Back to a Classroom
clips.topic: Military Years and a First Job
clips.summary: She tells of leaving Fisk to spend two years in the service, marrying Roscoe Jenkins, basic training in Virginia, special services at Fort Dix, and a still-segregated posting at Fort Custer in Michigan, then after the birth of her daughter Sherry returning to finish at Fisk, divorcing Roscoe of North Carolina and later remarrying, losing a promised Albany job over a certificate mismatch, and being hired by her pastor Rev. James Grant, principal at East Baker High School.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: I Would Never Invite One of You to My Home
clips.summary: At Baker County she says she learned what white power could do through two incidents: a white supervisor, wife of the county school superintendent, firing a young teacher on the spot with no recourse over a weekend incident, and the same supervisor, the year the Supreme Court ruled on school desegregation, assembling the Black teachers to argue against integration and declaring she would never invite one of them to her home.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Army Barracks for a Classroom
clips.summary: Jenkins describes leaving Baker County for Carver Junior High School in Dougherty County, teaching two months in army barracks behind the building before getting a room, still using hand-me-down second books, and her view, looking at the gangs of today, that discipline in the late 1950s was both possible and needed.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: A Biracial Committee Refused
clips.summary: She recalls the quiet pre-movement activity in Black Albany, a group of men in the Criterion Club asking the City Commission for a biracial committee and being turned down, Dr. William G. Anderson refused over flooding in his neighborhood, the movement's own representatives later told the commission had no way to form such a committee, the all-negative portrayal of Blacks in the white-owned Albany Herald, and the locally edited Southwest Georgian as the community's only Black paper.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: The Crowds, the Singing, the Crying
clips.topic: The First Mass Meeting and King's Office
clips.summary: Jenkins describes going to the first mass meeting at Mount Zion Baptist Church under Rev. James Grant, the crowds, singing, and crying, song leaders Charles Sherrod, Bernice Johnson Reagon, and Rutha Mae Harris, her wish to get segregation out of her children's lives, and volunteering in the office set up in Dr. William G. Anderson's house for Martin Luther King Jr., answering the phone and once dialing a number King handed her for the calypso singer Harry Belafonte.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Notes Written on Fans
clips.summary: Jenkins recalls King's meetings with women, whom he called the movers and shakers of the world, and how, listening to people return from jail describing their treatment, she realized the unnamed many would be forgotten and began taking notes, sometimes on paper fans when her purse ran out, material she hopes one day to give to the museum, the seed of her book.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: My Husband Pulled Me Out of the Line
clips.summary: She describes the threat that arrested teachers would be fired and rumors of people checking car tags at mass meetings, her decision not to march, and the one Sunday she nearly did, rising with her four-year-old son in her arms after Rev. James Grant left the pulpit to act alone, until her husband gently pulled her from the line, the same day Chief Laurie Pritchett chose to arrest no one.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Carry the Report Cards to the Principal
clips.summary: Jenkins relates the humiliations Black teachers faced once integration began, some isolated with no colleagues, others required to carry report cards to the white principal so he could check whether white students had been given proper grades, then turns to the book she wrote, prompted by her late daughter's urging that she assemble her material.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: I Had to Pay for Every Mistake
clips.summary: She describes how, with her material already organized, assembling the book was easy, but she knew nothing of publishing and took it to a religious-book company in Columbus, Georgia, where a copy editor she believes disliked the content introduced errors Jenkins had to pay to fix, until she sold out the first edition.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: They Used Third Edition
clips.topic: A Dropped Picture and a Poetry Book
clips.summary: Jenkins recounts finding, before a book signing, a page printed with a blank spot where a directed picture was never dropped in, deciding to hold the signing and promise corrected copies, the publisher reprinting and labeling it the third edition, the warm community response that carried the book to a fourth edition, and her new project retelling the movement in poetry because children respond to it.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: The Laws That Helped Us Are Challenged Now
clips.summary: Asked about the Albany movement's impact, Jenkins says she cannot know how long change would have taken without it, points to Black people working behind store counters as proof of what was won, warns the journey is not over, and closes on Martin Luther King Jr.'s legacy of service, the students who entered the bus station to enforce the Interstate Commerce Commission ban on segregated terminals, the six girls who integrated the Albany high school after C.B. King's eight-year struggle, and her warning that the laws that once helped are now being challenged.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Born on Grandmother's Slot of Land
clips.summary: Interviewed by Willie Griffin on the campus of Albany State University in March 2013, Mary Jones recalls being born in Albany, Georgia, on July 24, 1933, and being reared by her grandmother Bertha McIntosh on a small plot of land the family had bought, a place where churchgoing and weekend work went together.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Picking Cotton and Pulling Corn
clips.summary: Jones describes leaving school each fall to work the white man's farm gathering peanuts, picking cotton, digging potatoes, pulling corn, and gathering pecans, missing the first two months of school until the harvest was in around October or November, and starting that work at about eight years old under the taskmaster Miss Alice Porter.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Seventy-Five Cents for School Clothes
clips.summary: She recalls hoeing the grass around bird seed planted on a small farm owned by Mr. Kina, who later became mayor, earning about seventy-five cents that the children saved to buy school clothes, working from before sunup to around five, fed on the job, and walking together home through the neighborhood.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: School in the Baptist Church
clips.summary: Jones recounts attending school inside one of the oldest Baptist churches in Southwest Georgia, connected to W.E.B. Du Bois, the grades divided by corners of the one building up through seventh grade at a school named Shepherd after the man who gave the congregation the ground, with teachers like Miss Willie Mae Jackson and Miss Celeste Scott who kept the children in line.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Married the Tuesday After the Friday Game
clips.summary: She describes coming into town to attend Monroe Comprehensive High School, staying with her aunt Ella Holloway and three cousins in the Black community, playing basketball and running track, and falling in love with Jimmy Lee Jones, whom she married in 1951 instead of finishing her senior year, days before he left for the Army.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: The Principal Called Them Monkeys
clips.summary: Jones tells of following news of integration through the radio and the Pittsburgh Courier and A.C. Searles's Southwest Georgian, wanting better for her children, and signing her oldest daughter into the newly integrated high school, where Principal McNair called the Black students little monkeys and they faced fights nearly every day.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: She Hung In There
clips.summary: She recalls her daughter being spit at and called names in the cafeteria but hanging in there because friends had registered alongside her, and describes how black and white teachers were transferred between schools, though only one or two Black teachers went to the formerly white schools and her daughter found she could no longer play basketball there.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: A House Mama Raising Somebody
clips.summary: Jones describes her husband serving two years and later being recalled to active duty as a reservist during the movement, while she stayed home as a house mama running the children to school and meetings, teaching them that they were somebody regardless of skin color, that God loves everyone, and to go to school to make something of themselves.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: After Rosa Parks, the Criterion Club
clips.summary: Having heard of the Montgomery incident and Rosa Parks, Jones recounts the Criterion Club coming together and her work in voter registration, including a small school set up in an office to teach people to read because applicants had to read the Georgia Constitution to register.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Registered Is Not Voted
clips.summary: She describes canvassing the projects where she lived, reaching people too scared to go register because of the reading requirement, building their trust so they would come to her, and the disappointment of a woman she carried to register who never actually voted, teaching Jones that registering alone counts for nothing.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Seven Sent to See Pritchett
clips.summary: Jones tells of serving on the movement's police committee, one of seven members chosen to talk with Chief Laurie Pritchett, who ignored them the first several visits while a deputy left them sitting for hours, until McCree Harris, who worked at Monroe High and was known to him, pressed him and he claimed no Black applicants had come forward.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Walking a Beat With No Patrol Car
clips.summary: She recalls McCree Harris taking applications door to door so that about five Black officers were hired, among them Etheridge Smith, David Hyde, and Robert Benning Jr., though they were given no patrol cars and made to walk a beat, and explains the committee acted because Black men were being killed nearly every week, often by white police.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Choosing Dr. Anderson to Lead
clips.summary: Jones names the leaders of the Criterion Club who, already active in registration, helped organize the Albany Movement, including Dr. William G. Anderson, C.B. and Slater King, Thomas Chapman, and Marion S. Page, and recalls choosing Dr. Anderson as president of the movement and Mr. Page as secretary.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Welcoming the Freedom Riders
clips.summary: She describes the Freedom Riders arriving by train and being arrested as the community went down to welcome them, the mass meetings held most nights, and the church burnings that frightened some ministers off, after which Dr. King and his organizations helped rebuild the burned churches more soundly than before.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: The Children Marched First
clips.summary: Jones recalls anger at James Gray's paper claiming the grown people pushed the children out front because they were too scared to march, the adults organizing right there at a mass meeting, and roughly seven hundred of them marching from the corner of Washington and Broad Avenue past the old jail.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Seven Hundred in a Tiny Jail
clips.summary: She tells of marching even after being warned to stop and go home, being arrested and held from Monday to Thursday, taken to Baker County jail, the Billy clubs, standing in pouring rain for hours before being booked, and packed with about twenty-five people into a cell built for eight or ten with one bed.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Singing Louder Than the Dogs
clips.summary: Jones describes the jailers threatening to loose dogs on the prisoners and the marchers singing and praying louder in response, the hard black-eyed peas and grits with bread thrown on top they were fed, and her resolve that it was rough but worth it, trying to march again only to find they would no longer arrest her.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: A Black Christmas Downtown
clips.summary: She recounts the white friends who quietly helped, the rule that anyone who sided with Black people got treated as they were, the leaders' rebuffed attempts to talk with Pritchett and the city manager, and the boycott in which people cut ties with downtown, got their layaway goods out before Christmas, and shopped instead in Macon.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: My Father Bonded Me Out
clips.summary: Jones describes the relief of seeing her children after her father bonded her out, her suspicion that officials profited from the bail money, and how she stayed involved once she could no longer be arrested by hosting daily meetings at her home in the Washington projects and serving as a voting captain who told her neighbors who to vote for.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Good Jobs and Cash Registers
clips.summary: Looking back, Jones counts the movement a success rather than a failure, pointing to integrated schools, parents raising their children up right, and good jobs opening so that Black people could run cash registers instead of only cleaning, and names the legacy she wants remembered: much prayer, sticking together, and leaving the old Uncle Tom ways behind.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: The Teachers Who Could Not March
clips.summary: She explains that the superintendent forbade teachers to march on pain of losing their jobs, so instead they supported the marchers by buying food and paying light bills and rent, and tells of an older woman who, with no money tied up in stores to boycott, went to jail several times and had her rent paid and food waiting when she got out.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Where My Education Came From
clips.topic: Head Start and the War on Poverty
clips.summary: Jones closes by crediting the movement and the War on Poverty under President Johnson and Sargent Shriver, through which she went into early childhood education with Head Start, for her own schooling, and explains why she stands up whenever there is talk of cutting Head Start funds.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Born the Year the War Began
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in San Diego for the Civil Rights History Project, with Guha Shankar of the Library of Congress and cameraman John Bishop, Mateo Camarillo opens by placing his birth in Tijuana in 1941, his US-citizen mother Rufina who built B-29 bombers at Convair and worked the tuna factory in Barrio Logan, his father from Nuevo Leon, and the family's 1950 immigration to San Diego with his brother and two sisters.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: What Did She Say?
clips.topic: A Spanish Speaker in Second Grade
clips.summary: Camarillo describes a mother from Campo whose two brothers died serving in World War II, his own later sense of obligation to serve, and arriving in the second grade with no English and no bilingual education, failing the English test, and huddling with other Spanish-speaking children in a two-grade farm-labor schoolroom asking each other what the teacher had said.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: We Were Human Beings Just Like They Were
clips.summary: He recalls first thinking he and his classmates were mentally retarded because they could not communicate, learning English by trial and error on the playground where Anglo students wanted them on their teams, his mother's job in agricultural Mission Valley near the Junipero Serra mission, and starting work at age ten in an immigrant community that served as a support system.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Others Defined Us and We Accepted It
clips.summary: Camarillo traces how the family was identified as Mexican, then gradually called themselves Mexican American as they strove to participate, and how Chicano, at first a slur, was later adopted as a proud term for those struggling for equal rights, noting that schools taught nothing about Mexico so he stayed connected by visiting Tijuana.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: The Crossing Guard Captain
clips.summary: He describes building confidence and leadership through sports, heading the junior crossing-patrol guards, running cross country and track from junior high through Herbert Hoover High School, winning two scholarships including one from Allied Gardens, and becoming the first in his family to attend college while holding three jobs.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Viva Kennedy
clips.summary: Camarillo recounts graduating from San Diego State in four years, President Kennedy delivering the 1962 commencement address, his involvement in the Viva Kennedy Clubs, becoming a naturalized citizen at twenty-one through his mother, and launching the first multicultural voter registration project in San Diego with the Southwest Voter Registration Education Project, reaching African Americans, Asian Americans, and the county's fourteen Native American tribes.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: You're Not Even Going to Be Served
clips.topic: First Taste of the Deep South
clips.summary: Camarillo tells of volunteering for the Army in 1965 out of gratitude rather than fleeing to Canada or Mexico like some friends, being sent to Georgia because of spinal meningitis in California, and a Charleston, South Carolina restaurant where he, an African American, and a Puerto Rican soldier waited hours only to be told they would not be served.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: We Were All on the Same Side
clips.summary: He recounts being refused entry to Atlanta nightclubs for lack of a membership card, contrasting that blatant Southern discrimination with California, and describing the Army as very integrated, the track team they called the road rats, and a basic-training ethic of taking orders where Anglo officers led a mostly minority enlisted ranks.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Scoutmaster of Europe
clips.summary: Camarillo explains being spared Vietnam when the Army shifted toward experienced soldiers, sent instead to Germany as backfill in the Fourth Armored battalion, finishing a week of training-officer work in a single day, and being named Scoutmaster of the Transatlantic Council, traveling across Europe with the dependent children of soldiers.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Sixty Cases, or Six Hundred
clips.summary: Returning to San Diego, he joins the welfare department as one of very few Spanish-speaking social workers, where English-speaking colleagues carried caseloads of sixty families while he carried six hundred, prompting him to organize across probation, the sheriff, and other departments and to found Trabajadores de la Raza to recruit bilingual workers.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: A National Network of Workers
clips.summary: Camarillo describes Trabajadores de la Raza becoming a national organization with chapters from Los Angeles and San Jose to San Antonio, Houston, and the East Coast, the San Diego chapter serving all eighteen cities of the county, the work done after hours and funded at first through membership dues.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: A Bonus for Two Languages
clips.summary: He recounts persuading the county board of supervisors to pay a ten percent bonus to Spanish-speaking workers doing twice the work, then winning the same from the city and from San Diego Gas and Electric, and working with legislators Richard Alatorre and Mervyn Dymally to establish a statewide bilingual pay program in the late 1970s.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Bilingual Education Called Un-American
clips.topic: The Grand Jury and a Food Program
clips.summary: Camarillo names equal treatment as the era's central issue, telling how the San Diego County Grand Jury called bilingual education un-American until a fifteen-organization ad hoc committee demanded the jurors be replaced, and how, through Trabajadores de la Raza, the coalition won the county its first food program, San Diego being the only one of California's fifty-eight counties without one, starting in San Ysidro on the border with the San Ysidro Women's Club.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: They Risked Losing Their Jobs
clips.summary: As executive director of the Chicano Federation, an umbrella for Spanish-speaking organizations, Camarillo recounts county employees streaming into his office over job discrimination, gathering six Latino attorneys in 1974, and warning the workers that litigation could cost them their jobs, a risk they accepted in pursuit of justice.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Sixty-Seven Percent Turned Down
clips.summary: He describes suing San Diego County under newly passed equal employment opportunity law in what he calls the longest-lasting civil rights case in US history, joined by African American, Asian American, and Filipino plaintiffs, overturning a system in which sixty-seven percent of Hispanic applicants were rejected, and reflecting that no single group could have removed the barriers alone because all the county's minority communities shared the same lack of opportunity.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Not the Intent of Congress
clips.summary: Camarillo recounts how Nixon's General Revenue Sharing sent no-strings funds to local government, how the Chicano Federation with chairman Dr. Alfredo Velasco allied with the largely Anglo Community Congress of the hippie beach communities and traveled to Washington, and how they rallied social service agencies and United Way to sue when the city tried to redefine who was poor or senior, terms already fixed by the Older Americans Act, winning over five million dollars the first year and over six million the next.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Chapters Across the Country
clips.summary: He describes staying in communication with Trabajadores de la Raza chapters in Los Angeles, San Jose, Houston, New York, and Denver, the Spanish-speaking organizations being the largest ethnic group, and joining the staff of the Chicano Federation, the first multi-discipline Spanish-speaking organization in the county, as executive director.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: I Trained Under Saul Alinsky
clips.topic: San Jose State and the School of Social Work
clips.summary: Camarillo recounts being recruited by the strong San Jose chapter to help start a school of social work, using his GI Bill to earn a master's in community organizing at San Diego State while working for the county, and winning a Ford Foundation Fellowship to train under Saul Alinsky in Chicago, the organizer who he says trained Cesar Chavez and the founders of the National Council of La Raza.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Recruiting Herman Gallegos
clips.summary: He describes leaving San Diego State to become coordinator of field instruction at San Jose, building the school of social work, and recruiting Herman Gallegos, a National Council of La Raza founder who grew up in Sal Si Puedes alongside Cesar Chavez and who knew how to access Ford and Rockefeller Foundation capital the state would not provide.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: They Took the Land With Picks and Shovels
clips.summary: Camarillo explains that his Chicano Federation offices sat under the San Diego-Coronado Bridge, and recounts how, when the city reneged on a promised park and planned a highway patrol station on the state land, his sister Tomasa Camarillo led the community to physically occupy the site and build Chicano Park by hand.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: The Birth of the Mural Movement
clips.summary: He describes the artists among the Chicano Park steering committee deciding to beautify the raw cement bridge pillars, giving birth to the mural movement that depicts the history of the Chicano movement, and the federally funded facility built there instead of a highway patrol station that housed the Brown Berets, the Pinto Union of ex-convicts, state employment counseling, and welfare social workers in a humane, multicultural demonstration project.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: We Exchanged Phone Numbers
clips.topic: An Alliance With the Police Chief
clips.summary: He describes testifying for new police chief Bill Kolender, who as a Jewish man understood discrimination, the two agreeing to meet regularly and trade phone numbers so a single call could stop police in hot pursuit from running through his building full of seniors and women, and a similar standing arrangement with Mayor Pete Wilson.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: You Better Get Your Ass Down Here
clips.summary: Camarillo tells how two officers off shift at 2 a.m. donned gloves and beat members of the Brown Image low-rider car club and their girlfriends in Barrio Logan, how he summoned Mayor Pete Wilson, Chief Kolender, and the city manager to a 5 p.m. meeting with five hundred low riders where the police apologized, and how a single call to Kolender also ended police directing taxi drivers to take Mexican-looking residents to immigration, the kind of communication that spared San Diego the riots of Watts and Detroit.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Stupid They Are Not
clips.summary: Camarillo argues that officials paid attention because Hispanics were the largest minority and held the power of the vote, that public officials prefer peaceful resolution to violence in which they too might be hurt, and that the movement worked through litigation, negotiation, and mediation first, joining Cesar Chavez's nonviolent boycott marches when needed.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: When the Federation Spoke
clips.summary: He describes drawing a salary leading a federation whose board were heads of other organizations, so it spoke for all the county's Spanish-speaking groups, and using a political action committee and self-formed advisory commissions to influence mayors, supervisors, and assembly members at a time when San Diego rarely had even two Latino council members.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Don't Bite the Hand That Feeds You
clips.summary: Camarillo explains that suing the city, county, and state while taking their grants was unsustainable, so in 1976 he chose the private sector for an independent income, researching the food industry and settling on franchising as a proven system to learn business.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Why Am I Not Qualified?
clips.summary: Turned down twice as unqualified despite knowing Jack in the Box founder Robert Silverman, Camarillo recounts inviting the elected officials he knew, the congressman, councilman, supervisors, and assemblyman, to his third meeting with regional manager Dean, since restaurants need over a dozen government permits, asking Dean to explain to them why he was not qualified, winning an immediate yes, and going on to build half a dozen radio stations and over a dozen other businesses while giving free french fries for March Fong Eu's voter registration drive.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: A Voice for Spanish Speakers
clips.summary: Camarillo explains buying radio stations because information is freedom, San Diego having no Spanish-language stations and only distant signals from Tijuana and Los Angeles indifferent to local news, and recounts applying in 1980 after Reagan's deregulation freed a 1040 AM frequency on the West Coast, competing against applicants statewide and prevailing with his multicultural company Quetzal Bilingual Communications, named for the Aztec bird.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: How Many Meals Can You Eat?
clips.summary: He describes leveraging the win to build satellite-connected stations up the California coast, the Port Commission calling him to broadcast announcements when junkyards, Monsanto, and the tuna factory released toxic gas over Barrio Logan, and reflects that he has dedicated his life to public service even in the private sector, giving away product and registering voters without pay because he believes America is about helping your fellow man.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: A Little Sugar in the Conversation
clips.summary: Camarillo explains pressuring officials with common sense and softening it with money through political action committees, organizing MacPack among McDonald's franchisees and the Hispanic Golden 500 whose members were taxed five hundred dollars, and contributing to Jerry Brown, Gray Davis, and Cruz Bustamante.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Six Judges at Once
clips.summary: He recounts that when Jerry Brown, sympathetic and dating Linda Ronstadt, had six judicial openings in San Diego, Camarillo nominated all six Hispanic lawyers and all were appointed, that he was placed on a pension-fund board, and that even Pete Wilson took his contributions despite a hostility to Hispanics whose anti-immigrant resolution he argues cost Republicans every statewide race for over twenty years except Arnold Schwarzenegger's.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: A Horrific Graduation Rate
clips.summary: He observes that public schools have changed but not enough, over half of entering students being Hispanic yet the graduation rate horrific, defending bilingual education as a transitional starting point and the California push for multicultural curriculum so students learn about their own cultures in a Southwest that was Mexican for centuries.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: La Raza Cosmica
clips.summary: Camarillo recounts a college teacher expert in the Aztecs, Mayans, and Toltecs inspiring him to name companies Quetzal, Quinto Sol, and Aztlan, arguing that knowing other cultures is the sign of an educated person, and tracing the term La Raza to Jose Vasconcelos's post-Revolution book La Raza Cosmica.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Pardon for Those Already Here
clips.summary: He describes joining the National Council of La Raza, serving the maximum terms, working with President Reagan and Senators Orrin Hatch and Edward Kennedy on the immigration reform Reagan signed, which he frames as legalizing people already here and employed, and serving over twenty years on the Greenlining Institute board, named as the opposite of redlining, chairing the state's telephone deregulation and delivering services in ten languages.
related_events: Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Hot Lunches Over Education
clips.summary: Camarillo describes his first run for office, for the district-wide school board because education is key, against a senior citizen backed by an assemblyman's resources, and his post-election analysis finding only nineteen percent of voters had children in school while over sixty percent were seniors interested in hot lunches.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: A Brother to Defend Him
clips.summary: He recounts running next for the Southwestern Community College district adjacent to Tijuana, where most underserved students go after high school, securing a unanimous promise of support from Latino leaders, only to be undercut when a professor in trouble had his brother run, splitting the Hispanic vote against a wealthy Coronado candidate.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: One Person, One Vote
clips.topic: Redrawing the Lines and a Last Run
clips.summary: Camarillo describes thirty years of redistricting beginning in 1980, redrawing state assembly and senate lines, then county lines, then being selected by judges to redistrict the city of San Diego, and chairing the Latino redistricting committee after the 2010 census to create the first majority-Latino Ninth District, where he was asked to run for city council but fewer than ten percent of voters turned out and a well-known newscaster incumbent won, his last campaign.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Breathalyzers and a German Maker
clips.summary: Camarillo describes the for-profit business with a social benefit he runs with his wife, installing breathalyzer devices in the cars of people cited for driving under the influence and representing a German manufacturer that has made gas-activation devices since World War I, and recounts his wife pushing him to write his autobiography Journey in Search of the American Dream, reaffirming that everything meaningful he has done was done with others.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: A Country of Immigrants
clips.summary: Camarillo argues the United States is a country of immigrants going back before the Pilgrims, symbolized by the French gift of the Statue of Liberty, and that immigrants take jobs others reject, recalling strawberries rotting in Oregon's Willamette Valley for want of workers and schools closing early so children could pick them.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Walls Are Not the Answer
clips.summary: He notes research showing immigrants contribute billions and high-tech innovation, yet face the charge of taking jobs and newer fears since the Middle East wars of hostile arrivals, and references a presidential candidate talking of building walls and deporting, concluding that a regulated process makes sense but walls do not.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Get It Into the Hands of Those Who Need It
clips.summary: Camarillo closes by saying he is honored to share experiences of contributions long ignored, expressing his wish to learn how the project's end product will be distributed and made accessible, since education opens minds to the full society, before the recording ends with the standard Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: A Veteran Before the Law School
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in June 2011 at the federal courthouse that bears his name in Columbia, South Carolina, Judge Matthew J. Perry Jr. asks to begin before law school, recounting that he was born in Columbia in 1921, was a South Carolina State College student when Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, received his draft notice, and reported for duty in January 1943 to a segregated Quartermaster Corps that trained at Camp Van Dorn, Mississippi, having grown up accustomed to the racial segregation imposed by law.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: A Window Outside the Kitchen
clips.summary: Perry describes the awakening that began as he grew intellectually and questioned segregation's justification, centering on a weekend pass home by train at the end of basic training, when he changed trains in Birmingham, Alabama, and during a two-hour layover in damp weather could not enter the terminal restaurant, having to order a sandwich through a window outside the kitchen alongside other Black soldiers and passengers.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: They Served the Italian Prisoners
clips.summary: He recounts looking into that restaurant and seeing Italian prisoners of war, enemy combatants in uniform, being served courteously by young waitresses while he, an American soldier in his country's uniform fresh from basic training, was denied the privilege, a contrast that turned familiar indignity into a feeling of insult and left him wondering what could be done, a memory that stayed with him as he served in England, France, Belgium, and Germany before his discharge in January 1946.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Two Cases Tried Back to Back
clips.summary: Returning to Columbia to finish his undergraduate work at South Carolina State, Perry tells how he and fellow veterans went to the federal court on Laurel Street as spectators and watched two cases tried back to back, the first brought by George Elmore against South Carolina election officials challenging the white primaries that denied Black citizens the vote, argued by Thurgood Marshall, whom Perry had never seen before, and Robert L. Carter, building on the Smith v. Allwright precedent he only later understood.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: A Name We Found Distasteful
clips.summary: Perry describes the second case, brought by John H. Wrighten, a young Black American suing for entry to the University of South Carolina's law school he and other Black students could not attend, and notes that South Carolina State College, the only state-supported Black institution, was named in statute the colored normal agricultural and mechanical college at Orangeburg, a name students found distasteful and popularly replaced.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: What Will My Life's Work Be
clips.summary: Impressed by the lawyers' performance and contemplating his life's work as he developed into manhood, Perry recounts weighing business and medicine before deciding against them, drawn instead toward a field that might equip him to understand the reasoning behind the racial practices he had become aware of and to help find solutions, settling privately on the study of law at Howard University where the lawyers he admired had trained.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: A Jim Crow Dump
clips.summary: Perry recounts Judge J. Waties Waring deciding for Elmore in the voting case and for Wrighten in the school case, ordering South Carolina either to admit Wrighten to the university law school, provide a separate but substantially equal one, or cease offering legal education, and how the state opened the very inadequate law school at South Carolina State that Thurgood Marshall called a Jim Crow dump, hiring a dean and teachers while Perry finished his undergraduate studies.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: You Better Understand What You Face
clips.summary: Perry tells how the new law school needed students and word spread of a veteran named Perry who claimed he would study law, prompting someone to urge him to sit in and understand what he would face at Howard, and how, having only part-time courses, he monitored classes and ended up remaining at the institution, where well-trained teachers from Harvard, Columbia, Kansas, and Iowa instilled a concept of hard work.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Blame Me for the Bar Exam
clips.summary: Perry explains South Carolina's diploma privilege, by which law graduates were admitted without a bar examination, and how officials creating the South Carolina State school persuaded the legislature to require the examination of all graduates, so that his 1951 class was the first to sit it while the prior class of 1950 was exempt, and he, the only one of his group to pass, jokes that anyone who has taken the bar since 1951 can blame him.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Rubbernecking Into Briggs v. Elliott
clips.summary: Having just graduated and taken the bar with results still unpublished, Perry travels with a few others to Charleston for the well-publicized Briggs v. Elliott trial, a major challenge to segregated education, and recalls hordes of people and how, being tall with a long neck, he rubbernecked over the crowd and squeezed into the courtroom to witness the trial, then turns to the difficulty of earning a living as one of the few Black lawyers in South Carolina.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: A One-Room Office and an Underwood
clips.summary: Unable to find any job or law clerk position, Perry explains that opening his own office became inevitable, and how on his mother and a successful funeral-home cousin's advice he chose Spartanburg, which had no Black attorneys while Columbia with about three was considered crowded, setting up a one-room secondhand office with an old Underwood typewriter and typing his own documents, struggling to attract business as a young inexperienced Black lawyer until his cousin's bond signing brought a first traffic case to municipal court.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: A Notice of Appeal in the Briefcase
clips.summary: Perry recounts his first foray into the municipal court of a judge he calls a racist of the first order, who let young white lawyers' clients off with small fines but found Perry's client guilty without hearing evidence and imposed the maximum thirty days or a hundred dollars, a crushing result for his reputation, and how he learned to draft a notice of appeal within the five-day window on grounds the decision was contrary to the evidence, so that on a later case he pulled the notice from his still-new briefcase, asked the shocked judge to accept service, told him it had been his decision every time, and won a reversal before the circuit judge.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Day Number Six in Union County
clips.summary: Perry tells how word spread and calls came from adjoining counties, including the Union County Voters League about a young illiterate man given a gargantuan sentence by a magistrate, who reached Perry only on the sixth day after the five-day appeal window had expired, leading him to file a petition for a writ of habeas corpus for the jailed man whose family had no resources, and to win his first victory in the South Carolina Supreme Court.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: A Cooperating Attorney
clips.summary: Perry explains becoming associated with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund as a cooperating attorney, an unpaid role that drew a small stipend per supported case, and separately with the NAACP Office of General Counsel, two distinct organizations whose division the public did not know, attending seminars and national conventions where lawyers called in from around the country formed a network of associates, friendships, and shared knowledge of what was happening in various places.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Moot Court at Howard
clips.summary: Perry recalls traveling to Washington with South Carolina colleagues when the cases were set for Supreme Court argument and how they were first argued moot-court style at Howard University Law School, where professors and distinguished lawyers mimicked the sitting justices and their judicial philosophies, an exhilarating dry run featuring Thurgood Marshall, Robert L. Carter, Spottswood W. Robinson III, and Jack Greenberg, with Constance Baker Motley then a neophyte on staff who argued neither case.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: To the Back of the Bus
clips.summary: Perry turns to Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company, describing Columbia's segregated bus service that required Black riders to the rear under state law, and the case of Sarah Mae Fleming, a young domestic who sat near the dividing line and, ordered by the driver to move and barred from the front door, was shoved and claimed she was struck in the abdomen, after which she made her situation known to local people.
related_events: Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: The First One on the Record
clips.summary: Perry recounts Modjeska Monteith Simkins sending Fleming to white attorney Phil Wittenberg, who filed suit, incurred the white community's wrath including a cross burned in his yard, and got out of the case after the Fourth Circuit reversed a dismissal, after which Perry joined Lincoln Jenkins Jr. at the trial level and lost before an unfriendly judge though the precedent stood, concluding that Fleming's case, set in motion June 22, 1954, was the first bus case on the record, preceding Browder v. Gayle and Rosa Parks in Montgomery, before the interview closes in the courthouse named for him.
related_events: Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: The Crater Petersburg Never Forgot
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline at Wesley Theological Seminary on December 14, 2015, Maynard E. Moore, a Washington resident of 35 years, says he was born in Petersburg, Virginia in 1938, and recounts the Civil War battle there in which Union troops tunneled under the Confederate works and blew them up by an accidental trigger, the site now preserved as the Crater and remembered as a prominent event in the town's history.

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: Washington Street and the Methodist Schism
clips.summary: Moore describes Petersburg as a commercial railhead for tobacco boosted by the Army quartermaster depot at Fort Lee in World War II, his Methodist family at Washington Street United Methodist Church among the town's ten Methodist churches, and a placard marking his home church as the 1846 site where the Methodist Episcopal Church, South broke from the northern church over slavery, the church where he felt the call to ministry and was elected to statewide office in the Methodist Student Movement.

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: No More Welcome in the House
clips.summary: Moore recalls that racial issues went unaddressed by design in his childhood, that he lived on a main street while Black residents lived on the less cared-for cross streets, and that he played with Black children and invited them in to watch Howdy Doody until, at the time for junior high school, his mother explained they would no longer be welcome in the house.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: If You Can Read, You Can Do Anything
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline at his Los Angeles home in June 2016, storyteller Michael D. McCarty, born in Chicago in 1950 and raised on the West Side, credits his Barbadian mother with making him a reader and instilling the conviction that if you saw something wrong you were obligated to act, the lesson that turned an aspiring physicist and astronaut into a revolutionary.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Everything but a Child of God
clips.topic: Racism in Cicero and a Mob
clips.summary: McCarty describes the sundown town of Cicero where a Black person caught after dark could be killed, an early scare at a gas station, and the Boy Scout cycling trip near Crestwood where, drinking at a water fountain by a Little League game, he looked up to find an all-white crowd charging him, led by a small white woman with a brick.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: No Johnny, Don't Do It
