clips.summary: He recounts the night a South Side gang beat him and a friend until they tired, a young man putting a gun to his head and pulling the trigger only for a girl he calls an angel to snatch his hand away, and the white police officer who, indifferent to his injuries, drove him back into danger, an experience he calls his personal wake-up call.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: A Black Student Organization
clips.summary: McCarty tells how he joined the NAACP Youth Council and, with his friend Tyler, started a Black student organization at St. Ignatius College Prep, a mostly white school set in the Black community, where the Jesuits initially supported panels on race relations sent to other Catholic schools.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Thirty-Nine Years to a Diploma
clips.summary: He describes the list of demands for Black studies and more minority students, the press-covered walkout that got him and Tyler expelled in his senior year of 1969, his mother weeping at the news, and how, thirty-nine years later, classmate Greg Myers organized the school to apologize and grant the diplomas at a ceremony with city and congressional honors.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: I Ain't Seen Nothing Like Chicago
clips.summary: McCarty recalls Martin Luther King staying on Hamlin near Douglas Park during the Chicago campaign, his friend Tyler exchanging a greeting with King, King's remark that he had never seen racism like Chicago's, a priest preaching racism from the pulpit in Mayor Daley's neighborhood, and white residents in Bridgeport threatening Black students doing a summer job.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: The Political Education Cadre
clips.summary: After his expulsion he became a full-time member of the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party, drawn by Ramparts coverage of the armed Sacramento protest, found his old track acquaintance Billy Che Brooks at the Madison Street office, and joined the education cadre running reading-based political education classes, sometimes speaking in place of Fred Hampton.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: That's Not How We Do Things
clips.summary: He remembers Fred Hampton, assassinated at twenty-one yet an old soul who had organized Maywood high schools over a segregated pool, as a leader who taught him commitment and turned away a recruit who wanted a gun to kill white people, insisting the party was anti-injustice rather than anti-white and that race was a tool used to divide people who should work together.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Them Black Panthers Going for Us
clips.summary: McCarty explains how Fred Hampton and the Illinois chapter built the Original Rainbow Coalition with the Young Patriots of poor Appalachian whites and a former Puerto Rican street gang, and tells how field secretary Bob Lee won over a skeptical white community and was later freed from a police car by white men, women, and children who surrounded it.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: We Knew but We Didn't Know
clips.summary: He describes the party's work turning street gangs like the Black P. Stone Nation political, the FBI's COINTELPRO effort to sabotage that work with planted letters and the security chief William O'Neal as an informant, the justified paranoia that resulted, and the activists' 1971 break-in at the Media, Pennsylvania FBI office that exposed COINTELPRO.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: The Most Dangerous Thing We Did
clips.summary: McCarty recounts the FBI driving a Los Angeles actress and party supporter to suicide with a false rumor, the party's resolve to keep serving through the breakfast program and People's Free Medical Clinics that J. Edgar Hoover called their most dangerous work, and the wider view of the chapters he gained at the party's fortieth anniversary in Oakland.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Three Counties Where Blacks Did Not Vote
clips.topic: A Family's Push for the Vote
clips.summary: Interviewed with her husband present, Mildred Bond Roxborough opens on her childhood in Haywood County, Tennessee, one of three counties in the state where Black citizens could not vote, and recounts how her parents campaigned to register and organized the Black majority who outnumbered whites three to one, in Brownsville, the county seat where she was born.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Get Walter White to Send Us a Charter
clips.summary: Roxborough describes her parents organizing the first NAACP branch in Brownsville, needing people who could withstand the anticipated pressure, and writing away for a charter from executive secretary Walter White, and recalls being about seven and reading The Crisis magazine her parents subscribed to.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Can I Write to Roy Wilkins?
clips.summary: She recounts learning of The Crisis through her mother reading Langston Hughes aloud, and at nine writing editor Roy Wilkins herself to ask permission to sell subscriptions, treasuring the signed reply she received from the editor.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Take Care of Your Paw, He's Been Hurt
clips.summary: Roxborough tells how by 1939 her father was branch president and her mother secretary, how he was repeatedly arrested, and how law officers dumped him badly beaten on the couch while she was home alone at nine, saying he would be in jail if he lacked connections.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Only the Howard Theater Welcomed Us
clips.topic: From Dr. Severe to Segregated Washington
clips.summary: Her mother explains that the white county coroner Dr. Severe, her grandmother's half brother, likely interceded to keep her father out of jail; then, recalling her wartime years at Howard from 1943 to 1945 and later NYU's Washington Square campus, Roxborough describes attending the Howard Theater because other theaters admitted Black patrons only on a segregated basis, students testing restaurants, and the uniformed servicemen in the federal training program.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Why Are You Doing That to That Girl?
clips.summary: Roxborough recounts joining the NAACP staff in the fall of 1953 to run a six-week membership campaign for Gloster B. Current in Baltimore, Thurgood Marshall laughing that Current should be ashamed for sending her to the demanding Dr. Lillie M. Jackson, and surviving the experience with Jackson's daughter Juanita Jackson Mitchell and the support of Henry G. Parks Jr. and the gambler known as Little Willie Adams.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: I Never Heard So Many Cuss Words
clips.summary: She gives her impressions of Thurgood Marshall as a profane, humorous raconteur who could make difficult things sound easy and talked her into Hoxie, Arkansas, and of Roy Wilkins as an elegant, unpretentious purist who rode the nickel subway, refused bodyguards, and demanded staff dedicate themselves to the work and present the NAACP as a serious organization.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: He Could Live in Two Worlds
clips.summary: Roxborough describes Walter White as a sociable man who loved the spotlight and, able to pass as Caucasian, investigated lynchings in Mississippi, Georgia, and Arkansas without being assaulted, then turns to Daisy Bates of Little Rock, a stylish, talkative people-person who drew barely literate members into the NAACP during the period leading to the integration of Central High under segregationist governor Orval Faubus.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Quietly Militant
clips.summary: She remembers staying in Rosa Parks's Montgomery home before Parks left Alabama, describing Parks as a charming, laconic branch secretary, quietly militant with her husband and focused on organizing youth, and recalls E.D. Nixon as a dedicated figure who refused to surrender the Alabama membership rolls to the attorney general before the NAACP was banned in the state.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Mississippi Going Out of Bounds
clips.summary: Roxborough recalls the office reaction to Emmett Till's murder, coming just after the killing of branch president Gus Courts amid the voter-registration push, the sense that nothing was sacred in Mississippi, and arranging a tour of branches for Till's mother, Mamie Till Bradley, who grew more militant and insisted on the open casket at the Chicago funeral.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: If She's Here, She'll Help Me
clips.summary: Roxborough tells how, while executive assistant to Roy Wilkins, she was called to the front office to meet a man who turned out to be Clarence Norris, a fugitive Scottsboro defendant, who asked for the office manager Bobbie Branch by name; Branch welcomed him and arranged for him to meet Dr. John Morsell.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: I Want to Clear My Name Before I Die
clips.summary: She explains that after the Communist Party's earlier involvement had led the NAACP to step aside, Norris returned asking first for money and then to clear his name, and how General Counsel Nathaniel R. Jones assigned attorney James I. Meyerson, leading Meyerson, Wilkins, Jones, and Norris to Alabama to secure a full pardon from the governor.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Every Five Years a New Job
clips.summary: After noting the Scottsboro story produced the book The Last of the Scottsboro Boys, Roxborough clarifies she was never director of education (June Shagaloff was) but held many roles, and describes the ACT-SO academic, cultural, scientific Olympics founded by Benjamin L. Hooks and Vernon Jarrett to develop youth achievement across twenty-four fields through local, regional, and national competitions, a program grown to some 600 units with the Metropolitan New York chapter the largest.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Wilkins in Elegant Prose, Hooks in the Gospel Style
clips.summary: She recounts Roy Wilkins succeeding Walter White, who died suddenly in 1955 while on leave after Wilkins had served as acting executive, and contrasts the intellectual Wilkins, who laid out facts in elegant prose, with his successor Benjamin L. Hooks, a dramatic Baptist minister who used a gospel approach to inspire constituents and keep the branches engaged.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: We Forget Our History
clips.summary: Noting the roughly 1700 branches and 500 youth and college units and asked why the NAACP's achievements are not better known after a century, Roxborough says people do not focus on their history and take for granted hard-won gains like school desegregation, public accommodations, and Boynton v. Virginia, forgetting the organization that was often the only instrument able to achieve them.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: All of Us Could Sit at That Counter
clips.summary: Roxborough credits the SCLC for bringing religious leaders and their congregations into the struggle and moving flexibly community to community, and praises the student sit-ins of SNCC for showing older people a simple act anyone could do, walking into a lunch counter and refusing to move, which inspired adults to demonstrate.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: It Veered Toward Separatism
clips.summary: She describes CORE as a more intellectual, racially integrated group under James Farmer, a former NAACP program director, that drew younger members and encouraged white participation, and judges the Black Panther Party as a militant group with energy that she believed misdirected itself toward Black separatism she considered self-defeating.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Done Without Any Legal Action
clips.summary: Rejecting the idea that the NAACP changed things only through the courts, Roxborough cites Executive Order 8802, won through the association's lobbying and the relationship among Walter White, Eleanor Roosevelt, and A. Philip Randolph, and the first Civil Rights Law passed through the Washington Bureau's lobbying rather than litigation.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Thirty-Six Times Before the Supreme Court
clips.summary: She suggests the NAACP is seen as primarily a legal body because its lawyers appeared so often before the Supreme Court, General Counsel Robert Carter making some thirty-six appearances and winning about thirty, and Thurgood Marshall arguing more than two dozen, even though grassroots lobbying was equally essential to the progress won.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Discipline Could Be Achieved in Leadership
clips.summary: Roxborough offers her opinion that Malcolm X showed discipline could be achieved in leadership, drawing former street people into a cohesive group, that his death seriously affected the community, and that especially among NAACP youth there were those who admired his discipline and conviction even as he modified his early near-anarchic rhetoric to pressure the power structure.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: The Lead Lawyer in Those Cases
clips.summary: Roxborough describes her husband John, who chaired the Legal Redress Committee of the Detroit branch and was lead lawyer in housing and education cases won before the Michigan Supreme Court, work she encountered while organizing the branch's first Freedom Fund life membership dinner with Arthur L. Johnson, and which earned commendations from Thurgood Marshall's national office.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: It's the Leadership
clips.summary: Reflecting that she and John shared a civil rights interest in their early days, Roxborough answers what separates an effective branch from a lackluster one: leadership, the extent to which leaders inspire the board and members determines how creative and forceful the branch becomes.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: We Are Not Going to Give Those Lists Up
clips.summary: Roxborough recounts the defiant national-office response when Alabama and another state demanded the membership rolls, and how Ruby Hurley, who had organized southeastern units from Birmingham before the ban moved her office to Atlanta, returned with volunteers to work under cover of night, her life repeatedly threatened, getting people to register and continue the fight.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Send People to Fill the Void
clips.summary: She describes the New York office sending people into Mississippi after Medgar Evers's assassination to reassure constituents the NAACP was not intimidated, with Evers's brother Charles taking over as field director and later becoming a Mississippi mayor.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: That's My Shotgun You Have Your Feet On
clips.summary: Roxborough recounts being sent to Mississippi in the 1960s to interview Delta farmers denied winter credit as economic reprisal for NAACP activity, riding with field director Medgar Evers, discovering her bare feet resting on his shotgun one night, and driving back each evening to a motel in the Black town of Mound Bayou amid two recent branch-president killings.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Carefully Tended for Their Security
clips.summary: Roxborough recalls meeting the Little Rock students as a carefully guarded group at gatherings Daisy Bates arranged before Central High's integration, and remembers most the quiet determination of Ernest Green, who later joined the NAACP board, and the strong personality of Minnijean Brown, while the others moved forward quietly wanting to finish school.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: You Didn't Have Many Women Beyond Support Levels
clips.topic: Ella Baker and the Early Women
clips.summary: Roxborough says she knew Septima Clark and Bernice Robinson only by reputation, recalls the forceful Ella Baker, who worked in the Department of Branches and helped found SNCC, and notes how few women rose beyond support roles in those years, citing Lucille Black, who began as a secretary at seventeen and became membership secretary, and Bobbie Branch, who rose from clerk to office manager.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: No Eye Was Not Dotted
clips.summary: She remembers Jack Greenberg as a young lawyer working alongside Thurgood Marshall on the education cases assembled in some six or eight communities from the South to Kansas that became Brown, and Bob Carter as the quieter second attorney who meticulously built the South Carolina cases, leaving nothing unturned in work that could feel laborious to his researchers.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: The Organizing Meeting Was a Woman
clips.summary: Roxborough contrasts the clerical roles women held at headquarters with the branch presidencies they won, and names women central to the NAACP: Daisy E. Lampkin, who traveled organizing branches in the 1930s and 1940s; Althea T.L. Simmons, who headed the Washington Bureau after Clarence Mitchell; Constance Baker Motley in the legal defense fund; and Frances Hooks, who founded WIN, the women's structure within the NAACP.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: A Female Brought the Report Back
clips.summary: Roxborough notes the second female board chair and her successor Roslyn M. Brock, then recounts that the investigation of lynchings in Arkansas and Georgia behind the silent protest march in New York was carried out by a woman who brought the 1918 report to W.E.B. Du Bois, who received the publicity while the woman did the work that caused the 1919 march.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Send $5
clips.summary: Asked about W.E.B. Du Bois, who predated her at the NAACP, Roxborough says she met him only briefly and once wrote for a copy of one of his papers, which he agreed to send for five dollars, and declines to repeat colleagues' stories about him rather than mislead the history.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Don't Hide Your Light Under a Bushel
clips.summary: Roxborough resists naming a single proud accomplishment, explaining she worked full-time until 1997 and moved every five years into a new department, holding eight or ten jobs in nearly every part of the association except accounting, finding satisfaction in learning how the NAACP functioned and how she could make it work better.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: An Education in Diplomacy and Self-Protection
clips.summary: She describes the singular experience of traveling as a young unattached woman in the early 1950s, guarding against male-female complications and navigating branch presidents' authority, learning diplomacy and self-protection, then rising from field secretary through executive assistant, director of operations and programs, to development director, taking courses in the technical side of fundraising.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Walking the Children to School
clips.summary: Roxborough names keeping the Hoxie, Arkansas parents inspired and walking their children to school as a source of satisfaction, and reflects that in danger, such as crossing cotton fields at night to local meetings under patrol, the fear came only afterward because there was no time to be afraid in the moment.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: You're an Idiot If You Get Afraid
clips.summary: She relays her parents' lesson that fear makes you your own worst enemy and recalls traveling some forty states over five years as a field secretary, including small Arkansas towns like El Dorado and Cotton Plant where a visitor from the New York NAACP was the worst thing the white power structure could hear of, with Mississippi, Georgia, and Alabama the most tense.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: People Felt They Could Get Help
clips.summary: Roxborough closes reflecting that staying in fearful families' homes and bringing them danger made her more humane and convinced her people are imperfect but worth any help, and that communities believed the NAACP would act on their behalf, placing on its leaders the responsibility to be effective and inspire confidence; the interview ends with thanks.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: The Only New Book Was Carter G. Woodson
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in San Mateo, California, on March 1, 2013, Mildred Pitts Walter opens with her birth in Louisiana in 1922, her father's work with the Long-Bell Lumber Company, and growing up in De Ridder between Lake Charles and Shreveport, at a parish high school where the only new book she ever had was Carter G. Woodson's history of the Negro while every other book had pages torn out.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Humility Could Not Be Humiliated
clips.summary: Walter describes her all-Black school, the new building with electric light wired by a capable principal, the African American teachers who taught the children they were not inferior, and the church and ministers who insisted they hold their heads high, do everything with excellence, and meet those who hated them with a humility that could not be humiliated.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: She Insisted I Sit and Eat
clips.summary: She recounts working from the age of seven, minding a white child, cleaning a beauty shop and homes while attending high school, and a woman from Ohio whose husband was a soldier at the nearby camp during World War II, who hired her, insisted she sit at the table and eat, gave her magazines to discuss, and said she was the only person there she felt comfortable with.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: To Earn Enough to Finish Southern
clips.topic: Southern University and the War North
clips.summary: Walter recalls attending Southern University in Scotlandville, Louisiana, where leaders such as Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and W.E.B. Du Bois came to inspire the students, and how in her senior year she followed a sister to Longview, Washington, to work in the shipyards and earn enough money to finish Southern.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: They Laughed at My Application
clips.summary: She describes applying for a personnel job at the shipyards with experience working under the dean of women, only to be laughed at amid wartime discrimination, ending up cleaning ships and working in gyro rooms, and waking her exhausted Black and white coworkers on the graveyard shift before the supervisor arrived.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: All the Water in the World
clips.summary: On her first time outside the South, Walter observes that African Americans in the little Washington town lived in a segregated neighborhood though children could attend an integrated school, and remembers seeing the Pacific Ocean for the first time, the roses of Portland, Oregon, and Mount St. Helens snow-capped beyond her back door.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Fifty Dollars a Month to Teach
clips.summary: Walter explains that after the shipyards she would not work for the wages Louisiana paid, where a college graduate could only teach or do maintenance, and she turned down a Shreveport offer of fifty dollars a month to teach high school English and run the library, while her mother warned that college washes you down so you no longer fit the place.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Go See Mary
clips.topic: A Healer Mother and Her Cures
clips.summary: The youngest of seven and the first to attend college, Walter describes her mother, a midwife and healer of little formal education who cured pneumonia before penicillin with a tea made from moldy pig feed, treated kidney problems with red-hot oyster shells in cooled water, and to whom doctors sent patients they could not help, saying go see Mary.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: A Woman on the Bus Named Mozelle Moore
clips.summary: Walter recounts going to Los Angeles in 1944, staying first in a Skid Row hotel on Fifth Street, finding no YWCA for Black women, and meeting on a bus a friendly, intelligent woman she had been taught she could trust, Mozelle Moore, who took her in, introduced her to her sorority sisters, and set her on her way.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: A California Credential
clips.summary: She describes finding work right away in Los Angeles, where there was only one Black high school teacher and a California credential was required, so she worked first as a clerk in the classified section of the Los Angeles City Schools, returned to California State University, Los Angeles for her credential, and then began teaching elementary school.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: You Don't Want to Get Involved With Him
clips.summary: Walter tells of meeting her husband at a Methodist church, warned by a sorority sister that he had many girlfriends, of his lending her money for a hat and dress because he knew she would not want anything held over her, and of marrying in 1947 a man already active since 1942 in CORE and an anti-segregation organization called Drew.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Negotiating the Stores and Banks
clips.summary: She describes CORE's growing work by 1958 around Central Avenue in Los Angeles, picketing and negotiating with stores and banks that hired Black people only as janitors, winning sales and boxboy jobs and management training in the grocery stores, and pressing the more reluctant banks until they hired tellers and trained people for management.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Testing the Law With Suitcases
clips.summary: Walter recounts CORE's interracial work and its alliance with the ACLU, and how, after Jesse Unruh's 1959 bill outlawed segregated accommodations, members drove to motels with suitcases and children to be denied rooms a white couple would then receive, suing with a pro bono lawyer on the conviction that a law is worth little until it has been tested.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Frederick Douglass on the Wall
clips.summary: Walter explains that acting against injustice came naturally from being taught to resist with dignity, that she began teaching in 1955 among many young African American teachers, and that as an active, demonstrating teacher who put Frederick Douglass on the wall beside Washington and Lincoln, she once overheard a vice principal tell a white pupil he need not stay at the school.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Beautiful Because Teacher Told Me So
clips.summary: She describes wearing her hair natural since the 1950s, inspired by an African friend, when almost no one but the singer Abbey Lincoln did, her husband's pride in it, setting out brown paint so children could paint themselves, and the precious moment a girl called black in tears came days later to declare herself beautiful because her teacher told her so.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: If You Write Them, I'll Publish Them
clips.topic: From Lillie of Watts to Reading Rainbow
clips.summary: Walter recounts how, wanting positive books for her children when so few existed, a Los Angeles publisher who issued her Lillie of Watts told her that if she wrote the books he would publish them, and how after many rejection slips a young Harvard graduate at Scholastic took her up, leading to editor Barbara Lalicki and Ty's One-Man Band, the very first book on Reading Rainbow.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: My Purpose Was for Children to See Themselves
clips.summary: She laments the near absence of Black editors in major publishing houses and of books by and for Black children, recalls refusing to let an illustrator make the boy in My Mama Needs Me white because her purpose was for children to see themselves, praises Ezra Jack Keats's The Snowy Day, and explains that every character in her books must make a choice, find courage, and so grow and change.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: We Came to Take Our Seats
clips.topic: Award Books and the Mississippi Challenge
clips.summary: Walter tells of touring the nation reading to children and winning prizes, the Coretta Scott King Award for Justin and the Best Biscuits in the World, a Jane Addams Children's Book Award, a young-adult book on Elizabeth Mum Bett Freeman winning her freedom under the Massachusetts Constitution, and a book on the 1964 and 1968 Mississippi Challenge in which Fannie Lou Hamer told the Democratic National Convention they had not come to do nothing but to take their seats.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: There Can Be No Peace Without Justice
clips.summary: She describes her piece for The Big Book for Peace on the MFDP's wait outside Congress and the path to the Voting Rights Act of 1965, her worry that the right to vote is again in danger, and her hope that her books show children, by characters like the enslaved boy who insists on learning to read in Alec's Primer, that a good choice is one good for all living things, that it takes courage, and that courage brings growth.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: I Rebutted His Review of Baldwin
clips.topic: Reviewing for the Los Angeles Times
clips.summary: Walter recounts reviewing books for the Los Angeles Times when funding opened for multicultural material, reading the works of Du Bois and Nelson Mandela's I Shall Not Be Moved, and rebutting a Times reviewer who called James Baldwin a bigot, proving from Baldwin's own closing words that he sought to save the nation and give it justice, after which she decided she would rather write books than criticize others' work.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: A Lone Voice Sang Oh Freedom
clips.topic: Nazis at a Carson Housing Picket
clips.summary: She describes marching in Los Angeles housing tracts that would not sell to Black families, and a Saturday picket in present-day Carson where carloads of white men in Nazi uniforms and swastikas arrived with placards reading that ovens were too good for them, how the survivor Herbert who had been in a German concentration camp staggered from the line, and how a lone voice singing Oh Freedom lifted her anger and left her at peace.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: They Said She's a White Woman
clips.topic: A Festival in Nigeria and Ghana
clips.summary: Walter recalls being invited as the only children's book writer with a group of artists and sculptors to the African Festival of Culture in Nigeria, then traveling to Ghana and Cameroon, where market women debated which ethnic group she belonged to until they heard her voice and exclaimed she was a white woman, stirring the same feeling as a soldier in her Louisiana youth calling out to come see a pretty Black girl.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: The Door of No Return
clips.summary: She recounts the Ghanaian poet Kofi Awoonor taking her to Elmina Castle, where a guide spoke of the captives without connection, and how standing by the door of no return and the guns trained on the slave ships, told her group could not carry the flag at the festival, she broke down and cried, realizing she was neither African nor American but no three-fifths of a person, her blackness not a lack but a unique wholeness.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: From Guinea to Haiti to Louisiana
clips.summary: Walter describes returning unable to speak of feeling unique for fear of seeming arrogant, then setting out to learn her heritage, tracing a grandmother from Guinea who was part French and reached Louisiana from Haiti after the Toussaint Louverture revolution, studying African and Christian religions, and seeing in the comparison why her people could turn the other cheek and walk the second mile.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Acknowledge the Pain on Both Sides
clips.summary: She argues that the United States, like South Africa, needs a real discourse on race to be healed, that the Kerner Commission's call to confront racism was denied, and that the oppressed and the descendants of oppressors must sit together and acknowledge a shared pain of guilt and anger, citing relatives of oppressors too depressed to leave their houses on learning what their families had done.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Breaking Bread Breaks Barriers
clips.summary: Walter recalls a peace march to Moscow where each town greeted the walkers with a round loaf of bread and salt, so that sharing the bread broke barriers and made strangers no longer the other, helping her understand at last why the South had forbidden eating together, and how, awarded the Coretta Scott King prize during the walk, she chose to keep walking for peace rather than attend the ceremony.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: When You Know It, You Will Do It
clips.summary: She affirms there can be no peace without justice, that the freedom struggle was the work of many young people and named leaders long before the 1960s, that real change comes from those who truly listen and recognize that all are one, and that, as Howard Thurman stressed, when you know a thing you will do it, the faith that keeps her bringing a message fit for the time.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: All Things Being Equal
clips.topic: Age, a Memoir, and Her Sons
clips.summary: At ninety years old, Walter wishes she were twenty years younger and that she could publish a memoir though publishers now want celebrity stories, and acknowledges her two sons, one who died in 2008 after work in the Black Studies department at Cal State Los Angeles, the other in Santa Monica, and the grandson who brought her back to California and takes good care of her.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: We Could Not Turn White, Red, or Blue
clips.summary: Walter closes by recalling work in the 1970s, sponsored by the Western Interstate Commission for Higher Education under nurse Marie Branch, in which a group coined the term people of color rather than minorities and reformed nursing care, teaching that shock could not be read by red, white, and blue on dark skin, opening visiting hours and family rooms for transitions, and respecting cultural needs in food and water.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Born Right Here in 1914
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby on December 3rd, 2015 in his home in Claiborne County, Mississippi, on the land where he was born, with John Bishop and Guha Shankar present, Nathaniel "Nate" Hawthorne Jones recounts being born in 1914, the first of the family's children born here after his people moved in from Lorman, Mississippi, and notes that he is 101 years old.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: My Mother Left Ten
clips.summary: Jones describes his family of farmers and sharecroppers, his mother dying when he was five, and his father remarrying a woman who lived in the old house (a building dating to the 1800s) and who brought five children, so that the blended household held fifteen children, ten left by his mother and five by his stepmother.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Six Months of Schooling
clips.summary: Jones explains that the local schools offered only six months of schooling, and that once he reached about nine years old he had to leave for the field to keep the crop for the landlord, going to school only when rain made field work impossible, so he came to hope for rainy days.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: From the Foot of the Class to the Head
clips.summary: He recounts walking miles around a flooded creek to reach school, the good teachers, and how his little sister Emma, who got the full six months, carried his assignments home so he could study; through arithmetic and spelling at the board he could rise from the foot of the class to the head before having to return to the field.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: If We Had Owned Our Own Land
clips.summary: Jones reflects that families who owned their own land could send children to school every day for the full six-month term, while sharecropping families like his could not, so he sometimes had to repeat a grade and kept up only because his baby sister carried his lessons home.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Cotton, Corn, and Peas
clips.summary: He describes the three crops the family had to raise, cotton, corn, and peas, the peas planted to climb the laid-by corn and filling the rooms of the house in the fall, with the landowner taking one-fourth of everything; because the family owned its own horses and mules they worked on the fourth rather than the half.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: The Best Team in the South
clips.summary: He recalls going to every commencement at Alcorn to watch its baseball team, which he considered the best in the South, a pitcher who went on to Chicago and the big leagues, his own wish to be a pitcher or boxer, and meeting a seller of the Pittsburgh Courier, a paper he kept up with for years and had sent to him in the service.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Reverend Martin and the Underground
clips.summary: Jones tells how he first learned of the NAACP through Reverend Martin, the minister of the Egypt church where he had attended school and Sunday school, a man who worked quietly on civil rights, and explains that membership had to be kept secret and could not be registered openly.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: A Charter Member in 1952
clips.summary: He recounts meeting secretly at the Methodist church on Saturday afternoons when people came to town by wagon, a friend related to Reverend Martin helping him join, becoming a charter member around 1952, keeping his card hidden because the Klan was active, and saying he never felt fear because he wanted to be free, having heard talk of freedom and of crossing the Mason-Dixon Line and subscribed to the Grit and other papers.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Drafted Into the Navy
clips.summary: He describes being drafted into the Navy around 1944 by way of Camp Shelby and Jackson, going through boot camp, shipping out toward Hawaii where the men separated, and traveling on toward Saipan and Majuro, getting scared only around Saipan.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Up to My Neck in the Lagoon
clips.summary: Jones recounts being assigned as a steward and cook, earning his cook's certificate from Washington, and how during air raids and ship raids at the air base the men had to run out into the lagoon, standing in water up to his neck, three times going into the ocean.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: A Hunger Strike That Worked
clips.summary: He describes a hunger strike among the Black cooks and stewards, twenty-nine men who refused to be barred from eating with the officers, a strike that lasted three days and worked, and recalls voting absentee while in service though he believed the ballot was thrown out.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: The Mississippi Constitution Test
clips.topic: Poll Tax and the Registration Test
clips.summary: Jones explains that he had paid his poll tax at twenty-one before going into service, and recounts the hard time registering to vote afterward, being given a part of the Mississippi Constitution to copy and interpret, three of them trying together.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: You Didn't Make It This Time
clips.summary: He recalls the three men attempting registration, two of them (one with a sixth-grade learning) and himself, being failed by the registrar for small mistakes, told he had erred in writing it down, and finally passing on a later attempt, encouraged all along by a good officer he had served under who told him he ought to vote.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Tests Upstairs, Police Below
clips.summary: Jones recalls that when R.L.T. Smith was running for Congress a young man came through and held voter-registration classes upstairs in a building, with police waiting below to break up the meetings and frighten people away, though he himself was not scared.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: It Was Moses
clips.summary: He describes the police waiting outside as the class came down, the beatings that frightened most of the others off, and confirms, after struggling to recall, that the young man from New York who led the registration work was Bob Moses, who called out the names at the first meeting until only three returned.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: A Girl Across the Creek
clips.summary: Jones tells how he came to know his future wife, who as a young girl lived across the creek and came to visit her uncle and his married brother, helping care for the brother's first child; fetching milk and supplies he would try to head her and her sister off through the woods to get acquainted, and recalls that when she was eighteen he was twenty-two.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Saving the Navy Pay
clips.summary: Jones explains that they were married seventy-two years, and describes how, while he was in the Navy earning $109 a month after making his rate, he sent his pay home and his wife banked it, the two writing each other every day and planning to save all they could, so that after the service they bought 126 acres for roughly fourteen hundred dollars.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Who They Let Vote
clips.topic: Land, Voting, and the Movement Arriving
clips.summary: Jones says he does not think owning land affected his own registration, names a handful of Black landowners who were allowed to vote because they were considered higher up, and describes the movement picking up when organizers came in, the white community forming a biracial committee of preachers and teachers who feared for their jobs to try to prevent it, while the people pushed back wanting to be free, to march, and to protest.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Rudy Shields Pushed the Vote
clips.topic: Charles Evers, Rudy Shields, and Becoming an Officer
clips.summary: Jones credits Charles Evers and the organizer Rudy Shields, whom he calls a brilliant fellow who went out pushing people to reach the vote, especially on the plantations where most disfranchised voters lived, says he worked with that group, and describes agreeing to serve as an NAACP officer when the chapter needed officers, his neighbors going along because they had confidence in him.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Hired at That Table
clips.summary: Jones describes the school superintendent, whom he counted a friend and who hunted on his land, sitting at his kitchen table over coffee and hiring his wife into the school system and Jones himself to drive a school bus, and recounts farming and driving the bus at once, working alongside white neighbors at the sawmill and laboring together with his stepbrothers.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: We Have Been Good to You
clips.topic: The Banker's Warning and a Reluctant Reverend
clips.summary: Jones recalls a bank vice president, a hunting companion who had loaned him money, reminding him after he joined the movement that they had been good to him, and Jones answering that he had paid his bills, and describes a reverend on the biracial committee, part of the scared group that held back from the protests, who in time changed and came around.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: They Tried to Talk Me Out of It
clips.topic: Going Public and What the Protest Was About
clips.summary: Jones recounts how many of the farmers he knew, most of whom had never been in the service among people who had been free, tried to talk him out of going public with the NAACP, but he kept on, and explains that the protests were largely about the students at Alcorn and the campus workers who were not paid what they should have been.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Leading Them Around on Horseback
clips.summary: Jones describes the tear gas at Alcorn, how he knew the back ways and rode his horse to lead the students around the patrolmen and back toward campus, getting them away when the patrol grew angry and began throwing tear gas.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Don't Hit Him
clips.summary: He recounts leading the students back to the dormitory by the back roads, where their shouting and singing enraged the patrolmen into throwing more tear gas, and how, used to tear gas from his Navy training, he was spared by a voice saying not to hit him while two men, one the uncle of a man named in the interview and the other a preacher from Hattiesburg, were beaten badly enough to be taken to the hospital.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Arrested on the March
clips.summary: He places the Alcorn marches in 1966 and recounts being arrested at the foot of the march and jailed overnight, his wife taking over the school bus route, which she had never driven, to pick up the children while he was held.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: A March Around Town
clips.summary: Jones describes the mass meetings full of clapping and singing that ended with a march around the town, the police sometimes pulling their billy clubs but at one point a chief telling them to let the people make their procession so long as no rocks were thrown, with some white residents objecting to the police stopping the march.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: It Was About the Pay
clips.topic: Why They Boycotted and the Difficulty of Organizing
clips.summary: Jones explains that the Port Gibson boycott was about workers not being paid right and about poor working conditions, the pay the chief grievance, and how hard it was to organize because many people were afraid, plantation owners would not let their children participate, and those who stayed on the plantations risked losing their place.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: The Blackheads
clips.summary: Jones recounts keeping his young son out of a group of children, called the blackheads, who threw rocks at cars during the school desegregation, the group eventually broken up, says he did not have to stand guard at his own house though Reverend Dawson needed protection, and describes his children going through freedom of choice to the white school at Port Gibson, three of them among the early ones.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Brave Enough to Run
clips.topic: Finding Candidates and the First Win
clips.summary: Jones describes the black voting majority emerging after the Voting Rights Act and how hard it was, going through the county on the committee, to find people brave enough to run for office, recounts fielding candidates around 1967 including himself, his wife, and others such as a Mr. Collins, and how on the first run three people won, among them three black women.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: When Miss Jones Got Elected
clips.topic: His Wife Elected and Movement Singing
clips.summary: Jones describes his wife being elected circuit clerk in 1971, helped by people at the bank, and feeling good and unafraid when she won, then recalls the singing in the movement, "We Shall Overcome" and freedom songs led nightly by the children, and a woman, Miss Crowder, who helped lead the young people alongside a youth choir.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Twenty-Five Dollars a Share
clips.summary: Jones identifies the white lawyer Fred Berger, who had represented him, and tells the story of Armart, the grocery store that came out of the movement and required shareholders to start, with shares at twenty-five dollars, competition with white merchants intensifying once it opened, and around seventy-some shareholders, himself the top shareholder and a member of the board.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: A Quarter Million a Year
clips.summary: Jones recounts R.L.T. Smith, who ran stores up in Jackson, helping them learn to run the store and get stock, the monthly shareholder meetings, paying off shares each year while some reinvested, and the store turning over a quarter million dollars a year despite heavy overhead and a failed effort to open another location.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Claiborne Hardware Versus the NAACP
clips.topic: Charles Evers and the Boycott Lawsuit
clips.summary: Jones describes Charles Evers as a straightforward person and good leader who worked at Port Gibson for the whole community, and explains the Claiborne Hardware v. NAACP suit brought against them over the boycott, the merchants claiming lost profit, a long case they finally won in 1982, his property tied up though it did not make farming harder because his standing at the bank let him get what he needed.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: The Card and the Burned Car
clips.summary: Asked what he wants people to know, Jones reflects on how civil rights brought them through and on the hope he held since the 1930s when he longed to join the NAACP but did not know how, and describes the danger of being known as a card holder, the fear after a boy was reportedly killed for having such a card, hiding the card, and how fear once led them to burn the first car they got though it had not been threatened.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: We Picked Cotton on the Halves
clips.topic: Secret Meetings and a Hardworking Family
clips.summary: Jones recalls the preacher teaching them to gather at the church across the way when they came to town by wagon, the small meetings of ten or twelve before they had cars, and closes by describing his large hardworking family who logged, farmed, and picked cotton on the big plantations, three or four of them topping two hundred pounds a day, earning respect because they worked even when the pay was low, the interview ending with thanks and the Library of Congress and Smithsonian credit.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: The Sun Returns
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Los Angeles in June 2016, Norma Mtume gives her birth name Norma Stoker, her married names Armour and Mtume, and explains that her husband Omawale Mtume took a Kiswahili name meaning the sun returns, shedding the slave names many were discarding in the 1960s.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Twenty-First and Naomi
clips.summary: Mtume describes her parents, a Louisiana-raised mother and a Texas-born father who became a librarian, the family's move from San Diego to Los Angeles, and growing up poor in South Central around 21st and Naomi, where children wore cardboard in their shoes and no one felt poor because everyone had the same.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: A Scholarship of Two Hundred Fifty Dollars
clips.summary: She recalls boyfriends and neighbors being drafted to Vietnam and not coming back the same, graduating from the nearly all-Black Jefferson High School in 1967 just after the Watts uprising, and going on to Cal State LA on a $250 scholarship to study math and physical education.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: The Blue VW and the December Raid
clips.summary: Mtume tells how she met her first husband Albert Armour, a Black Student Union activist with a blue VW, Panther stickers, and a Muhammad Speaks newspaper that upset her mother, and how after the December 1969 police raid hospitalized him with a broken arm and damaged eye socket she began volunteering at the 44th Street office, typing memos and working the breakfast program.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Nineteen and Running the Clinic
clips.summary: At nineteen Mtume became coordinator of the clinic with help from volunteer professionals, the RN and UCLA instructor Marie Branch and the psychiatrist Dr. Terry Kupers, who recruited residents, doctors, and nursing students; she connects this to her present work on health disparities at Charles R. Drew University.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: A Storefront and Point Number Four
clips.summary: Mtume describes the self-study that prepared her and the storefront clinic at 32nd and Central, built out into three exam rooms, a pharmacy, and a lab and stocked with free drug samples the doctors got from pharmaceutical reps, all answering point number four of the party's ten-point platform for decent health care.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Up at Five-Thirty for the Breakfast Program
clips.summary: Mtume recounts giving up her apartment to live in the party facilities with her son, the nursery that cared for members' children, and the punishing days that began before dawn with the breakfast program and ran past ten with evening clinics, political education classes, and the guard shifts everyone took so that what happened to Fred Hampton would not happen to them.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: That's My Brother
clips.summary: Mtume tells of being stopped while leaving to do communal laundry, an officer producing a portfolio of photos of party members while she called her husband's picture her brother, the constant surveillance that fell hardest on the men, and the high stress that drove some members to alcohol and drugs despite the party's rule that they clean up.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Served the People Body and Soul
clips.summary: Mtume reflects on the party's principle of equality between men and women against the chauvinism members carried in from a patriarchal society, the growing number of women in leadership, and the deep lifelong bond among veteran Panther women who trusted each other with their lives.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: A Bus to San Quentin
clips.topic: Family and the Free Busing Program
clips.summary: Mtume describes the party's free busing-to-prisons program that finally let her mother visit her oldest brother at San Quentin, easing her mother's fears, and her father's worry for her safety, then turns to her earliest memory of discrimination on childhood drives to Texas around 1955 and 1956.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: He Went Around to the Back Door
clips.summary: She recalls a Texas trip at about age nine when, with the Black motels in El Paso full during a Baptist convention, her father slept by the roadside under willow trees and later entered a whites-only restaurant by the back door for coffee and food, then showed her the separate Black pool, school, and the bleachers far behind the drive-in screen.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Pre-Food-Bank Survival
clips.summary: Mtume catalogs the survival programs: free food before food banks existed, free coats for cold cities, the SAFE program escorting seniors, outreach clinics modeled on the barefoot doctors of Cuba and China, legal aid and paralegals, and the Oakland nursery and school that cared for children around the clock so parents could work long hours.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: The Bunchy Carter People's Free Clinic
clips.topic: Naming the Clinic and the US Rivalry
clips.summary: Mtume explains the clinic was named the Alprentice Bunchy Carter People's Free Clinic for the man who, with John Huggins, founded the Los Angeles chapter, recalls meeting Carter once at a rally, and recounts the rivalry with Ron Karenga's US Organization, learned firsthand from her husband at UCLA when Carter and Huggins were killed, contrasting the Panthers' Russian, Chinese, and Cuban revolutionary ideology with the US Organization's Afrocentric focus.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: A Girl With Six Brothers
clips.summary: Mtume tells of being attacked by US Organization members while selling papers pregnant and fending them off, her lasting refusal to be in Karenga's presence over later acts she attributes to him, and her belief that Karenga gave the order, possibly with police collaboration, to shoot Carter and Huggins.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Corners and Ideologies
clips.summary: She places the Carter and Huggins killing in early 1969 before she joined, describes the street corners staked out by the Black Panthers, the US Organization, the Nation of Islam, and the Holy Ghost Christians, and the Los Angeles gangs like the Slausons and Pueblos whose members brought misplaced aggression the party rechanneled once they gained consciousness.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: The George Jackson Free Clinic
clips.summary: Mtume describes being assigned during the move north to direct the George Jackson Free Clinic in Berkeley, collaborating with the Berkeley Free Clinic, the Women's Health Collective, and the Haight-Ashbury Free Clinic, and how UC Berkeley's Black Health Science Caucus and Vietnam corpsmen taught the workers to draw blood, do stitches on pigs' feet, and perform exams they practiced on each other, a practice that caught her own cervical cancer early.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Read It Like a Novel
clips.summary: Mtume describes devouring the Merck Manual and the women's health collective book, teaching preventive self-care, running a communicable disease control program against shigella and chickenpox outbreaks, and in the free-love era screening every newcomer to town and publishing a list of those barred from sex until treated.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Minister of Finance
clips.topic: Headquarters Books and a Strained Marriage
clips.summary: Mtume describes her Bay Area years from 1972 to 1977, running the clinic until 1974, and how after Huey Newton and Bobby Seale left and Elaine Brown took charge, Brown asked her to become Minister of Finance, collecting the ten-percent tithe everyone with income paid and learning real bookkeeping once the school won grants, while the strain of Oakland kept her apart from her Los Angeles husband, who after developing multiple sclerosis became her finance assistant.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: When Huey Came Back From Cuba
clips.summary: She describes how the party shifted under Elaine Brown toward mainstream funding, including a law-enforcement grant and a social-service nonprofit wing housing the clinic, seniors, food, and clothing programs, and how Huey Newton's return from Cuba changed everything as his forward-thinking ideas strained the people around him.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: The Wrong House
clips.summary: Mtume relates that Newton, charged with shooting a prostitute, sent someone to kill the witness whose address had been published, that the team went to the wrong house where the woman shot through the door killing one of them, and that because members lived communally the dead man's license led police to search her home and haul out hidden weapons cases.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Knowledge, Possession, and Control
clips.summary: She describes being arrested as members kept leaving, being kicked out of the party so its resources could defend Newton's case, fighting her own case for a year, and a two-year California state prison sentence built on the doctrine of knowledge, possession, and control because the house and utilities were in her name.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Do Not Pass Go
clips.summary: Mtume recounts three months in the Frontera reception center while officials watched for her to start a riot, a single cell, becoming secretary of the Women's Advisory Council, and earning an AA inside, then after release earning a bachelor's and master's in health administration and at fifty-five a master's in marriage and family therapy.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: A Condemned Trailer
clips.summary: Mtume describes leaving paper-pushing county work to write grants for substance-abuse programs serving women whose children were born drug-exposed, a Diane Watson bill that followed over twelve hundred such births a year at Martin Luther King Jr. Drew Medical Center, and how she and her boss left the county to found their own nonprofit, SHIELDS, in a dilapidated trailer on Charles R. Drew land with a half-million-dollar grant and fourteen employees, soon buying apartments to house the near-homeless.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: They Mirror the Party's Work
clips.summary: Mtume describes adding mental health, vocational, and AIDS services as she became health officer and chief operating officer, growing SHIELDS over twenty-four years to three hundred fifty employees and twenty-five million dollars before she retired, and noting how its programs mirror the work she did in the party across Compton, Watts, and Willowbrook.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Two Steps Forward, Ten Back
clips.summary: She voices concern that the Affordable Care Act is pushing substance-abuse treatment back into a medical model and excluding the recovering people who did the work, the discouragement of watching hard-won gains cycle backward, and her move into community faculty at Charles R. Drew University after retiring from SHIELDS.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Social Determinants of Health
clips.summary: Mtume explains the community-faculty mission of teaching medical, nursing, and public-health students the social determinants of health, why a patient may not fill or trust a prescription, the resistance of older professors set in their ways, and her diversity-curriculum work with UCLA doctors and nurses now that she is semi-retired.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Survival Pending Revolution
clips.summary: Asked how the Panthers are remembered, Mtume insists that self-defense mattered because the dead cannot fight, but that guns were never the point: the party ran a plethora of survival programs under the banner survival pending revolution to give all poor and oppressed people the best life possible. She speaks of her educator daughter who keeps lifelong party friends, her wish that young people step around the ditch the Panthers fell into, and coalitions with the Brown Berets and with Cesar Chavez and the farm workers.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: A Day-to-Day Grinder
clips.summary: Mtume describes coalitions with the Gray Panthers, students, and the anti-war and women's movements, joining the executive committee only as finance minister, and reflects that she never saw herself as a visionary but as a day-to-day grinder who, even through prison, kept moving up and never expected to reach where she is.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: It's Your Interview, You're in Charge
clips.summary: David Cline of Virginia Tech and the Southern Oral History Program opens the Civil Rights History Project session in Richmond, Virginia, on August 17, 2013, with John Bishop behind the camera, explaining his chronological family-history approach before introducing Oliver W. Hill Jr.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: A Circumscribed World
clips.summary: Hill recounts being born in 1949 in Richmond's Black hospital, now a Virginia Union University dorm, and the self-contained, class-heterogeneous Black neighborhood where a lawyer lived beside a plumber and one rarely needed to leave, the hub of his first dozen years.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Somebody Else's Pathology
clips.summary: Hill describes his father's NAACP Legal Defense Fund work, the telephone threats that kept him from answering the phone and a cross burning when he was six or seven, his parents' effort to teach him that prejudice was others' pathology, and the white-normed media that still shaped a boy who wanted wavy hair like Elvis Presley.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Raised by the Pentecosts
clips.summary: Hill traces his father Oliver Hill Sr., born in Richmond in 1907, deserted by his own father, raised partly by a stern, well-read foster family named Pentecost in Roanoke while his mother took seasonal hotel work, and sent to Washington's prestigious Dunbar High School because Roanoke had no Black high school.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Social Engineer or Social Parasite
clips.topic: Howard Law and the Plessy Strategy
clips.summary: Hill tells how an uncle's annotated constitution made his father question segregation's legality and drew him to Howard Law, remade by President Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and dean Charles Hamilton Houston, where classmate Thurgood Marshall and Houston's charge to be social engineers launched the strategy of attacking Plessy v. Ferguson.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Waiting Tables in the Depression
clips.topic: Building the LDF and the War
clips.summary: Hill recounts his father graduating in 1933, waiting tables in Depression-era Roanoke, joining the close Howard-graduate circle of NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund lawyers, winning the Virginia teacher salary case, and being drafted in his forties for World War II while repeatedly demanding officer training.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Knocking on White Doors
clips.summary: Hill describes his father serving in an all-Black unit in England and France, then returning to become the first Black person elected to office in the South since Reconstruction on the Richmond City Council, canvassing white neighborhoods, losing a House of Delegates race by about forty votes, and attending every political meeting in town.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Tar Paper Shacks in Farmville
clips.summary: Hill recounts the call from Barbara Rose Johns, a high school girl who organized a walkout over Farmville's tar-paper-shack conditions, and how her persistence drew his father to a parents' meeting where he warned the case would now attack segregation per se, making Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County part of the Brown package.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Ten Years Before Rosa Parks
clips.summary: Hill notes that the Davis case took from 1952 to the 1954 Brown decision, that Barbara Rose Johns suffered and left Farmville, that her uncle was the preacher Vernon Johns who preceded Martin Luther King in Alabama, and that a decade before Rosa Parks his father's firm won the Irene Morgan case ending interstate transportation segregation.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Strategy Sessions at the Breakfast Table
clips.summary: Hill describes the lawyers' incremental two-decade strategy, traveling attorneys like Thurgood Marshall and Robert L. Carter lodging at the Hill home because hotels were closed to them, his father's partnership with Spottswood W. Robinson III, and Virginia's massive resistance under the Byrd Machine and Governor J. Lindsay Almond Jr.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Desegregation but Not Integration
clips.summary: Hill explains the post-Brown decade of compliance cases, his own place among the first Virginia students to desegregate schools around 1961, the pupil placement board that assigned schools, his father's good relationship with progressive school-board head Lewis Powell, and his father's distinction that a token few Black students made a school desegregated but not integrated.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Walking Up the Empty Steps
clips.summary: Hill recalls not wanting to leave his Black-school friends, officials measuring distances with yardsticks to exclude a friend three feet too far from Chandler Middle School, the surreal first day when his father's partner Henry L. Marsh III drove him to a deserted-looking white school where Black students were marched two by two to homerooms, and the football team that, over the years, turned even prejudiced classmates into teammates as a Black prom queen and easing social segregation followed.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: A Four-to-Four Split
clips.summary: Hill recounts busing as a remedy and Judge Robert R. Merhige Jr ordering cross-county busing despite threats and his dog being killed, then the Supreme Court's four-to-four split, after Lewis F. Powell Jr recused himself over his Richmond school ties while William Rehnquist did not, upholding the lower court and killing the county-inclusive busing plan.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Race Morphed Into Class
clips.summary: Hill compares Louisville merging city and county and describes Richmond's white power structure annexing county land until the Voting Rights Act blocked it as vote dilution, then an annexation moratorium leaving the city land-locked, and the old racial politics of Virginia's independent cities now clothed as class conflict.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Tarzan Movies to African Civilizations
clips.summary: A little miffed but persuaded of the cause, Hill describes walking to Chandler Middle School from Northside, then Howard University in the late sixties, an eye-opening African history class after a childhood that knew only Tarzan movies, and campus black power figures Stokely Carmichael and H. Rap Brown challenging King's nonviolence with black nationalism.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: An Inward Turn to Indian Philosophy
clips.summary: Hill recounts black nationalism holding sway a year or two before an inward turn to Eastern philosophy, a senior perception-psychology course on how belief constructs reality, becoming a meditation teacher in Boston and Detroit en route to the University of Michigan, and framing nonviolence and dignified non-reaction as a practice of mindfulness rooted in Gandhi and ahimsa.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: The Best and Brightest Left Teaching
clips.summary: Hill argues integration cost Black schools their gifted women teachers as the women's movement opened other careers, displaced Black principals, and ended the mission-driven, class-mixed schools of the segregation era, leaving inner-city hubs of poverty, and points to Petersburg's lowest-performing schools, eroding tax base, and revolving superintendents as the result.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Slavery as a Benign Institution
clips.summary: Hill recalls Virginia history class teaching slavery as a benign, family-like institution he knew was false, and a succession of governors beginning with L. Douglas Wilder adding the Black story to the record, acknowledging that more civil rights cases arose in Virginia than any other Southern state in a climate that, unlike Mississippi, let people feel safer standing up.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Arthur Ashe on Monument Avenue
clips.topic: Confederate Statues and a New Memorial
clips.summary: Hill describes Monument Avenue's towering Confederate generals facing north or south by their war fate, the uproar over adding an Arthur Ashe statue, an earlier objection to a Lincoln statue at the Tredegar Iron Works, and the Capitol-grounds Virginia civil rights memorial picturing Barbara Rose Johns, his father, and Robinson near a statue of Harry F. Byrd Sr.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: His Name on the Old Finance Building
clips.summary: Hill notes the irony of his father's name on the Capitol's old finance building, where Byrd's circle plotted massive resistance and where his father once had to fight segregated seating to take the bar exam, and recounts the national recognition his father received in his last decade, including the 1999 Presidential Medal of Freedom, before the interview turns to Hill's own career.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Orange and Blue Hand-Me-Downs
clips.summary: Hill describes his career at Virginia State University, an HBCU whose orange-and-blue colors came from UVA football hand-me-downs, the politics of state funding, and his research on Black education and cognitive factors, including how early childhood stimulation and the once-fixed-now-malleable view of cognitive ability shape achievement.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: A Civil Rights Approach to Math
clips.topic: Bob Moses and the Algebra Project
clips.summary: Hill recounts partnering with Bob Moses and the Algebra Project in Petersburg, Moses being a sixties Mississippi voter-registration organizer who earned a Harvard math PhD and taught abstract math through children's life experiences while applying civil rights community-organizing to school reform, and an interview with Los Angeles educator Mildred Pitts Walter who wrote her own Black children's books.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: A Framework for the Content
clips.summary: Hill explains looking for transfer from cognitive-skill practice like attention and short-term memory to math performance, likening cognitive training to a framework that content must adhere to, laments a dumbing down of education, and blames well-meaning liberal teachers who held Black students to lower standards, recalling his educator mother's observation, citing George Bush's phrase about low expectations, and arguing that challenging curricula, not remediation, are what works.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Everything Is About the Test
clips.summary: Hill calls Virginia's ironically named Standards of Learning a barrier to innovation in districts like Petersburg where everything turns on test scores, describes Virginia Tech workshops where teachers hungry for African American and civil rights history struggle to fit it into the SOLs, and concludes the factory model demands radical restructuring.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Where Is the Outrage
clips.summary: Hill voices optimism that an entrenched educational bureaucracy will change slowly, predicts MOOCs and the decline of the university, recalls inspiring teachers who passed on excitement rather than rote test prep, and frames the present fight as the same battle in a new form: racial segregation become class segregation in disgraceful city schools, with the Iraq war's trillion dollars found while education is neglected, naming education the movement's legacy and asking where the outrage is over school conditions and mass incarceration, citing The New Jim Crow before closing.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: You Go Reddish to Vote
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the McCain Library at the University of Southern Mississippi on November 30, 2015, Peggy Jean Connor describes being born in Hattiesburg, a shy student who made good grades, and her teacher Ruby M. Tatum, who taught seven grades and told her class that when they turned 21 they should go to the circuit clerk's office and register to vote.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: I Collected Poll Tax in My Beauty Shop
clips.summary: Connor recounts trying to register at 21 and taking about ten years to succeed, paying the two-dollar poll tax for two consecutive years, and collecting poll tax money from her beauty-shop patrons who were afraid to go themselves, walking lists of names into the office and bringing back receipts for everyone.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Washing Windows All Downtown
clips.summary: She describes her father working at a lumber yard and later driving an owner around and washing windows for nearly every business downtown, her mother as a housewife, and her aunt giving her the beauty shop when she left Hattiesburg, which is how Connor came to own it.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: A Family of Beauticians
clips.summary: Connor tells how she began at her aunt's shop at age 11 earning ten cents a shampoo, graduated from her aunt's beauty school in 1946, and describes a wide family of beauticians, her aunt married to a carpenter-minister, and an uncle who ran Franklin Beauty School in Texas.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: He Made the Girls Mow Yard Too
clips.summary: She recalls being paid the full price like her aunt, her strict father who made his three daughters mow the yard and his one son do housework, the sisters spoiling their baby brother, her mother working in white families' homes and being paid in cast-off goods, and that citizenship was taught in her Black school.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Twelve Days Before Freedom Day
clips.summary: Connor describes how long it took her to register, asking her retired teacher who admitted she herself had never registered, and finally getting registered in January 1964, twelve days before Freedom Day, after a court order opened the rolls, though she received no voter card.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Eighteen of Us Walked In
clips.summary: She recounts taking 18 people to the white Democratic precinct meeting at the library on Main Street in 1964, where the regulars, who voted Republican but met as Democrats, did not know what to do and telephoned the registrar Theron Lynd for instructions.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: The Only One Who Could Participate
clips.topic: A Rule Made on the Spot
clips.summary: Connor explains that Theron Lynd ruled by phone that only she, the single registered voter among the 18, could participate, that she was voted to go on to the county meeting in 1964, helped count paper ballots at the polls, and was later voted to the district level in 1968.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Secretary of Both Organizations
clips.summary: She describes her father as secretary of both the NAACP and another political group tied to the post-Brown era, having her and her sister Joyce write meeting letters as children, his job as a porter at the Hattiesburg American, and his anger at learning some NAACP members were reporting to whites.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: A Different Person When He Came Back
clips.summary: Connor tells of marrying a schoolmate who returned from the Korean War an alcoholic who loved to fight, their son and daughter born while he was stationed in Greenville, his drunken outburst at her father's house, and his mother coming from Gulfport to take him away for good.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: She Was in an Iron Lung
clips.summary: She recounts her second daughter contracting polio at age two during a local epidemic, the pediatrician Dr. Temple arranging emergency trips by car to iron lungs in Big Spring, Texas and later closer to home, the child's aneurysm and seizures, and her sharp wit watching television from her sickbed.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: He Cried Like a Baby
clips.summary: Connor describes the girl's father coming to the funeral and weeping, confessing a guilty act, her children all calling her own father Daddy after she moved back to her parents' home, and her recollection that the daughter, very close to her brother, died around 1953.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: The COFO Office Across the Street
clips.summary: She tells how the COFO office sat across Mobile Street from her beauty shop in a building a woman donated rent-free, how she watched the commotion from her doorway not being the type to walk over and ask, and recalls that the brick building later burned and Dr. Tussle saved some bricks for the archive.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
