clips.title: That Woman Set Me on Fire
clips.summary: Connor recalls a SNCC worker coming across to invite her to a mass meeting where Fannie Lou Hamer was the speaker who set her on fire, after which Victoria Gray Adams invited her to a week-long nonviolence workshop taught by Dorothy F. Cotton and Andrew Young.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Bring It Back to True Light
clips.summary: She describes the Dorchester Center workshop on the South Carolina-Georgia line, returning home charged to start citizenship classes, asking the pastor of True Light church who let her hold them after Wednesday prayer meeting, and that her students went to register though none could do it right away because of the court order.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: He Really Listened
clips.summary: Connor praises the leader she calls G.I., describing him as a good listener who could explain people's reasoning back to them and did not back down, and recalls him speaking against the Vietnam War on the courthouse steps to community disapproval and the discovery that an informant was reporting on the meetings.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: I Joined the NAACP for Insurance
clips.summary: She explains joining the NAACP for the legal help it offered, worrying over her son and brother, and recounts her son Johnny being harassed and arrested by police at her father's shoeshine parlor, the two officers contradicting each other in court, and the judge advising her father to send the boy away, after which Johnny left Mississippi for Louisiana.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: The Humblest He Could Be
clips.summary: Connor remembers Clyde Kennard, who tried to enter Southern, as a humble and determined man she saw getting coffee at Fats Kitchen on Mobile Street, recalls not following his trial closely, and describes Medgar Evers coming through in the 1950s to organize and a starched, proud woman singing Go Tell It on the Mountain at a mass meeting.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: She Could Wear Those Shorts
clips.summary: Placing the mass meeting after the March on Washington in late 1963, Connor recalls the organizer Jean Wheeler, who stayed at the school and helped organize the MFDP before moving away, and who spent the summer of 1964 in Philadelphia, Mississippi to face down the Klan.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: You'll Get Burned Up in That Shop
clips.summary: She describes managing her shop alone while attending movement events, rival beauticians frightening her clients with warnings they would be burned up in her shop, the customer whose Klansman husband talked openly in front of her, and her work at the precincts during the Meredith crisis at Oxford.
related_events: Integration of the University of Mississippi

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: They Don't Count Us
clips.summary: Connor recounts working the polls and counting votes for years without being bothered, often for little or no pay, telling another worker that white officials talked freely in front of them because they did not count Black people as a threat, and her uncertainty about how the election commission worked back then.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: I Was Getting My Freedom
clips.summary: She describes the Freedom Day march looping all the way around the courthouse past the Masonic Temple and furniture store, the menace of out-of-state lawmen brought in, the worker who arrived the night before whom she liked despite his reputation, and telling a writer she was out there getting her freedom.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: A Shotgun Across His Leg
clips.topic: Ministers and Guards at Her House
clips.summary: Connor recalls the pouring rain on Freedom Day, a downtown store giving the police rain coats, John Conyers Jr. staying at her house with FBI agents to guard the congressman, and her father, recovering from paralysis, sitting up at night with a cane that people rumored was a shotgun across his leg.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: My First Time on an Airplane
clips.summary: She tells of returning from the Atlantic City convention in New Jersey, flying for the first time to New York to campaign for the MFDP ticket while another delegate went to Philadelphia, and a sudden demand that her overdue telephone bill be paid before she could travel.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: What School You Want to Send That Girl To
clips.summary: Connor describes her daughter Felicia being one of six to desegregate the white schools under freedom of choice, returning from California to register her in time, learning the school was named Jefferson Davis Elementary, and how white children were seated before Felicia was walked in so there would be no incident.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: March On Back to That House
clips.summary: She recounts being arrested while picketing the courthouse, an officer telling the marchers they looked good enough to march back to the jail, being moved from the jail to a work farm to keep leaders from the others, and a jail crowd of about a hundred singing songs and telling stories with a single dipper of water on the floor.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Nine O'Clock We Were Ready
clips.topic: A Week in Jail and Bond
clips.summary: Connor describes not worrying about her children in jail, her mother and children never attending mass meetings while she and her father did, walking from the COFO office up Mobile Street to the courthouse knowing they would be jailed, and a couple posting their thousand-dollar house as bond that everyone got back.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Mine Was the Only Vote
clips.summary: She explains how, after the Dorchester workshop, SNCC organizers had them attend the white primary meetings, how the 18 had only her single registered vote, and how she alone was elected to the county meeting while getting no vote at the heated district meeting, trusting the Lord to take care of her.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: I Bought a Box of Dixie Paper
clips.summary: Connor tells of integrating a downtown drugstore in July 1964 after a longtime customer urged her, bringing her pregnant sister-in-law, sitting on separate stools while the men scattered with their plates, the owner saying he was sorry but could not serve them, and her filing suit.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: You Can Sit Between My Legs
clips.summary: She recounts the four-year course of her suit and a letter the owner sent her, learning the man seated beside her at the drugstore was the manager of Butler's shoe store who had fitted her wide feet, and confronting him that he could kneel between her legs to sell shoes but would not share a lunch counter, then walking out.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: They Moved Me Out of There
clips.summary: Connor reflects that she had always been shy and would never have let her son sit in, recalls her daughter wanting a treat at a counter, and tells how giving her first oral history on September 11 changed her, talking two hours, and how she and that interviewer mark each anniversary by calling each other.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Better Than Three Thousand
clips.summary: She describes canvassing door to door, some people uninterested and others won over once the students arrived, and how registering for the Freedom Democratic Party, which did not require a courthouse visit, brought in better than three thousand people.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: The Women That Saved Us
clips.summary: Connor recalls riding three buses to Atlantic City with all their papers, believing they would be seated until Lyndon Johnson blocked it, and how the men of the group, listening to Adam Clayton Powell Jr. and others, wanted the two-seat compromise while the women, led by Fannie Lou Hamer, refused it.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Just Me and Her in Her Car
clips.summary: She describes traveling the state with Victoria Gray Adams, going to a late-night WDAM interview, never being asked to join the Jackson organizing, and how for years the delegation photographs and history left her out because she was quiet, until Adams's room produced the pictures.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: She Don't Do That Anymore
clips.summary: Connor tells how, returning to the COFO office, a worker said papers were ready for her and an unseen voice said she did not do that anymore, because Annie Devine had been made secretary of the party, and how she walked out that day and never went back, unwilling to do anything to hurt the movement.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: It Was So Pretty Down There
clips.summary: She recounts going to Washington with the group for the congressional challenge, some shunning her, Annie Devine, Mrs. Hamer, and Mrs. Adams getting on the floor, and the delegation of people lining the hallway between the House buildings as the congressmen walked through.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: He Thinks It Worked
clips.summary: Connor recalls Michael Thelwell of SNCC, mostly in Washington, telling her the hallway line had moved some votes, attending statewide COFO and MFDP meetings, and being the first to sign a petition at a heated meeting and getting nominated to head the party though the people did not know her, naming others from Hattiesburg who went.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Some Strong Women
clips.summary: She names SNCC people who stood out, Prathia Hall and Ella Baker, who spoke at Hattiesburg mass meetings, recalls her long talks with Fannie Lou Hamer and learning belatedly of Hamer's death around 1977 and of Victoria Gray Adams's death, and that Adams was funeralized at a Hattiesburg church.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: They Put Him Out to Get You
clips.summary: Connor describes knowing the movement supporter she calls Mr. Damon, hearing the NAACP had put him out for working with SNCC and housing SNCC people, seeing the ministers' letter, and learning the local chapter would not help when his house was firebombed because he had sheltered the movement.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Still Smoking That Morning
clips.summary: She recalls going out early to his house still smoking, visiting Mr. Damon dying in the hospital where he was talking when she left, her disbelief that he died of smoke from the fire, and the shotgun and .38 her family kept locked up at home on busy Mobile Street.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Somebody in the White Community
clips.summary: Connor tells of a carpenter warning her a man was asking where she lived, a William Carey College professor who helped rebuild the burned house, and her sense that some white people quietly aided the movement from the start, and explains she skipped the Freedom Schools to earn money in her shop.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: The First One in the United States
clips.summary: She recounts the Child Development Group of Mississippi, the delegates meeting Sargent Shriver in Washington who told them theirs would be the first Head Start in the United States, the program being taken away after Mississippi first refused it, and her own community-worker job around 1965.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: You Couldn't Do Political Work
clips.summary: Connor describes taking a Head Start community-worker job because she needed money even though it barred political work, suspecting that rule cost her the position, and explaining that her name was on the Connor v. Johnson suit because she was the party's executive secretary and record keeper the government would pursue.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: He Took Me Down to the Bank
clips.summary: She compares Guyot to a drill sergeant insisting on good financial records, recalls him taking her to open her first checking account though she had run a cash business for years, his staying less than a year, and his later life after being badly beaten in New York, helped by the worker Sheila.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: The Lawyers in Khaki Pants
clips.topic: Board Meetings and the New York Lawyers
clips.summary: Connor recalls the worker Sheila who ran the office, housing movement people including a small woman named Mary, the open-door spirit where no one thought themselves better, MFDP board meetings at Rev. R.L.T. Smith's house in Jackson, and the New York lawyers in khaki pants whose word always held.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Where We Got Black Officials
clips.summary: She explains the Connor v. Johnson case as two suits, one local and one state, over getting people elected to office and redrawing district lines, which produced many Black elected officials in Jackson and a Black congressman, and recalls voting every time for Percy Watson, who did not recognize her until about four years ago.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: They Forget How They Got There
clips.summary: Connor describes Percy Watson presenting her a framed proclamation in Jackson and inviting her to his Christmas party, her muted feeling about the honor, and her view that many Black elected officials do not want to be associated with her and forget how they reached office.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: As Long As We Keep Bickering
clips.summary: She questions whether Black elected officials have changed people's lives, names the main thing still missing as love among the people, and recalls being arrested in Jackson and put on the third floor away from the others while Charles Evers, who took over after Mr. Damon's death, ran out of his own demonstration.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Voting to Stop Demonstrations
clips.summary: Connor recounts Monica, who first worked in Hattiesburg and stayed at her house before moving to Jackson, both of them jailed and bailed by her father on the same day, and a Jackson capitol protest against a special session voting to stop demonstrations.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Keep It Out of the News
clips.topic: A Hometown Governor and Southern Today
clips.summary: She explains that Governor Paul Johnson Jr. being from Hattiesburg led local white leaders to keep movement news quiet, including during school integration, and closes reflecting that the University of Southern Mississippi, once segregated, now has among the most Black students of any school in the state before the interview's thanks and sign-off.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: A Beautiful Day in Beacon
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the Civil Rights History Project interview with Pete Seeger at his home in Beacon, New York, in July 2011, and asks him to recall the September 1949 Paul Robeson concert at Peekskill, where Seeger opened with a few songs.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Wake Up America, Peekskill Did
clips.summary: Seeger describes the two 1949 attacks on the Robeson concert as a near-miss with fascism, recounting J. Edgar Hoover's prepared detention camps, Truman's official giving the American Legion a green light, police trucking teenagers to gather throwing stones, the Ku Klux Klan and Lake Valhalla German Americans, and pre-printed hate signs that echoed Kristallnacht before the backlash turned public opinion.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: No Anniversary Without Music
clips.summary: Seeger recalls Alan Lomax telling him about Highlander Folk School, the 1940 photograph of him with Woody Guthrie, and being called by Myles Horton after Zilphia Horton's death to sing at the school's 25th anniversary in 1957, where he first met King, Abernathy, and Rosa Parks and was photographed before the barn after King spoke.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: He's Here, He's Here
clips.summary: Seeger describes the only other time he met King, a year before the assassination, singing at a massive United Nations peace rally the day after King's Riverside Church Beyond Vietnam speech, watching six men hold back an adulatory crowd around King's car, and hearing King call his country the greatest purveyor of violence, with a speculative aside about Lyndon Johnson's fury and a call to Hoover.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: If We Are Nonviolent
clips.summary: Seeger tells how a two-inch New York Times item led him to the Montgomery Improvement Association and E.D. Nixon, who explained calling King to host the protest meeting and arranging it before the deacons even approved, and how King insisted the boycott would be won only through nonviolence, mediating between angry young people and cautious professionals until they reached a compromise.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Stay Out of the Way of Bull Connor
clips.summary: Seeger recounts learning the name Eugene Bull Connor while hitchhiking through the South in 1940 and staying with Joe Gelders, who had been badly beaten, and argues that Connor's use of fire hoses and dogs against children opened the country's eyes to how terrible the segregation system was.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: You Don't Have the Song Right
clips.summary: Seeger explains that Zilphia Horton taught him We Shall Overcome and he printed it imperfectly in People's Songs, but Guy Carawan and Frank Hamilton made it spread by setting it in 12/8 time learned from gospel singing at a Baptist church, underscoring how rhythm is essential to African American music.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Cross Your Hands
clips.topic: SNCC and Singing in the Movement
clips.summary: Seeger describes Guy Carawan's 1960 Singing in the Movement weekend at Highlander, drawing fifty or sixty young people from Texas to Florida, where We Shall Overcome became the hit, and how weeks later at the Raleigh founding of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, Carawan taught the crowd the crossed-hands, body-swaying way of singing rooted in African tradition.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Answer Back in the Harmony
clips.summary: Seeger recalls a Carnegie Hall concert before taking his family on a 35-country world tour, including Toshi's father reuniting with his Japanese family after fifty years, and bringing Black and white neighbors from Beacon to sit on either side of him and learn the call-and-response harmony so they would get the audience singing.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: From Spirituals to Swing
clips.summary: Seeger describes the hundred-thousand-dollar union stage-crew cost of recording at Carnegie covered by Columbia, and John Hammond Jr., great-grandson of Cornelius Vanderbilt, who discovered Brownie McGhee and Sonny Terry and staged the 1938 From Spirituals to Swing concert that Seeger attended in his last weeks at Harvard.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Just Like the Fisk Jubilee Singers
clips.summary: Seeger recounts performing in Albany, Georgia in 1962 to a tepid response when he sang English ballads, meeting Bernice Johnson Reagon and Cordell Reagon, and suggesting to the SNCC office in Atlanta that singers could raise money traveling like the Fisk Jubilee Singers, leading to the Freedom Singers whose first tour Toshi set up from her list of college contacts.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: They Found the Bodies
clips.summary: Seeger tells of flying to Jackson with William Kunstler in the summer of 1964, a hostile passenger threatening him outside the airport, singing in Hattiesburg and Meridian, and announcing to two hundred people in a church that the bodies of Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney had been found, then singing Fred Hellerman's Healing River in the silence.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: What Do We Want? Freedom!
clips.summary: Seeger recalls the telegram from King inviting him and Toshi to the Selma to Montgomery march, joining some three hundred marchers in the rain with helicopters watching the cornfields for snipers, Harry Belafonte urging them on, and first hearing the new call-and-response of We Shall Overcome answered with shouts of Freedom, before a break and a song about the river by Storm King Mountain.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Pick Up the Torch
clips.summary: Seeger reflects that King brought nonviolence, anti-war, environment, social justice, racial equality, and women's concerns together as one broad arena, and that knowing he would be assassinated King used his final speech to tell people to pick up the torch, with Seeger giving humanity a fifty-fifty chance of surviving the century.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: The Spirit of Beacon Day
clips.summary: Seeger describes a widowed Beacon woman who, after a high school race riot, gathered other women to close Main Street one day a year for the Spirit of Beacon Day, inviting churches, civic clubs, and the Beacon Sloop Club, an event that grew until two-thirds of the town turned out for a parade, dancing, and music from saris to the River Town Kids' new songs.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Don't Try to Get Reelected
clips.summary: Asked about the first Black president amid a long war, Seeger says he expected compromises but not so many, and regrets that in his ten seconds with Obama, who told him his mother played his records, he did not advise the president to do the best job he could rather than aim to get reelected in 2012.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: A Government of Corporations
clips.topic: Rutherford B. Hayes and the Courts
clips.summary: Seeger tells the story of Rutherford B. Hayes, whom he calls the worst president for withdrawing federal troops from the South after the Civil War in a deadlocked election, and the later Santa Clara County v. Southern Pacific Railroad ruling that ended corporate charter revocation, prompting Hayes to declare it a government of corporations rather than of the people.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: The Cow Was the Union Organizer
clips.summary: Seeger recalls the radical Jewish garment workers' Camp Nitgedaiget near Beacon and the puppet troupe he built in the 1930s, performing a skit during the New York Dairy Farmers Union strike in which a cow advises an underpaid farmer to organize with the others, with Seeger playing the cow as they passed the hat at strike meetings.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: They Knew They Could Be Killed
clips.topic: Fannie Lou Hamer and Medgar Evers
clips.summary: Seeger speaks of Stacey White's annual gathering in Mississippi remembering Fannie Lou Hamer, who was beaten so badly she later died of her injuries, the heroism it took for Medgar Evers and the three slain workers who knew assassins were coming, and an FBI agent present who did nothing, before the interview closes with thanks and the Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Streetcar Conductor and a Maid's Niece
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Oakland at the Black Alliance for Just Immigration in September 2011, Phil Hutchings opens on his family's migration to Cleveland: a father from Macon, Georgia, whose own father went north to drive trackless trolleys, and a mother born outside Memphis who, orphaned young, was raised by an aunt who worked as a maid.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The Hatch Act and the Saint James Forum
clips.summary: He describes his lower-middle-class household, his father's steady job as a postal clerk that, under the Hatch Act, barred him from open political activism, and how his father channeled his interests through church forums like the Saint James Forum, where the boy heard local politicians speak.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: He Never Did a Real Day's Work
clips.summary: Hutchings recalls shaking Senator Robert A. Taft's soft hands at age eight and his Democrat father's retort, his mother going to law school alongside Carl Stokes, who as Cleveland's first Black mayor appointed her chief referee of the Civil Service Commission, and listening to Lowell Thomas and tracing places on a globe.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Mother Runs for State Representative
clips.summary: He sketches Cleveland's liberal, ethnically mixed, Irish-machine Democratic politics, his mother's run for state representative on which he campaigned by stuffing her literature into the newspapers he delivered, and a youth civics group the children named the Archegos Club after a Greek word for civic duty.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Small Piece of the East Side
clips.topic: Youth Councils and the Wider World
clips.summary: Hutchings describes growing up confined almost entirely to the African American East Side, the Council on Human Relations youth council that let him meet teenagers across the city, a World Council on Human Affairs mock assembly where his school played France during the Algerian war, and his father teaching him the meaning of a labor union.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Born in the Battle of Stalingrad
clips.summary: He recounts the 1958 right-to-work fights in Ohio and California, where Senate and gubernatorial candidates jockeyed and unions flexed their power one last time, his father convincing him never to cross a picket line, and his realization that he was born in 1942 amid Stalingrad and Midway.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The Call and Post Inside the Cleveland Press
clips.summary: Hutchings reflects on how Emmett Till frightened him as a teenager, his early childhood summers riding segregated trains down to family near Memphis without understanding it, and reading the Black newspaper the Call and Post, sometimes folded inside the Cleveland Press, as he became aware of the NAACP, Roy Wilkins, and Walter White.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Picketing the Dime Stores
clips.summary: He recalls joining the spring 1960 response to the Greensboro sit-ins, picketing the concentrated dime stores along Euclid Avenue with informational signs about their Southern counters, action that alarmed the cautious local NAACP.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Two Out of Three
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the NAACP, seeing itself as lawyers and wary of street action that might upset sweetheart deals with Cleveland's white elite, dissolving the youth chapter, the radical ideas he absorbed from the heavily Jewish, Communist-tied Circle Pines camp club, his Democratic-politician mother's three warnings against pregnancy, drug addiction, and communism as she worried over the Fair Play for Cuba group, and reading C. Wright Mills on the power elite.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: You're Going to Howard
clips.summary: Hutchings tells how, already signed up at Ohio State with a dorm and roommate, his mother phoned to redirect him to Howard University, wanting him among aspiring middle-class Black students, a fraternity, and a future wife, and noting that Howard was actually cheaper than in-state Ohio State.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Kennedy Comes to the Chapel
clips.summary: He describes Howard's foreign students from the Caribbean, Africa, and Iran, the charged Kennedy-era Washington atmosphere of activist government and intellectuals, and seeing John F. Kennedy speak in the Howard chapel after a debate with Nixon, then later glimpsing Nixon at a Maryland event.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Watching the Soviet Boats
clips.summary: Hutchings recalls living through the Berlin and October 1962 Cuban missile crises in the capital, professors debating whether Washington would be nuked, and his own start as a campus political joiner who ran for office, lost, and managed a freshman-class campaign for a Baltimore student named Nathaniel Knight.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Who Are These Folks in Overalls
clips.summary: He explains being drawn to the Nonviolent Action Group students who returned to campus in Southern overalls and one-man-one-vote buttons, people like Stokely Carmichael of the Bronx, Courtland Cox, and Ed Brown, who kept one foot in Howard and spent summers in the South, and who taught him that politics should be about something.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Put Your Body on the Line
clips.topic: High School Activists and the Left
clips.summary: Hutchings describes Washington as neutral ground where Southern movement people came up for air, and how high school students challenging segregation laws in Virginia and Maryland, living a life of action rather than preparation, overturned his mother's plan of a slow climb to a profession, then recalls Tom Kahn, a white League for Industrial Democracy activist influenced by Max Shachtman, teaching him the history of the U.S. left as Project Awareness brought controversial speakers like Malcolm X to Howard.
keywords: put your body on the line

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Live Communist on Federal Soil
clips.summary: Hutchings tells how Project Awareness also brought the Communist Herbert Aptheker to a Howard nervous about its federal money, and how McCarthy-era fear had silenced brilliant political scientists who could lecture but dared not publish their views, lessons in the repercussions of taking political stands.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The White Rice Inn
clips.summary: He recounts a spring 1961 action with CORE under Julius Hobson, sent with a racially mixed group to a Chinese-owned Baltimore restaurant called the White Rice Inn, where the proprietor, calling himself a colored man too, pleaded with them to leave and offered a free meal at his other restaurant.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: That's What I Mean by Freedom
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the group leaving, deciding they had punked out, and then returning to sit on the restaurant steps and link arms until the police pried them apart and jailed them, a moment of liberation when he realized he was making a stand for the life he wanted and crossing a Rubicon.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Like a Thirteenth Grade
clips.summary: He gives his disappointment with Howard as a colonial-feeling thirteenth grade anxious about its federal masters, naming a Democratic-active political science teacher named Martin and a French-resistance veteran professor who brought first-class thinking, against a boring Negro History course of dates, and recalls the campus on the day Kennedy was killed in November 1963.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: We Didn't Think About Gender
clips.summary: Hutchings reflects on the active women of NAG, naming Jeannie Bell, Bill Mahoney, Jean Wheeler who married Frank Smith, and Ruth Howard, and explains how NAG's repeated failure to win campus recognition turned into a strength, freeing the group to meet off campus and connect with students across Washington's universities.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Records Out of the State
clips.summary: Hutchings recounts a summer trip back to Tennessee, probably 1962, to see cousins near Memphis where he finally registered the segregation signs and the harassment of voter-registration efforts, then his particular task in Mississippi in 1964: duplicating Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party voter records and getting them out of the state, moving among second-district projects including Ruleville where Fannie Lou Hamer was, valuing most the grassroots democracy of community meetings.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Song That Read the Room
clips.summary: He explains the organizers' lesson of speaking complicated ideas in people's own language without dumbing them down, and watching Fannie Lou Hamer break into a song that reflected the mood of the room, a profound learning about culture used in a political way, while distancing himself from church and faith.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Mississippi, God Damn
clips.summary: Hutchings likens Mississippi to a Vietnam-style pressure cooker, citing his project-house friends Wazir Willie Peacock and Hollis Watkins, where activists faced snipers and hostile officials, the FBI watching but doing nothing, and the constant possibility of being killed.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Paul and Silas, Bound in Jail
clips.summary: He reflects that ninety percent of what he did in the movement he could only do because others did it with him, the church as Southern sanctuary, prisoners singing to drive jailers crazy and create community in jail, and getting tear-gassed at a 1964 Cambridge, Maryland march when George Wallace came to town.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Depression Settled on the Movement
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the depression that settled over the movement when the MFDP was not seated at Atlantic City while dealing with Lyndon Johnson, a delayed shutdown reaction he likens to mourning his mother in 1991, and going north to work in Robert Kennedy's 1964 Senate campaign in the research and speakers' bureau, distinguishing a liberal Democrat from a liberal Republican, on the rationale that Kennedy understood the movement's experience and could grow.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Both SNCC and SDS
clips.summary: Hutchings explains his unusual position in both SNCC and SDS, meeting Tom Hayden, Al Haber, and Richard Flacks through an old Circle Pines friend, David Strauss, at the University of Michigan, and learning of SDS's Economic Research and Action Project to build an interracial movement of the poor in Northern cities.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Turn Birmingham Into Chicago
clips.summary: He recalls Tom Hayden recruiting him over drinks to come see the Newark project, the appeal of a statewide New Jersey movement linked by the turnpike, and his conviction that defeating Jim Crow would only place Black people in the real economic and political problems facing the whole country, so the North had to be organized.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Housing, Rent, and Police
clips.summary: Hutchings describes arriving to find no interracial movement of the poor and no white people to organize, doing unemployment surveys but finding that residents wanted to work on housing, rent, and police brutality, breaking with the liberal Clinton Hill association, and watching the 1965 Watts rebellion on television with Tom Hayden.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Colonial African Nation
clips.summary: He recounts developing a two-year game plan after the Watts conversation, and how, as the movement turned more racially conscious, the Newark organizers framed a predominantly Black city against an Italian power structure as a colonial African nation, arguing for Black institutions, community control, and even Black policemen so people could see the system at work through Black agents.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Real Grassroots Power
clips.summary: Hutchings describes having real grassroots power that made the mayor and city council leery, the Negro machine politicians who broke their windows and slashed tires, a near-altercation on Springfield Avenue with the local Democratic boss, and funding that came mostly from labor unions like the UAW and the Episcopal church, the latter connecting him to Nathan Wright Jr.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The Limits of White Organizing
clips.summary: He explains how by 1965 the white SDS organizers of the NCUP project, recognizing the limits of white organizing in a Black community with no interracial movement materializing, shifted to fundraising and research on city structures, while he answered LeRoi Jones's pointed question about whom he represented by pointing to the ninety-nine-percent Black Central Ward.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Let's Become the Poverty Program
clips.topic: Taking Over the War on Poverty
clips.summary: Hutchings recounts debating whether to ignore or capture the federal poverty program, a strategy seeded by an earlier United Freedom Party third-party gambit, and deciding to seize it, installing their own people like Jesse Allen and chairperson Bessie Smith on the boards and effectively controlling the two poorest Newark districts.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Welcome Guest at Dinner
clips.summary: Hutchings reflects on how the poverty program's push-button phones and leather couches drew organizers off the streets and eroded the community ownership that storefront offices with donated furniture had built, how the community nominated him for a twelve-thousand-dollar community-action-director post the top officials denied him, and how he could never account economically for surviving the 1960s, with a planned system of dropping by households at dinner time bringing neighborhood news.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The Arch Bad Guy
clips.summary: He recounts making the mayor's chief of staff Donald Malafronte his public villain, a wealthy white supporter named Derek Wynans who started a community newspaper called the Newark Advance for which Hutchings wrote a column, and a sympathetic Star-Ledger reporter, all signs the organizers had become a force noticed well beyond the neighborhood.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The March to Name the System
clips.summary: Hutchings describes being released from Newark to organize, with SDS lead organizer Paul Booth, the April 1965 march in Washington that became the first major protest against the Vietnam War, drawing on his earlier 1963 experience helping Bayard Rustin's March on Washington committee mobilize the people of D.C.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Gradual Shift
clips.summary: He recounts organizing D.C. churches and community groups for the 1965 march alongside Norman Hill of CORE, then returning to Newark, and reflects on the gradual shift as SDS people moved toward the antiwar movement while Tom Hayden, after traveling to Vietnam, returned to humanize the Vietnamese, before Stokely Carmichael came to Newark inviting Hutchings into SNCC's new direction.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The True Bearers of Change
clips.summary: Hutchings lays out his philosophy, formed through SNCC and the Newark SDS experience, that the true bearers of future change were the grassroots poor with the least stake in the existing order, building a prototype of the future society with allies in the church, unions, and universities, just as Carmichael, in spring 1966, prepared to replace John Lewis as SNCC chair.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Come On In at the Bottom
clips.topic: The Northern Machine and the Press
clips.summary: Hutchings explains that the Southern movement had fought to get into a Democratic Party that kept it out, but when Daley welcomed King into Chicago at the bottom of the machine, neither had an answer to racism without segregation laws, and describes the United Freedom Party alliance with former assemblyman George Richardson as beyond the door-knockers' scope, the movement's lack of its own institutions forcing reliance on a white press that framed Stokely Carmichael as a volatile wild man.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Sense of Black Manhood
clips.summary: He characterizes his bond with Stokely Carmichael as both personal and political, knowing his family and once briefly courting his sister, a relationship of mutual challenge and admiration, and recounts traveling with Carmichael on the speaking trips that earned SNCC badly needed thousand-dollar fees as the Newark rebellion of 1967 approached.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: You Got Back Just in Time to Start the Riot
clips.summary: Hutchings describes debriefing Carmichael on messaging during plane trips with Cleveland Sellers and Charles Lloyd, his father's wry remark that he had returned just in time to start the riot, and the widening gulf with a father who thought his son, the first in the family to nearly finish college, was wasting his life.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: All Hell Broke Loose
clips.summary: He recounts the night the Newark rebellion began: returning from visiting a VISTA volunteer past midnight, hearing sirens and gunshots, and learning that all hell had broken loose at the police station a block from his home where a taxi driver had been beaten and held inside while a crowd threw rocks, with his neighbor and guide BJ at the scene.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Only What Is White-Owned
clips.topic: In the Streets of the Rebellion
clips.summary: Hutchings describes how he and BJ, who knew everyone in the Central Ward, agreed not to stop the uprising but to direct it only against white-owned property, distinguishing their clear intent from Detroit SNCC, which told people off the streets, and recounts cockily yelling black power near the Hayes Homes projects, being stopped by eight policemen with a bartender named Joe Whitley, and being beaten with rubber truncheons that left no marks, his nonviolent training letting him absorb the blows.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Rebellion, Not a Riot
clips.summary: Hutchings tells of being caught out past curfew, taken in by residents who knew the Newark group's work while snipers fired at police, and overhearing an older Black woman say it was about time the rebellion came here, a sign their racial organizing had taken effect, and insists on calling it a rebellion rather than a riot so participants would feel unapologetic, emerging more inspired by the community's self-assertion than discouraged by the state's police response.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A Black Mayor for Newark
clips.summary: Hutchings describes turning the rebellion toward Black political power, a 1966 mock mayoral campaign modeled on the MFDP to get people imagining a Black mayor, settling on civil engineer Kenneth Gibson, and the 1970 Black and Puerto Rican convention, with Hilda Hidalgo's support, that nominated Gibson, who won when four Italians split the vote.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Newark Is Our Battleground
clips.summary: He recounts working with Nathan Wright Jr. to organize the first National Conference on Black Power after the rebellion, uniting progressive Black politicians, businesspeople, and nationalists amid the era's Motown and Aretha Franklin soundtrack, with LeRoi Jones framing a modern nationalism rooted in Newark rather than Africa and bringing in the Nation of Islam toward a Black United Front.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Shooting Ourselves in the Foot
clips.topic: The Cost of Opposing the War
clips.summary: Hutchings reflects on the financial and political repercussions SNCC suffered, like King's SCLC, for opposing the Vietnam War, since civil rights was seen as a domestic Black matter and stepping into foreign policy lost mainstream supporters who had backed the Mississippi challenge, the freedom party, and the freedom schools.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The Paper Sold Out in Five Minutes
clips.summary: He recounts SNCC researcher Jack Minnis's 1966 paper that led the organization to come out against Zionism and Israel as an oppressor of the Palestinians, a stir in liberal circles, and being physically mobbed at a Manhattan meeting by people seizing every copy of the SNCC newspaper carrying the article.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Agents in the Movement
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the liberalism-versus-radicalism tensions over Israel and Vietnam, SNCC's support for the National Liberation Front, and the dawning awareness of COINTELPRO and FBI efforts to divide the Black movement and pit the Panthers against SNCC, including a New Jersey leader offered a hundred dollars on his bed by agents.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Alliance or Merger
clips.summary: Hutchings recounts becoming SNCC chairman as the Panthers drafted Stokely Carmichael as Prime Minister, James Forman as Minister of Foreign Affairs, and Rap Brown as Minister of Justice in 1967, celebrated at Huey Newton's birthday party in Oakland, amid tensions over whether SNCC's intellectuals should become Panthers, and how he voted the Panther program good but not SNCC's own, declaring as chairman there would be no merger and not even a clear alliance before turning to local groups like the Black Liberators of St. Louis.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Treated Like a Mafia Don
clips.summary: He tells of getting arrested with the Black Liberators under an old anti-labor unlawful-assembly law, his lawyer uncle joining William Kunstler on the defense and playing his speech to the jury, who acquitted him, and being treated as a celebrity in the workhouse of the Black town of Kinloch, where the warden let them use the phone for the press.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: On a Downward Cycle Since Atlantic City
clips.summary: Hutchings reflects on taking SNCC's helm amid declining membership and money, dispirited meetings, and the divisive expulsion of white members, his belief he could turn it around in a different form, and the core difference from the Panthers' chapter model: SNCC always built people's own organizations like the Lowndes County movement and the MFDP, knowing the organizers would leave.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: A National Consortium of the Grassroots
clips.summary: He describes his focus on joining radical grassroots groups around the country (the Black Liberators in St. Louis, JOMO in Florida, Watts-rooted groups in Los Angeles) into a national consortium empowered by national networks, contrasting it with Carmichael's Black United Front idea, which he doubted could hold disparate interests together against the white power elite.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Somewhere Closer That Needs Your Help
clips.summary: Hutchings recounts how the Venceremos Brigade arose when the Vietnamese, inspired by the Abraham Lincoln Brigade idea, redirected American radicals' wish to fight in Vietnam toward helping Cuba instead, and how the movements organized the first brigades, the first drawing about two hundred mostly white volunteers against a projected nine hundred, the second over eight hundred in March 1970.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: I Can't Dance in My Revolution
clips.summary: Hutchings reflects on how the brigade let him view the U.S. movement objectively and meet people from across the country, his identification with Cubans who cut cane all day then danced and drank rum, a debate with Grenadians who said their liberation needed more space for God, and the lesson in discipline when the Cubans synchronized watches for a six o'clock meeting, a corrective to the movement's casual CP time.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Can You Talk About Your Country
clips.summary: He recalls Cuba as a center for revolutionaries worldwide, meeting an Angolan named Ernesto and Brazilians and Vietnamese who could analyze their whole countries, and a Vietnamese man who pressed him for two hours to dissect the U.S. labor movement past his stereotype of sellouts, a learning process about serious conversation grounded in real things.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Wondering What Halibut Tastes Like
clips.summary: Hutchings tells of staying on at the Havana Libre, the former Hilton, learning to like fresh fish that Japanese-introduced refrigeration had spread across the island, helping plan with the Yippies and the Vietnamese an antiwar conference held in Cuba for its access, and a young Vietnamese man who after intensive U.S. bombing said he was too anti-American to meet that day but it was not personal, amid the easy affection and liberated humor among the Vietnamese.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: The Government Is the Problem
clips.topic: A Reversed Logic, a Continuing Question
clips.summary: Hutchings contrasts the sixties logic of forcing the federal government in against racist state officials with his present immigration work, where the government is the problem, since a world system of corporations and laws like NAFTA drives migration the U.S. press discusses only at the receiving end, and closes that the work continues to refine concepts around how race, class, and increasingly gender come together, a question he began in the sixties and carries into the twenty-first century.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: A Heavenly Place to Grow Up
clips.summary: After Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the interview in St. Augustine for the Smithsonian and the Library of Congress, Conway recalls growing up in Lincolnville, a self-sufficient Black community where all the adults watched the children, being raised from age five by his great-great-grandmother, a Seminole and Spanish woman, and spending his days at the little links park that today houses the Galimore Center, the only place Black residents could go for recreation.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: A Paper Boy at the Castillo
clips.summary: At ten Conway crosses into the white area to win a paper route from the St. Augustine Record, makes only ten cents selling within Lincolnville, then moves downtown to the Castillo de San Marcos where he sells out repeatedly until two white paper boys follow him, take positions beside him, and tourists begin buying from them instead.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: You Don't Want to Get Involved
clips.summary: Disenchanted in high school, Conway challenges teachers over outdated textbooks and reads the books his uncle Edward Norton left behind, notices his mother forced to call white clerks miss and mister while being called only Julia, and enrolls in a work-credit program called the DCT where a grocery owner he had done accounting for refuses to sign his form until Conway threatens to quit.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: The Only Pool in Town
clips.summary: Conway recalls friends who drowned for lack of a place to swim and the pool Florida Memorial College opened to Black kids in summer, where, born in 1948 and now about fourteen or fifteen, he first hears young male college students talk about demonstrating and civil rights, and learns their training in nonviolence as a tactic.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: They Were Like in a Trance
clips.summary: Drawn more to direct speakers like Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X but persuaded by the college students that nonviolent demonstration is the way, Conway tells his reluctant mother he will start demonstrating, and struggles to understand why the adults seem in a trance, accepting their treatment as more slave labor than labor.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: A Sandwich by the Dog's Tray
clips.summary: Hired to cut the grass of a white woman his mother worked for, Conway is offered a cool drink and sandwich only to find it set down beside the dog's food tray, and when his mother hears what happened on the phone she comes immediately, and from that day begins getting involved in the movement herself.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Don't Get Arrested
clips.summary: Conway promises his great-great-grandmother he will not get arrested so someone can tend her, stays at the rear of marches and is often chased but never caught by police, and recalls the night his mother, father, and three brothers were all arrested while he alone stayed free, in a town where arrest for the movement became a badge of honor.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: A Flower on the Wall
clips.summary: Conway describes feeling like a flower on the wall as figures like Dr. Robert Hayling, Dr. King, Ralph Abernathy, and Hosea Williams began coming around, realizing only gradually that a movement had started, and recalls being pressed to help recruit people to test the newly enacted law.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Stand Your Ground
clips.summary: On a demonstration at St. Augustine Beach with four other teens and a tall white man, Conway is in the surf when a crowd of whites converges from north and south, a frightened Black girl locks the group out of the car, and the mob surrounds them with weapons; a white youth punches Conway, the man with them is struck over the head with a pipe and collapses bloodied, and the mob disperses only when a lone police car approaches.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Beaten Into Submission
clips.summary: After Hosea Williams vows to return stronger, a second demonstration two weeks later brings a large group back to St. Augustine Beach between a cordon of helmeted police, where Florida state troopers wade into the surf and beat the Klan and their sympathizers into submission, dragging them to the shoreline in a melee Conway had never seen the like of.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: The Water Was Too Bloody
clips.summary: Told by the troopers' commander that the water is safe though he cannot guarantee its quality, Conway finds the surf so thick with blood he will not go in, and is left confused that white men would fight other white men for his civil rights, an action he attributes to orders from Governor Farris Bryant and pressure from the Justice Department.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: A Knife on a Bicycle
clips.summary: Conway recalls that police were not sympathetic to the demonstrators and his fear of being beaten if arrested, then tells how a young white tutor took a small group of Black children to a downtown movie theater against his caution, and within minutes the children come running and screaming with a white man on a bicycle chasing them with a knife.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: If He Moves, Kill Him
clips.topic: The Police Dog and the Church Yard
clips.summary: Intervening, Conway is surrounded by two policemen with a dog until his friends push the officers and run, drawing them off, and a cop orders the German Shepherd to kill him if he moves before Conway turns slowly and outruns the dog to safety on Washington Street, then hides two days and nights in a church yard while neighbors bring him water and sandwiches.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: They Picketed Their Own Car
clips.summary: Conway describes how white college tutors, including Gail Blattenberg of Princeton and students from Massachusetts, changed his belief that all whites hated Black people, and how when they invited Black kids to their northern homes his reluctant mother finally relented after the students made picket signs and picketed in their car to win her over.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: This Is the Law Now
clips.summary: The two-week trip north changes Conway's whole perspective on white people, and back home over the year or so after the Civil Rights Act he watches the white business community come around to obeying the law, returning to the same movie theater to watch a James Bond film and beginning to eat at the restaurants without trouble.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Two White Men Get the Certificates
clips.summary: Conway enters a junior college trade school in Daytona Beach studying brick masonry, where he meets his future wife, and after a year and a half two white students arrive, are allowed within months to test and become masons, and when his instructor protests he is fired and the Black students are offered only a carpentry path, so they all refuse and receive nothing.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Worse in the North
clips.summary: Married to a woman from New York, Conway moves north and finds racism worse than the South, overt below the Mason-Dixon line but a pat on the back and a smile while they stab you in the north, and works five years in the post office doing a supervisor's accounting for less pay before passing the police department's test.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: You Curse Me, I Curse Back
clips.summary: In the New York City police department Conway finds rampant, rank-based racism but refuses the disrespect as he had as a teen, demanding to be treated as a human being regardless of rank, joined by other officers who came out of the civil rights movement, and sues the department and city over discriminatory promotional exams, winning.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Quota Sergeants
clips.summary: Conway recounts winning the lawsuit whose criteria were used nationally and the sergeants test that produced over three hundred minority candidates derided as quota sergeants, and how, retiring as a lieutenant, he trained hundreds of officers, mostly white, charging them to treat every woman in a Black neighborhood like their own mother or answer to him.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Drug-Ravaged and Red-Lined
clips.summary: Bringing his children down every summer over the years, Conway grows disheartened at the non-improvement of Black life in St. Augustine, returning to abandoned houses, drug-ravaged areas, and Black seniors unable to keep their properties, recognizing the red-lining he had witnessed in New York playing out in the gentrification of Lincolnville.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: The Buddy System
clips.summary: Asked how his St. Augustine experience shaped his later life, Conway, raised to be neither violent nor passive, describes challenging the buddy system in the police department as a quiet form of racism, since the qualified person should come first and a buddy is the one you live and drink with, and he was not drinking with the men in charge.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: More Than Just a Park
clips.summary: Now living in Palm Coast, Conway names his passion the little links park and its Galimore Center pool, closed for over two years, which he has petitioned the city commissioners to restore as the one sanctuary children had from the hatred of the era, and argues that a people kept out of the political spectrum for decades cannot be expected to flood into civic life the moment a bill passes.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: If He Goes, I Go
clips.summary: At Dr. Hayling's suggestion Conway pays tribute to his mother Julia Conway, whose passion was for her sons to excel, and his father George Conway, who delivered groceries on Anastasia Island and was so loved across the white community that a judge stopped a bank manager from giving the family a hard time over a court order, and whose white butcher saved his job by telling the owner that if his father went for marching, he would go too.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: They Fired Her Before Ten Years
clips.summary: Conway describes his mother, a smart and loving woman who got a decent job at Flagler Hospital training nurses and giving newborns home remedies yet was fired before her tenth year to deny her a pension, and explains that the scarcity of pensions in Florida is what drew him north to land decent jobs and a pension of his own.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Praise for the ACCORD
clips.summary: In closing the interview turns to Dr. Hayling's continued activism and to Conway's praise for the ACCORD organization, which Gwendolyn Duncan and her family took on years ago, and for Barbara Vickers, who worked to build a monument to the foot soldiers of the civil rights movement in St. Augustine.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Free State of Jones
clips.summary: Emilye Crosby opens the December 1, 2015 joint interview at the University of Southern Mississippi with Jeanette Smith and Raylawni Branch, and Smith describes being born in 1940 in Jones County, the Free State of Jones, where Captain Newton Knight bought her great-great-grandmother Rachel as a slave, fell in love with her, deserted the Confederacy for the Union, and started the family documented in Victoria Bynum's book and a coming movie.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: A Shotgun House on Whitney Street
clips.summary: Branch tells of being born in 1941 in her grandmother's three-room shotgun house in Hattiesburg, with family roots in Mount Carmel near Prentiss where great-grandparents bought their own 80 acres, disputes the forty-acres-and-a-mule story as something that never really happened, and insists civil rights is still a present problem.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Poor White Folks Some People Call Them
clips.summary: Branch describes growing up among blonde, blue-eyed kin so that she knew no racial difference until the death of Emmett Till, and recalls the poor white neighbor who helped her great-grandmother can and shell while her great-grandmother fed the woman's children, and the reciprocal relationships between women of color and the big houses in town.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Furniture in the Alley
clips.topic: A Family's Hard Years in Chicago
clips.summary: Branch recounts her fair-skinned mother being hired at Brock Candy Company because they thought she was white and fired on the spot for sitting to eat with the one Black woman there, her farmer father who died in Chicago, riding the L past the homes of Oak Park, and how, after he fell ill and her mother was pregnant, the family lost their home and ended up homeless with their furniture in the alley.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Descendants of Newton Knight
clips.summary: Smith explains that as descendants of Newton Knight her family had their own school and were called the lightning girls, that her father insisted his children attend public school and live in the real world without hatred for dark or white people, and that as a girl she peppered her mother with questions about being mixed and unaccepted on either side.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: I Want to Be Just an American
clips.summary: Smith recalls being called a devil's child for her green eyes, having her hair pulled at school, wanting to be simply an American rather than black or white, her mother telling her not to dwell on the past, and how her grandmother, called butter gal for her blondeness, had been born after a plantation owner raped Smith's great-grandmother near Prentiss.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Shotgun Houses From Camp Shelby
clips.summary: Smith describes her great-grandmother buying one of the World War I shotgun houses moved up from Camp Shelby and lined beside the railroad tracks, and marrying a Cajun out of southern Louisiana, Frenchmen mixed with Native Americans and Africans, whom her grandmother stopped having children with after he was killed working the railroad in 1932.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: She Never Knew Her Real Name
clips.summary: Smith tells how her mother kept her origins a taboo subject and died at sixty-seven never knowing her real name, listed on her birth certificate as Christine Holloway when the family name was Phanimae and her known name was after her Louisiana father, and reflects on the one-drop rule, being mistaken for Asian or Pacific Islander, and learning to love herself.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: The Store That Could Sell Guns
clips.summary: Smith describes the timber land her grandmother inherited and gave on to children and grandchildren, the seasonal community of canning, syrup-making, and smoking meat, and her green-eyed, blonde uncle who, because of who his family was, was allowed to run a village store and sell guns and ammunition when Black people legally could not.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: The Armstrong-Branch Plaza
clips.summary: Branch points to the Armstrong-Branch Plaza on the Southern campus, two blocks long with the international building she requested placed on it, the lecture series begun in the early 1990s, and says she would rather the money go to scholarships than things, before the talk turns to the recent February 10, 2013 tornado.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: We Didn't Have Any Problems at All
clips.summary: Branch credits Vernon Dahmer, James Bourne and others for the path she and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong walked when they integrated the University of Southern Mississippi in 1965, contrasts the relative calm they met with the violence and deaths surrounding James Meredith at Ole Miss, and notes she was called a slur only once.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Watching for My Tail
clips.summary: Branch recalls white girls in the gym checking the mirror to see if she had a tail, being alphabetized to the back of the class and printed all the same color in the yearbook, her science-department advisor Dr. Fish, and President McCain being warned that Governor Ross Barnett would close the school if anyone was admitted, noting the McCain library they sit in and the plaza built out of guilt.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: You Could Just Walk Up and Shoot Them
clips.summary: Branch says that at the time a person could be shot with no real trial, citing the Eyes on the Prize footage of a white man shooting a Black would-be voter in McComb and the state representative Herbert Lee, and Smith says she knew Clyde Kennard personally from the restaurant on Mobile Street where he delivered eggs and leaders met at Smith's drugstore.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: To Me They Were Sell-Outs
clips.summary: Smith describes behind-the-scenes conversations in which Black principals Mr. Berger and Mr. Roy, wanting a Black junior college built in Hattiesburg, tried to talk Kennard out of coming, calling them sell-outs because the state-funded school should have been open to all, while McCain, a John Birch Society member, let Ole Miss and Meredith go first and called the women angels.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: I Met Him in the Forrest County Jail
clips.summary: Smith explains she knew Kennard a different way, meeting him sick in the Forrest County jail where her husband, the doctor the Moon brothers and NAACP wanted to examine him, found he needed care before he was sent to Parchman and checked out too late with advanced cancer; she recalls first meeting Raylawni Branch as a young NAACP secretary, defends her as a lifelong fighter, and places Kennard's home in the Macedonia, Kelly settlement community.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: The Chicken-Feed Frame
clips.summary: Smith recounts that Kennard came to Hattiesburg to help his widowed mother run a chicken farm and applied to the school three times amid talks involving McCain and the governor, that police arrested him first on a whiskey charge and then for stealing chicken feed, when in truth a young friend, Johnny Lee Roberts, swept up damp re-bagged feed and brought it because the feed houses refused to sell to Kennard.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: A Comedy of a Court Case
clips.topic: Seven Years for Trying to Enroll
clips.summary: Smith tells how she and J.C. Fairley reached Dick Gregory and Charles Evers, brother of the slain Medgar Evers, who flew them on a Dick Gregory-funded plane to persuade the frightened nineteen-year-old witness to tell the truth, but the boy said whatever the lawyer said and Kennard got seven years, a man she says went to jail not for stealing but for trying to come to the school.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: The Backbone of Everything
clips.summary: Smith credits the NAACP, with a branch in place since 1946 and men who filed a voting suit in the 1950s, as the backbone of integrating public accommodations and voting, laments that outsiders who came down briefly got the recognition while local people who risked their lives went uncredited, and repeats a Pennsylvania judge's line that if it's not local it's not real, recalling a journalist friend who fought in Hattiesburg yet lived in her own segregated community.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: The Only Race That Came Against Its Wishes
clips.topic: Equal Rights and a Model President
clips.summary: Smith, who counts herself one-quarter white, black, and Spanish, declines to profit from a casino-tribe enrollment because she wants only equal treatment, reflects that hers is the only race brought to America against its will, and holds up A. Philip Randolph and a president who answers cruelty without bitterness as models of restraint over self-defense.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: He Couldn't Swallow the Steak
clips.summary: Circling back, Smith describes flying the terrified state witness to Chicago, where the group took him to dinner but he could not eat the steak with a white woman seated beside him and slipped away back to Hattiesburg, and she notes the Clyde Kennard Day now observed on campus and at the church where he is buried.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: I Got the Principal Fired at Four and a Half
clips.summary: Branch tells how at four and a half in Mount Carmel she walked off the stage and out of school after a principal whipped her for something she had not done, getting him fired that night, and recounts her great-grandmother, mother of two Pullman porters, riding free on a family pass to Federated Women's Club conventions and rebuking a conductor who called her Amy.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: A Shotgun at the Door
clips.summary: Branch describes learning to shoot as a matter of country survival and the shotgun kept at the door against intruders, even as she calls herself against guns and the NRA shameful, frames forgiveness as understanding people gripped by fear, and notes the gun her niece keeps in the woods for self-defense, before recalling that she left in 1966, the year Vernon Dahmer died.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: The First One Hired
clips.summary: Branch recounts a stealth campaign before 1964: integrating a downtown hotel for a speaker, being the first person hired at the telephone company but unable to take nights with no childcare and a mentally ill husband gone, so Joyce Brown stepped up instead, and being the first clerk hired at Sears, hidden in shipping until she opened the candy counter for others.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Climbing Across the Man's Desk
clips.summary: Branch tells of becoming the first Black woman hired at Big Yank clothing, a plant of four hundred women who refused to work with her, after she climbed across the unemployment officer's desk demanding a job other than Miss Anne's kitchen, recruited three other women to run a permanent-press shift in competition with a nearby plant, stood on a box because she was told she was too short for the number-one machine, and walked home through the shadows to avoid being caught.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: The Boy in the Casket
clips.summary: Branch recounts a white child crawling under a Chicago church table to peer at her, the women saying nothing, and picking up the Jet Magazine with Emmett Till in his casket, an image that frightened her so much she ran all the way home, hardening her family's resolve, rooted in the protective shotgun of her country childhood, that no one would take one of their boys.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: A Dark, Dingy Basement
clips.summary: Branch and Smith describe Branch's husband building a clinic because as a Black doctor he had to hand hospital patients to a white doctor, the charity hospital in Laurel where Black patients were treated in a windowless basement past the furnace room, the privileged five o'clock appointments Smith's family got because of who her mother was, and Dr. Thacker's brick wall after the Klan threatened to kidnap his daughters for treating Black patients.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Could Not Read or Write
clips.summary: Branch explains a mother's fear most for the family's males, the bathrooms and hotels closed downtown, and her carpenter father who, literate with beautiful handwriting, was made a Navy instructor because most white Mississippians could not read or write, then turns to present-day recruiting problems, certificates-of-attendance GEDs, and jail become a business.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: They Never Gave Them Groceries
clips.summary: Branch and Smith describe a fighting Forrest County NAACP of life members that drew outsiders, Smith's frustration that the visitors who wrote the books got the credit and funds while locals who housed and fed them were never reimbursed, and how from 1961 churches supplied food the women cooked on Mobile Street for hungry workers, tracing lifelong NAACP membership through grandmothers whose receipts were found tucked in their Bibles.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: He Pulled On Their Sheets All Day
clips.summary: Smith, listed as crazy and unafraid of the police, explains that the Klan went to tear up other communities but feared Jones County because people there knew who was behind the sheets, recounts her grandfather donning a sheet to run off Klansmen headed to cut down Forrest County, and says she survived because her kin claimed her, with Newton Knight named as father on the birth certificate.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Lee Val Graves Went to Register
clips.summary: Smith describes a flyer showing her grandfather Lee Val Graves at Theron Lynd's counter to register, recounts being among the first and youngest to register at twenty-one in 1961 with J.C. Fairley, James Bourne, and Vernon Dahmer at her back, and credits world-history teacher Marjorie James, later pulled to the white school for being a good teacher, with preparing the students so well that the towering, small-headed Lynd passed her constitutional answer without reading it.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: He Was Going to Integrate Everything
clips.summary: Branch tells how her husband, who came from Memphis through Meharry to pay off a state loan and stayed for the medical need, took his licensing tests on a freight elevator yet alongside everyone, filed a complaint with the federal government, and set out to integrate the hospitals and doctors' offices as soon as they took Medicare money, before she pivots to the head-start story.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Send Them to the White House
clips.summary: Branch recounts telling Johnny Lee Roberts on the phone, with no car, no bus fare, and no daycare, that she ought to send her children to the White House for Lady Bird Johnson to mind, and how he found the money to fill a Greyhound with five hundred preschoolers sent to Washington in January, met there by women with proper winter clothing, after which Head Start came.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Not All White People Were Bad
clips.summary: Branch says the one thing she learned from the students and outsiders who came was that not all white people were devils, crediting some who went back and did wonderful things, among them Jill Wakeman, who had stayed with a local woman, mailed Branch ten dollars a month through her two and a half years of nursing school in New York, and opened one of the biggest soup kitchens in San Francisco.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Thank My Daddy for Finding Me a Husband
clips.topic: A Doctor at the Church Dedication
clips.summary: Branch describes her husband working through an organization of Black doctors and a Black hospital where he transferred patients, how he met her when she came to a church dedication in Laurel after her father took pneumonia while she was bound for Delaware State, and how her father talked her up to the doctor until she married him within four months instead of college, reflecting that education is a continuing thing and a person remains a sponge for learning.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Born to Leroy and Leo Vester
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Library of Congress on December 11, 2015, Reginald Robinson opens by recounting his birth in Baltimore in 1939 to Leroy and Leo Vester Robinson, his father's thirty-nine years at Bethlehem Steel and death a month after retiring, his mother's piecework, and his two older siblings Leroy and Lula Jean.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Streets Won
clips.summary: Robinson tells how he left Dunbar High in the tenth grade for the streets until a job fell apart, and how his cousin Josephine Thorn convinced his father to give him another chance at Cortez Peters Business College, founded by a Black man who could type 150 words a minute and taught Black students to pass government tests.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: When Things Broke Loose in Sixty
clips.summary: After the Greensboro sit-ins of 1960, Robinson describes the student Civic Interest Group forming in Baltimore, his school dean and city councilman Walter Dixon ordering him to represent the school at the big meetings, meeting students from Maryland colleges with Morgan in the lead, and moving from direct action toward voter registration.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Seat From Maryland
clips.topic: Becoming Treasurer and Taking the SNCC Seat
clips.summary: Robinson recounts becoming treasurer of the Civic Interest Group through his accounting background, registering some thirty thousand people in Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s area, and how, when the Mitchell family's NAACP would no longer bail the group and Clarence stepped aside, he took the open Maryland seat at SNCC, arriving with a black patch over an eye hit by a pretzel.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Son, I Know Who You Are
clips.summary: At the June 1961 SNCC meeting in Louisville, Kentucky, where everyone assumed his eye patch was a battle injury, Robinson describes standing on Anne Braden's porch with a beer when Ella Baker walked up, traced his family through Smithfield, Virginia and his mother Leo Vester, declared he was just like his uncle McKinley who worked with her in New York, and gave him standing in the group.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Collection Before the Preacher
clips.summary: Robinson tells of the big rally at the Baltimore Masonic Temple where King came to convince the students not to go South, and how he arranged to take the collection before Wyatt Tee Walker and the preachers arrived, splitting the proceeds between SNCC and the Civic Interest Group before Walker could call for it himself.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: They Thought I Was an Expert
