clips.summary: Robinson explains that his Baltimore voter registration, sound trucks and school buses and big parties that signed up thousands, made the group think him an expert, so he was elected to join Bob Moses in Mississippi, reasoning his life was in as much jeopardy in Baltimore and choosing to be productive in the South.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Top Hat and Tails in Atlanta
clips.topic: Ed King and a Detour to McComb
clips.summary: Robinson recalls reaching Atlanta to change trains, met by executive director Ed King in top hat and tails after integrating the National Symphony the night before, being shown around Auburn Avenue near King's SCLC office where Wyatt Tee Walker tried to recruit him, and getting word to meet Bob Moses in McComb rather than the Delta.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Super Cool Daddy
clips.summary: Robinson describes arriving in McComb, met by local cab driver George Hitz and welcomed by C.C. Bryant and Bob Moses, then walking off with Webb Owens, known as Super Cool Daddy, to arrange housing at Mama Cotton's, a deal at a local restaurant, a SNCC bank account, and a rented mimeograph machine.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Write That, Read That
clips.summary: Robinson recounts his fear canvassing the projects near white workmen in a sewer hole, and the school they set up over Burglund Supermarket to teach people to read and interpret the Mississippi constitution, since a registrar could flip to any page and fail an applicant whose interpretation he disliked.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Build Something and Leave
clips.topic: Herbert Lee and Ella Baker's Method
clips.summary: Robinson tells of walking applicants to the registrar, Bob Moses being beaten, Herbert Lee killed in the cotton gin by State Representative E.H. Hurst, and the lesson from his training rooted in Ella Baker, that organizers go in to duplicate themselves and leave something behind, working themselves out of a job rather than making a home.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: I Got Elected to Go to the Jail
clips.summary: Robinson recounts the students Curtis Hayes, Hollis Watkins, Bobby Talbert and Brenda Travis getting arrested at the bus station, and being chosen to visit them in jail at Magnolia, riding down with George Hitz, who pulled out a silver-handled pistol, while Robinson, dressed like a lawyer, faced a tobacco-chewing jailer.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Have Your Black Ass Get Out of Here
clips.summary: Robinson describes the doors slamming behind him as he climbed to the cell, finally seeing Curtis and Hollis, calming them and relaying the jailer's words, then calling the jailer to be let out and reporting to Julian Bond that everyone was fine, while noting he could not see Brenda Travis and the girls.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: East Baltimore, Strike One
clips.summary: Robinson backtracks to Baltimore, where coming from East Baltimore among upper-class West Baltimore students was a strike against him, and how he came to coordinate the demonstrations, piling seven or eight people into each of three Renaults to hit the biggest restaurants at lunch and dodge arrest after the police read the Miranda warning.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Little Willie Came to the Rescue
clips.summary: Robinson names the businessman William Little Willie Adams, who helped with bond when the NAACP argued the public accommodations bill was already in the courts, and the union representative Troy Brailey, who knew his father, sat on the students' advisory committee, and helped him into the treasurer's stipend.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Soothing My Mother
clips.summary: Robinson credits Elizabeth Murphy Oliver and her daughter Marcia of the Baltimore Afro-American newspaper family for introducing him around and the Jet and Ebony stringer Larry Steele, who on his runs through Baltimore would stop to reassure Robinson's mother he was safe, and reflects on his family's reactions, his proud father, his uncertain brother, and his own resolve that if he survived where he was going he would be all right the rest of his life.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Sound Getting Louder
clips.summary: Returning to McComb, Robinson describes the day the students walked out of school as the worst, with a warrant out for Diane Nash and Bob Moses begged not to come in, the sixteen of them singing and praying, then breaking out the signs and marching the students into town while Robinson took up an observer's post to phone reports to Julian Bond.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Through the Black Space
clips.topic: Escape Across the Masonic Temple Roof
clips.summary: Robinson recounts the violence breaking loose downtown, the police beating Bob Moses and picking up Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon, and how, watching from the Masonic Temple as a big sergeant climbed the stairs, he led Diane Nash and a man named Gaston through a black cloth onto the roof, where Gaston leapt off and was caught on the highway while Robinson got Diane Nash safely back inside.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: No Room in the Inn
clips.topic: The Crisfield Sit-In and His Only Arrest
clips.summary: Robinson tells how, McComb too hot, the group regrouped at B.B. Beamon's in Atlanta and divided the work before a call from Ruby Doris Smith Robinson sent him to the CORE office over untested New York volunteers, Faith Holsaert, Peggy Dammond, Angela Butler and others joined by Bill Hansen, who staged a Christmas 1961 sit-in at Governor Tawes's restaurant in Crisfield, Maryland, where the police arrested them, the only time in six years Robinson was locked up.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: We Build, We Grow, We Leave
clips.summary: Robinson recounts being jailed in Princess Anne with the Mitchell family as attorneys, the bondsmen Freddie St. Clair and Walter Black, and the group deciding on Freddie St. Clair's invitation to take a look at Cambridge, where weekend demonstrations grew as the community joined, the SNCC rule was to build then leave, and St. Clair gave up the organization to his cousin Gloria Richardson, of an old Cambridge family.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: You Cannot Stay Out of the Vote
clips.summary: Robinson recounts clashing with Gloria Richardson when the white establishment called a referendum on public accommodations and she urged Black residents not to vote, his argument that a SNCC affiliate could not advocate no vote while people died for the vote in the South, and the Cambridge board siding with him.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Get Your Man in Check
clips.summary: Sent with Bill Hansen to talk up a conference on the Black and white campuses, Robinson reflects on the white organizer he felt was hiding from his own community, his arrogant manner that drove Sal Mineo from a New York party with people like Godfrey Cambridge, and his announcing their movements aloud as they slipped into Cambridge at night.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Twelve High
clips.summary: Robinson tells how Ruby Doris split them up, Hansen to the white campuses and himself to the Black ones, his being smuggled onto a campus for two weeks, scouting the Air Force Academy area, and meeting twelve ministers they called the twelve high and Amelia Boynton, a connection that led to Bernard Lafayette being sent to Selma.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: A White Boy in the March
clips.topic: Why They Held Bob Moses Back
clips.summary: Asked to explain the McComb march, Robinson recounts the group's reasoning that a white man visible in the march would make it far rougher, contrasting Bob Moses, who moved openly with his community and was not hiding, with Bill Hansen, and how Moses insisting on coming aggravated the police, who beat him.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Amzie Convinced Us to Stay
clips.summary: Robinson describes how the young workers' first instinct after the killings was to run, until Amzie Moore counseled them to show they were unafraid, turning a funeral into a mass meeting where Bob Moses and McDew spoke and Robinson was assigned to photograph Louis Allen, who was later killed, and how James Forman's arrival organized SNCC.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: He Kept Trying to Find His Freedom
clips.summary: Robinson portrays Amzie Moore as a military veteran and postal worker who could not abide what he found back in Mississippi, joined the NAACP, and organized fearlessly, and explains how Moore, C.C. Bryant, Webb Owens, Aaron Henry and others were a network already built, so the workers walked into something needing only organizers, with McComb chosen over a harder county.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: How Direct Is a Crack on the Head
clips.summary: Robinson reflects on the 1961 SNCC debate between direct action and voter registration, saying he saw little difference since both got you beaten, and on confronting the National Guard in Cambridge, which made the accommodations fight there more intense than Mississippi, while Bobby Kennedy was the same adversary in McComb, Mississippi and Cambridge.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Watch Out, the White Folks Might Turn Green
clips.topic: John Doar and Affidavits in the Dark
clips.summary: Robinson recalls Robert Kennedy noting that John Doar had spoken of him from Mississippi, the night Bob Moses, John Doar, Marion Barry, George Hitz and he went to a farmer's house in an open field for a registration affidavit as the old man feared white folks might turn green, and how John Hardy, badly beaten at Walthall, was hurt that Doar pretended not to know him in the cell block, though Doar became a friend who later brought a Justice Department man to calm Cambridge with the profane Pop Herb St. Clair.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: A Penny Piece of Money
clips.summary: Robinson explains that after Mississippi and Alabama were funded a little Voter Education Project money was left for Orangeburg, South Carolina, where the NAACP wanted no SNCC people until Randolph Blackwell and Dr. Thomas at South Carolina State arranged for him to come in only to match registered voters to precinct captains, conferring with the state representative Pete Bryant while staying off Claflin's campus, a cafeteria demonstration breaking out when he was out of town, before he was sent to support Dottie Burlage's Northern Student Movement project in Raleigh.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: They Sent In George Gelston
clips.summary: Robinson describes Cambridge shut down and the Eastern Shore's mayor upset, the National Guard sent in under General George Gelston to quell things, and how Gelston became an ally, meeting with Robinson, Gloria Richardson and lawyer Fred Weisgal in Baltimore, even though Robinson warned the Guard would only make things worse.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: They Inkblots
clips.summary: Robinson recounts telling General Gelston he was classified four-F and mentally incompetent, and the story of his draft board exam, where he insisted to the sergeant that the inkblots were only inkblots, reasoning that if the army told him they were inkblots he would believe they were inkblots, which got him classified incompetent.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Muster Gas Over the Town
clips.topic: Tear Gas and the Tawes Nephew
clips.summary: Robinson tells how Gelston would let a kneel-and-pray demonstration pass and hold his troops, but one night when Gelston was away and his deputy Colonel Tawes commanded, Cliff Vaughs charged the young soldiers, the troops fired tear gas over the Black section, Courtland and Stokely were arrested, and Robinson helped people hit by canisters before getting everyone out.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Fire Returned
clips.summary: Robinson recounts negotiating over the absence of Black soldiers in the Guard until a Black colonel from Baltimore arrived and it was integrated, the Guard then observing the negotiations, and how before Gelston the town nearly erupted, with gunfire across an open field between the Black and white communities answered shot for shot.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Promises and a Strong Community
clips.summary: Robinson reflects that Cambridge's fight was about jobs and access like later Northern movements, with factories closing and watermen idle, many promises made by figures like Robert C. Weaver at HUD that he is unsure were ever kept, but a strong community left behind, which was always the purpose.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: She Followed the People
clips.summary: Robinson describes Gloria Richardson as a leader who took direction from her board and the demands of the people before later running with the New York act group, crediting her and the St. Clair name with making the Cambridge movement, and explaining the NAACP's stance that with cases in court there was no need to spend money bailing out demonstrators.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Building Clarences
clips.summary: Robinson explains that pushing the envelope when the NAACP thought them finished kept the movement alive until Henry G. Parks Jr. and others backed them, and recalls how voter registration's records required a secretary, Norma, who came up through Clarence Logan and went to Atlanta to work, in an office so small the early staff could barely fit.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Adult Crowd
clips.summary: Robinson recounts knowing the historian August Meier, a Morgan teacher close to the students, and the adult advisory group including Reverend Bascom, Reverend Vernon Dobson, Reverend Frank Williams and a Miss Adams, whose advice was not always taken, though their churches and approval were needed to release funds.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: They Bumped Our Speech
clips.summary: Robinson says he attended the March on Washington only to show up and be with his group, singing and eating, irritated that the organizers had already cut what the students wanted to say, his view of the power game then as now, and describes Judy Richardson emerging beside him in Cambridge as someone who simply came along and stuck.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Outsider Who Could Unite Them
clips.summary: Robinson describes the competition among NAG, Nashville and Atlanta hubs over leadership and nonviolence, contrasting Chuck McDew of Massillon, Ohio, whom one could not easily convert to nonviolence, with the divinity-minded Bernard Lafayette under Jim Lawson, and how the two could still act together under the nonviolent banner.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: He Didn't Want It
clips.summary: Robinson recounts taking McDew as his chairman and becoming close brothers, and his understanding that Ella Baker favored McDew for chair precisely because he was not lobbying for it and stood outside the school rivalries that would have stalled her vision, an outsider, like Robinson himself, who could bring the competing groups together.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: A Doodle of Hearts
clips.summary: Robinson tells of first sitting across from Diane Nash at a conference, transfixed by the most beautiful woman he had seen as he stared with his eye patch, and how Connie Curry later teased him about the heart he had doodled, until Ella Baker drew him in and he and Nash became friends, with Robinson later assigned to protect her during the McComb warrant.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Beauty in All the Other Ways
clips.summary: Robinson praises the brave and beautiful women of the movement, Diane Nash going to jail while pregnant, Grace Brooks leaving South Carolina State for the Free Southern Theater, and a woman who married Bill Withers, insisting that beauty was not only facial but in all the ways the sisters gave themselves.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: We Lived in a Whole House
clips.summary: Robinson names more of the women, Judy, Bobbi Yancy, Ruby Doris, Jessie Johnson who handled the money, Connie Curry and Casey Hayden, and recalls that, glad for anywhere to stay when no one else would take them, the workers once lived in what they only later understood was a whorehouse.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: It Was the Underworld
clips.topic: Who Led Cambridge and What Remains
clips.summary: Robinson observes that in Cambridge it was the men always being jailed, the pool-room crowd, who first joined the demonstrations because they were unafraid and had nothing to lose, and reflects that he cannot say people are happy there or in Mississippi today, where Black officials are many but production and businesses on Pine Street are gone.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: A Bank on the Corner
clips.topic: An Economic Program for the Community
clips.summary: Robinson lays out his recurring dream of a financial institution on a corner of a small town like Greenwood that would seed a laundromat, a general store, a filling station and a circuit-board factory, keeping money moving in the community, and jokes that he has had no money since SNCC paid him nine dollars and sixty-four cents yet counts himself rich.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: John's First Campaign
clips.summary: Robinson recalls working John Lewis's first campaign and the question of who ran against Julian Bond, then turns to leaving SNCC and the many directions people scattered, naming the Deacons of Defense work and Lowndes County, as he sets up his own post-movement path.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: You Boys
clips.summary: Robinson recounts leaving SNCC in 1965 to be McDew's second at a United Planning Organization center in DC, then Ed Brown bringing him to the Child Development Group of Mississippi as social service director, where he was fired after challenging Senator John C. Stennis for prefacing every remark with you boys.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: A Way Into the Panthers
clips.summary: Robinson describes joining an organization run by Peggy Dammond's husband on housing projects in Detroit, then Kaiser Engineers' forty-year plan to acquire and build on land in East Oakland with the Black Panthers, his being sent to reach them through his ties to Stokely, and the Oakland Panthers refusing him for arriving in a Kaiser Cadillac.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: West Project Equal
clips.summary: Robinson tells how, rejected by the Panthers, he switched the deal to a community group through Tim Jenkins's contact Don Warden, assembling local people who partnered with Kaiser to acquire land for the senior housing he says now stands as West Project Equal, and how on Kaiser's later model-cities contracts he was always sent to address the Black community but barred from the white meetings, and told he did not need to know, until he was out on the street again.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Six Hours of Sleep
clips.topic: The Pills and a Hard Choice
clips.summary: Married and suffering sharp back pains, Robinson describes a doctor prescribing pills that required two hours of sleep three times a day, and how, sitting in his chair with Jack Daniels and a joint, he reasoned he could not sleep six hours and support his family, and began instead to examine the habits weighing on him.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Up Three to Nothing
clips.summary: Robinson recounts quitting cigarettes after a friend told him three days would make it, then coffee, which he had drunk before every bus, train and phone call, switching to tea, while feeling trapped working jobs he did not like and bound, like his father before him, to take care of family first.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Job and the Marriage
clips.topic: Western University and a Failing Marriage
clips.summary: Robinson describes leaving OSTI for a welfare-program school running kindergarten through high school for grown women and derelict men, his marriage to a psychiatric social worker straining over their agreement that once she finished her master's she would support his turn to school, an agreement coming due as everything frayed.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: All My Records and My Chair
clips.summary: Robinson tells of a Bobbi Yancy call to a Memphis Black caucus dinner project, coming home to find his wife leaving as he had once told her she was free to, asking her to wait six weeks, then going on to the project for A.L. Nellum and Associates and, on returning to Boston, deciding to leave for good, settling in DC since 1981.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Only Computer on the Ground
clips.summary: Robinson recounts surviving in DC on consultancies and loans, getting into the charter with John A. Wilson, and joining a computer company, Afro-American Data Namix at Fourteenth and Kenyon, with a DEC PDP-11/45 that ran Marion Barry's first poll, until the electric bills outran the contracts and the lights went out.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Forty Thousand in a Summer
clips.topic: The Summer Youth Program and Retirement
clips.summary: Robinson describes joining Marion Barry's summer youth program, matching forty thousand young people to jobs one summer after a disastrous first try, then working as an unemployment-insurance investigator during the shift from paper to data, rising to assistant LAN administrator over a hundred machines before leaving government in May 2001.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The Red Comma FBP
clips.summary: Robinson recounts walking out of government the day an incoming administration herded busy workers to hear a paid incentive speaker, filing his papers at sixty-two exactly forty years after starting in May 1961, and signing his emails Robinson comma FBP, a free Black person who does anything he wants and counts himself rich though he has no money.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Fundraising Through Sixty-Four
clips.topic: The Tydings Job and the MFDP
clips.summary: Asked what he did in 1964, Robinson recalls leaving to work for Senator Joseph Tydings with Ella Baker's blessing, lobbying for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in California and Oregon, and being sent to Atlantic City to find housing, where he and Mary Lovelace O'Neal of NAG secured hotel rooms through one of the city's biggest gangsters, then fundraising in New York and Philadelphia until a man named Fred Mealy undid the Philly gains and Forman pulled him toward Texas as 1965 fell into disarray.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Call Me the Pickup Man
clips.summary: Robinson describes his role as the advance and pickup man who, when reports stopped coming from the field, drove with Jim Forman through Mississippi, Georgia and Alabama, even checking the Deacons of Defense in North Carolina, and characterizes Forman as a matter-of-fact military thinker who would give you the plan and your spot in it.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Ride the Bus Up Front
clips.summary: Robinson recounts staying out of the philosophical talk of Fanon and Douglass to focus on strategy, and his Freedom Ride work, helping arrange for riders like Gwen Green, Travis Britt and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson to come down to McComb for voter registration, and his own assignment to ride the bus up front when Montgomery was opened.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: A Charm to Work With
clips.summary: Robinson explains his unease that Bill Hansen announced their moves aloud when entering Cambridge by night through a dark bus stop, notes Hansen later becoming a professor in Nigeria, and concedes that Hansen, for all that, was knowledgeable about organization, someone you could walk the line with in the struggle.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Not the Time to Die for a Piss
clips.topic: Forman and the Gun in Albany
clips.summary: In Albany, Robinson recalls Jim Forman returning to the car still needing the bathroom because a man inside had a gun, joking he would not give his life for the revolution just to take a piss, and Robinson's own assignment hanging around Dr. Anderson's house listening as King's people came in.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Old Poke Chops
clips.summary: Robinson describes Wyatt Tee Walker driving him to the Albany jail to deliver a tray, the deal that only movement food reached King and Abernathy, the two in silk pajamas, King thanking him and eating his pork chops while Abernathy, whom Robinson called Old Poke Chops, asked where the ice cream was, before Robinson went back to follow Charles Sherrod's orders in Baker and Terrell.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: The First SNCC Worker in Selma
clips.summary: Robinson recounts Bob Moses taking him back to Selma years later for the Jubilee, introducing him as the first SNCC worker to land there, and describes Moses's organization the Trail and the Tent City on Highway 80 in Lowndes County, whose records Moses's family still withholds as he wished.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Ella and My Mother
clips.topic: A Family Bond With Ella Baker
clips.summary: Robinson describes how Ella Baker became a close mother figure through her bond with his own mother, who fixed lunch for Baker and Fannie Lou Hamer in DC, and how the night before his wedding Ella Baker and Joanne Grant took him out and got him drunk, before he married at Harry Belafonte's house with Baker and his mother as his support.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Naked in the Mountain Stream
clips.summary: Robinson tells how, worn down in Cambridge, Ella Baker sent him to the psychiatrist Joe Baker of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, who sent him to rest a week in Peekskill, New York around a Paul Robeson fundraiser, where he was taken to the home of a doctor who had gone to Russia for Robeson and sat naked in a sauna and an icy mountain stream beside Paul Robeson himself.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Denim Closer to the People
clips.topic: Chasing the Silk Suits Out of Cambridge
clips.summary: Robinson recounts planting a story with a reporter contrasting organizers in denim who worked close to local people with outsiders in shiny silk suits who came to make collections, which the Cambridge community traced to the NAACP and ran them out of town, and reflects that SNCC was making good on Cambridge without needing to control it like a big organization.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: One Side Junkies, One Side Millions
clips.topic: Baltimore, Freddie Gray, and the Police
clips.summary: Robinson connects the Freddie Gray unrest to Baltimore he grew up in, describing a Pikesville street with a multimillion-dollar operation on one side and junkies on the other, arguing nothing changes while people are out of work, that the police are part of the power that controls, and that taking away people's income only makes them fight.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: We Were Not Talking About Guns
clips.topic: Voter Registration, a Black President, and Black Power
clips.summary: Robinson argues that voter registration produced only leaders bound by the same rules and that he felt no different when a Black president came, citing a Black man arrested entering his own house, and contends the move toward Black power was about money and opportunity, schools and recreation, not guns, distorted by a media that latched onto the Panthers' rifles, insisting a funded economic base is the real way to fight.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: They Are the Leaders
clips.summary: Asked what people should know about the movement, Robinson credits Ella Baker's simple plan of taking people from where they are and lifting them, those on top supporting those at the bottom, and the lesson that there is no one leader but that anyone who rises to do something becomes a leader and gathers followers, so they need no other Martin Luther King.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Martin Ain't Drove No Damn Way
clips.summary: Robinson criticizes the film Selma for inventing scenes of Joseph Rauh reprimanding Forman and of John riding with King driving, when Forman had his own organization and standing and King did not drive, praising instead Freedom Song for showing organizers registering people, and insisting John Lewis chose to leave SNCC and was not put out.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Coming Back to Organize
clips.summary: Robinson values the SNCC Legacy Project for letting the organizers tell their own story against efforts to rewrite or erase them, says he answered a call that he is available to go to Selma or Ferguson and do whatever is needed short of his piano lessons and fishing, and returns to his dream of an honest, gutsy pooling of Black resources for the community.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Henry Parks and the Policy Book
clips.topic: A Sausage Fortune and a Closing
clips.summary: Robinson holds up Henry G. Parks Jr., the hidden Black owner of Parks Sausage Company and the Royal Theatre on the Chitlin' Circuit who moved money through the community from a policy book, as proof that seven or eight pooling their money could seed a bank, a laundromat and a factory in a small town and tell the Klan they had enough to do it, then closes by noting outside buyers would only bring more economics, thanking the interviewers, and restating his policy of granting interviews only in the context of the Legacy Project, vouched for through Charlie.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: My Wife Liberated Me From the Plantation
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline at Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport, Louisiana, in October 2013, Harry Blake recounts being born in Arkansas in 1934 to a Mississippi-born sharecropper father who could not read or write and a mother who finished third grade, and living on a plantation until he finished college at twenty-four, the year he says his wife liberated him from the plantation.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: I Taught the Mule My Language
clips.topic: A One-Room School and the Mule
clips.summary: Blake describes a one-room church school taught by a college sophomore for grades one through seven, learning to read only as he neared his teens, and how, plowing mules in high school, he refused to learn mule language and instead taught the mule to stop and turn on his own spoken words paired with the pull of the reins.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Salt the Diet
clips.summary: He draws lessons from his father's saying that salt in a horse's diet will make it find water on its own, applying it to making a congregation hungry for a mission, and warns, citing Jim Jones and David Koresh, that knowing how to lead means a clergyman must keep his motives right to lead people in the right direction.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: From One-Sunday Churches to Allendale
clips.summary: Blake names the two plantations he lived on, the Ashley place in Madison Parish near Tallulah and the Wood Spur place north of Shreveport, and traces his first rural pastorates in one-Sunday and other part-time churches before coming in 1966 to this church in the densely populated Allendale slum, where the dilapidated building he inherited later collapsed.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: You Will Never Be My Equal
clips.summary: Asked about relationships with white people, Blake describes a confined cordiality in which the white man's favored worker got what he needed, was kept off the World War II draft by farmers on the draft board, and was told to stay out of the cemetery and out of the white part of town, a paternalism he sums up as handing down to a man but never out to him.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: You Owed Your Soul to the Store
clips.summary: He lays out the commissary system in which the planter advanced food, work clothing, mules, and implements against a sharecropper's account, split a twenty-bale crop ten and ten, and at year's end declared the worker had only broken even, so a family could leave only if another farmer paid to move them, his own father somehow clearing two to five hundred dollars a year.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Interpret the Constitution
clips.summary: Blake states that no black person on his plantation was registered to vote when he turned twenty, and tells how he registered at twenty-one by giving a friend's city address and studying the required literacy test, which demanded a white voter to vouch for you, identification, interpreting the United States Constitution to a registrar's satisfaction, and computing your exact age in years, months, and days, a barrier he later helped others overcome teaching voter education on Martin Luther King's staff.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: You Are a Big Dog
clips.topic: A Father Who Worked the System
clips.summary: Blake describes his illiterate father's gift for working the system, paying outward homage to white men he called Captain and Boss while teaching by demonstration, once telling the boss admiring his new car that he was a big dog so he could call him a dog and get by with it, never diminishing his own manhood.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Because My Wife Likes You
clips.summary: He recounts a burning yearning to change what he saw was wrong, and how around 1952 in the little town of Dixie he asked store owner H.L. Whitlow for a summer job, was turned away until Whitlow's wife took a liking to him, and got hired at a store he says practiced equal employment in the fifties, with a white Louisiana Tech student and a black Grambling student both working for him.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: What in the Hell You Think You're Worth
clips.summary: Blake tells how, offered fifteen dollars for six longer days when the plantation paid fifteen for five, he offered to donate the week and was challenged to name his worth, demanded twenty-five dollars, and got it thrown at him, then drove Whitlow's car to school and rose to three hundred dollars a month, more than any black person he knew, under a man white friends called a nigger lover for treating people fairly.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: The Deaf and Dumb Worker
clips.summary: He describes refusing to answer a white postmaster-farmer who called him boy outside the store, reasoning that no law required him to respond to a man who did not sign his check, until the man told Whitlow he had found a deaf and dumb worker, an act of quiet resistance Blake calls his early beginnings of fighting the system, with his father as the model.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Martin Was My Commencement Speaker
clips.summary: Blake recalls following the Baton Rouge bus boycott led by Reverend T.J. Jemison that King studied for Montgomery, reading King's Stride Toward Freedom, and having King as his commencement speaker at Bishop College in Marshall, Texas, before local leader Dr. C.O. Simkins arranged the interview that brought him onto King's staff in February 1960 to work southern Louisiana.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Arrested for Mental Observation
clips.topic: First Arrest and a Bizarre Charge
clips.summary: He recounts being arrested on his first day of work, March 1st, 1960, while sitting in a parked car downtown, fingerprinted, warned by the chief not to ruin his future fooling with the communist Martin Luther King, and charged with careless and reckless driving though no officer had seen him drive, then his most memorable arrest, when he and executive director Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker were jailed thirty-six hours for mental observation after the Klan protested a King service and NAACP youth counter-protested.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Four Holes in the Coat
clips.topic: Assassination Attempt and the Hardest City
clips.summary: He explains the authorities could only invent that he took orders from Moscow, and recounts an assassination attempt on October 12th, 1960, when a car fired a bullet that shattered his radio and left four holes in his coat, after which the sheriff reported the owner said he was bowling, then frames Shreveport, which the Civil Rights Commission called the most difficult city in the South, as a plantation environment without an immortalized Medgar Evers that kept black people disciplined to obedience.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: I Never Taught My Daddy to Write His Name
clips.summary: He recounts warning his parents that joining King could mean injury or death for them as well as him, his father answering he would be disappointed if Blake did anything different, learning only later that police humiliated his father by calling him a stupid nigger, and the celebration of helping his father pass the voter test set against the regret that he never taught him to write his name.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Five Minutes to Make My Decision
clips.summary: After the four girls were killed in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing and President Kennedy declared a day of mourning, Blake sought a permit for a memorial march in Shreveport on September 22nd, 1963, from Booker Washington High School to Little Union Baptist Church, was stalled until Saturday noon and refused, and was warned the public-safety commissioner would arrest everyone who marched.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: They Left Me for Dead on the Lawn
clips.summary: Blake describes telling people to gather and simply enter the church rather than march while police on horses with bayonets and garbage trucks filled the community, and how, going to the vestibule as the service ended, he was beaten by the commissioner and other policemen, each striking his head with a nightstick, dragged outside and left for dead on the lawn on September 22nd, 1963.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: It Looked Like a War Zone
clips.summary: He recounts his family fearing white doctors and sending him to Dallas, students at the high school launching a protest met with beatings, tear gas, and an arrested white teacher, Shreveport entering its greatest protest movement whose fiftieth anniversary had just been marked, and his own recovery in faculty housing arranged by the president of Bishop College.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: A Public Proclamation of Apology
clips.topic: D'Artois, a Jailed Student, and an Apology
clips.summary: Blake explains that the protest came from students because parents feared the violent Commissioner D'Artois, that the city jailed one student singled out as a leader for some thirty to forty days and barred him from school until a diploma came at the fiftieth anniversary, and that forty years after the beating the city issued a public proclamation of apology that Blake keeps on his wall.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Property Shall Be Sold to Mount Canaan
clips.topic: Deals With the Mayor and Land for Housing
clips.summary: As a new pastor in 1966 Blake confronted the commissioner who beat him and the mayor, telling each he might repeat his past actions if he deemed it necessary, and the mayor, a politician who had quietly advised the churches against him, asked what he could do, leading to an ordinance that sold eight acres of city park land to Mount Canaan Baptist Church for thirty-five thousand dollars limited to housing, recreation, education, or religious use, on which the church built a 120-unit housing complex, a daycare, and later a senior citizens high-rise.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: The Immoral Things
clips.summary: Blake frames community work as part of the movement, which targeted immoral things like paying bus fare to stand in the back, the hamburger window, and white-only restrooms, and argues that he and King sought equal funding and capable teachers for black schools rather than the integration that bused children to white schools and closed the black ones.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: We Got Duped
clips.summary: He contends black people got duped by an integration they did not seek, that open hotels and restaurants mean little without a decent income, and recalls being a cashier handling money in the Whitlow country store ten to fifteen years before any black cashier appeared in the city, adding that he wants not to hold office but to determine who does.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: A Captive Audience Every Sunday
clips.summary: Asked about black power and allies, Blake locates lasting power in the black church, a captive Sunday audience where a leader can salt the diet, and recalls that blacks once feared King's presence in Shreveport, that several churches voted he could not preach, and that around 1961 this church voted against calling Blake for fear it would be bombed or burned.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: How Many Prison Cells by Fourth Grade
clips.summary: Nearing eighty and winding down, Blake describes a national crisis in schools and the statistic that prison capacity is projected from fourth-grade boys' performance, leading him to a ministry to develop strong, moral black men, a summer boys academy that he says raised one boy from a second to a sixth grade reading level.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: We Got to Have Walter
clips.summary: Blake tells of a twelve-year-old named Walter who could not read past fourth-grade material, prompting him to start a virtual homeschool inside the church with a recently retired teacher of more than thirty years, now serving five boys daily from eight to three, the church giving him full range to try anything that helps people.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Best Thing in the Center of the Hood
clips.summary: Blake closes by describing one-on-one intervention as the heart of even a mass movement, declining thanks for himself, and calling Mount Canaan church, set in the center of the hood and giving him full range to bring about change, the best thing that ever happened to him, before the interview ends.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Our Host in St. Augustine
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the September 14, 2011 session at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine, Florida, thanking local civil rights pioneer Dr. Robert B. Hayling, who recounts being born in Tallahassee on November 20, 1929, son of a Florida A&M tailoring instructor and a homemaker.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Every Child an Advanced Degree
clips.summary: Hayling describes his Creole-descended mother from New Orleans and his father from Grenada who taught at Florida A&M for some three decades, his older brother Charles Jr. and sisters Frances and Yvonne, and a family in which all four children earned college and advanced degrees, three of them in education.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Stranger Than Hen's Teeth
clips.summary: He recounts entering the Air Force in 1951, training as a medical and laboratory technician at Sheppard Air Force Base, earning his commission as a second lieutenant through Officer Candidate School in December 1952, and a coveted assignment in aeromedical toxicology research at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, where his fellow officers were captains, majors, and colonels.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Always Wanted to Be a Dentist
clips.summary: Hayling tells how he turned down an early promotion to captain to take an early release for dental school, having always wanted to be a dentist or veterinarian, and how he and his new wife, a Miami schoolteacher he brought to Dayton, gave up the comforts of officer life and a beloved boxer puppy named Mandana as she returned to Miami before he started at Meharry in September 1955.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: A Class of Tremendous Honors
clips.summary: He describes the difficult adjustment to study among accomplished peers at Meharry Medical College in Nashville, naming classmate Fred Fielder who later rose to be dean of the dental school, and a cohort whose members went on to honors in dentistry, entrepreneurship, and financial consulting.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Looby's House Was Dynamited
clips.summary: Hayling recalls his dental-law instructor Z. Alexander Looby, lead attorney for school desegregation, having his home dynamited in 1960, blowing out windows of Hayling's nearby Meharry dormitory, the movement leaders he came to know (John Lewis, Diane Nash, C.T. Vivian, James Bevel), and slipping out to join the April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall protesting the bombing.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: C.K. Steele and the Bus Boycott
clips.summary: Hayling recounts the two Tallahassee movements he knew on visits home: Reverend C.K. Steele's bus boycott that bankrupted the transit system and earned him a monument at the bus station, and the work of Patricia and Priscilla Stephens and attorney John Due, who were jailed in a wire-fenced setup with no privacy and later wrote the book Freedom in the Family.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: The Thousand-Dollar Supplement
clips.topic: How Florida Paid for Dental School
clips.summary: He explains that Florida, lacking a dental school for Black students, paid Meharry a thousand-dollar yearly supplement for each Black Floridian enrolled, which along with his service record and the GI Bill financed his education and spared him the heavy student debt of later generations.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Sky Cap at Newark Airport
clips.summary: Hayling describes financing dental school by working New York summers, as an overnight sky cap at Newark Airport for the Port Authority and as a waiter at a famous Sheepshead Bay seafood restaurant near Coney Island that employed a hundred or more waiters and owned its own shrimp boats, staying with his father's sister and other relatives in Brooklyn.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Driving the Coast for an Office
clips.summary: He recounts taking the Florida dental board exam over the July 4 weekend of 1960 and driving the state from the east coast to Key West and up to Pensacola scouting cities with Black dentists, settling on St. Augustine, where the late Dr. Gordon's office had become available after a fatal car accident, over a Panama City option in the isolated panhandle.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: They Rolled Up the Sidewalks
clips.summary: Hayling tells how Dr. Gordon's widow welcomed him and introduced him to her late husband's largely white patient base, how the office still reeked of the expensive Cuban cigars Gordon smoked by the carton, and how his practice boomed at a December 1960 housewarming as patients who had gone two years without care returned, even as he drove to Jacksonville nightspots to escape a town that drew its curtains at sundown.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: The Jail Dentist
clips.summary: He describes becoming the city, county, and state-prison-farm jail dentist of St. Johns County and St. Augustine, scheduling jail patients apart so armed guards would not unsettle others, and treating many deputies and even Sheriff L.O. Davis on the house under no formal arrangement, insisting he was just a good fellow until, in his words, he became radioactive once he got involved with civil rights.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: A Delegation Came to My Office
clips.summary: Hayling explains he was never formally sent by the NAACP, and recounts how a youth protest movement begun under Reverend Thomas A. Wright lost its advisor when Wright's wife was fired and he was pressured out of St. Augustine to Gainesville, after which a delegation of youngsters asked Hayling, around 1963, to advise their picketing and demonstrations.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: A Hamburger and a Coke
clips.summary: He describes the youth following the rules of the sidewalk while merchants merely tolerated them, the lunch-counter request the group devised for the news media (ordering a hamburger and a coke), and the local NAACP women who supported them: chapter president Fannie Fulwood and secretary Elizabeth Hawthorne, who rode her bicycle across town despite a spinal deformity to serve as the youth council's stabilizing secretary.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Not One Black Appointed
clips.summary: Hayling recounts regional NAACP director Ruby Hurley warning that the organization was not ready for a major St. Augustine campaign, just as the city prepared to spend a federal $400,000 appropriation on its 400th-anniversary celebration without appointing a single Black person to any committee, despite Black principals, professors, and a school superintendent, Mrs. Reddick, and college president Dr. Puryear.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: A Letter to the Vice President
clips.summary: He describes the grand anniversary dinner at the Ponce de Leon Hotel in late spring 1963, drawing the ambassador from Spain, the vice president, senators, and the governor, and how the group wrote Vice President Lyndon Johnson asking him not to attend a segregated function, generating enough heat that Johnson sent aide George Reedy ahead to meet repeatedly with Hayling.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: A Secretary and a Tape Recorder
clips.summary: Hayling recounts being seated in an alcove overlooking the Grand Ballroom, dining only alongside Secret Service and FBI agents, and the promised next-morning meeting with the City Commission that turned out to be only the mayor's secretary and her tape recorder, the group's request for a biracial committee taped and never answered, and the stunt of dressing the plainclothes Lincolnville officer in a uniform downtown to claim the city already had a Black policeman.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Back to the Think Tanks
clips.summary: Hayling admits the group had no battle plan and had to regroup after each defeat made them look foolish, while the national and regional NAACP warned they were generating more heat than it could handle, and credits a restaurant across from his office run by Mr. Pennington, who fed any demonstrator connected to the movement and was reimbursed at day's end.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Cut Off at the Knees
clips.summary: He thanks the Elks, who lent the second floor of the Elks Rest for meetings as the press gathered, describes the dual-headed structure in which the youth stayed out of negotiations while adult spokesmen like Henry Twine, Oscar Turner, and Clyde Jenkins led, the formal demands to let Black residents sit for the fireman's and policeman's exams, and the establishment ordering the college's faculty and 300 to 400 students not to participate on pain of losing their jobs.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Tobias Simon, ACLU Fireball
clips.summary: He recounts threatening the college with a lawsuit when instructors denied makeup work to student demonstrators, and acquiring the services of Miami ACLU attorney Tobias Simon, a civil rights fireball lost too soon to a heart attack after running a Boston Marathon, before turning to a prominent local student then at a North Carolina college, Hank Thomas.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Sammy Davis Put Up the Bond
clips.summary: Hayling tells how the Freedom Rider Hank Thomas, who later owned McDonald's franchises and Marriott hotels in Atlanta, staged a one-man drugstore sit-in in St. Augustine and was jailed on a hundred-dollar bond that the NAACP let it be known Sammy Davis Jr. had paid, then was ordered out of town, but the arrest had started the movement and spread word through the community.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: The Meeting Was Canceled
clips.summary: He recounts becoming the first African American admitted with full privileges to the local and state dental association, and how, when his rotating position made him due for election to leadership, pressure on the other dentists got the meeting abruptly canceled, with members afterward phoning to ask why he had missed gatherings his secretary was never told about.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Connie Lynch Comes to Town
clips.summary: Hayling describes the local Klan, calling itself the Klavern, papering downtown with handbills for a major September 1963 rally on a field along US 1, bringing in out-of-state leaders Connie Lynch of California and J.B. Stoner of Atlanta, and how he and three others drove out that night to park on the roadside and watch the rally from across the highway.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Piled Like Cordwood
clips.summary: He recounts being spotted with James Jackson, James Hauser, and Clyde Jenkins, fleeing down a side road only to meet a moat the Klan had dug across it, being dragged from the car, robbed, and exposed as an NAACP member, then beaten with ax handles and baseball bats and piled semiconscious like cordwood on the rally stage.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Smelling a Burning
clips.summary: Hayling describes an Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach who infiltrated the rally with a recorder, then phoned the Tallahassee highway patrol, and the announcer asking the crowd whether they had smelled a man burn and threatening to douse the captives with gasoline, before law enforcement arrived and the men were taken first to Flagler Hospital's emergency room, then by mortician E.G. Chase's hearse to the Black Brewster Hospital in Jacksonville.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Damage to a Dentist's Arm
clips.summary: He summarizes injuries he would take to his grave, losing eleven teeth and suffering deliberate damage to the right arm of a right-handed dentist that he had to rehabilitate to keep practicing, and reflects that four years of honorable Air Force service did not win him a white citizen's rights, that L.O. Davis later branded him a communist, and that the local Klan leader was Holstead Hoss Manucy, a convicted moonshiner deputized with many Klansmen as auxiliary officers by the sheriff he was close to.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: The Mailman Will Bring Your Charter
clips.summary: He recounts repeated calls from Roy Wilkins's New York office instructing the movement how to proceed, telling Wilkins to send anyone who could lead St. Augustine better, and when Wilkins threatened to withdraw the charter, replying that the mailman would bring it back, mailing the charter to New York by special delivery to resign before the NAACP could fire them.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Up the Creek Without a Paddle
clips.topic: Seeking SCLC and Refusing to Stop
clips.summary: Hayling describes weekend trips to Atlanta after the break, meeting Julian Bond, the SNCC staff, and Ella Baker, and people connected to Daddy King and Ebenezer Baptist Church, with C.K. Steele on the SCLC board helping make contacts, and how, when President Johnson asked civil rights groups for a two-week halt to demonstrations, the twice-double-crossed St. Augustine chapter refused to stop until the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was signed, with King in St. Augustine on June 30, 1964.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Yale Students for Spring Break
clips.summary: Hayling recounts Reverend William Sloane Coffin Jr. of Yale Divinity School arranging for Yale students to spend their spring break in St. Augustine running a tutorial and fix-up program rather than going to the beaches, a publicity coup funded by the students and their families, the white volunteers housed in Black families' homes, and a fleeting chance to speak with Dr. King when a St. Augustine group traveled to a King rally in Tallahassee.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: A Play About St. Augustine
clips.summary: Hayling recounts weekend trips to New York and Boston, visits to Harvard and MIT, the Yale students staging a drama about St. Augustine with characters playing L.O. Davis and Judge Mathis, and the wealthy supporters who funded the movement, including Esther Burgess, wife of the first Black Episcopal bishop John Burgess, and Mary Peabody, the seventy-two-year-old mother of the sitting Massachusetts governor, arrested alongside Hayling at the Monson Motor Lodge.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Acid in the Swimming Pool
clips.summary: Asked to reflect on the campaign's culmination in the June 1964 signing of the Civil Rights Act, Hayling recalls Monson Motor Lodge owner James Brock pouring acid into the pool as demonstrators swam, a picture that circled the world and even ran on a Russian front page, and a man who died an uncompromising segregationist though his daughter came to differ.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Lyndon Johnson Caught Us Again
clips.summary: Hayling explains that after fulfilling his five-year obligation to Florida by practicing in St. Augustine, he opened a booming practice in Cocoa near Cape Kennedy with help from Black mortician Rudy Stone and physician Dr. Jerkins, only for Johnson to move the space center's control center and payroll to the Johnson Space Center, and why he chose Cocoa's younger NASA-technician community over inheriting a Tallahassee practice from his family dentist Dr. Campbell.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: The Award of Valor
clips.summary: Hayling describes receiving the city of St. Augustine's Award of Valor, presented by the mayor and commissioners at ACCORD where Dorothy Cotton of the SCLC spoke, and honoring the family of Reverend Goldie Eubanks with a Waterford crystal eagle-in-flight award, attended by his Yale- and Stanford-educated daughter, her Princeton- and Harvard-trained husband Chris, and their two grandsons from Singapore.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: While I Can Still Smell the Roses
clips.topic: A Street and the Foot Soldiers
clips.summary: He recalls the June 20, 2003 renaming of Scott Street to Dr. Hayling Place, attended by twenty-one Haylings and his infant grandson Roman, Commissioner Earl Jones's wish to honor him while he could still smell the roses, praise for Jones's mother Maddie Jones, who worked at his office for years when others shunned him as a leper for fear it would be shot up or dynamited, and the street sign he carries to gatherings of the movement's foot soldiers as the interview closes.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: It Was Explained I Would Be a Lawyer
clips.summary: Interviewed at his New Orleans home with his wife Anne, Richard Sobol sketches growing up on the west side of Manhattan, his lawyer father and mathematics-teacher mother, the Bronx High School of Science, Union College, and Columbia Law School, where he wrote a published note on the Supreme Court's Alabama gerrymander decision.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Stars in My Eyes
clips.summary: Sobol traces his path from clerking for Judge Paul R. Hays on the Second Circuit to a stint at the Federal Trade Commission and an associate's job at Arnold, Fortas & Porter in 1963, drawn by Abe Fortas's right-to-counsel case Gideon, only to find the firm quite different from his idealistic expectations.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Assigned to New Orleans
clips.summary: Turned down in 1964 for lack of a Southern bar admission, Sobol describes meeting Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee director Henry Schwarzschild that winter and being sent in August 1965 for a three-week volunteer stint, disappointed to draw New Orleans rather than Selma or Jackson, a letter he says changed his life.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Relief in Eight Days
clips.topic: A School Case Before Judge West
clips.summary: Sobol recounts filing a West Feliciana Parish school desegregation case before the racist Judge E. Gordon West in Baton Rouge, its dismissal the next day, and the Fifth Circuit's summary reversal a week later, convincing him the South needed permanent lawyers rather than vacationing volunteers.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: A Permanent Lawyer in New Orleans
clips.summary: He describes befriending the Collins, Douglas, and Elie firm, the most important civil rights firm in Louisiana and counsel to the Congress of Racial Equality, and how Schwarzschild raised the money over the winter for Sobol to return in summer 1966 as the permanent New Orleans lawyer, his early contact with Bogalusa figures Bob Hicks, Gayle Jenkins, and A.Z. Young.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: A Bottle of Scotch in the Bottom Drawer
clips.topic: The Black Lawyers of New Orleans
clips.summary: Sobol portrays the three Black attorneys, Robert F. Collins, later the first Black federal judge in the South, Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie, scraping out a living on accident and bankruptcy work in a poor neighborhood while serving CORE, gathering each evening in Collins's office for a drink and to talk over the day's work.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Hot and Smelly From the Paper Mill
clips.summary: He describes Bogalusa's nightly mass meetings and Main Street marches against store owners, the focus on Crown Zellerbach as Title VII took effect, and his first appearance before the town's angry, crutch-wielding city judge, who slammed his crutch on the table when Nils Douglas introduced Sobol as coming from the firm of the newest Supreme Court nominee.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: The File Cards Were Four Inches Thick
clips.summary: Sobol tells of moving down with his wife and two children despite a bomb thrown at the Collins firm's hallway, working through a thick stack of pending-case file cards built by volunteer Jerry Gutman, and persuading Schwarzschild that hiring a local lawyer like Tulane graduate Don Juneau was cheaper than flying volunteers in.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Starting Over on a Blank Sheet
clips.topic: CORE, LDF, and the EEOC Charge
clips.summary: He distinguishes the CORE legal network, funded through New York and Carl Rachlin, from the NAACP Legal Defense Fund, and describes rewriting the rushed July 1965 Crown Zellerbach EEOC charge to spell out testing, seniority, and segregated-local-union practices that became prominent issues.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: It Became Active Because of Them
clips.summary: Sobol attributes Bogalusa's intensity to three committed local leaders, a unionized Crown Zellerbach workforce accustomed to organization, and a San Francisco support group, the company's home city, that sent money and flew each leader west, and calls it the hardest-line segregationist city he saw, recalling that decades later in the 1990s the mayor still would not grant Black police officers modest requests like posting training opportunities.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: She Wanted a Man to Lead It
clips.summary: Sobol credits Gayle Jenkins as the spark of the Bogalusa Voters League who, by her account, recruited the outspoken A.Z. Young to lead it because she felt a man would be more effective, and brought in her cousin Bob Hicks and his wife, all of whom played significant roles.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Rifles on the Roof
clips.summary: He describes the Hicks home on what became Robert Hicks Avenue as the hub for everyone coming to Bogalusa, where James Farmer and CORE's Lincoln Lynch visited, recounts the men who guarded the roof with rifles against threats, and contrasts the frontman A.Z. Young with the detail-minded Hicks who became plaintiff in many suits.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Consolidated With the Government
clips.summary: With additional plant cases filed and the Free Southern Theater and SNCC Freedom Singers passing through, Sobol describes bringing the Crown Zellerbach case to trial in 1967, consolidated with United States versus Local 189, applying the methodical discovery, depositions, and document demands he had learned doing antitrust work, with George Cooper researching employment testing.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: A Two-Day Hearing on the Prom Queen
clips.summary: Sobol recounts how unexpectedly sympathetic Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe handled all three Bogalusa employment cases and the school case, treating even a two-day hearing on prom-queen selection seriously, and praises a Fifth Circuit that gave litigants what they were entitled to overnight, conditions he credits more than any special skill.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: There Were Black Jobs and White Jobs
clips.summary: He recalls drawing on the Virginia precedent Quarles versus Philip Morris and learning from Department of Justice lawyers Robert Moore and David Rose how seniority systems harmed Black employees barred from white jobs, so that by trial in the Local 189 case Judge Heebe issued an injunction and preliminary judgment.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Freedom of Choice Was Token
clips.summary: Sobol describes the most token desegregation under freedom of choice, where a few Black children pushed by their parents entered white schools, the dilapidated Black school buildings and underpaid teachers he saw firsthand, and the office's drive to abolish freedom of choice, first ordered in Washington Parish by Judge Heebe.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Two or Three Days, Not Twenty-Five Years
clips.summary: Granted a court order to view the plant, Sobol recounts seeing that the corrugated-board jobs were simple and repetitive, learnable in days rather than the twenty-five-year wait the company demanded, while the highest-paid Black job sat a notch below the lowest white job on a pay scale that climbed six or seven steps.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: I Never Slept in Bogalusa
clips.summary: He explains the overlapping sex and race discrimination across the box plant, bag plants, and paper mill, where women worked only in the all-white bag plants, and tells of his rule never to sleep in Bogalusa or such towns, crossing into Natchez or Vicksburg out of a fear of violence.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: An Unorthodox View of the Deacons
clips.summary: Sobol offers a skeptical account of the Deacons for Defense, saying he never heard the name while working in Bogalusa and saw no guns, only friends of the Hicks family guarding the roof with rifles, a view he says Lolis Elie shares, suggesting the Deacons story is attractive to historians.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Troopers Between the Marchers
clips.summary: He recalls Klan violence he heard of but rarely saw, the earlier Hicks against Knight injunction against Bogalusa police concluded the day he arrived, and the march to Baton Rouge through Livingston Parish, the Klan's reputed headquarters, where guardsmen lined the route and soda bottles flew over the police.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: He Opened the Trunk of Books
clips.summary: Returning to the 1967 trial before Judge Heebe, Sobol describes George Cooper arriving with a camp trunk full of books, piling them on the counsel table and confronting each Crown witness with their own prior writings on testing, and the charts they made on the seniority issue, with Bob Hicks at his side through eight days as Bogalusa workers filled the courtroom.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: What Is RSVP
clips.summary: He recaps the Wonderlic test, a short general-knowledge exam never required of incumbent white workers until the Civil Rights Act, whose questions like the meaning of RSVP carried cultural bias, disqualifying eight of ten Black applicants in the first half hour, with no demonstrated job-relatedness to making paper.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Fifteen Promotions to the Top
clips.summary: Sobol explains how Crown Zellerbach merged the racially labeled progression lines by pay, leaving all Black jobs at the bottom under a job-seniority system, so a Black worker could enter only the lowest white job and inch up vacancy by vacancy, the remedy of promotion by plant seniority for any qualified bidder, and the Justice Department lawyers' further idea of eliminating the line of progression entirely, over the defense that a collective bargaining agreement could not be illegal.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Job Assignments by Lottery
clips.topic: The Seniority Theory and the Article
clips.summary: Sobol gives his second seniority example, the all-white New Orleans transit system, where his case Fagan versus NOPSI before Judge Heebe won assignment of shifts by lottery, then the theory that a seniority system could itself be discriminatory by its impact, and the article he co-wrote with his Washington friend George Cooper, rearranged and published by the Harvard Law Review as one of the most cited in years, until the Supreme Court ruled the other way in the Teamsters case.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: This Traitorous Thing
clips.summary: Sobol recounts moving to Washington, briefly teaching, then later representing unions in Title VII cases, where the AFL-CIO establishment still resented him for the article they saw as betrayal, and reflects on critics who said he had polluted scholarship to advance civil rights, a charge he partly grants.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: It's All How the Judge Feels
clips.summary: He argues that civil rights outcomes turned on which judge heard the case, recalling weekends at judges' homes and July 4, 1967, at Judge Herbert W. Christenberry's French Quarter house as the judge phoned Bogalusa threatening to send marshals if jailed clients were not released, and laments today's hostile New Orleans federal bench.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: A Special Place With the Alligators
clips.topic: Leander Perez and the Battery Charge
clips.summary: Sobol opens the Gary Duncan story with Leander Perez, the racist Plaquemines Parish boss who controlled the judges and threatened to jail activists with the alligators, against whom Judge Christenberry had ordered desegregation, and recounts how nineteen-year-old Duncan, intervening as his nephews faced four white boys on a roadside, touched one on the elbow and told him to go on, was reported as having slapped him, and was charged with battery before being sent to Sobol by the Justice Department.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Practicing Law Without a License
clips.summary: Sobol describes challenging Louisiana's denial of jury trials for crimes punishable by two years or less, the conviction and sixty-day sentence before Perez's appointed judge, his exhaustion of remedies through the Louisiana Supreme Court, and his arrest by Perez and a sheriff for practicing law without a license as he left after securing a stay.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: A Hearing on the State of Law Practice
clips.summary: He tells of being bailed out by Don Juneau, then filing to enjoin his prosecution and declare bans on outside civil rights lawyers unconstitutional, a case before a three-judge court including Judge Heebe that became a weeks-long, daily-reported trial in which the government, Perez, the state, and the Louisiana State Bar were all parties, the court ultimately finding Sobol was temporarily in the state on leave from Arnold, Fortas & Porter.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: They Tried Duncan Again
clips.summary: Sobol recounts the federal court's injunction aided by the government's intervention, then winning Duncan versus Louisiana in the Supreme Court, which held the Fourteenth Amendment requires jury trials in state cases for all but the most minor crimes, and Duncan versus Perez before Judge Cassibry, which enjoined the malicious reprosecution, a trifecta capped by Duncan becoming a shrimper and parish leader.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Ballots to the Bedside
clips.topic: Discrimination in Fact in Madison Parish
clips.summary: Sobol describes voting rights work after the Voting Rights Act brought federal registrars, the case of Madison Parish Voters League head Zelma Wyche, who narrowly lost a police-chief race after the clerk of court harvested some two hundred ballots from white nursing homes, and a later case where school-board candidate Harrison Brown was disconnected from the Democratic rooster-lever line, both set aside by Judge Dawkins under his novel discrimination-in-fact doctrine and rerun, with Brown winning his.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Turn Off Those Bulldozers
clips.summary: Sobol tells how Bob Hicks spotted that planned Bogalusa public housing was placed deep in segregated neighborhoods, leading to a fair-housing suit before Judge Cassibry that halted the bulldozers, and how Hicks pursued every detail down to football players and cheerleaders in the case Hicks versus Weaver, never settling for half a loaf.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Charged With Welfare Fraud
clips.summary: Sobol recounts how he, his wife Anne, and Lolis Elie defended some twenty women prosecuted for welfare fraud in New Orleans federal court, challenging the grand jury's racial and economic composition with computer analysis of food-stamp and voter tapes showing only thirty percent of food-stamp recipients were registered against seventy-eight percent of others.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Twenty Years Before Batson
clips.summary: He describes the prosecutor striking every Black juror with peremptory challenges, and convincing Judge Alvin B. Rubin that any reason but race was permissible, yielding the decision United States versus McDaniels, a holding the Supreme Court adopted nationally two decades later in Batson versus Kentucky.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: A Schedule of Thursday and Friday
clips.summary: Sobol lays out his chronology after LCDC: to Washington and the Institute for Policy Studies, a disliked semester teaching at the University of Michigan Law School while still flying to Louisiana for ongoing cases, two years with Marian Wright Edelman at the Washington Research Project, and a 1971 return to Louisiana with Anne.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: The White Hauler Took the Profit
clips.summary: He tells of sweet-potato farmers near Opelousas who, with CORE worker John Zippert and Father Albert J. McKnight, formed a cooperative to process and sell their own crop, resented by white haulers, whose office the district attorney had police raid, leading Sobol to win a federal injunction protecting the Southern Consumers Cooperative.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: He Did Go to the Wedding
clips.summary: Sobol recounts befriending John Zippert through the cooperative case, and how, when the clerk of court refused Zippert a marriage license to wed Carol because the couple was mixed-race, he walked in with a suit to compel it just after the Supreme Court decided Loving versus Virginia, and got to attend the happy wedding.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: It Was Like a Contagion
clips.summary: Reflecting at the interview's close, Sobol observes that even the short-term volunteer lawyers and law students he once doubted went home transformed into public service work, a contagion he caught in a very big dose alongside his anti-war involvement, so that after two years he could not return to commercial antitrust work, and the two union lawyers gave him a paying client that let him sustain a civil rights practice on his own terms.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: He Died on the Petersburg Front
clips.summary: Interviewed in Culver City, California, on April 11, 2013, by David Cline with videographer John Bishop, Rick Tuttle opens with his birth in New Haven into a New England family settled there since 1638, and the family story of his grandmother's grandfather, who in the assault wave after the Massachusetts 54th was cut down at Fort Wagner near Charleston and later died on the Petersburg front, reflecting on Lincoln's refusal of a Confederate peace to abolish slavery.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Clatter of Dishes at the Table
clips.summary: Tuttle describes being the eldest grandchild raised on clashing Civil War memories, his father's Connecticut mother and his mother's Arkansas mother who told bitter stories of torture when the Union army came through, the two grandmothers unable even to agree whether to call it the Civil War or the war between the states, sometimes storming away from the dinner table, and his own father going into the Marine Corps at thirty-six.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Card Was the NAACP
clips.summary: He remembers traveling as a small boy with his pregnant mother and brothers from Jonesboro, Arkansas, north through Memphis and Cincinnati in 1944-45 on jammed wartime trains, and how, with the New York connection about to pull out across distant tracks, his father appealed to the Black sleeping-car porter, showed him a card, and the porters held the train; the card was his father's NAACP membership, his first lesson in what organization can do.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Finest Track Coach in New England
clips.summary: Tuttle describes his father, a great athlete, Marine, and high school track coach who won nine of ten Connecticut state championships and coached many young Black runners, later a principal at a predominantly African American school in the Dixwell Avenue area of New Haven, civically engaged though not electorally political, and his own move through ten schools to Plattsburgh High and Wesleyan University.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: We Gave Them One Year, Then We Fired Them
clips.summary: He recounts joining the Alpha Chi Rho fraternity at Wesleyan, where his chapter had found a clause requiring allegiance to Jesus Christ and, by report, a hidden whites-only clause, becoming house president and leading the multi-year battle to keep the chapter's property while severing from the national, giving them one year to change and, when they refused, cutting them loose, a fight that taught him the vocabulary of civil rights.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Little Rock and a Call of Conscience
clips.summary: Tuttle recalls Little Rock unfolding in the fall of 1957, his settled conviction that segregation was a bad idea, harmful and crippling for whites, and the Wesleyan religion instructor John Maguire, a Yale Divinity man from Alabama who had marched with Martin Luther King and, after the Anniston bus burning, declared a call of conscience to join the Freedom Ride.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Wesleyan Went on the Freedom Ride
