clips.summary: He describes department chair David Swift, on hearing Maguire's call of conscience, telephoning his wife to pack his bags and joining the Freedom Ride himself, and how Tuttle and his roommate, primed enough to want to go after Anniston, were not operational and missed the bus that pulled out before they could get there.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Genius of the Freedom Rides
clips.summary: Tuttle lays out the genius of the Freedom Rides as interstate travel that forced the commerce-law issue, recalls earlier 1940s efforts out of Cleveland, his graduation in 1962, and credits fraternity brothers Stephen S. Trott and Bruce Corwin, who had forced the desegregation issue by refusing to join if the clause affected anyone.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: I Came 3,000 Miles to Work With You
clips.summary: Tuttle recounts coming west to Los Angeles in 1962 for sunshine, basketball, and graduate study in history at UCLA, marching into the office hours of progressive-movement historian George Mowry, who had been close to Frank Graham at Chapel Hill, to win a place in his full seminar.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: West Coast Riders and a Familiar Face
clips.summary: He describes defending the National Student Association in the UCLA Daily Bruin, meeting Steve McNichols who became the NSA representative, a circle of West Coast Freedom Riders including Bob Singleton, Helen Bailey Singleton, and Robert Farrell, the Singletons jailed at Parchman and McNichols beaten on the Houston ride, and McNichols bringing speakers including Chuck McDew of SNCC and Malcolm X, whom Tuttle had hosted at his Wesleyan fraternity and who laughed to find him following along as a graduate student.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: All Hell Broke Loose in Birmingham
clips.summary: He recalls reading John Poppy's magazine account of Sam Block, Charles McLaurin, and Willie Wazir Peacock registering voters in the Mississippi Delta near where Emmett Till was killed, being moved by their harrowing experiences, watching the Birmingham fire hoses in May 1963 while serving as a weekend nanny for the family of Norman Raskin, and Raskin encouraging him over graham crackers and milk to go if he ever could.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: My Man, It Looks Like You're Going to Mississippi
clips.summary: Tuttle describes weighing the choice with no debt and no spouse, bumping into McNichols at the Kerckhoff Hall mailbox, who said Sam Block and Charles McLaurin would be at the campus religious center that night and could arrange a ride, the decision sealed when a young woman walked up and Steve announced Rick was going to Mississippi to help SNCC.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Some People You Need to Meet
clips.summary: He recounts the civil rights conference at the campus religious center where Block and McLaurin told their story, and McNichols taking him with graduate student Paul Albert to a Hollywood Hills party celebrating the election of Congressman George E. Brown Jr and city councilman Tom Bradley, hosted around the circle Californians for Liberal Representation.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: A Letter on Congressional Letterhead
clips.summary: Tuttle explains the plan for Congressman George E. Brown Jr to write him a letter on official letterhead saying he had been asked to observe voter registration practices in the South, meant not to sway officials but to give pause to private attackers who might hesitate if they thought him federal, and his call home where his mother begged, honey, not Mississippi, before he drove to Greenwood with Block and McLaurin.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Go Down Moses, Way Down in Freedom Land
clips.topic: Church and Rally in the Delta
clips.summary: He describes arriving in Greenwood with the organizers Sam Block and Charles McLaurin, hearing in a small rural church the Exodus story preached with powerful linkages between the Hebrew people and Black Mississippians, and a voter registration rally on a Canton baseball field in 1963 where Willie Wazir Peacock told him it was time to go as carloads of men with Confederate flags gathered at dusk.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: It Was a COFO Operation
clips.summary: Tuttle clarifies that the Greenwood work was the Council of Federated Organizations, COFO, joining SNCC, NAACP, CORE, and SCLC at the office at 708 Avenue N, and describes sleeping in the same bed as Lawrence Guyot, who one Sunday morning rose to bail out Fannie Lou Hamer and young women arrested in Winona, only to be handcuffed and beaten with baseball bats and jailed.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Bureau Was More Interested in Communists
clips.summary: He describes Willie Wazir Peacock taking charge of the office, the near-absence of friendly law enforcement in 1963 Mississippi with the local FBI more interested in communists than civil rights, and Peacock having him telephone Julian Bond and reporters Claude Sitton of the New York Times and Karl Fleming of Newsweek, then call Medgar Evers about bail money just as Evers was leaving for a meeting.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: By Randela Back With Murder
clips.summary: Tuttle recounts that Medgar Evers was murdered that night before he could call back, all hell breaking loose in Jackson and John Doar walking down the street holding up his Justice Department credentials, and the desperate fear that Hamer and the young women, including June Johnson, could not be found and might be moved around in danger as people turned up dead and missing across segregated Mississippi.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Are You Threatening Me, Son?
clips.summary: He describes being scripted by Willie Wazir Peacock to telephone the governor, identifying himself as a graduate student from California helping the civil rights effort, and warning that unless Hamer, Guyot, Annelle Ponder, June Johnson, and the others were located and released there would be major demonstrations in the Delta; the governor asked if he was threatening him, and Tuttle insisted he was only reporting.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: He Was Going to Kill the White Boy
clips.summary: Tuttle recalls the highway patrol revealing before the deadline that the prisoners were at the Winona jail where they could be bailed out, Andrew Young driving in with bail money through the valley of the shadow of death, and word that Byron De La Beckwith, the Greenwood man who had killed Medgar Evers, meant to kill the white boy, so they hid Tuttle under a blanket to fly him to the SNCC office in Atlanta.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Lester Maddox With a Pickaxe Handle
clips.summary: Flown to Memphis and on to the Atlanta SNCC office, which pushed past a settlement that had cooled lunch-counter activity, Tuttle joined a group walking to desegregate a counter, passing within clear sight of Lester Maddox gesturing in front of his restaurant, and sat in as an upset waitress stood over him holding a pot of boiling water with Black and white demonstrators seated together.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: You Folks Down to Savannah
clips.summary: He recounts the tense sit-in ending when a police officer called the waitress by name to settle down, reporters behind them including John Kifner, a Wesleyan man covering the movement for the Berkshire Eagle, and returning to the Atlanta SNCC office where Casey Hayden and Dion Diamond asked him, Bruce Gordon, and Haverford student Joe Iyer to drive to Savannah to support the Chatham County Crusade for Voters.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Spellbinding Oratory in a Packed Church
clips.summary: Tuttle recalls arriving at night to a packed Savannah church where the Chatham County Crusade for Voters, led by Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark, had mobilized major night marches for desegregation of public facilities, hotels, and employment, and the spellbinding oratory of Hosea Williams, a wounded World War II veteran and government chemist.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Put Your Things in These Baskets
clips.summary: He describes the scene before each night march, when leaders asked everyone to put weapons in baskets so nothing could discredit them, the clatter of brass knuckles and switchblades as people gave up what they normally carried for self-defense, and how the marchers stayed upstairs in a house of prostitution whose owners supported the movement pro bono and even put up bail money.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Eyes and Ears at the Meetings
clips.summary: Tuttle recounts the daring assignment Benjamin Van Clark gave him, to move to separate quarters away from the cameras of the marches and attend white supremacist public meetings on the outskirts of Savannah as eyes and ears, where police officers were introduced as friends, spending his days at a single movie and the library before slipping out to the gatherings.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: A Building Burns in Savannah
clips.summary: He describes being called back to the movement office, John Kifner arriving to put away his reporter's notebook and join the effort, and a night when he happened to be sick as a fire broke out and a building burned with the leadership being jailed, leaving Savannah's structure leaderless, until Andrew Young and James Bevel came to the office.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: They Came for John Doe, White
clips.summary: Tuttle recounts Savannah police entering the Chatham County Crusade for Voters office to arrest him, his realizing his one assignment was to keep them from noticing Andrew Young and James Bevel as Young eased away, the warrant that named not Rick Tuttle but John Doe, white, and being cuffed and led past a United Press reporter into solitary confinement, where a disembodied voice in the dark repeated that the man was not in the Klan but his brother was, on a night the Klan was marching in Savannah.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Seven Peace Bonds Apiece
clips.summary: Tuttle describes being brought to court with some forty-five civil rights people including Hosea Williams, represented by James Nabrit III and advised to give unsworn testimony, before Judge Victor Mullen, a blind segregationist of the Talmadge faction who revived stacked peace bonds, his own seven, each requiring unencumbered property no one could supply, while the complainant against him could not identify him in the courtroom.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: You Leave This Guy Alone
clips.summary: He recounts a Ronald Goldfarb lecture at Yale Law that later cited the onerous bail case, being moved into the white cell block across a wire screen from the Black male prisoners and Bruce Gordon, and staying fit with constant push-ups so the car-theft and drunk-driving inmates never touched him.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Trustees Put It on the Line
clips.summary: Tuttle tells the story of the jail trustees, the bridge boys including a welterweight boxer and longshoreman known as Felian Popeye, who warned the white prisoners during strip searches to leave him alone or be beaten, risking their easy time and a transfer to hard time at a state prison farm for a relative stranger they accepted as a civil rights worker.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: We're Going to Run a Freedom School
clips.summary: He describes how he and Bruce Gordon, refusing to behave like criminals, started a freedom school in the jail with Tuttle teaching history, Gordon theater arts, and someone else mathematics in clusters through the day, the visible effect on the guards, and his parents working to represent him to a judge who took him for someone big-time.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: He Shows Up to Bless Me
clips.summary: Tuttle recounts the allies who answered, the Episcopal campus priest Father Crowder, who had raised money for his trip and battled segregationist fraternities and was later Bishop of Kimberley expelled by apartheid, stopping on his way to a Toronto conference to give him communion in jail, and aides sent down by Congressman George E. Brown Jr.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Fifth Circuit and a Familiar Name
clips.topic: A Tangle of Bail and Courts
clips.summary: He describes sitting in custody under tough bail terms during a summer bracketed by Birmingham and the March on Washington and the church bombing that killed the children, hopes of relief from a Fifth Circuit hesitant amid Senator Eastland's threat to split it, and the presiding judge Elbert Parr Tuttle sharing his surname though no relation, while his NASA-employed father drew minor press notice.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: A Stranger in a Strange Land
clips.summary: Tuttle reflects that white volunteers were meant to do as told and, by drawing fire, draw attention to the cause, and traces how word reached New Haven, where the Anti-Defamation League's Izzy Waxler, a fellow World War II-era family man close to his father, used his contacts to reach the ADL network in Atlanta and Savannah, leading toward attorney Aaron Buchsbaum.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich
clips.summary: He describes how parallel tracks, the ADL contacts and his Harvard Law friend Stephen S. Trott, both led to Savannah attorney Aaron Buchsbaum, who agreed to represent him, and Dr. William Waxler, a former alderman and Buchsbaum's relative by marriage, who put up his property, and how Buchsbaum brought him that book just as a story moved across the Atlanta Constitution that the Crusade for Voters office was rented from the Mine, Mill, and Smelter Workers, expelled from the CIO in 1947 as communist-controlled, a story both men saw was aimed at him.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Rick Tuttle Is Not a Communist
clips.summary: He describes his father reaching the Corwin family, Victor Carter, and Sherrill Corwin, who reached California Attorney General Stanley Mosk, Mosk writing the Georgia attorney general with copy to the Atlanta Constitution to vouch that Rick Tuttle was no communist, and his mother reaching a girlfriend married to former Arkansas governor Frank Cherry, head of the federal Subversive Activities Control Board, who phoned the publisher to say knock it off, so the story died; Tuttle reflects he had brought a considerable constellation of forces to the table, perhaps part of the insight behind bringing volunteers for Freedom Summer.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The First City in the South to Desegregate
clips.summary: He describes Buchsbaum and Waxler taking the case to another Savannah judge who ordered his release on a single property bond, the original charge of seven peace bonds for disturbing the peace, his word to leave Savannah so everyone could get out, and that through a variety of techniques Savannah became, before the Civil Rights Act, the first city in the South to desegregate under moderate mayor Malcolm Maclean.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: We Could Hear the March on Washington
clips.summary: Tuttle recounts being in custody for a month and a half, the freedom schools and the March on Washington heard on the upstairs television prompting a perceptible shift even among hard-line guards toward recognition that the civil rights side might win, and reflects again that Medgar Evers in death was helpful in securing the Winona prisoners' release.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: The Networks That Built the March
clips.summary: He describes organizing crowds in Atlanta to gather as Medgar Evers' funeral train came through, his belief that the very relationships built to assemble people along that route were shortly redeployed to carpool, bus, and train people into Washington for the March, and his intention to write Mrs. Evers as the fiftieth anniversary approaches before returning to California.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Not the Man Who Tells the Stories
clips.summary: Tuttle says one of his better decisions was not to become someone endlessly telling these stories, recounts marrying Rebecca and revisiting the civil rights sites in 2008, Winona, Greenwood, Canton, and Savannah, where he saw his old jail cell, the Hosea Williams collection in Atlanta, and a Telfair Museum exhibit quoting him, and a moving return to 708 Avenue N in Greenwood, where a woman who had been around the COFO office as a teenager recognized them and the group broke into song as wary young men came back in.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: No on Proposition 14
clips.topic: Fair Housing and Friends of SNCC
clips.summary: Back in 1963 California, Tuttle describes forming a student coordinating committee at UCLA co-chaired with Bob Singleton and Russ Ellis, mobilizing over a hundred students each weekend and nine hundred on Election Day in the losing fight against Proposition 14 for fair housing, and serving as a Friends of SNCC group lobbying Congressmen Gus Hawkins, Phil Burton, and William Fitts Ryan to hold hearings in Mississippi.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Five Assassinations in Five Years
clips.summary: He traces the connection to the Berkeley Free Speech Movement and Jack Weinberg, the James Jones Board of Education campaign leading into Tom Bradley's mayoral runs, meeting his first wife in Robert Kennedy's campaign where they ran into John Lewis, the toll of five assassinations in less than five years from Medgar Evers through Robert Kennedy, and his conviction that there was a conspiracy to kill King.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Standing on the Shoulders of the Turnout
clips.topic: City Controller and the Private Clubs
clips.summary: Tuttle describes how Bradley, seeing the African American and Latino turnout achieved with United Farm Workers help in 1968, won the mayoralty on the second try, his own service as a UCLA administrator, the LA Community College Board, and four terms as Los Angeles City Controller, where he led the way to an ordinance barring discrimination at clubs like the Jonathan Club and California Club.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Nothing Like a Sworn Affidavit
clips.summary: He recounts pushing the private-club desegregation hard despite resistance, then turning as controller to the anti-apartheid fight, winning a battle with Zev Yaroslavsky and Robert Farrell to make Los Angeles divest from companies doing business in South Africa, using sworn affidavits to force firms that had lied or chosen not to know about their ties to disclose them.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: These Battles Go On
clips.summary: Tuttle mentions redlining-disclosure ordinances pursued as controller, a resolution he would carry to the California Democratic Party urging the Supreme Court not to reverse the 1965 Voting Rights Act and its federal preclearance, and his closing reflection that the voting-rights battle naturally flowed into gender equity and rights for gay, lesbian, and transgender Americans, before David Cline thanks him and the interview ends.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: The Slave Mart in Fayetteville
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in High Point, North Carolina, on October 1, 2013, Robert Brown opens with the grandmother who raised him, Nelly Marshall Brown of Anson County, and her father Joseph Kimmel Sr., a slave used to build North Carolina's railroads, noting that he himself was later appointed by Governor Jim Martin to head the North Carolina Railroad Company.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: This Was the Last Place
clips.topic: The Trip to the Slave Mart
clips.summary: Brown recounts his grandmother taking him and his brother as small boys by bus through Fayetteville to stand at the old slave mart, the last place her father was sold, and visits to her run-down clapboard childhood home in Wadesboro with its old Rosenwald school, a trip that gave him an insatiable appetite for the history of slavery and Reconstruction.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: White Water and Colored Water
clips.summary: Born in High Point in February 1935 into poverty and segregation, Brown remembers his favorite Woolworth store and the day his grandmother caught him drinking from the white fountain, telling him it was all God's water and that one day God would fix it, a day she lived to see.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: My First Major Client
clips.summary: Brown describes how in 1961 his first major client became the F.W. Woolworth Company, which he urged to get ahead of the coming change, advising its board of major financiers like John J. McCloy and Fred Kirby on integrating the company, then being sent by them to counsel other CEOs across America.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: An Escort for Mary McLeod Bethune
clips.summary: Returning to his schooling, Brown recalls Leonard Street School and William Penn High School, founded by the Quakers, and being chosen by the principal to escort visitor Mary McLeod Bethune around the school for hours as she counseled him, alongside other returning speakers like Langston Hughes.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Mrs. Loudon's Newspapers
clips.summary: Brown tells how, too poor to buy books, he scrounged magazines and newspapers and became editor of his school paper, and how a neighbor, Mrs. Loudon, who worked for a wealthy white family, began bringing him boxes of discarded New York newspapers and magazines two or three times a month, feeding a reading habit he keeps to this day.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: You Can Put the Dots Together
clips.summary: Brown explains that he still reads many newspapers daily from different parts of the country so that when he sits on corporate boards or makes presentations in New York, London, or Paris, he speaks from fact rather than theory, able to connect events across places because he built the knowledge base to do it.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Coming Around Again
clips.summary: Brown argues that race-related problems are returning to American companies because current management has no frame of reference for them, the people who lived through the earlier phase having died, retired, or gone, leaving firms unprepared to deal with issues that recur in full circle every thirty or forty years.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: The Highest Mark on the Exam
clips.summary: Brown recounts a year at Virginia Union, returning home to North Carolina A&T when his grandparents fell ill, taking a police exam tied to a sociology course, and the visit from Captain Johnson of the detectives offering the alarmed twenty-one-year-old a job after he scored highest on the examination, which he took for a couple of years.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: You Are Under Arrest
clips.summary: Brown describes the unwritten rule that black officers were not to arrest white people, and the day he chased a fleeing white man into a branch where he grew up, subdued him with his blackjack, and dragged him out to sign a warrant, an unprecedented arrest around 1956 that, to his surprise, no one challenged.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: On the Washington Street Beat
clips.summary: Brown tells of arresting a drunken white man who cursed him in a black area while other black residents watched, and how his police work led to a job as a federal agent in New York, where he worked organized-crime cases and testified in the Vito Genovese mafia case before deciding after three years to leave.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Going Back Where the Action Was
clips.summary: Brown recalls his supervisor warning that he could not run a public relations business in North Carolina, and his answer that he was going home to be in the arena where the sit-ins signaled coming change, founding his firm at the end of 1960 and naming it B and C Associates after a friend named Campbell who soon left.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: I Underwrote Them
clips.summary: Brown explains how he came to work with Dr. King and his executive vice president Wyatt Tee Walker of the SCLC, using his corporate access to pay hotel and meeting bills the movement could not cover and charging the cost off to major clients like Woolworth as business development.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Just Do the Right Thing
clips.summary: Brown describes selling reluctant executives on change for which there was no Harvard Business School equation, drawing on his grandmother's counsel to just do the right thing and to keep helping Dr. King, while making speeches on race relations and crisis handling to company heads at the Westchester Country Club and beyond.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: The Power of the Black Dollar
clips.summary: Brown tells how he hired A&T economist Sidney Evans and others to document the black consumer market, church, and schools as latent power, pressing Woolworth to add black secretaries, managers, board members, and buyers, and to back black-owned store ownership through sale-leaseback deals across the country.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: The Big Daddy of Retail
clips.summary: Brown recounts the resistance he met in both black and white communities, leveraging Woolworth's standing in the National Retail Association to push Birmingham stores toward integration, and being sent by Wyatt Tee Walker to test the Peabody Hotel in Memphis, where he became the first black man to take a suite.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: A Brilliant Tactician
clips.summary: Brown praises Wyatt Tee Walker as a brilliant, under-credited tactician of the movement, then turns to the perilous year of 1968, when he was working with Dr. King on the Poor People's Campaign before King was killed and he went with the King family to Memphis to bring back the body.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Bring Us Together
clips.summary: Brown describes the night before King's funeral hosting figures like Sidney Poitier and Robert F. Kennedy, working briefly on Kennedy's campaign with Earl Graves until Kennedy too was killed, then being recruited through Clarence Townes and Bob Finch onto Nixon's whistle-stop train across Ohio, where a girl's sign reading bring us together became the campaign theme.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: We Need Your Help
clips.summary: Brown gives his first impression of Nixon as smart, intuitive, and not the racist others tagged, and tells how he first met Dr. King through journalist Stanley Scott and Wyatt Tee Walker over lunch in Atlanta, King telling him plainly that the movement needed the best help it could get.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Bring Bob On In
clips.summary: Brown recounts resisting a White House job until Haldeman summoned him to Nixon's transition suite at the Pierre Hotel, where Nixon interrupted a meeting with Herbert Brownell and William Rogers to introduce him as a future principal assistant whom everyone would be seeing a lot of.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: You Have Total Access
clips.summary: Brown relays Nixon telling him the story of E. Frederic Morrow, the brilliant black aide left sitting by the door without responsibility, and pledging that Brown would instead have total access, which Brown says was never overruled in his four years and three months, even when an association that had funded the campaign tried to get him fired and the President backed him.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: You Handle This
clips.summary: Brown describes Nixon at the Pierre Hotel handing him the charge to run the black capitalism and minority business effort he had spoken of in the campaign, and promising him a seat at every cabinet meeting he could reach, a standing he held as one of only a handful of senior staff present.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: This Is Not the Christian Way
clips.summary: Brown tells how, after earlier trips taking black college presidents to South Africa, Coretta Scott King asked his help getting a visa she had been refused, and how he appealed to ambassador Piet Koornhof as a Christian brother over lunch to secure visas and raise money for her delegation around 1986.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: She Would Not Go Against Them
clips.summary: Brown recounts the delegation meeting Allan Boesak and other South African leaders who, over an all-night discussion, urged Coretta Scott King not to meet President Botha to ask for Mandela's release, and her decision to write Botha a letter of regret instead, which Brown was chosen to deliver to the State House.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Send Our Children to School
clips.summary: Brown describes flying with the delegation to Soweto to see Winnie Mandela, who said the thing she and Nelson wanted most was for their children to be able to study in America, a promise others had made and never kept, and how Brown personally paid for seven years of schooling at Boston University for the Mandela grandchildren.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: You Have Permission
clips.summary: Brown tells how Winnie Mandela petitioned President Botha for him to visit Nelson Mandela, whom no one had seen in over twenty years, and how a call from Niel van Heerden granted permission to see him at Pollsmoor Prison, where the chief warden met Brown warmly and led him to a room to talk as long as he wished.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: The Memo From the Gate
clips.summary: Brown recounts talking with Mandela for about two hours about what it would take to make peace, writing a memo from scratch notes as soon as he left, and passing it to Winnie Mandela and the ANC, to George Shultz, and to President Reagan through Piet Koornhof, after which real conversations with Mandela began.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Half a Box of Books
clips.summary: Brown describes visiting black townships and finding schools with almost no libraries, so he shipped boxes labeled shoes and clothes that were half-filled with school books, moving them free on South African highways, and over the years setting up more than three hundred library areas including in Zululand with the Zulu king.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: A Devout Quaker, Not a Racist
clips.summary: Brown defends Nixon as a devout Quaker who, whatever language he used off camera, was not a racist, and who genuinely wanted blacks, Hispanics, and others to get a shot in business, making the growth of minority enterprise Brown's charge to carry out.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: It Did Not Happen by Osmosis
clips.summary: Brown recounts organizing a national commission on minority enterprise chaired by GM's James Roche, who brought automakers to give dealerships to blacks where only two or three had owned them, and crediting such concrete work for Nixon winning more than twenty percent of the black vote in 1972, a share no Republican has matched since.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: The Pictures No Paper Printed
clips.summary: Brown lists the figures the administration brought in, James Farmer Jr. of CORE as an assistant secretary of HEW and Arthur Fletcher of the Philadelphia Plan among them, the first major federal money for black colleges, and his complaint that not one major newspaper printed the photographs of these meetings.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Thirteen Million to a Hundred Forty-Two
clips.summary: Brown describes revamping the EEOC under chairman William H. Brown III, working with Commerce Secretary Maurice Stans through the new Office of Minority Business Enterprise, and pushing every department to award contracts and grants to black firms and colleges, raising federal purchases from black businesses from thirteen million to a hundred forty-two million dollars.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Send It Right On Back
clips.summary: Brown tells how, with few black flag officers when Nixon took office, he and General Daniel Chappie James pressed the issue, and how the President agreed to have Brown review the monthly flag-officer list and send it back whenever no minorities appeared, a tactic that opened a path to many black generals before Brown left in 1973.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Landing the Plane Unannounced
clips.summary: Brown describes setting up a special commission to investigate race-relations problems on military bases around the world and visiting them himself unannounced, including Hawaii, where the head of CINCPAC was Admiral John S. McCain Jr., father of John McCain, with whom he became friends.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: They Won't Even Meet With Me
clips.summary: Brown recounts Dr. King summoning him to Cleveland over Sealtest dairy, which refused to meet King about hiring black truck drivers, and Brown flying to New York to tell the CEO of A&P, the largest supermarket chain, that the trouble could reach A&P's stores across many states.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: We Had Bob Brown on Our Side
clips.summary: Brown tells how the A&P chief ordered Sealtest told that its products would be pulled from every store in Ohio if it did not settle with King, after which Sealtest called King repeatedly begging to meet, and King praised Brown by name at a mass rally at Odie Hoover's Cleveland church.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: It Did Not Happen Like That
clips.summary: Brown insists that change came not just from marches but from pressure and arm-twisting behind the scenes over jobs, contracts, and opportunity, noting that milk companies once employed thousands of drivers and not one black, and warning that black colleges face closures as student loan money tightens.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: The Most Proud Spot in the Capital
clips.summary: Brown closes by thanking the interviewers and praising the new National Museum of African American History and Culture as a guidepost for ages yet unborn, recalling that tears well up when he passes its proud spot in Washington, and pledging to bring young people from Africa, where he still sends books, to see it.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: The Only Address I Ever Had
clips.summary: Interviewed by Dr. John Dittmer in Pickens, Mississippi in March 2013, Robert G. Clark Jr., the first African American elected to the Mississippi House of Representatives in the 20th century, opens in the house his father built about a hundred years earlier, on land where his great-grandparents had been enslaved and which the former slave master sold to his great-grandfather after slavery.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Come Back Home to the Place
clips.summary: Clark recounts how, dreaming of leaving for Chicago after he finished twelfth grade, he was summoned by his father and his Aunt Annie, who frightened him before asking that he come back and stay on the family place, and how, after arguing hard, he agreed, returning to the plantation where his great-grandfather had been enslaved.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Jailed With His Father in 1940
clips.summary: Clark describes his siblings leaving for California and Memphis to attend high school the county denied Black students, then turns to the 1940 case Jack Bass recounted in Melany Neilson's book: at eleven or twelve he and his brother were jailed overnight when officers suspected his father in a death found off their property, a white Lexington lawyer winning acquittal on grounds of self-defense.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Four to Five Months of School
clips.topic: A Childhood Apart From the Plantation
clips.summary: Clark explains that because the family worked its own land he never labored in a white man's cotton field, which made other Black families see him as different, and that the cotton calendar dictated a four- or five-month grammar-school year, with a three-mile walk through rain, sleet, and snow to a school at the place called Sawhill.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Catching the 2 A.M. Train to Durant
clips.summary: Clark recalls white children throwing spitballs from their buses and his answering with gravel, the two-teacher grammar school that went through eighth grade, and boarding away for high school: a year in his uncle's home at Mount Olive Vocational High, then enrolling at Holmes County Training School in Durant and stepping off the porch every Monday to catch a 2 a.m. train.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Plowing at Seven Years Old
clips.summary: Clark describes the family's cows, hogs, chickens, turkeys, geese, horses, and mules, starting a full day in the field plowing at seven when he had to reach over his head for the plow handles, a mule that trotted instead of walking, and a plow that struck a stump and knocked him flat, alongside his resolve not to farm for a living.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Confessed Christ at Three and a Half
clips.summary: Clark says his aunts farmed the 228-acre family place plus 82 acres he later bought back, and that religion was very important: he confessed Christ at three and a half, was baptized on his mother's assurance he knew what he was doing, and attended Pleasant Green, a small wooden church the congregation could not rebuild because it stood on land a white owner had merely let them use.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Just As Much As Those White Boys
clips.summary: Clark recalls his mother telling him and his brother, as they rode horses to Ebenezer, that they were just as good as the white boys but the law forbade certain things, his father's father, enslaved in Hinds County until age eleven at emancipation, who described slavery and charged the boy that young men like him would have to grow up and make a difference, the care a Black boy took never to infuriate a white person or look at a white lady, and his lifelong certainty that he was equal, if not as smart, as anybody.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Less Than Five Dollars in My Pocket
clips.summary: Clark explains he first wanted Tougaloo to study pre-law and become a lawyer like Perry W. Howard, but a work-aid student there would take eight years, so he chose Jackson College, where his father had gone, and left home with a dollar and a quarter and a dollar and fifty cents from relatives, riding the Trailways and Lynch Street buses in with under five dollars.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Bump Your Head Against the Wall
clips.summary: Clark recounts arriving with no money and being told to go home, sitting a day without food, then being sent to President Jacob L. Reddix, who said you can lead a horse to water but cannot make it drink and arranged custodial work at twenty-five cents an hour plus a garbage job in the women's dormitories to cover tuition, while Clark went out for basketball and track.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Pack Your Clothes and Get Away
clips.summary: Clark tells how he won Jackson State's first full track scholarship, then how the dean of students, pressing a finger near his nostrils, told him they would not let him use the teaching profession to become a lawyer and that if he wanted law he should leave, so he packed to go home, was never called back, and turned toward teaching since that was all an African American was allowed to do.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: I Wasn't Going to Stay Five Years
clips.summary: Clark describes Dean H.T. Sampson's new health and physical education program that required five chosen students to return a fifth summer, his refusal to stay a fifth year when Sampson would not set up a program for one student, his dropping out at the end of the fall quarter to teach at Sullivan where he had finished high school, and his later return to earn the degree.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Fired for Saying It Was Fine
clips.summary: Clark recalls the watchword segregation now and segregation forever after the 1954 Brown decision and how anyone who endorsed it was released, illustrated by his seven years teaching at Louise, Mississippi, where, after he told Superintendent Brown that white and black children going to school together was fine, the superintendent walked out and word later came that Clark was no longer needed.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Glad It Did Not Happen to Me
clips.summary: Clark places the 1955 murder of Emmett Till at the time of a Southwestern Athletic Conference fall workshop, says he was not surprised given what happened to people around Louise, and recounts three men, a quiet parent, a teacher whose wife also taught, and a non-pastor preacher, urging him to stay even after the superintendent did not want him, advice he felt God led him to take.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Shot for Asking People to Register
clips.topic: The Killing of a Belzoni Man
clips.summary: Clark tells of teaching at the Humphreys County Training School in the Delta around 1956 and 1957 and a barbershop in Belzoni where a man took the chair after him, then was shot and killed about two hours later for asking people to register and vote, with money said to have been given to send away those who knew something, as the Citizens' Council formed against the Brown decision.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: The Black Rabbit and the White Rabbit
clips.topic: The Sovereignty Commission and a Father Fired
clips.summary: Clark describes the Citizens' Council and the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission keeping files and circulating teachers' names to superintendents, a report claiming Humphreys County had gotten rid of all the troublemakers, his own father fired for teaching voter-registration classes and then unable to find work in Mississippi, and a principal at Louise who tried to make Clark pull a library book showing a black rabbit and a white rabbit.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: To See If I Measured Up
clips.topic: Michigan State and the End of Fear
clips.summary: Clark explains that, taught all his life that ability is not given by race, he applied to Michigan State, Notre Dame, and the University of California to test whether he was equal, choosing Michigan State because California was too far and Notre Dame taught too much religion, since Mississippi offered no place for a Black person to earn a master's, and tells how, the only African American in a class of thirty-five, he answered a question two white classmates had gotten wrong, the professor's confirmation curing him of fear so that being one of twenty-one quiet members never daunted him.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Sending Up the Mileston Fourteen
clips.summary: Clark recounts coaching at Lexington from 1961 to 1966 as the movement got underway, remembering being sent uptown the day the Mileston group of fourteen tried to register, his father among the last Black names struck from the books, and how, suspected by some Blacks of spying for white people, he entered the movement through the white couple Henry and Sue Lorenzi, who came to Holmes County in the fall of 1964.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Don't Bring No Crowd to the Board
clips.topic: Freedom Summer and an Adult School Denied
clips.summary: Clark remembers students joining the movement, one football player punched in the side, the 1964 Freedom Summer that brought about thirty Northern volunteers to Holmes County, and his push for a state-authorized adult education program: told by his superintendent not to bring a crowd, he brought teachers Laplace Polk and Curtis Lodge and warned the board he would run for superintendent, only to have his contract dropped and two legislators pass a local-and-private act, since ruled unconstitutional, letting a county where Highway 12 and Highway 17 intersect appoint its superintendent, which steered him toward a race for the legislature.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: You Don't Know How Cruel It Was
clips.summary: Clark describes Holmes County schools being forced to desegregate in 1965 with only Black students moving into white schools and paying a heavy price, a young woman who later told him at Saints that she would never be the same, and a Durant girl in a rural area whom a superintendent put out of school until Clark and others gave him enough trouble that he did not do it again.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Three Hundred Adults, Six Days a Week
clips.summary: Clark recounts that after being fired he went to Dr. Arenia C. Mallory at Saints Junior College in Lexington, where she ran 300 adults six days a week, made him coordinator and then director within three months with classes in Pickens, West, and Tchula, and how those adult schools later taught J.P. Love to read and write, undercutting Love's claim that Clark had forged signatures.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: The First Integrated Meeting in the County
clips.summary: Clark describes serving briefly in 1966 on the Lyndon Johnson Great Society community action board, the county's first integrated meeting at the courthouse with Blacks and whites around the table joining Central Mississippi Inc., and the hundred gallons of red Kool-Aid he made because Black folk had never before been able to drink red Kool-Aid there.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Head Start Ought to Be Pre-K
clips.topic: The Cause and the CAP Program
clips.summary: Clark, unhappy with the board's direction, insists the cause is more important than any individual and drafts a letter from the Freedom Democratic Party of Holmes County telling elected officials to do their job or resign, then traces the CAP program from a social service into a jobs program and his work with Rust College on night school for Head Start teachers, arguing Head Start should be a pre-K program so children do not start school behind.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Let Them Know I Am One of You
clips.summary: Clark explains he volunteered to run after the superintendent office was made appointive, and credits his win to presenting himself as one of the people rather than a big shot, to the strong Holmes County civil rights movement, and above all to his contact as director with the 300-plus adults at the migrant-farmer school, whom he let in late after a long day and who helped put him over.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: When Durant Came In
clips.topic: An Independent Wins a Contested Seat
clips.summary: Clark recounts running as an independent in 1967 because Black candidates could not be Democrats and reasoning the people might not return a second time, facing J.P. Love after Love beat Wilbur Hooks in a redistricted single seat, the contest that ran all the way with Marian Wright as his lead lawyer, the unofficial watchers of God he posted at every voting place so Black voters would not be intimidated, and the count when he trailed until the Durant votes, organized by an ally he calls Jumbo Brouche, put him over.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: One Black Among a Hundred Twenty-One
clips.summary: Clark recalls Marian Wright crossing the Capitol's south side to tell him the House had decided to seat him, says the experience of being one Black member among 121 white ones did not intimidate him because he knew education better than anyone there, and explains the seating rules under which his delegation's senior member, then the speaker, chose where he would sit, leaving him eight years with an empty seat beside him.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: You're Doing What They Want You to Do
clips.summary: Clark describes being denied the floor as the speaker recognized others to cut him out, walking out one night with his desk cleared until Bill Minor said he was doing exactly what they wanted, at which Clark dropped his hand and returned to a hooraying chamber that fell silent, and how the preparation habits he built at Michigan State let him master coming legislation by burning the midnight hour.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Point to My Nostrils to Vote No
clips.topic: Earning a Vote After Eight Years
clips.summary: Clark recounts that after about eight years a group of Black legislators was elected from Jackson and the older white gentlemen who had opposed seating him began looking to him at vote time, so he signaled how to vote with a finger up for aye or a point to his nostrils for no, never directing them wrong even when fellow Black folk called him an Uncle Tom, and a white seatmate who feared Clark was trying to get him defeated.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: A White Man on His Knee
clips.summary: Clark tells how, tired of following so-called liberal friends who got good appointments while he got nothing, he signed the petition that gave C.B. Buddie Newman the majority he needed for Speaker Pro Tem when Speaker John R. Junkin fell sick, and how Newman dropped to a knee and kissed the pen, marveling that a white man knelt, to which Clark answered he would do much more for Black folk in the future.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Potential Negro Chairman Gives Black Power Speech
clips.topic: A Keynote and a Vice Chairmanship
clips.summary: Clark recounts Newman making him vice chairman of education after Junkin died, his NAACP convention keynote in Vicksburg, in Newman's district, telling Black folk to get up and do for themselves, the bold headline calling it a Black Power speech, and Newman summoning him to show three boxes of papers yet promising to approach him when an education chairmanship came vacant, which changed Clark's attitude toward him.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Get My Bills Out First
clips.topic: Building the Reform Bill With Governor Winter
clips.summary: Clark, now chairman, describes a white Lexington lawyer tipping him to what was coming on the floor, his long push for progressive education dating to a bill killed the day he went to Dr. King's funeral in Atlanta, and how after William Winter became governor they assembled the education reform bill, which Clark got out of committee in the first week even as the Rules Committee repeatedly kicked it back to the heel of the calendar.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: There Ain't Supposed to Be No Boys
clips.topic: Forcing the Bill to the Floor
clips.summary: Clark recounts telling the speaker he would call the bill to the top of the calendar, the speaker warning some of these boys could not stand the heat and refusing to recognize him, his answer that any boys should go home, his vice chairman vowing to make the same motion, the House erupting until it had to adjourn, and the anonymous letters threatening colleagues' wives and families if they voted for the bill.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Take It to the People
clips.summary: Clark describes telling Governor Winter he could not keep putting his members through the bloodbath without support and urging him to take it to old-line organizations like the Mississippi Southern Baptists and the Farm Bureau, the governor's Greenville meeting drawing crowds Clark could not get within three blocks of, and Winter summoning six legislators to the mansion to announce he would set a date for a press conference on a special session.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: I Put Myself Into a Jail
clips.summary: Clark tells the speaker and lieutenant governor that if not removed he will get a bill out and passed once Winter calls the session, then chairs the conference committee where Ellis Bodron's blind son maneuvers him, starting with Clark, into conceding a half-percent tax after he vowed to accept none, leaving the bill tied up three weeks as opponents, including a Grenada member the speaker added, tried to kill it.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Have the Senator Make My Speech
clips.topic: Saving the Bill and What It Did
clips.summary: Clark recounts Newman warning that long meetings tend to kill legislation, his stratagem of quietly arming a senator to argue his no-tax position back to him because he believed the money already existed and taxes were a way to kill the bill, its passage as the Education Reform Act restoring compulsory school attendance repealed since around the Brown decision and adding early childhood kindergarten, and an earlier committee tactic of hiding members in the cloakroom, closet, and bathroom to break opponents' walkout and force the bill to the top of the calendar.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Fight for the Farmers
clips.topic: The First Run Against Webb Franklin
clips.summary: Clark explains he ran for Congress to fight for farmers without the mentality that nothing helpful could come to the Delta, notes the long power of Delta planters and his friendships with figures like John Stennis and James Eastland in a time when important Democrats backed him, then describes his well-funded opponent Webb Franklin, the low turnout in Holmes County because he would not embrace the indicted Tchula mayor Eddie Carthan and instead let the courts operate, and Franklin's slogan he's one of us, which Clark calls racism played to white people.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: I'm Running Anyway
clips.summary: Clark recounts declining to run for Speaker Pro Tem the first time because some African Americans would not back him and he did not want grandchildren asked why they refused a Black man, then running the second time and, with 17 of 35 African American votes, winning 87 percent of the whole Mississippi House, members who saw he was Black but voted for him as a person, and his refusal to spend money to spin a budget.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: If the Delta Farmers Had Supported Me
clips.topic: The Second Congressional Race and Redistricting
clips.summary: Clark says his second run against Franklin was much the same though better organized, that he would have won, especially the second time, had Delta farmers backed him, that he was identified with the civil rights movement while the Black candidate who eventually won was not, and that the district was gerrymandered against Black voters until later made overwhelmingly Black, which made it harder for Democrats to win another seat.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: My Education Was Important
clips.topic: Family and the Boys He Raised
clips.summary: Clark, elected Speaker Pro Tem in 1992 among a growing number of Black representatives, turns to family: married in 1971 around age 38, he helped raise and educate his sister's children, including a nephew who became a young cardiologist in California after the University of Michigan, and taught his own two sons, both now lawyers, that they were somebody and that education mattered.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Government by the Party, Not for the People
clips.summary: Asked about problems and changes, Clark names political parties as Mississippi's greatest hindrance, governing by party rather than for the people as in Washington, calls the freedom to go, eat, and stay anywhere the greatest change since the civil rights movement, and warns that without action history may record that for lack of leadership the movement's good works died, work he says he is still doing.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Not Named for Robert E. Lee
clips.summary: Robert L. Carter opens by saying his name, Robert Lee Carter, born in Caryville, Florida, and reflects on whether he was named after Robert E. Lee, since Southern generals were considered heroic by some Black people of that period.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Free Sixty Years After the War
clips.summary: Carter describes being born in 1917 in Caryville, his family of free Black people who left the deep South in the Great Migration about sixty years after the Civil War, and his father, a migrant agricultural worker sent north to work in a factory in Newark.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Washing Clothes for White People
clips.summary: Carter recounts his father dying when he was a year old, his mother having too little schooling for any work but washing white people's clothes, the family living in poverty until he was twelve, and his older sister marrying a man who owned a bar in Linden, New Jersey, which lifted the family's living conditions.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Bathroom of My Own
clips.summary: He describes his sister's marriage opening the door to mainstream prerogatives, having an indoor toilet for the first time after years of outhouses, and his reflections on how the poor live their lives on the street while middle-class people do the same behind doors.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Library Close By
clips.summary: Carter explains that, not skillful with his hands, he saw a good education as his only way into the mainstream, was considered smart and skipped grades, and spent much of his time reading novels and history in a nearby public library, though he calls his reading undisciplined.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Gods After My Sisters
clips.summary: He recalls the segregated poverty of Newark, his sisters at age sixteen amid the migration from the South, and his mother's desperation to get the family out, fearing the men on the street would get her daughters pregnant, a worry she did not extend to him.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Stay With Your Own Kind
clips.summary: Carter remembers the sixth grade, a white girl and a Black girl who were close friends, and a teacher who told the white girl to stay with her own kind, and contrasts Newark teachers who let things play out with East Orange teachers who actively intervened to impose segregation.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Pool Cleaned After Us
clips.summary: He describes the gym-class swimming pool that Black students could use only on separate days before it was drained and cleaned, and how, learning of a Supreme Court ruling that school facilities had to be open to all, he decided he would use the pool.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: They Closed It Rather Than Open It
clips.summary: Carter recounts going to the pool alone every gym day though he could not swim and was terrified of the water, being threatened by teachers, and the school responding not by opening the pool to everyone but by closing it entirely, costing it a championship swim team and teaching him the price whites paid for segregation.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Good Test Taker at Lincoln
clips.summary: He tells of enrolling at Lincoln University at sixteen, being considered brilliant and a very good test taker, and reflects that learning to take tests is a gift that does not necessarily mean intellectual gifts, while calling Lincoln a wonderful, isolated experience.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Predominantly Black Campus
clips.summary: Carter describes Lincoln as one of the great times of his life for the friends he made on an isolated, predominantly Black campus, an all-male school whose professors were retired from Princeton, and the rowdy student behavior that earlier generations had been allowed to indulge in.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Debating the Small White Colleges
clips.summary: He recalls his generation agitating for Black faculty when only one professor was Black, and joining the debating team that competed against small white colleges like Haverford, which gave him the courage and understanding that he could compete with white people.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: You Did Not Need a Big Voice
clips.summary: Carter explains going to Howard University School of Law on scholarship, his early fear that a good lawyer needed a big voice, and learning from his idol, Dean William Hastie, that what mattered was knowing all the facts and being conversant with the law rather than oratory.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Program to Fight Segregation
clips.summary: He describes Charles Hamilton Houston's program to fight the separate-but-equal doctrine of Plessy v. Ferguson by forcing states to provide duplicate courses for Black students, leading graduate schools to begin accepting Black students, and his own first Supreme Court case, McLaurin, where a student was made to sit at a separate seat and table.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Day Before the Argument
clips.topic: Watching Houston in the Gaines Case
clips.summary: Carter recalls Houston, while Carter was a student, trying the Gaines case, Missouri ex rel. Gaines, in which a Black applicant sought equal educational opportunity where the state had no law school for Black students, and notes a tradition of arguing each case at the law school the day before the Supreme Court.
related_events: Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Tradition of Moot Court
clips.summary: He explains the tradition by which every Supreme Court argument brought under NAACP and Legal Defense Fund auspices was first argued at Howard's law school the day before, a justice once echoing a question a student had posed, and reflects on his own nervousness watching Houston argue.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: He Turned His Back on Houston
clips.summary: Carter recounts the story that Justice James Clark McReynolds turned his back throughout Houston's argument in the Gaines case, and the barber's tale that a Black barber told McReynolds that Houston was qualified to sit on the Supreme Court and would represent all the people, and remembers Houston as a formidable man who left the NAACP over money worries while Hastie remained his icon.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: I Just Went to the Concert
clips.summary: He tells how, working as a busboy and waiter to support his Howard scholarship, he was refused the day off for Marian Anderson's Lincoln Memorial concert, arranged by Mrs. Roosevelt after the Daughters of the American Revolution barred her from Constitution Hall, and went anyway, weeping at the historic performance.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Fired and Then a Full Scholarship
clips.summary: Carter recalls returning to a chaotic dining room and being fired as expected, then receiving a full scholarship to work part-time in the library, and describes the integrated crowd in tears at the Lincoln Memorial, where Marian Anderson, the first Black woman to sing there, seemed to be making history.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A First Amendment Thesis
clips.summary: He describes leaving Howard for Columbia on a Rosenwald Fellowship, working toward a doctorate on a thesis that the First Amendment right to strike was essential to the democratic process, and weighing whether to write part of his dissertation or take a full load of courses for his master's.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Court Follows the Vote
clips.summary: Carter recounts choosing the technician Noel Dowling as his advisor, a man who thought Supreme Court differences turned on small legal changes rather than the social scene, and his own belief that the Court's changes followed the vote and reflected political change, while learning that arguing fine distinctions let the Court rule without admitting it was being political.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: I Did Not Resist the Draft
clips.summary: Carter explains that, feeling he had exhausted the avenues of fighting segregation and worried about his career, he did not resist the draft in the summer of 1941, and was already in the Army when Pearl Harbor brought the United States into World War II.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Clearing Bush in Augusta
clips.topic: Black Recruits and a Staff Sergeant
clips.summary: He describes the NAACP's fight for Black men to enter the Army as more than servants, the long-serving Black staff sergeants who were the only ones with standing, and his own assignment clearing trees in Augusta, Georgia, in the Army Air Corps under an ignorant sergeant.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: His Personal Lawyer
clips.summary: Carter recalls becoming the legal advisor to the staff sergeant in charge of him, handling the man's domestic problems to get out of the work detail, and frames the Army experience, with its devastating racism, as what set him on the course of a civil rights lawyer.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Officer Candidate School
clips.summary: He describes the agitation that opened officer candidate school to Black recruits, his becoming a member of the quartermaster corps in the still-segregated Army, and reacting to his own written line that the Army experience made a militant of him, since enlisted whites had no respect for Black officers.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Accused of Inciting a Riot
clips.summary: Carter recounts becoming an officer, a second lieutenant denied respect, bringing actions against several who would not treat him properly, and being accused by the base officer of trying to incite a riot by himself, while refusing the offer to live off the base.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: When You Learn Some Manners
clips.topic: The Lieutenant Colonel and the Hanger
clips.summary: He tells of a lieutenant colonel who barged into his quarters looking for a coat hanger, called him a racial slur on seeing he was Black, invoked his many grades of seniority, and was told by Carter that until he learned some manners and apologized he would get no respect, and to get the hell out of his room.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: I Was Not Trying to Revolutionize Things
clips.summary: Carter recounts bringing charges against a couple of white officers who showed him no respect, the base commander pressing him over the trouble, his insistence that as an officer he was entitled to respect or he would take the fight to Washington, and his prompt transfer.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Bartender Looked as Frightened as I Felt
clips.topic: A Drink at the Officers' Club
clips.summary: He describes going to the bar at the officers' club where a Black bartender, looking as frightened as Carter felt, hesitated to serve him, finally poured the drink, could not take his cash, and a white officer put it on his bill, an experience so devastating that Carter never tried it again.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Vicious and Virulent
clips.summary: Carter reflects that before the Army his friendships had led him to think discrimination was not really an issue and that hard work could beat the system, until the vicious, virulent hatred against Black soldiers opened his eyes that segregation had to be fought and that education did not spare a person from it.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Army That Changed the Most
clips.summary: He recalls coming out of the Army determined to devote his life to fighting segregation, and marvels at the irony that the Army and Air Force became the institutions in the country most accepting of diversity and equal opportunity.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: I Had Never Heard of Thurgood Marshall
clips.topic: A Country Bumpkin From East Orange
clips.summary: Carter describes coming out of the Army in 1944 as a country bumpkin from East Orange who had never heard of Thurgood Marshall, William Hastie, Roy Wilkins, or the NAACP tradition, having gotten into trouble in the Army for refusing to accept segregation.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Taken On to Be a Snitch
clips.summary: He recounts the Army taking him on as a suspected snitch meant to report troublemakers, the officers' amusement turning to labeling him a troublemaker himself when they found he took a liberal newspaper, and his reconnecting after discharge with William Hastie, then dean of Howard Law, who with Marshall recruited him.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Drafted Again
clips.topic: Settling With the Army and Joining the Staff
clips.summary: Carter tells of being drafted again, his trouble with the Army, following Hastie's advice to seek to rejoin in some capacity only to be turned down by a general, his reflection that he could have been a better soldier with some freedom of action, and his resolve to spend his life fighting segregation at the NAACP.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Cadres to Their Hometowns
clips.summary: He describes the NAACP program at Howard designing cases to desegregate state institutions, training cadres of Black lawyers to return to their hometowns and bring litigation, and Houston's strategy of forcing states either to set up duplicate programs and accept Black students or face suits over separate-but-equal facilities.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Couple of Rooms to Myself
clips.summary: Carter recalls 1944 and his first contact with Thurgood Marshall, being given a couple of small offices because of his education while the rest of the staff worked in a big room, and a moment when the staff had to prepare a briefing on a separate-but-equal case while Marshall was out at lunch.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Brief Was Done When He Got Back
clips.summary: He tells how, reluctant but told by Marshall's secretary to do the briefing during the long lunch, he had the brief done by the time Marshall returned, who looked it over and sent it out, and from then on Carter became the one writing the briefs behind the scenes, long without credit.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The First Black Woman Lawyer They Had Seen
clips.summary: Carter describes traveling the South to try cases, the teacher-salary case with Constance Baker Motley, the first Black woman lawyer many in Mississippi had seen, and the people's ecstatic reaction to watching them put a white superintendent on the hot seat under cross-examination.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Reenacted in the Barbershops
clips.summary: He recounts how, because federal courtrooms were not segregated, Black spectators packed them to watch, vicariously living through the spectacle of white superintendents made to answer questions, and reenacting the courtroom drama afterward in barbershops and beauty parlors across town.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Hotels for Black Friends
clips.summary: Carter reflects on the courtroom theater fading over time, and describes traveling the segregated South as a young lawyer with poor or no hotel accommodations, arguing the figure case among the first of the Brown cases with Jack Greenberg, and eventually staying with Black friends to find decent lodging.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Rope Around Him
clips.summary: He describes McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents, his first Supreme Court case, in which McLaurin had the same books and professors but was made to sit in a special seat walled off from his classmates, and the argument that segregation denied him the intellectual intercourse essential to equal education.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Good Publicist for the Cause
clips.summary: Carter recounts the doubt among supporters that the McLaurin differences were too insubstantial to win, and how Thurgood Marshall, a shrewd publicist, brought such cases and made public statements to deny segregation's supporters their day, proving correct even as critics predicted defeat in the next case.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Attacking Plessy Directly
clips.summary: He explains the June 1950 shift to taking only cases attacking segregation per se as unconstitutional rather than concentrating on unequal facilities, since a state could equalize facilities and the side might still win, while keeping unequal facilities as a fallback argument.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Make the Cases Too Expensive
clips.topic: From Graduate Schools to Grade Schools
clips.summary: Carter describes Houston's thesis of making segregation cases so expensive that states would admit Black students at the graduate and college level, where the schools were clearly distinct, and the new difficulty at the grade-school level in Brown, where schools were presumably alike, requiring the argument that segregation per se impaired Black children's ability to learn.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Longer They Stayed, the Higher the Scores
clips.summary: He recounts preparing the Clarendon County case and learning that Otto Klineberg, a social scientist at Columbia, had a study showing that Black children who migrated from segregated Southern schools to integrated Philadelphia scored higher the longer they stayed in that system, exactly the evidence the lawyers needed.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Good Doll, the Bad Doll
clips.topic: Kenneth and Mamie Clark's Doll Test
clips.summary: Carter tells of Klineberg referring him to Kenneth and Mamie Clark, whose Northside Center for Child Development tested segregation's impact, and their doll test showing children a Black doll and a white doll, the bad doll always identified as Black while the child being interviewed identified with the white one.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Running Around With My Dolls
clips.topic: The Doll Test in Clarendon County
clips.summary: He describes the theory that segregation impaired Black children's ability to learn and gave them a sense of inferiority, recalls the Clarendon County case as the only one where the doll test was used in presentation, and the comic moment of Spottswood Robinson watching him run around a South Carolina station with the dolls before understanding their purpose.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Stay Within Your Field
clips.summary: Carter recounts Kenneth Clark helping recruit a roster of professional expert witnesses, and his own insistence that they testify only to what they could support and never stray outside their field, since the lawyers' job in cross-examination was to become an expert for that moment and punish any witness who overreached.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Make Them Pay for It
clips.summary: He explains that once the academics relaxed and testified only within their expertise they made good witnesses, while experts on the other side who strayed beyond their level could, with Kenneth Clark's help in evaluation, be made to pay for it under cross-examination.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: It Was Not Hard Science
clips.summary: Carter describes assembling social scientists to sign a brief showing segregation was harmful, the doubts of committee members who distrusted it as not hard science, Marshall allowing him to take the risk because they had nothing else, and William T. Coleman Jr. coming to agree the social science was excellent.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: It Had Never Been Argued
clips.topic: The Argument and Opinion in Brown
clips.summary: He recounts the consolidated cases coming out of Topeka, Kansas, the argument that equal educational opportunity had never actually been argued in an education case though courts had assumed it, and that the chief justice bought the argument and wrote the opinion overturning separate but equal as unconstitutional.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: All Deliberate Speed
clips.summary: Carter explains expecting the Court to rule unanimously, that the implementation would come with all deliberate speed, and how Topeka, ready to concede and wanting out, led the Court to call the test moot, so it took ten or twenty years before Topeka again reached the point of being willing to end segregation.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Massive Resistance
clips.summary: He describes all deliberate speed running out only after ten or twenty years of further fighting, the massive resistance across the South after Brown, the absence of meaningful school desegregation until after 1968 and into 1970-71, and reflects on the significance of the decision beyond schools.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Voluntary Segregation Now
clips.summary: Carter frames Brown's significance as the holding that enforced segregation by law is no longer viable in the country, contrasts it with the voluntary segregation people still accept without a public urge to end it, and expresses hope that a public push will someday make that voluntary segregation no longer viable.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: They Went After Us
clips.summary: He describes the backlash after Brown when Southern states, who had not bothered with the NAACP before, went after it on the theory that outlawing the association would end the problem, and Carter spending a grueling stretch fighting in various states to keep the NAACP alive.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: The Right to Seek Opportunity
clips.topic: NAACP v. Alabama and NAACP v. Button
clips.summary: Carter recounts arguing that outlawing the association would only push the fight under another name, and the Supreme Court cases NAACP v. Alabama and NAACP v. Button, which upheld the NAACP's right to seek educational opportunity free of state coercion, important for all civil rights organizations.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Reduced to No Counsel
clips.topic: Becoming General Counsel and the Greenberg Conflict
clips.summary: He describes becoming General Counsel of the NAACP in 1955 and turning attention to school segregation in the North, and his conflict with Jack Greenberg, who on Thurgood Marshall's departure wanted to run the Legal Defense Fund and reduce Carter to a counsel with no right to bring segregation cases.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: I Was Not Going to Turn Them Over
clips.summary: Carter recounts his bitterness that Greenberg, after Marshall went to the Second Circuit Court of Appeals, tried to get Roy Wilkins to bar Carter from bringing segregation cases and send them all to the Legal Defense Fund, and how, holding all the cases in his own hands, he refused to turn them over and Wilkins would not risk the public fight.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A Black Man Resigning a Good Job
clips.summary: Carter tells how the NAACP fired staff member Lewis Steel without consulting him over an article, the board of nine men and one Black member meeting the day it appeared, an angry Thurgood Marshall then on the court, and Carter and his whole staff threatening to resign if Steel were not reinstated, a stand he calls a hero's irony for a Black man quitting a good job over a white man's mistreatment.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: A New Lease on Life
clips.topic: Revitalization and the Path to a Judgeship
clips.summary: Carter describes the resignation giving him a new lease on life when he had felt he had exhausted the avenues of school segregation, probably helping him toward a judgeship, and the earlier Kennedy-era story of Thurgood Marshall being offered only a district seat while Carter was sought instead.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Asked to Pay for the Judgeship
