clips.summary: He recounts Adam Clayton Powell Jr. indicating he had to pay a certain amount of money for a judgeship, telling his advisors which caused an uproar, his people backing down over the politics, and his claim that the fuss they raised got Thurgood Marshall his Court of Appeals appointment while Carter thought his own chance had ended, only to come to pass years later.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Segregation Was Nationwide
clips.summary: Carter reflects on his pioneering fight in the North through the Ocean Hill-Brownsville and Intermediate School 201 cases, framing segregation as a nationwide phenomenon of keeping Black and white separate rather than a North-South issue, and his hope that a public push will someday confront voluntary school segregation.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: We Were All Here That Night
clips.summary: He discusses the significance of Obama's election, the unexpected passage of one of Obama's education bills, watching the election night, and his own insistence that his goal was never a Black president but equal educational opportunity for all, for the poor and for Black people, as the key to equality.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Younger People Ought to Come After Me
clips.summary: Carter reflects that his elitist-sounding view of equal education may be right, that he is too old to pursue it and does not want to stay on the stage past his usefulness, trusting younger people to take it over, and admits to feeling mentally fatigued and doing only a little writing now.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: His Name Was George
clips.summary: In a recorded pickup, Carter states that his client George W. McLaurin applied to the University of Oklahoma graduate school and was given only a separate seat in the lunchroom and classroom while taught by the same faculty in the same class, the epitome of separate-but-equal, closing the Library of Congress and Smithsonian presentation.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Born at Phoebe Putney
clips.summary: Interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany, Georgia, on March 9, 2013, Robert McClary recalls being born on October 19th at Phoebe Putney Hospital, his mother dying when he was young, and his father, who worked a job on the river, moving what was left of the family.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Learning to Walk at Seven
clips.summary: McClary describes the family falling into poverty after his mother's death, being raised with a sister and brother by his father, learning to walk at about seven years old, the trouble over whether he would go to school, and how the men were paid every two weeks for their work.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: He Knocked the White Man Down
clips.summary: He recounts his father, who had come up from South Carolina, being insulted by a white man and knocking him down in a confrontation, and remembers attending a two-room school with a football field he had to cross.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: A New School and the Albany Movement
clips.summary: McClary describes a high school newly built for Black families, the first families chosen to begin integrating, an incident involving a young white boy and a Black girl, and the early stirring of what became known as the Albany Movement before he himself got involved.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Charles Sherrod and Nine Counties
clips.summary: He tells of writing for the movement paper and meeting Charles Sherrod, the plan to organize roughly nine counties across Southwest Georgia and take over a county to elect local people, and joining the effort, before the interview pauses for a technical delay.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Tenants and Welfare
clips.summary: After the recording resumes, McClary describes the everyday work he did with Charles Sherrod: helping tenants and people who were denied welfare, filing appeals when benefits were taken away, and the white residents who had largely left the area.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: The Casino Cafe Closed Its Doors
clips.summary: He recounts an attempt to desegregate a cafe in his hometown, remembered as a casino, where Black customers were directed to a back window, an activist who had come down from New York joined the effort, and the owner closed the place rather than serve them at the front door.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Attacked in the Office at Night
clips.topic: A Night Raid and Its Aftermath
clips.summary: McClary describes being attacked while sleeping at the movement office, a man forcing his way in during the night, his escape and the police taking the man away that same night, and afterward learning skills like typing as part of the project.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Self-Defense and the Board
clips.summary: Working for the Southwest Georgia project, McClary reflects on self-defense as something he would not retire from, and recounts a confrontation tied to a local board in a small town.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: More Hopeful Than Afraid
clips.topic: A First Black Official and His Father
clips.summary: He recalls helping people meet challenges and the town electing its first Black official, says he was more hopeful and excited than afraid, points to his father as a man who would not back down to white intimidation, and the interview closes with thanks.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: My Grandfather Was a Slave
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in San Diego on June 29, 2016, Roberta Alexander, born December 1946 in Berkeley, opens on the family that shaped her, her brother, and her sister, including a grandfather born in 1842 who had been a slave, rode the Pony Express on the promise to tell white people nothing of the Indians, and spoke Winnebago, Omaha, and Sioux while living between the Omaha Reservation and Sioux City, Iowa.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Riding the Rails to the Harvest
clips.topic: The Grandfather and Her Father's Boyhood
clips.summary: Alexander describes her grandfather as an itinerant Jew's harp musician, bootlegger, and cattle wrangler, rarely home yet father to ten children with her Scot Irish grandmother, and her father, born 1914, one of ten siblings, three sisters dead of the flu pandemic, tuberculosis, and a fire, a homeless boy of many jobs who at twelve hopped a freight from the reservation to seek harvest work in Minnesota.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Fifty Cents a Day
clips.summary: Alexander tells how her father, twelve and hungry, walked five miles to a Minnesota farm, did the chores, and was paid fifty cents a day, until a Scandinavian bunkhouse worker who first called him a coon and a shine demanded the farmer pay him a dollar a day like the white men or face a strike.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: A Little Red Card
clips.summary: She describes the strike threat that won her father a dollar a day, the multiracial Sioux City packing-house strikes and Klan terror against strikers he had witnessed, and how at the harvest's end the worker enrolled him in the Industrial Workers of the World for his thirty dollars, leaving the young Black boy a member's red card.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Call Me by My Name
clips.summary: Alexander reflects that white men standing up for her father's rights was formative for a boy raised among Native Americans and African Americans who knew few white people, recalls him asking the men to stop calling him shine and coon, and notes she learned the union details only through an oral history they recorded with him in 1975.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: She Threw the Ink Well
clips.summary: She recounts her father's grandmother as a fighter who, when a substitute teacher struck the children with a ruler, marched to school and threw an ink well at the woman, and who pulled her shoeless children out of school when they were denied entry, ending her father's education at the fifth grade.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: From Vilna, Anti-Czar and Jewish
clips.summary: Alexander traces her mother's family from Vilna in Lithuania, revolutionary anti-czar Jews who emigrated after the 1905 revolution, her bitter grandfather a St. Louis shoe-repair man who disowned her mother for marrying a Black man, and her grandmother, an illiterate garment worker who left him rather than disown her daughter, culturally Jewish, Yiddish-speaking, and progressive.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: A Shoe Shine and a Manifesto
clips.summary: She tells how her parents met at a Young Communist League party in Los Angeles, and how a young woman left her shoe-shine father a copy of the Communist Manifesto he could not yet read, his boss and a banker both dismissing it, until a welcoming, racially mixed party of people amazed by his background drew him into the movement and a real packing-house job at sixteen.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Hold the Line
clips.topic: Packing-House Strike and the Civil Rights Congress
clips.summary: Alexander describes her father's account of brutal Sioux City and Omaha packing-house labor, ten-to-thirteen-hour days amid blood and manure as the line sped from 250 to 350 animals, the wildcat strike he helped call, for which he was fired and blacklisted before coming to California, his organizing up and down the Central Valley for the CIO and the ILWU, and his directing the Civil Rights Congress in Oakland on police brutality and housing, holding that communists organized for basic people's rights rather than armed revolution, her parents leaving the party in the early 1960s.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: My Father Went Underground
clips.summary: Alexander, the youngest child, describes living with her uncle Mark in Perris, California, one of the few places Black people could buy land, after her father went underground during the McCarthy era, a frugal hardworking family of country kids who scavenged a grocery store's discards each week and whose apolitical uncle was fired from Camp Pendleton simply for being her father's brother.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Parked in the Sun
clips.summary: She recalls the Pentecostal church the family attended several times a week, where people caught the holy ghost and spoke in tongues, the nightmares of a chasing devil it gave her, and the FBI cars that tailed her uncle until, to mess with them, he parked under a tree on a dead-end street and left the agents baking in hundred-degree heat.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: A Shop Across From City Hall
clips.summary: Alexander describes moving to Venice Beach near her grandmother and seeing her estranged grandfather just once, running away briefly at four or five missing her father, and her father, after a heart attack, learning shoe repair as an apprentice and opening shops, including one across from Los Angeles City Hall, where all five of them worked.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Shot Into in the Night
clips.summary: She recounts her father being summoned before the House Un-American Activities Committee, his name in the Los Angeles Times, and the shoe shop being shot into months later, and the integration camp Anytown USA run by the National Conference of Christians and Jews, where her sister was elected mayor only to be stripped of the title once the director learned whose daughter she was.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Birdie, You Have to Understand
clips.summary: Alexander remembers her first political conversation, when her father showed her the Jet magazine photographs of the murdered Emmett Till and told her things were not as she thought, leaving her horrified, and recalls a friend of her father's brother beaten and killed in police custody, for whose funeral the intimidated churches would not open their doors.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: You're Negro, Don't Let Anybody Tell You Different
clips.summary: She describes the stares the interracial family drew, her fair-skinned brother getting into fistfights insisting he was Negro, her father's instruction that they were Negro and should let no one say otherwise, and her arrival, the darkest of the siblings, at mixed Los Angeles High School where she took up folk dancing, guitar, and banjo.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: I Arrived in Berkeley in 1964
clips.summary: Alexander recounts graduating high school in 1964 after joining Los Angeles Woolworth's demonstrations, watching the Montgomery boycott and Southern sit-ins on the news from a distance, and choosing Berkeley because of its Sheraton Palace Hotel protests and movement ferment, getting in late on the strength of her senior grades.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Bettina Aptheker Took Me In
clips.summary: She describes arriving with the dorms and co-ops full, her father introducing her to Bay Area movement friends like Jessica Mitford and Roscoe Proctor, and Bettina Aptheker, daughter of the communist scholar Herbert Aptheker, befriending her, planning her classes, and connecting her to the W.E.B. Du Bois Clubs of America and the Communist Party at Berkeley.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Arrested in Sproul Hall
clips.summary: Alexander explains that the 1964 Free Speech Movement grew from Berkeley students mobilizing for the civil rights movement in Oakland and San Francisco, some of them returned Freedom Riders, and recounts her own arrest in Sproul Hall at seventeen, when she told police she was eighteen to avoid juvenile hall and spare her parents the trip, while Mario Savio and Bettina Aptheker spoke at near-daily rallies.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: The Grises on Every Corner
clips.summary: A Spanish major because of her Los Angeles upbringing, Alexander describes studying abroad in Madrid in 1966 and 1967 amid constant Berkeley activism, finding a repressive regime with gray-uniformed grises bearing machine guns, and setting up a table in the Facultad de Filosofia y Letras to gather signatures against the Vietnam War rather than risk touching Franco, with the press asking to be alerted to any action.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Yankis Fuera de Vietnam
clips.topic: The Director and the Antiwar Speech
clips.summary: She recalls her program director Carlos Blanco Aguinaga, a refugee of the Spanish Civil War, urging her to smuggle an antiwar sign to protest a visiting Ronald Reagan, the embassy warning it could not protect students at the April 28, 1967 demonstration, and the event in an occupied Madrid building hung with a banner reading Yankis fuera de Vietnam, where after chaos and a dead sound system she rose to a standing ovation before students rushed her out a back forest path to a taxi with instructions to flee to Andalusia.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: We're the Plainclothes Police
clips.summary: She recounts the Franco-sympathizer landlady warning that police had come looking, a cab driver recognizing her from a radio bulletin as the chica de colores, and being taken by plainclothes police down to the dungeon jail at the Puerta del Sol where prisoners rounded up before May Day sang We Shall Overcome, told at every level that the American embassy, not the Spanish state, wanted her removed.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: A Limousine From CBS
clips.topic: From Jail to the George V
clips.summary: Alexander calls the expulsion more a kidnapping by the Spanish state under American orders, and describes the guarded train past Guardia Civil at every stop to Hendaye, where Carlos Blanco Aguinaga waited with money, the onward train to Paris sharing food with Moroccan workers, and a CBS man who whisked her, Carol Watanabe, and Carol Wynn to the Hotel George V for a friendly human-interest interview.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: The Soviet Youth Was Fifty
clips.summary: Back through New York to Berkeley, Alexander recounts disappointment that the Du Bois Clubs and Communist Party met her return with little fervor, a trip with Abraham Lincoln Brigade veterans to an East German celebration of the Nazis' fall where good food masked dead political conversation and a fifty-year-old stood in as the Soviet youth delegate, Cuban printers the one lively exception, all souring her on the Soviet bloc.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Black People Have to Join the Panthers
clips.summary: Alexander describes passing through sectarian groups, including Bob Avakian's Revolutionary Union, which told her Black people had to join the Black Panther Party, and warming to the Panthers as they began community programs like free breakfast for school children, which she entered through the Oakland church where Charles Bursey was famously photographed, the leadership soon assigning the fast-typing recruit to the Black Panther newspaper.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Laying Out the Paper at the Printers
clips.summary: She recounts round-the-clock layout sessions at a Tenderloin printer under editor Elbert Big Man Howard, where the party artist Emory Douglas brought his art, the full-time all-consuming life taking orders from a teenage officer of the day, and the harassment from men who framed sex as a revolutionary duty she refused, the relationship with Bobby Bowen from Richmond offering some protection.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Chivas Regal and Bitter Dog
clips.summary: Alexander recalls Eldridge Cleaver already gone and his rationalized rape of white women, which struck her as no revolutionary act, names the male leadership of Bobby Seale, David Hilliard, Masai Hewitt, and Huey Newton above all, a class divide where leaders drank Chivas Regal and the rank and file drank Tony port and lemon juice called bitter dog, and recounts being told to drive David Hilliard home, his persistent advances over the ribs she was preparing for a picnic, and the crowd next morning assuming she had slept with him.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Women Are the Other Half
clips.topic: Speaking at United Front Against Fascism
clips.summary: Alexander recounts speaking on women at the United Front Against Fascism in the summer of 1969, building on Eldridge Cleaver's line that women are the other half, arguing the right place for Black women was inside the Panthers rather than the women's liberation movement, after which Angela Davis, not yet famous and sent by Carlos Blanco Aguinaga, introduced herself, and the party arranging a trip to Japan with Elbert Howard.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: The Japanese Red Army Picked Us Up
clips.summary: She describes going to Japan on tickets from the Zengakuren, agreeing to a tag-team approach favoring the Panthers' internationalist line over straight nationalism, a cloak-and-dagger airport pickup by people who turned out to be the Japanese Red Army whose railroad bombings the party had to disavow, the leftist film outfit Ogawa Productions taking them in hand, and earning their keep by charging for interviews on one-way tickets.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: The Second and Third Sons
clips.summary: Alexander explains the trip arose by accident when Japanese contacts offered a ticket in a chaotic, infiltrated 1969, and recounts spreading the Panther message and supporting Japanese movements with the film company's help, meeting little old women sitting in on US military airstrips, the second and third farm sons relegated to a separate caste doing construction work, and Korean immigrant workers, the party meanwhile having met leaders in China and Algeria.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: They Threw the Night Soil
clips.summary: She describes being followed everywhere except with one interpreter, the mass demonstrations of hundreds of thousands against a new airport built on prime farmland where she and Howard spoke, the Japanese protesters locking arms and running while elders flung night soil at netted police, and meeting peasant farmers, before Howard left for Europe and she returned alone after about a month.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: They Would Vamp on the Office
clips.topic: A Police Raid Plan and Three Reasons to Leave
clips.summary: Alexander recounts a separate customs search on her return and refusing to surrender her telephone book, the metal-plated, sandbagged South Berkeley office and gun activities she avoided, firing a .357 Magnum once, and a Berkeley newspaper's discovery of the police plan to vamp on the office with fire engines and machine guns while she typed upstairs, and explains she walked away for three reasons: she did not want to die for the party, she believed it infiltrated by provocateurs, and she was pregnant, after which David Hilliard seized her library.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: They Called It Lumumba-Zapata
clips.summary: Back with her parents and typing for work, Alexander recounts Carlos Blanco Aguinaga calling her to San Diego to help launch what students including Angela Davis had won as Lumumba-Zapata College, a place to teach their own history, which the university renamed Third College, where at about twenty-three she was hired as resident dean for its first year.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Infiltrated From the Beginning
clips.summary: She describes the college infiltrated from the start, administrators the university forced on the student-led committee, one sowing division by claiming it served whites and Chicanos rather than Black people, the intimidating alpha students who fixed her with unbroken stares in the fishbowl of the resident dean's house, her being attacked at a Black student union meeting for her wide-ranging background, suspecting COINTELPRO as with the Panthers, being threatened one night at gunpoint, and quitting after nine months.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: A PhD Over Harvard
clips.summary: She describes turning down a full Harvard scholarship as too cold and a little insulting and earning a PhD in comparative literature at UC San Diego on a Ford Foundation fellowship, with a dissertation on the fictional portrayal of popular movements across Haiti, Cuba, Mexico, and the United States, and reflects via the ribs and an uneaten tuna casserole on skin-color antagonism and the privilege read onto lighter-skinned Black people.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: I Didn't Want the Academy
clips.summary: Alexander recounts deciding against the academy's publish-or-perish competition and teaching adult-school ESL, much of it to Vietnamese refugees, for about twelve years, then being recruited to organize the ESL program at City College, where she spent twenty-five years, her chief contributions becoming English department chair and diversifying its faculty into a dedicated, functioning department.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: They Wouldn't Send the People
clips.summary: She describes running the floundering labor studies program with its associate's degree, introducing occupational safety and health classes taught with the UCLA labor program, and why it struggled in San Diego, union leaders reluctant to develop successors and the Central Labor Council praising the program but not sending students, contrasting it with LA Trade Tech's success, and notes she also taught happily in Chicano Studies.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: We've Gone Back
clips.summary: Alexander observes that Third College, now Thurgood Marshall, is no longer the revolutionary experiment it began as, that Black enrollment at UC campuses has fallen toward its 1964 lows despite affirmative action, and that packing houses, non-union and as dangerous as in her father's day, and police brutality and killings in Oakland show how far the country has slid back.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: We Got It All Covered
clips.summary: Alexander introduces Pillars of the Community, a faith-based Muslim organization in Encanto in Southeast San Diego she is active in, joking that her family, already Black and Jewish, gained Islam when her son converted twenty years ago, giving her Urdu-speaking grandsons, and describes its Encanto storefront, reminiscent of the old Panther storefronts, helping people leaving prison and organizing politically.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Penal Code 182.5
clips.summary: She explains the organization's San Diego 33 campaign against California penal code 182.5, gang-documentation law by which wearing green, standing near a flagged house, or posting rap online can brand someone from age nine a gang member, and recounts the district attorney arresting thirty-three Black men, among them Aaron Harvey, held seven months on charges tied to violence he had nothing to do with though he was in Las Vegas, and how his family, joining Pillars of the Community, fought rather than accept the plea bargains most take in terror of fifty-year sentences, galvanizing a movement that also runs know-your-rights workshops.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Write Your Story
clips.topic: A Storytelling Project and City Works Press
clips.summary: She describes her role beginning with a meaningful rap album her Muslim son helped produce, then a storytelling program she helped start where people of color write one piece of their story and read it to a small gathering, done three times, surfacing harrowing and triumphant memories from a mother's murder to Aaron Harvey's account of being jailed himself, half the authors connected to City College, and the plan to publish nineteen of them through the independent City Works Press with a blurb from Elbert Howard and ties to writers Jimmy Santiago Baca and Luis Rodriguez.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: I'm Not a Star
clips.summary: She describes speaking to classes and panels at San Diego State and UC San Diego, often amid organizing, including after the Compton Cookout, nooses, and anti-Black graffiti incidents, presenting Elbert Howard when he visited, and insisting she is not a star but someone who happened to be in many places doing things without knowing the risks, a serious year in the Panthers among them.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: My Way to Deal Is to Forget
clips.summary: Alexander reflects that the Panthers did not take care of themselves, leaving no time for relationships among women and no space to discuss the sexual harassment, and that she copes with high stress, her worst being Third College, by forgetting, having blocked out the gunpoint threat her director's family still remembers, an emotional toll she suppressed to keep going.
keywords: did not take care of ourselves

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: I Walk Through the World as African American
clips.summary: She reflects on being born out of wedlock because interracial marriage was illegal, the difficulty of mixed race in a time of identity politics, her closeness to her Jewish grandmother who opposed Israeli policy toward Palestine, her refusal of strictly race-based gatherings she is always invited to, and her admiration that the Panthers reached out to other groups rather than fighting over crumbs.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: The Solid Rock Behind It All
clips.summary: Alexander closes on her siblings, a sister who devoted her life to health care in the African American community and a brother who, after suffering most in the McCarthy era, became chief medical officer of a Kaiser section in Los Angeles, and on her one regret, that she interviewed her aunt, brother, sister, and cousins but never her mother, the quiet solid rock who held the family together.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: A Carpenter and a Sharecropper's Daughter
clips.topic: Family, Farm, and the Move to Greenwood
clips.summary: Interviewed by Dr. John Dittmer in Tchula, Mississippi, in March 2013, Rosie Head opens on her birth in Holmes County to a father who was both carpenter and sharecropper, a working farm with cows, horses, mules, chickens, and hogs, a family of two brothers and seven sisters in which she fell about sixth, and the move from Lexington to Greenwood at six, where the children chopped and picked cotton from age seven.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: One Room, One Teacher, Eighth Grade
clips.summary: She describes missing first grade because the Hickory Springs school in Lexington lay across a creek she was too little to cross, then walking miles to a one-room church school in Greenwood where a single teacher taught every grade through eighth, and her love of reading.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: She Had to Call Her Miss Corrine
clips.summary: Head recalls carrying her shoes and washing her dusty legs on the hot walk to Sunday school, then becoming aware of segregation in Greenwood, her mother cooking for the plantation owner, having to address a little white girl named Corrine as Miss Corrine, being allowed to play outside but never enter the house, and a family that talked at home so the children knew the rules.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: You Came Out in the Hole Again
clips.summary: She describes the family picking seventy-five to eighty bales of cotton a year yet being told at the year-end settlement that they cleared nothing, her father returning from the plantation owner's office to tell her mother they came out in the hole again, the company store advancing twelve people thirty-five dollars a month, and her hurt that the family was cheated of the Christmas they looked forward to.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: She Heard It on the Radio
clips.summary: Head recounts hearing of the Emmett Till lynching on the family radio while her mother cooked, the killing tied to a store near her school across the railroad where the children spent pennies and nickels, the storekeeper who watched them as if they would steal, and a boy about her own age whose death made the children realize it could be them.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: He Laid His Keys Down on the Counter
clips.summary: Head tells how, in the late 1950s, her father saved to buy a new car, and how the plantation boss, who wanted the men to send their wives to collect their pay, withheld her father's check when he refused to send her mother, seized the car keys off the counter, and claimed the car, prompting her father to use a spare set of keys.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: We Left That Plantation That Night
clips.summary: She describes the family fleeing the plantation that night for Tchula, where an aunt lived, the boss scaring the neighbor holding their furniture into giving it up, losing the car when the man cut off her father's work and credit, getting one of the project houses near Mileston, and at seventeen picking and chopping cotton by the day for a dollar fifty.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: A Way I Knew Was Better
clips.summary: Head recounts first hearing in 1964 from Greenwood people about outsiders coming to help Mississippians register, attending meetings in Greenwood until a young man came to Tchula and they began their own meetings and a freedom school in a church, and explains that years of hearing her father say something had to be done, plus knowing how the people in charge treated them, drew her in.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: The SNCC People in Greenwood
clips.summary: Head names the SNCC organizers she encountered around Greenwood, including a worker she calls John Ball, Stokely Carmichael, Bob Moses, Lawrence Guyot, and Hollis Watkins, and describes meetings spent talking about segregated schools, courthouses, water fountains, and restaurants, that Black people were second class citizens paying first class taxes, and that the vote was where the power lay.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: He Was Fearless
clips.summary: Head contrasts two local leaders: the man whose name she renders as Turnbow, fearless, who would rouse a crowd, sip from his bowl during an armed standoff a visiting nurse witnessed, and predict the prisons that would fill with young Black men; and a quieter farmer who thought everything out and hung back to bail people out of jail. She also recalls hearing Medgar Evers speak and feeling his assassination like a family death.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: A Mock Election to Prove the Point
clips.summary: Head describes her work in the fall 1963 Freedom Vote, the mock election with Aaron Henry and Ed King meant to prove Black Mississippians would vote if given the chance, canvassing every house across Holmes County for the vote and for an integrated Head Start program needed to draw federal money, people afraid of eviction, white farmers who cursed canvassers off their property, and polling places in Thornton and Mileston.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: They Would Shoot Up in There
clips.summary: Head describes Klan activity in Holmes County, nights when about six of them worked late on newsletters at the Holmes County Community Center while trucks circled the building shouting threats and firing into it, and how a lookout across the railroad track gave just enough warning for the night riders to flee.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Almost Twenty-Four Hours a Day
clips.summary: Head recalls overcoming constant fear through prayer, then the 1964 summer project that brought hundreds of mostly white Northern students to Mississippi, the roughly thirty volunteers in Holmes County, and her near round-the-clock canvassing. She recounts a march from Valley State toward Jackson pelted with rocks from boxcars, a man called Sam House arrested repeatedly, and a boycott march where police pulled a woman down in the street.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: We Couldn't Believe It
clips.summary: She recalls a man from California named Abe Osheroff coming to build the Mileston Community Center, her father helping construct it, the disbelief at strangers volunteering, neighbors gathering to watch the building go up, and the harassment that followed when authorities refused gas and telephone service and the highway patrol ticketed people crossing the railroad to reach it.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Everybody Took a Liking to Sue and Henry
clips.topic: The Organizing Couple and the Center
clips.summary: Head describes the couple she calls Sue and Henry settling in once they saw what the community needed and becoming beloved fixtures, Henry urging her to manage the center as they prepared to leave and telling her she should write a book, and her own work keeping all the records, taking messages from across and outside the state, sending newsletters, and supervising about four other young women.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: A Little Room Built for the Clinic
clips.topic: Bringing Health Care to the County
clips.summary: Head recounts the medical workers, among them the nurse Josephine Disparti and a woman she names as Helen, who gave physical exams at the center until a small clinic room was built and people came from all over the county, the Medical Committee for Human Rights and a young doctor she calls Albert, a beginning she ties to comprehensive community health centers, and a childhood of home remedies with no Black doctors, when she never saw a doctor until her first child.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: That's the Best School in the World
clips.summary: Head describes setting up a makeshift kindergarten at the center two years before federal money arrived, with help from Sue and Henry and a truck Abe Osheroff left behind, driving around to collect children, feeding them grits and oatmeal, sewing their clothes, and a boy called Short who cried not to come but came to call it the best school in the world because it fed you and let you rest.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Dogs at the Courthouse Door
clips.summary: Head recounts trying to register before the Voting Rights Act, going to the courthouse with five women where the sheriff and deputies came out with dogs, clubs, and guns, the dogs walked deliberately close, and being shut alone in a closet-like room with a desk to fill out the paperwork.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: They Never Told Us We Had Passed
clips.summary: She describes a dog left growling to guard the door so she was held some four hours, a chancery clerk who told her to go home and do as her mother and grandmother had, and the registrars never telling applicants they passed until federal registrars came after the 1965 Voting Rights Act, found the records, and revealed they had passed all along.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Truckloads to the Registrar
clips.summary: Head describes hauling truckloads of fearful people to register because few would go on their own, the long coaxing it took, and the teachers and preachers who at first stayed away, thinking they stood in good with white folks or fearing for their jobs, until a teacher she calls Bernice Montgomery came early and hosted meetings at her house.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Transportation Available to You
clips.summary: Head explains mobilizing voters for the 1966 congressional race with a candidate she calls Mr. Hayes, arranging gas and cars, posting people at the center to take calls, finding someone in each area to count how many needed rides, and organizing it all under the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party rather than the regular Democratic Party.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: He Came In Drunk and Cursing
clips.summary: Head describes serving as receiving and returning manager, a circuit clerk pressuring her to keep doing the work after a new white woman was given her title, and a log-truck driver who came drunk and cursing to drag his wife out of the polling place until local people removed him, plus the long bitter-cold counts that ran to three or four in the morning.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Looking Over Your Shoulder
clips.summary: Head recalls rough-looking white men parking at the door and standing over the count, poll watching she did in Canton where the sight of such men turned Black families' cars away, helping voters who could not read by directing them to authorized assistants, and a woman who, after asking her for help, was told she could not vote and then had her food stamps and welfare check cut off until it was straightened out.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Everybody Knew Robert Clark
clips.summary: Head describes the 1967 local elections and Robert Clark's race for the state legislature, a well-known former schoolteacher whom whites tried everything to defeat, the MFDP stationing helpers inside and outside every polling place against intimidation, the word-of-mouth network that told neighbors which candidates to support, and Clark becoming the first African American in the state legislature in the twentieth century.
related_events: Election of Robert Clark to Mississippi Legislature

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: The Only Center in the Program
clips.summary: Head recounts the Child Development Group of Mississippi Head Start, brought to them by Sue and Henry, a six-week funded program for which Mileston was the only center, traveling to Mount Beulah for a week of federal training, working with Polly Greenberg and Tom Levin, and serving as secretary and assistant teacher while driving the payroll to Jackson each week.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: The Bank Wouldn't Cash Our Checks
clips.summary: She describes the Tchula bank refusing to cash CDGM checks until a supermarket owner talked them into it, white merchants she names as Cohen and others pressing the group at a night meeting to let them take over the funds and being refused, overhearing those men say on the stairs that the program would fail because Black people could not handle money, and the first six weeks ending with officials coming to take the furniture.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: They Had Never Seen a Shower
clips.summary: Head describes the program once funds came, restaurant-quality meals of steak and pork chops, equipment donated by farmers and carpenters, teaching children who had never seen a shower or indoor toilet, writing down and reading back the things they knew, and a graduation where teachers could not believe the children could write and recognize their names.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: They Said It Was a Civil Rights Outfit
clips.summary: Head recounts CDGM losing its funding despite being judged the best program in the country, attributed to Senator John Stennis and others calling it a civil rights outfit, the people working all over again like the movement, and a busload of children and a teacher she names as Zellma traveling to Washington to lobby for Head Start funds.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Still There After Seven Years
clips.summary: Head closes by recounting working three months without funds until refunding came the next May, her years of schooling since the movement, a two-year degree in 1978 and a CDA credential, repeated returns to college as requirements changed, health setbacks as a diabetic that left her a few hours short of graduating, and the interviewer's thanks on behalf of the Smithsonian.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: A Father Who Gave Us a World
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier for the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian NMAAHC in Columbus, Georgia, in April 2011, Ruby Sales opens with her father, the son of a formerly enslaved teacher and an itinerant Baptist preacher, an Army chaplain radicalized by serving in a segregated military who taught his children about Port Chicago and asked why Black men died abroad but could not vote at home, and her mother, who would confront any white person who disrespected her in a downtown store.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: A Sales, Not a Fletcher
clips.summary: Sales describes the town where her grandmother lived, where she estimates thirty percent of the people were Sales relatives and generations of the family lay in the cemetery, and how being told she was just like her aunt Nen or aunt Rae made her see herself all her life as a Sales in temperament, looks, and love of knowledge rather than a Fletcher, her mother's family.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Truman's Army and Childish Rebellions
clips.summary: She recalls that because of Truman's integration of the Army, her family near Fort Benning could ride the front of the bus and use integrated theaters, restaurants, and hospitals, an experience not replicated in civil society, and how she and her friends intuitively challenged segregation by trying on bathing suits in white stores and ringing the bus buzzer before running off laughing.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: A Training Ground for Citizenship
clips.summary: Sales recounts moving from a Catholic school to Carver Elementary and Carver High in the Carver Heights subdivision built on FHA loans for military families, where teachers under principal S.P. Charleston insulated students from segregation so they would not be broken-wing birds and connected their education to the project of freedom and the advancement of the whole community.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: A Lot of Mouth for a Young Girl
clips.summary: She describes herself as a cheerleader, newspaper editor, and Honor Society member who was a well-known rebel allowed to challenge rules because her powerful home teacher saw something worth cultivating and her teachers understood rebellion as a substance of survival, not wanting students to accommodate segregation or get killed.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Going to Tuskegee at Sixteen
clips.summary: Sales explains that a regional program let students who passed a test enter college early, so at sixteen she followed her home teacher Marian Armstrong to Tuskegee Institute in the fall of 1963, after abandoned fantasies of joining the Army and attending Drake, and finds a campus already charged with movement consciousness through Gwen Patton and Professor Charles Gomillion's challenge to gerrymandering.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: No Chariot, No God
clips.topic: Galvanized and Locked in at the Capitol
clips.summary: Sales recalls wanting to be a beatnik who quoted Allen Ginsberg until Stokely Carmichael and people from Lowndes County came to campus and galvanized her, so that she threw away her guitar pick and grew her hair natural, and describes the Tuskegee student demonstration over the Edmund Pettus Bridge beatings, being locked in at the Montgomery Capitol amid mounted men with billy clubs, and how, waiting in vain for a delivering God, she lost her Southern Baptist religion while watching a SNCC woman face down a club-raising officer.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Sleeping in the Same Room
clips.topic: Breaking Boundaries at the Freedom House
clips.summary: She recounts her home teacher Marian Armstrong driving eighty miles to ask her to leave the church, a request she refused as a defining break from older adults' approval, and how moving into a freedom house meant sleeping in the same room as men without sex, redefining the relationship between men and women as comradeship and friendship.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Stunned, Not Angry
clips.summary: Sales reflects on her amazement that movement-supporting church deacons could lock the students out, deny them food, and want them gone, on Stokely Carmichael's nervous breakdown in the Montgomery airport from years under siege, and on why she held no grudge, reared to question elders without harboring deep hate.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Rethinking What a Woman Is
clips.summary: She describes the profound shift in how she saw herself as a young woman, questioning whether she had to marry and have children, and how working alongside men and depending on them led her to relate to them through friendship and trust rather than reducing them to sexual objects.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: There Was Always My Mouth
clips.summary: Sales answers why she was willing to go into Lowndes County while others were not, crediting parents who spoke out against injustice, her principal's prediction that her big mouth would make her a great civil rights lawyer, her rebellious search for larger meaning, and a self-image, as a champion marble player who beat boys at basketball, that refused the small circumscribed space reserved for women.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: An Integrated Hell Tonight
clips.summary: She recalls her first day registering voters when a sheriff drew a gun on Stokely Carmichael and threatened to blow his brains out, Carmichael answering that hell would be an integrated hell that night, the stories of violent Bloody Lowndes told by local people, and the bravery and hospitality of residents like Mr. Jackson, who gave them a freedom house, and Clara Maul, who risked her life driving them.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Ordinary People, Extraordinary Things
clips.summary: Sales describes a typical day in Lowndes County knocking on doors and seeing extreme poverty, hungry shoeless children, and people picking cotton and cucumbers, and how she measured progress not by her own work but by watching local people execute leadership in mass meetings, organize neighbors, and refuse to be intimidated, developing a lifelong respect for ordinary people doing extraordinary things.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: He Showed Up Every Time
clips.summary: She gives portraits of John Hulett, the first uneducated man she had seen in a leadership position, whom she trusted because he kept his word in violent circumstances and never hogged the microphone, and Clara Maul, a married mother who drove a hundred miles an hour and, protected because her grandmother owned a bar and had a liaison with a rich white man, could move through the county freely and was relied on even by Stokely.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: How Do You Squelch Their Spirit
clips.topic: The SNCC Debate Over Fort Deposit
clips.summary: Around the August 1965 signing of the Voting Rights Act, Sales recounts the young people of Fort Deposit, angry that their sharecropping parents were cheated at the plantation store, bringing SNCC a plan to demonstrate, and the hours-long debate in which she argued the action was too dangerous while Stokely Carmichael and Jonathan Daniels argued to keep the movement open to all, a democratizing vision that won her over.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: A Garbage Truck to Hayneville
clips.topic: The Mob, the Jail, and Release
clips.summary: She describes the Saturday demonstration confronted by a mob of white men armed with baseball bats and anything they could grab, the arrest she believes also spared their lives, being loaded onto a garbage truck and taken to the barbaric Hayneville jail where big-mouthed women were threatened with rape, her developing an ulcer while begging to see a doctor, and the jailer suddenly turning them loose, which terrified her because the same had happened to Goodman and Chaney.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Bitch, I'll Blow Your Brains Out
clips.summary: Sales recounts walking with the group to a corner store after release, a later account by Willie Vaughn that an armed man waited there, and Tom Coleman appearing in the doorway and aiming at her in front before she was pulled backward and heard the shot that killed Jonathan Daniels instantly and a second that wounded Father Richard Morrisroe, who lay moaning for water; hearing Joyce Bailey call her name, she realized she was alive and crawled away while Coleman flung his gun and threatened to kill anyone who helped the priest.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: On Top of Jonathan's Body
clips.summary: She recounts Stokely Carmichael and Silas Norman finding the downtown streets cleaned and Daniels and Morrisroe missing, several days of Ruby Doris Smith Robinson and the SNCC office unable to learn their whereabouts even through John Doar of the Justice Department, and the discovery that Morrisroe had been carried to the hospital in a hearse on top of Daniels's dead body and left in the hallway for hours.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: From Big Mouth to Silence
clips.summary: Sales reflects that none of the young workers, none over twenty-two, had faced such Southern white murder before, how the group was shattered yet felt responsible for one another and dreaded facing Daniels's mother at the funeral, and how she went from a big-mouth person to one who rarely spoke, spending much of her adult life, even at Princeton and Manhattanville, never telling anyone she had been in the movement.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: If You Testify, They Will Kill You
clips.summary: She describes returning home to supportive parents, white men coming to the backyard while her mother hung wash to warn that she would be killed if she testified, her mother who never told her not to testify, and John Doar's words that the wheels of justice move very slowly.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Sleeping With His Whore
clips.summary: Sales recounts her determination to testify and meet the defense lawyer word for word in a courtroom where hostility hung like icicles, and how the lawyer, said to be one of the greatest in Alabama history, insinuated she was a whore and that Daniels, like white men in the South, was sleeping with her, trying to build the case on the South's unspoken truth, though she and Daniels had no such relationship.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: The Commitment Was Solidified
clips.summary: Asked whether the trauma made her reconsider nonviolence, Sales reaffirms that she never considered anything else, finding killing repugnant even as part of the movement took up arms, and explains that the killing did not defeat the Lowndes County movement, where people who had lived with white violence for generations were bound together by collective grief over Daniels and grew more determined to move forward.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: The Second Indigenous Movement
clips.summary: She describes Tent City in 1966, sharecroppers evicted for registering to vote who chose to live in tents rather than be controlled by economic penalties, which she frames as part of the second indigenous Southern freedom movement after the runaway slave movement, a vision rooted in the will of the people that fed the later Washington Tent City under Abernathy, the Black Panther symbol, and an effort to build a party improving on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: The Girl That Jonathan Saved
clips.summary: Sales recounts the years after Lowndes County: a speaking tour, a brief stint in Albany working with Charles Sherrod, resettling in Washington with Gene Wiley, living in James Baldwin's apartment in New York while he was away, becoming involved in the feminist movement and a global consciousness, and wrestling with how to carry the label of the girl Jonathan saved while building a life beyond it.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Not Civil Rights, but Freedom
clips.topic: Why It Was a Freedom Movement
clips.summary: Sales explains why she calls it the Southern Freedom Movement rather than the civil rights movement: it sought human dignity and an end to a century of white terrorism, larger creative and political space, and benefits for white people too, and was a dynamic process connected to earlier efforts like the Southern Negro Youth Cooperative, while pointing out that Rosa Parks's act was also a rebellion of working-class maids assaulted on Montgomery buses.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Out of the Ashes of Segregation
clips.summary: Drawing on her graduate study, Sales critiques narratives dominated by whites that assumed Black people had second-class minds, ignored the network Black educators built across the South as described by Vanessa Siddle Walker, and reduced Black life to deficit and pathology rather than what, as Ralph Ellison said, was made out of the ashes of segregation, and notes that historians credited Crozer and Boston University for King while missing the cultural resources he drew on to testify with the people.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: A Culture of Intimacy
clips.summary: She argues the movement happened because of a shared Southern intimacy that historians overlook, a closed society bound by blood and common experience, where Bernice Reagon brought what she had learned as a Southern Black girl, where people sang the same songs and prayed the same prayers, stayed with one another because hotels were closed to them, and where the playground was an unrecognized training ground for young people.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Snake, Snake, and Enslavement
clips.summary: Sales explains two deliberate language choices: refusing the defense lawyer's familiar pronunciation of SNCC because he lacked the intimacy to claim that territory, and as a history major preferring enslavement over slavery and enslaver over master, terms that name the perpetrator and the brutality of captivity rather than implying passive submission or a benign Atlantic passage, insisting that history make white men the visible subjects responsible for their deeds.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Stilling History
clips.summary: Returning to Lowndes County in about 2005 for public events few whites attended, Sales describes how those in power maintain it through dismembering and revisionist history that wipes their hands of the blood, makes the oppressor the good guy, and feeds a post-racial myth, and she ties this to cultural wars that turned liberation theology into prosperity gospel, tore down Black schools as David Cecelski documents, and fired tens of thousands of Black teachers across Southern states.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: A New Plantation System
clips.summary: Sales argues that Black people did not endure a century through the veil of segregation merely for one seat in the White House but to advance democracy for all, and that a revisionist climate criminalizing Black men opened the door to a prison industrial complex, a new plantation system where young men work in jail for corporations, while Southern culture's materialism and anti-intellectualism, exported north through a tax on liberalism, came to hold both worlds together.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: The Cultural Wars They Won
clips.summary: She contends that the South lost the freedom-movement war but won the cultural wars, so that by the 1980s Northern and Southern politicians were galvanized around the same whiteness, and frames the twenty-first century as an anti-human technocracy in which only a few matter and the rest, including obsolete white males who blame others, are treated as collateral damage and human waste, a fascist drift she traces to the heartbeat of Southern politics and the destruction of the New Deal's cap on corporate greed.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: How We Live in Relation to Each Other
clips.summary: Sales closes by describing Spirit House, named for breath and the integration of inner conviction with outward living in relation to one another, which brings people of all colors together on education, economics, and racial justice, and for two years has studied the segregation-era counterculture of education to learn from the Southern Black teachers who made outstanding students, work she connects to the survival of HBCUs being folded into struggling white colleges, before the interview closes with thanks.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: You Knew Your Place
clips.summary: Interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries at Albany State University in March 2013, Sam Mahone recalls being born in 1945 in Americus, Sumter County, Georgia, where the lines of separation were stark, and how as a child he read the wrongness of the system in his parents' downcast eyes and lack of eye contact with white people.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The Policy Man Walked Right In
clips.topic: A Family Dependent on White Services
clips.summary: Mahone names his father Bergen Mahon, a basket-factory worker, and his mother Lula Bell, a domestic, and describes the dirt roads, the outdoor toilet he had until his senior year, and the white insurance agent who would walk into Black homes without knocking, the everyday liberties that taught a child to know his place out of fear.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The Americus Voters League
clips.summary: Around tenth grade, before any civil rights organization reached Americus, Mahone joined a voter registration project started by local ministers called the Americus Voters League, talking to high school classmates about registration, drawn to it as something different to do in a small town with few outlets.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Disengaged but Not Against It
clips.summary: Mahone describes his working father's silence and his mother's quiet support of his activity, his older brothers already away in the military and a younger sister who left for Florida, and the field jobs he held as a student: picking cotton, pulling peanuts, picking peaches, and cleaning a clothing store.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: SNCC Came in Late 1962
clips.summary: Mahone recounts SNCC workers Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen arriving in Americus in late 1962, dispatched from Albany by project director Charles Sherrod, drawn by demographics where Black residents were half the population on a median income near twelve hundred dollars a year, and the second-class schools where he never received a new book.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The SNCC House Became a Hangout
clips.topic: The Freedom House and Black Authors
clips.summary: Mahone describes the SNCC workers with Northern dialects setting up a shotgun freedom house, introducing students to Richard Wright and James Baldwin, and running mimeograph machines to print leaflets, so the house became an after-school hangout where his activism began and he recognized the duality of the system.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Voting Is Power
clips.summary: Mahone explains months of door-to-door canvassing in the city and counties, the Freedom School set up to teach people to read and pass the literacy test, and the nonviolence preparation where workers rehearsed curling into a fetal position and absorbing spit and blows for the violence they knew was coming.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Eleven of Us Went to the Front
clips.summary: Mahone tells how students, not SNCC workers, pushed to test public accommodations at the downtown movie theater where Black patrons climbed three flights up a dark alley, how eleven of them tried to buy tickets at the front and were jailed for disorderly conduct, and how a second arrest a week later sent four of them to the Sumter County prison farm.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Seven Days in the Hole
clips.summary: Mahone describes being sentenced to begin time immediately, refusing road work, and being put with another boy in a four-by-six concrete hole fed bread and water each day, where they kept a hunger strike in total darkness for about seven days until they passed out and were hospitalized, then made to shovel out pools of raw sewage at the Americus treatment plants, the worst job saved for the movement prisoners alone.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: They Stood By Me
clips.summary: Mahone remembers his father angry and fearful and his mother bringing him things, and how working the streets let people pass by with news of the movement and money, where he picked up smoking, while watching new arrivals brought to the jail each night kept the jailed students going.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The Meanest Man in the World
clips.topic: Sheriff Fred Chappell and the Inmates
clips.summary: Mahone describes the cold, racist jailers and Sheriff Fred Chappell, whom King had called the meanest man in the world after being jailed in Americus in 1961 and whom Mahone likens to Jim Clark and Al Lingo, contrasted with the older inmates who praised the students, protected them like big brothers, and became people to organize.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Tire Irons at the Hasty House
clips.summary: Released and now committed, Mahone finished high school and joined SNCC, and on the night the Civil Rights Bill was signed in July 1964 he, John Perdew, and Bob Mants of Morehouse tried to order at the Hasty House restaurant, where whites from a service station attacked them with tire irons and baseball bats as they reached their convertible, beating them on the head while one of their group, attacked at the outside restroom, fled and was not seen for two days.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: A Century-Old Sedition Law
clips.summary: Mahone recounts a 1963 night march from a mass meeting met with dogs and water hoses, after which SNCC workers Don Harris, John Perdew, Ralph Allen and CORE worker Zev Aelony were charged under a century-old sedition law carrying the death penalty, held some eighty-four days until a three-judge federal panel and a legal team including C.B. King of Albany and Donald Hollowell of Atlanta won their release.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The Nation Turned Its Eyes
clips.summary: Mahone describes how the possibility of execution stayed on people's minds, the nationwide fundraising and letter-writing campaign from college campuses and legal groups, and the jubilation when the case went to the Supreme Court and the lower-court ruling was upheld, in a courthouse that is now the welcome center holding the movement's memorabilia.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The Leesburg Stockade
clips.summary: Mahone explains that as the Americus jails filled, authorities used abandoned camps in Terrell County and the Leesburg, Georgia stockade, where juvenile girls under sixteen were held in an all-concrete building with one toilet for thirty-five to forty girls, no bedding or showers, a guard who threw a rattlesnake into the holding area, and white men who came asking to have fun with the girls; police had hooded the girls into a tractor trailer in the night so parents did not know where they were for over a week, the episode later told by Jet Magazine as the stolen girls.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The Maids Union
clips.summary: Mahone describes how domestic workers were fired when their children joined the movement, so they formed the first Maids Union, started by the mother of several of the jailed girls, to ask for better wages, and how the movement organized a national book drive that brought twenty thousand books to set up a library in the former Colored Hospital that had trained Black doctors.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Voting in the White Line
clips.summary: Mahone tells of Mary Kate Bell Fikes, the first Black woman to qualify for the Democratic primary in Sumter County running for Justice of the Peace, and how she with Mamie Campbell, Gloria Wise, and Lena Turner tried to vote in the white voting line in 1965, were arrested, and set off a daily deluge of demonstrations, until the women refused a bond the fearful white merchants offered, sent a letter citing the merchants' hypocrisy, and a federal judge dropped the charges and declared Sumter County's segregated voting lines illegal.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: A Scholarship to Tougaloo
clips.summary: Mahone describes taking a SNCC scholarship in 1966 to attend Tougaloo College in Mississippi, planning to major in sociology, finding Tougaloo at the forefront of civil rights activity and the only Black institution actively protesting the war, and leaving after two years because he spent his time as an off-campus activist and there was more work to be done.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The Lawyer Run Out of Town
clips.summary: Mahone tells of Warren Fortson, a white Americus lawyer and school board head whose brother Ben Fortson was Georgia Secretary of State, who defended Roberta Freeman, an early integrator of the white high school, and her boyfriend on charges Mahone believed were meant to stop her graduating, and who was hounded out of Americus by the Klan, later steering Mahone toward a job in Atlanta.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Ancestral Arts Gallery
clips.summary: Mahone recounts taking a job as an apprentice in paper conservation at the Georgia Department of Archives for some twelve to fifteen years, studying photography, and opening Ancestral Arts Gallery, which he ran full time as another form of activism elevating African American artists who lacked venues in Atlanta and hosting poetry sessions and community meetings.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: A Holdover Company
clips.summary: Mahone describes being drafted in 1969 despite his anti-war protests, kept at Fort Benning after basic training and denied a security clearance over his Americus arrest record, assigned to a holdover company of duty work digging ditches and building an archery course alongside men given a choice between prison and the war, then sent to a material command in Germany where, idle in the back of an office, he first picked up a camera and spent weekends driving to Paris until his release in New Jersey in 1971.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: We Convened on Americus Again
clips.summary: Mahone explains how in 2007 he and other Americus movement veterans, realizing the history was being lost and unshared with their children, gathered at the Bottoms funeral home whose family had been the movement's bail bondsman, and resolved to hold a tribute every two years and one day build a museum to house the photographs, flyers, and Voice of Americus newsletter they had begun to collect.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: That's Me in Her Belly
clips.summary: Mahone describes reproducing news articles and photographs from the Atlanta Journal, Columbus Ledger-Enquirer, and Macon Telegraph that the Americus Times-Recorder never reported, displaying them at each celebration, and a young woman who recognized her pregnant mother in a photograph and learned for the first time that her mother had been a foot soldier of the movement.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: They Stand on Shoulders
clips.summary: Mahone closes on the mission's purpose in Jimmy Carter's hometown: scholarships to nurture a new generation of young activists who must understand they stand on the shoulders of those who made tremendous sacrifices, so that the work continues only if they take the lead, a charge passed to them every two years.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Born on the Cusp of Brown
clips.summary: Interviewed by Willie Griffin of the Southern Oral History Program in Albany, Georgia, in March 2013, Sam Young, Jr. recounts being born in Worth County, Sylvester, Georgia, on March 13, 1950, just as Brown v. Board of Education loomed, and a father of the same name who was active in local politics and kept the family abreast of the issues by making them watch the news every day.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Freedom of Choice and the School Bus
clips.summary: Young describes how Freedom of Choice let Black students stay in the all-Black school or transfer to the white one, his decision to transfer, walking to school past churches while white children on the buses hung out the windows yelling racial names, and the children throwing rocks at the bus and slipping through the cleared turpentine paths in the woods.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Held Back Because I Could Not Read
clips.summary: He tells how his father kept him back a grade because he could not read, putting him in school alongside a sister so people thought they were twins, recalls strict teachers with no cheating and the time he was caught sitting on a spelling book and spanked, and the lesson his parents drilled in: never follow the crowd, you are born an individual and you die an individual.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: The Devil's Den and the Detention Center
clips.summary: Young recounts vandals painting his Worth County Baptist church and writing that it was the devil's den, a local who was made to repaint the whole thing, and two of his cousins, around fourteen, arrested off the school bus after one beat a white boy and taken to the Youth Detention Center in Albany, where the sheriff hid their location for three days until attorney C.B. King threatened to have him arrested.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Shot for Refusing to Say Mister
clips.summary: He describes being in the presence of shootings, including a deacon and Sunday school man whose teenage employer's son demanded to be called Mister, and when the man refused, the boy's father came out with a shotgun and shot him, the buckshot lodging in his face and hands that he had thrown up to shield his head, and names the shooter as Albert Gill and the wounded man as C.T. Rockwell.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: No Land to Pass On
clips.topic: Foreclosures and a Line of Credit
clips.summary: Young reflects on the racial pressure aimed at his father, the foreclosures that took Black land, and a local white grocer and hardware owner who extended his father a line of credit to farm, lamenting that unlike white families whose land passed down through generations, there was no such inheritance for Black people until recent ASCS, later Farm Service Agency, programs.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Ten Sisters and Seven Brothers
clips.summary: He lists the cotton, corn, peanuts, and vegetables he grew up planting, explains that the large family of ten sisters and seven brothers existed in part to gather the crops, and recalls being the oldest son with two older sisters and graduating from Worth County High School in Sylvester in 1968, where he believed he was the only male in a class of four or five young women.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Firestone and a Need for Education
clips.summary: Young recounts his first public job at Firestone after high school, running into tough civil rights problems and being let go, the brutal labor of busting concrete in deep foundations that convinced him he needed more education, and learning of the Southwest Georgia Project for Community Education through two older sisters who already worked there.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: The Mentors Who Took Me In
clips.summary: He describes working with the Southwest Georgia Project until 1983 and attending Albany Technical College for graphic art to help start a newspaper, explains the Project as a community education group covering the region with a scholarship program, and credits the mentors who took him in and taught him politics, Charles Sherrod, Randolph Battle, and Robert Mants, along with an attorney named Mary Young who worked alongside C.B. King.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: A Huge Tract for Self-Sufficiency
clips.summary: Young explains New Communities Inc as the land arm of the Southwest Georgia Project, how Charles Sherrod learned in New York of an unposted tract for sale in Southwest Georgia and pursued the idea of buying it to build a self-sufficient Black community, the land secured around 1968, the youth who came from different states to learn farming, and the peanut allotment of more than three hundred acres.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Years and Years Before the Suit Paid
clips.summary: He recounts the class action suit in which the federal government finally admitted discrimination against Black farmers by the ASCS, the long wait from denials in the late 1980s and mid 1990s until money came through in 2009 when many had given up, the farm being disqualified because of the Project's other activities, and Shirley and Charles Sherrod later buying a new plot of land in the area.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Denied a Phone, Denied a Future
clips.topic: Living on the Farm, Then Letting Go
clips.summary: Young describes the dwellings on the farm where he moved his wife and children after marrying in 1974, the local families and project people who lived there too, and how they were denied phones and never taken kindly by locals and higher-ups when Black people gained control of something better, and reflects on years of disappointment until reconnecting by chance with Ms. Sherrod inspired him to get back involved for the sake of Black youth.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Address the Issue Then
clips.summary: He affirms that the Southwest Georgia Project never died out and was always active even as he went his own separate way, and offers his words of wisdom: that you have to stand up and address an issue when it arises rather than wait, so that even if it does not go your way you will have made your discontent known.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Synectics and Seeing the Country
clips.summary: Young recalls how the Project let him travel to New York City and the Carolinas and see far more than other young people his age, a New York seminar called Synectics that taught group problem solving on camera, the first time he saw himself recorded, and how he brought those skills back to his group, closing with gratitude for the chance to share his story.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Born Into a Church Home in Flint
clips.summary: Interviewed in Seattle on April 17 by David Cline for the Library of Congress, the Smithsonian, and the Southern Oral History Program, Reverend Dr. Samuel Berry McKinney opens by placing his birth on December 28, 1926, in Flint, Michigan, and his father, a Baptist minister born near Gainesville, Georgia, in 1892, one of twelve children of a Georgia sharecropper named Wade Hampton McKinney.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: From the Fifth Grade to Morehouse
clips.summary: McKinney recounts his father, at eighteen and only in the fifth grade, being encouraged by his Gainesville pastor toward education, entering Atlanta Baptist College in 1910 (renamed Morehouse College in 1914), earning a high school diploma in 1916 and a Morehouse B.A. in 1920, then going on to Rochester Theological Seminary.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Two Sisters in a Church Basement
clips.summary: He traces the schools' origins: an 1867 Augusta Baptist college that became Atlanta Baptist and then Morehouse, named for Henry Lyman Morehouse of the Northern Baptist Convention, and a women's college that Packard and Giles started in the basement of Friendship Baptist Church, becoming Spelman after their schoolmate, married to Laura Spelman Rockefeller, helped fund it.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: A Spelman Girl From Birmingham
clips.topic: Mother and the Atlanta University System
clips.summary: McKinney describes the 1929 reorganization that created the Atlanta University system, with Morehouse for men and Spelman for women keeping their autonomy, and introduces his mother, Annie Ruth Berry, the youngest of six who came from Birmingham to Spelman in 1916 and was among the few women who technically graduated from Morehouse before Spelman itself became a college.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Let Down Your Bucket Where You Are
clips.topic: Booker T. Washington and Owning Land
clips.summary: He explains how many Alabama Black families, including his maternal grandfather, took Booker T. Washington's counsel to let down your bucket and own land, and notes that when Dr. King marched from Selma to Montgomery, every stop sat on land owned by Black people, so King was never an intruder.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Raised by Black Puritans
clips.summary: McKinney tells how his mother, orphaned of her own mother at age two, was reared by a seventeen-year-old sister among women he calls Black puritans who stood for moral purity against the notion that any Black woman was prey for white men, and traces his mother's teaching at Jackson College and Roger Williams College up to her 1924 marriage and his father's first church in Flint.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: A Desire for Justice, Planted at Morehouse
clips.topic: Antioch and the Social Justice Ethic
clips.summary: He notes his father's 1928 call to Antioch Baptist Church, which he served thirty-four and a half years until his death in January 1963, and describes how Morehouse graduates, able to teach only at Black colleges, carried south an emphasis on social justice, shaped under President John Hope, so that the later emergence of Martin Luther King Jr. was no surprise.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Religion Functions on the Front End
clips.summary: McKinney defines the social gospel through the passage from Isaiah that Jesus read in Luke about being anointed to free the captives, names Walter Rauschenbusch, who taught at Rochester and died while his father was still a student, and notes Benjamin Elijah Mays of South Carolina becoming Morehouse president four years before he himself entered.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: A Fifteen-Year-Old Squirt Named King
