clips.summary: He recalls entering Morehouse in 1944 at seventeen, facing the draft at eighteen, and a freshman class of over four hundred against only fifty upperclassmen, in which the youngest was a little fellow from Atlanta nicknamed Squirt, the fifteen-year-old Martin Luther King Jr., who had skipped twelfth grade under a wartime Georgia program, and remembers King as a city student who drove a car and never won an oratorical contest.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Watching His Father Speak Out
clips.summary: McKinney describes growing up in Cleveland from eighteen months old, watching his father confront injustice rather than walk away from it, and the notable guests his father hosted, including A. Philip Randolph, Walter White of the NAACP, and Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, president of Howard and a Morehouse mentor to his father.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Don't Patronize Where You Cannot Work
clips.topic: Boycotts, War Industry, and the GI Bill
clips.summary: He recalls Cleveland's Future Outlook League boycotting milkmen and the transit system to force hiring, the wartime industrial boom and second great migration his parents were part of, his father's fifty-nine days in the Army in World War I, and the GI Bill that he calls the best the country ever had and that put both him and his brother through college.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: German Prisoners at the Tablecloth
clips.topic: Segregation in the Army Air Corps
clips.summary: Drafted into the Army Air Corps and trained at Sheppard Field, Texas, McKinney recounts a lunch stop in West Texas where Black soldiers in U.S. uniform ate in a colored dining room while German prisoners of war sat at tablecloths with silverware in the white dining room, a sight that left him asking how far it was to Cleveland and thinking about going AWOL.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Squadron F and a Dollar for a Salute
clips.topic: Daily Life on a Segregated Base
clips.summary: He describes the enlisted rule against saluting anyone under captain, officers who paid a dollar to the first enlisted man who saluted them, being assigned to Squadron F where all Black servicemen were grouped regardless of trade, segregated colored and white USOs, and hitchhiking troubles getting back to base past hostile Southern whites.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: From Mississippi for a Night
clips.summary: McKinney tells of faking a Mississippi origin to get a hitchhiking ride from a Southern white driver, the Army halting overseas shipment of Black soldiers, reenlistment pay-line indignities, and the rule that 1945 draftees be out by Christmas 1946, after which he resumed a pre-law track at Morehouse and went on to Colgate Rochester Divinity School.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: An Ambulance in the Valley or a Fence
clips.summary: He recounts how a required Bible course under George Kelsey turned him from law to ministry, with Kelsey's teaching that the law functions on the back end while religion functions on the front end and his image of whether to put an ambulance in the valley for those who fall or a fence at the cliff's edge, and remembers Benjamin Elijah Mays, president of Morehouse from 1941 to 1967, who told students to note the name and badge number of any white streetcar driver who wronged them and report it, modeling the witness for justice he taught.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Finding the Longest Extension Cord
clips.topic: Joe Louis as a Community Hero
clips.summary: He evokes how the community rallied around Joe Louis, with neighbors running extension cords to bring radios onto front porches so no one missed a fight, the same way they followed Jackie Robinson and Cleveland's Larry Doby, and recalls a four-hundred-pound visitor from Mississippi shouting down his aunt's porch when Louis knocked out Max Schmeling, a victory the Jewish community shared since Schmeling was one of Hitler's.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: On Your Mark in Front of Jesse Owens's House
clips.topic: Role Models and a Path to Ministry
clips.summary: McKinney recalls Jesse Owens's family living a few doors north in Cleveland, where children would line up at a starting crouch passing his house, the Cedar Avenue YMCA that reached nearly every Black youth, and the models who shaped his sense of ministry, his father, Mays, and Mordecai Johnson, in a father's church that at its height held over three thousand members and hosted Adam Clayton Powell Jr.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: His Last Trip Anywhere Alone
clips.summary: He tells of inviting Martin Luther King Jr. to speak on the brotherhood of the church in 1961, a visit King told him was the last trip he would ever take by himself, certain that assassins were out there after the near misses and escapes he had already had.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: We Don't Want Your Money
clips.summary: With the 1962 Seattle World's Fair making his own facilities too small, McKinney recounts arranging to use the large downtown First Presbyterian Church, then receiving a letter barring the event for supposedly skipping protocol, and a meeting where a white-haired lawyer heading the church council coldly forbade it and offered to reimburse his advertising, which he refused, vowing to tell the world the truth and walking straight down the hill from Eighth and Madison to another building where the event was successfully held.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: An Apology Thirty-Eight Years Late
clips.summary: McKinney recalls almost tossing a typed envelope just before his 1998 retirement that turned out to be First Presbyterian apologizing for the injustice done decades earlier, the younger minister who came in person, and his own reply that white people owe an apology not for owning slaves but for the white privilege by which certain things come automatically.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Better Late Than Never
clips.summary: He argues that reparations, modeled on the apologies and payments made to Japanese American families interned after Pearl Harbor, whose seized farmland he could see from the hills, are part of a prophetic healing that names injustice and makes forgiveness possible, and responds to James Forman's 1969 Black Manifesto call for reparations as part of people's eyes opening to four hundred years of slavery.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Illegal to Settle in Oregon
clips.summary: McKinney insists no part of the country escaped the poison of racism, citing an Oregon territory law making it illegal for Black people to permanently settle, not repealed until 1926, and sketching the Louisiana Purchase that Jefferson bought from Napoleon after the Haitian revolt, the carving of some twenty states from it, and a stretch of Highway 99 north of Seattle once called Confederate Highway.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Fasten Your Seat Belt
clips.topic: George Washington Bush and a Sober Heckler
clips.summary: He tells of George Washington Bush leading a wagon train west from Missouri, barred from settling in Oregon and crossing to Fort Vancouver toward Puget Sound, and of a drunken heckler at a Washington event who demanded to know what Blacks had contributed to the state, whom McKinney promised to leave sober by the time he finished, noting the slow bumpkin enforcement that escorted Black settlers county to county.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Saved From Becoming British Columbia
clips.topic: Bush, the Pig War, and the Border
clips.summary: McKinney recounts Bush's company crossing the Columbia to settle near present-day Olympia amid complaints the neighborhood was changing, the boundary dispute settled at the 49th parallel that stranded Point Roberts so its schoolchildren bus sixty miles a day, the Pig War that nearly brought conflict with Britain, and his retort to the heckler that without this Black man crossing the border, Washington might have become part of British Columbia.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Where Your Members Migrated From
clips.topic: Arriving at Mount Zion and Migration Patterns
clips.summary: Called in late November 1957 and beginning the first Sunday of February 1958, McKinney offers younger ministers his method of learning where a congregation migrated from, tracing how Providence drew Virginians, Cleveland drew Georgians and Alabamians, Chicago drew Mississippians, and Seattle drew people from west of the Mississippi, Louisiana, Texas, Arkansas, and Oklahoma.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: The Juneteenth Factor
clips.topic: Emancipation, Juneteenth, and a Post-Racial Myth
clips.summary: He explains the Juneteenth factor he first met in Army basic training in Texas, contrasting the January 1, 1863, Emancipation Proclamation and the watch-meeting services that counted down to it with the news not reaching Texas until June 19, 1865, and links the lag, and modern talk of Texas secession over Obama, to a Civil War he frames as less about freeing the enslaved than breaking the union.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: A Vein of Coal Under the Pews
clips.topic: How Black People Came to the Area
clips.summary: McKinney notes that before World War I only two Army forts, Fort Lewis and one in New Jersey, let interracial couples live there, dates his church's 1890 founding to a year after Washington statehood, and traces the region's coal economy, the Welsh and Scottish miners buried young in a mass grave after an explosion, the Black scab laborers brought to Roslyn to break the union and to Kennydale, and the transcontinental railroads that drew people west.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: They Could Tell From My Voice
clips.summary: He acknowledges that beneath Seattle's better surface lay real prejudice, recalling restaurants that had to be reminded he might refuse their tainted food and the difficulty finding housing, with landlords asking if he was colored over the phone, until his wife Carol, a purist in speech more English than the British, would call and a different welcome would await when they arrived.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: The March on Frederick & Nelson
clips.topic: Opening Jobs and the Founding of CACRC
clips.summary: McKinney describes marching on the downtown department store Frederick and Nelson, now the Nordstrom building, to open it to Black employment, names the Urban League and NAACP as allies with CORE arriving later, and recounts helping organize in 1963 the Central Area Civil Rights Committee, a coalition of groups in which Reverend John Adams, later an AME bishop, was the most successful leader.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Too Close in the South, Too High in the North
clips.topic: Two Fronts and a Slow State Board
clips.summary: He recounts a school strike and stay-at-home over segregated, inferior schools under the Central Area Civil Rights Committee, working on two fronts in harmony with the South while fighting local racism, captures the difference in the saying that the South did not care how close Blacks got so long as they did not get too high while the North did not care how high so long as they did not get too close, and notes a slow state anti-discrimination board and a once-critical congregant who was first to apply when the Frederick and Nelson march succeeded.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Too Radical or Not Radical Enough
clips.summary: McKinney reflects that some always think you too radical and others not radical enough, and describes the late-1960s rise of more militant groups including a Black Panther chapter, the strained relations between them and the ministers some Panthers saw as targets of their anger, his sense of being played one group against another, and his belief that the Panthers could have been more effective than they were.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Park the Cars and Babysit the Children
clips.topic: Buying Land and a Church Credit Union
clips.summary: Asked about Liberty Bank, the Opportunities Industrialization Center, and the Samuel Berry McKinney Manor, he begins with his drive to buy land so Mount Zion could build with parking and child care to grow rather than plateau, contrasts it with gentrification surrounding a sister church, and tells how, refused by local banks, the congregation established a credit union and built its sanctuary with help from the American Baptist Home Mission Society.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Reagan Pulled the Rug Out
clips.topic: OIC, Liberty Bank, and Institution Building
clips.summary: McKinney recounts building the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center on Twenty-Second and Jackson, founded in Leon Sullivan's movement to train and educate, whose funding President Reagan cut until Representative Jim McDermott had the state take it over on the verge of bankruptcy, and his part in organizing Liberty Bank at Twenty-Fourth and Union, framing his career as protesting and building alike.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Community When You No Longer Have Neighborhood
clips.summary: He frames a central problem of how to have community once a neighborhood disperses, looking to how Jewish and Asian communities stay united while scattered, recalls asking his congregation how many lived within a mile and finding the crowd thinned the closer in he asked, traces the two old Black pockets off Madison and Jackson that grew into the Central Area, and notes that the largest number of Black people now moving in come from across the African continent.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Africa Is Not a Country
clips.topic: A Tense Meeting on Race and Tone
clips.summary: McKinney recounts a man at a meeting expecting him to be crushed by the news that African Americans were now the third-largest non-white group, and his retort that what unites Hispanic people is not ethnicity but tone, so there are Black Hispanics, that the comparison lumped all Blacks against single segments of others, and that Africa is a continent with as many differences among its peoples as among whites.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: An Offer I Couldn't Refuse
clips.topic: Selling Property and a Wife's Home
clips.summary: He tells of warning Central Area residents not to sell their valuable property, only to be asked by one who left whether he had not given that advice, answering that he too got an offer he could not refuse, and recounts moving in 1958 to Madrona for forty-two years before his wife found this house, which she marked with new floors and windows, the wife who passed in August.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: They Did Not Count on the Coming Together
clips.summary: Reflecting on progress across his lifetime, McKinney rejects the idea that Obama's election settles everything, contrasting the reaction against Obama with the tolerance shown a Bush he calls the worst president, faulting Obama for bending too far toward people who would rather see the country suffer than credit him, and warning of the old trick of playing Blacks and Latinos against each other that did not reckon with communities coming together.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Praying That Sense Will Prevail
clips.summary: He closes with cautious hope, supposing the country headed toward a woman president and that Hillary Clinton would do well, praying that the nuts and fools who feel their lives depend on guns will come to their senses and that uncommon sense will prevail, as the interviewers thank him and note that the material will go to the Library of Congress.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Lincoln's Friend Leonard Swett
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in his home in Sewanee, Tennessee, in June 2013, Scott Bates opens with his Evanston childhood near Lake Michigan, his 1923 birth into a family of Chicago lawyers descended from his great-uncle Leonard Swett, a friend of Abraham Lincoln, and his delight that dry Evanston drove him to Carleton and dry Tennessee ran with the moonshine that once filled Al Capone's Chicago trucks.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Lying on Omaha Beach
clips.summary: Drafted in 1942 and trained in French through the Army's ASTP program at the University of Chicago, Bates describes landing under fire at Omaha Beach with no air cover, waking in a field hospital with the beach sand still in his hair, the 1,300 bombers that reduced Saint-Lo to rubble, and the drive to Avranches with Patton's Third Army as he became a French interpreter expecting to help run occupied Germany.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: The Horror Houses of Romilly
clips.summary: Stationed near Romilly for a winter while Eisenhower kept his unit out of the Battle of the Bulge to run Germany, Bates recounts supervising the legal military brothels, the stark contrast between the two regulated houses serving white troops and the many unsupervised illegal houses left to Black troops, and the disease that followed.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: The Worst Was Not D-Day
clips.summary: Bates describes Avranches as the key point between Brittany and Normandy, first glimpsing Mont-Saint-Michel across the bay, enduring three weeks of Hitler's counterattack and dive-bombing he calls worse than D-Day, and sheltering in a convent with a sergeant, two PFCs, and a French girl whose head had been shaved for sleeping with Germans while the enemy targeted the bridge below the town.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Walking the MP Beat in French
clips.topic: Race in the Army and Back Home
clips.summary: Returning to race, Bates describes how the Army separated Black and white troops to the town's segregated brothels after fights with white Southern soldiers, his nights on military police patrol with a Black soldier from Lincoln College discussing seventeenth-century English literature, and the Black classmates and Howard Street drinking of his Chicago boyhood that had made integration familiar.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: A One-Year Job That Lasted a Lifetime
clips.summary: Bates traces his path back to Carleton, a doctorate at the University of Wisconsin on the GI Bill, and a one-year French post at Sewanee, the University of the South, a white boys' college of 400 in a Tennessee town of 400, recalling a boyhood stay in Orangeburg, South Carolina and a Black maid in Evanston, and how the job became permanent.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Seminaries Across the Street
clips.summary: Serving on the board of trustees of the heavily Episcopal, roughly ninety percent segregationist University of the South, Bates describes the fight to desegregate after closing Black Episcopal seminaries had to send would-be priests somewhere, naming Seabury-Western and Garrett seminaries in Evanston and the theologian Rosemary Radford Ruether.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: From the Fellowship to the War Resisters
clips.summary: Bates recounts the Fellowship of Reconciliation's anti-war work, reading the pacifist theologian John Swomley, then moving to the War Resisters League in Greenwich Village, and an activist he calls John Mac whose daily broadcasts tracked anti-war and socialist action across America and Egypt.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: A Calendar of Anti-War Poems
clips.summary: He tells of a long-distance call from Bayard Rustin asking to use the anti-war poetry he had collected since high school, the resulting War Resisters League calendars that ran a poem each year, and the published collection of poems of war resistance that sold thirteen thousand copies and was preached from pulpits across the country.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: A Poem a Week, Free to All
clips.summary: Bates describes running a poem a week in the local Sewanee paper, first typed and published by his wife and later carried by the Sewanee Mountain Messenger, choosing out-of-copyright anti-war and holiday verse handed out free so no one could pursue him for copyright.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Hitched to the Sewanee Review
clips.summary: He describes Sewanee as a liberal arts school open to New Yorker and progressive poems, and an English department that, though housing many Unitarians and former Episcopal priests, was tied to the Sewanee Review, the oldest literary magazine in the country, where his own poems occasionally appeared.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: V. Gene Robinson and the First Black Graduate
clips.summary: Bates recalls graduate V. Gene Robinson, the first openly gay Episcopal bishop, the campus gay-straight alliance, and the long board fights to admit women in 1969 and Black students through the decade, including a top honors student from Alabama who became a federal judge as the first Black graduate, as priests and alumni left the desegregating church.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: A Union School in the Hills
clips.summary: Asked for background, Bates explains that Highlander Folk School was the union organizing school of the South, that he had never heard of it before arriving and found in it his life's dedication, and points to the University of North Carolina account of how the unions founded it on the social gospel as far back as 1905.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Myles Horton at Union Seminary
clips.summary: He sketches Myles Horton, a sharecropper's son from East Tennessee who rose from a country college to Union Theological Seminary in New York to study under Reinhold Niebuhr, alongside a radical Christian educator Bates struggles to name, the two figures who most shaped Horton's thinking.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: From Jane Addams to a Danish Model
clips.summary: Bates recounts how Niebuhr sent Horton to Jane Addams in Chicago and to study the Danish folk schools, and how Horton combined them with a retired Memphis woman educator's mountain home to start a folk school in Grundy County, one of the poorest counties in the country, near Tracy City and Monteagle.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Three in the Morning with a Bouncer
clips.summary: He describes Grundy County's moonshine and his doctor, a 350-pound man who at thirteen had been a nightclub bouncer, telling stories at three in the morning during Bates's hospital stay, and frames the wooded mountain with its deer and panthers as a Shangri-La for a Michigan-raised bird watcher.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Victor Hugo Against the Gun
clips.summary: Bates connects his leftist French literature teaching, including a Victor Hugo poem against gun control he still places in local papers, to his War Resisters League stance and the small anti-Vietnam movement he led among his students, naming the poet Richard Tillinghast, a good student who later turned conservative.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Kids Running the Discussion
clips.summary: He recalls his first Highlander meeting, where white University of Chattanooga and Knoxville students working to integrate the University of Tennessee met Black students from Talladega and ran the whole discussion themselves while Myles Horton stayed out of it, and he describes Horton's teasing, martini-fueled, storytelling style that Studs Terkel prized.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Greensboro and Nashville Come Together
clips.summary: Bates describes the Highlander meeting where the Greensboro and Nashville sit-in students came together and Horton told them they had to do it themselves, then recalls a later three-thousand-person march down State Street in Chicago after the Cicero riots, shouting down Mayor Daley at Grant Park where Obama would later speak.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: We'll Take It, But Not Publicly
clips.summary: He recounts a national NAACP meeting where Roger Wilkins told him the organization would take what SNCC and the Southern groups offered but would not recognize them publicly, and how he started a local NAACP chapter under Septima Clark, Highlander's education director, against the grain of a cautious Black establishment.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Half the Church Walked Out
clips.summary: Bates describes Septima Clark desegregating the Sewanee chapel, two Black women walking to the front while half the congregation rose and left for good, and the school-desegregation court case in Franklin County where Avon Williams Jr. returned every week for two years before an armed courtroom the police would not let erupt.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: License Plates Sent to the FBI
clips.topic: The John Birch Society and Surveillance
clips.summary: He recounts Southern stalling after the Supreme Court ruling, a millionaire student organizing the John Birch Society around Sewanee, a restaurant owner taking down Highlander visitors' license plates for the FBI, and a sit-in at Tubby's beer house where the agents investigated the activists rather than the segregationists.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: The Communist Training School Billboard
clips.summary: Bates tells of bringing in the Nashville lawyers Avon Williams and Looby for the desegregation case, the Sewanee hero Mrs. Fowler who ran the NAACP chapter, and the 1957 Highlander photograph of Martin Luther King and Rosa Parks turned into billboards branding the school a Communist training center, with Bates himself visible against the wall.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: A Stone House Burned by the Parish
clips.summary: He describes how the Highlander land was sold off cheap and gentrified into a summer suburb after the school was run out, the cemetery across the road where the Horton family and May Justice are buried, the highway marker finally raised, and Myles Horton's mountain-stone house burned down by a parish whose minister begged God's pardon for it.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: The Red Dean and Seditious Books
clips.topic: Highlander's Place and a Long Feud
clips.summary: Bates reflects that Highlander went unmentioned for years until the Obama era restored its history, recounts his twenty-year campus feud with an anti-gay, anti-Black dean called the Red Dean, and ties his French teaching to seditious banned books like the Marquis de Sade kept on reserve in the library.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: A Poem Nobody Will Publish
clips.topic: Banned French Verse and His Library
clips.summary: He describes the socialist, Christian-socialist radicalism behind a French anti-gun poem he says The Nation and others refuse to print, and a passage from a writer answering Sade that remains banned in France yet circulates in underground book series and Paris bookstores, then offers to show the interviewers his cabin library.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Places That Trained and Sent Them On
clips.topic: Aldon Morris and the Social Gospel
clips.summary: Bates engages the sociologist Aldon Morris, whose book calls Highlander and Hull House movement halfway houses that trained and inspired organizers, distinguishes him from the Episcopal activist John B. Morris, and explains why the social-gospel Horton distrusted official bodies like the NAACP and went to the grassroots.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Thoreau Fetched From Sewanee
clips.summary: Returning to the Highlander meeting that preceded the Shaw University gathering where SNCC was founded, Bates recalls his student Richard Tillinghast driving back to Sewanee to fetch Thoreau's Civil Disobedience for John Lewis, Stokely Carmichael, and the others, Horton's line that they had to do it themselves because the whites would not stick with them, and the no-guns rule that turned away armed Chicago Black Panthers.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: A Busload to Johns Island
clips.topic: Esau Jenkins and the Charleston Coast
clips.summary: Bates describes a Knoxville Black district where he, Esau Jenkins, and Andrew Young stayed at a little church, a bus trip taking Black children from Johns Island to the white-only resort islands off Charleston where white youths jeered and Septima Clark chided their manners, staying with Guy Carawan near Charleston, a closely guarded Martin Luther King lecture, and an all-day beach meeting circled by police cars.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Still Singing the Same Songs
clips.topic: A Lifetime on the Highlander Board
clips.summary: Bates closes on his time on the Highlander board since 1958 and as president through the 1960s, the field meetings in Jackson and Memphis he reported back, how he ended the written minutes so members spoke truth at oral meetings, and his sense that it remains the same freedom movement, still singing the same songs, now joining gay and Black action, with a Southern Echo leader who opens every meeting with a song.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Picked Them Up in the 1870 Census
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Albany, Georgia, in September 2011, Shirley Miller Sherrod opens on her family in Baker County, Georgia, whose roots she traced to the 1870 census, moving from sharecropping to buying land in what became known as the Hawkins family settlement, where her grandmother Hawkins and grandfather Julius Miller began with 31 acres and left each of their children at least 31 acres.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Gator Ran Baker County
clips.summary: Sherrod describes Baker County's reputation and Sheriff L. Warren "Gator" Johnson, who controlled everything in the county and ran a speed trap where a Black driver could pay or lose his life, and recalls her first segregated school in army barracks before a new building was built as Georgia's answer to separate but equal.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: He Wanted a Son
clips.summary: As the oldest of five girls, Sherrod recalls her father wanting a son and giving each daughter a boy's nickname, calling her Bill, and learning from her best friend that her mother was pregnant again because he had handed out cigars at the store, a baby who would be born two months after he was murdered, and her family's central place in their church.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Cucumber Stain on Your Hands
clips.summary: She contrasts children whose families owned land with those living on plantations like Robert Woodruff's Ichauway and the Mellon family's Pineland, and remembers being picked up from school on cucumber days to go home and pick, a hard task she came to see as a source of pride in working land her family owned.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Superintendent's Wife Took Over
clips.summary: Sherrod recalls the worst buses that broke down and caught fire and the hand-me-down textbooks with torn-out pages from the white school, and the day the superintendent's wife walked into her French class, made the teacher feel like nothing, and drilled the students until they pronounced words with a Southern twang, sharpening her resolve to leave the South.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Black Dentist and the Movement
clips.summary: She describes traveling only as far as Texas through the New Homemakers of America, the segregated parallel to the white Future Homemakers, and her family's awareness of the Albany Movement: members stopping at her church to take up collections, and a Black dentist in Albany who talked with her father about eventually getting the movement started in Baker County.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: They Bombed the Houses
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts her father deciding he should register to vote as a taxpayer, and earlier efforts in the 1950s by relatives including Uncle Josh, Benjamin T. Coney, Ike King, and Carl Broadway, whose houses and cars were bombed and on whose place moonshine was planted, and how a Black man was told he could only borrow to build a house of blocks or wood, never brick.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: He Came to Get the Cows
clips.summary: Sherrod describes her father's happiness in early 1965 with a new baby expected and a son's room being built, and recounts the morning a white farmer named Cal Hall stopped them on the way to church about a cow, leading to a meeting at the pasture the next day where Cal Hall tried to claim five or six cows that were not his.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: We'll Just Go to Court
clips.topic: The Murder and the Grand Jury
clips.summary: She tells how, when her father said they would settle the cattle argument in court and turned toward his truck, he was shot, lingering ten days before he died on March 25, 1965, and how the white grand jury refused to indict, a later civil trial in which attorney C.B. King exposed jurors' contempt for Black testimony still ending in the verdict they expected.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: A Cross Burned at the House
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts confronting the Gator while trying to register and being pushed out of the courthouse, then how an uncle from Calhoun helped her enroll at Fort Valley State after her father's death left the family without support, and the call two weeks in that white men had burned a cross at the house, surrounded by armed Black men the movement had organized.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: You Can Stay in the South to Work for Change
clips.topic: The Night of Her Father's Death
clips.summary: She describes the night of her father's death, going alone into a room to pray as the house filled with mourners, and the sudden calm that came when she resolved to give up her dream of the North and stay in the South to work for change, a commitment that became clear at her first mass meeting watching plantation people refuse to be afraid.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Five Carloads to Washington
clips.topic: The Trip to Testify in Washington
clips.summary: Sherrod names early movement figures including Charles Sherrod and recounts planning a Washington trip to testify before Congress and march at the Justice Department about Baker County, leaving Thankful Baptist Church in a caravan that routed east through Mitchell County to evade the Gator, with Aunt Josie talking her way past a state trooper by saying she was taking children to see the White House, and reflects on the Justice Department's limits and the injunction the summer's work finally won against the Gator.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Whites Did Not Have to Leave
clips.summary: Sherrod describes returning to enroll at Albany State around 1967 to be near the work, and how when Stokely Carmichael took over SNCC from John Lewis and said all whites had to leave under the Black power movement, she and Charles Sherrod disagreed, broke with SNCC, and incorporated their own organization to keep the white workers, the Southwest Georgia Project.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: They Set the House on Fire
clips.summary: She paints Reverend Charles Sherrod as fearless despite beatings and threats, recounts marrying him about a year after they met in 1967, and tells how, while printing a newsletter, white men took a stuttering boy named Grady Christian and set their house on fire, after which a flat tire on the dark road to Baker County left them at the mercy of white men who, not recognizing Sherrod, changed the tire.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: He Spoke, Then Took It Back
clips.summary: Sherrod describes her mother's fear yet growing strength, and her shock when, eleven years after her husband's murder, her mother ran for office against the backdrop of the Gator's retirement and his son Scrut's campaign, recounting an election-night moment when the Gator, realizing he had greeted Charles Sherrod, took it back and stared him down with a gun on his hip until his son pulled him away.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: He Refused to Leave
clips.topic: A Chance to Move to Kentucky
clips.summary: She recalls a hard stretch around the late 1980s after the loss of the land at New Communities, when a friend who directed the Presbyterian Hunger Program suggested Charles Sherrod take a church job in Louisville, Kentucky, where Sherrod could have continued her Federation work, and how, though no one locally would hire him because of his civil rights history, he refused to leave, saying he had work to do in the area.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Seven People Went to Israel
clips.summary: Sherrod explains how families kicked off white-owned land for enrolling children in white schools led to the idea of building a community, with seven people studying the Israeli kibbutz and returning with a model that became New Communities, an ambitious plan to buy land across the country, beginning with a 6,000-acre plot, and a planning grant from the Office of Economic Opportunity.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: We Could Dream the Community
clips.summary: She describes the charrette sessions where future residents and experts planned housing, villages, education, health, industry, and a rail spur, and the committee structure for the farm, run not as a plantation but as something new and equal, where she and Charles Sherrod deliberately did not take the big house, holding the land as a trust owned not by him or her but by all of them.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Money From the Government
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts how Slater King, C.B. King's brother, arranged interim financing on the expectation of federal money, but when Governor Lester Maddox blocked the OEO funds the project faced foreclosure, held together for years by Prudential's first mortgage and creative financing, and explains the choice of the Moshav rather than the kibbutz model, since the kibbutz kept children from their families.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Way Ahead of Our Time
clips.summary: She describes the innovations at New Communities that others tried to use to divide them by warning residents they would own nothing: muscadine grapes no one else grew in the 1970s, a 75-brood-sow operation, and a farmers' market on Highway 19 with an old-fashioned smokehouse and sugar cane mill known for cured meats and sugar cane syrup.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Learn Everything You Can Learn
clips.summary: Sherrod tells how, needing skills New Communities could not pay for, she took night business classes at Albany State toward an MBA through Valdosta State, organized the first day care center in Baker, and pulled farmers together to elect the first Black person to the ASCS committee by dividing up the voter list, learning to do everything herself while working.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: A Loan Over My Dead Body
clips.summary: She recounts the three-year fight for emergency farm money after droughts beginning around 1976, when a county supervisor said they would get a loan over his dead body, and how farmers home administration tied up all of New Communities' available assets, four and a half million in worth, before moving in for the kill, leading her to apply to the Rural Development Leadership Network to pursue a master's.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: They Pushed the Buildings Over
clips.summary: Sherrod describes attending the Leadership Network institute at UC Davis from May to June 1985, missing her daughter's graduation but avoiding the courthouse sale, joining the Federation of Southern Cooperatives' staff that July, and how after the land sold in September the new owner dug holes and pushed the houses, smokehouse, and sugar cane mill into them, leaving her to pour everything into helping other Black farmers.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Will You Put That in Writing
clips.summary: Working the issue of land loss with the Federation, Sherrod recounts learning the regulations better than the agency, and a confrontation in Blakely, Georgia, where she stopped a county supervisor threatening to foreclose on Marvel Smith's farm and home, demanding he put it in writing until he refused, a fight that helped push the Credit Act of 1987 and eventually drove the supervisor from the agency.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Slick Lawyer Out of Texas
clips.summary: Sherrod describes deciding with the Federation, the Land Loss Prevention Project, and Farmers' Legal Action Group to file suit, first to force USDA to produce complaint records a judge threatened jail to obtain, then preparing six test cases that a lawyer out of Texas pulled away by signing the farmers to a retainer, and the choice the Pigford class faced between trial and settling given many farmers lacked full documentation.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Track A and Track B
clips.summary: She explains the settlement's two classes: Track A claimants needed little proof and would receive fifty thousand dollars, though the government later taxed the whole amount and wrote off debt only for the single cited instance of discrimination, while Track B farmers with more documentation got a day in court, and how of roughly 22,000 claimants who heard in time, only about 180 filed Track B in what she calls Pigford One.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: We Could File a Claim
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts the night, driving from Eufaula, Alabama, when she suddenly realized that because New Communities was farming in 1981 it too could file a Pigford claim, rushing home to tell her husband, and how in the Lee County courthouse they finally saw the documentation that big plantations had been lent the money denied to them, the buyer who paid a million for their land lent $950,000 three weeks later.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Wife of the Chief Adjudicator
clips.summary: She describes the 2002 hearing where the hearing officer was the wife of Pigford's chief adjudicator, an incompetent DOJ lawyer who, when the farmers home witness began telling the truth, called a break and brought him back lying, and how, though they appeared to win in the room, in October they received a letter ruling against them, which Sherrod attributes to politics over the cost of early Pigford payouts.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: She Said It's Twelve
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts appealing to the monitor, a friend who recused herself, and a reviewer who spent four years producing a fifty- or sixty-page document on the miscarriage of justice, then the chief adjudicator's three-year silence until a July 2009 call telling her they had won twelve million dollars, a decision she says she cries over each time she reads its point-by-point reasoning.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Why Would We Want an Antebellum Home
clips.summary: With the award the dream continued, and Sherrod tells of looking at a hunting place before a second property with an antebellum home built in 1851 and restored at a cost said to be three million, which she opposed while her husband and others favored it, leading her to bring in an outside expert who recommended the antebellum place over the one she preferred.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Wealthiest Slave Owner in Georgia
clips.summary: She recounts negotiating the price from a listed 21 million down to 4.5 million with equipment for the place now called Cypress Pond Plantation, and learning it had belonged to the wealthiest slave owner in Georgia, who held about a thousand slaves across three places and 150 there, and that Jefferson Davis stayed there planning Civil War activities, a history of layered irony.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: It Was More About Being Poor
clips.summary: Asked to reflect, Sherrod tells how her work led white farmers to seek her help, the Spooners being the first white family, and how, after working through what happened to her father in order to help a white farmer, she came to see the issue as more about being poor than about race, a lesson she has spent twenty-five years urging people in the area to act on by working together.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: You Have to Accept Training
clips.summary: She recounts organizing a Baker County feeder-pig cooperative whose farmers insisted they already knew how to raise hogs, and the visit with the Heifer Project man to a farmer who bragged he raised his on concrete, where they found hogs amid broken glass and contaminated water and crippled pigs under a peanut trailer, after which she started the training herself and never had the problem again.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: They Said It Was His Story
clips.topic: Ben and Jerry's and the Credit
clips.summary: Sherrod describes the commercial kitchen and Head Start she built in the old 1957 school building, the Ben and Jerry's effort to fight Black land loss by buying pecans, where processors refused to handle Black farmers' nuts until the company found its own supplier, and how she had farmers save half the premium toward a women-run facility while a man named Rapunzel Page was widely credited for work that was hers, closing on the present effort to bring more women back into agriculture as the session ends.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Founders of Tel Aviv
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Berlin, California, in March, Steve McNichols opens on his origins, maternal grandparents who emigrated from Eastern Europe to Palestine in 1890 under a Rothschild-sponsored movement and were among the families who founded Tel Aviv, where his mother was born before the family moved to Geneva and she became a classically trained violinist who toured with Joseph Szigeti.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Only White Kid on 133rd Street
clips.topic: A Father and a Tough Neighborhood
clips.summary: Born in New York in 1939, McNichols describes his father, raised in a tough St. Louis neighborhood by a mother murdered when the father was three, a smart, self-educated man, and his own childhood as the only white kid on 133rd Street in a poor Puerto Rican neighborhood while attending the Dalton School on full scholarship alongside children of the wealthiest families in the country.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Get the Hell Out
clips.summary: He recalls being conscious of race and class while shuttling between a private school and the ghetto, his mother driving him to succeed so he would escape her kind of life, and his father, who worked an animal-lab job then taught himself electronics to become a top trainee repairman for an X-ray corporation despite peers holding engineering degrees.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: A Breakdown in the Time-Life Lobby
clips.topic: His Mother's Illness and His Own
clips.summary: McNichols recounts his parents separating, his mother prospering by teaching French to Time-Life employees before developing psychiatric symptoms and a breakdown in the Time-Life lobby that left her institutionalized, his own depression and suicidal ideation from age fifteen, and the bipolar condition undiagnosed until age sixty-five when lithium finally lifted it.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Play Tennis, Go to UCLA
clips.summary: He describes the years after his mother's hospitalization, drifting apart from his father, and, asked at eighteen what he would do after Stuyvesant, declaring he would go to Los Angeles to play tennis and attend UCLA, then driving to Denver and on to LA where he took a room at the YMCA and put himself through UCLA.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: They Blew the Operation
clips.summary: McNichols tells how, years later, his mother's untreated brain tumor left her unable to walk, and how surgeons removed it but failed to replace it so the walls of the brain collapsed and she died half an hour after the operation, the end of a cultured, educated woman who he says never had a chance.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: A CIA Front for Students
clips.summary: He recounts entering UCLA in 1958, feeling alien among middle-class students, plunging into campus politics with figures like Howard Berman and Henry Waxman, and rising in the National Student Association, only to find its international Cold War work against a communist student union was a CIA-funded network whose head Bob Kiley had been an NSA president.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: I Wanted to Be a Freedom Rider
clips.summary: McNichols describes being electrified by news of the sit-ins, which reached Los Angeles through an East-to-West pipeline before the LA Times covered the movement, his disbelief that Black people could be refused service at lunch counters, and his and fellow UCLA students' move into demonstrations outside Kresge's stores.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Two Black Attorneys in Houston
clips.summary: He recalls struggling to get apathetic UCLA students involved, then explains how the Houston Freedom Ride was arranged by two driving Black civil rights attorneys in Houston, one named George Washington Jr., who challenged the local Black establishment and formed their own club, joined by a third figure he describes as a businessman and fence who put up the cash.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Send Us Some Freedom Riders
clips.summary: McNichols tells how the Houston organizers called James Farmer, head of CORE and an original Freedom Rider, offering to cover costs and provide counsel if riders were sent, how Los Angeles CORE took up the call, and how he, a UCLA student, learned of it and volunteered for what became the Houston Freedom Ride.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: They Never Showed Up
clips.summary: He describes the LA CORE director, challenged by ambitious rivals, two of whom loudly vowed to join the Houston ride but vanished from the platform, and the two days of training in someone's home that focused entirely on being attacked by a white mob at the station and offered almost no help for what to do after arrest.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Wrong Railroad
clips.summary: McNichols recounts how a CORE member bought tickets on Southern Pacific without knowing which line served Houston, that the destination station there had replaced its lunch counter with vending machines, and how a group of eleven riders, including a Berkeley man elected to lead them, set out for Houston as part of the West Coast student movement.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: An FBI Agent Joins the Car
clips.summary: He describes the train trip as uneventful except for a large, well-dressed FBI agent who boarded at El Paso, whom the Black porters had warned them about, and who, claiming to work for the railroad, sat near the riders for the rest of the journey to Houston.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Houston Was the Target
clips.summary: McNichols tells of arriving in Houston to find no lunch counter, being picked up by the attorneys and the local progressive student association, and clarifies that, contrary to those who think they were always bound for Jackson, Houston itself was the target where they aimed to challenge Texas segregation statutes and be arrested.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Soul Food and a Held Door
clips.topic: The Local Allies and First Sit-In
clips.summary: He recalls his first soul-food meal at a Black restaurant after being picked up, joining forces with the progressive student association, and the union-station lunch counter where the racist owner held the door shut against the demonstrators until he stepped away so he could have them arrested.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Waitresses Were All for Us
clips.summary: McNichols describes the eighteen well-dressed demonstrators in coats and ties forming a line, walking in to sit at the counter and a booth when the owner stepped aside, and the white waitresses who whispered support but could not say so openly for fear of losing their jobs as a crowd gathered and police were called.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: I Wasn't Nonviolent
clips.summary: He recalls a hostile crowd and threats outside, then reflects that while CORE was genuinely committed to nonviolence, he himself, raised in tough New York, was not, adhering to nonviolent standards out of devotion to CORE and the Freedom Ride rather than conviction.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Singing in the Bar Car
clips.topic: The Ride's Spirit and the Arrest
clips.summary: McNichols tells how, warned to stay out of the bar car and not sing freedom songs, the riders were singing there within fifteen minutes, his growing fear before the action, a local student calming him, and the moment of arrest when one rider's defiant announcement set even the threatening white onlookers laughing before they were driven carefully to jail.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Sorted Into the Tanks
clips.summary: He describes the booking at the county jail, where prisoners were pulled from the holding cells and placed in segregated tanks, white men, white women, and Black prisoners each in their own, the riders booked around two in the morning, and a fellow rider named Steve talking back to the processing officer in a way McNichols feared would invite a beating.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: One of Those Nigger Lovers
clips.summary: McNichols recounts being put in the white male misdemeanor tank and ordered by a deputy to tell the prisoners what they were arrested for, his naive announcement of unlawful assembly, a prisoner's slur, and being quickly surrounded by tough men and walked to the back of a tank of a hundred and seven prisoners and told to lie down and take off their shoes.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Lie Still and Take It
clips.summary: He describes the beating once they lay down, a sadistic prisoner pounding Steve's ribs and others kicking him, a man landing on McNichols and beating him until he lay still as his ghetto youth had taught him, while the youngest rider, Joe Stevenson, flipped onto his stomach and was overlooked, the prisoners then circling and threatening through the night.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Dawn in the Tank
clips.summary: McNichols catalogs the injuries, his own hurt knee, a two-inch wound behind one rider's ear, another kicked in the eye, and explains the guards had told the prisoners to beat them, then describes the slow coming of dawn, coffee and breakfast arriving, and the riders being gradually allowed to stand among the hundred and seven prisoners.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: A Prisoner Offered His Fork
clips.summary: He recalls being treated decently once breakfast came, prisoners approaching individually and some claiming to support them though they would not when it counted, and one inmate who offered McNichols a carved fork as a gesture of sympathy that he hesitated to accept, a sign that some were watching what the riders were doing.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Commander Called Garland
clips.summary: McNichols explains CORE's plan was to bail out rather than serve time, which they did after two days as their lives were threatened, and describes the misdemeanor tank ruled by a clique under a head convict called the commander, Garland, in cell number one, and his lieutenant Omer Street who roamed the overcrowded tank as the strong arm of the guards.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Most Dangerous Man in the Jail
clips.summary: He recounts the prisoners insisting the riders were lucky to be alive, and the most dangerous inmate, a man arrested for murder who had cut three attackers in a felony tank, kept in solitary six days a week and brought to the misdemeanor tank once a week so he would not go crazy, deferring even to Garland after a brief challenge before being returned to isolation.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Shoes for Protection
clips.summary: McNichols describes other criminals in the tank, a violent pimp and professional thieves, the tense second night when the sadist again wanted to beat them, and how Omer Street, who had asked McNichols for shoes, recognized his fear and struck a quid pro quo, announcing he wanted nothing to happen to the riders in exchange for the shoes.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: He Took the Article Personally
clips.topic: An LA Times Story Reaches the Jail
clips.summary: He recounts Garland confronting him for being called a serenade, and a copy of an LA Times article about the arrest reaching the tank that Garland took personally, fearing it identified him, until the attorney George Washington Jr., visiting the white realms last, asked one beaten rider what had happened and was shown the wound behind his ear.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: I Thought We Were Dead
clips.summary: McNichols describes Garland, already blamed for the beating, going berserk and running screaming around the tank threatening them again, the attorney having a beaten rider pulled out to a consultation cell, and how the youngest rider sat down and calmly studied Garland while McNichols was sure they would be killed, until guards and the supervisor talked Garland down over twenty minutes.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Bonded Out at Midnight
clips.summary: He tells of being moved to an isolation cell and finally bonded out after the situation calmed, an argument with the youngest rider who wanted to stay rather than worry about being beaten, retrieving their clothes and money, and walking out into the parking lot while the other three riders remained in their separate tanks unaware of what had happened.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Driving Around for Bail Money
clips.topic: Charges and the Wait for Trial
clips.summary: McNichols recounts being taken to the businessman's home near midnight to recount the experience over a drink, being charged with unlawful assembly, all the riders released within about a week, and spending three weeks driving around making club speeches to raise funds while limited local media let the sheriff's denial of the beating outweigh their account.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Union Center or Union Coffee Shop
clips.summary: He describes the first trial ending in a mistrial after the district attorney dropped Union Center to avoid an interstate-commerce question, the attorney George Washington's thorough motion that the charge naming the wrong coffee shop created a fatal conflict, and a newly appointed conservative judge who, realizing the attorney was right, phoned for guidance and dismissed the case.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Difference Between Texas and Mississippi
clips.summary: McNichols contrasts Texas with Mississippi, noting the Houston attorneys and judges accommodated the riders by setting a quick trial date so they could return to register for school, that the lawyers feared offending judges they would work with again, and how at the second trial a hostile jury convicted them as they kept a poker face, leaving them free on appeal.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: A Bitter Train North
clips.summary: He recounts leaving on Union Pacific as bitter men after the conviction, two riders having pressured Black women travelers in Houston for sex which upset the women, and how, having been beaten and convicted without justification, the group fought with the porters on the way home instead of working with them as they had going down, with no freedom songs this time.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Steve, Stop Talking
clips.summary: McNichols describes the Freedom Ride ending back at Union Station, a professor arranging for him to record an oral history at the UCLA project, his account so detailed it filled four hundred pages and a typist worked for weeks, and his later return to Houston at his own expense to learn more about the businessman's bail bond and the progressive youth association organizers.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: A Hernia, a Disk, and Mr. Freedom Rider
clips.summary: He tells of digging into the Houston organizers' backgrounds and their ties to a Senate candidate, failing to get the group's leader to sue the sheriff because no law yet supported it, and returning to UCLA where, nursing a painful ruptured disk, he leveraged his standing as a beaten Freedom Rider, the only one on the West Coast, into a leadership role.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Only One on the West Coast
clips.summary: McNichols reflects on how being known as a Freedom Rider beaten in jail reinforced his standing so that anyone passing through Los Angeles checked in with him, his loose ongoing contact with CORE and James Farmer, and the appeal reaching the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals, which reversed the convictions on a Texas statute barring race discrimination in transportation that a well-educated Black rider had found.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Civil Rights Director in Philadelphia
clips.summary: He describes balancing the National Student Association with the movement over several years, a bad Congress where he failed to win national office, and instead becoming the NSA civil rights director and moving to Philadelphia, his first event a conference in Atlanta and a drive to McComb, Mississippi, where there had been eleven bombings in twenty-two days after the murders of the summer.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Closer to Jackson, the More Scared
clips.summary: McNichols recounts flying into Meridian and growing more afraid the closer he got to Jackson, his year of nightly dreams reliving the beating, checking in with SNCC and driving to McComb for a rally, and being escorted out at four in the morning by a police officer who put his hands on his head and told him not to come back before he flew home.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: The Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom
clips.summary: He describes raising thirty-eight thousand dollars through the 1964 Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom to buy canned goods for Mississippi, then, feeling he had exploited the movement and wanting to do right, conceiving a scheme to petition the Department of Agriculture and partnering with the National Council of Churches Commission on Religion and Race, Robert Spike, and the Delta Ministry's Art Thomas.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Knocking Off Counties
clips.summary: McNichols explains the gambit by which a surplus-food official, threatening to route distribution through civil rights groups no county wanted, pressured county after county to enroll in the federal surplus food program, so that by spring every county in Mississippi had joined, raising rural participation from a low fraction to a hundred percent and getting food to Black people who could not get it before.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Books and the White House Pen
clips.topic: Civil Rights and Voting Rights Acts
clips.summary: He recounts a second program, Books for Equal Opportunity, sending donated books to Mississippi, the NSA's deep involvement in the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights and passage of the 1964 Civil Rights Act and the Voting Rights Act, his invitation to a White House signing ceremony where he received one of the pens, and the resentment he stirred by always pushing the conference to do more.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Rita Schwerner, a Conscience
clips.topic: The MFDP and a Widow's Resolve
clips.summary: McNichols describes spending time in New York on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party effort to organize Congress members to seat the MFDP and unseat the regular Mississippi delegation, and meeting Rita Schwerner, widow of the murdered Mickey Schwerner, who became close to him and a militant conscience, stronger and more committed than he was.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: On the Strategy Committee
clips.summary: He recalls becoming a member of the MFDP Congressional Strategy Committee, going to Washington weekly for urgent strategy meetings, arranging through the National Council of Churches to spend two weeks investigating the challenge, and establishing that the White House had worked behind the scenes to oppose it, a fact then unknown.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: They Were Convinced They Were Communists
clips.summary: McNichols closes on his lament that the AFL-CIO and NAACP leadership in the Leadership Conference, populated by veterans of 1930s labor organizing who had fought the communists, adamantly opposed the SNCC and movement people as communists, many of whose parents had been, a divide he found shameful but true.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Fired Over Five Dollars
clips.topic: Parents and a Lost Teaching Job
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Prospect, Pennsylvania, in September 2011, Thomas Walter Gaither introduces his parents, Walter B. Gaither and Fannie Mae Little Gaither, who met at Friendship Junior College and settled in Great Falls, South Carolina, and recounts how his father was fired from teaching for questioning a five-dollar discrepancy a white school-board member was pocketing.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A One-Room School at Pleasant Grove
clips.summary: Gaither describes his first seven years of schooling in a one-room school owned by Pleasant Grove AME Zion Church, with a single drum heater that smoked the room, no library beyond his mother's dictionary, hand-me-down textbooks from white children, and the rush of new black elementary schools the South built as the 1954 Supreme Court decision loomed.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Fireman and Watchman at the Mill
clips.summary: Gaither recalls church at Pleasant Grove as the center of family life, learning only later that his father's teaching termination was decided by an all-white trustee board with a figurehead black board chosen not to rock the boat, and his father's later work at a cotton mill as a boiler fireman and night watchman before losing that job too.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Let's Get Organized
clips.summary: Gaither tells how his father went back to Benedict College for a sociology degree, was denied the local black high school principalship after a Baptist minister opposed him, and rallied the family with the cry let's get organized, loading a 1939 Buick with bricks and mortar to take handyman and masonry jobs that funded the children's path to college.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Valedictorian and Salutatorian
clips.topic: A Brick Mason Pays the Way
clips.summary: Gaither describes his father becoming a brick mason who traveled across the Charlotte-Columbia-Orangeburg region and stayed on as master mason through the winter to fund tuition, and how he as valedictorian and his brother as salutatorian won small scholarships, seventy-five and fifty dollars, and chose Claflin College in Orangeburg.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: The Till Case at the Dinner Table
clips.summary: Resuming after a break, Gaither recalls race openly discussed at the dinner table, his father's horror at the Emmett Till case as a motivating force for his later activism, his father hosting union organizers at the mill, and the family's open support for the NAACP, including a suit to equalize teacher salaries that divided relatives who were teachers.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Not a Pauper, Not a Chicken Thief
clips.summary: Gaither recounts his parents being registered voters and his own determination to vote, once driving three hundred miles round trip to cast a ballot, and describes the South Carolina elector's certificate that required interpreting the state constitution and swearing one was not a pauper or convicted of crimes like stealing chickens or wife beating, which he compares to modern voter-ID laws.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Same-Class Brother
clips.summary: Gaither explains adjusting academically at Claflin College against students from stronger schools, graduating with honors in general science with a biology concentration, how he and his younger brother ended up in the same graduating class because their mother brought the boy to her school early, and why a black college was the only choice available.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Eighty-Five Degrees and the GRE
clips.summary: Gaither tells the story of taking the Graduate Record Exam, when about a dozen black students from South Carolina State and Claflin were barred from the University of South Carolina campus and forced into a sweltering eighty-five-degree upstairs room across the street, and how his father from then on refused to root for the university and backed Clemson instead.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Youth Chapter Grows
clips.summary: Gaither recounts becoming president of the Claflin NAACP youth chapter, the small membership that swelled after the sit-ins began, students who apologized for fearing reprisals against their parents, the regional meetings where he met Ruby Hurley and Medgar Evers, and how the Greensboro and Rock Hill sit-ins of February 1960 spurred Orangeburg students to organize.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Cracking the Color Line
clips.summary: Gaither describes James T. McCain bringing CORE's organizing tools, the booklet CORE Rules for Action, and James Peck's Cracking the Color Line, students from Claflin and South Carolina State meeting in the Claflin gymnasium to build sociodramas inspired by King's Stride Toward Freedom, and the private-college freedom Claflin had while state-school administrators were chosen to suppress student protest.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Idealists and the Fear of Reprisal
clips.summary: Gaither explains that the early sit-in organizing fell mostly to men because women had a seven o'clock dormitory curfew, the mix of idealism and fear of reprisals against parents that drove the students, and why they chose nonviolent direct action over the NAACP's preference for a single test-case arrest and a court ruling.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Three Hundred Fifty Arrested
clips.summary: Gaither recounts the mass arrest in Orangeburg, three hundred fifty people taken in until the city and county jails filled and an open-air stockade was used, his own arrest for walking the courthouse square while talking to NAACP youth secretary Herbert Wright, and meeting attorney Matthew J. Perry Jr., who graciously promised the NAACP's resources for their defense.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: We Have Been Nonviolent Too Long
clips.summary: Gaither describes first hearing Glenn Smiley of the Fellowship of Reconciliation lecture on nonviolence and naively objecting that black people had been nonviolent too long, then coming to embrace it through James T. McCain, whom he calls his civil rights mentor, a low-key tactical practitioner who pointed him toward Gandhi's writings.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Ejection of Undesirable Guests
clips.summary: Gaither recounts McCain persuading him to attend CORE's Action Institute in Miami in the summer of 1960, his second arrest at a restaurant in Hollywood, Florida alongside Dottie Miller and Bernard Lafayette, ten or twelve days in the Dade County jail, and the unusual charge brought against them, ejection of undesirable guests.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: How Would Gandhi Behave
clips.summary: Gaither explains that he came to nonviolence in tactical terms first, and reflects, jumping ahead to the Rock Hill road gang, on weighing how Gandhi would have acted, believing Gandhi would clean the toilets or shovel sand until he collapsed, and on the gradual growth from nonviolence as technique into nonviolence as a way of life he says few people achieve.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: The Lord Will Take Care of Him
clips.topic: Family Support and a Burned Cross
clips.summary: Gaither recounts his deeply religious family's faith that the Lord would protect him, their own danger in a small community where they were not nonviolent, local men offering to guard his father's house while he was on the Rock Hill road gang, and the cross burned on his parents' lawn that his father vowed would have been the burner's last.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Mostly White National Office
clips.summary: Gaither describes his growing sense of CORE at the national level after visiting its New York office, then mostly white apart from James T. McCain, led by executive secretary James R. Jim Robinson, an organization founded in 1942 at the University of Chicago that championed nonviolent direct action, and his first airplane flight from Columbia to reach it.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: If My Preacher Says It's Okay
clips.summary: Gaither remembers a summer of voter registration with Frank Robinson and Jim McCain, going door to door at housing projects and finding that resistant people, especially older women, would register and vote only if their preacher gave his blessing, with modest but real success.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Willing to Suffer
clips.summary: Gaither reflects that the early sit-ins ended in arrests and posted bonds the state could exploit, leading him to seek a deeper level of commitment, the jail-no-bail policy with the Friendship 9 staged almost exactly a year after the first sit-ins, meant to appeal to people of goodwill through a visible willingness to suffer.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: The Right Place at the Right Time
clips.summary: Gaither acknowledges an earlier CORE-affiliated jail-no-bail by the Tallahassee group of Patricia and Priscilla Stephens, but argues that historical significance comes from context, that the Friendship 9 acted at a strategic moment after hundreds of students had been arrested following Greensboro, making theirs the protest that did the critical thing.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Hot Chocolate in the Cell Block
clips.summary: Gaither explains that the Miami arrest was essentially accidental as observers and test groups sometimes both got taken in, and describes his extended stay in a Dade County cell block of thirty-five men including murderers, the prisoner hierarchy that beat an elderly man into the shower, and inmates melting candy bars by a lamp to make hot chocolate.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Field Secretary on the Road
clips.summary: Gaither recounts his delight at joining CORE full time despite disagreeing with the NAACP's philosophy, his early assignments in Kentucky, California, and Arizona training local people in nonviolent direct action, meeting Medgar Evers as his one contact in Jackson, and being chosen as scout for the Freedom Ride because he had helped originate the idea.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Why January 31, Not February 1
clips.summary: Gaither describes training the Friendship College students at Claflin with Gordon Carey on sociodramas, marveling that college students with no knowledge of tactical nonviolence pulled off a major protest, and explaining that the date was set for January 31, 1961 rather than the February 1 anniversary so the students could register for classes and not lose a whole semester to thirty days on the road gang.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Thousand People We Could Not See
clips.topic: On the Rock Hill Road Gang
clips.summary: Gaither reflects on the pivotal jail-no-bail, recalling Clara Luper's Oklahoma City sit-ins that few knew of, and describes life on the Rock Hill road gang where authorities limited visitors to a line of about a hundred while a thousand gathered unseen, a solitary-confinement punishment over building a fence, and learning only later of the impact outside.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: An Idea on a Bus North
clips.summary: Gaither recounts riding a bus back to New York with Gordon Carey in December 1960, talking over Louis Fischer's book on Gandhi and the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation scouted by George Houser, and how the idea of a Freedom Ride emerged, with him taking on the scout's role of sending back details on towns, bus schedules, and stations and choosing stops near black colleges that could house the riders.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: If We Get Through Alabama
clips.summary: Gaither explains the audacious plan to push through the Deep South into Mississippi, Alabama, and Louisiana, his belief that federal protection would be needed past Alabama, why SCLC went in where the NAACP was outlawed, the timing as James Farmer became CORE's executive secretary, and how no one estimated the ride would become a signature protest of the movement.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: King Was Not a Freedom Rider
clips.summary: Gaither says he never expected King to join the ride and holds no reservation about it, crediting King's national advisory role with CORE and his work flushing out nonviolence as strategy, and recounts his own assignment after the Anniston bus burning to Montgomery, where he lived in Ralph Abernathy's home during martial law.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Rap on the Phone Booth
clips.summary: Gaither describes meeting unidentified incoming Freedom Riders at the Montgomery bus stations under martial law, National Guard jeeps escorting them only to within a block, and his signal of rapping on the phone booth as a rider dialed Ralph Abernathy's number, the guard reappearing to return them to Abernathy's black neighborhood before he was assigned to Jackson.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Gratification and Responsibility
clips.summary: Gaither, then in his early twenties, reflects on watching his vision unfold amid violence and federal momentum with both gratification and a sense of responsibility for those injured, his resolve that violence must not intimidate the movement, and his view that the federal government and Kennedy administration were not allies, annoyed by black and white riders challenging segregation.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Doar Welcomed, the FBI Not
clips.summary: Gaither contrasts the welcomed and sincere Justice Department presence of John Doar in Mississippi voter-registration work with the FBI, which he found could not be trusted in any community he worked, recalling an agent who hoped to implicate him in a conspiracy, and his judgment that to the Kennedys the matter was politics rather than moral commitment.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Cross Section of America
