clips.summary: Gaither recalls how the campaign in Jackson and at Parchman Farm drew religious groups and people from across the country, his sense as the summer of 1961 closed that nonviolent direct action could promote change even as impatient voices turned toward violence, and his view that King's star had waned by his assassination as violent elements grew.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Visionary Named Bob Moses
clips.topic: Founding COFO and Working With Moses
clips.summary: Gaither describes being present at the founding of COFO and respecting how each organization had different strengths against a segregationist culture that saw NAACP, CORE, SNCC, and SCLC as all the same, and his high regard for Bob Moses, a visionary who managed local ministers' congressional campaigns and pushed issues rather than himself.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Not Morally Fit to Serve
clips.summary: Gaither recounts receiving a 1A classification and an induction notice to Fort Jackson while working in Jackson, having missed claiming conscientious-objector status, and how the sergeant, on seeing his arrest record, declared him not morally fit to serve and released him, sending him quickly to resurrect graduate-school offers and enroll at Atlanta University.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Law School Letter Arrives Late
clips.summary: Gaither tells how a University of Wisconsin professor named Rice recommended him to law school after he spoke there on the Freedom Rides, and how the admission letter arrived only after he was already halfway through a biology master's degree, so he stayed in biology, won teaching assistantships, and chose the University of Iowa.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Nucleus of Local Lawyers
clips.summary: Gaither honors the little-known black lawyers who tried the Freedom Rider cases in Jackson, Jack H. Young Sr. who studied law by correspondence, R. Jess Brown the former social-studies teacher, and Carsie Hall, the local nucleus reinforced on federal cases by figures like Derrick Bell, Constance Baker Motley, William Kunstler, and Carl Rachlin.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: A Freedom School in Clarksdale
clips.summary: Gaither describes his final three months in the movement working with Aaron Henry in Clarksdale, Mississippi on a selective-buying boycott of downtown stores, running a freedom school of twenty to twenty-five young people daily near Henry's drugstore, and choosing to stay in Clarksdale over the March on Washington while helping make a sign the local youth carried there.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: All One-Sided People
clips.summary: Gaither recaps the Council of Federated Organizations as the union of SNCC, SCLC, the NAACP, and CORE that white Mississippi segregationists saw as all one-sided people, then turns to his graduate study at the University of Iowa, where he was the only African American in the department and served as a teaching assistant in a large introductory biology course.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Why Iowa, Not Texas
clips.summary: Gaither recounts being compared to a previous African American student from Louisiana, finding enough respect among professors to avoid major problems despite some who merely tolerated him, and explaining that he chose Iowa because the leading scholar in his field had moved to Austin, and he did not want to fight discrimination in its bladed form again in Texas.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Threads Under the Electron Microscope
clips.summary: Gaither describes his research in electron microscopy, studying tiny thread-like structures, from Latin for thread, that stagger an organism's release of reproductive structures over time, and using the microscope's superior resolution to answer questions of speciation that a light microscope could not resolve.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Not Rookies Who Didn't Know
clips.summary: Gaither argues the Freedom Rides were among the most inclusive and integrative civil rights protests, conceived and executed primarily by CORE, and voices his disappointment with histories that, while crediting the Nashville students who continued the ride after the Anniston bus burning, wrongly cast CORE as rookies rather than originators of an ongoing process.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Surface Change, Same Shot-Callers
clips.summary: Gaither, never having lived in the South again, contends the fundamental infrastructure of racism remains under different labels, that black officials can be constrained by the same people who called the shots fifty years ago, points to Katrina and unequal GI benefits, yet affirms his lasting faith that nonviolent tactics can work given a residual moral conscience among many Americans.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Surprised by Obama
clips.summary: Gaither recounts his surprise at Barack Obama's election by young people who then failed to persist in defending the change, his hope the nation might reach a more just order, and reflects on the personal cost of pouring his energy into a movement simply to be accepted as a citizen, time a white American would not have had to spend.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: But for the Grace of God
clips.topic: Great Falls and the Scholar's Loneliness
clips.summary: Gaither closes by picturing Great Falls, South Carolina as a place of men his age idling without hope under a tree, where the South's effort to keep black people down also cheated poor whites, and reflects on the loneliness of being a scholar whose work, a doctorate he frames as a contribution to the whole nation, even his own parents could not fully grasp.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A Barber Shop of Mixed Clientele
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Library of Congress in December 2015, Timothy Jenkins opens on his birth in Philadelphia in 1938, his father's barber shop with its mix of professionals and laypeople, his maternal grandfather William Albert Jones who served on the school board and founded a Black Republican group called Remember John Brown, and his paternal grandfather who founded a Rosenwald school in Courtland, Virginia.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Reverend O.G. Jenkins of Courtland
clips.summary: Jenkins describes the Remember John Brown organization that supported Black businesses and education in Philadelphia, the photograph of his paternal grandfather Reverend O.G. Jenkins seated before the Courtland Normal and Industrial Institute, and how he only later discovered as an adult that this grandfather had also founded Hood Memorial Church in Richmond, the largest African American Zion Episcopal church there.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: From Thaddeus Stevens to a Howard Scholarship
clips.summary: He recounts his education at the Thaddeus Stevens School of Practice, a teacher-training facility with a heavily integrated student body and extensive museum field trips that left him fascinated by the Philadelphia Museum of Art's Scholars Room, and how a recruiter at Central High School won him a full tuition scholarship to Howard, reinforced by family ties to historically Black colleges and a neighbor whose sister was Howard's dean of women.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Rayford Logan and Sterling Brown
clips.summary: Entering Howard in 1956, Jenkins describes a stimulating campus of debate and philosophy clubs and a faculty steeped in Black social action: historian Rayford Logan, general secretary of the Pan-African movement under W.E.B. Du Bois, and the poet and dialect critic Sterling Brown, who opened his home to students like Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, and Jean Wheeler and whose father may have started the first correspondence school for Black ministers.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Little Forum
clips.summary: Jenkins honors political scientist Harold Lewis and sociologist E. Franklin Frazier, author of the study of the Black bourgeoisie, and describes the Little Forum, a monthly reading club in Cook Hall where a student and a faculty member each presented one book as peers, the one setting where student opinion was weighted as heavily as faculty and a melting pot for formal study and current events.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Granite Bench Behind Founders
clips.summary: As student body president, Jenkins gained access to Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, Howard's first Black president, who during the McCarthy era refused to dismiss faculty pursued by the House Un-American Activities Committee and would not expel students like Pauli Murray, arrested for a 1940s sit-in at the House of Representatives cafeteria; Jenkins waylaid him on the granite bench behind Founders Library for informal talks the secretary would never schedule.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A More Radical Dimension
clips.summary: Jenkins describes the campus grievances Howard students pressed before the 1960s and how off-campus activism came through his selection as Howard's delegate to the United States National Student Association and the CIA-linked International Student Relations Seminar at Harvard, where he met students like future congressman Barney Frank and the Students for a Democratic Society, just as the Greensboro sit-in erupted toward the end of his term as student body president.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Howard Fervor
clips.summary: Jenkins points to a tradition of Howard protest against segregated department stores and drinking fountains in the 1940s and 1950s, and to Jonathan Holloway's book on Howard, which treats E. Franklin Frazier, Ralph Bunche, and economist Abram Harris and the faculty's conflicts with the administration, showing Howard's nexus with nearly every aspect of African American political activity.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Compelled Contributions
clips.summary: In his final year the sit-ins erupted and the student council voted to support the legal defense of arrested students, but the administration barred using compulsory student funds for off-campus political activity; Jenkins describes the twist of funding transportation to a convention in Canada instead, and Dean Armour Blackburn's opposition overruled by Mordecai Johnson.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A Man Among Boys
clips.summary: Graduating in June 1960, Jenkins notes that NAACP chapters had run sit-ins long before 1960, and recalls that as he left, Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, Dion Diamond, and Jean Wheeler entered as freshmen, the future stalwarts of SNCC; Carmichael's memoir Ready for Revolution devotes nearly a hundred pages to Howard and credits Jenkins's handoff, calling him a man among boys.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Nonviolent Action Group
clips.summary: Jenkins recounts being elected National Affairs Vice President of the United States National Student Association the year after graduating, returning often to Howard, where the new Nonviolent Action Group had formed to integrate lunch counters and amusement parks in Washington, and his place on the executive committees of both SNCC and SDS, though he does not recall attending the Shaw University founding meeting.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Preempted by Acclamation
clips.summary: Jenkins describes his original aim to run for International Affairs Vice President, his trips to Cuba and his hosting of African and Caribbean students, and how he learned only later that the CIA funded the International Student Relations Seminar and ran operatives in the NSA; distrusting his civil rights ties, they maneuvered to name him National Affairs Vice President by acclamation, blocking his international bid.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A Slave-Owning Fascist Mentality
clips.topic: NSA Battles Over Integration and the Right
clips.summary: He explains how the NSA had passed integration resolutions since Brown v. Board of Education over Southern objection, and how the sit-in resolution he supported cost the association many Southern schools who walked out, then recounts confronting the rising right-wing Young Americans for Freedom by rebutting their invited speaker William F. Buckley Jr. as a slave-owning fascist mentality, prompting a censure motion he survived.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Godmother of SNCC
clips.summary: Jenkins describes Ella Baker as a great eminence of civil rights, tied to the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters and A. Philip Randolph, instrumental in founding the Southern Christian Leadership Conference, and sheltered by the YWCA, who became the godmother of SNCC; he was surprised to find in old minutes that he chaired the December executive committee meeting at Atlanta's Butler Street YMCA where Marion Barry was replaced by Chuck McDew.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A Northern Voice for SNCC
clips.summary: He explains that the Freedom Rides came in 1961 as he was leaving the NSA and enrolling in law school, so he never rode but supported them through Northern fundraising, and recounts Yale chaplain William Sloane Coffin Jr.'s participation and the funds Coffin raised through the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom that Jenkins later carried to Medgar Evers's widow.
keywords: Women's International League for Peace and Freedom

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Religious and the Secular
clips.summary: Jenkins frames the division of emphasis within SNCC between the religious Nashville contingent influenced by Jim Lawson, Diane Nash, and James Bevel, who treated direct action and nonviolence as Christian expression, and those like himself drawn to the politics of integration as civic responsibility rather than religious witness.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Organs of State
clips.summary: He recounts the danger of SNCC splitting along religious and secular lines and Ella Baker's role in harmonizing them, and lays out his own conviction, shared with Chuck McDew and Charles Jones, that sit-ins and pray-ins were cosmetic and that real change required taking control of the organs of state that determined the police, the roads, the budgets, and public employment, borrowing Nkrumah's Consciencism and the gospel call to seek first the political kingdom.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: We Were Co-opting Them
clips.summary: Jenkins describes the meeting at Capahosic, Virginia where Kennedy administration operatives sought to steer the movement from volatile street demonstrations toward voter registration, which carried more constitutional justification for federal involvement, and disputes the historians' claim, including Howard Zinn's, that SNCC was co-opted, arguing SNCC co-opted the administration into launching a south-wide voter education campaign attended by Charles Jones, Charles Sherrod, Chuck McDew, and Burke Marshall.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Local Draft Boards
clips.summary: Jenkins explains that local draft boards reflecting Mississippi's politicians used their authority to target SNCC and civil rights activists for the draft, and recalls the National Conference of Black Lawyers later challenging General Lewis Hershey's pursuit of activists, including a case where a student over twenty-three was imprisoned despite the statute and the conviction was thrown out.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A Three-Judge Panel
clips.summary: He describes SNCC's major contribution in the voting rights testimony, forcing an amendment so voting cases were heard by a three-judge panel rather than a single segregationist district judge, with two of three judges named by the chief judge of the Fifth Circuit, where Eisenhower-appointed Republican judges allowed most such cases to be won over Kennedy's opposition.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A Cram Course in History
clips.summary: Jenkins recounts organizing a seminar in Nashville in the summer of 1961 to replicate Howard's Little Forum, inviting sit-in leaders to study Black history and the politics of race with top minds, funded by the New World and Taconic Foundations with letters of support from Dr. King and Ella Baker, which he calls the first Freedom School, and notes Nashville was chosen for Fisk University's Race Relations Institute rather than the religious persuasion of Lawson or Nash.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Pushed Off the Highway
clips.summary: Jenkins recalls the many meetings at Highlander Folk School and a drive from Monteagle toward Chattanooga when a truck driver, seeing an integrated group singing in a Volkswagen bus, pushed them off the mountain highway, their fall halted by a tree above the ravine until a pickup truck pulled them out; he remembers Ella Baker, skeptical of ministers, motioning him close on her stretcher to say, thank God for Jesus.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Out of the Reach of Disaster
clips.summary: Jenkins explains his dual purpose in entering Yale law school that fall: to gain skills relevant to social change, and to serve as one of the SNCC people kept out of reach of murder, incarceration, or the draft so the organization could be rebuilt if its Southern projects were wiped out overnight, a role he likens to Che Guevara returning from the Sierra Maestra.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Northern Student Movement
clips.summary: He describes Yale as a northern adjunct to SNCC, where he organized Freedom Singers tours and recruited William Sloane Coffin Jr. as a freedom rider, and where he met Peter and Joan Countryman, founders of the Northern Student Movement, which did in the North what SNCC did in the South and which he counts among the unsung heroes of the era.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Backbone of Voter Registration
clips.summary: Jenkins notes other satellite student groups like the Young Christian Students and SDS that aided the Southern movement, and the June 1961 letter in which Ella Baker introduced him to Bob Moses, Amzie Moore, and the people who became the backbone of voter registration, as SNCC chose McComb, rural and isolated, to be a catalyst for a statewide effort.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Nobody Wanted McComb
clips.summary: He explains that while the NAACP wanted Atlanta, SCLC Montgomery, and CORE Louisiana, nobody wanted McComb, so SNCC went into one of the most hostile and remote areas to make a beachhead, relying on Ella Baker, Amzie Moore, and Bob Moses for contacts and on a Black Masonic lodge the police would not defile to house meetings there and in Jackson.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Welcome to the Magnolia State
clips.summary: Jenkins tells of driving from Chicago to McComb with Chuck McDew, Charles Sherrod, and Charles Jones when they hit a dog in a small Alabama town, a hostile crowd forming to demand compensation, until an elderly white man in a white linen suit and straw hat parted the crowd, said the boys could not have avoided it, then told them to keep quiet and get out, after which they drove without stopping into Mississippi.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: It's Still There
clips.summary: He assesses McComb by its survival, with students still doing civil rights projects, scholarship programs, and a Black history curriculum, and credits Teaching for Change as the institutional arm that made McComb a poster child for what comes from working with teachers and parents to deepen a community's grasp of its own history and social engagement.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Not Letting Herbert Lee Die in Vain
clips.summary: Jenkins points to the murder of Herbert Lee by a state representative as a teaching tool he used in a 1962 or 1963 Holmes County speech, and to Bob Moses living among the people in an intellectual guerrilla fashion to build a movement from the ground up, the model Teaching for Change carries forward through its website and ties to civil rights veterans and the One Person One Vote organization.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Defenders of Those Who Did Want to Test
clips.summary: He recalls being filmed rabble-rousing and singing alongside Bob Dylan, Pete Seeger, and Theodore Bikel in Greenwood, where Mrs. McGhee defended her farm with firearms against the Klan and local people with no prior preparation for politics took up voter registration, and where even those who could not pass the literacy test drove others to register, captured in the short film The Streets of Greenwood.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Turn Us Around
clips.summary: Jenkins describes the song line about not letting Laurie Pritchett turn them around and Albany, Georgia, where Charles Sherrod chose neglected Southwest Georgia with its heavy Black population, mobilizing a grassroots organization in Ella Baker's spirit; he calls Albany the most ferocious expression of police-sanctioned violence in the country, where Dr. King and SCLC eventually joined the demonstrations.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A Slow Boiling Success
clips.summary: Responding to scholars and SCLC voices who call the Albany Movement a failure, Jenkins argues it depends on expectations, that Albany was a rural area off the side roads of history with no celebrated victories, yet a tremendous success as people kept their homes through farmers' home support and learned to navigate the agriculture department, illustrated when Charles Sherrod's wife Shirley became a Department of Agriculture administrator in the area.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Greenwood Folk Festival
clips.summary: He describes the Greenwood folk festival on Mrs. McGhee's farm, intended to dispute the notion that the South had no culture and to let the larger entertainment community show support, drawing names like Seeger, Dylan, Joan Baez, and Bikel, and traces SNCC's mobilization through song to the Jubilee Singers of Fisk and the Howard Gospel Singers.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: We Shall Overcome
clips.summary: Jenkins recounts that We Shall Overcome was written as a religious ballad by Charles Albert Tindley, a Methodist minister in his hometown of Philadelphia, and turned into a political anthem with Guy Carawan, Pete Seeger, and the Freedom Singers, and describes how the songs gave demonstrators solidarity and courage in jail, so deeply connected to the movement that SNCC veterans still grow misty-eyed singing them.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: First to the Widow's Door
clips.summary: Jenkins recalls being a law student in New Haven when Medgar Evers was assassinated, and how William Sloane Coffin Jr. and the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom, a group on the House Un-American Activities Committee list, raised money so he could travel to Jackson and be the first person from out of state to bring a check to Evers's door.
keywords: Women's International League for Peace and Freedom

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Stop in the Name of Medgar
clips.summary: He distinguishes the public and private sides of the June 1963 Jackson march after Evers's funeral, when youngsters broke from the police-escorted procession and a confrontation followed, captured in Eyes on the Prize; privately, told that armed Black veterans on a rooftop had John Doar in their telescopic sights, mistaking him for the police, Jenkins reached the roof and cried, stop in the name of Medgar, and they stopped.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Take It to King's House
clips.summary: Jenkins names his three brushes with history, the first Freedom School, the John Doar roof, and a second moment in Atlanta when the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights planned a demonstration at Rich's department store against the wishes of a Black leadership establishment, including Martin Luther King Sr.; as a final speaker he urged not only going forward but routing the march to King's house to ask him to come out.
keywords: Committee on Appeal for Human Rights

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Equidistant Between Kennedy and Nixon
clips.topic: The Arrest That Moved an Election
clips.summary: He recounts that King joined the demonstration and was arrested two days before the 1960 election, that Robert Kennedy intervened and a telegram of support went to Coretta King, that the United Auto Workers spread the story in leaflets across the country, and that with the Black vote equidistant between Kennedy and Nixon, some 250,000 unprepared voters turned out for Kennedy, who won by 100,000.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Guiding Spirit, William Higgs
clips.summary: Asked whether the Atlanta demonstration tied to the October 1960 SNCC conference, Jenkins is unsure, then turns to William L. Higgs, a native Mississippian and Ole Miss and Harvard Law graduate who made his career in civil rights litigation and was the guiding spirit behind the three-judge-panel amendment, defending Black lawyers degraded in the court of Judge William Harold Cox, an appointee of Senator James Eastland.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: A Reconstruction-Era Hook
clips.summary: Jenkins describes working with Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party president Lawrence Guyot and fellow Yale law student Bill Wood, finding in the Mississippi Code a provision originally meant to protect white people excluded by the Republican Party during Reconstruction that allowed protest votes, challenges, and independent elections, the foundation for the MFDP's mock elections and eventual participation in the Democratic Party.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Voter Education Project
clips.summary: He recounts statewide voter mobilization after SNCC through the Black leadership forum, the unions, and the Voter Education Project headed by Leo Branton and then Vernon Jordan, which channeled tax-exempt philanthropy into registration, and notes that the project refused to enter Mississippi as too hot, leaving Bob Moses and SNCC on their own in McComb and Greenwood, making their intervention all the more important.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Locked in His Room With Gandhi
clips.summary: Citing Larry Rivers's article on the Rankin Chapel connection to King, Jenkins describes how Mordecai Johnson, mentor and devotee of Gandhi whose vice president William Stuart Nelson lived in India, spoke in Philadelphia while King was a student at Crozer, and how King, hearing him, locked himself in his room for ten days reading Gandhi and credited Johnson rather than Thoreau or Emerson, insisting the techniques and DNA of SNCC carry into the women's, disability, and poor people's movements.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The National Conference of Black Lawyers
clips.summary: He records the National Conference of Black Lawyers, founded in 1968 on a call from Floyd McKissick to move the Black bar beyond civil rights into economic development, poverty, and housing law, naming judges and lawyers like Leon Higginbotham, Nathaniel Jones, Bruce Wright, and Raymond Pace Alexander, who won the Girard College case barring discrimination in charitable organizations.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Mayor for Life
clips.summary: Jenkins explains the strong SNCC presence in Washington through Marion Barry, who as a student wrote him a four-page letter on Black people taking over local political structures, and who as mayor integrated every level of hierarchy, advanced women, and funded neglected areas, tracing the same lineage to Harold Washington in Chicago and to lawyers shaped by the Black Panthers, Attica, and the Soledad Brothers.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Reversing Woodrow Wilson
clips.summary: Reflecting on his career, Jenkins recounts serving by appointment of President Carter on the Postal Service governing board, where as chair of the Corporate Responsibility Committee he reversed the segregation Woodrow Wilson had imposed across the federal government, brought in Kenneth Clark, and restored Amzie Moore's pension, then describes consulting for the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, teaching law, serving as president of the University of the District of Columbia, and building WHUT-TV.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Into the Belly of the Beast
clips.summary: Jenkins recalls writing John Lewis in SNCC's twilight that its people should professionally infiltrate the machinery created by the legislation they drafted, migrating into the EEOC, the National Labor Relations Board, and the Federal Reserve, and laments that unlike labor the movement did not put its own people forward to administer its laws, a shortcoming he frames not as selling out but as a career mission.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: The Next SNCC
clips.summary: Jenkins closes on the failure, amid black power's egotism and myopia, to keep alliances with progressive whites and to mobilize the poor white community to its real interests, which he ties to today's red-state politics and Kentucky voters rejecting health care to avoid association with Obama, and to the unfinished human-rights mission, recalling the Southern Negro Youth Congress as the first SNCC and hoping for another to come.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Born in the Kelly Settlement
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop and Guha Shankar at the University of Southern Mississippi in December 2015, Vernon F. Dahmer Jr. opens by placing his birth in the Kelly Settlement five miles north of Hattiesburg in August 1929, and names his father, Vernon Dahmer Sr., the youngest of twelve children born to George Dahmer and Ellie Kelly.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: The Daughters Sent North
clips.summary: Dahmer explains that his grandfather, fearing the danger white men posed to his six daughters in Mississippi, sent them north at a young age, where they married Black men, while the light-skinned sons except his father moved to Chicago and assimilated as white men in search of better employment.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: They Looked Like Whites
clips.topic: Passing and Comfort in Black Identity
clips.summary: He reflects that the assimilated uncles stayed in touch and sometimes brought white wives to visit, that the family's light complexion traced back to slavery and racial mixing, and that he himself, raised in an all-Black community and married to a dark-skinned woman, never felt drawn to pass.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Where All the White Negroes Live
clips.summary: Dahmer traces his grandfather George Dahmer, whose father was the German Peter Dahmer of Darmstadt and whose mother Laura Barnes was raised by a mulatto woman running a boarding house in Covington County, then married the ex-slave Charlie Craft, with whom she had nine mixed-race children, and explains why the Kelly Settlement, home to the Kelly, Wilson, and Eaton families, accepted them, a community known by the slur as the place where all the white Negroes lived.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Several Hundred Acres
clips.summary: Dahmer describes his grandfather George as a successful farmer of several hundred acres who fed twelve children off the land, and his father, given a start in land after marrying, becoming a landowner who in the mid-1930s bought a school bus to carry Black children to their segregated school.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: A Tractor and a Grocery Store
clips.summary: He recalls his father leasing the bus to the county and hauling people to conventions and games, then using the wartime economic upturn around Camp Shelby to buy the neighborhood's first tractor in 1940 and open a grocery store around 1942, expanding the farm and serving Black and some white customers.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Hauling Three Hundred Pound Blocks of Ice
clips.topic: The Ice House and a Boy's Chores
clips.summary: Dahmer describes the country grocery store stocked with canned goods, the homemade ice house his father built beside it, and his own job at fourteen of driving the school bus to buy three-hundred-pound blocks of ice, delivering them, and chipping them into smaller blocks for customers without electricity.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Three Areas of Income
clips.summary: He recounts learning to cut meat and butcher hogs in the grocery business, the row of sons providing cheap labor for room, board, and weekend spending money, and his father adding a small sawmill around 1945 to sell lumber to local farmers, giving the family three streams of income.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Three Sets of Kids
clips.summary: Dahmer explains the family's three sets of children: three by his mother, who divorced his father around 1935; three by a second wife who died of a muscular disease; and the existing mother his father married in 1952, and describes his father keeping the two older boys himself rather than placing them with grandparents after the divorce.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: We All Danced by the Same Music
clips.topic: A Father Who Was the Focal Point
clips.summary: He describes being raised largely without a mother as older women came to cook and clean, his father meeting the need for both parents while working, and the unusual unity and bonding this produced among five sons who all followed one direction and remained close into adulthood.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Warren Kelly Gave the Land
clips.summary: Dahmer credits his great-grandfather Warren Kelly, son of slave master Green Kelly and an African mistress named Sarah, with using his land to build the first church and school and structure the community, the first school opening in 1879 after earlier classes met in the Shady Grove Baptist Church, established in 1864.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: A Church and a School Out of Slavery
clips.summary: He recounts that Black members left the white Providence Baptist Church to found Shady Grove in a one-room log cabin that also served as the first school, taught by family members from the 1870s, and that his grandfather George had to attend Jackson State to win permission to marry the educated Ellen Kelly.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Not Sending You to Carry a Lunch
clips.summary: Dahmer describes the country school half a mile from his home as a big family reunion where everyone knew one another, his father's saying that he was not sent to school to carry a lunch since work waited at home, and the steady loss of classmates to fieldwork that left only eight in his graduating class.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: He Bumped Me Upside the Head
clips.summary: He recalls favoring math and reading and struggling with grammar, and how his father, watching him misspell words at the board when picking up the bus, drilled him at home by the kerosene lamp, rapping his head for each missed word until he learned to break words into syllables.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: A Little Thin Green Black History Book
clips.summary: Dahmer describes a progressive principal who taught African American history in the 1940s, the thin green book on Booker T. Washington, George Washington Carver, and the Underground Railroad used in seventh and eighth grade, and the hand-me-down white-school textbooks with pages missing and other children's names inside.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Poor White Folks Picking Cotton
clips.topic: Race and Custom at the Store
clips.summary: He recalls extremely poor white people who came to pick cotton in segments of the field, and white customers who charged groceries and sometimes walked off owing his father, including a returning veteran who left several hundred dollars unpaid, as Dahmer worked the store cutting meat, selling ice, and pumping gas.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: A Drink and a Moon Pie
clips.summary: Dahmer describes his father giving poor Black families who walked in from the woods a drink and a moon pie and extra meat, and traces that open heart to George Dahmer, who treated his sharecroppers as family in a communal arrangement Dahmer links to the interdependence that came out of slavery.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: To My Own Self, Not on Bad
clips.summary: He recounts buying back, for nine hundred fifty dollars, the inherited land of a nineteen-year-old who had let his taxes lapse, setting the young man on a payment plan and teaching him to pay taxes and claim the homestead exemption, work the young man now does cutting sugar cane and mowing yards for the family.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Tougaloo and Alcorn
clips.summary: After noting a Claiborne County man who sold land cheaply in what became Jones Village, Dahmer describes enrolling at Tougaloo, falling seriously ill his first semester, then attending Alcorn State, leaving to help the family business when his stepmother fell ill and died, and returning until the draft ended his schooling with thirty-eight credits earned.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Counting Crickets
clips.summary: He recalls Alcorn State as a remote agricultural school with a work program, strict rules forbidding any contact when walking a girl to the dormitory, and his comfort there among rural farming students, majoring in agriculture and working in the school dairy, and notes Tougaloo had white faculty teaching Black students.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Twice as Good to Stay Out of Trouble
clips.summary: Dahmer says his father was never registered to vote and was conscious of being denied the same opportunity as whites, never speaking meanly of them but telling his boys they had to be twice as good and work twice as hard because it was not even, counsel that steadied him later in the Air Force.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: News On, You Be Quiet
clips.topic: A Household That Followed the News
clips.summary: He describes his father drawing power from the school bus battery to run a small RCA radio, requiring the family to sit quietly for John Cameron Swayze's evening news alongside Lum and Abner, Amos and Andy, and the Grand Ole Opry, and his parable about the hog eating acorns without looking up to see where they come from.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Nine Black Men and a Secret Branch
clips.summary: Dahmer recounts his father and eight other Black men founding the county branch of the NAACP around 1945 as an underground organization with secret night meetings, his father reporting back what was said about voting and politics, and taking the teenaged Dahmer along to pay the poll tax at the sheriff's office.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Tell Me What You Read
clips.summary: He describes watching the white registrar hand his father a card to read aloud, then declare what his father said was wrong, denying registration to a business owner who had helped Dahmer with his own lessons, a denial that aggravated the boy and that he later connected to his father becoming NAACP president in the mid-1950s after Brown v. Board.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: You Mean Mister Son
clips.summary: Dahmer recounts how, home as an Air Force staff sergeant around 1955, the rising racial tension showed in small things, a store owner's wife correcting him to call her teenage son Mister and a grocer quizzing him on whether integrated Nebraska had ruined him, and how his father warned him on visits home to stay away from windows at night and be aware of a gun kept in the room.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Picking Up Rocks at San Antonio
clips.summary: Dahmer recounts being drafted during the Korean War, traveling by Trailways bus to Jackson and lodging on Farish Street because there was no hotel for the white recruits, then induction and weeks at a San Antonio Air Force base sleeping in tents and moving rocks from pile to pile before basic training in Wichita Falls.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Don't Get Caught Up in This Army Thing
clips.summary: He explains that after his draft notice ordered him to report in September 1951, an uncle who had served as a ground soldier in the 92nd Infantry Division and other combat veterans warned him away from the Army, so he procrastinated, then volunteered with the downtown Air Force recruiter and shipped out the next morning.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: You Know You're a Draft Dodger
clips.summary: Dahmer tells of being called over the loudspeaker during drill at Sheppard Air Force Base and confronted by two federal investigators who flashed badges and accused him of being a draft dodger for an induction notice he had never signed for, and how the twenty-two-year-old talked his way out of it on his feet.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: The Best Decision Somebody Else Made
clips.summary: He recounts being assigned to teletype school at Francis E. Warren Air Force Base, resenting the typewriter and angling through the first sergeant to transfer to truck-driving school, until the captain refused and ordered him to become the best teletype operator in the outfit, a posting that carried him through communications to computers and the rank of senior master sergeant.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Watching the Russians From Shemya
clips.topic: Settling Into an Air Force Career
clips.summary: Dahmer describes marrying a month before entering the Air Force, a radar posting in Omaha, a year on isolated Shemya in the Aleutians watching Soviet aircraft, and a base near home in Panama City, Florida, where his World War II warrant officer boss and a top sergeant called him crazy for thinking of leaving, so he reenlisted for six years and was paid a stack of twenty-dollar bills that made him feel rich.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: A Telephone Call Before Dawn
clips.summary: Dahmer recounts being stationed at March Air Force Base in California as a master sergeant, having seen the high racial tension on a 1964 visit home, when on the morning of January 10, 1966 an aunt in Compton telephoned before dawn to tell him the family home had been firebombed.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: You Doubt the Integrity of the FBI
clips.topic: Going to the FBI in Riverside
clips.summary: He describes deciding, out of distrust of Mississippi's justice system, to go to the FBI office in the Riverside federal building before work and ask that no Mississippi law enforcement handle his father's case, an agent demanding whether he doubted the FBI's integrity, and his own top-secret clearance and sensitive communications post drawing surveillance.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Somebody on Me the Whole Way
clips.summary: Dahmer recounts being warned by his colonel not to compromise his position, traveling home through New Orleans, and noticing only later the same man shadowing him from Los Angeles to Mississippi, while messages tracking his own activity passed through the base facility he commanded without anyone realizing he would see them.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Seventy-Eight Years on Active Duty
clips.topic: A Country That Would Not Protect Him
clips.summary: He recounts that his father raised eight children and that seven of his sons served a combined seventy-eight years of active duty during the Korean and Vietnam eras, his parents sleeping in shifts for years, and his arrival home to the burned site and the morgue, the worst day of his life, as the oldest son shouldering the burden.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: My Country Could Not Protect My Family
clips.summary: Dahmer reflects that he and his brothers lived up to their military oath while the people sworn to protect his family did not, that he loved his country yet faulted it because the killing did not have to happen, and names the four sons who came home in uniform to bury their father while another, Harold, had been in the house that night.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: She Held Everything Intact
clips.topic: A Mother Who Rebuilt and Stayed
clips.summary: Dahmer honors his stepmother as the leader of the family who, with two children to raise, declined relatives' offers to move to Washington, rebuilt the house on the same spot, raised Dennis Dahmer and Betty Dahmer to college degrees, served three terms as election commissioner in the district where his father was killed, and testified many times as the fourteen indicted Klansmen were repeatedly tried in the 1960s without a final conviction.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: You Can Kill a Black Male
clips.topic: Sam Bowers and Justice at Last
clips.summary: Dahmer identifies Sam Bowers as the leader of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan, said by former Klansmen to wield power equal to the governor's and to have told members they could kill a Black man without fear of jail, and describes leading the family effort to bring Bowers back into the courtroom, his conviction by jury verdict on August 21, 1998, his death in prison in November 2006, and the yearly January 10th memorial services and Hub City Professional Men's Club that kept the case alive across thirty-two years.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: This Is the Burned-Out Site
clips.summary: Dahmer closes by describing photographs for the record: his father Vernon Dahmer Sr. as a young man, the home site destroyed by the firebombing carried out by the White Knights led by Sam Bowers, the burned-out family home and grocery store with his brothers in uniform standing over the debris, and the family at the funeral.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Named for My Grandmother
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Northborough, Massachusetts Free Library in August 2013, Virginia Simms George explains that she was named for her grandmother, not the state, was born in Newport News on the tidewater, has a brother thirteen months older, and was raised mostly by her father after her parents separated when she was young.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: They Were Such Good People
clips.summary: George recounts moving to the suburbs as white families fled, a departing neighbor telling her father she would never have sold had she known they were good people, a bus ride at about twelve when a white woman recoiled from sitting beside her, and her brother back from Germany rebuking a hardware clerk who called their father a boy.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: No One Can Take It From You
clips.summary: She tells how the words boy and girl were used in Virginia to demean Black people, how she resolved to become a lawyer and fight for civil rights and majored in history at Virginia Union, and how her postman father, active in organizations, pushed education as the thing no one could take from you.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Our Neighborhoods, Our Schools
clips.summary: George describes the totally separated world of segregated Newport News, watching the Brown decision and Little Rock on television, Black neighborhoods, restaurants and stores, and all-Black schools where the principal, counselors and teachers were Black, while her postman father worked a white route and came home at Christmas with bags of presents.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Where the Ships Came In
clips.summary: She explains Newport News expanding toward Warwick as white residents left, that African Americans there could register and vote, and the shipyard and military influx that filled the town with sailors at the first of the month, so that she could not go out except chaperoned by her brother or a neighbor.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: A Debutante in a Cadillac
clips.summary: George recalls the self-contained Black community with its own funeral homes, churches and drugstores, working as a soda jerk in a drugstore owned by Dr. Jones and Dr. Tucker, and being presented as a debutante at sixteen in white dress, gloves and pearls, riding to the event in her uncle's Cadillac in a dress that filled the back seat.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Attorney Marion Poe
clips.summary: She names the people who supported her, a friend named Mary whose husband was Virginia's first Black urologist, and Marion L. Poe, the first African American woman lawyer she knew, from her church, whom she admired and hoped to emulate, while crediting the bus incident with teaching her there was a difference because she was Black.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: My First Time Away From Home
clips.summary: George describes starting at Virginia Union University in fall 1959, the rules of the Baptist school requiring students to sign out, sneaking to Richmond on her own, and meeting Marie Sallason and Patricia Washington, roommates across the hall, with whom she got grounded by the dorm matron for laughing and carrying on.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: She Could Pass for White
clips.summary: She recounts sneaking down to basement dances and turning the clock back to beat curfew, driving to the Penn Relays in Philadelphia and staying at Patricia Washington's home, and how the light-skinned Patricia walked into a white barbershop in Richmond and got her hair cut while the others waited outside, a moment of resistance they found funny.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Two Ministers Pulled Us Together
clips.topic: Hearing About the Sit-Ins and the Arrest
clips.summary: George tells how news of the sit-ins spread from the Woolworth in North Carolina, how ministers Charles Sherrod and Frank Pinkston led the students in meetings and strategy in Richmond and prepared them for arrest and nonviolence, how she felt excited rather than afraid and never spoke of it, and how she came home, saw herself led out of the store on the night news, and knew she had to call her father.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: The Richmond Room
clips.summary: George describes marching downtown to the department store Thalhimers, where Black shoppers could spend money but had to eat at a basement stand and were barred from the segregated Richmond Room, which became the target because their money was good but their color shut them out, and recalls that they faced no sanctions and were greeted at churches with dinners while lawyers bailed them out.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: A White Man Spit on Me
clips.topic: The Arrest and Holding to Nonviolence
clips.summary: George tells how the next step was buses into Mississippi until her father forbade it because he did not want her dead, recounts the arrest with her long fingernails making her hard to fingerprint and her lawyer arriving to bail her out, and describes a large white man spitting on her as she walked out after dark, when, separated from her friends, she was ready to do battle but stayed peaceful as required.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: I Never Went In That Room
clips.topic: Teaching, Her Father, and the Rules
clips.summary: George explains that she later worked at Thalhimers over Christmas selling books but never entered the Richmond Room because access was what mattered, that after graduating she taught history about three years at Maggie L. Walker High School near Virginia Union, that her father told her on the phone only that as long as she was okay it was fine, and that the students accepted Virginia Union's restrictions and found ways around them without protesting.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: We Didn't Think About Gender
clips.summary: George says the students never thought about gender, grade or age, that she was eighteen or just nineteen, and that they only wanted to desegregate Richmond, ride at the front of the bus, and let Patricia Washington get her hair cut anywhere; she frames the February 1960 action as one incident and recalls Ella Baker's April meeting of student leaders at Shaw University, which Charles Sherrod attended.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Off the Welfare Rolls
clips.summary: George describes earning a master's at the University of Maryland to become a guidance counselor, then working in the late 1960s for a private company on a federal contract in inner-city Washington, counseling Black people to get off welfare, off drugs and out of jail and helping single mothers in housing, and ties the work to the movement as helping people gain their rights and become viable citizens.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: I Like to Give Back
clips.summary: She recounts mentoring and resume counseling at Alfred University, where she was appointed to the Leadership Advisory Board and named an Honorary Alumna, the renaming of the Women's Leadership Center as the Judson Leadership Center after a sponsor died in an accident, and the training she continues to do there.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Credit for Desegregating Richmond
clips.topic: The Fiftieth Anniversary and Her Silence
clips.summary: George explains that she never talked about the sit-in until the fiftieth anniversary two years earlier, when, urged to attend, she went back with her niece and nephew and found a plaque bearing her name and learned the group was credited with desegregating Richmond, an effect she had never realized, and reflects that she does not brag, preferring to talk about her husband and son, while acknowledging her students might find it powerful to hear someone who acted at their age.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Six Facets of Service
clips.summary: George describes Links, Incorporated, a national organization of predominantly African American women founded in Philadelphia, its six facets of service, and how her Springfield chapter mentors young girls and parents and supports a residence for women who have been incarcerated and on drugs, helping them overcome addiction, become better parents and find jobs and homes.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: A Write-In Win
clips.summary: She recounts the Links members who are lawyers, a judge and a UMass linguist, and her own civic roles in Northborough, Massachusetts: library trustee, chair of the Community Affairs Committee and the personnel board, and a reluctant write-in campaign her husband engineered that won her a seat on the Assabet Valley Regional Vocational Technical School committee.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: He Is My Boss
clips.summary: George explains her HR work and alternative dispute resolution that lets employees present grievances, and describes her husband Frederick's service as a selectman, board chair, and town moderator for ten years who facilitates the town meeting, appoints her to the personnel board, and reminds her that he is her boss, even calling the town administrator to ask how she had done her job.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: We Both Keep Busy
clips.summary: She describes how she and her husband both enjoy community work and support each other, his running the town's Applefest celebration with parades and fireworks, his human-capital consulting practice they can bounce ideas off, and why she would not want to be town moderator, lacking his patience and his ability to show no favoritism.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Just Keep Focused
clips.summary: George rejects the image of a wholly nonviolent movement, saying it is hard not to be angry when spit on, recalls people yelling names as they marched while they avoided eye contact and stayed focused, and remembers the reunion where she reconnected with Marie Sallason and Patricia Washington and learned the group of thirty-four were called heroes for desegregating Richmond.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Racism Is Still Alive
clips.summary: She shares that she believes racism is still rampant, that she was shocked to see an African American president elected and thought it was something her son would see, and that racism underlay the opposition Obama faced from senators and representatives who refused to let a brilliant man succeed.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: He Doesn't See Color
clips.topic: A Son in a Different World
clips.summary: George contrasts her generation with her son, who never knew the back of the bus, whose friends are mostly white, who wanted his hair braided in seventh grade as the only African American in his class and now wears it large like Bob Marley without hiding his ethnicity, and who, unlike her, does not read setbacks as being about race.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Civil Rights in My Blood
clips.summary: George says she has always had the civil rights fight in her blood and helps people who come to her at work, describing the subtle racism she sees, hiring a person of color or a woman but not giving them the full job or letting them advance, so the company loses them to another employer anyway.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Two Gender-Neutral Bathrooms
clips.summary: She recounts taking on transgender issues at her manufacturing company of over four thousand people, pushing to win two gender-neutral bathrooms near the manufacturing floor, and rewriting training so managers and employees stop asking inappropriate questions about a person transitioning, framing it as understanding and valuing difference and letting people live their choices.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: What's My Next Step
clips.summary: George closes by wondering about her next step, whether to teach or do more community work, taking pride in winning the two bathrooms once the budget comes through and in completing the manager training, and the interview ends with thanks for her story as part of the Civil Rights History Project.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: A Little Community Called Rolling Wall
clips.summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer with videographer John Bishop in Durant on March 11, 2013, Walter Bruce, a leader of the Holmes County Movement, recalls being born and raised in the country community of Rolling Wall to parents who sharecropped a few acres, and moving to Durant in the early 1960s.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: A Baseball League and a Gospel Group
clips.summary: Bruce describes running a community baseball little league for fifteen or sixteen years and starting a gospel group, finishing the eighth grade at the Rolling Wall school and the twelfth grade at Ambrose High in Lexington, being the youngest of thirteen children (six brothers and seven sisters), and the family's Methodist church reached by walking and crossing a creek on a felled tree.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: He Couldn't Give Me the Dollar
clips.topic: The Land and a Boyhood Standoff
clips.summary: Bruce recounts the family farming two or three acres they did not own and his learning to plow behind the mule, and a boyhood confrontation in which a white man kept an animal that Bruce believed was owed to him, refused to release it, and Bruce went and took it himself, an early instance of standing his ground.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: I Never Hated White for Right
clips.summary: Bruce reflects on living under Jim Crow segregation in a county that was mostly black, the all-white control and law enforcement, his limited contact with whites while growing up, and his refusal to hate white people even when he disliked how they acted.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: A Great Gift From God
clips.summary: Bruce explains that he became a carpenter from about his sixteenth birthday without ever going to school for it, calling it a gift from God, working with two friends as a crew and building houses for both white and black families across the county, homes he says are all still standing.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Fifty-Four Years of Singing
clips.topic: The Gospel Group and Movement Music
clips.summary: Bruce describes the gospel group he kept for fifty-four years, singing at homes and across Tallahatchie and Leflore counties and as far as Chicago, doing radio broadcasts and making recordings, outliving the other original members, and reflects on why gospel singing mattered so much in the mass meetings of the movement.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: What Good Is a Job If You Ain't Got No Freedom
clips.summary: Bruce recalls weighing whether taking part in the civil rights movement would cost him the building jobs he depended on as a contractor, deciding that a job without freedom was worthless, and how, going out to meetings at night across the county, he credited God that he was never harmed.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Not Too Far From Here
clips.topic: Emmett Till and the Early Network
clips.summary: Bruce remembers the community's reaction to the lynching of Emmett Till near Greenwood about fifty or sixty miles away, his sense that Till may have been involved in civil rights, and how he later came to know Medgar Evers and the NAACP and Aaron Henry, seeing in the Delta how white people treated black people and getting ticketed on Highway 12 to discourage him from the meetings, where SNCC was very active.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Registered in the Basement of the Post Office
clips.summary: Bruce recounts the 1964 Freedom Summer registration effort, when black residents first registered in the basement of the post office before being allowed into the courthouse, the literacy questions used to fail people (counting strands of hair or bubbles in a bucket of water), and the volunteers who stayed with black families and were not afraid.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: The First Fourteen to the Courthouse
clips.summary: Bruce describes the Meredith March Against Fear and the Freedom Democratic Party spreading across sixteen or seventeen communities, then the first fourteen Holmes County black residents who went to the courthouse to register in 1963, among them Hartman Turnbow, whose powerful voice he recalls, and the independent Mileston landowners who, owning their own farms, could not be evicted; he notes the firebombing of Turnbow's house, Bob Moses, and Alma Mitchell Carnegie.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: They Found Him Dead
clips.summary: Bruce tells of people from Holmes County jailed at the fairgrounds during the Jackson demonstrations, and of J.J. Young, a ballplayer he knew not to drink who was locked up as if drunk, had a bad heart, was refused his medicine, and was found dead, after which Bruce insisted on viewing the body, found no scars, and the community decided to begin a boycott.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: We All Going to Stay in There Together
clips.summary: Bruce recounts being arrested in Lexington over a march, accused of beating a drum he insisted was his to play, and locked up with about twelve people; he refused to be bailed out alone so the group would leave together, then describes a second march that so filled the square the police could not move their car, the demonstrators winning what they asked before calling it off, and his being treated decently in his only arrest.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Still President of the Freedom Democratic Party
clips.summary: Bruce explains how the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, formed to challenge the all-white delegation at the national convention in Atlantic City, elected him president, a post he still held at the time of the interview, and made him coordinator over seventeen or eighteen communities; he describes the precinct and county-wide meetings that chose delegates and the January 1965 congressional challenge to seat Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: You Can't Read a Blueprint
clips.summary: Bruce discusses why most preachers and teachers stayed out of the movement early, the teachers fearing white superintendents and the preachers wary of bank loans, with some ministers trading favors with whites and passing information back; he then tells how, after a teacher was fired, he answered the teachers who doubted him by saying they could not read a blueprint and threatened a boycott that got the teacher rehired, his lesson that divided people are kept powerless.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: The Clinic Started Right Here
clips.topic: The Lorenzis, the Center, and Health Care
clips.summary: Bruce recalls the white volunteers Sue and Henry Lorenzi, who came in the fall of 1964 and stayed five years, and the volunteer-built community center that he and Henry spearheaded, where a health clinic began in 1964; he contrasts the earlier days when black families could not afford a hospital and relied on a white country doctor's house calls and segregated waiting rooms with the community health centers now serving Lexington and Durant, part of a national movement he says started there.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Two Black Members on the Board
clips.topic: The School Board and the Cafe
clips.summary: Bruce describes his political organizing in Durant beginning with the segregated city school, where the mayor, a lawyer, helped get him and another black man appointed to the school board, and the failed push to merge with the county school that whites fought until a separate school was made and many white families moved out of town; he then tells of integrating the town cafe, where black residents were afraid until a few went in and met no further trouble.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Take Down That Sign
clips.topic: The Ice Cream Stand and the Dog
clips.summary: Bruce recounts integrating the ice cream stand that kept separate black and white water fountains and signs, where a man tied up a German police dog until Bruce warned the mayor to have it moved, and how he forced the colored and white signs taken down, after which black residents could go in either side, part of reclaiming the square where his group also sang.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Keep On Hauling and Haul Them All Back
clips.summary: Bruce tells how a grocery store fired a black worker after a fight with a white worker, prompting a Wednesday-night decision to boycott; several white families honored it, saying they could shop elsewhere, and a driver hauling a load of chickens turned back rather than unload, telling the owner to keep hauling them all back, and the store eventually went out of business.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Nothing But Blue Lights
clips.summary: Bruce recounts being called to a bad incident at a school in a neighboring county after desegregation began, driving over without taking a gun so he could not be jailed, finding the place surrounded by blue lights and gathered white people, and walking in to ask for the principal as a man pressed him on whether he was FBI.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: They Were Going Together
clips.summary: Bruce describes finding the black students too afraid to talk and tracking down the story behind the arrest of a young man: that he and a girl had been seeing each other, that she went home with him after her parents found out, and that he was jailed on a rape accusation, a charge later dropped when the girl could not be produced for the court date.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: They Named Them Christian Schools
clips.summary: Bruce recalls the 1969 U.S. Supreme Court Holmes County decision ending all deliberate speed and ordering immediate integration, and how some white parents enrolled their children while many withdrew to a private academy in West that the citizens' council started and renamed a Christian school, leaving the public schools largely black with black teachers and a black superintendent.
related_events: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Whatever I Said, Someone Would Try It
clips.summary: Bruce reflects on Sue Lorenzi Sojourner's account that his Durant meetings drew more people, money, and participation than anywhere in Holmes County, and on how he moved people past the fear of losing their jobs with patience and persistence, describing the country shotgun house where they met every Wednesday night and the carloads of white people who shot up the top of the building.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Shoot All the Building
clips.summary: Bruce tells how he organized three or four armed men on each side of the meeting house to return fire the next Wednesday when the carloads came through, the whites fleeing as if they had met machine guns, and how, after moving the meetings to a country church, SNCC members arriving spotted a gas-soaked rope the night riders had readied to burn the church down.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: We Going to Be Calling You for the Ambulance
clips.summary: Bruce recounts phoning the sheriff he calls a redneck, who would not act, and warning him that the next call would be for an ambulance because they would kill anyone who came, after which a deputy was sent; he explains that he himself never carried a gun, knowing he would be framed if caught with one, and only meant to call an ambulance the night he thought a boy had been killed.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: What Good Is Freedom Without It
clips.topic: Self-Defense and the Voting Rights Act
clips.summary: Bruce describes keeping armed people at the country meetings to make sure no one stopped them from gathering, and how the 1965 Voting Rights Act, with federal registrars, changed the movement and finally drew in the teachers and preachers who had been too afraid before, the teachers reassured once black students were attending.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Robert Clark Goes to the Legislature
clips.summary: Bruce explains how candidates for the 1967 elections were chosen at the third-Sunday county-wide meeting, following Ralph Hayes's 1966 run for Congress, with Robert Clark becoming the first African American elected to the Mississippi state legislature in the twentieth century; he describes vetting candidates by their support of the community meetings and the decision to run an independent slate to the November election rather than the Democratic primary, judging the odds better.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: They Knew We Were Together
clips.summary: Bruce describes working as coordinator on election day, driving community to community across the county where every place had a meeting and poll watchers, and how intimidation at the polls stayed limited because whites knew the black community was organized and unafraid, with Holmes County the strongest of its kind while neighboring Madison County still saw intimidation.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: I Never Put No Money in My Pocket
clips.summary: Bruce closes on Holmes County remaining the strongest movement county with the Freedom Democratic Party still alive, the support of his wife through years of constant night meetings, and his insistence that he never hated white people but only how they treated black people, was always outspoken, and never took money for himself.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: It Should Be Mixed
clips.summary: Bruce gives his final reflection that being the majority does not make a thing right, that he never believed in an all-black or all-white arrangement but always in a mixed one, and thanks the interviewers as the recording closes with the Library of Congress and Smithsonian presentation tag.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Young Communists Calmed by the Depression
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Louisville in June 2013, Walter Tillow, seventy-three and raised in the Bronx, describes a middle-class childhood with a mother who worked in New York State's unemployment insurance division and a father who ran coin-operated laundromats, parents who had been Young Communist League members in the 1920s before the Depression calmed them down.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: His Father Took Him to the Rosenberg Funeral
clips.summary: Tillow recalls being thirteen when the Rosenbergs were executed, his parents debating in the kitchen whether his mother should attend the funeral, his father deciding to take him instead, and a split family with radical aunts (one a Communist Party member) on one side and conservatives on the other.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: A Year for Apartments to Decontrol
clips.summary: He describes Parkchester, a Metropolitan Life development that quietly refused to rent to African Americans and kept apartments vacant a year to decontrol them, the housing movement that chained itself to those doors, and Cold War scenes of his father discarding old newspapers and an aunt unloading the works of Lenin she could not safely keep.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: A Clipping That Convinced Student Government
clips.summary: Tillow recounts attending Harpur College, a cheap state school full of New York City kids, and how in 1960 as the sit-in movement broke out the students won a sympathy picket of the local Woolworth by producing a newspaper clipping showing Harvard had done the same, overcoming the administration's resistance.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Standing in the Quad Against the Take-Cover Drill
clips.summary: He describes moving to the new campus, the statewide one-day shelter drill of the Cold War, and how fifty to seventy students defied the noon take-cover siren by standing in the quad, a dean defending them, after which they organized a Young Socialists group recognized and funded by student government.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: The Auditorium Was Packed for the Communist Historian
clips.summary: Tillow tells how the president barred a socialist candidate from speaking, the faculty stripped his power to bar speakers, and the next invited name, communist historian Herbert Aptheker, drew seven or eight hundred people despite an American Legion picket threat, then moved to graduate study at the University of Washington and Cornell in economic history, where breakfast gatherings of students formed.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: The Baltimore Afro-American Led Him South
clips.summary: During a New York newspaper strike, Tillow read clippings from the Baltimore Afro-American posted around Cornell, saw notice of SNCC's 1963 Easter conference at Gammon Theological Seminary in Atlanta, drove down with classmates, met Anne Braden, who steered three of them, including Charlie and Bunny Haney and their infant, to a movement that needed help in Fayette County, Tennessee.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: A Murder Trial That Started the Movement
clips.summary: He explains Fayette and Haywood County's high Black land ownership, how a murder trial in which prospective jurors swore they had no objection to African Americans voting prompted Korean War veterans to register voters, the white retaliation expelling sharecroppers, the tents set up on Black-owned land, and the Chicago support movement sending food and clothes for the cold winter.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Sleeping in a House With No Walls
clips.topic: Forman, Albany Teens, and the March
clips.summary: Tillow recalls that a Chicago packinghouse-workers committee had hired James Forman, later SNCC executive director, to coordinate Fayette County support, how he met Forman in Atlanta that summer, sheltered two or three Albany teenagers Charles Sherrod needed out of South Georgia, stayed in an unfinished house with no walls or plumbing, and after the March on Washington went back to Atlanta to work for SNCC.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Courting the Black Vote Privately
clips.summary: He clarifies that the Fayette County project belonged to the Bradens and SCEF, not SNCC, that a white farmer named Redfearn who had run for sheriff could only shelter them briefly because voter registration was dangerous and politically costly, and describes daily registration work, getting jumped once, and taking children swimming in Memphis with donated bathing suits rounded up by friends at Cornell.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Black Farmers Who Owned Their Own Land
clips.summary: Tillow describes John McFerren, the movement's leader who ran a gas station and grocery boycotted by whites, a rape accusation against him that the workers helped his family weather, and a draining registration process in Tennessee requiring applicants to read the Constitution before whatever clerk made each session take two or three hours.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Like Road Rage on a Country Road
clips.summary: He recounts that only the brave went to register, that ministers who allowed church speaking yielded some recruits, a courthouse-square march, and being waylaid and beaten by a lone man on the road, while noting the worst violence had passed with the tent-city beatings and drive-by shootings, and three newcomers in a ninety-percent-Black county restarted a movement that had slowed.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Five Pieces of Paper for One Stamp
clips.summary: Arriving in Atlanta, Tillow worked with Julian Bond in the communications office putting out news releases three times a week, writing outrage letters for John Lewis to quote condemning the Justice Department and FBI, and seizing on stories like the federal government buying post-office land from Byron De La Beckwith while he was tried for murdering Medgar Evers.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Getting Bonanza to Cancel
clips.summary: He describes using SNCC's new WATS line to track down performers booked before segregated audiences in Jackson, hotel by hotel, persuading the Bonanza television cast to refuse to appear where Black patrons were seated upstairs, a major publicity coup, and the four-page Student Voice newsletter mailed to several thousand Friends of SNCC off mimeograph stencils.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Above a Barber Shop on Raymond Street
clips.summary: Tillow describes the cramped seven-room office above a barber shop, the IBM Selectric typewriters with interchangeable type balls that let them dress up releases, the staff (Bob, Mike Sair, himself, Julian Bond, Dinky Romilly, Forman, the bookkeeper, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson), and how they took the building next door for an AB Dick offset press to do their own printing.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: A SNCC Record That Never Got Distributed
