clips.summary: He recalls the storeroom of unsold SNCC records, the Freedom in the Air album recorded with Pete Seeger that went undistributed after Mercury signed the Freedom Singers (Bertha Gober, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Cordell Reagon, Rutha Mae Harris) to a better deal, the Danny Lyon fundraising posters, and his year of this work until spring 1964, when Ella Baker and Bob Moses asked him to go to Washington for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Mirroring the Mississippi Democratic Party
clips.summary: Tillow lays out the 1964 summer project's plan to mirror the segregated Mississippi Democratic Party's precinct, county, and state meetings, get turned away, and hold parallel ones to build an integrated delegation for the Atlantic City convention, and recounts Bob Moses outlining it at a Washington fundraiser attended by Joseph Rauh.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: If You Bring an Integrated Delegation
clips.topic: Rauh, Ella Baker, and Adam Clayton Powell
clips.summary: He recounts Joseph Rauh, UAW general counsel and Humphrey ally from the 1948 convention fight, pledging publicly to get an integrated Mississippi delegation seated, the decision to open a Washington office staffed by Tillow, Frank Smith, and Barbara Jones under Ella Baker, and visiting Adam Clayton Powell Jr., who greeted Baker warmly but, as she predicted, did nothing.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: A Seedy Strip That Later Burned
clips.summary: Tillow describes opening the office at Fourteenth and U in Washington's African American commercial district, attending the Minnesota, Wisconsin, and Michigan state conventions to win support resolutions, finding help from Congressman Robert Kastenmeier, and being cautioned by Walter Mondale about a graduate student manning his table.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Ten Percent for a Minority Report
clips.summary: He explains the strategy worked out in a meeting at Bayard Rustin's office with Forman, Ella Baker, and Frank Smith: securing ten percent of the credentials committee to sign a minority report and ten state delegations to force a roll-call vote, and tracking who sat on the committee, including Edith Green of Oregon, who became a strong supporter after Hamer's testimony.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Renting a Hundred Rooms in Atlantic City
clips.summary: Tillow describes pressuring Mississippi parents while the freedom schools and the Schwerner-Goodman-Chaney crisis unfolded, Ella Baker keynoting the state convention, Reggie Robinson finding a hundred-room hotel for the roughly sixty-eight delegates, and the delegation co-chaired by Ed King of Tougaloo and NAACP state president Aaron Henry, with Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: After the Women Were Crying
clips.summary: He recounts the credentials committee meeting the weekend before a convention with no suspense but the vice-presidential pick, how Fannie Lou Hamer's televised account of being beaten and jailed moved the committee women, and how President Johnson cut away the coverage by calling a hasty press conference about nothing, an early lesson in television's power.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: They Sent a No-Name Delegation
clips.summary: Tillow describes the regular Mississippians sending a no-name delegation to avoid the attention figures like Eastland, Stennis, or Governor Barnett would draw, that they later worked for Goldwater who carried the state, and how the Freedom Democrats distributed five thousand copies of their legal brief while the regulars, counting on Johnson, said and circulated little.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Two Seats in the Balcony
clips.summary: He recalls the boardwalk vigil and delegates lending their badges, the regular Mississippians going home to empty seats, and the final offer that the Freedom Democrats receive only two at-large observer seats in the balcony, with the convention naming Ed King and Aaron Henry, while Humphrey and Reuther twisted arms and spread word the compromise had been accepted.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Luminaries Came to the Church
clips.summary: Tillow describes the morning church meeting where Bayard Rustin, King, Jack Pratt, James Farmer, and Wayne Morse pressed the delegation to accept the compromise, Morse warning that forcing a walkout would help Goldwater, and young SNCC people getting their first taste of Democratic Party politics as the steam went out of the wavering delegates.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Evans and Novak Started Red-Baiting
clips.topic: From Atlantic City to the House
clips.summary: He recounts the disillusionment that hardened after volunteers watched the compromise forced down their throats, the syndicated Evans and Novak column red-baiting SNCC, and how the episode led into the 1965 challenge that forced three Mississippi Delta congressmen including Jamie Whitten to stand aside, work taken up by lawyers Kunstler and Kinoy and figures like John Conyers and Elizabeth Holtzman.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: More Disillusioning Than Energizing
clips.summary: Tillow reflects that Atlantic City was more disillusioning than energizing, seeding bickering and the beginnings of black power as attention turned to Alabama and Stokely Carmichael in Lowndes County, and recounts being in King's hotel room amid the arm-twisting, where Rauh told the delegation he could not be with them if no one else was.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Only Castenmeier and Green Stood
clips.summary: He describes the pressure put on credentials-committee members and delegates to accept the compromise, a California judgeship and other favors dangled to make people fall in line, only Kastenmeier and Edith Green holding firm, and the sense that the insiders who knew Reuther and Johnson understood the deal better than the movement did.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Some Illusions Were Stripped Away
clips.summary: Tillow gives his reading that the Freedom Democrats came up against the Democratic Party and had illusions stripped away, that the rank-and-file delegates who were freedom fighters in their own communities saw through the two-seat compromise and refused it, and that, with no closing meeting, everyone moved on to the next thing, Lawrence Guyot and others continuing the Mississippi work.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: A Letter to Found a White Student Group
clips.summary: He recounts going back to encourage the labor movement into civil rights and southern organizing, running workshops at Highlander with leftist union figures like Jesse Prosten of the packinghouse workers, and helping Carl Braden and Jim Williams found a white southern student group that became SOC, building on Bob Zellner, Jane Stembridge, and Sam Shirah's campus work.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: They Met at Highlander
clips.topic: Going to Work for the UE
clips.summary: Tillow describes leaving for the United Electrical Workers in late 1965, meeting his future wife Kay Marlowe at a Highlander workshop, her path through the University of Illinois NAACP chapter and freedom-vote work to organizing a wildcat miners' movement in eastern Kentucky led by Berman Gibson, where men were charged with conspiring to blow up a bridge still standing.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: The Police Rounded Up Everyone With Big Ears
clips.summary: He recounts being sent to Lynchburg, Virginia to organize a General Electric plant, observing a rape trial with attorneys William Kunstler and Philip Hirschkop in which the police arrested every man with big ears on the victim's sole description, a youngster with a shaky alibi was convicted then later cleared, and the local paper was required to brand Kunstler a radical New York lawyer in every story.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Twenty Years in Pittsburgh
clips.topic: Anti-War Work and the Hospital Union
clips.summary: Tillow describes losing the Lynchburg election, marrying Kay, and moving to Pittsburgh for about twenty years of organizing, involvement in the anti-Vietnam-War movement and the 1965 Washington peace demonstrations, and joining the staff of the 1199 hospital workers union as it weighed expanding from Johns Hopkins to Charleston.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Living on One Income
clips.topic: The Communist Party and the Print Shop
clips.summary: He recounts that Pennsylvania's 1969 public-employee bargaining law let Kay organize community hospitals and nursing homes, that he joined the Communist Party and worked largely unpaid after the 1972 McGovern campaign, ran the print shop that produced the People's Weekly World in Camden from 1990 to 1995, and was fired from jobs at the Amalgamated and US Steel once his party membership was discovered.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: The Dollar Bill With Gregory's Picture
clips.summary: Tillow lists later efforts: trying to run a King-Spock ticket in 1968 and getting Dick Gregory and Mark Lane on Pennsylvania's Peace and Freedom Party ballot, John Conyers's 1978 shorter-work-week bill, anti-Reagan work, and solidarity campaigns for Cuba and for Chile after the 1973 coup against Allende, including helping a Chilean machinist resettle in Pittsburgh.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Closest to Her Mother
clips.summary: He describes Kay leaving Pittsburgh in 1990 to organize nurses in Paducah and at Audubon Hospital in Louisville while they commuted for fifteen years, his retirement and move in 2005, and settling in Louisville, Kay's home state and Braden territory, to be near her mother, who lived to about a hundred and one.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Illusions Stripped Away, and Recreated
clips.summary: Tillow offers his interpretation that Atlantic City stripped away illusions about the Democratic Party that have since been recreated, the absurdity in retrospect of an issue the party would not concede, and points to the Eisenhower-appointed Fifth Circuit judges, Minor Wisdom, Elbert Tuttle, and Frank Johnson, as the titans who saved the system judicially against an Eastland-and-Stennis-approved bench.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: They Just Weren't Compromisers
clips.summary: He closes that without the Fifth Circuit judges, aggressive Guild lawyers like Ben Smith and Bruce Waltzer and Len Holt, and the principled non-compromisers of the left such as the Bradens, Virginia Durr, and Ella Baker, the movement would have been in deep trouble, that the chance to beat the Dixiecrats through the Black vote went unused because Reuther and Humphrey had bigger fish to fry, and the interview ends with mutual thanks.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: A Marker on the Freedom Trail
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Summit (Argo), Illinois, in May 2011, Rev. Wheeler Parker Jr. opens with the recent unveiling of the Emmett Till marker at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market, one of about 25 planned Mississippi Freedom Trail markers, a moving event right down the road from where he once lived that recalled where they were in 1955 and what remains to be done.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Rich Delta Soil and Fifty People
clips.summary: Parker, who moved to Argo outside Chicago in 1947 but returns to Mississippi about twice a year, describes getting off the train in Money in the summer of 1955, a tiny place of maybe fifty people on rich Delta soil where the boys grabbed cotton sacks for the fields, and recalls his grandfather Papa, a no-nonsense man of means respected for carrying himself with dignity.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: The Shotgun House on Dark Fear Road
clips.summary: He describes his easygoing grandmother and his schoolteacher mother, the brand-new shotgun house next to his grandmother's on Dark Fear Road near a body of water that flooded under the house, the neighboring sawmill with its screaming saw, the surrounding cotton fields, and the one-room church school where a single teacher kept order and once sent his uncle out for switches.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: You Could Not Pick Cotton on Cornflakes
clips.summary: Parker recalls moving up to the uptown school with real classrooms, the church cemetery where Emmett's grave would later be dug, the few uptown stores, and a farm world with no fast food where you ate rabbit, chicken, and cornbread to work the field, and where children started school only after the cotton was in, so a boy might be eighteen finishing eighth grade.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Going Behind the Iron Curtain
clips.topic: Awareness of Danger and the First Days
clips.summary: He explains that everyone heard the stories and knew exactly where they were going, the South equated with going behind the Iron Curtain to Russia, a place with no protection under the law, yet the friends had so much fun with nothing in those first days, picking cotton half the day in the late-August heat, swimming where snakes were said not to bite, raiding watermelon patches, and riding around with his uncle Jai, while remaining aware of what could happen there.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Then Emmett Whistled
clips.summary: He describes riding three miles to the little town on Wednesday near the end of the day, going into the store to buy something and feeling apprehension when Emmett came in, then Emmett and Simeon Wright leaving and Emmett whistling at Carolyn Bryant Donham as she came out, an act so out of the blue that Parker says even now they do not know what possessed a boy with no idea of the danger.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Dust Flying on the Gravel Road
clips.summary: Parker recounts the boys rushing to the crowded car when someone said the woman was getting a gun, Emmett urging his uncle to drive while they found a dropped cigarette, the terror when a car appeared behind them in the dust on the gravel road, leaping out to hide and falling among the cotton bolls as the car passed, then regrouping while a girl with them warned that she knew those people and it was not over.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: A Dog and a Boy Who Loved Animals
clips.summary: He describes how the incident was not taken seriously and went unmentioned through Friday and into Saturday, the big shopping day when everyone crowded into Greenwood and Johnson Street, a stop at a juke joint called Four Fifths, and the drive home when his uncle Maurice ran over a dog and Emmett, fond of animals, started crying, revealing his sensitive side.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: He Was Leo the Lion
clips.summary: Parker reflects on Emmett, his next-door neighbor in Chicago, as a prankster whose idea of funny was not always funny, who once announced on a street that his two big cousins from Argo could beat anybody when Chicago gangs were dangerous, a natural center of attraction Parker calls Leo the lion, never angry or mean-spirited but always doing something to get attention, and explains the age distinction between a sixteen-year-old and the fourteen-year-old who looked up to him as the upperclassman.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: We're Looking for a Fat Boy From Chicago
clips.topic: The Men Arrive in the Night
clips.summary: Parker describes the large house with its screened-in porch and four big rooms, getting to bed around midnight in pitch darkness, and waking around two-thirty to men talking about the store and saying they were looking for a fat boy from Chicago, which sent him into pure terror and prayer, recalling a World War II film of a man promising God he would change if he survived a shark.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: A Pistol in One Hand, a Flashlight in the Other
clips.summary: He recounts lying shaking like a leaf, terrified of guns since a childhood incident, as a man with a pistol and flashlight walked past him and his sleeping cousins Curtis and Maurice Wright into the third room where Emmett slept, roused him while Emmett tried to put his socks on not knowing who he was dealing with, the men fierce and, in hindsight, likely drinking.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: The Protection Is Gone
clips.summary: Parker describes hearing the angry men, learning later his grandmother had tried to intercede, and the unbearable terror after both grandparents left and his grandmother was taken to her brother's house, leaving him certain the men, Milam and Bryant, would return, so he put his shoes on and waited alone through the night, never waking his uncle, planning to flee into the woods.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: They Said They'd Send Me Up There Too
clips.summary: He recalls his courageous cousin Curtis calling for help, his mother getting involved, the calls being made, someone in the crowd joking they would send him up there to get him because they still did not believe Emmett would be killed, and how, sleepless for over twenty-four hours, he was finally put on a train through Memphis to Chicago, scared again at the washroom signs.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Go Hold Your Mother
clips.topic: Return to Chicago and Mamie Till
clips.summary: Parker describes reaching Mamie Till-Mobley's house at 64th and St. Lawrence where the family sat grieving before the body was found, being told to go hold his mother, returning to Argo as if to a nightmare, and later being shaken to the bone seeing a jeweler named JP who looked just like one of the jurors, an encounter that he says changed his whole life.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: I Showed No Remorse at the Funeral
clips.summary: He reflects on how his religious upbringing taught him to exhale and let trouble go, and on the funeral, where he showed no remorse or sadness because he believed he would see Emmett again, though he weeps telling it now, describing a solemn crowd filled with disbelief that it could happen to a child, since children were for the most part exempt, referencing the book Without Sanctuary.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Six Years to Keep a Promise
clips.topic: Conversion and the Promise to God
clips.summary: Parker recounts visiting Mamie by bus as she went on with her life, earned a college degree, and remarried, and explains that the promise he made to God that terrifying night stayed with him until at twenty-two, six years later, he finally kept it and changed, finding peace and happiness with no regrets across what is now fifty years.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: His Granddaddy Was a Slave
clips.summary: He describes returning to Mississippi around age twenty-six, cautious about where he stopped, visiting the uncle who sheltered his grandmother that night and now lived past a hundred, and another uncle whose grandfather had been a slave, and reflects on his grandparents adjusting to Chicago, his grandfather who had planned to die in the South taking up farming near the Teal center.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: An Idea Whose Time Had Come
clips.summary: Parker recalls that in 1985 Rich Samuels of NBC, who met Mamie supporting the family of slain basketball player Ben Wilson, came and did a documentary on the Till case, the first real interest after years when people felt the boy got what he deserved, and how from there others began coming, an idea whose time had come, even as Parker insists on telling history truthfully rather than embellishing it.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: The Law Cannot Legislate the Heart
clips.summary: Asked about Reverend King's 1966 visit to Chicago, Parker, just back from the Army and starting his business, remembers the hate and animosity and people saying King should go back south, and articulates the conviction that recurs through his testimony, that the law can make you behave within a certain realm but cannot legislate what is deep in the heart.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Alvin Sykes and the Till Bill
clips.topic: The Push to Reopen the Case
clips.summary: Parker credits the unpretentious Alvin Sykes, who came with one reason to get the case opened after a Kansas case, with a pivotal role, recounting how they met with Eric Holder, who called Sykes a legend in his own time, and how Sykes pursued the Till Bill that President George W. Bush signed, allotting eleven million dollars a year for ten years to pursue civil rights cases dating back from 1970.
related_events: Emmett Till Unsolved Civil Rights Crime Act

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: All the White Men in Mississippi
clips.summary: On the 2005 reopening by the Justice Department and Mississippi officials, Parker describes moving from a pessimistic view that white men in Mississippi would never open the case, to cautious hope when he learned a woman, then a Black woman, would lead it, and acknowledges the FBI doing an investigation it would not have done fifty years earlier, while he remained a realist about the country's attitude.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: I Just Want to Know What Happened
clips.summary: Parker recounts the FBI taking the case to a grand jury that declined to indict Carolyn Bryant Donham, and the man from Ohio whose son sought immunity claiming he was not there, and insists he wants not jail for anyone but closure and the facts of what happened, which Carolyn Bryant Donham knows, reflecting too on a tense visit to Mississippi and on dealing always with human beings who can only go so far.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: All I Did Was Survive
clips.summary: Parker finds the deepest meaning in telling history so children can benefit, paying his own way to the marker ceremony so the story gets out, recalling a friend, Mike Smolow of Dominican High School, who was a white pallbearer at Mamie's funeral, and refusing to be made a hero since all he did was survive, scared, to tell the story that people deeply appreciate hearing truthfully.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Markers by the River and the Barn
clips.summary: He describes the designated stretch of highway, presented to the body by a senator and torn down many times, and the Tallahatchie County effort that placed markers at the riverside where the body came out, at the funeral home, at the Sumner courthouse they hope to restore, and at the barn where Emmett was tortured, things Parker says are good for history even when some people take advantage of them.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: You Cannot Change a Die-Hard Heart
clips.summary: Parker assesses the people working to right wrongs as sincere but bound to be frustrated because such efforts do not change hearts, warns against expecting more than they will get amid what he sees as a revival of racism, and urges them to know where they are and keep changing what change can be made, slowly, hoping God touches people's hearts.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: The Fire in Somebody's Belly
clips.summary: Parker closes on finding younger people more receptive than college students he met at the University of Missouri, telling both that progress always required someone, often a white person, to put the fire in their belly and stand up, citing Lincoln, Kennedy, and Johnson signing the Civil Rights Bill, urging Black and white youth to work together since the young will have to make the changes the case still demands fifty-seven years later.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: You're Going to College
clips.summary: Interviewed in Detroit in July 2011 by Joe Mosnier, William G. Anderson opens on growing up in Americus, Georgia, a farming town of peanuts, cotton, and pecans about 170 miles from Martin Luther King Jr.'s home, and on parents who prized education: a Morehouse-attending father and a mother who spent twenty summers earning her B.S. while teaching, making college non-negotiable for their three children.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Get Off the Sidewalk
clips.summary: Anderson describes total segregation in Americus: separate schools with secondhand torn books, a whites-only lunch counter, the segregated movie house buzzard roost, and being told as a boy to step off the sidewalk for a white man, conditions he tolerated only because he knew no other way until he resolved to leave for college, following his older brother to Fort Valley State.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: I Drove for Horace Mann Bond
clips.topic: Fort Valley and a Premier Educator
clips.summary: He explains the family could not afford the twenty-five-dollar monthly tuition at Fort Valley State, so he worked as a mail carrier and cafeteria hand, and at sixteen drove for college president Horace Mann Bond, the renowned Black educator and father of Julian Bond, traveling Georgia, Alabama, and Mississippi as Bond studied education for Black students.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: I Want Those Japanese
clips.summary: Anderson recalls the Americus Times-Recorder extra announcing the attack on Pearl Harbor and feeling personally attacked despite living under segregation, trying at fourteen to volunteer for the Army until his mother stopped him, then enlisting in the Navy at sixteen and refusing the steward's branch because he had not joined the military to be anyone's servant.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: The Most Educated Company in the Navy
clips.summary: He reflects, comparing his willingness to fight for a society that enslaved him to a slave told of emancipation, and describes his all-Black Navy company of seventy-five men, every one a high school graduate, as a deliberate test that excelled and became the first Black company to integrate a battalion at Great Lakes.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Doctors in Six Weeks
clips.summary: Anderson recounts being assigned to the Seventh Fleet under Admiral Halsey and dropped at Leyte Gulf in the Philippines where Japanese soldiers still hid in palm trees after the war, and how, having two years of pre-med, he answered a call for hospital corpsmen and trained for six weeks to serve as a primary care physician for servicemen, confirming his desire to become a doctor.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: I Married the Right Girl
clips.topic: Meeting His Wife and Martin Luther King
clips.summary: Out of the Navy and turned down by every accredited medical school because unaccredited Fort Valley State was not recognized, Anderson moved to Atlanta with his new wife, whose brother was a close friend of a high school senior named Martin Luther King Jr., a meeting he calls prophetic, and enrolled in mortuary science because its curriculum mirrored pre-med.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: I Met Ralph Abernathy
clips.summary: Anderson tells of a Montgomery funeral director named Loveless offering him fifteen dollars a week and a flexible schedule to finish his degree, where he met and grew close to a schoolmate named Ralph David Abernathy, who became godfather to his oldest child, while Anderson graduated from Alabama State College for Negroes and joked about blacking out the word Negro on tour buses.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: You Could Do Right Radio
clips.summary: He describes singing in a radio choir at Queen Street Church in Atlanta when program director Ken Knight of station WERD, the Good Words station, invited him to audition, and he became an afternoon radio announcer alongside a Patrick known as Alley Pat, gaining the broadcasting experience that would later help fund medical school.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: I Want to Be a Real Doctor
clips.summary: Anderson recounts his father urging him to meet Dr. William L. Reese in Albany, a graduate of the Des Moines Still College of Osteopathic Medicine with the biggest practice in town, doing minor surgery and delivering babies as a Black doctor barred from hospitals, and how Reese's appeal to the dean won Anderson acceptance to Des Moines in 1952 after passing the entrance exams on the spot.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: A Mortician and a Radio Man
clips.summary: He explains how three trades funded his osteopathic schooling at Des Moines: preparing cadavers as a mortician, and reviving a money-losing one-hour ethnic-music radio segment into a four-hour nightly program he called Gala Time by offering to work for free and sell the ads himself, broadcasting until midnight and studying the rest of the night.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: You're a Clandestine Mason
clips.summary: Anderson describes confronting segregation in Des Moines when, a Mason since his Alabama State days, he tried to join the campus Square and Compass Club and was told his colored Masonic order was clandestine and he did not belong with the real Masons, a slight he let pass rather than risk being put out of school.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: I Danced With Her Anyway
clips.summary: He recounts the dean warning him not to date or socialize with white women, and the dilemma when the white chemistry department chair, after a few drinks, summoned him to dance at a school function: dance and risk expulsion, or refuse and fail her course, until he had a beer, danced, and nothing happened, a moment he calls the beginning of the desegregation of Des Moines.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: No Blacks at Babe's Pizza
clips.summary: Anderson recalls Babe's Pizza, a popular Des Moines hangout that served no Black customers, and how during his interview trip he stayed at the YMCA, one of the few places in the country that never segregated, which led him to raise money for the Y for the rest of his life, and notes how slowly news of distant events like Brown and the Montgomery Bus Boycott reached Des Moines.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: He's Not a Troublemaker
clips.summary: Anderson tells how the same dean who warned him off white women personally phoned a Flint hospital administrator, Keith Balker, vouching for him as a fine student and no troublemaker, and how the house staff training committee convened a special meeting for him until a family practitioner moved to accept him, making him in 1955 the first Black to train outside a college-owned hospital.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: You Made a Promise
clips.summary: He describes returning to Albany in 1957 drawn not by the segregated South but by his wife, and how a Flint couple of professors, Bird and Ginger Edwards, both PhDs who spent evenings talking with the Andersons, helped his wife hold him to his vow that once educated he would go back to the rural South that needed him.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: This Woman Remembers Everything
clips.summary: Anderson recounts Flint surgeons offering to set him up in practice on a border street predicting he would draw white and Black patients alike, an offer with the ulterior motive of capturing his surgical referrals, and how his wife, sent room to room by the Edwardses, reminded him of his promise and steered him back South, crediting her with every major decision of their sixty-one-year marriage.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: They Will Be as Good to You
clips.summary: He explains weighing where to practice as a Black osteopath barred from most hospital privileges, and choosing Albany because his insurance-man father knew everyone there and because attorney C.B. King and Dr. William L. Reese welcomed him, with King's words that the people would be as good to him as he was to them deciding it, after which patients lined up the day his office opened.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Just Give Them Digitalis
clips.topic: A Country Doctor and the Trust He Built
clips.summary: Anderson describes the inadequate care Black patients had received, examined together around a desk and asked what's wrong with you boy, and chronic heart-failure patients given weekly mercurial diuretic shots when cheap whole-leaf digitalis would have helped, and recounts charging three dollars a visit, taking payment in hams and eggs, and never turning anyone away, a trust he ties to the thousand people who later followed him to jail.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: The Most Racist County in the South
clips.summary: Anderson describes attending Mount Zion Baptist, whose ninety-year-old pastor E. James Grant courageously served as principal of a school in Baker County, reputedly the most violent racist county in the South where Black people jailed in Newton were never heard from again, and how he gained privileges at a Black-owned hospital fifty miles away in Bainbridge.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: A Pistol on the Seat
clips.summary: He recounts keeping a pistol on his car seat when driving through Newton on house calls, resolved to take a ticket but never go to jail there because too many Black people had been lynched, describes Klan rallies one block from his home with flaming crosses he could see from his backyard, recalls attorney C.B. King being beaten by the sheriff inside the jail, and notes that Black people arrested were frequently beaten.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Officials Who Would Meet but Not Act
clips.summary: Anderson surveys Albany's Black civic life, the Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League, and women's neighborhood groups petitioning for paved streets and lights, and the white officials who would talk but not act, Mayor Asa Kelly and police chief Laurie Pritchett, both fairly liberal yet held captive by segregation, alongside rare fair employers like Miss B of Bob's Candy.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: They're Doing That for Me
clips.summary: Anderson recounts Pritchett asking whether this was how he thought he could make people like him, and tells of SNCC workers arriving quietly months before October 1961 to canvass for voter registration against a registrar always on vacation and a literacy test no one could pass, then escalating to small sign-carrying demonstrations downtown that drew arrests he watched from his office.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: They Had Nothing to Lose
clips.summary: From his office in the Harlem section two blocks from downtown, Anderson watched SNCC workers like Charles Sherrod take two or three people to register and get cursed and arrested, and describes them as reckless and fearless young people with no jobs, property, or money to lose, who had already slammed their wills and made out their wishes in case worse came to worst.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Let's Call It a Movement
clips.summary: Anderson tells how his stirred conscience drove him to convene the Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League, and women's clubs at dentist Ed Hamilton's home, where the name Albany Movement emerged and he was elected president because he depended on Black patients, had made no enemies, and was young enough for the youth to follow.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: All Across the Spectrum
clips.summary: He describes the range of Black response in Albany, from those who feared losing jobs and homes and stayed away entirely, to those in the middle who gave money but would not march, to those willing to throw caution to the wind, and credits a pent-up desire for freedom that Martin Luther King's example finally showed could be met without the historic resort to violence.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: I Know Somebody Who Does
clips.summary: Anderson explains that, none of them experienced in protest, he turned to the men his marriage had introduced him to, Martin Luther King and Ralph Abernathy, and how the Albany Movement agreed to invite them on the condition that SCLC would not come in and take over, then summoned thousands to Shiloh and Mount Zion churches across the street from each other.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: He Got Stronger Every Time
clips.summary: He recounts King arriving with Andrew Young, Wyatt Walker, and Abernathy intending only to speak and return to Atlanta, but speaking four times across both overflowing churches, growing stronger each time as Abernathy warmed up the crowd, until Anderson announced to the people that King was committed to the Albany Movement, a commitment Young later said Anderson had tricked from him.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: You Don't Face Down the Klan Intellectually
clips.summary: Anderson describes the board that ran the movement, Slater King as vice president, Marion Page as secretary-treasurer, and nightly mass meetings that ran for hours, and insists the risks were taken at an emotional rather than an intellectual level, since no one weighing the bills rationally would walk into the fire or face down the Ku Klux Klan.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Ready to Go to Jail Monday Morning
clips.summary: He recounts telling his three children at breakfast that he would go to jail not for any wrong but for their rights, and leading a couple hundred people who answered the Sunday call around the courthouse square; nothing happened twice, so he peeled off to his office, and on the third time around the marchers, including his wife, were all locked up.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: She Saw Something I Did Not
clips.summary: With his wife jailed, Anderson reflects on her: very smart, able to get him to do what he meant not to, the maker of every major decision in their marriage, who sacrificed to put him through school, grew up the eldest of four raising her siblings after their father abandoned them, and saw in him a future he could not see in himself.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: You Got to Have Your Family Here
clips.summary: He recalls leaving his wife in Atlanta when he first went to Des Moines with only six hundred dollars, until his rooming-house landlady Lola Reeves, seeing him pace and unable to concentrate, partitioned her single room into a living room, dining room, and kitchen so his family could join him, after which his grades rose and he found work, his wife teaching in Des Moines and then Flint.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: They Took Her to Lee County
clips.topic: His Wife in the Prison Farm
clips.summary: Anderson describes being devastated when his wife was taken to the Lee County prison farm with no word of her treatment, and how the Chatwell family, John and Richard Dean Chatwell, cared for his four children while he slept in a different place every night to avoid the Klan, and reflects that one can be afraid without being paralyzed given the Klan's reputation for murder.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: What About Our Petition?
clips.summary: He recounts personally handing Mayor Asa Kelly a petition asking for a biracial committee, then standing alone in the city council chambers to ask its fate and being told the council had decided in executive session there were no common grounds for agreement, after which the Albany Herald printed his name and address and the threatening phone calls began.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Hit Me First
clips.summary: Anderson notes that publisher James Gray, a rabid segregationist who later partnered in business with a Black doctor, and tells of the march of over a thousand people down through Harlem where a National Guardsman raised his gun and ordered them to stop, and Anderson answered that if he hit anyone in the line he should hit Anderson first, at which the guardsman lowered the gun and stepped aside.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: We Drew Straws
clips.summary: He recounts being jailed with King and Abernathy when moderator Lawrence Spivak kept phoning chief Pritchett demanding King appear on Meet the Press, King refusing to leave jail until officials would talk, then deciding the program was too important a venue to miss, so the three drew straws in the cell and Anderson drew the short straw and was flown to New York and briefed overnight by King's lawyer Clarence B. Jones.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: No Written Document
clips.summary: Anderson describes a second round of arrests and a closed-door meeting of three Black and three white negotiators that produced only a verbal promise of a biracial committee and a study of segregation, with attorney Donald Hollowell and King pressing for a written statement or press conference that never came, leaving King to say in the courtroom that he did not like it.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: They Closed Every Facility
clips.summary: He recounts being called back for trial with King and Abernathy before a judge whose decision was already written, the city reneging on everything promised, and the report that King had failed at Albany because he won nothing, even as Albany closed every public facility, the library, parks, swimming pool, buses, and lunch counters, rather than integrate.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Constance Motley Without a Note
clips.summary: Anderson describes King returning with SCLC staff, the injunction against further protest issued by hostile federal judge J. Robert Elliott, and the appeal before Judge Elbert Tuttle in Atlanta where Constance Baker Motley, facing lawyers brought from Emory and the University of Georgia, refuted every cited case without a single note as Tuttle watched in wonder.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: A 757 and I Can't Fly a Kite
clips.summary: Responding to Coretta Scott King's view that the failure to defy the injunction broke the movement's backbone, Anderson argues the real break was the city's reneging, and that though Albany won nothing tangible, like winning a 757 he could not fly, it changed white perceptions that Black people liked servitude and gave Black people the courage to refuse it nonviolently, which he counts as the end of segregation as it had been known.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: She Took Me to Tuskegee
clips.summary: Anderson recalls being physically exhausted by late summer of 1962 until his wife took him to the Tuskegee Veterans Hospital to be checked and then to her grandfather's home in Sylacauga, a minister, for spiritual revitalization, and reflects that without claiming to be a religious fanatic he believes a divine plan put him in Albany, since marrying his wife was what led him to King and Abernathy.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: You'll Never Arrest Him Here
clips.summary: Anderson tells how, returning to Albany years later, a policeman tried to jail him over an alleged illegal turn until Pritchett recognized him, voided the ticket, and joked that arresting Doc Anderson would only start another demonstration, and how the two reconnected during the filming of Eyes on the Prize, dining together with their wives and remaining friends until Pritchett's death.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: A Decent Man, Trapped
clips.summary: Anderson argues Pritchett was fundamentally a decent man, not a racist, who refused to let his police beat marchers because Bull Connor had shown that brutality before the world's cameras was the last thing to do, and describes returning to a desegregated Albany with a white colleague, Rhea Hyle, finding the federal courthouse named for C.B. King and the mayor, sheriff, and former police chief all Black.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: The Penthouse at the Windsor
clips.summary: Anderson recounts the crowning moment of that return trip, staying in the penthouse suite of the Windsor Hotel in Americus where he had once worked as a thirteen-year-old bellhop, and reflects on his children, two old enough to want to march and go to jail as high school students who found it fun, singing and ignoring the sheriff, until the dangers led him to keep them with the Chatwells.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Talk to the People Who Lived It
clips.summary: Anderson describes the visiting minority faculty series he has produced at Michigan State University for twelve years, bringing distinguished civil rights figures each February, among them Wyatt Tee Walker, Juanita Abernathy, Joseph Lowery, and Dorothy Cotton, and likens his students to doubting Thomas, urging them to come and talk to the people who went to jail and whose houses were bombed, because these were real people and not fiction.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: False Face to America, We Have Found You Out
clips.summary: William Lamar Strickland is introduced by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of Massachusetts in Amherst on September 23, 2011, for the Civil Rights History Project, and opens by crediting Worth Long, the poet of SNCC, with the line falsely attributed to Stokely Carmichael, false face to America, we have found you out.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Six of Us Were Black
clips.summary: Strickland recounts a teacher steering him to apply to Boston Latin School, where of nearly 700 boys only about 30 graduated and six were Black, and traces his mother's family to land near Macon, Georgia, in a town called Haddock, inherited from a Confederate-era plantation owner, Madison Gray, who left part of his land to the family.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: The Man Too Mean to Die
clips.topic: Family and How He Met Malcolm
clips.summary: Strickland says his father was killed in the Italian campaign in World War II and his mother worked for Raytheon, recalls a West Indian uncle, Jake Edman, a numbers banker he called the man too mean to die, and tells how his cousin Leslie landed in Charlestown State Prison at the same time as Malcolm X, so that when Malcolm later spoke at Harvard, Strickland mentioned Roxbury and Leslie and the two stayed close until Malcolm died.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Eleven of Us at Harvard
clips.topic: Harvard and the Secret of Writing
clips.summary: Strickland describes being one of only eleven Black students at Harvard, a tight nurturing community told to prove they were geniuses and not merely somebody's son, invokes Du Bois failing freshman English there, and recalls his teacher Mr. Russo telling him the secret of good writing is rewriting.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Free by '63
clips.topic: NAACP Youth Council and Race Awareness
clips.summary: Graduating Boston Latin in 1954 around the time of Brown, Strickland recalls his NAACP Youth Council involvement and its Free by '63 slogan, his uncle Gray who fled Georgia after beating a white man, and being admitted at Harvard into a graduate seminar with C. Eric Lincoln while attending Temple No. 11 to hear Louis X, drawn to Malcolm X's worldview.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Take Your Behind to Cambridge
clips.summary: Strickland recounts nearly accepting a scholarship to Howard after seeing a campus full of beautiful women, his friend Carl McCall who went to Dartmouth, and his mother and community urging him toward Harvard, in a Roxbury where the whole community knew your accomplishments and held you responsible to the race, told to be twice as good as white competitors.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Albany, Georgia, Not Albany, New York
clips.summary: Strickland tells how he assumed Marine orders to Albany meant New York but found they meant Albany, Georgia, calls the Corps a formative encounter with white America, a fundamentally Southern institution of officers and enlisted men many sent by a judge choosing jail or the Marines, and recalls catching himself humming the Everly Brothers at Parris Island and Camp Lejeune, a lesson in how an environment seeps into your consciousness.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: The Picture in Jet
clips.topic: Emmett Till and Northern Student Movement
clips.summary: Back at Harvard in the late 1950s, Strickland names the murder of Emmett Till, its picture displayed in Jet, as what struck his generation, recalls Howard Mumford Jones assigning Richard Wright's Uncle Tom's Children and Baldwin's Notes of a Native Son, and his joining the Northern Student Movement, founded by Yale undergraduate Peter Countryman with city projects across the country.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: You Belonged to Everybody
clips.topic: His Mother and a Lost Community
clips.summary: Asked to say more about his mother, Strickland calls her influence incalculable and says she devoted her life to him, then mourns the loss of the dense Walnut Avenue community of Jewish, Irish, and Armenian neighbors and shared churches, contrasted with Grace Boggs telling him organizers can no longer safely walk Detroit's streets.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: You Didn't Have to Teach People to Be Black
clips.summary: Strickland argues the younger generation is ahistorical through no fault of its own, lacking the Black newspapers and radio stations that gave continuity, with five corporations controlling the media, and describes new confusion over gender, sexual orientation, and identity among Black people from Africa and the West Indies who lack America's history.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Religious When You're Being Shot At
clips.summary: Strickland names his Boston church as Charles Street AME and says he is not particularly spiritual, joking that you become religious only when being shot at, but that he enjoys the church experience and will respond to a good preacher independent of whether he believes the theology.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: The Secret of Organizing
clips.topic: Jesse Gray and the Rent Strikes
clips.summary: Returning to Northern Student Movement work in an office near Harlem, Strickland honors Jesse Gray, the Harlem rent-strike leader and former merchant seaman who introduced him to the history of Black communists and taught that the secret of organizing is convincing people to organize themselves, collecting and banking rent money during strikes against landlords.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: A Bad Motorcycle
clips.summary: Strickland says SNCC was a major influence on Northern Student Movement's turn toward community organizing, and tells of Detroit's Kenny Cockrel, an organizer with General Baker who became a lawyer able to rap almost like Malcolm X, won acquittals for a Black policewoman and a Black autoworker, then resigned from the Detroit City Council as duplicitous before dying of a heart attack in his early fifties.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: He Never Tried to Convert Me
clips.summary: Relocating to New York in 1963, Strickland describes Malcolm X being everywhere, holding rallies on 125th Street near the Hotel Theresa alongside figures like Michaux, and recalls that although Malcolm visited his Boston home and they reconnected at Harvard, he never tried to convert Strickland, who would not give up spare ribs and chasing girls to join the Nation.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: The Fundamental Critic of American Racism
clips.summary: Strickland frames Malcolm X as the fundamental critic of American racism the way Marx critiqued capitalism, as hard on Black people as on white, exposing how they had been brainwashed and reframing the sit-ins, and a prodigious reader of several papers and magazines a day whom he once caught reading William F. Buckley's National Review because you never can tell where you come across a good idea.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: And There Was No New Law
clips.summary: Strickland recounts Malcolm X's point that when Bull Connor used fire hoses Kennedy claimed he could not intervene, but nationalized the Guard once Birmingham's Black masses erupted after the bombing of the A.G. Gaston Motel and A.D. King's house in May 1963, and that the FBI merely took notes while SNCC workers were arrested and after Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman J. Edgar Hoover said civil rights workers could not be protected.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: E.T. Comes Down From Space
clips.summary: Strickland relays Malcolm X teaching from Dwight Lowell Dumond's Antislavery and using the slave power and broken Indian treaties to make people rethink what they were taught, posing the thought experiment of an extraterrestrial learning that fifteen of the sixteen presidents before Lincoln were slave owners and that all four hundred treaties with the Indians were broken.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: A Media Cocoon
clips.summary: Strickland describes pressing his students on how the media brainwashes them, contrasting on-site Arab Spring coverage from Tunisia and Egypt with the absence of coverage from Iraq, Afghanistan, or Gaza, and recounting the protests against George W. Bush abroad, including a London visit so unwelcome the Lord Mayor compared him to William of Orange, none of it shared with the American people.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Congress Makes Its Own Laws
clips.topic: The Freedom Vote and the Challenge
clips.summary: Strickland explains the move from sit-ins to the vote as Robert Parris Moses went to Mississippi, the 1963 Freedom Vote, and the MFDP challenging three white congressmen by nominating Fannie Lou Hamer, Victoria Gray Adams, and Annie Devine, and credits William Fitts Ryan, who put forward the resolution forcing the white congressmen to step aside, drawing fifty or sixty members to rise and a vote of 149, short of a majority but enough to show how crooked the system was.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Washing the Blood From His Shirt
clips.topic: The Humbling of Fannie Lou Hamer
clips.summary: Strickland says the deepest impact of the MFDP was not political but sitting with Fannie Lou Hamer and grasping that her clarity about the world was infinitely superior to all his book learning, and recounts Bob Moses, beaten while taking people to register near Amzie Moore, washing the blood from his shirt so it would not scare the people.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: In Case I Got Killed
clips.summary: Strickland explains he married a woman he met in New York partly to partake of life in case he was killed in Mississippi, and recalls being at a Northern Student Movement meeting upstate on February 21, 1965, when word came that Malcolm X had been killed, an irreplaceable loss of someone who embodied the urban Northern Black person as Martin embodied the South.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Black Heritage on CBS
clips.topic: The First Black Studies on Television
clips.summary: Strickland describes putting together the television program Black Heritage: A History of Afro-Americans on CBS with Vincent Harding and John Henrik Clarke, the first representation of black studies on television, aired by CBS at five or six in the morning, later linked to figures Harding gathered when Coretta Scott King asked him to direct the King Center.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: From Freud to Fanon
clips.summary: Strickland tells how he found a new hero in Frantz Fanon, plunging into the Algerian war and the broader history of colonialism, including French African troops barred from marching in the liberation of Paris, and teaches his students that history is not abstract dates but the flow of how things are connected from A to B to C to D.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Are You One, Two?
clips.summary: Strickland recounts studying with Erik Erikson and Edwin Boring at Harvard intending to become a psychoanalyst out of love for Freud, a non-directive-therapy session at a Waltham mental hospital with a Black World War I veteran driven mad by racism, and his switch from Freud to Fanon and liberation as the field moved from psychoanalysis to drug therapy.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: He Turned Us Around
clips.topic: Selma and a Skeptical View of King
clips.summary: Strickland recalls meeting Martin Luther King a few times without being impressed, being on the third Selma march, captured on the Northern Student Movement bus in the Eyes on the Prize episode Bridge to Freedom, and how learning a deal had been made when King turned the marchers around did not enhance his view, which only later shifted through researching with a colleague who had been in Albany with King.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Martin Was Converging Toward Malcolm
clips.summary: Through teaching on Martin and Malcolm X and reading Garrow's Bearing the Cross, Strickland gained admiration for King, citing King's statement six months before his assassination that the whole thing must be done away with, the same disillusionment Black Power and most movement people went through, and asking why so many were sympathetic to Elijah Muhammad's description of the white man as the devil.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Go Organize Your Mama
clips.summary: Strickland tells how a colleague brought him to the Institute of the Black World in Atlanta after the formal movement died, amid Pan-Africanism and the name-changing he found ridiculous, recalls debating Stokely Carmichael's All-African People's Revolutionary Party by insisting the struggle was here, and a Howard concert bringing Walter Rodney and Garvey scholar Robert Hill.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: How Are the Green Bay Packers Doing
clips.summary: Strickland recounts Walter Rodney barred by Jamaica and Guyana and teaching at the University of Dar es Salaam, his own 1971 or 1972 visit to a Tanzania idolizing Nyerere, and Black American expatriates there who never learned the language and asked how the Green Bay Packers were doing, a lesson reinforced in Cuba about how deeply your own society has socialized you.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: We Ain't Doing That
clips.topic: IBW Splits From the King Center
clips.summary: Strickland explains that the Institute of the Black World refused the King Center advisors' wish to glorify Martin and take an oath of nonviolence, becoming independent with newsletters and students kicked out of Morehouse, and names the faculty drawn to UMass, Ekwueme Michael Thelwell, Chester Davis, and others he knew from SNCC, who invited him to take their places.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Ninety Bucks to Atlanta
clips.summary: Strickland describes commuting between New York and Atlanta for ninety dollars to teach, naming George Napper who became Atlanta police commissioner, Howard Dodson who became IBW director, and Jan Douglass and Pat Daly who ran it until the Institute of the Black World ended in 1983.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: A Left Nationalist by Way of Malcolm
clips.summary: Responding to Derrick White's history of IBW describing him as a student of Malcolm X and a political nationalist, Strickland says if he is anything he is a Malcolm X man, recounts Manning Marable calling him and James Turner left nationalists, and frames black studies through an African fable about who runs the school and Baldwin and Malcolm X as the two sharpest critics of American contradictions.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: There Is a River
clips.summary: Strickland names Vincent Harding a tremendous influence, citing his history There Is a River, a planned WETA series preempted when Roots aired and one Black show was deemed enough so Harding published the work as The Other American Revolution, and Harding's book on Martin Luther King as the inconvenient hero whose last five years were sent down the memory hole.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Every Rebellion Was a Response
clips.summary: Strickland says the surveillance of Malcolm X reached MI5 and Mossad and that the police are the military arm of the racist state, argues nearly every urban rebellion answered an act of police racism, and describes the purposeful targeting of exploitative merchants in Detroit in 1967 and after Newark, with the National Guard and media smashing everything to portray the revolts as mindless.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Why Malcolm X Was a Great Man
clips.summary: Strickland describes how Make It Plain grew from the Eyes on the Prize episode The Time Has Come, the most-responded-to episode, leading Henry Hampton's Blackside to partner with PBS, his role as chief consultant, and his being asked to write the companion book's introduction to describe as objectively as he could why Malcolm X was a great man.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Re-Invention as Method
clips.summary: Strickland summarizes his published critique of Manning Marable's Malcolm X biography as full of historical omissions, gaps, and unverified speculation, argues the re-invention trope places agency in the interpreter and could be applied nefariously to anyone, and notes the assassins were already named by Talmadge Hayer and in Karl Evanzz's The Judas Factor, from whom Marable borrowed heavily without attribution.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: The First Racial Division of Labor
clips.summary: Strickland recounts good early relations with SDS, their ERAP turn to community organizing in Newark with Junius Williams and Stanley Aronowitz, and the parting of ways when he removed white women from Northern Student Movement's Harlem organizing after finding them in basements drinking wine and singing Wobbly songs, exposing the people to a life they could never have.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Are You Crazy?
clips.topic: White Allies and the Limits of Love
clips.summary: Strickland tells of a white woman in Mississippi wanting to hold his hand at a voter registration as a symbol of togetherness, which he saw as a way to get them both killed because white people do not understand what other white people are capable of, and argues you need not love those marching beside you against the bomb, separating the wheat from the chaff among well-meaning white supporters.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: The Quintessential Anti-Bush
clips.summary: Asked about Barack Obama's election, Strickland calls it a potential turning point he did not get ecstatic about, noting the same share of white people who backed McCain had backed Bush in 2004 and that Obama won only because Black voters turned out heavily in Virginia, Florida, and North Carolina, while the world saw him as the quintessential anti-Bush.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Necessary but No Longer Sufficient
clips.topic: Black Studies and a Corrupt System
clips.summary: Strickland paraphrases Stokely that black studies is necessary but no longer sufficient because all are on a ship called America heading to hell, citing corporations paying a small share of the federal budget while the masses pay most through income and payroll taxes, the fifteen trillion dollar debt, exported jobs, and laws written by lobbying interests rather than the public.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: The Foxes Are in the Hen House
clips.summary: Strickland argues the regulators are captured, the foxes in the SEC, NLRB, and FAA, points to a graduate student earning twenty-two thousand dollars audited while the wealthy escape and the SEC destroyed investigation documents, and asks why there is no revolution and no opposition to explain the problems, naming a climate-denying Rick Perry while much of Texas burns.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: You Don't Drink Wine With the People You Organize
clips.topic: Discipline, the Panthers, and the Police
clips.summary: Returning to why he moved white women out of urban organizing, Strickland says you do not drink wine or sing Wobbly songs with the people you organize, criticizes the radical-chic lionizing of the Black Panthers by the white and European left, and warns that confronting the police, as an ex-Marine, means understanding what you are doing rather than announcing it, as friends killed like Fred Hampton and Mark Clark show.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: The Ballot, the Bullet, and Algeria
clips.summary: Strickland says America admits only little blips and never that the whole thing must be done away with, as Martin came to say, and that Malcolm X was working out how to really change the country through a national strategy of the ballot or the bullet joined to international struggle, invited with Che to a conference in Algiers and conveniently killed two and a half weeks before leaving for Algeria.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Once Upon a Time
clips.summary: Thanked for the long interview, Strickland offers final thanks, regrets the many people he should have mentioned, and says being lucky enough to meet great people and explain their significance, as he tried to do with Malcolm X, is what moved him to write a memoir called Once Upon a Time, closing the Library of Congress and Smithsonian presentation.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Recruited for the War Effort
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Smithsonian offices in June 2013, William Lucy describes being born in Memphis to parents from Catherine and Uniontown, Alabama, and his family's 1942 move to the Bay Area after his father, a Memphis Light, Gas and Water worker, was recruited along with many others to work for the Kaiser shipyards.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Everybody Lived Together
clips.summary: Lucy recalls growing up in wartime defense housing in Richmond, California, where Mexican American and African American families lived in the same projects and nearly everyone worked for Standard Oil, Ford, or Kaiser, and contrasts the segregated but good schooling there with the harsher segregation of Memphis.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: From Unskilled Laborer to Journeyman Welder
clips.summary: Lucy describes his largely self-taught father, who became a journeyman welder six weeks after starting at Kaiser and later opened his own mechanic shop, his seamstress mother who ran a restaurant, his older brother Joe Lucy Jr., and a typical youth of baseball, basketball, and football culminating at El Cerrito High School.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Materials and Research at the County
clips.summary: Lucy recounts working at Mare Island Naval Shipyard, then joining Contra Costa County's engineering department in 1953, studying materials research at the University of California at Berkeley to advance his career, and rising over thirteen years from entry-level engineering aid to the county's systems materials and research engineer.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Why Are We Not a Union
clips.summary: Lucy explains how, invited to join an employee association at the county, he came to believe the civil service system had overstepped its mandate by setting wages and benefits, how the membership voted after a year-long forum to become a collective bargaining union, and credits two old trade unionists, Ben Russell and the blacksmith Red Aiello, with shaping his conviction that workers had a right to a voice.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: A Bus Driver in Two Sectors
clips.summary: Lucy describes the fight to organize public employees, whom Franklin D. Roosevelt had excluded from the National Labor Relations Act, his argument that a public and a private bus driver deserve the same rights, and how the group drew the attention of AFSCME as reformer Jerry Wurf challenged longtime president Arnold S. Zander.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Out of My Zip Code One Time
clips.summary: Lucy tells how, after Wurf won AFSCME's leadership in 1964, he was asked to join the national staff, hesitated because he had only left his zip code once, and in June 1966 left county service for Washington as Associate Director of the Department of Legislation and Community Affairs, believing a union could be a vehicle for social change.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Chavez, Itliong, and the Grapes
clips.summary: Lucy connects his union work to the wider freedom struggle, recalling the United Farm Workers organizing led by Cesar Chavez and Larry Itliong against Gallo and the grape industry, and his view that both the farm workers' fight and the Southern civil rights struggle were fundamentally about economic exploitation.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: A Floor, Not a Ceiling
clips.summary: Lucy describes early department victories, securing survivor benefits for descendants of Panama Canal construction workers and extending minimum wage coverage under the interstate commerce clause to private hospital and nursing home workers, and argues the minimum wage can be both a blessing and a curse for low-wage workers.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: The Same Rights as Anyone
clips.summary: Lucy recalls Al Bilik as an influence in Washington and lays out the union's case that public employees deserved the same right to organize and bargain as private-sector workers, a presentation so new that public workers joined by the thousands even though few states had laws protecting the process.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Crushed in the Back of the Truck
clips.summary: Lucy recounts how in 1968 Jerry Wurf sent him from Detroit to look at unrest in the Memphis sanitation department, where men earned wages low enough to qualify for every poverty program, and describes the standoff with newly elected Mayor Henry Loeb III and the catalyst that pushed frustrated workers to strike: two men crushed to death by a garbage truck's packing mechanism.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: A City With Power, Workers With an Idea
clips.summary: Lucy tells of returning from Detroit to Memphis to work alongside organizers Jesse Epps and Joe Paisley, the strike growing into a sixty-seven-day classic confrontation between a city with total power and workers with only a good idea, and how Martin Luther King Jr. came to see it as a great contradiction.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: More Important Than a Lawyer in July
clips.topic: The Hidden Value of Sanitation Work
clips.summary: Lucy explains the early effort to keep the strike from becoming a racial polarization and how King made the analogy of people who work every day yet stay in poverty, with 85 to 90 percent of the workers qualifying for public aid, and recounts a Westchester County radio caller he answered by noting that by July a sanitation worker matters more than a lawyer for public health.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: A Leaflet at Every Home
clips.summary: Lucy describes asking the strikers to talk to their wives and leave a leaflet at every home they serviced, winning solid support in the African American community and lukewarm support among whites who believed the National had come to take advantage of the city, a suspicion fostered by the FBI under J. Edgar Hoover.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: From Boy to Uncle to Grandpa
clips.topic: The Sign and the Women's Role
clips.summary: Lucy reflects that beyond wages and safety the core of the strike was a demand for respect and dignity, and recounts how he and a local minister, working in the Peabody Hotel from a phrase by Reverend James M. Lawson Jr., produced the blocked-letter sign reading I Am a Man, which defined the struggle for men who had gone from boy to uncle to grandpa without ever being called men, and insists the strike could not have succeeded without the women.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: The Most Hated Man in Memphis
clips.topic: Reverend Lawson and the Black Church
clips.summary: Lucy portrays Reverend James M. Lawson Jr., a longtime ally of King who had studied Gandhi's tactics and became known as the most hated African American in Memphis, and complicates the story that the Black church joined instantly, noting the church had little union experience and took time to grasp the issues.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: The Ultimate Contradiction
clips.summary: Lucy recalls King aligning the strike with the Poor People's Campaign and coming to Memphis twice, his second visit ending in his April 4 assassination after the I've Been to the Mountaintop speech, his doubt that James Earl Ray acted alone, and the violence on the day of King's last march, which he argues, citing the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence, was largely the work of provocateurs rather than the community-minded young Invaders.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: There Has to Be Another March
clips.topic: Managing the Strike After the Killing
clips.summary: Lucy describes how the violence polarized the city and brought in the National Guard, his single meeting with King at the Rivermont Hotel to analyze the broken-up march, the decision that there had to be another march, and the work after the assassination of containing street violence and building a sense of protection for strikers facing an openly hostile police force.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: A Mediator From Washington
clips.summary: Lucy describes how, after the memorial service, President Lyndon Johnson assigned mediator James J. Reynolds, who brought in Frank Miles, and how the strike came to be seen as a civil rights issue even before King arrived, with well-to-do white women visiting sanitation workers' homes and pressure mounting on the stonewalling mayor.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Mayor Capitulates
clips.summary: Lucy recounts the strike being resolved twice before it finally ended: a recognition clause worked out with city attorney Janati that Loeb rejected, and a later agreement that collapsed when someone leaked it to the press under the headline Mayor Capitulates, after which Loeb, watching his poll numbers, walked back from the table for good.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Abe Plough Puts Up the Money
clips.summary: Lucy tells of councilman Lewis Donelson coming one vote short of forcing a settlement and blaming himself for King's assassination, the final agreement moving through the city council with industrialist Abe Plough quietly putting up the settlement money, and the irony of Loeb later joining a farmers' tractor protest.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: A Position of Neutrality
clips.summary: Lucy turns to the founding of the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, born as a reaction to the AFL-CIO's 1972 decision to stay neutral between George McGovern and Richard Nixon despite Nixon's record, and recalls how he and colleagues Nelson Jack Edwards, Charles A. Hayes, Cleveland Robinson, and William H. Simons called a Chicago meeting by word of mouth where 1,300 trade unionists unexpectedly showed up.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: From a Black Perspective
clips.topic: Naming the Coalition and Its Impact
clips.summary: Lucy describes taking a year to consult workers and labor leaders before a second convention voted, with malice aforethought, to name the body the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, open to all but addressing issues from a Black perspective, and credits it with helping diversify the AFL-CIO executive council, once nearly all white but for A. Philip Randolph and C.L. Dellums, into a body of full partners.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: A Special Brand of Evil
clips.summary: Lucy recounts how the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, holding apartheid to be an abomination, began around 1984 to challenge a free labor movement working alongside it, partnering with Randall Robinson's TransAfrica to put Black thought into foreign policy and forming the Free South Africa Movement under President Reagan.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Twenty-Seven Years in Prison
clips.summary: Lucy describes Nelson Mandela's eventual release and presidency and explains the dual purpose of Mandela's 1990 tour, to let him project his vision of a non-racial South Africa and to let the country see a man who had spent twenty-seven years in prison for that thought, and reflects on America's long accommodation of apartheid.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: The Problem of Our Country Is Race
clips.summary: Lucy closes by weighing whether America needs a truth and reconciliation commission like South Africa's, arguing for mandatory voting to expose the divisions over who should participate in democracy, and naming race, carried into the immigration debate over who was here first, as the enduring problem of the country.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: My Dad Just Got Back From the War
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Malibu in April 2013, Willie Leventhal opens with his roots: born in New York in 1946 to a chiropractor father and a teacher mother who became a USC special-education professor, a family of educators and immigrant shoe-store owners on Staten Island, and the postwar move to California where Hubert Humphrey and the 1948 Democratic convention were his father's lodestars.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Ramona Gardens and East LA
clips.summary: Leventhal describes living in the Ramona Gardens federal housing project around 1948, his mother working in the campaign of Edward R. Roybal, his grandfather Morris Segal who ran New York's veterans and vocational education, and a childhood mixing Jewish, Hispanic, and Black East LA before the family moved to North Hollywood and then the South Bay after his parents divorced.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: The Word Kike and the Jackie Robinson Story
clips.summary: He recalls being one of few Jewish kids among Catholic ballplayers who used the word kike as a casual slur, the painful confusion of the Christ-killer charge of that era, and seeing the film The Jackie Robinson Story at age five, which fused his love of baseball with his first reckoning with racial prejudice.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: He Was a Healer, Not Just a Ballplayer
clips.summary: Leventhal explains that what fascinated him about Robinson was not athletic skill but that Robinson confronted the people hurting others and healed the wound, recounts his mother forcing his Jewish confirmation at fifteen after he resisted, and names the athletic Jewish role models, Hank Greenberg, Sandy Koufax, Al Rosen, and his friend Mike Vogel, who countered the Woody Allen stereotype.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Dr. King Found Me Before the Peace Corps Did
clips.summary: He describes John F. Kennedy as a generational hero whose call to service resonated, the Brooklyn Dodgers arriving with three Jewish players, and Dr. King coming to UCLA in 1965 when Leventhal was eighteen to ask for volunteers for the Summer Community Organization and Political Education project, known as SCOPE.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
