clips.title: This Is About Voting
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts that before King came, Jackie Robinson had been a larger influence than King, but he followed the news when King and the SCLC entered Selma on January 1st at the request of local educators and leaders like Amelia Boynton, and how he argued with a girlfriend's stepfather that registering to vote in a democracy could not be wrong.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Scared, But Ready to Go
clips.summary: He tells how King's appeal at UCLA left him wanting to go but afraid, until a Jewish friend he had played football with confronted him, and describes the campus organizers Joel Siegel and Rick Tuttle, his first arrest in Americus, and his relief when the police car turned toward the jail rather than out into the country.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: The Three Versions of Love
clips.summary: Leventhal recalls King recruiting at UCLA in person and the two things that struck him in the speech: King's discussion of the Greek words for love, agape, philia, and eros, and his joke that Baptist preachers do not give short sermons, presenting a soft-spoken, likable man rather than the famous orator.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: A Pulitzer I Would Not Want
clips.summary: He describes Franklin Murphy introducing King as the moral leader of the country while the FBI tried to sabotage every venue, and recounts how a publisher wanted sex material about King added to his manuscript, which he refused, contrasting himself with David Garrow and Taylor Branch, who used Hoover's files and won Pulitzer Prizes.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: An Editorial on My Friend the Football Player
clips.summary: Leventhal reflects on the stress his involvement placed on his ailing mother, the religious connection he found in his Judaism, and an editorial he wrote in high school about an African American all-American football player from a party who was denied a home because he was Negro, written in support of the spirit of the March on Washington he was too young to attend.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: By the End of Summer, the White People Looked Different
clips.summary: He frames his conversion as the immersion of living in the Black community, where within weeks Black neighbors came to look ordinary and individual rather than different, and where, because white people tried to kill him, by summer's end it was white people who looked different, an acculturation his sociology background helped him name.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: He Was the General, We Were the Troops
clips.summary: Leventhal explains that SCOPE, developed at Hosea Williams's request and approved by the SCLC board in December, targeted 120 counties like a military battle plan drawn by a World War II veteran of Patton's tank brigade, and mentions Jim Colaianni of Ramparts who declined to co-found Rolling Stone with the young Jann Wenner.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: They Wanted 2,000, They Got 500
clips.summary: He describes the 120 chosen counties as winnable swing counties where 30 or 40 percent more Negro voters could oust the racists, contrasts them with all-Black-excluded Lowndes County and its Freedom Organization, and notes that more civil rights workers were killed in 1965 than in 1964, so only 500 of a hoped-for 2,000 volunteers came, almost all white.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: The Best Week of Education I Ever Had
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts the SCOPE orientation at Morehouse, recruiting teams sent out during Selma, and an all-star roster of speakers including King, Bayard Rustin, A. Philip Randolph, C. Vann Woodward, John Hope Franklin, Septima Clark, Vernon Jordan, Charles Morgan, Clarence Mitchell, Michael Harrington, and John Doar, who taught them how to file reports.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Muslims and Hindus and Non-Believers
clips.summary: He remembers King closing his orientation address by naming Jews and Gentiles, Black and white, Muslims and Hindus and non-believers, a far-reaching call to heal divisions of race and religion that Leventhal traces to King's time in India, and a Swedish runner's reminder that the world saw Alabama as being about America itself.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: It Freed the Whites Too
clips.summary: Leventhal notes Alabama's later unanimous pardon of the Scottsboro Boys, white allies like Clifford and Virginia Durr and Aubrey Williams, and his visit to Koinonia Farm near Americus, home of Clarence Jordan and birthplace of Habitat through Millard and Linda Fuller, where he took his rest leave after jail.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: A Cow Pasture and a Great Local Leader
clips.summary: He describes a water hole at Koinonia and a baseball field in a Round Oak cow pasture as his two country firsts, then his assignment to Bibb County and Macon, Georgia, where the SCLC sent volunteers because of strong local leaders William P. Randall and the lawyer Tom Jackson, who became the first Black judge there.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: You Go Where I Tell You
clips.summary: Leventhal tells how Larry Scott Butler's group was reassigned to Barbour County, the home of George Wallace, to raise Helen, and how Hosea Williams ended any debate by declaring he was the general and the volunteers were the troops, with Willie Bolden enforcing that the SCLC, not the white students, ran everything.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Jackie on the Front Lines
clips.summary: He recounts that SNCC disliked SCOPE workers being told to dress preppy and avoid flaunting interracial couples, and praises Jackie Robinson going at King's request into Birmingham, St. Augustine, and burned-out Albany churches in rural Southwest Georgia, photographed with Wyatt Tee Walker, putting himself in harm's way again and again.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: My First Summer Off the Diamond
clips.summary: Leventhal explains this was to be his first summer without baseball since age ten after an injury-shortened high school career on a team that sent eleven players to Division One and two to the majors, and how, back at UCLA, his letter about playing in the South got him a tryout, while fellow worker Willie Bowens came home with him.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Lots of Willies
clips.summary: He describes William P. Randall taking the UCLA students to a different Macon church every night for mass meetings, recruiting young people like Willie Bowens from East Macon where Leventhal lived with the Simmons family, and Willie Bowens offering to get him a tryout with the Macon Bombers, which he attended wearing his football jersey.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: The Bread Truck Team Bus
clips.topic: Getting to Round Oak and a First Play
clips.summary: Leventhal recalls Terrell Simmons, whose bread truck served as the team bus, riding forty miles to a cow-pasture diamond in Round Oak where he first noticed the cow patties, and his first ball in the field bobbling out of his glove before a quick recovery and throw, a close play ruled out that he believed the umpire gave him on purpose as a welcome to the white kid working with Dr. King.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Eleven Times On Base
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts batting without any practice because he did voter registration on weekdays, reaching base eleven of fifteen times mostly on long fouled-off walks, driving in a tying run with a sacrifice fly, and a hit deep to center in Milledgeville that brought a runner home standing up.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Ralph Saved Me That Game
clips.topic: Errors, a Slugger, and a Reunion
clips.summary: He describes an error at the integrated Air Force field in Warner Robins, his teammate Ralph Eason hitting a ball clear out of the lights to win the game, and a later Macon Bombers reunion and clinic where a longtime policeman, knowing the only white Bomber must have been a civil rights worker, shared a moment of recognition, at historic Luther Williams Field where Jackie Robinson once played.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: You Want Birmingham, or You Want Business
clips.summary: He recounts being the only one arrested in Macon while Rev. Schiller was the only one beaten, explaining that William P. Randall had pressed the town's leaders to choose business and quiet over the publicity of Birmingham or Selma, with the white citizens council photographing the workers from across the courthouse.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: We Have to Start Making Some Numbers
clips.summary: Leventhal describes Diane Hirsch and Gloria Wise, the stalling of the voting rights bill so Southern legislators could wait the volunteers out, and the decision, after wasting gas on people too afraid to come downtown, to bring registrants directly to the courthouse, where he signed up seven more in twenty minutes.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Nigger Lover and a Smart Aleck
clips.summary: He tells how, working the bars and pool halls of Broadway, his roommate Ken Long's car was ticketed and an officer, believing Leventhal had made a remark, arrested him as he phoned the office from a barbershop, and how he sat nervous in the back seat over which way the car would turn before it turned right toward the jail.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Chief, What Are Your Officers Doing
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts being out of jail within ten minutes when William P. Randall intervened, filmed crossing to Randall's office by Fred Hoffman, the SCOPE office that later became Phil Walden's Capricorn Records, and Randall barking the police chief into dropping the unknown charge so that the apology spread among the officers, leaving Macon safer than Americus where shots came without warning.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: He Hit the Gas Instead of the Brake
clips.summary: Leventhal tells of his second Macon arrest when William P. Randall's nephew William C., sixteen and eager to impress two Black girls, drove Ken's car without power steering into a parked car in a Black man's yard, leading to arrests for the crash and a knife, and a sixty-dollar fine his father grudgingly paid.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Riflemen at the Church Doors
clips.summary: He recounts being called to Americus the night after Andrew Whatley was killed, with four women jailed, Dick Gregory leading marches, and Hosea Williams running night marches, sleeping in church pews guarded by two young Black men with rifles, brought in as reinforcements for what Williams meant to make the Selma of the summer.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: You Better Take King and Not SNCC
clips.summary: Leventhal frames nonviolence as the dramatizing of injustice from King's Letter from Birmingham Jail, argues the movement's whole death toll was less than a single day of the Arab Spring, and recounts the Kennedy Library tape that David Garrow misrepresented, in which Kennedy told three Birmingham businessmen their laws were the problem and they should be glad it was King and not the tougher SNCC, sons of bitches he meant as a compliment.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: The Kid Who Kept Raising His Hand
clips.summary: Leventhal describes becoming known for volunteering at every mass meeting, being sent with Rev. Schiller in their suits to tell the media of a press conference at Andrew Whatley's funeral, and his father's silent pride, then his first words, when Leventhal told him he had decided to go South.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: But I'm a Baptist and This Is a Baptist Church
clips.topic: John Lewis at the Church Door
clips.summary: He recalls going with John Lewis to integrate a Baptist church, where Fire Chief Henderson, tape recorder in hand, refused them and Lewis, a former seminarian, quoted scripture on the equality of all in Christ until the flustered chief ordered their arrest, footage that ran on CBS and the national networks.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: We Are Going to Dramatize the Churches
clips.summary: Leventhal explains that Hosea Williams targeted the First Baptist and First Methodist churches because the SNCC-then-SCOPE worker Lena Turner had written every white church and only those two refused, that the Methodist national body had forbidden exclusion, and praises Sammy Mahone's active Sumter County movement organization today.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: You Lead the Group
clips.topic: The Methodist March and a Segregated Jail
clips.summary: He recounts Willie Bolden handing him the march to the Methodist church because Bolden could not be arrested, the line met by men with blackjacks, a prayer before arrest, the photograph that ran on front pages from the LA Times to the New York Times, and Sheriff Fred Chappell segregating them in jail against the 1964 Civil Rights Act while firemen fired slingshots and ball bearings at released prisoners.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Too Prominent to Kill
clips.summary: He tells of Warren Fortson, Jimmy Carter's lawyer and brother of the sitting secretary of state, run out of town for proposing a biracial council, of Red Heffner who left McComb after hosting interracial meetings, and of a Selma family solving an interracial romance by simply declaring their daughter no longer white.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: I Could Not Live It
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts being jailed with white men who had thrown glass at Black children and who talked so vilely he covered his ears, and admits that by summer's end, between football, the shootings, and daily fear, he found he could practice nonviolence as a tactic but could not be spiritually nonviolent as King could.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: A Guy From Grant High, Down in Americus
clips.summary: He tells of getting shot at from six feet away, then running into Rich Adler, a Hall of Fame gymnast from his Los Angeles high school who had joined SNCC and been beaten, and Adler's idea to organize the maids working eighty hours for twenty dollars a week by driving them to the Freedom Center.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: They Wouldn't Get in the Car
clips.summary: Leventhal describes driving Fred Hoffman's Citroen, the only one in the state and a marker of outsiders, around scattered Black neighborhoods to recruit maids who feared riding with two white boys lest the shooters miss them and hit the Black passengers, and noticing a pickup truck beginning to follow them.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Bang, Bang, Bang From the Next Truck
clips.summary: He recounts rolling down the window expecting a thrown brick because his housemate Bill Raul had been beaten with one, only for a pickup to pull alongside and a passenger to fire a gun a few feet away, and finding no bullet holes afterward, leaving him unsure to this day whether the rounds were real or blanks.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: The Black Guys Knew to Duck
clips.topic: A Second Shot and a Lasting Fear
clips.summary: Leventhal describes being shot at again the next day, hearing the bullet hit the bushes, and diving for cover while the Black men beside him, knowing the shooters wanted the white worker, did not, and explains that whites hated white civil rights workers as traitors and white niggers more than they hated Black activists.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: You Can't Leave, We Need You Here
clips.summary: He recounts staying nearly three extra weeks past the August project end for the September election in Macon after registering over three thousand voters, while everyone else went home, and the candidacy of Rev. Marcus Moon, the first Black to run for Macon office since Reconstruction.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Signed by the Sixth, Watts Blew on the Eleventh
clips.summary: Leventhal relays Rick Tuttle's argument that SCOPE was critical because it kept pressure on to get the Voting Rights Act signed on August 6th, before the Watts riots blew up on the 11th, since the Southern legislators were stalling to strip the enforcement clause that the 1957 law had lacked.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: It's Not 1965, I Have to Calm Myself Down
clips.summary: He describes the post-traumatic stress that followed him home, the lifelong unease at cars behind him, the campus psychologists at UCLA likening it to Peace Corps readjustment, and his insistence that SNCC has never come to grips with how PTSD shaped their hostility toward King and their sense of unrecognized sacrifice.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: The First Person I Saw Was a Black Sheriff
clips.summary: Leventhal tells of his dread of going to Hayneville, where Jonathan Daniels was killed, dissolving when the first person he saw there was a Black sheriff, and recalls white Macon allies like Betty Adams, the first white woman in the postal department, who hosted the town's first integrated church meeting at her home.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: If You're Still Here in 24 Hours, You're Dead
clips.summary: He recounts election night, taking a call at the SCOPE office from a man whose voice turned from friendly to menacing with a death threat for carrying Negroes to vote, and William P. Randall insisting, against Leventhal's drunken refusal, on driving him to the airport himself the next morning.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Commit a Year of Your Life
clips.summary: Leventhal describes being among about forty asked to join the SCLC field staff and commit a year, his decision to stay only two weeks for the election since joining would have cost his student deferment, and the August 28th gathering at Abernathy's West Hunter Street Baptist Church where King came to thank them.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: He Was Like the Youth Minister
clips.summary: He recounts King reviewing their tally of registered voters, explaining his controversial 1965 stand against the war so they could defend it, and taking questions in a small group, where Leventhal, not awestruck because he had met his heroes, found King soft-spoken and kind, a famous man at pains to put young people at ease.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Taylor Is a Coward
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts being jailed under a police chief who was a former FBI agent and a psychiatrist tied to Hoover, and charges that Taylor Branch and David Garrow made money exploiting the FBI's acknowledged smear campaign against King, trashed SCOPE, and cut allies like Harris Wofford from their accounts out of ego.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: We're Going to Sabotage SCOPE
clips.summary: He describes Willie Ricks telling him to leave Americus, the Holly Springs meeting of May 1965 where Stokely Carmichael ordered SCOPE sabotaged over Ralph Featherstone's objection, and the late-life moment when Carmichael, beside Louis Farrakhan, told followers never to disparage King because King never attacked back.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: It Only Took You 40 Years
clips.summary: Leventhal tells of Willie Ricks finally befriending him forty-five years later after telling him in 1965 that a Black state trooper's beating had made him hate all white people, likens it to Malcolm X's change after Mecca, and insists the true history of SCOPE includes the 500 who went South and lived with Black families.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Time to Get Over It
clips.summary: He argues that the criticism of King for not riding the buses ignored that King had children while the riders were single, attributes much SNCC resentment to post-traumatic stress and depression, and praises Sammy Mahone, the former SNCC leader in Americus, as the model of inclusion who embraced both SNCC and SCOPE.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Our Coordinator Is Here
clips.topic: Named to Lead in the West
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts returning to a meeting where Gwen Green announced the coordinator had arrived, looking behind him before realizing she meant him, and describes the Western Christian Leadership Conference under Rev. Thomas Kilgore, the SCLC's regional office, and an Operation Breadbasket effort modeled on Jesse Jackson's in Chicago.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Work to Do Where I Live
clips.summary: He describes the WCLC board and its multi-state western region, Gwen Green fighting as a woman to lead it, his own modest organizing in Northern and Southern California, the white Macon critics who asked whether LA had no problems of its own, and King's posthumous Look magazine appeal that drew him toward the Poor People's Campaign.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: They're Going to Bomb My Place
clips.summary: Leventhal tells how his father threatened to cut him off if he joined the Poor People's Campaign, and how, after Black children visited his Santa Monica apartment, his landlord relayed a bomb threat, leading him, on his rabbi's and the police's advice, to buy a shotgun for protection.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Drop the Gun, They Counted to One
clips.summary: He recounts calling the ex-cop landlord to verify the threat, the landlord reporting him as menacing with a shotgun, and city attorney Herbert Lapine and two officers confronting him in his alley with guns drawn, counting down as he insisted the open shotgun could not fire before he dropped it and they seized it without taking his report.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Don't Touch Me, You Can Arrest Me
clips.summary: Leventhal describes returning repeatedly for a property receipt until officers arrested him in the phone booth for disturbing the peace, twisting his arm, and how outside the booking room he knocked an officer down before four others pulled him off and slammed his head on the ground until he passed out, then declined a deal to leave town.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Eight of Ten Said I Wasn't Crazy
clips.summary: He recounts a threatening-phone-call charge and a psychiatric hold meant to lead to commitment, the jail psychiatrist Ronald Markman clearing him as sane, a second bogus battery arrest and 112 days held while chief psychiatrist Marcus Crahan wrote a damning report, and how, needing two psychiatrists to commit him, the system sent him to ten, the last ones quietly warning he was being railroaded as Herbert Lapine vowed to put him away for life.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Two Hundred and Twelve Days Later
clips.summary: He recounts a Nation of Islam inmate cutting his eye with a razor-bladed bar of soap, the deputies warming to him afterward, and how, with no psychiatrists left to enable a commitment, the court commuted his six-month sentence to time served on the condition of outpatient counseling, a deal his father urged him to take.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Think What They Can Do to a Poor Black Person
clips.topic: Qualified Immunity and the Innocence Project
clips.summary: Leventhal closes by signing a stipulation of probable cause that left him depressed and lifelong distrustful of the justice system, and connects his ordeal to today's arguable probable cause and qualified immunity, an Iowa civil rights case he took toward the Supreme Court, and the overload of the Innocence Project.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Picked Up With a Tag
clips.summary: Interviewed by Kerry Taylor at North Charleston in June 2011, William Saunders recounts being born in New York City in 1935 and sent at eighteen months on a train, wearing a tag, to be picked up by his grandfather on Johns Island, South Carolina, where his mother's parents raised him apart from his siblings.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Can to Can't
clips.summary: Saunders describes Johns Island as a paradise of subsistence where families ate from the river and woods and everyone had access to land, his self-taught grandfather who surveyed acres and planted boundary trees still standing, and how hand-me-down shoes, a roadside burn, and selling lightwood and his grandmother's chickens at five dollars apiece first brought him to the Charleston market.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: They Just Stopped Having Elections
clips.topic: Esau Jenkins and the School Board
clips.summary: Saunders recalls knowing Esau Jenkins from childhood church and Jenkins's bus contracts for Black students sent to Burke High in Charleston, how Charleston County simply stopped holding School Board elections from 1956 to 1970 to keep Black candidates off, and how, after Stokely Carmichael came in 1967 and Saunders demanded three Black members on the District 9 board, the county appointed Jenkins to set the King-aligned founder against the Carmichael-aligned Saunders.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: They Let My Age Be Eighteen
clips.topic: The Army and the Korean War
clips.summary: Saunders describes the teasing he endured as a poorly dressed island student and how at sixteen he and classmates volunteered for the Army, his age recorded as eighteen, trained in Hawaii and shipped to the Korean War, where he says he suffered more racism than anywhere in his life, with four Black soldiers in his company just after the 1951 integration and a first sergeant who held a forty-five on him on the front line.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: You Don't Belong in Here
clips.summary: Saunders recounts returning as a wounded hero yet riding segregated train coaches, being confronted at the Columbia Greyhound station by a policeman who told him he did not belong while his white Army buddies stayed silent, and describes stepping on a booby-trap trip flare on December 26, 1951 as the last man in a squad's single track through deep snow, then being sent back to the Korean front after four months in a Japanese hospital.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: This Nonviolent Thing Is Not for Me
clips.topic: Voter Teaching and the Highlander Raid
clips.summary: Saunders recounts becoming a company clerk and sergeant at Fort Jackson, returning to Johns Island to teach people the constitutional passages required to register, some memorizing the whole text, his work as business manager of the Progressive Club and Esau Jenkins's adult school, and his 1959 trip to the Highlander Research Center where a police raid arrested Septima Clark on a beer pretext and convinced him nonviolence was not for him.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: A Class System, Not a Race System
clips.summary: Saunders recounts returning to Harlem in 1959 and explains that the Highlander gatherings brought Southerners together, including poor Appalachian whites facing strip mining, leading him to see the country as a class system in which race sits at the bottom, and recalls director Myles Horton's lesson that you help people get started and then let them decide what is best.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: I Only Had One Cheek
clips.summary: He clarifies that he did not oppose nonviolence so much as the idea of letting people beat him for nothing, did all the supportive paperwork, food, and background work for the movement without marching, and recalls Fannie Lou Hamer and others staying on Johns Island, Hamer cursing to avoid being beaten while demonstrating.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Lazy People Find an Easier Way
clips.summary: Saunders recounts working twenty years in a mattress factory, a job he chose because activism could not get him fired, where his boss Julius Walsh made him foreman and gave him a private telephone for movement calls, and how, jailed repeatedly on pretexts, he founded the Low Country Newsletter with jailed Army doctor Howard Levy, using an American Friends Service Committee press to publish photo comparisons of the unequal Black and white island high schools.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: American-aided Integration
clips.summary: Saunders explains how Charleston leaders defeated Black power on the schools by stacking a consolidated nine-member board with at-large voting atop the existing eight boards, controlling all money and teachers, and why, contrasting the NAACP's interest in sending a few elite Black children to white schools with King's demand of good education for everyone, he favored the first desegregation ruling but fought the busing order, making enemies of Charleston's Black leaders.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: They Asked the Governor Not to Certify Me
clips.summary: He recounts winning election to the Office of Economic Opportunity regional board in 1967 only to have Charleston's Black leadership and NAACP petition the governor against certifying him, and how a Georgia official, Henrietta Canty, froze a million dollars for the county until they relented, after which the governor simply stopped certifying anyone so Saunders could serve.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Human Rights Before Civil Rights
clips.summary: Saunders frames his work as about human rights rather than civil rights, and describes how the Low Country Newsletter exposed conditions for Black workers at the medical college, including the state's refusal to pay minimum wage, and how he helped five Black nurses fired for refusing improper duties get reinstated through Reginald Barrett of the civil rights commission.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Bring Six, Then Bring Twelve
clips.summary: He recounts organizing the reinstated nurses by asking each to bring more people week after week until the group of three hundred outgrew Barrett's house and moved to the union hall, the workers seeking money but also respect and job classifications in a hospital where Black staff ate in the boiler room and pay depended on a supervisor's favor.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: You Are Somebody
clips.summary: Saunders credits Black Muslim leader Otis Robinson, echoed by Jesse Jackson, with the I-am-somebody affirmation telling hospital workers who fed and cleaned patients around the clock that they mattered, insists the union-hall meetings were a full community effort of women and men, recounts turning a windowless cell into singing the first night he was arrested, and describes how the few white workers who joined were exposed by Black spies and lost their jobs.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Guards Who Took Over the Jail
clips.summary: Saunders recounts how Local 1199 out of New York and SCLC, including Andrew Young, joined the organized workers in January 1969, and how he recruited young Black men, some previously in trouble, as guards who deliberately got arrested to protect jailed workers from inside, many later returning to school, one becoming a minister.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Skip the One They Fight Over
clips.summary: He says the strike first got Black people elected to public office in South Carolina, and describes meeting secretly during the curfew with a medical university vice president to relay positions between the trustees and the union and Andrew Young, arranging a private leadership meeting where, of ten demands, he set aside the ones the trustees resisted and won agreement on eight, framing himself as the man who builds the stage rather than the star who plays on it.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: We Won't Have a Riot, We'll Have a War
clips.summary: He says his only quarrel with SCLC and the union was their failure to train the worker leaders to run the local afterward, not nonviolence, and recounts warning the FBI that Charleston would never riot or burn its own community but would wage a war if pushed, since the people were equipped to fight and only wanted fair treatment.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: I Looked in the Trunk a Good While
clips.summary: Saunders tells of the state law enforcement chief summoning him to a motel lined with police, where Saunders defiantly stood at his open car trunk before learning the chief wanted to offer protection against a plot on his life, and how he told him not to save him, citing King and Kennedy, since he and other Black men kept their own offensive apparatus for self-defense.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Why Don't You Send Me a Telegram
clips.summary: He recounts Governor Robert McNair asking him to tell congregations at two AME churches that the strike would end the next day, Saunders sensing a trap and demanding a telegram the governor could not provide, then going home to Johns Island while roughly two hundred people were arrested at the church, refusing to be the man blamed for telling a lie.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Cut Off the Money
clips.summary: Saunders explains that banker Hugh Lane, asked through the Nixon administration how to end the strike, advised cutting off the roughly seventy-five million dollars of federal HEW money to the medical college, and laments that SCLC and the union, not the local people who built the stage and took the beatings, received all the credit while Andrew Young's career rose.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: I'm Going to Give You Access
clips.summary: He calls the settlement nearly nothing but credits the strike with creating the South Carolina Human Affairs Commission and electing the first Black members to City Council, the County Council, and the legislature, and reflects on raising ten children to whom he gave the access he never had, refusing to let a hard childhood excuse hurting others.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: They Switched In Mean Black Guys
clips.summary: Saunders recounts moving from the hospital strike straight into the sanitation strike, targeting state institutions so a win would bind the whole state to minimum wage and training, and notes the bitter irony that after Black workers won the right to drive and ride up front, the system replaced mean white supervisors with mean Black ones who mistreated the workers worse.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: They Wanted Their Own First Senator
clips.summary: He recounts his 1980 run for state senate, winning the Democratic primary and runoff before the NAACP brought in outsiders to oppose him over his humble family background, having already arranged for I. DeQuincey Newman to become the first Black state senator, and attributes his close loss partly to Ronald Reagan drawing white voters to a straight ticket and to opponents who, like Americans who love politicians who lie, promised a bridge no freshman senator could deliver.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Nobody Could Change the Station
clips.summary: Saunders reflects on the bitterness of a life lived without the right family background, then describes building a number-one-rated daytime radio station out of about twenty-seven by serving the public with gospel, talk, and jazz, listeners so loyal they removed the tuning knob, and laments deregulation under Reagan that ended public-service requirements.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Making Robots Out of Young People
clips.summary: He worries that teaching children to depend on gadgets rather than think is making robots of them, recounts getting Gullah recognized as a language around 1969 or 1970, and describes a Johns Island concerned-citizens group fighting a road project where former Klansmen and farmers sit together by addressing only right and wrong, never religion or politics.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Never Been Indicted
clips.summary: Saunders names his proudest achievement at seventy-six as never having been indicted by a government that gets rid of people like him, says no one taught him to organize because losing was his greatest teacher, recounts an algebra teacher who marked his correct answers wrong for using his own formula until the principal vindicated him, and teaches that people must enjoy the process rather than chase end results.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Still Run It Today
clips.summary: Saunders says Korea enlightened him toward helping people, and recounts founding the Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program around 1970, still running despite a seventy-five percent budget cut, in an area with the highest sickle cell rates in the country, while criticizing South Carolina's Republican leadership for opposing good education, health care, and equal justice.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: We Got to Do Something About Tomorrow
clips.summary: Saunders voices concern for the environment and worries that buying three or four politicians is enough to get any outcome, citing a county council that keeps voting on an island road until it wins, describes developers giving thousand-dollar gifts to children and churches to take over the groups that once helped people, and closes, drinking cherry wine and praying, by warning that the country must act for tomorrow or be lost across generations.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Born at Duke Hospital, 1936
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in Jackson, Mississippi, on December 6, 2015, Worth W. Long opens by recounting his birth as Worth Westmoreland Long Jr. at Duke Hospital in Durham, North Carolina, on January 15, 1936, to Gertrude Watson Long and the Reverend William Worth Long Sr., an AME Zion minister born in Newell, North Carolina.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: The Presiding Elder and His Administrator
clips.summary: Long describes his father as a presiding elder of the AME Zion Church responsible for at least ten churches, riding circuit to hold quarterly conferences that handled general claims, mortgages, membership, and missionary funding for Africa, while his mother served not as a secretary but as an administrator managing the finances of both the home and the broad church network.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: A Row to Talk Across
clips.topic: The Farm and the Milk Route
clips.summary: He recalls the family farm five miles from Durham that supplied their milk and garden food, his father driving out daily by jeep, and his own weekend chore of pedaling a bicycle out to milk the cow, feed the two hogs and the mule, and carry the milk carefully home, treasuring the rare unhurried talk when he and his formal, folk-spoken country-preacher father worked adjoining rows.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: The Broom That Sealed the Deal
clips.summary: Long traces his father's African lineage and first marriage into a free Negro family of Chatham County, recounted against Carter G. Woodson's census work, his year at Hood Theological Seminary near Livingstone College, and the arrival at his first church, Mitchell's Chapel AME Zion in Pittsboro, where the mother-in-law swept the dirt-floored parsonage with a homemade straw broom and sealed his thirty-year tenure on the district.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Gertrude's Table
clips.summary: He describes his father's marriage to Gertrude Watson, a widow with three daughters whose settlement let her buy a Durham duplex, her work cleaning at Liggett and Myers and cooking for part of the Duke family, and the AME Zion officials she entertained, including Bishop Spottswood and her stepbrother Bishop E.B. Watson, who once gave young Worth a handmade gold palm clip that ruined his best tie.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: They Had a Root Consciousness
clips.summary: Long recalls NAACP figures such as Alex Barnes, who headed the AME Zion paper, coming to sit at Gertrude's table while he listened from the living room to talk of the church's business and the plight of Africans and the poor of the world, a household carrying at once a world consciousness and a deep African and root consciousness drawn from both parents' rural backgrounds.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: The High-Class Neighborhood Came Up
clips.summary: He explains that the family's lumber house, inherited near the planned North Carolina Central University, was soon surrounded on Formosa Avenue by the brick-and-stone homes of an arriving Black professional class: insurance men like Henry McKinley Michaux, a future president of North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company, and the piano studio of a daughter of C.C. Spaulding, so that as Long puts it the high-class neighborhood came up around them.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: A Different World on the Block
clips.summary: Long contrasts his under-heated, modest household with neighbors who sent their children to Palmer Memorial Institute and Camp Atwater in Massachusetts, situating it in Durham's standing as a financial center for Black America and crediting a women-founded business school that trained the competent accountants and typists North Carolina Mutual needed.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Can We Get Somebody to Fly All Guy
clips.topic: The Manure Wagon and Paper Route
clips.summary: He tells of dreading a daytime manure haul through the wealthy block and so driving the mule wagon in before dawn, only to be spotted and teased from the neighbors' porches, and of the paper route he ran from age five-thirty in the morning, collecting on weekends and absorbing the tidbits of folk advice and success ethic that customers pressed on him at every stop.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: You Sponsor Their Education
clips.summary: Long catalogs the self-sustaining Black district of Hayti, its Rialto and Booker T theaters, Bull City Drug Store, hotel and printing company, and describes his parents practicing community uplift by housing as many as eight college women who were required to live with a certified family when the girls' dormitory filled, each one adding to the counsel and encouragement of the household.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Wait a Minute, Seats in the Balcony
clips.summary: He recounts his next-door neighbor Doc Holmes, a language professor who taught him to listen and pronounce German and French through Christmas carols and who, as head of the Lyceum program at North Carolina Central, slipped him into ballgames and then into balcony seats to hear Marian Anderson, Paul Robeson, and the pianist Philippa Schuyler, opening a whole new realm to him.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: They Built a Law School Instead
clips.topic: McKissick and the UNC Color Line
clips.summary: After a brief camera break, Long introduces Floyd B. McKissick, the young attorney who taught him civics, and recalls that rather than admit a Black man, the University of North Carolina built an entire law program at North Carolina Central, and that his Hillside High School teachers held master's degrees the state had subsidized at Chicago or Columbia rather than seat them in white universities, an absurdity of the system.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Should I Fill It Up, Preach It?
clips.summary: He describes riding with his minister father to the ten churches and a wartime gas stop near Chatham County where a filling-station boy, unsure how to address the well-dressed Black man and unwilling to say sir, settled on calling him preach it, an exchange Long reads as a human relationship built at a peer level and a lesson that self-image and change come only through engagement.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: A Chapter in Every Church
clips.topic: McKissick, the NAACP, and the Church as Center
clips.summary: Long returns to Floyd McKissick as the family lawyer and local hero with early nationalist ideas who would become national chairman of CORE, and to his father's insistence on an NAACP chapter in every church of the large Durham district, framing a vision in which the AME Zion church was never removed from the community but a center sponsoring education, economic development, and self-sufficiency, even buying and reselling mortgaged land.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Chief of Staff to Dr. King
clips.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Chester, Virginia, on June 20, 2014, the Reverend Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker says his one claim to fame is having been chief of staff to Martin Luther King Jr., and describes growing up a preacher's kid whose father pastored a small South Jersey congregation, read Greek and Hebrew daily, and at first opposed his joining King and leaving the Petersburg church.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: You're Colored
clips.summary: Walker recalls losing his first girlfriend, a neighbor named Nancy who said she could not play with him because he was colored, attending segregated schools until junior high, the Ku Klux Klan burning a cross at a Father Divine headquarters, and a father who was a race man with a portrait of Frederick Douglass on the wall, in a part of North South Jersey so bad they called it Little Georgia.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: The Freest Man in Society
clips.summary: Walker explains that though he majored in physics and chemistry and wanted to be a physician, his experiences with segregation in Richmond made the call to ministry more pressing, that he saw the Black preacher as the freest man in society because accountable to the Black community, and that as a Harlem pastor he marched against the drug traffic and became New York State's largest developer of affordable housing.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Twenty-Two Years Old at Gillfield
clips.summary: Walker recounts attending Virginia Union University and its graduate school of religion for his MDiv, being called to the Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg in his last year at age twenty-two, discovering only recently that his birth year was 1928 rather than 1929, serving Petersburg eight years, then moving to New York as a publishing marketing director before pastoring in Harlem for thirty-seven years.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Through the White Door
clips.summary: Walker describes integrating the segregated Petersburg ministerial alliance with the chaplain of Virginia State and pastor R.G. Williams, then leading a demonstration on February 27, 1960, after the city council refused to integrate the library, when he and Williams went through the white door, were arrested, and chose to stay in jail for a couple of days, in what he says was the first library desegregated in the South.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: I Put My Gun Down
clips.summary: Walker recalls asking for Douglas Southall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee, whom he thought guilty of treason, to see why the South made Lee a hero, and how he had known King since about 1952 when he joined the board and did press releases; he tells how he carried a nickel-plated Smith & Wesson .32 to Petersburg until King's influence made him put the gun down, an inherited rage tracing to his father, who in a whites-only train station near Norfolk gripped his pistol against a constable, and how he had fantasized in college about plastic bombs aimed at segregationists like Bilbo and Eastland before becoming King's right-hand man in nonviolence.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: An Idea in Dr. King's Briefcase
clips.topic: Growing the SCLC as Executive Director
clips.summary: Walker recounts joining the SCLC at the Clarksdale, Mississippi meeting in 1958 and becoming Executive Director in 1960 after Ella Baker's interim tenure, finding the organization mainly an idea in King's briefcase and installing a fundraising system that grew the budget from $57,000 to over a million dollars and the staff to a hundred full-time employees; he calls money the ammunition needed to train people in the novel concept of nonviolence and notes that behind the movement in the streets lay enormous planning, with Birmingham started a year ahead of time.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Too Many Targets in Albany
clips.summary: Walker argues that Albany, considered a failure by the media, was in his view a success despite mistakes, hampered by too many targets and by a campaign initiated by SNCC workers that exhausted the community, leading SNCC to invite King in; he describes sharp organizational competition among SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and the SCLC, journalist Louis Lomax claiming King left with his tail between his legs, and the lasting lesson of how to mobilize an entire community.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: I Raised Him in My Congregation
clips.summary: Walker says he raised SNCC's Charles Sherrod in his Petersburg congregation and got a Woolworth grant to help finance Sherrod's education, and recalls Sherrod telling how, in a Washington meeting where Robert Kennedy pressed the movement toward voter registration over direct action, Sherrod grew so angry he nearly took a swing at the Attorney General, Walker insisting the protest marches and direct action were what made the movement.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Project C for Confrontation
clips.summary: Walker explains that the main lesson carried from Albany was how to organize an entire community by moving mass meetings from place to place, sustained in Birmingham for forty-three nights despite a bus strike, and describes being assigned by King to build the strategy, going a year ahead to measure how long it took people to reach the stores and count seats, producing Project C, which stood for confrontation of the evil of segregation, with the expectation that Bull Connor would react in a way that helped them, which his resistance to letting marchers go downtown did.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: The Children's March
clips.summary: Walker recalls that the nonviolent credo allowed anyone to join, that Al Hibbler was arrested in Birmingham, and that he, King, and James Bevel decided to use the children despite expected criticism, because the sight of firemen turning hoses on children running down the sidewalk made the nation say this will not do, leading a Mississippi physician to declare that segregation was over.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: I Got It
clips.summary: Walker describes deliberately working to get three or four minutes on the Huntley-Brinkley report, realizing while a thousand onlookers gathered for a delayed march that slowing the demonstrations would draw the crowd and the cameras, so that when Bull Connor put the dogs and water hoses on them and they made the broadcast several nights in a row, he felt they were home free, the use of media being a key to Birmingham's success.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Beaten on Mother's Day
clips.summary: Walker explains that the NAACP, outlawed in Alabama and reliant on the courts, lacked an action program, that winning integration through courts alone would take a hundred years, and recounts the personal cost: his wife assaulted by an Alabama state trooper with a rifle, and himself beaten on Mother's Day with a broken wrist, three broken ribs, and a dislocated back from troopers' batons, dangers they never considered because of their religious conviction.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Crack Birmingham, Crack the South
clips.summary: Walker recalls King saying that if they could crack Birmingham they could crack the South, the euphoria of feeling like heavyweight champions, his role as Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington, and how Project C served as the outline for later campaigns with a planned Danville action left unscheduled after King's assassination; he attributes to Birmingham the Public Accommodations Act of 1964 and credits Birmingham with leading to Selma and the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Rallies Were Services
clips.topic: The Black Church and the Movement
clips.summary: Walker credits the religious infrastructure of the Black community with everything the movement achieved, describing rallies that were services using century-old spirituals recast with new lyrics as freedom songs, the confederation of Black churches that made the movement possible drawing in adults and young people alike, and King's passing criticism of the preacher in the big Cadillac.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Letter From the Birmingham Jail
clips.summary: Walker tells how he was the only one in Birmingham who could read King's chicken-scratch writing, transcribing the letter over two or three nights from newspaper edges and toilet paper while his secretary typed to exhaustion, recognizing it as an unusual document he calls the most important of the twentieth century, equal to the Gettysburg Address, and how he renamed the Quakers' first printing from Tears of Love to Letter from the Birmingham Jail.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: They Froze My Salary at Ten Thousand
clips.summary: Walker explains that the SCLC board froze his salary at ten thousand dollars while he administered a million-dollar budget with a wife and four children, so when a New York publisher dangled twenty-five thousand he left, recalling Martin Luther King Sr. coming to his Atlanta home by the little stream to beg him to stay, and his refusal to let Black folks exploit him any more than white folks would.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: The Picture in the Birmingham Jail
clips.summary: Walker says he remained King's man in New York and representative to the UN, recounts going back to jail in Birmingham in 1967 over a parading-without-a-permit case where he and King took each other's photographs, the famous jail picture whose credit others tried to claim, and his Harlem congregation of eighteen hundred members, eighty percent tithing, that gave over a million dollars in his last two years.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: The Charter School Fight
clips.summary: Walker reflects that after King's assassination he felt the church could again lead against the residual racism in cities like New York, becoming immersed in the charter school movement and a central figure who, with Rubén Díaz Sr. and Floyd H. Flake, pushed charter school legislation in New York State and opened the first charter school in the city with Steve Klinsky.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: The Struggle on Two Continents
clips.summary: Walker recounts being recruited by people from the African National Congress to mobilize three hundred churches into a federation to get the truth out about apartheid, working in the election of Mandela as president, taking twenty-two ministers to South Africa to see a racism in which indigenous people could not own land or move freely and had to carry pass books, and casting racism as the outgrowth of hatred against the downtrodden.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John
clips.summary: Walker grounds his theological position in the New Testament and the teachings of Jesus, recalling a biographer who, asking who influenced King most and hearing names like Martin Buber, Thoreau, and Paul Tillich, was told by Andrew Young that King was influenced by Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John, that King was above all a Christian pastor whose imitation-of-Christ paradigm gave the movement its strength, the beloved community articulated by figures like Vernon Johns and Gardner C. Taylor, and that there is no post-racial era despite Obama's election.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: The Invaders and the FBI
clips.summary: Answering questions from the room, Walker explains that the Memphis march was disrupted by a group called the Invaders who he believed were put up to it by the FBI, that King wanted help straightening it out, that the federal government, particularly the Kennedy administration, repeatedly tried to disrupt the movement, and that King's later focus on jobs grew out of the lack of meaningful employment and poor schools that racism creates.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: The Truth Is Always Inflammatory
clips.topic: Telling the Truth and Honoring His Wife
clips.summary: Walker says his style is to tell the truth, quoting his mentor Vernon Johns that the truth is always inflammatory, and turns to honor his wife as the only genuine wife of a civil rights icon still living, furious that a book on the women of the movement left her out though she was a freedom rider beaten with a rifle butt by an Alabama highwayman, with their home assaulted by guns and rocks in Petersburg.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Arrested With Five Children
clips.summary: Mrs. Walker recounts the 1963 Mother's Day weekend when King asked her husband to stay in Birmingham after a bomb at Dr. King's brother's home set off rioting, her being struck in the head by a National Guardsman at the Gaston Motel while retrieving her sleeping children, then being arrested with her five children at East Point near the Atlanta airport, calling Andrew Young's wife Jean to reach Dr. King, until Martin Luther King Sr. came at midnight with a property owner to make her bail.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: This Is My Chance to Rest
clips.topic: The Trial and a Beating at the Motel
clips.summary: Mrs. Walker describes returning for trial, being fined eighty-four dollars, refusing to pay and going back to a clean jail she treated as a rest until Robert J. Brown paid the fine and her husband, spotting her car, got her out, while Walker recounts being beaten by three highwaymen at the motel as he retrieved her things, suffering a broken wrist, three fractured ribs, and a dislocated spine, a terrible weekend for the family.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Homeschooled Before It Was Popular
clips.topic: Books, a Barred Daughter, and Achievement
clips.summary: Walker notes his wife went to Mississippi as a freedom rider in a cell of two holding fourteen other women, that she has read all the manuscripts of his fifty-five books, and Mrs. Walker tells how their daughter was barred from all Petersburg public schools over his work as an agitator and president of the Petersburg Improvement Association, CORE, and SCLC, so she homeschooled the girl before homeschooling was popular, the daughter finishing Howard in three years, a Yale master's, and becoming deputy director of the National Endowment for the Arts.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Too Handsome for Virtue
clips.summary: Asked whether he would have done anything differently, Walker says no, that he once thought of vengeance but the Lord removed the bitterness and the sixties were an exciting time to be alive, and closes with Maya Angelou, who ran his New York fundraising office, recalling her writing in I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings that she saw him with Dr. King and found him too young for wisdom and too handsome for virtue.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Made on the Boat
clips.summary: Alfred Moldovan recounts being born in 1921 in the Hungarian Yorkville section of Manhattan to immigrants from a shtetl on the Hungary-Romania-Poland-Czechoslovakia borderlands, his father a Carpathian lumberjack turned bread baker at Pechter Baking, his seamstress mother, and a Bronx childhood so steeped in Yiddish that he did not speak English until public school.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: He Read the Tag Cover to Cover
clips.summary: Moldovan describes a home with no political life beyond his father dutifully walking union picket lines and reading the Democratic paper the Tag, his mother sewing neckties for extra money, and his own arrival at City College, the Harvard of the working class, where he worked mornings and nights and had no time for politics.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Justify Your Existence
clips.summary: Moldovan explains his slim odds of medical school as a Jewish pre-med, his motto to justify your existence, and how after Pearl Harbor he enlisted as a cadet, trained as a radar specialist at Yale, Harvard, and MIT, and shipped to Foggia, Italy with the 455th Bomb Group overseeing two hundred mechanics on B-24s and B-17s.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: One of Three B Schools
clips.summary: Called home from Italy by a telegram about his sick mother, Moldovan recounts attending Chicago Medical School, one of the schools the Flexner Report had graded a B, and how dean John J. Scheinen single-handedly raised it to Grade A so its graduates, backed by the GI Bill and his own night work, could practice anywhere.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Red-Baited Out of Existence
clips.summary: Moldovan describes becoming political in medical school, helping found the Association of Interns and Medical Students and the American Veterans Committee, both red-baited out of existence, and joining classmates to integrate South Chicago beaches and lecture in Illinois high schools to recruit minority and women students into a white male profession.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: What Should He Be, a Street Cleaner
clips.topic: Family and the Choice to Serve
clips.summary: Moldovan recalls his craftsman father, who wanted only a big house to putter in and was disappointed his son chose East Harlem over Park Avenue, and a family so undramatic about achievement that his sister, asked if she was proud her brother was a doctor, replied, what should he be, a street cleaner.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: We Need Doctors
clips.topic: From the Medical Society to MCHR
clips.summary: After internship and residencies, Moldovan opened his East Harlem practice serving working-class patients, joined the Physicians Forum and the County Medical Society where he fought a deliberately dysfunctional early Medicaid, and recounts the call from civil rights activists, we need doctors, that led toward the Medical Committee for Human Rights described in the book The Good Doctors.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: My Wife Took the Bail Money
clips.summary: Moldovan tells of becoming politically active only with the Medical Committee for Human Rights, taking off work so often his accountant warned he would lose his practice, leaving his wife packets of cash as bail money she spent on trips with the kids, and being elected secretary-treasurer who helped raise half a million dollars in 1964.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: They Found the Bodies in the Dam
clips.summary: Moldovan recounts his first tour in Meridian, Mississippi in August 1964, arriving as the bodies of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner were found in the dam, helping bring Brooklyn pathologist David Spain to perform a proper autopsy after the official one proved false, and his own shock as an ignorant Northern white, recalling a Florida bus driver who once ordered him out of the back.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: We Will Be There
clips.topic: The Meaning of Lending a Presence
clips.summary: Moldovan explains that doctors in Mississippi could not legally practice medicine, only lend a presence so people knew they were watched, a phrase he distilled as we will be there, and revisits the demand for David Spain's autopsy after the Meridian pathologist's report on Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner proved atrocious and untrue.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: If You Go, I'll Blow Your Head Off
clips.summary: Sent to Selma with Virginia Wells in spring 1965, Moldovan recounts the young people marching on Montgomery without Dr. King's permission, the Committee's makeshift ambulances at the Edmund Pettus Bridge, a sheriff threatening to blow his head off, and how he alone crossed to drag gassed people from houses amid clubbing goons, with John Lewis beaten and concussed.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Make Sure You Have Somebody With You
clips.topic: The Second March and a Secret Deal
clips.summary: Moldovan describes speaking at Brown Chapel the night before the second march, joking that anyone knocked unconscious should make sure someone was with them, the column kneeling and praying at the edge of the bridge, and learning only later that King had cut a deal with Washington's Attorney General not to cross.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: I Was Sure I'd Take a Bullet for Him
clips.summary: Moldovan recounts riding beside Dr. King so that he and Virginia Wells would absorb anything thrown through the car windows, his certainty he would take a bullet, his refusal of paralysis traced to icy wartime roads near Naples, and his affection for King, who laughingly told Andy Young, look who's an atheist, when Moldovan named himself a Jewish atheist.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: A Moment in History
clips.topic: The Third March and the Gas Mask
clips.summary: Moldovan reflects that he never expected to change the world but played a moment in history, recalls quoting Lenin's One Step Forward and Two Steps Back to King, and that he had left Selma before the final Montgomery march, appearing in a photograph carrying King's gas mask under his coat as the senior physician who assigned himself the role of protecting him.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Every Leader on One Stage
clips.summary: Moldovan recalls the women in nursing roles, his indifference to the beloved-community Christological language of King and SCLC, and a seminal Committee meeting he helped organize in a Washington auditorium that put the heads of the NAACP, CORE, SCLC, and every major Afro-American leader on one stage at once, the only such gathering he knew.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: That Was Not My Business
clips.summary: Moldovan voices his grim view of an American medical system that spends more and produces less than any other, and recounts opposing war personally while arguing the Committee had no right to take an antiwar stand at the March 1966 Chicago meeting because donors who supported the war funded it, a split with the younger members who, he jokes, thought him antediluvian.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Two Kinds of Motivation
clips.summary: Moldovan distinguishes two groups in the Committee, people like him moved by civil rights as righting wrongs, and figures like Jack Geiger who built lasting Southern medical organizations, and explains the dichotomy over money he wanted to fund doctors and nurses going South, not to establish clinics, as his active involvement wound down in 1966.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: I Went to Be There
clips.summary: Moldovan closes with later acts of bearing witness, going to occupied Columbia where he called the police pigs and was rebuked by Margaret Mead, and flying to Israel when the SCUD missiles fell in 1991 to be with family, returning to his rule, justify your existence, and a parting joke that his time is up.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Publishing Houses of Nashville
clips.summary: Interviewed by Taylor Branch at his Atlanta home in March 2011 for the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture, C.T. Vivian opens with why the Sunday-school publishing houses settled in Nashville, then turns to his own birth in Howard County, Missouri, and his family's move to Illinois so he could attend a non-segregated school.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Fire Under My Bedroom
clips.summary: Vivian tells how his grandmother, a Church of God in Christ woman, took in a woman a common-law husband was putting into the snow, and how the angered man set a fire in the coal cellar under Vivian's bedroom in the night, his grandmother screaming his name until he ran out moments before the room collapsed into the fire.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Two Women and a College Town
clips.summary: Vivian describes the Depression destroying the family's three farms and prompting divorces among his mother and grandmother, and the two women deciding in 1930 to move to Macomb, Illinois, chosen because it had a university, so that the six-year-old could one day go to college, the dream his grandmother fulfilled through him.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Sit-In in Peoria
clips.summary: Vivian recounts going back to Peoria to work and return to school, rooming with chemist Ben Alexander, and joining a church group's direct action using techniques from CORE, the Congress of Racial Equality founded by Jim Farmer in 1941, to open restaurants and lunch counters in Peoria in 1947, years before Greensboro.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Too Nice to Let You Sit Alone
clips.summary: Vivian tells how he met his wife Octavia at a party welcoming her as women's and girls' director at Carver Community Center, the largest African American center between Chicago and St. Louis, and how he called to take her out for her February birthday, after which the two were inseparable.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: From Pontiac to Ted's Trailer
clips.summary: Vivian describes Octavia's upbringing in Pontiac, Michigan, where a doctor-led group of young people published a newspaper and registered voters, and her later role in a Peoria lunch-counter action at a drive-in called Ted's Trailer, where her pastor took young people inside to eat rather than accept bags at the car.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Up South
clips.summary: Asked where his and Octavia's families came from, Vivian traces her people to rice-growing Arkansas and his to Missouri, where his grandfather was fired as a principal for teaching Black children algebra, and explains the phrase up south, the recognition that the North was only marginally better than the South.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: He Refused His Owner's Name
clips.summary: Vivian recounts his great-great-grandfather leaving slavery to join the northern armies and, on returning to Boonville, refusing to take his former owner's name, choosing his wife's name instead, and traces the family names Tyndall, Woods, and Vivian, including a search for Vivians as far as Vivian, Louisiana.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Pastor's Savings and a Seminary
clips.summary: Vivian explains that though he was set to attend Colgate Rochester after being picked among the hot 100, his call to the ministry in Peoria revealed his pastor had been saving money to send him to American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville, the turn that put him into the movement, where the leaders, Kelly Miller Smith of First Baptist and seminary students like Bernard Lafayette, Jim Bevel, and John Lewis, came from the religious side of life.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Booming Voice Through the Roof
clips.summary: Vivian recounts the moment of his call while crossing the floor at Helen Gallagher's mail-order company, hearing a booming voice through the ceiling say it wanted him to work eight or ten hours a day, and the strangeness that the packagers around him went on with their work as though they had heard nothing.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: An African American Mail-Order House
clips.summary: Vivian describes Helen Gallagher Foster's rooster-themed mail-order goods advertised in House Beautiful, and his own plan to start an African American mail-order house to reach Black customers with new postwar money before Johnson Publications did, a venture he dropped when the call to ministry came.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Whose Faith Is in Question Now
clips.summary: Vivian tells how, the night he learned Octavia was expecting their first child, he resolved not to leave her in Peoria for seminary, until her words, whose faith is in question now, sent him to Nashville, and how she soon called to say she was coming to stay, and a job and a dorm room fell into place just in time.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Jim Lawson Comes to Town
clips.summary: Vivian recounts that the son born in Nashville had cerebral palsy, that he and Octavia produced a twenty-four-page piece on King the publishing house feared printing, and that after Kelly Miller Smith gathered a handful of ministers, Jim Lawson arrived, anchored at Vanderbilt and working for the Fellowship of Reconciliation, bringing the training in nonviolence no one had yet provided.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Just Back From India
clips.summary: Vivian recalls meeting Lawson, who lived across the street, and welcoming his deeper knowledge of nonviolence, and how Lawson, fresh from India, taught skeptical students for months, including Diane Nash, who, taught that government is a monopoly of violence, was horrified by the slapping exercises and wondered how nonviolence could work.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Students of the Workshops
clips.summary: Vivian remembers a Methodist minister burned by a cigarette who insisted they buy him a new suit, notes that many SCLC figures like Abernathy, Coretta, and Fred Shuttlesworth came out of Alabama, and recalls the students in Lawson's workshops, John Lewis, Jim Bevel, Bernard Lafayette, Diane Nash, a few seminary men not fully engaged, and the Southern Baptist who stood out among a handful of white students.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The President Who Wrote on Jesus
clips.summary: Vivian reflects cautiously on hearing untouchables at a South Africa conference describe Gandhi's attitude toward them differently than Americans understood, and tells the irony that Jim Lawson was expelled from Vanderbilt by a president who had written the very book on the life of Jesus used in their seminary, a text the students saw as refuting the South.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Killing Communists in Greensboro
clips.summary: Vivian notes the Greensboro sit-ins began February 1, 1960, the night after King's farewell from Dexter, and that the Nashville group had been preparing for a year when Greensboro acted without preparation, recalling a later visit to Greensboro where a police chief showed no regret over killing people he called communists.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Religious Confrontation
clips.summary: Vivian argues that bringing segregation into the open as a religious confrontation in the Bible Belt changed everything, and describes his workshop method of asking whether one can be a Christian and a racist at the same time, keeping the rooms mostly white but with Black participants to answer the white doubters directly.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: State the Scripture and They'd Look It Up
clips.summary: Vivian contends the South believed in the Bible above theology, so that citing scripture on loving one's neighbor sent people home to verify and return convinced, and frames two movements running together: by any means necessary and nonviolent direct action with a biblical base founded on Jesus.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Look Them in the Eye
clips.summary: Vivian discusses the workshop training to keep eye contact rather than curl into a fetal position, distinguishing SCLC's upright stance from SNCC's, recounts Fred Shuttlesworth's story of King keeping his pace as a car bore down on them, and sets the Nashville timeline toward the April 19 march as he listened to Lawson telephone a Greensboro leader troubled the students had sat down unprepared.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Alabama, Then Mississippi If Necessary
clips.summary: Vivian explains why trained people were kept from the front lines and lays out a geographic strategy: SCLC chose Alabama, where the method came in Montgomery, the civil rights bill in Birmingham, and the voting rights bill in Selma, while SNCC took the youngest people into the hardest place, Mississippi, aiming to change towns rather than national law.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Bombing During the Meeting
clips.topic: SNCC's Formation and the Looby Bombing
clips.summary: Vivian clarifies that SNCC formed at the Shaw meeting in Raleigh as the sit-ins spread and that John Lewis was leaving SNCC by Bloody Sunday, then recounts an early-morning leadership meeting at a church near Fisk when a distant blast first thought to be at Meharry sent Bernard Lafayette to Tennessee A&I to organize before they learned the home of attorney Z. Alexander Looby had been bombed.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Nothing but the Feet
clips.summary: Vivian describes the march forming from Tennessee A&I after lunch, gathering Fisk and Pearl High School students, growing as people came off porches, and falling into a silent march into downtown where nothing could be heard but feet, a technique he says had been pioneered in New York in the 1920s.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Backed Up Against the Buildings
clips.summary: Vivian recalls factory workers playing catch who fell silent and backed against the walls as the marchers passed, the crowd swelling to four thousand though papers reported two thousand, and the procession reaching City Hall where he and Diane Nash awaited the mayor, who would have to answer her planned final question.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: I Could Not Justify It
clips.summary: Vivian recounts Mayor Ben West saying he would examine the matter, then opening everything in Nashville a week later, the televised exchange in which Diane Nash pressed him until he said he could not justify segregation, and a portrait of Z. Alexander Looby, the lead Black lawyer who had defended the arrested demonstrators, which is why the white community reacted to the bombing of his home.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Marching Through the Yellow Pages
clips.summary: Vivian notes the mayor opened most places but not the theaters, recalls John Lewis's line about marching through the yellow pages, and describes returning from Denver electrified by footage of a leader struck and bleeding, explaining that because theaters open at night the campaign required night marches that gave attackers the cover of darkness and demanded extra commitment.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Picnic Interrupted
clips.summary: Vivian recalls a picnic celebrating the end of the theater campaign on Mother's Day 1961 when word came of buses burned and riders beaten in Anniston and Birmingham, the evening meeting led by Diane Nash where, agreeing to go to Jackson, some wept and ten students stood ready, and the restated principle that they would never let violence stop nonviolence.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Militia on the Bus
clips.summary: Vivian recounts an earlier Birmingham bus-station incident where a policeman leaned over him but did not have him removed, tied to the interstate-versus-intrastate distinction, then describes boarding in Montgomery with white passengers up front, state troopers filling the front rows with Alabama militia, a long trail of news cars to the state line, and letting Jim Lawson explain nonviolence to reporters.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Asking for Trouble and Getting It
