clips.summary: Vivian recalls a Mississippi colonel unfastening the speedometer and ordering the driver to barrel into Jackson, people waving silently from porches, the Life magazine framing asking for trouble and getting it, and how, arguing with Colonel Birdsong over a bathroom stop, he was told to stay on the back because they needed experience at the end, making him the last rider off in Jackson.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Whether He Would Get on the Bus
clips.summary: Vivian recalls the riders besieged in Abernathy's Montgomery church until Robert Kennedy sent marshals, the meeting at pharmacist Richard Harris's home where Diane Nash asked King to ride and an argument arose over whether he would board, his own decision with Jim Lawson to continue, and patting the Jackson police captain on the back to say he was with them, perhaps the first ever to ask to go to jail.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: You Promised Me Only One Bus
clips.topic: The Second Bus and States' Rights
clips.summary: Vivian recounts that Jim Farmer, who as CORE director launched the original adult ride, rejoined on the second bus, which was a terrible surprise to government, the Mississippi governor protesting to Robert Kennedy that he had been promised only one bus, and explains the stakes of interstate versus intrastate travel: the segregationists wanted segregation kept a state matter while the movement wanted federal enforcement.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Two People Delivering the Food
clips.summary: Vivian describes the riders setting up a routine in the Jackson jail with meditation, strategy, and a Sunday service organized through the bars, stunning the guards by seating themselves and having two of their own deliver the food, winning the Black trustees' respect, and recounts Jim Bevel explaining that a lieutenant's rage came from Vivian looking him straight in the eye.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Modeled on a Plantation
clips.summary: Vivian describes being moved early to Parchman, a farm-prison he calls one of the most vicious ever, modeled on a plantation with two burial grounds, and the questioning where, after he answered yes sir and no sir, the warden suddenly asked whether he had syphilis and four or five men jumped him.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: As Soon as the Blood Spurted
clips.summary: Vivian recounts warding off blows until the warden's embellished leather-and-steel blackjack cut him down the side, the men jumping back at the sight of blood, and how, led toward the cells, he felt something strange down his neck and turned at the steel gate to find the guard pointing a thirty-eight at his head, looking him in the eye until the guard lowered the gun and slammed the door.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Saved by the Blood
clips.summary: Vivian recounts that a released white rider's words to the press brought Washington's calls and Kennedy's word that he had been beaten until he bled, which made the fearful warden dread removal, that Kelly Miller Smith arrived within hours to take him back to Nashville, and reflects on how local officials grew uncertain once Washington took notice, returning to the gun as one of a few truly religious experiences of his life.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Fingers Behind My Head
clips.summary: Vivian considers how being a freedom rider shaped his identity, describes the crooked Parchman cellblock that funneled prisoners single file past the police, and recounts a second truly religious experience in an Alabama jail where he got his fingers behind his head just in time so a billy club struck them rather than his skull, an event he ties to the day of his confrontation with Sheriff Clark in Selma.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Let Me Hit Him
clips.summary: Vivian describes a huge jailer taking him up in a small hand-operated elevator, a second large man asking let me hit him, and bringing his fingers together just as the club came down, then being shoved into a cell where he dropped to his knees in thanks not for surviving but for having done and said all the right things, noting the Selma confrontation sounds scripted yet came without any idea beforehand of what he would say.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Forty People up the Steps
clips.summary: Vivian sets the courthouse confrontation on February 15, 1965, three weeks before Bloody Sunday, describing Sheriff Clark coming out and closing the door with his men ranged on the top step as about forty people marched up the bottom steps, and ties this mystery to the gun pulled on him in Parchman.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Take Their Money, Vote Like You Want
clips.summary: Vivian explains how he became SCLC's director of affiliates, leaving Nashville to pastor in Chattanooga where a fellow minister had the voting statistics he needed, and describes winning three-fifths of the city council across three elections, including one rainy day, with a strategy against the bought Black vote: take the buyer's money but vote your conscience, illustrated by a man who pocketed the pay yet voted for whom he chose.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Name for All the Things
clips.summary: Vivian recounts that his Chattanooga and statewide voter work led Dorothy Cotton to recommend him to King, who through Wyatt Tee Walker brought him to Atlanta as national director of affiliates, overseeing SCLC-affiliated groups funded from the national office, a role that made him a traveling troubleshooter and led him to give up his church.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Necessary Thing
clips.summary: Vivian recounts that his first national-office assignment was Birmingham, King's go-for-broke campaign launched at the 1962 convention with the warning that it took twenty-three thousand soldiers to enroll James Meredith at Ole Miss, and Wyatt Walker's plan called Project C for confrontation, and tells how he and Walker spotted government men on the telephone-company balconies and amused themselves making calls to watch them hurry inside.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Dogs and the Hoses
clips.summary: Vivian describes the May 3, 1963 tactic of sending students out the church front door toward the hoses while others slipped out the back to occupy downtown, so that Bull Connor found the city taken and the hoses pointless, beginning the deal, and how the dogs and hoses backfired, the image of ripped trousers and a lunging dog showing the world the brutality and drawing ministers from across the country.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Silent March Against Lynching
clips.summary: Vivian explains the 1920s silent march in New York organized by the NAACP and W.E.B. Du Bois against lynching, with banners and only the sound of feet, which the Nashville marchers consciously echoed downtown, and notes that on the Birmingham jail decision he defers to Dorothy Cotton, who tells it best.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Never Too Young to Fight
clips.summary: Vivian recounts the argument over using young people, won by Jim Bevel, who held that one is never too young to fight for freedom, calling it the only children's movement he knows in nonviolent work, remembers urging high-school students who climbed out of locked windows to cross over Jordan, and tells how Birmingham parents who opposed their children marching were converted when the children went to jail.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Keeping the Legislation Moving
clips.summary: Vivian declines a memory of the Birmingham church bombing funeral while affirming he was at Jimmie Lee Jackson's, treats a 1963 Richmond panel on nonviolence as one of many speeches, and argues St. Augustine's real purpose in 1964 was not the town but keeping the civil rights legislation moving through Congress and pressing Johnson, explaining why he, unlike Bevel, wanted to go.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Human Net in the Ocean
clips.summary: Vivian describes St. Augustine's 400th anniversary and its segregated 1600 feet of beach, where white swimmers would form a human net and drown anyone who walked in, and how, leading a wade-in with Fred Shuttlesworth who could not swim, they went in at an angle until one of the men jumped too soon.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: I Got a Right to Be on This Ocean
clips.summary: Vivian recounts a man jumping on top of him and driving his face into the sand as he gave himself up for dead, until a policeman jerked the man off and pulled him to the surface, and how, when the officer moved to arrest him, Vivian insisted on his right to the ocean until the attacker fled and the policeman, persuaded, brought him towels.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Slave Market as a Classroom
clips.summary: Vivian describes turning St. Augustine's old downtown slave market into a teaching center at noon for tourists eating lunch, and a midnight march he and Dorothy Cotton led where police cut them off, unknowingly from Hoss Manucy's men waiting with chains and bars, the night a voice shouted that they had no god.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Whether Anybody Goes or Not, I'm Going
clips.summary: Vivian recounts a night march where attackers threw things from the beautiful walls and forty marchers were sent to the hospital, regrouping at a church where he declared he would go whether anyone followed and walked down the aisle until people rose behind him, and notes that King, Fred Shuttlesworth, and he went to jail in St. Augustine along with nineteen rabbis, after which came the landmark Civil Rights Act.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Not Just the Town
clips.summary: Vivian explains that as national affiliates director he went to Selma to gauge how prepared people were and to secure an invitation, King having largely decided already, that Selma meant not just the town but the surrounding counties from which Black people came, and recalls a St. Augustine state legislator intimidated to tears with his windows knocked out before a planned meeting.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: This Is the End of the Line
clips.summary: Vivian recounts the controversial decision to take the movement north to Chicago, his readiness to leave after concluding upon winning Selma that the work must now train people in the locals, and a call from Jim Morton to lead the Ford Fellowship program at a new urban training center, which he accepted from a filling-station telephone while driving across Indiana, reaching Chicago before King did and disclaiming any role in the Meredith march.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Who Made What Happen
clips.summary: Vivian recalls breakfasting at the Gaston Motel when word came that Meredith had been shot near Memphis, and gives his retrospective verdict that those who stayed with nonviolence won, judging movements by who made what happen, noting SNCC ended without students, nonviolence, or a committee for lack of a base while SCLC survived through the church.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Prophet of Our Age
clips.summary: Vivian recounts his first real conversation with King on the Fisk campus after the Montgomery victory, when King came for an honorary doctorate and Vivian was covering him for the Sunday-school publishing house, and how he concluded King could weather the storm beyond Montgomery, calling him the prophet of the age whose change was moral and spiritual.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: A Must-Is, Not a Has-Been
clips.summary: Vivian relays King's joke about a horse buyer wanting not a has-been or a will-be but a must-is, recalls that Abernathy could have led SCLC but gave it to King and settled disputes for him, and remembers King rocking on his heels singing There Is a Balm in Gilead, his founding a philosophical organization in college, and above all his understanding of timing.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Education Must Follow Freedom
clips.summary: Asked about his legacy, Vivian says only that he wants to keep fighting the struggle on a multiplicity of fronts, and describes building an organization to produce up to a million Black students a year who pass the ACT at a high level, having concluded that education must always follow freedom.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Hoses Did Not Turn On
clips.summary: Vivian tells the story of Birmingham ministers leading their congregations straight from church to sing to the children in jail, meeting Bull Connor and a line of firemen with hoses, and how, after the people prayed and rose, the firemen got up and walked away and the hoses never turned on.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: He Crawled Out Onto the Road
clips.summary: Vivian closes on Charles Billups, who led the Red Sea March prayers, an immaculate salesman taken by nonviolence who, after Klansmen tied him to a tree and beat him, crawled onto a highway certain he would die until a car carried him to the hospital, and who, after King was shot, flew to the funeral with a suitcase holding nothing but ties.
related_events: assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Satoko Ito's Path From Kobe to Yankton
clips.summary: Satoko Ito Ackerman recounts being raised in the Osaka area and attending Kobe College in Nishinomiya, a women's mission school where she was introduced to English and Christianity. A missionary, Angie Crew, arranged a scholarship through the South Dakota Conference of Congregational Churches, and she arrived at Yankton College in August 1959, later returning through the Toko Fund for seminary graduate work.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: David Ackerman's Mt. Carroll Childhood and Brother's Death
clips.summary: David Ackerman describes growing up in Mt. Carroll, Illinois, a farming town of two thousand, and majoring in history and political science at Knox College in Galesburg. During his senior year his brother died by suicide after the military discovered he was gay during Ranger training at Fort Benning and threatened him with dishonorable discharge, which led David to Chicago Theological Seminary.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Satoko's First Awareness of Race
clips.summary: Satoko recalls that race only entered her consciousness with the idea of going to Selma, after she saw the film of Bloody Sunday on television and heard Jesse Jackson speak. She qualifies this by remembering that African students at Yankton College could not get haircuts in town, an incident that outraged her at the time but soon faded from her thoughts.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Mt. Carroll, Grief, and Meeting in Chicago
clips.summary: David describes Mt. Carroll as a lily-white community with an air of tolerance rarely put to the test, recalling one Black family that was graciously received while he was in high school. He notes that his brother's death struck him as personal grief rather than political tragedy at the time, and that he and Satoko met and fell in love at the seminary the year before Selma.
related_events: David and Satoko meet at Chicago Theological Seminary

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Seminary at Hyde Park and Woodlawn
clips.summary: David explains that Chicago Theological Seminary sits on the University of Chicago campus in Hyde Park, just across the Midway from Woodlawn, a predominantly Black and economically depressed community. Clinical activities took students into the Black parts of Chicago, where he was struck by the extent of poverty and the difference between the North Side and South Side.
related_events: Seminary clinical work in Black Chicago

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: From the Early Sixties to Jesse Jackson's Class
clips.summary: David surveys the civil rights decade he lived through at a distance, from Birmingham and the March on Washington to the Montgomery Bus Boycott, and his resonance with Kennedy's call to public service. He notes that Selma became personal almost by accident because Jesse Jackson was part of his seminary class, an accomplished preacher who had come to CTS for formal training, and that Bloody Sunday occurred on March 7, 1965 as the SCLC made Selma a focal point for voting rights legislation.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Jesse Jackson Commandeers the Convocation
clips.summary: David recounts the seminary's weekly convocation of faculty and students at which Jesse Jackson, in his powerful style, commandeered the meeting and made Selma a personal moral issue, relaying Dr. King's call for all people of good will and asking directly who would go with him. Satoko describes feeling immediately called to go despite being a foreign student and a woman, while David grew upset because he felt he would have to take care of her.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Jesse Jackson, From Self-Important to Leader
clips.summary: After a recording break, both Ackermans reflect on Jesse Jackson as a young classmate they barely knew personally and initially found self-important and marked by vanity. Each recalls that on the night of the convocation he became a genuine leader, speaking with such passion, sincerity, and integrity that it was impossible not to respond.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Seminary Discussions and the Protector Impulse
clips.summary: Asked whether race and gender were discussed in seminary training, both Ackermans say they recall little beyond a course on the sociology of Chicago and its ecological barriers. David explains that his concern about Satoko joining the trip was driven by the prospect of violence and his playing the traditional male role of protector, which Satoko has continuously educated him was neither needed nor desired.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Two Vans, Moral Wrestling, and the Texas Classmate
clips.summary: David recounts that twelve students traveled in two vans with Illinois plates, taking turns driving roughly six hundred and fifty miles of two-lane road to Selma. He frames the challenge as a moral wrestling placed before the whole community, remembering most vividly a close friend from Amarillo, Texas who agonized over the trip and ultimately stayed behind because his parents would disown him and cut off his seminary funding.
related_events: Twelve seminarians decide to go to Selma

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Whether to Tell Their Parents
clips.summary: Asked whether they consulted their parents before leaving, Satoko says she had been making decisions independently since coming to the States and that her parents may never have known. She contrasts her own decision with the Texas friend's far harder struggle, while David says his parents might have been supportive but he was not willing to test it, and his scholarship meant no funding was at risk.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Dr. Schomer Says Do Not Go
clips.summary: The Ackermans recall that no one at the seminary called the trip a bad idea, since the students accepted it as a moral challenge fitting people studying theology and ethics. Seminary president Dr. Howard Schomer told them not to go, citing upcoming exams and the students' place being at the seminary, even though he was going himself out of concern that violence could be an issue.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Schomer's Sincerity and the Seminary's Makeup
clips.summary: Both Ackermans say they did not take Dr. Schomer's warning to heart, feeling they were needed in Selma, and neither recalls anyone taking his words seriously. Asked about the seminary's composition, they estimate a small nonwhite enrollment, perhaps ten to fifteen percent at most with only three or four African American students, out of a total of roughly seventy or eighty people.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Gas Station Threat in Alabama
clips.summary: Once across the state line into Alabama, the group stopped at a gas station where Satoko and her friend Ginny walked around, oblivious to danger. Jesse Jackson suddenly shouted for everyone to get in the car after white men loitering at the station went to a payphone to make a call, and the group took off fast with a car following them for some distance before it turned away.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Ministerial Collars and Arrival
clips.summary: Shortly after the pursuit the group stopped and put on ministerial collars they had brought along, though none were ordained, for whatever protective help they might provide. David recalls leaving Chicago on Tuesday mid-afternoon and arriving in Selma early morning or midday on Wednesday after driving straight through the night.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Stark Racial Geography of Selma
clips.summary: David and Satoko describe the stark difference between the white and Black sides of Selma. Crossing into the Black side, the streets were no longer paved, there were no sidewalks or streetlights, the houses looked shabby, and there was simply no evidence of public services.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Brown Chapel Rally and the Power of Singing
clips.summary: The group reached Brown Chapel A.M.E. Church, where people white and Black milled around and a rally built toward hearing from Dr. King, the church overflowing to the rafters. Satoko describes encountering powerful civil rights singing for the first time, more powerful than anything she had heard.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The News of Jim Reeb's Beating
clips.summary: At Brown Chapel the Ackermans heard about Jim Reeb, a Unitarian minister who had come quickly in response to Dr. King's call. He had walked into the white part of the community for dinner on Tuesday night and, with one or two companions, had been brutally beaten and was lying in critical condition at the hospital.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Welcomed and Fed by the Community
clips.summary: Satoko describes the welcome at the church as overwhelming, with community members thanking the visitors profusely and making her feel more appreciated than she ever had been. They kept bringing food to tables that were never empty, and it was clear the visitors were the community's guests.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Schoolteachers Who Stood Guard
clips.summary: Satoko and Ginny were told by women at the church to come with two schoolteachers who had built a new home on the outskirts of Selma. The hosts made them comfortable and left, and in the morning the family revealed they had been so worried that someone stayed guard outside all night, which moved Satoko deeply since she had slept without knowing.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: David's Lodging and Stories of Injustice
clips.summary: David stayed in what seemed like public housing in the Black community, where people were incredibly gracious in feeding and housing the visitors. That night he heard for the first time stories of the rapes and periodic lynchings of young men in Selma, and got the sense that no justice system operated there.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Climate of Concern for Safety
clips.summary: Asked about concern for personal safety, David says that oddly he did not feel endangered that first night, even though he and Ginny had walked to the edge where the Black and white communities met and faced vile accusations from white youth across the line. He had not yet heard about Jim Reeb and was somehow blocking out what had happened on Bloody Sunday.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Dr. King Speaks at Brown Chapel
clips.summary: Returning to the rally, Satoko recalls her first real hearing of Dr. King, who spoke powerfully and thanked those whose conscience had brought them to Selma. He explained that a court appearance would keep him from leading the march in body though he would be with them in spirit, and that Reverend Ralph Abernathy would lead in his place.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Forming the Thursday Line of March
clips.summary: David recalls his understanding that an attempted Tuesday march had reached only the foot of the Pettus Bridge, and that another march was set for Thursday. Satoko describes how the marchers lined up on a dirt road outside the church in deliberate order, Black women in the middle, Black men flanking them, then white women, then white men on the outside, fifteen to twenty abreast in their Sunday best.
related_events: Thursday march toward Edmund Pettus Bridge

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Locked Arms and the Troopers Appear
clips.summary: Satoko describes how locking arms at dusk sent something like electricity through her body, the most powerful thing she had ever experienced, even as she felt afraid. As the marchers stood, Alabama troopers with reflective sunglasses, helmets, and batons suddenly surrounded the formation, looking fierce, yet she felt she could do anything with this group, both protector and protected.
related_events: Thursday march toward Edmund Pettus Bridge

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Jim Clark Halts the March
clips.summary: After three or four steps a booming voice from the head of the troopers, Sheriff Jim Clark of Dallas County, declared they could go no further. Reverend Abernathy said let us pray and the marchers knelt, beginning a night of vigil in which Satoko heard for the first time amazing life stories of what it was like to live in the South.
related_events: Thursday march stopped at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: A Night of Testimony From Selma's People
clips.summary: Satoko recounts the testimony shared during the vigil, of people who summoned great courage to register to vote only to be turned away on made-up reasons, and of friends' daughters who were raped and people abused in many ways. Despite a whole night of such stories, the people refused to give up and kept saying they were glad the visitors were there to help them win.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: David's Fear and the Journalists' Lights
clips.summary: David shares similar recollections with one difference, that as a white man he had the place of honor on the outside of the line of march and felt fear and vulnerability there, with Bloody Sunday vivid in his mind and troopers arrayed across the road. He emphasizes that the television networks kept their lights on all night, which he calls their guardian angel against what could have been another bloody scene.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Immense Respect for Reverend Abernathy
clips.summary: David expresses immense respect for Dr. Abernathy, noting that as an SCLC leader Abernathy was a little bumbling and not the greatest organizer, but could pray as if he had a direct pipeline to God. Both agree it was Abernathy who kept the whole march together that night and nobody else.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Helmets Come Off at Sunrise
clips.summary: Toward early morning the sun came up and Abernathy told the marchers to rise and sing the national anthem. Satoko describes the troopers' quandary over whether to keep their helmets on, until everyone looked to a flustered Jim Clark, who finally removed his helmet and the others immediately followed, a moment that felt like a communication and a community beyond the marchers.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Address to the Oppressor
clips.summary: David calls the helmets-off moment a wonderful representation of what the nonviolent movement was about, since King and others addressed not only the oppressed but the oppressor, trying to call forth the better angels of our natures. The interviewer remarks on the many ironies embedded in the scene.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: No Roller Coaster, Only the Vigil
clips.summary: Asked whether the night brought a jumble of feeling or a crystallization, David says he does not remember any roller coaster of emotion. Once the marchers came to a halt after a few steps and were caught up in the all-night vigil, his sense of fear disappeared and the whole night became an uplifting spiritual experience.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: A Community of Atonement
clips.summary: Satoko frames the experience as a community of atonement, pronounced at-one-ment, in which disparate people locked arms and marched together toward justice and truth, a healing that brought together all the ills and produced new life. She explains the march became a paradigm and an Aha moment for her life, and that she wrote her seminary thesis afterward on atoning community in nursery school children.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Friday Breakfast With Jim Farmer
clips.summary: David recalls that the experience carried through to Friday, though his memory becomes vague, with the twelve students and Jesse Jackson going out to breakfast. Jackson had arranged for Jim Farmer, head of the Congress of Racial Equality, to join them, a very dynamic man, and after the breakfast the group, including Jackson, left for home on an uneventful return to Chicago.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Long View of Civil Rights
clips.summary: Taking the long view, David reflects that the past half-century has been an extraordinary stretch for civil rights in this country, and not only for Black members of society. He traces the widening movement from there to women's liberation, to the disabled and handicapped, and finally to gay, lesbian, and transgender people.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Don't Ask Don't Tell Ends on Interview Day
clips.summary: The interviewer notes for the record that they meet on the first day the U.S. military no longer bars gay servicemembers. Given what happened to his brother, David says he celebrates the day, calling the abolition of the Don't Ask Don't Tell rule too long in coming.
related_events: Repeal of Don't Ask Don't Tell

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: From Seminary to Law and Public Service
clips.summary: David says what most stayed with him was the clear absence of a justice structure in Selma and the incredible disparity between the Black and white communities, which played a role in his dropping out of seminary to attend law school instead. Answering Kennedy's call to public service, he first worked for the Washington office of the National Council of Churches doing anti-poverty advocacy and then moved into congressional research, all underlain by what Dr. King stood for.
related_events: David Ackerman's transition to law and public service

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: A Black Family Moves to Bradford Road
clips.summary: Satoko recounts that after moving to a suburb of Washington, D.C., she found racial discrimination was not finished. In the early 1970s on Bradford Road in their all-white community, a Black family with two children moved in across the street, and she delightedly introduced their little girl to five girls in the house behind them, who all had a good time together that first day.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Vera Turned Away
clips.summary: The next day the little girl, Vera, returned crestfallen because the other children told her their father had said she could not come and play anymore. Satoko, angry both at having put Vera in that position and at the family's discrimination, marched out and gathered the girls together to ask why Vera could not play, and the oldest, around twelve or thirteen, said it was because she was Black.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Play With Anybody in Our Yard
clips.summary: Satoko told the girls that while she knew they had to obey their father, when they came to play in the Ackermans' yard between the two houses they would have to play with anybody there, including Vera, and they agreed and did so a few times. She concludes that the experience showed her things were not finished and that vigilance is needed because prejudice is passed on in little ways that affect little children.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Boulder Up the Mountain
clips.summary: Satoko confirms the community was Silver Spring, Maryland, a northern suburb about ten miles from the White House in Montgomery County. Thanked for sharing their history, David offers a final thought that the battle goes on, pointing to drug laws used against the Black community and new attempts to limit voting rights, so that somehow that boulder keeps having to be pushed up the mountain.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Back to the 88-Acre Farm
clips.summary: Interviewed in Marvell, Arkansas, Gertrude Jackson says she was born in Madison, Illinois in 1923, and that after her grandfather died her father moved the family back to Arkansas to take over the family's 88-acre cotton farm for his mother. She names her six brothers and four sisters and recalls the shock of a country childhood after Illinois.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Everybody in the Same Room
clips.summary: Jackson contrasts her short walk to school in Illinois with the four or five miles she walked in Arkansas to a single room holding first through eighth grade around a central heater, about sixty children with nails on the back wall for their coats, tracing her own schooling to tenth grade in Marvell, a later GED, and college courses through Mid-Delta. She remembers the white schools beautiful and whole while Black children walked as the bus passed, and a white boy chanting a slur from the bus window until a boy waited with a stick and popped his hand.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: So We Can Have Some Swings and Seesaws
clips.topic: A Daughter Wishes She Were White
clips.summary: Jackson tells, then retells for the camera, how her daughter standing in the kitchen said she wished she were white; pressed gently for the reason, the girl explained it was so she could have swings, seesaws, and the pretty white school, while their own school had only the naked ground. Jackson says at the time she let it pass because she knew that was just the way it was.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: The Bathroom Flooded Today
clips.summary: Jackson explains what first moved her to organize: the Marvell district built a school joining about six rural communities, but after a few years its sewage began backing up, flooding the bathrooms until the boys had to clean it with boxes from the store. When repeated trips to the school board brought only the retort that the children did not know how to use the bathroom, her husband resolved to act.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: If I Could Get These People to Take the Children Out
clips.summary: Jackson recounts her husband, whom people called Hog, going through the community and reaching parents through the men at their country store to ask that the children be kept home, so that only eight or ten pupils showed up and the school sat near empty for most of the term. She describes how the superintendent's brother first declared the plumbing fine until Howard called the state sanitation office from Little Rock, which made them dig it up and redo it so the children could return.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: It's Somebody Here From the Civil Rights
clips.summary: Jackson describes how civil rights workers came and met at neighbor Overtis Adams's house, prompting four or five people from her Jones Ridge community to gather and listen. She names the SNCC organizers Myrtle Glascoe and Howard Himmelbaum, who filled a church every Thursday night but pushed the local people to lead themselves rather than always being out front.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Like Another One of My Children
clips.summary: Jackson recalls Howard Himmelbaum first living with her uncle until the uncle grew nervous about a white man being seen leaving his house by the store, then moving to live with her cousin Carrie Davis, and Myrtle Glascoe being like another daughter among her children. She says the organizers urged people to stand up and be bold, but many came only to listen out of fear, teachers were kept from meetings to protect their jobs, and one man told her husband Jack he was for the cause but too scared to join.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: There She Is, There She Is
clips.summary: Jackson recalls a woman in the drugstore pointing her out to a white man as the one doing civil rights work, and remembers crosses burned at the homes of Reverend Masco Anderson and the leader John Hamilton. She describes how, hearing a gang planned to attack Hamilton's remote house, ten or twelve men met there with guns and stood watch all night.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: The News Is Good Tonight
clips.summary: Jackson says the local civil rights activity never made the news, but recalls how the family hushed the children whenever the television showed the Little Rock school integration, and how her excited little girl once announced that the news was good that night. She describes telling her children that, as in Marvell, whites and Blacks went to separate schools and troops came when Little Rock tried to integrate.
related_events: Little Rock Central High School integration

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Let's Boycott That Fair
clips.summary: Jackson recounts deciding at a boycott meeting, on Reverend Anderson's suggestion, to also boycott the county fair while the children were out of school. She describes urging a Pine Bluff college choir not to perform, parking by the empty fairgrounds with another woman, white and Black boys running off after hearing they were not supposed to attend, and the fair making no money that year.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: The Shop's on Fire
clips.topic: Crosses, a Burned Truck, and Sabotage
clips.summary: Jackson tells how a cross was burned in the shape into the grass of her own yard, and how one night her daughter saw the equipment shop on fire; her husband, expecting an attack, ran out without his shoes and dragged the burning truck by tractor across the field, the fire set by a rag stuffed in the gas tank. She adds that someone locked up a cotton picker in a far field, believed done by putting sugar in the tank, though her brother-in-law James repaired it and they never learned who did it.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: He Shot Across the Hood of My Car
clips.topic: Shots Fired on the Road Home
clips.summary: Jackson describes a truck slowing beside her as she drove home from work, then speeding past while the passenger fired a pistol across the front of her car; she kept it from her husband for a month so he would not put himself in danger searching for the men. She recalls him later leaving for a contested farm road with his pistol, saying they might get him but he could get some of them too.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Just Something for Children
clips.topic: The Idea for a Community Center
clips.summary: Jackson says the idea for the center came to her at her Mid-Delta desk as a persistent inner urging of just something for children, until Gale Thrower, the agriculture teacher, offered to fix up an unused room. She describes her aim that every child see a book before starting school, sending retired teachers like Maxine Hathaway and Anna May Smith into homes to read, and arranging nurses from Helena to give the children their shots.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Forty Miles to Head Start
clips.summary: Jackson recounts how, once BGACDC was founded and a building added, Helena staff brought every service to the center, and describes her first Mid-Delta job transporting children to Head Start, driving roughly forty miles each morning and evening across the fields. She tells how the agency sought her out as the only person in the district with a station wagon, which her husband at first refused for her.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: The Twin Who Knocked the Work Off the Wall
clips.summary: Jackson remembers a disruptive twin boy in Head Start who would circle the group hitting children and knocking work off the wall, until home visits revealed he was not coming from a comfortable home; she would calm him with walks and field trips and kept him the two years a four-year-old could attend.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Stay Off That Man's Place
clips.summary: Jackson tells of a family living on a white man's place whose boss ordered them to keep the woman driving the white station wagon away because he wanted no one connected to Martin Luther King on his land; within a year the family had built their own house. She reflects that the move came just in time, since by then her home-start work would have brought ten families together for monthly meetings.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Stand Up for Yourself
clips.summary: Jackson says her father, a farmer, left Arkansas for a job at a factory called Commonwealth before returning, then answers what she most wants her descendants to know of the movement work: to stand up for themselves, get involved, and attend PTA and school meetings, a charge she repeats after noise interrupts the take.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Trucks Bumper to Bumper
clips.topic: The Armed Standoff at the School
clips.summary: Jackson explains that when the district's grade-by-grade integration reached year two the white community balked, so when Myrtle proposed simply walking the children over without the choice forms, word leaked and the school block was ringed with trucks bumper to bumper, two or three armed men on each. She and her cousin drove around to see it, while the boycotting children read books at her church.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: We Wanted to Go to Court
clips.topic: A Plaintiff, a Judge, and the Yellow Bus
clips.summary: Jackson recounts how Mr. Himmelbaum said they needed someone willing to be the plaintiff, and her husband volunteered because no one else would, leading to a court case where, after a couple of days, Judge Owen Harris ordered integration and an end to separate white and Black buses on the same route, with young lawyer John Walker correcting white witnesses who used a slur. She closes the story, told twice for the camera, of her son answering the family's habitual question of whether the coming bus was white or black with the word yellow.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Four Components in Poplar Grove
clips.summary: Reverend Jerome Turner, youth and family director, describes BGACDC near Poplar Grove and Marvell in Phillips County, laying out its four components: youth and family services, economic development, housing, and administration. He details the BGACDC Manor low-income housing, the after-school Delta Reach and Teach program co-run with the Marvell-Elaine district, the Best Food in Town cafeteria with summer jobs, and the USDA- and FDA-partnered Delta Obesity Research Project that found residents deficient in vitamin D despite ample sunlight.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: We Call Her Our Ambassador
clips.topic: Mrs. Jackson's Legacy at the Center
clips.summary: Turner introduces Gertrude Jackson as the center's ambassador, in her eighties and present since the late 1970s, who founded it with Gale Thrower and other community members so boys, girls, and adults alike would have a place, giving it the name Boys Girls Adults Community Development Center. He says hundreds of young people, among them returning investment consultant Marcus Thrower, came through its mentoring programs to become entrepreneurs, lawyers, doctors, and educators.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: God Just Put Him in My Eyesight
clips.summary: Turner tells how Mrs. Jackson, having seen him speak at a meeting years earlier, sought him out to work at BGACDC and praises her willingness to give everything and to see the good in people. Jackson adds her own account of spotting the young man at a meeting, knowing he would be great, and waiting fifteen years for him to grow into the role.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: On the Highway Where People Can See It
clips.summary: Jackson describes how executive director Beatrice wanted housing, so they worked with the FHA to build it over white opposition that claimed a housing project would bring drugs, and how, after the first site fell through, they insisted on a highway location rather than a hidden country road. She says the project holds about thirty-nine units, with twenty-some more across town.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: It Was Just Another Day
clips.summary: Theodore Eldridge, called TJ, introduces himself as Jackson's grandson through her oldest daughter Juanita and a USDA farm service agent who oversees her land, recalling driving tractors from age eleven. He describes his grandparents as great storytellers who recounted hardships without bitterness or a victim's framing, telling it simply as what happened and what they did to get over it.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Oh Yeah, We Know Her
clips.topic: The Center as the Community's Place
clips.summary: TJ reflects that his grandmother's nonchalant idea to help children grew into a center affecting hundreds of families, so that from the police and mayor to state representatives everyone in the Marvell area knows the Jackson family. He and Jackson describe the center as the community's only gathering place, packed for every holiday with decorations the women prepared, and note that after the highway took their parking there is nowhere else to meet, the center standing as an example of when a no-longer-tight community came together to do something for themselves.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Kind of Like a Fairytale
clips.summary: Asked by her grandson whether she enjoyed the movement work, Jackson says she really did, finding the word not fear but being out there in action and never fearing for a friend. TJ reflects that he never heard a story of fear, that it felt almost an afterthought, like a fairytale, even as Martin Luther King and others were dying for the same things.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Several Other Communities Were Influenced
clips.summary: Reverend Turner closes by naming centers influenced by BGACDC, including the Lee County Community Development Center in Marianna and efforts in Elaine and across an Arkansas, Mississippi, and Louisiana collaborative under the Foundation of Midsouth, crediting the travels of Mrs. Jackson and the executive director. Jackson remarks that this is what the Lord showed her in the young man years before.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: She Always Had Money
clips.summary: Interviewed on November 17, 2010, Myrtle Gonza Glascoe, born and raised in Washington D.C., opens by saying her family was not political and nothing in her childhood pointed to the movement, except that she was always too frank, and recalls hauling groceries by bicycle for a quarter and negotiating carnival money from her father.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: I Went to School and I Partied
clips.summary: She recounts graduating from Dunbar High School and Howard University without any political spark, classmates remembering she always had something going, catching a classmate plagiarizing an Esquire essay, and her easy facility for taking notes and earning excellent grades while knowing nothing of the politics around her.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: They Asked Me to Leave
clips.summary: As a health and physical education major she took her first job at a Catholic boarding school for young Black women, let the girls dance to the records they wanted, diagrammed for a student how she could get pregnant, stopped attending mass, and was told by the Mother Superior and principal that she had to go by that night, negotiating pay for the rest of the year.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: A Rod of Steel Up My Back
clips.summary: After earning a social work degree from the University of Pennsylvania and working at Rosewood State Hospital outside Baltimore, a coworker who chaired Baltimore CORE brought her to a meeting; refusing to walk the picket line because she did not believe in nonviolence, she did support work until a greasy counterman ordered her group out of the White Coffee Pot, an anger she felt all the way home that turned her toward the movement.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: These Are the Youth and They Need Support
clips.summary: She describes joining the Route 40 demonstrations, learning that going to jail was a deliberate choice people prepared for with a toothbrush, driving Howard students like Courtland Cox and Bill Mahoney to actions, and explaining that her refusal of nonviolence was a gut response because she had attended no sessions and knew nothing of its philosophy.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: Like I Was Eating Dinner Every Day
clips.topic: From Baltimore to San Francisco SNCC
clips.summary: She recounts leaving Baltimore in 1962 for Los Angeles, reconnecting with CORE, then through a national CORE officer she had befriended meeting Mike Miller, the SNCC support chair in San Francisco; she moved north, devoured the papers Mike brought back from the South, and met Lawrence Guyot, Ivanhoe Donaldson, and Fannie Lou Hamer, whom she drove around and who pulled out her tobacco for a good chew.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: There's No Reason You Can't Go
clips.summary: Sitting on a committee to choose people for the 1965 summer after many white volunteers had stayed on, she decided to go herself, prompted by a young Chinese coworker, recently widowed, who asked why she could not go and ticked off that she had no bills, no husband, no children, and recalls finding in Arkansas the same joy of dancing she had known visiting cousins in southern Maryland.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: Stay in Your Seat
clips.summary: Becoming Director of the Freedom School in West Helena, Arkansas, she ran it as a day camp drawing on years of camp work, and names as the summer's most important lesson the guidance from Mike and the project director to watch, listen, and learn rather than lead, to attend community meetings but write down her thoughts and stay in her seat, doing voter registration as one of two Black volunteers among nine.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: Feces Floating Down the Hall
clips.topic: The Turner School and the Boycott
clips.summary: Describing the method of helping people solve their own problems, she tells the Jones Ridge, Arkansas case where families at Turner Elementary, plagued by toilets backing up and feces floating down the hall whenever it rained, were brought by her coworker to take their complaint to the Little Rock Health Department, found the pipes had been laid uphill, and gained the confidence that grew into a school boycott to force desegregation in the summer of 1966.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: All of My Kids Hate White Folks
clips.summary: She describes holding 1500 of 1800 Black children out of the segregated school for six weeks in 1966 until the district desegregated, her own growing doubt that they should be entering hostile white schools rather than building their own, and Gertrude Jackson telling her years later that all her children hated white folks after being barred from the band and treated with condescension.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: Sugar in the Cotton Picker
clips.summary: She talks about the Jackson family of Marvell, the project's center, Gertrude and Earlis Jackson with their eleven children, his brother James, and a cousin who owned the store, all staunch for change, and how when sugar was put in the gas tank of their cotton picker the brothers calmly took the engine apart, cleaned it, reassembled it, and kept working.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: This Is Your Idea, Not Mine
clips.topic: Making a Leader Stand on Her Own
clips.summary: She recounts realizing that Gertrude Jackson kept planting ideas on her to present at meetings, and how she insisted Mrs. Jackson present her own ideas so the community would know she was the one who had them, a tense first attempt that grew until Mrs. Jackson pulled people together, got them organized, and helped found a community center that still exists.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: Building Blocks to Being in Charge
clips.summary: She frames her goal as creating a process where local people got in touch with their own capability and developed the confidence to act on their own, contrasting Gertrude Jackson, who learned to trust herself and connected with a Connecticut woman to send all her girls to college, with her husband and others who wanted to lead but did not yet trust themselves.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: Stuff Inside Me I Didn't Know I Had
clips.topic: White Workers Leave the Black Communities
clips.summary: She describes her Jewish coworker Howard Himmelbaum, with whom she worked well, returning from a New Orleans meeting where SNCC decided white workers should leave the Black communities to work in white ones, and how, scared at first, she discovered after he left a capacity to make decisions and generate ideas she had not known were in her.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: Not Seen as Someone in Charge
clips.topic: Authority, Race, and the Man-Woman Role
clips.summary: She reflects that she had been deferring to Howard in a traditional man-woman role she had not recognized, and analyzes the deeper challenge of getting Black people to act for themselves without seeing a college-educated outsider with a master's degree as an authority figure, a difficulty sharper still for a white worker, while noting Howard's willingness to reflect on how he affected the community.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: It Gets Inside You
clips.topic: Activism and the First Head Start
clips.summary: Leaving SNCC in the fall of 1967 broke and taking a Head Start job, she explains that becoming an activist is permanent, and recounts how Mississippi's 1965 program, the first Head Start in the country, was set up by returning white volunteers but, built on the wheels and infrastructure of the movement, began to destruct that infrastructure once paid jobs and board decisions divided people who had organized together for free.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: I Had the Job Because I Had the Job
clips.topic: Senator Stennis and the Painful Politics
clips.summary: She describes how Senator Stennis fought the Head Start program because staff kept doing movement work, bringing it to repeated dead stops over jobs and board seats until coworkers who had struggled alongside her grew angry that she still held a job, a strain so painful she finally decided to quit each time the program stopped and wait until it returned.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: What's in Your Suitcase
clips.topic: Tougaloo and the Small Group in Process
clips.summary: She recounts teaching at Tougaloo College from 1969 to 1972 under Dr. Ernst Borinski, becoming a Muslim in the Nation and sharing how to eat to live, and developing a course called The Small Group in Process focused on knowing oneself and one's community, which she still salts into her teaching by asking students what beliefs and attitudes they carry in their suitcase into a classroom of children.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: In Support of the Work
clips.topic: West Point and the Food Stamps
clips.summary: She clarifies her role in John Buffington's West Point, Mississippi project, where she was not on her own but supported work already underway, attended meetings, helped with the restaurant John had started, and ran the food stamps, buying all the food and drawing complaints for not buying what people wanted, doing no voter registration there as she had in Arkansas.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: If He Did Not Say No, I Was Gone
clips.summary: She tells how she was invited in 1985 to be Founding Director of the Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture, housed in the building of the Avery School, founded in 1865 as the first academic high school for Black people in Charleston and reputed within the Black community to favor light-skinned students, and how graduates lobbied to reclaim it as an institution to support Black education.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: Bringing the Carawan Collection Home
clips.topic: Spending the Budget to Build It
clips.summary: She describes inheriting a deteriorating building and a single office, choosing to spend her leftover first-year budget by purchasing Guy and Candy Carawan's collection of John's Island music, art, and photographs after they spoke of the islanders wanting the materials available to them, and planning a conference on the work of Black women in South Carolina to show the center was alive.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: I'm Not Going to Ask Nobody
clips.summary: She recounts justifying each year's budget to South Carolina decision-makers by listing what she had done and intended to do, partnering with a Black Charlestonian legislator and an earlier graduates' commitment from the legislature, and her activism of telling her boss David, the Director of Libraries, her plans so that unless he said no outright she went ahead and spent every dollar on books, tapes, equipment, and a copy of Du Bois's papers.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: This Place Has a Life of Its Own
clips.topic: Leaving Avery, Then the Education Course
clips.summary: She explains that the center took on a life of its own that the college did not want, preferring it sit as a showpiece they had given Black people, so she left rather than just move papers, and then in the School of Education was handed a syllabus about only white people, which she immediately revised to introduce Native, African, and Asian peoples without asking permission.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: On One Hand African, On the Other American
clips.summary: Asked about her research on W.E.B. Du Bois and Booker T. Washington, she discusses her published work on the evolution of Du Bois's educational thought, a paper at Greeley, Colorado on his role founding the NAACP, and her reading of two-ness as the African and American halves children must hold, along with Du Bois's later turn on the Talented Tenth toward the most gifted coming forward to serve.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: It's Alive and Well and It Eats on Us
clips.summary: She frames the struggle as preparing young people to know who they are while seeing clearly the contradictions of a society whose desegregated education does not serve them, naming Marcus Garvey's Universal Negro Improvement Association calling for dual citizenship, and arguing that teachers of every race, taught to accept the innate inferiority of children of color, do not believe Black children can learn.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: My Child Has to Learn How to Think
clips.summary: She recounts pressing future teachers who came back wanting to recite book answers, refusing to set a memorization test and instead asking them to apply what they read to what is happening in schools, insisting that a teacher who cannot think cannot teach her child, and that this is true for all children, including poor white ones.

clips.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
clips.title: You Will Never Get a Husband Down There
clips.summary: She closes by recalling that she told no one she was going South and just went, that her mother arrived for a visit transformed into an activist whose nurses' group sent down clothes, yet greeted her at home with the warning that she would never get a husband there, a mother she later understood to have been terrified and wishing her on the path to marriage and children.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Born and Bred in Mississippi
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program with videographer John Bishop for the Smithsonian and Library of Congress in Argo, Illinois, on May 23, 2011, Simeon Wright opens with his parents: a hard but fair cotton farmer who vowed he was born and bred in Mississippi and would die there, and his easy, singing mother, raised in Hazlehurst and married in Sumner in 1925.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: To Correct the Inaccuracies
clips.summary: Wright explains that after watching documentaries that presented untruths about the Emmett Till case, his wife urged him to write Simeon's Story to tell what happened at the store and in his bedroom and to rebut myths, including the false claims that he and his brother Maurice dared Emmett to speak to Carolyn Bryant and that his father escaped Mississippi in a coffin.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Someone From the North
clips.summary: Nearly thirteen in the late August of 1955, Wright describes the excitement when he learned cousins Emmett Till and Wheeler Parker Jr. were coming from Chicago, Emmett a great storyteller who told of Lincoln Park and North Avenue Beach, places Wright still visits with his Sunday school picnic.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: How You Swim in Mississippi
clips.summary: Wright recounts welcoming Emmett and Wheeler, sharing a bed with Emmett, and teaching them country ways: running snakes out of the water before swimming and borrowing a neighbor's watermelons, and he refutes the false account that the boys stole his father's car and drove into Money, noting his father had stopped preaching in 1949.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Emmett Whistled at Her
clips.summary: Picking up after a recording pause, Wright fixes the date as Wednesday, August 24, 1955, and describes the brief trip to Bryant's Store with his brother Maurice, Wheeler, Emmett, and Roosevelt Crawford, where Maurice sent him in to watch Emmett, who paid for his items, and as Carolyn Bryant walked to her car Emmett whistled at her, sending the terrified boys racing for the car.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: She Made That Up
clips.summary: Wright lists the six boys who went, names another possible neighbor he is unsure of, and describes the wooden-floored store with its counter, rejecting Carolyn Bryant's trial claim that Emmett put his arms around her and asked for a date, testimony she gave when not under oath because, as he believes, her lawyers knew it could be perjury.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: He Pulled His Gun and Made Him Close
clips.summary: Wright explains that stores in Money closed early on Wednesdays, and the family ordinarily would not shop at Bryant's, but Bryant kept his open in defiance of the rule, once even pulling a gun to make another owner close, and he reaffirms that Emmett completed a routine purchase and whistled only when Carolyn came out toward her car.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Don't Tell Daddy
clips.summary: Wright tells of fleeing down the seven-mile Dark Fear Road, his brother Maurice stopping when headlights appeared that turned out to be neighbors, and Emmett begging the boys not to tell his father, having no notion he could be killed for the whistle; Wright also recalls Carolyn gave a long look as they ran.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: None of That Happened
clips.summary: Wright disputes the circulated stories that the boys lollygagged outside, that Emmett carried a photo of a white girlfriend, and that the cousins dared him to enter, noting a nephew said so on film for Eyes on the Prize though he was not there, and recalls the neighbor girl Rutha Mae Crawford warning the next day that they would hear more about it.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: This Is the Wrong Boy
clips.topic: Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam Come
clips.summary: After a Saturday trip to Greenwood, Wright describes how about two a.m. Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam came to the four-bedroom house, woke Wheeler and said they wanted the fat boy from Chicago, then marched to Wright's room where Milam, with gun and flashlight, made Emmett dress; his mother pleaded and offered money, Milam threatened his sixty-four-year-old father, and a woman's voice, which Wright believes was Carolyn Bryant's, confirmed Emmett as the right one.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: I Can't Stay Here Another Night
clips.topic: The Aftermath and the Call to Chicago
clips.summary: Wright recalls that he never slept again that night, his mother ran to a straw boss named Chamblee who would not get involved, and with no phone and no police his father drove her to her brother's home in Sumner, from which she later left for Chicago and never returned to the house; he describes using the bossman's telephone and how a boy who would later falsely claim to have been at the store had in fact arrived that night, slept through the kidnapping, and phoned Wright's oldest sister Willie Mae, who reached Emmett's mother Mamie.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: There Will Be No Burial Here Today
clips.topic: The Body Found and Sheriff Smith
clips.summary: Wright recounts staying with neighbors who owned their own land, returning to picking cotton while hoping Emmett was alive, and the discovery of the body in the Tallahatchie River about twenty miles north; when officials tried to bury it the same day, Leflore County sheriff George Smith halted the burial, a man Wright understands as fairer and unwilling to carry that stain.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Strider Testified for the Defense
clips.summary: Wright describes Sheriff Strider testifying for the defense and reversing himself on whether the river body was Black or white, the murder indictment sought in Tallahatchie County where they felt they had a better chance, and his own role identifying Emmett's ring (Bobo's ring) as a subpoenaed material witness, though in the end Mamie's identification meant he was not called.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: They Lost a Way of Life
clips.topic: The Witness Room and Where Hatred Came From
clips.summary: Wright recalls the circus atmosphere of the witness room, where the bailiff was unusually kind and he felt sure, from cowboy movies, that a conviction was a done deal, then reflects that the jury and judge's racism ran deeper than skin: a hatred he traces to the Civil War and a lost aristocratic way of life passed down through generations, unlike the white boys he played and hunted snakes with who were not born racist, and his refusal to start calling a playmate named Tommy Peterson Mister.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: We Can't Stay Here Any Longer
clips.topic: The Verdict and His Father's Courage
clips.summary: Picking up after a recording pause, Wright describes the not-guilty verdict that crushed his father and the segregationists' rejoicing, his father's decision to leave, and the courage of his father, warned by neighbors he would be killed and encouraged by Medgar Evers of the NAACP, to testify anyway, doing what no other Black man in Mississippi had done and lived.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: No Job for a Sixty-Three-Year-Old
clips.summary: Wright contrasts Mississippi life, where his father had winters to hunt and fish and once cleared six thousand dollars in 1948 under a fair German born-again Christian bossman, with the move to Argo Summit where factories would not hire a man past sixty and his father could only find restaurant cleanup work.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: She Never Sang Again
clips.summary: Wright recalls living rent-free in his brother James's four-flat, then describes how the murder took years off his mother, a churchwoman who sang every day in Mississippi but fell silent in Chicago, comforted only by a sense that the Lord had spared her family, and he defends her education against a writer who called her uneducated, recalling the stories she told of the Lindbergh kidnapping and a tracker called Hawkjaw Mullin.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: If They Slap Us, We Slap Back
clips.summary: Wright recalls Martin Luther King bringing his nonviolent message to Chicago and Marquette Park, and how he and his friends, knowing what Mississippi segregationists did, refused to march under a rule that they could not retaliate if slapped or spit on, until at age twenty-four, sitting half high in a tavern, he heard a voice warn that if he died in his sins he would go to hell, which began to change his life.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Harder Than Picking Cotton
clips.summary: Wright traces his working life from shining shoes on the street and in a parlor in Wheeler Parker's barbershop, to setting bowling pins at a hundred and five pounds, to Dearborn Glass, and finally to Reynolds Metals, where he entered a pipefitting apprenticeship under Union Local 597 and worked from 1968 until retiring in 1994, near the August anniversary of Emmett's abduction.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Something I Want
clips.summary: Wright explains that most of his mother's side stayed quiet about the case while his father's side sometimes raised it, and describes how he came to commit his life to Christ by watching the joy and peace in his cousin Wheeler Parker after Wheeler's own conversion; Parker, whose wedding he stood up in as best man, is now his pastor and Wright is a deacon who teaches second and third graders.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: You're Calling Me a Liar
clips.topic: The Inaccuracies That Brought Him Back
clips.summary: Wright tells how the case drew him back when reporters merged what he told them with false history, and he names the inaccuracies that anger him: that his father escaped in a coffin, that the cousins dared Emmett, that Emmett did not whistle, and James Hicks's claim that his father said Thar he, when the transcript shows he said There he is.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: He Would Lay Across Your Threshold
clips.summary: Wright recounts how filmmaker Keith Beauchamp, whom his wife urged him to talk to, drew out his account and, with Alvin Sykes, pieced together federal violations that justified reopening the case, leading to a meeting with the Northern District U.S. Attorney Mr. Greenleaf, who was visibly shaken and kept his promise to seek a reinvestigation jointly with Mississippi.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: We Agreed on That Already
clips.summary: Wright explains that Mississippi required proof the body at Burr Oak Cemetery was Emmett's, so the body was exhumed despite a protesting cousin he says was put up to it by so-called civil rights workers seeking TV time, and DNA testing, for which he was the secret donor, confirmed the identity and showed Emmett had not been castrated nor his teeth knocked out as some had said.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Not Enough Information
clips.summary: Wright recounts that after tracing the gun and shells back to Bryant and Milam, prosecutor Joyce Childs said there was not enough information to indict Carolyn Bryant despite a witness placing her in the truck that night, that the FBI rather than Childs questioned Bryant and that Bryant sought immunity to talk but was refused, leaving Wright greatly disappointed.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: In Honor of Emmett Till
clips.summary: Wright describes how Alvin Sykes of Kansas City pursued legislation through a senator to fund the pursuit of unsettled cold cases, how he and his wife drove to Washington and watched from the gallery as the bill stalled over one holdout senator before passing months later with about a million dollars, and his lingering wish that Mississippi or Leflore County would call the verdict unjust.
related_events: Emmett Till Unsolved Civil Rights Crime Act

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Thar He
clips.topic: James Hicks and the Coffin Story
clips.summary: Wright examines why even scholarly tellings went askew, focusing on James Hicks, the Black press reporter who claimed his father pointed at J. W. Milam and said Thar he when the transcript reads There he is, and who also spread the coffin-escape story with Medgar Evers, which Wright rebuts by asking how his father's three sons got out if there was only one coffin.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: He Never Interviewed Us
clips.summary: Wright rejects William Bradford Huie's Look magazine writing, the claim that he looked at Wright's mother and saw she was uneducated and a fabricated interview in Argo in which the family supposedly showed Huie where a white girlfriend lived, and he likewise dismisses a Hampton, Virginia play and the Emerge Magazine story that Maurice took fifty cents of store credit to lead the killers to the house, insisting the family had no credit at Bryant's.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: If You Die in Your Sins
clips.summary: Returning to the conversion he described earlier, Wright retells how at twenty-four, after King came to town in 1966 and he refused to march, he heard a voice in a tavern about two a.m. warning that if he died in his sins he would go to hell, and within two weeks in February 1967 quit drinking, smoking, and his girlfriend, committed his life to Christ, and came to see nonviolence as the only way to change a country.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Vengeance to God, Justice to the Government
clips.summary: Wright explains what it meant to forgive Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam: not surrendering the claim to justice but leaving vengeance to Almighty God, a difficult process rooted in the warning that if he did not forgive he would not be forgiven, and he describes how smells like honeysuckle and the sound of a passing car still bring back the night and the grief, leaving scar tissue on a healed heart.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Laws Can't Change a Man's Heart
clips.summary: Wright invokes Jeremiah at the potter's house as an image of his own remade life and assesses the case's legacy: federal law that trumps state law brought changes, but hearts have changed little and racism, though no longer in your face as under Jim Crow, persists in money-gated neighborhoods, even as new generations horrified by Emmett's story, like the prosecutor who convicted the Birmingham church bombers, vow to bring change.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: I'm From This Dirt
clips.summary: Wright reflects that Mississippi is both a place of horror and the home of his childhood, that he visits but would not live there, and he closes with his message to young people to go to school, respect their elders, love one another, and believe the system can change, while decrying a Chicago police beating of a bartender and insisting that whoever commits the crime should do the time, Black or white.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Born Three Months After Brown
clips.summary: Glenda Funchess introduces herself, born in Hattiesburg, Mississippi on August 17, 1954, which she notes was three months after the Brown v. Board of Education decision. She comes from a family of twelve children, with her mother originally from Alabama and her father from Jefferson Davis County, Mississippi.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Her Parents and Twelve Siblings
clips.summary: Funchess describes her mother's work as one of the first homemaker social-worker aides with the Department of Human Services and her father's long career as a chef at the University of Southern Mississippi. She was the sixth youngest of twelve, and her oldest sister had already joined the Great Migration to Cleveland, Ohio.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: A Segregated Town and Freedom Summer
clips.summary: Funchess recalls a segregated Hattiesburg with thriving Black businesses, cafes, cleaners, and hotels, and explains that she became politically aware at age nine during Freedom Summer, when SNCC students showed the community rights it was not exercising. She began school in a local activist's kindergarten before attending Mary Bethune Elementary.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: The Stair-Step Desegregation Plan
clips.summary: Funchess explains Hattiesburg's stair-step approach to dismantling the dual school system from 1965 through 1968, adding four grades each year until all twelve were desegregated. She was asked in the late 1960s to attend an affluent, distant white junior high that had enrolled only four or five Black students the prior year.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: The NAACP Education Committee Asks
clips.summary: A member of the NAACP education committee requested that Funchess and her sister, both strong students, transfer into the white junior high, and their mother agreed. Funchess recalls that her mother's employer, a paralyzed man whose family she nursed, demanded to know what the NAACP had given her to send her children there.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Expectation Versus Reality
clips.summary: A neighbor who had transferred to the poor-white school the year before briefed the children daily, leading them to believe the affluent white school would be friendlier because Freedom Summer had taught them that poorer whites embraced the Klan. They assumed the wealthier students would be easier, only to find a harsh reality.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Nine Months Alone at Lunch
