clips.summary: Funchess describes a hostile school where no one spoke to her and students jumped up whenever she set her lunch tray down, isolation she attributes to the principal setting the tone. Years later a former classmate wrote from Delaware to apologize, having seen an article about Funchess after she dedicated a civil rights marker honoring Dr. King's 1968 visit.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Seated Alone, Her Paper Destroyed
clips.summary: On her first day a science teacher moved Funchess to a solitary front table, where she sat the entire year. After she wrote a history paper on Dr. King, whom she had heard speak about the Poor People's Campaign at a local church weeks before his death, classmates cut her completed paper to pieces.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: No Counseling for the Children
clips.summary: Funchess recalls riding a neighbor's Volkswagen van with about fifteen children and argues the district failed them by providing no counseling amid resistance from the White Citizens' Council and the Sovereignty Commission. She recounts learning of an African American boy at the school who was struck in the face with his own shoes during her time there.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Choir Music and Racist Remarks
clips.summary: Funchess loved choir but recalls a classmate crying because she did not want her parents to see her standing next to Funchess at a graduation program, after which a student was moved. She also remembers boys in choir using racial slurs when joking about singing Negro spirituals.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Separated, Then Leaving the School
clips.summary: Funchess explains that she and the only other Black eighth grader were given identical schedules but placed in separate class periods, sharing only a twenty-minute homeroom. After a discomfiting nursing-home choir performance and morning football drills in which a girl's arm was broken, Funchess left the school because her grades were certain to drop.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: A Better School and Calls at Home
clips.summary: Funchess transferred to a poor-white school on the other side of town and credits its administrator with setting a welcoming tone, noting an African American girl co-hosted a program there. She received harassing phone calls at home from students at the affluent school, then returned to Rowan, the African American high school, after ninth grade.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Zoning Brings White Students to Rowan
clips.summary: Funchess describes a zoning plan in her eleventh-grade year that sent a handful of white students across the railroad tracks to Rowan, the African American high school. A Black history program later recognized two white students, including the son of a local college president, who had enrolled at the Black school.

clips.subject: Glenda Funchess
clips.title: Consolidation and a Segregation Academy
clips.summary: Funchess estimates the city was about thirty-five percent African American and recalls that most whites stayed when the two school systems consolidated in 1972 following the Alexander v. Holmes ruling. She notes that a local segregation academy founded in the mid-1960s offered some white families an alternative to the integrated public schools.
related_events: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Born in Newton, Baker County, 1930
clips.summary: Will Griffin opens the March 9, 2013 interview at Albany State University, recorded with videographer John Bishop for the Civil Rights History Project, introducing Louise Broadway of the Baker County Movement. She gives her full name, says she was born in 1930 in the small town of Newton in Baker County, Georgia, and recalls a childhood as the child of farming parents who owned no farm.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Walking to a Cold School as Buses Passed
clips.summary: Broadway describes walking to school every morning, sometimes ten or fifteen miles, hands too cold to feel until they warmed around a potbelly stove. She recalls that the white children rode school buses that passed the Black children walking the same roads in the same neighborhoods, and that she knew even then it was unfair.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Seven Grades in One Church Sanctuary
clips.summary: She explains that school was held inside a church with no partitions, one teacher seating the first through seventh grades by section across three pews and teaching each in turn while the others kept quiet. She names two of her teachers, Miss Salome and Miss Mary Hawkins, and recalls graduating from the seventh grade and going on to high school in Newton.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Two Colored High Schools and a House Full
clips.summary: Broadway recalls that Newton had two colored high schools, east and west, and that she attended Newton Colored High School, where classes met in army barracks brought in by the state. As the youngest of four, with one brother and two sisters, she describes a doting older sister and a family of six living in a one-room country shack with a single fireplace.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Church First, and a Dollar a Day
clips.summary: She recalls her parents teaching her to make do with what they had and to put church before any school activity, her father working at a grist mill grinding corn into meal and her mother working as a maid. After graduating in 1948 she wanted college but her sharecropper father and maid mother earned roughly fifty cents to a dollar a day, so her first job was as a maid for a dollar a day for a family she names as Mamie White.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Marrying Grady Broadway in 1949
clips.summary: Broadway recounts meeting Grady Broadway Junior, who was raised by an older brother after their parents died young, through visits to a cousin and at church where the two would only smile at one another. When he later moved into her community they reconnected as old friends, married in 1949, and went on to have five children together.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Knowing It Was Unfair
clips.summary: Asked when she first noticed racial conditions, Broadway points back to childhood, to being unable to ride the bus and to white children throwing spitballs at Black children walking to school. She says she always knew it was unfair, but felt there was nothing she could do until the Civil Rights Act was passed and the segregated schools came up for change.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Enrolling a Son in the White School
clips.summary: In the 1950s, while her husband sharecropped for a local white farmer, the Broadways enrolled their eldest child, Jesse Lee Broadway, in the all-white school. When the farmer heard, he told her husband to withdraw the boy or settle his entire debt before year's end, so they withdrew him until the year ended, after which her husband stopped working for that farmer and they were able to send three children to the school the next year.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: A Son Hospitalized, Racing Home to Protect Them
clips.summary: Broadway describes the widespread intimidation, including one son beaten so badly at school that he was hospitalized for days, and white youths following Black children off the bus to attack them. Working in Albany, she got a call that her children were under attack and drove home so fast the speedometer chain broke, and recalls that no one was punished though the school did begin patrolling its grounds.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Treating the Beaten on Bloody Saturday
clips.summary: Broadway worked thirty years as assistant to Dr. J. L. Shirley, a medical doctor in Albany, who treated for free the civil rights workers and citizens beaten by a mob on what she calls Bloody Saturday after the nearby town of Camilla refused them care. She recalls Sherrod being brought in bruised, and that her own St. Matthew's Missionary Baptist Church was fired upon as the marchers regrouped and lay down among the pews.
related_events: Bloody Saturday march in Baker County

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Secretary of the Baker County Movement
clips.summary: As one of the secretaries of the Baker County Movement, Broadway took notes at the meetings as citizens testified to mistreatment, such as a child struck in the head with a stick, and says some of those notes now rest at the East Baker Historical Society in Newton. She frames the demand not as integration for its own sake but as equal opportunity, recalling that Black schools received the white schools' worn and torn books a year later.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Dr. Shirley's Gift to the Movement
clips.summary: Broadway affirms that Dr. J. L. Shirley supported the movement, willingly treating the injured of Bloody Saturday rather than turning them away as the doctors in Camilla had. She notes that he knew she was involved in the movement.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: First Black Worker on the County Ambulance
clips.summary: Broadway recounts that the county commissioners offered a free emergency-care course, and that after her workdays at Dr. Shirley's office she trained at night to become, by her account, the first Black person in Baker County to complete it and ride the ambulance. She worked the ambulance without pay at all hours, motivated by a wish for people of her own color to be helped by someone of their color.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: The Bloodiest Day and the March to Register
clips.summary: Asked for anything more about her movement work, Broadway returns to the bloodiest day she witnessed, when a woman shielded Sherrod and may have saved his life as people were beaten to the ground. She recalls marching from a local church to the courthouse to register people to vote, with Sherrod among them.
related_events: Voter registration march in Baker County

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Knocked Down, Then Back the Next Morning
clips.summary: Broadway describes a county official confronting Sherrod as an outsider with no right to be there and knocking him into a corner before ordering him to leave, after which the marchers withdrew to the church. She stresses they returned the very next morning and kept on until things improved, and recalls that when she finally registered during this time she was allowed simply to sign her name without a literacy test.
related_events: Voter registration march in Baker County

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: What Became of the Injured, and a Cruel Man
clips.summary: Asked what happened to those beaten and injured, Broadway says they recovered and stayed with the movement until people were registered to vote, and that conditions in Baker County gradually calmed. She describes a local white man who harassed Black travelers, stopped them for tickets, ran a service station with whites-only signs, and was eventually felled by a stroke years before his death.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Freedom of Speech and a Doctor's Bills Forgiven
clips.summary: Broadway reflects that the movement won Black residents the right to speak freely where before a word to a white person could bring a beating, and that desegregating the schools gave people courage and wisdom to stand up. Returning to Dr. Shirley, she confirms he never charged the students and citizens beaten on Bloody Saturday, calling the free care his contribution to the movement.

clips.subject: Louise Broadway
clips.title: Closing Thanks
clips.summary: The interviewer thanks Louise Broadway for sharing her story, and the recording closes as a presentation of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Opening the Joint Interview in Albany
clips.summary: Hasan Kwame Jeffries opens the Civil Rights History Project interview on March 9, 2013, in Albany, Georgia, with Lucius Holloway Sr. and Emma Kate Holloway, and Mr. Holloway gives his birth in Terrell County, Georgia in 1932 to sharecropper parents.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Growing Up Sharecropping in Terrell County
clips.summary: Lucius Holloway describes a childhood of sharecropping where the landlord took out the bills and nothing was ever left, yet he remembers it as a good life with food, rest, and loving parents, in a county where cotton and farming were embedded in everything.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Enlisting to Help Support the Family
clips.summary: Holloway recounts entering military service in June 1952 as a way to help support his large family, drawing sixty dollars a month as a private and sending an allotment home to his mother.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Meeting His Future Wife in 1955
clips.summary: He tells how, only days after his discharge in June 1955, he met Emma Kate for the first time, a woman he had never seen or heard of before that day.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: The Best Cotton Picker in the Patch
clips.summary: Emma Kate Holloway recalls being a high-school junior in Terrell County who worked at Richmond's Cafe on Main Street and picked four hundred pounds of cotton a day, prouder than her brothers to be her father's best cotton picker.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Moving to Dawson and Buying a House
clips.summary: She describes the family's move to Dawson, Georgia, her father farming rented land for a white landlord, and how after a profitable year the landlord took most of the crop, leaving the family to buy a small house with what little remained while her parents took public work in town.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: A Ride Offered on the Walk Home
clips.summary: Working a job to buy school items, Emma Kate met the young military man Lucius when he rolled up beside her in his car and offered a ride, which she refused because her mother had warned her against riding with boys, especially military boys.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Nine O'Clock, a Proposal, and a Family
clips.summary: She recounts Lucius visiting under her mother's strict nine o'clock curfew, his proposal while she was in the twelfth grade, her condition that she still attend school, and their marriage in June 1957 followed by four children, a daughter, Lucius Jr., Charlene, and Patrick in 1964.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: A Beautician's Trade and a Lost Job
clips.summary: Emma Kate trained in cosmetology at Albany Tech and opened a hair business her husband built for her, but explains that he was fired from the post office for his SCLC voter-registration work, which she frames as a fight so Black people would not be kept down.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Why He Worked So Hard for the Vote
clips.summary: She explains that Lucius pressed the registration work so Black people could better themselves and escape the cotton and corn patches, even as her own customers grew scarce because white people warned them not to fool around with the Holloways.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Reprisals at Home: The Gas and the Job
clips.summary: Emma Kate recounts a bitter winter around 1959 with a newborn on her lap when someone cut the gas line and drained the tank, forcing the family to borrow a kerosene heater and then a wood heater, and describes how the post office took her husband's job over a pretext about his work hours.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: A Warning From the Cook
clips.summary: Emma Kate tells of a night she felt certain they would die, when a Black woman who cooked for white people in the community warned that the whites planned to kill Lucius Holloway that night for his registration work, and begged him not to go to the meeting.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Taking the Children to the Meeting
clips.summary: She recalls Lucius insisting he had to go that night and bringing his whole family along, the four children seated beside her in the church, while a young man outside warned her not to sit by the window because they meant to kill that night.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: The Swapped Car and the Ambush
clips.summary: A friend who had been in the military traded cars with Lucius at the meeting so the family could leave a safe way, and when that friend drove the Holloway car back toward Dawson by the creek, armed men waiting to kill Lucius shone flashlights in his face and let him pass.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: The Long Way Home, the Killer Spared
clips.summary: Emma Kate describes the family taking a roundabout route that turned a thirty-minute drive into roughly three hours, then tells of a man who came to the house a week or two later to kill Lucius, knelt at the door while the family stayed silent in the dark, and later said he simply could not do it and left town.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: First Vice President Since 1955
clips.summary: Lucius picks the story back up, recalling Emma Kate's marriage ultimatum and how the Terrell County NAACP appointed him first vice president in 1955, a position he has held ever since through presidents who were beaten, died, or were killed in office.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Lawsuits and Black Officeholders
clips.summary: He describes staying on course about the vote despite threats and even Black neighbors avoiding him out of fear, and how filing lawsuits in Terrell County got Black people elected and appointed across nearly every office, including a long-serving Black mayor, his own four years on the City Council, and more than seventeen years on the Board of County Commissioners.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Living to See the Change
clips.summary: Holloway reflects that he wanted from boyhood to help people and that the military showed him people living better elsewhere, and expresses amazement and gratitude at living to see the changes he struggled for, citing a newspaper article that named him the last man standing among four local organizers, two of whom were killed.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: Writing the Book of His Life
clips.summary: He mentions writing a book titled Civil Rights Through the Eyes of Lucius Holloway that gathers much of what they discussed, then is asked the closing question about what he wants younger generations to know about the movement years.

clips.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
clips.title: A Message to the Younger Generation
clips.summary: Lucius urges young people to trust God and stay focused on what they believe in, and Emma Kate adds that they can make a difference by respecting their elders and themselves, getting a good education, and staying in school, before the interview closes.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: A Mexican American Childhood in Orange County
clips.summary: Luis Zapata describes growing up in Orange County, California, in a household with a Mexican American father who worked as a mechanical engineer and an Anglo mother who ran a yarn shop. He recalls his parents as liberal Democrats in a politically conservative area.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Racial Identity Left Unspoken at Home
clips.summary: Zapata explains that racial and cultural identity were never discussed in his family, leaving him to navigate it alone. He recounts his older brother being barred from dating a young woman because of his ethnicity in the 1950s.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Leaving Home Young and Learning Trades
clips.summary: Having left home around age nine or ten, Zapata worked gardens, a paper route, and scraped boat hulls near Newport Beach, diving for coins thrown by tour boats. He became a dishwasher, busboy, short-order cook, and eventually a chef who could find work anywhere.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Becoming Political at San Jose State
clips.summary: Zapata recounts entering San Jose State College as a physics major and finding he could not relate to fellow physics students who ignored social issues. Amid the era of Berkeley activism and the Dominican Republic intervention, he grew increasingly political.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Farm Worker Organizing and the Call to Mississippi
clips.summary: While organizing alongside Cesar Chavez's farm workers near San Jose, Zapata heard that the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union needed volunteers and raised his hand. He drops out of college and notes that classmate Luis Valdez was among the many politically active Latinos in the area.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: From Latino Identity to Native American Focus
clips.summary: Zapata reflects that there was no space to explore Latino identity in Mississippi, and that after his time there he focused on Native American issues, arguing that brownness derives primarily from Indigenous heritage. He returns to the practical work of organizing farm workers in the fields.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: How Field Strikes Were Organized
clips.summary: Zapata describes infiltrating fields in ragged clothes because known organizers were barred by farm thugs, then convincing nearby workers to walk out on strike. He distinguishes the publicity-driven grape boycott from the strawberry and asparagus strikes he actually worked.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Rural Isolation and the SNCC Information Network
clips.summary: Zapata reflects on how isolated rural Southerners were, with local papers covering bass-fishing contests rather than national or world news. He notes that SNCC channels often delivered more news of events in Africa and New York than the local media did, and contrasts SNCC's militancy with SCLC.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Driving Donated Cars Into Mississippi
clips.summary: Zapata recalls first meeting the head of the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union and bringing down a donated car loaded with clothes for the movement. Over the years he drove three cars south, including a hearse they passed off as an ambulance so people would ride in it.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: A Militant Instinct and Learning by Doing
clips.summary: Zapata traces his belief in fighting back to childhood and describes how movement work, like ironworking and cooking before it, was learned on the run with little explanation. He notes SNCC expected a seasoned union organizer and he figured it out as he went.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Strong Women, Ministers, and SNCC's Grassroots Ethos
clips.summary: Zapata names the movement's local backbone as strong women and ministers, the latter being harder for the white establishment to fire. He contrasts SNCC's bottom-up, grassroots decision-making with the top-down style of other civil rights organizations.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The One-Room Shack in Cleveland
clips.summary: Zapata describes the one-room shack behind a house in Cleveland that served as the SNCC, COFO, and Mississippi Freedom Labor Union office. Workers slept on the floor behind stacked cinder blocks for protection against drive-by shootings.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The Plantation Family Who Hosted Voter Registration
clips.summary: Zapata recounts staying with a tall, well-respected Black landowner who farmed with his family and drove Zapata to clusters of houses for voter registration before sunup. The family fed him enormous breakfasts, and Zapata believes the location was in Washington or Sunflower County.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Amzie Moore, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Susie Ruffin
clips.summary: Zapata remembers Amzie Moore as quietly strong and a strategist rather than a church speaker, and recalls Fannie Lou Hamer as a tobacco-chewing force who commanded every room. He pairs Hamer with a close ally he identifies as Susie Ruffin.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Organizers: Donaldson, Carmichael, and Moses
clips.summary: Zapata praises Ivanhoe Donaldson's instinct for one-to-one organizing and notes Stokely Carmichael's fearlessness as an organizer. He casts Bob Moses as the intellectual whose thinking shaped SNCC's leadership and its turn toward pan-Africanism.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The Summer Project and an Unintended Legacy
clips.summary: Zapata recalls the 1964 Summer Project already being planned when he arrived, while he attended meetings as a newcomer outside the grand strategy. He argues that the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge helped trigger the white exodus into a solidly Republican South.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Volunteers, Head Start, and Registering for the MFDP
clips.summary: Zapata describes summer volunteers as green but useful in Head Start schools and clothing-distribution programs, while noting that one-on-one field organizing took more time than a summer allowed. He recalls meetings increasingly focused on signing people up for the MFDP.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Based in Cleveland and Organizing in Bars
clips.summary: Zapata recounts keeping his few belongings in Cleveland while sleeping in ditches at places like Delta Pine when being hunted. Unlike most SNCC workers, he organized young people in juke joints over beer, and drove vehicles to precinct meetings rather than taking a front-line role.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Driving the Challenge Delegation to Washington
clips.summary: Zapata explains he did not attend the Jackson convention or Atlantic City, but drove a delegation to Washington for the congressional challenge, lodging in All Souls Church basement and lobbying daily. He shares a story of a Washington officer baffled by his carload of bandana-wearing field workers with Mississippi tags.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Witnesses and Facilitators, Not Decision Makers
clips.summary: Zapata stresses that his job was logistics and turnout rather than strategy, and that the MFDP relied on local people for leadership. He recalls Lawrence Guyot as a powerful figure and stays neutral on the post-challenge split over endorsing Johnson and Humphrey.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Union Strategy and the Limits of Leverage
clips.summary: Zapata describes having more say in union work, including starting a cooperative and weighing whether strikes could hold against plantation owners bringing in replacement labor. He notes that mechanization had reduced leverage, though hand labor like chopping and picking cotton still gave organizers some power.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Small Strikes and Limited Wage Victories
clips.summary: Zapata recounts striking individual plantations, setting up food banks, and raising money up north to sustain striking families. The union never coordinated a Delta-wide strike, but won wage increases on several plantations that pressured neighboring owners to match them.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The Tent City and a Strike He Missed
clips.summary: Zapata recalls hearing of a strike that led to evictions and a tent city, which he places outside Bolivar County and associates with a local woman organizer named Blackwell. He explains the encampment was set up while he was up north and that it had internal problems.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Fundraising Trips and the SNCC Newsletter
clips.summary: Zapata explains SNCC scheduled his fundraising trips to California, New York, Boston, and Washington, where local SNCC offices collected checks, food, and clothing. He recalls staff staying connected through WATS-line phone calls and a New York newsletter he attributes to a SNCC artist whose name he is unsure of.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The Union Director and the Waveland Tensions
clips.summary: Zapata describes the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union director, whom he names as Lee Bankhead and identifies as a woman from the Cleveland area, as thoughtful and well organized. He recalls the resentment toward white volunteers who tried to make decisions, a tension discussed at the Waveland meeting he did not attend.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: An Office Colleague and Freedom Songs
clips.summary: Zapata mentions a dear friend, a SNCC office worker he describes as tremendously organized and analytical and likens to Bob Moses, though he says she rarely came into the field. He turns to the role of music and his favorite movement stories.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Deputy Weeks and a Firebombed Porch
clips.summary: Zapata tells of a brutal deputy he names as Weeks who once put a shotgun in his chest, and whose attitude changed sharply after someone threw a firebomb on his porch. He also recalls wiring SNCC cars so the taillights could be switched off when being chased at night.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The Deacons for Defense Visit Cleveland
clips.summary: Zapata recounts a small group from the Deacons for Defense and Justice coming up from Louisiana to meet local people, holding several meetings where he lived even though SNCC was officially nonviolent. He notes the standing debate over whether nonviolence was a moral commitment or a tactic.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Weighing Self-Defense Against Firepower
clips.summary: Zapata recalls telling local people that taking up their hunting rifles against the armed power structure would not work. He returns to the isolated case of Deputy Weeks, the on-camera shotgun incident, and the proliferation of out-of-control auxiliary police in Mississippi.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The Meredith March and News That Did Not Reach Him
clips.summary: Asked about the 1966 Meredith March where Carmichael raised the phrase black power in Greenwood, Zapata says he does not remember it because national news rarely reached the Delta. He describes SNCC turning the march into a voter-registration campaign after the Voting Rights Act.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Mass Arrest at the Jackson Fairgrounds
clips.summary: Zapata recalls the large Jackson demonstration, likely in 1965, where everyone was arrested and held at the fairgrounds, men and women in separate pens. He started in solitary confinement as a suspected organizer, and recounts witnessing a jailer assault a woman who fought back.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Shielding People and the Worst Jail
clips.summary: Zapata describes lying over people to shield them from clubs during demonstrations, and seeing an arrest record inches thick. He recalls the worst jail, a single sloped Black cell with an overflowing toilet and urine-soaked mattresses, and carving chess pieces from melted foam cups to pass time.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Adapting to Years in Mississippi
clips.summary: Reflecting on four years in Mississippi, Zapata says he believed in changing the system and accepted his role as a minion doing work he believed in. He recalls returning to California to share information with farm workers between stretches in the Delta.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Latinos in SNCC and the Segmented Farm Strikes
clips.summary: Zapata notes how few Latinos were in SNCC and that a researcher has since connected him with others, though he is unsure of their backgrounds. He describes California farm-worker organizing as segmented by crop, with separate strawberry, tomato, and grape strikes in the Delano and San Joaquin valleys.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Isolation After Leaving and the Union Buttons
clips.summary: Zapata reflects that once you left a place you were isolated, making it hard to learn what happened in Mississippi afterward. Shown old Mississippi Freedom Labor Union documents he believes he typed, he mentions keeping a SNCC pin and a Freedom Labor Union button depicting a hand holding a lantern.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Typing Flyers on a Portable Typewriter
clips.summary: Zapata describes typing union materials on a little portable typewriter and on church typewriters, with meetings run by the union director and attended by ministers, Susie Ruffin, and sometimes Fannie Lou Hamer. He explains copies were made by mimeograph and a stencil-cutting machine rather than photocopiers.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Mimeograph, the Atlanta Print Shop, and Sign-Ups
clips.summary: Zapata contrasts their hand-cranked mimeograph work with the print shop SNCC eventually ran in Atlanta, and recalls asking northern visitors to bring printed flyers. He describes signing people up at the back of churches after speeches and freedom singing, often filling in forms for those who could not read.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Hardship, Joy, and Freedom Songs
clips.summary: Zapata insists that alongside beatings there were good times: dancing, laughter, intimate relationships, and songs sung in jail, fields, and churches. He sings a verse of We Are Soldiers in the Army, recalling he sang bass and that the Freedom Singers would tear everyone up.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Delta Pine and Memories of the Communist Party
clips.summary: Zapata recalls voter-registration work at Delta Pine, an old-style plantation with sharecropper cabins, where elderly people by the river remembered Communist Party organizers floating in by boat decades earlier. He realized the 1960s were not the beginning of organizing in Mississippi.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Hiding Out and the Run to Yazoo City
clips.summary: Zapata recounts hiding in ditches for days while trucks searched for them at Delta Pine, eating uncooked greens, his companion forced to shave his matted hair. He recalls being met by police in Yazoo City and ordered to turn around or face months in jail, and his inability to recall the companion's name, a nephew of Fannie Lou Hamer.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Deciding It Was Time to Leave
clips.summary: Zapata explains he left Mississippi when many new groups and volunteers arrived and he no longer felt essential. While doing fundraising in Washington he was recruited by a cooks, hotel, and restaurant workers union that paid a real salary during a citywide strike.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Union Work, Mexico, and Native Religion
clips.summary: Zapata recounts staying in Washington for union work, then traveling extensively through Mexico, Central and South America, where he engaged deeply with traditional Native religion and became a priest able to perform marriages. He worked foundries in Minneapolis and ranches in Arizona while doing political work.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: What the Movement Taught Him About Fear
clips.summary: Zapata reflects that the movement taught him to organize across differences and broadened his horizons. He describes never having a good sense of fear, a trait that carried through farm-worker beatings and ironworking on towers and bridges, leaving him a bulldog for what he believes is right.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Returning for the Mike Espy Campaign
clips.summary: Zapata describes taking a van of volunteers back to the Delta for Mike Espy's congressional campaign and seeing how much the towns had changed while the rural areas had not. He recalls Espy winning by 82 votes and using that margin to show every volunteer that their work mattered.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The MFDP Button and a Political Quilt
clips.summary: Zapata shows an official Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party button he believes is among the few left, alongside his collection of SNCC and CORE buttons. He describes making his daughter a quilt stitched from decades of political-event T-shirts, a record of his activist history.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Fannie Lou Hamer's Command of the Room
clips.summary: Zapata returns to Fannie Lou Hamer, recalling that her real power was dominating meetings where major SNCC figures deferred to her rather than her public speeches. He remembers debates over running symbolic candidates and over the literacy tests, from interpreting the Constitution to guessing beans in a jar.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: A Hostile FBI and the Programs People Built
clips.summary: Zapata recalls that registering voters remained hard even after the Voting Rights Act, with a Southern-staffed FBI that mostly upheld the system. He describes well-funded groups like the Delta Ministry arriving, and the all-hands ethos of building Head Start programs and fixing up buildings around the clock.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Grenada, Failed Causes, and Moral Victories
clips.summary: Zapata describes designing ferrocement fishing boats for revolutionary Grenada's bottom-up government before the United States invasion ended the project. He recounts a fondness for difficult causes, including two Jesse Jackson campaigns and a losing race where he served as a coordinator against far better-funded opponents.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The Zapatistas and Indigenous Land in Chiapas
clips.summary: Zapata explains working with the Zapatistas in Chiapas, where mostly Mayan and other Indigenous groups reclaimed land held by absentee owners and faced hired paramilitary police. He recalls going down to document several massacres of men, women, and children.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Electoral Power as a Tool for Justice
clips.summary: Zapata describes a support group pressuring federal authorities in Mexico, with a phone call from a senator's office helping halt police mayhem. He argues that elected officials respond to the ability to turn out votes, crediting that grunt work for marriage equality and the Dream Act passing in Maryland.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The Voting Rights Decision and a Wider Lens
clips.summary: Zapata names the 2013 Supreme Court decision gutting part of the Voting Rights Act as a present concern, noting courts are hard to influence through political work. He reflects that his focus widened beyond the United States from the isolation of the South to struggles worldwide, including the Palestinian cause.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: La Raza and the Letter-Writing Era
clips.summary: Zapata says he was one of the original members of La Raza, a distant member based on the East Coast, alongside a colleague he describes as a close kinswoman. He reflects on how organizing once depended on letters, and that he still has boxes of political correspondence in his attic.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: The DC Statehood Party and Local Lefties
clips.summary: Zapata recalls working in the DC Statehood Party after arriving in Washington, alongside local movement people. He names key figures including Julius Hobson, Josephine Butler of a US-Soviet friendship society, and Hilda Mason.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Immigration, Borders, and Persistent Racism
clips.summary: Zapata argues that borders are artificial and questions who invited the pilgrims, framing immigration through the displacement of Indigenous people. He warns that despite political attention to Latino issues, struggling schools and persistent racism show the Voting Rights Act addressed only a small part of poverty and inequality.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: A Missing Generation of Activists
clips.summary: Zapata observes that some young people are deeply involved while a middle generation largely was not, and faults activists of his era for not raising their own children to be active. He notes that today's youth are far better informed about politics and the wider world than he was at twenty-one.

clips.subject: Luis Zapata
clips.title: Fighting It One Step at a Time
clips.summary: Zapata closes by warning of sophisticated corporate efforts to suppress change and urging that the struggle be fought one step at a time. He explains he works one-on-one rather than in classrooms, telling people it is not what it was like but what it is like, as the interview ends.
